════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #001: CONTEXT-AWARE RESPONSES
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Contextual Awareness
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           A
ESTIMATED HOURS: 12
OVERLAPS WITH:   #006 (Conversation Memory), #109 (Session History Awareness)
BLOCKS:          #003, #009, #042 (need context to generate relevant suggestions)
BLOCKED BY:      #037 (Streaming Text), #002 (Rich Response Formatting)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Context-Aware Responses is the foundational intelligence layer that makes Athena
fundamentally different from a generic chatbot. Every response Athena generates is
informed by the COMPLETE state of the research lineage being explored, not just the
user's message in isolation. When a user asks "what is important here?", a generic
chatbot would give a general answer about research importance. Athena knows that
THIS specific lineage has 601 papers, that 3 authors control 67% of structural
impact, that there are 13 active contradictions, and that 45% of the graph depends
on a single paper from 1996.

This feature exists because without it, Athena is just another chatbot. The entire
value proposition of Arivu is that analysis is DATA-DRIVEN, computed from the actual
citation graph, not recalled from LLM training data. Context awareness is what makes
"removing VGGNet collapses 42%" a computed fact rather than a hallucinated number.

The user experiences this transparently: they ask a question and the response contains
specific facts about THEIR lineage. They never need to provide context manually. The
system knows which papers are bottlenecks, which authors dominate, which clusters
exist, and what the user just clicked in the graph. Every response reflects this
knowledge automatically.

What makes this different from ChatGPT or Gemini: those systems know about ResNet
from training data. Athena knows about ResNet from COMPUTED GRAPH ANALYSIS. When
Athena says "removing VGGNet collapses 42%," it is stating a fact verified by BFS
pruning simulation on the actual citation graph, not recalling pre-training data.

This feature connects to virtually every other feature in Athena. Feature #003
(Quick Action Buttons) needs context to generate relevant suggestions. Features
#105-#108 (Click/Zoom/Filter/Prune-Aware) ARE dynamic context. Feature #042
(Suggested Follow-Ups) references context to suggest next steps.

The implementation uses two components: a StaticContextBuilder (computed once on
graph load, produces a ~500 token summary for Groq and full JSON for Gemini) and
a DynamicContextTracker (listens to graph events, maintains current user focus).
These are combined by a ContextInjector that formats context for each LLM model.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
Automatic. Context awareness activates the moment the graph loads. Every Athena
response is context-aware by default. No user action needed to enable it.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User loads a paper lineage. Within 500ms of graph load, StaticContextBuilder
   computes: paper count, edge count, year range, bottlenecks (top 5), author
   stats (top 5), cluster names, contradiction count, trust score. This context
   is stored in memory.

2. User opens Athena and types "What should I know about this?" The system prompt
   contains ~500 tokens of lineage context. The response references 601 papers,
   68 years, 3 dominant authors, 13 contradictions, and the 45% single-thread
   dependency. Every fact comes from graph data, not training data.

3. User clicks VGGNet in the graph. DynamicContextTracker captures the event
   within 50ms. Stores: paper title, ID, year, citations, depth, bottleneck status.

4. User types "Is this paper important?" The ContextInjector appends "User is
   currently looking at VGGNet (2014)" to the message. The LLM resolves "this
   paper" to VGGNet and responds with lineage-specific analysis.

5. User zooms into the detection cluster. Tracker identifies the cluster from
   visible nodes. User asks "what is happening here?" The LLM knows "here" means
   the detection cluster and responds specifically about those 42 papers.

6. User applies the "contradictions" filter. Tracker captures the filter type.
   User asks "tell me about these." The LLM knows "these" = contradiction edges
   because the context says "Active filter: contradictions (13 edges)."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Context awareness is never "complete." It continuously updates as the user
interacts with the graph. When the user navigates to a different paper lineage,
static context rebuilds from scratch and dynamic context resets to empty.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena/athena-context.js: StaticContextBuilder, DynamicContextTracker,
  ContextInjector classes. All context computation and prompt injection.
- static/js/athena/athena-engine.js: Main chat engine. Calls ContextInjector before
  every LLM request to build the full system prompt.
- app.py (POST /api/athena/message): Receives context JSON from frontend, builds
  system prompt with context appendix, routes to LLM pipeline.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
StaticContextBuilder: Runs once on graph load via arivu:graph-loaded event. Reads
the full graphData JSON, computes summary statistics, stores result in module-level
variable. Produces two outputs: toPromptString() for Groq (~500 tokens), toFullJSON()
for Gemini (full graph). Never recomputed unless arivu:graph-loaded fires again.

DynamicContextTracker: Listens to arivu:node-clicked, arivu:zoom-changed,
arivu:filter-applied, arivu:prune-complete. Each event updates internal state with
paper details + timestamp. toPromptString() generates ~100 token current-state
description. Uses past tense for events older than 5 minutes.

ContextInjector: Reads StaticContextBuilder + DynamicContextTracker + user profile.
Formats for each LLM: forGroq() returns ~600 token appendix, forGemini() returns
full graph JSON + history + profile.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
POST /api/athena/message receives static_context (summary object) and dynamic_context
(string) in the request body. Builds the system prompt by concatenating the master
prompt with the lineage context appendix. Routes to Groq for classification and draft,
then Gemini for refinement (both receive context).

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class StaticContextBuilder {
    build(graphData) {
        const nodes = graphData.nodes || [];
        const edges = graphData.edges || [];
        const meta = graphData.metadata || {};
        const years = nodes.map(n => n.year).filter(Boolean);
        return {
            totalPapers: nodes.length,
            totalEdges: edges.length,
            yearRange: { min: Math.min(...years), max: Math.max(...years) },
            seedPaper: { title: meta.seed_paper_title, id: meta.seed_paper_id },
            bottlenecks: nodes.filter(n => n.is_bottleneck)
                .sort((a, b) => (b.pruning_impact||0) - (a.pruning_impact||0)).slice(0, 5),
            topAuthors: this._computeAuthors(nodes).slice(0, 5),
            clusters: (graphData.dna_profile?.clusters || []).map(c => ({ name: c.name, count: c.paper_count||0 })),
            contradictions: edges.filter(e => e.mutation_type === 'contradiction').length,
            trustScore: Math.round(edges.filter(e => ['HIGH','MEDIUM'].includes(e.confidence_tier)).length / Math.max(edges.length, 1) * 100),
        };
    }
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
```
POST /api/athena/message
Request: {
    "message": "What should I know about this?",
    "conversation_id": "conv_abc123",
    "static_context": {
        "totalPapers": 601, "totalEdges": 968,
        "yearRange": {"min": 1955, "max": 2023},
        "seedPaper": {"title": "Deep Residual Learning...", "year": 2016},
        "bottlenecks": [...], "topAuthors": [...],
        "clusters": [...], "contradictions": 13, "trustScore": 19
    },
    "dynamic_context": "CURRENT STATE: User is looking at VGGNet (2014).",
    "user_profile": {"research_area": "efficient transformers"}
}
Response: SSE stream (see #037 Streaming Text)
```

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Context Loading (during graph load, before context ready)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  ATHENA                                                      |
|                                                              |
|  +- LOADING CONTEXT ----------------------------------------+|
|  |  o  Analyzing lineage structure...                       ||
|  |  o  Computing bottleneck papers...                       ||
|  |  o  Extracting author statistics...                      ||
|  |  oooooo....  50%                                         ||
|  +----------------------------------------------------------+|
|                                                              |
|  +------------------------------------------+                |
|  | Ask about this research...           [->]|                |
|  +------------------------------------------+                |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```
Note: o = dot matrix empty circle, filled circles for completed steps.
No emojis. Checkmarks rendered as 3-line SVG icons.

STATE: Context Active (normal operation)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  ATHENA                                                      |
|                                                              |
|  +- ATHENA IS ANALYZING ------------------------------------+|
|  |  [check] Lineage: 601 papers, 968 edges                 ||
|  |  [check] Focus: VGGNet (clicked 3s ago)                  ||
|  |  [check] Filter: none                                    ||
|  |  [check] Profile: efficient transformers                 ||
|  |  [dot]   Generating with full context...                 ||
|  +----------------------------------------------------------+|
|                                                              |
|  (response streams below)                                    |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```
[check] = dot matrix checkmark SVG, [dot] = filled circle

STATE: Context Error (graph data malformed or missing)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  ATHENA                                                      |
|                                                              |
|  +- ! CONTEXT UNAVAILABLE ----------------------------------+|
|  |                                                          ||
|  |  Could not load lineage context. Responses will be       ||
|  |  general rather than lineage-specific.                   ||
|  |                                                          ||
|  |  [Retry loading context]                                 ||
|  +----------------------------------------------------------+|
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```
! = monospace exclamation, not emoji.

TRANSITIONS:
Loading -> Active: Loading panel fades out, 300ms, ease-out.
Loading -> Error: Cross-fade to error panel, 200ms.
Error -> Loading: User clicks retry. Loading indicator shows.
Error -> Active: Retry succeeds. Loading fades out, 300ms.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Graph loads (from R2 cache or SSE build)
    |
StaticContextBuilder.build(graphData) [athena-context.js]
    |--- Computes all fields from graph JSON
    |--- Stores in module-level variable
    |
User interacts with graph
    |
DynamicContextTracker captures event [athena-context.js]
    |--- arivu:node-clicked -> paper details + timestamp
    |--- arivu:zoom-changed -> cluster identification
    |--- arivu:filter-applied -> filter type
    |--- arivu:prune-complete -> collapse results
    |
User sends Athena message
    |
ContextInjector combines static + dynamic [athena-context.js]
    |--- forGroq(): ~600 token string
    |--- forGemini(): full JSON + history + profile
    |
POST /api/athena/message [app.py]
    |--- Builds system prompt = master + context appendix
    |--- Routes to Groq (classify, draft) then Gemini (refine)
    |
LLM generates context-informed response
    |--- Groq uses compact context (~600 tokens)
    |--- Gemini uses full context (up to 200K tokens)
    |
SSE stream to frontend

ERROR PATH:
graphData.nodes missing/malformed
    |
StaticContextBuilder.build() catches error
    |--- Returns minimal context with error flag
    |--- Frontend shows CONTEXT ERROR state
    |--- User can still chat (responses are generic)

LLM call fails
    |
LLMRouter fallback: Groq -> Gemini -> Offline
    |--- Offline: template response from graph data only

DATA STORED:
Static context: in-memory JS variable. Not persisted to DB.
Rebuilt on every page load from graph JSON. No cross-session persistence.

DATA CACHED:
Static context computed once per graph load. TTL: until page navigation.
No external cache (Redis, sessionStorage). Invalidated by new graph load.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: Yes (context is injected INTO other features' LLM calls)

MODEL: Both Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile AND Gemini Flash 2.0
STAGE: Applied to ALL stages (Classification, Generation, Refinement)

PROMPT SECTION: "LINEAGE CONTEXT" appended to every system prompt

ADDITIONAL PROMPT (literal text, variables replaced at runtime):
```
LINEAGE CONTEXT:
You are analyzing the research lineage of "[seed_title]" ([seed_year]),
[seed_citations] citations. [totalPapers] papers, [totalEdges] edges,
spanning [yearMin]-[yearMax] ([yearSpan] years).
Clusters: [cluster_list].
Bottlenecks: [bottleneck_list with impact percentages].
Top authors: [author_list with paper counts].
Contradictions: [count]. Trust: [pct]%.

CURRENT USER STATE:
[Dynamic context string from DynamicContextTracker.toPromptString()]

USER RESEARCH PROFILE:
[If known: research area, competitors, knowledge level.
If unknown: "Research position not yet established."]

CONTEXT USAGE RULES:
- Reference specific papers by name and year when relevant
- Use exact numbers from data (216,247 citations, not "many")
- Resolve "this paper" / "that one" using CURRENT STATE
- Relate findings to user's research if profile is known
- Never fabricate data not present in the context
```

EXPECTED OUTPUT FORMAT:
This feature does not produce its own output. It modifies the prompts
of other features' LLM calls. The output format depends on the calling
feature. Example: when Feature #042 asks for follow-up suggestions,
the context enables suggestions like "Ask about the VGGNet you clicked"
instead of generic "Ask another question."

FALLBACK IF LLM FAILS:
If context causes token overflow for Groq: truncate to top 3 bottlenecks,
top 3 authors (reduces ~600 to ~200 tokens).
If context data unavailable (graph not loaded): omit LINEAGE CONTEXT
section, add note "Context unavailable. Analysis may be less specific."
If both LLMs down: offline mode uses graph data directly (no LLM context
injection needed since templates compute from data).

TOKEN BUDGET:
Groq input context: 500-800 tokens (within 128K limit)
Gemini input context: up to 200,000 tokens (within 1M limit)
Dynamic context: 50-150 tokens per message
User profile: 50-100 tokens

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Trigger                     | Element                    | Action                                         |
|-----------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| Graph loads                 | StaticContextBuilder       | Computes full static context, stores in memory  |
| Click paper in graph        | DynamicContextTracker      | Stores paper details + timestamp                |
| Zoom into area              | DynamicContextTracker      | Identifies cluster, updates state               |
| Apply filter                | DynamicContextTracker      | Stores filter type                              |
| Execute pruning             | DynamicContextTracker      | Stores collapse results                         |
| Send Athena message         | ContextInjector            | Combines static + dynamic for LLM prompt        |
| Navigate to new paper       | StaticContextBuilder       | Rebuilds static, resets dynamic                 |
| Click Retry on error        | StaticContextBuilder       | Re-attempts context computation                 |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Loading progress bar: dot matrix dots appear left to right, 50ms per dot, ease-out
- Loading -> Active: opacity 1 to 0, 300ms, ease-out
- Loading -> Error: cross-fade, 200ms, ease-out
- Error -> Loading: immediate (no fade), loading indicator appears instantly

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE (Athena uses no audio)

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Loading panel: role="status", aria-live="polite"
- Error panel: role="alert" for immediate screen reader announcement
- Retry button: tabindex="0", activates on Enter/Space
- Thinking display collapsible via Enter key on header
- All status changes announced by screen reader via aria-live region

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

EDGE CASE 1: User clicks paper then asks unrelated question
Scenario: User clicks VGGNet, then asks "what year was the oldest paper?"
Expected behavior: LLM answers directly (the oldest year) without forcing
a VGGNet reference. Context informs ambiguous references but does not
override specific questions.
Implementation: The prompt says "resolve AMBIGUOUS references using context"
not "always mention the clicked paper." Specific questions bypass context.

EDGE CASE 2: Very small graph (10-20 papers)
Scenario: Lineage has only 16 papers (e.g., a shallow build).
Expected behavior: Context is smaller. LLM acknowledges limited data when
making structural claims. Trust analysis caveats included.
Implementation: StaticContextBuilder checks nodes.length. If < 50, sets
limitedData flag. Prompt includes "Small lineage. Acknowledge limited data."

EDGE CASE 3: Stale context (click was 10+ minutes ago)
Scenario: User clicked VGGNet 15 minutes ago, asks "is this important?"
Expected behavior: LLM says "You previously looked at VGGNet" (past tense)
not "You are currently looking at" (present tense).
Implementation: DynamicContextTracker.toPromptString() checks timestamp.
If age > 300000ms (5 min): "previously looked at." If < 5 min: "is looking at."

EDGE CASE 4: Navigate to different lineage
Scenario: User was on ResNet (601 papers), navigates to BERT.
Expected behavior: Static context rebuilt for BERT. Dynamic context resets.
No ResNet data leaks. Cross-lineage memory (Feature #022) may reference
ResNet but active context is purely BERT.
Implementation: arivu:graph-loaded triggers build() with new data. reset()
clears DynamicContextTracker state.

EDGE CASE 5: Concurrent event during streaming
Scenario: User clicks a paper while response is streaming.
Expected behavior: Current stream uninterrupted. New click captured by
tracker. NEXT response uses the new click context, not the current one.
Implementation: Context snapshot taken once per message send. Dynamic
tracker updates independently. No mid-stream context injection.

EDGE CASE 6: Malformed graph JSON
Scenario: R2 returns graph where nodes is undefined or not an array.
Expected behavior: Error state shown. User can still chat generically.
Implementation: build() wraps all computation in try/catch. Returns
{totalPapers: 0, error: 'malformed_graph'}. Frontend shows error state.

EDGE CASE 7: Groq context overflow
Scenario: Static context string exceeds token budget.
Expected behavior: Progressive truncation preserving most important data.
Implementation: toPromptString() estimates tokens (words * 1.3). If over
budget, drops: mutation distribution first, then clusters, then authors
beyond top 3, then bottlenecks beyond top 3. Core stats never removed.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON (must be built first):
- Feature #037 (Streaming Text): Context injected into streaming pipeline.
  Without streaming, no LLM call exists to inject context into.
- Feature #002 (Rich Response Formatting): Context-informed responses use
  markdown, stat cards, and paper references requiring rich formatting.

ENABLES (can be built after this):
- Feature #003 (Quick Action Buttons): Generated based on context.
- Feature #009 (Proactive Suggestions): Need context awareness for relevance.
- Feature #042 (Suggested Follow-Ups): Reference current context.
- Feature #105 (Click-Aware Chat): Click awareness IS dynamic context.
- Feature #106 (Zoom-Aware Chat): Zoom awareness IS dynamic context.
- Feature #107 (Filter-Aware Chat): Filter awareness IS dynamic context.
- Feature #108 (Prune-Aware Chat): Prune awareness IS dynamic context.

ENHANCES (improves but does not require):
- Feature #006 (Conversation Memory): Memory + context = full awareness.
- Feature #109 (Session History): History + context = behavior + focus.

CONFLICTS WITH:
- Feature #049 (Mode Selector): Both modify system prompt. Resolution:
  mode appendix appended AFTER context appendix. Both coexist. Managed
  by system prompt builder which concatenates sections in fixed order.

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES:
- Groq API (groq==0.8.0): Primary LLM. Falls back to Gemini if unavailable.
- Gemini API (google-generativeai): Refinement LLM. Skipped if unavailable.
- arivu:* custom events: Dispatched by graph.js, tree-layout.js, pruning.js.
  If events not dispatched, dynamic context stays empty (static still works).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ StaticContextBuilder computes all fields from any valid graph JSON
□ toPromptString() output under 800 tokens for 601-paper graph
□ DynamicContextTracker captures arivu:node-clicked within 50ms
□ DynamicContextTracker captures arivu:zoom-changed and identifies cluster
□ DynamicContextTracker captures arivu:filter-applied
□ DynamicContextTracker captures arivu:prune-complete
□ Past tense for events older than 5 minutes
□ forGroq() produces compact context under 800 tokens
□ forGemini() includes full graph JSON, history, and profile
□ LLM responses reference specific lineage facts
□ LLM resolves "this paper" to last clicked paper

VISUAL:
□ Loading indicator uses dot matrix progress bar (not CSS bar)
□ Error state uses monospace "!" (not emoji), includes retry button
□ All thinking display indicators use dot matrix icons (no emojis)

QUALITY:
□ No console errors during context building or event tracking
□ Works with 0, 1, 16, and 601 papers without crash
□ Graceful degradation if graph JSON malformed (error state, not crash)

ACCESSIBILITY:
□ Loading panel has role="status" and aria-live="polite"
□ Error panel has role="alert"
□ Retry button keyboard accessible (Enter/Space)

INTEGRATION:
□ Does not break any existing Athena feature
□ Coexists with mode selector system prompt modifications

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round -- agent confirms ZERO remaining issues
□ BROWSER TEST: Feature verified in Chrome via browsermcp
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Implementation matches this spec document exactly

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-context.js (all 3 classes), athena-engine.js (context injection),
   app.py (POST /api/athena/message route)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Load 601-paper graph, verify static context computes all fields without NaN
   b. Load empty graph (0 nodes), verify error state appears with retry button
   c. Click paper in graph, send message, verify system prompt contains paper name
   d. Click paper, wait 6 minutes, send message, verify "previously" (past tense)
   e. Navigate to different paper, verify context resets (no old data leaks)
   f. Send message with full context, verify Groq receives <800 tokens
   g. Verify Gemini receives full graph JSON (check request payload size)
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Math.min(...years) returns Infinity when years array is empty
   b. DynamicContextTracker event listeners accumulate (memory leak on re-mount)
   c. Context appendix causes Groq prompt to exceed model context window
   d. Stale context from previous lineage appears in new lineage responses
5. VERIFY no regression in: #037 (Streaming Text), #002 (Rich Formatting)
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why none
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #002: RICH RESPONSE FORMATTING
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Core Infrastructure
PRIORITY:        P0
PHASE:           A
ESTIMATED HOURS: 8
OVERLAPS WITH:   #038 (Markdown Rendering)
BLOCKS:          #058-#067 (all response content blocks depend on the base formatting system)
BLOCKED BY:      NONE (this is a foundation feature)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Rich Response Formatting is the rendering engine that transforms structured JSON
blocks from the LLM pipeline into visually rich, interactive HTML inside the chat.
Without this feature, Athena responses would be plain text paragraphs. With it,
responses contain headings with monospace uppercase styling, bold and italic text,
inline code with syntax highlighting, ordered and numbered lists, paper names that
are clickable links to the graph, stat cards with large numbers, data tables with
sortable columns, quote blocks with colored left borders, warning and alert panels,
expandable sections, and takeaway boxes with gold backgrounds.

This feature exists because the core value of Athena is not just WHAT it says but
HOW it presents information. A table comparing two papers communicates structure
that a paragraph cannot. A stat card with "601 papers" has more visual impact than
the sentence "there are 601 papers." A warning block with a red border draws the
eye to risk information that would be lost in prose. The formatting IS the analysis.

The user experiences this as responses that look like a professional research report,
not a chat message. When Athena analyzes a lineage, the response might contain: a
heading ("KEY FINDINGS"), a stat grid (4 numbers), two paragraphs of analysis with
[paper names] as clickable links, an expandable evidence trail ("8 papers adopted
this, click to see"), a warning about a fragility risk, and a gold takeaway box.
Each element is a distinct visual component rendered from a JSON block.

What makes this different from ChatGPT or Claude: those systems render markdown into
HTML. Athena renders STRUCTURED BLOCKS into INTERACTIVE COMPONENTS. A paper_card
block creates a rich card with title, authors, year, citations, and a "zoom in graph"
button. A comparison block creates side-by-side cards with dot matrix metric bars.
These are not markdown. They are custom components specific to research analysis.

This connects to every response-producing feature in Athena. Features #058-#067
(paper cards, tables, comparisons, timelines, etc.) are all block types rendered by
this system. Feature #037 (Streaming Text) streams blocks that this feature renders.
Feature #001 (Context-Aware Responses) produces blocks that this feature displays.

The implementation uses a BlockRenderer object with a render(blocks, graphNodes)
method. Each block type maps to a rendering function. The renderer handles markdown
within prose blocks via a lightweight parser (not a full markdown library). Paper
names in [brackets] are automatically linked to graph nodes. All rendering uses
vanilla JS DOM manipulation with CSS classes for styling.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
Automatic. Every time the LLM pipeline produces a response, the BlockRenderer
transforms the JSON blocks into DOM elements in the chat area. The user never
interacts with this feature directly. They experience it through the visual
quality of every Athena response.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. The LLM pipeline completes and produces a JSON response:
   {"blocks": [{"type": "heading", "text": "KEY FINDINGS"}, {"type": "prose",
   "text": "[ResNet] introduced..."}, {"type": "stat_card", "label": "Papers",
   "value": "601"}]}. This arrives via SSE stream from the backend.

2. The chat engine receives each block via SSE events. For each block, it calls
   BlockRenderer.renderBlock(block, graphNodes). The renderer identifies the block
   type and calls the appropriate rendering function.

3. A "heading" block renders as: <p> with monospace font, uppercase, letter-spacing
   0.1em, color #374151, with a thin divider line below. The text "KEY FINDINGS"
   appears as a distinct section separator.

4. A "prose" block renders as: <p> with Inter font, 14px, line-height 1.6. Paper
   names in [brackets] are parsed and replaced with <a> tags that have click
   handlers zooming to the paper in the graph. Markdown bold (**text**) and italic
   (*text*) are parsed and rendered as <strong> and <em>.

5. A "stat_card" block renders as: a bordered card with the value "601" in large
   monospace font (28px), the label "Papers" below in small caps, and an optional
   detail line in muted text. The card has 1px #E5E7EB border and 8px border-radius.

6. Each rendered block is appended to the chat message container with a 200ms
   stagger (Response Reveal animation, Feature #075). The user sees blocks
   appearing one after another, not all at once.

AFTER COMPLETION:
The chat message is fully rendered with all blocks visible. Interactive elements
(paper links, expand buttons, table sort buttons) are active. The user can click
paper names, expand evidence trails, sort tables, and interact with all blocks.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena/block-renderer.js: The main BlockRenderer object with
  render(blocks, graphNodes) method and individual rendering functions for
  each of the 58 block types. Also contains linkifyPapers() for paper name
  detection and escapeHtml() for XSS prevention.
- static/js/athena/markdown-parser.js: Lightweight markdown parser for prose
  blocks. Handles: bold, italic, inline code, links, lists, headers. Does NOT
  use a third-party library. Custom 200-line parser.
- static/css/athena-blocks.css: All CSS for rendered blocks. Stat cards,
  tables, quotes, warnings, takeaways, expandable sections, paper cards.
- static/js/athena/athena-engine.js: Calls BlockRenderer.render() when
  processing SSE block events.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
BlockRenderer is a singleton object (not a class instance). It has a render()
method that takes an array of blocks and the graphNodes array (for paper name
linking). It returns an HTML string. Each block type has its own rendering
function: _prose(), _heading(), _statCard(), _table(), _paperCard(), etc.

The markdown parser handles prose block text. It runs AFTER XSS escaping but
BEFORE paper name linking. Order: escapeHtml(text) -> parseMarkdown(escaped)
-> linkifyPapers(parsed, graphNodes). This ensures user-generated content is
safe while allowing markdown formatting and paper links.

Paper name linking works by finding text in [brackets], looking up the bracket
content against graphNodes by title (case-insensitive, fuzzy matching for
truncated titles), and replacing with <a class="paper-link" data-id="xxx">.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No backend component. Rich formatting is entirely client-side. The backend
produces JSON blocks. The frontend renders them.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
const BlockRenderer = {
    render(blocks, graphNodes) {
        if (!blocks || !Array.isArray(blocks))
            return '<p style="color:#9CA3AF">No content available.</p>';
        return blocks.map(b => this._renderBlock(b, graphNodes)).join('');
    },

    _renderBlock(block, graphNodes) {
        switch (block.type) {
            case 'prose': return this._prose(block, graphNodes);
            case 'heading': return this._heading(block);
            case 'takeaway': return this._takeaway(block);
            case 'stat_card': return this._statCard(block);
            case 'table': return this._table(block);
            case 'paper_card': return this._paperCard(block, graphNodes);
            case 'comparison_card': return this._comparison(block, graphNodes);
            // ... 50+ more block types
            default: return '';
        }
    },

    _prose(block, graphNodes) {
        let text = this._esc(block.text || '');
        text = this._parseMarkdown(text);
        text = this._linkifyPapers(text, graphNodes);
        return '<p class="athena-prose">' + text + '</p>';
    },

    _linkifyPapers(text, graphNodes) {
        return text.replace(/\[([^\]]+)\]/g, (match, name) => {
            const node = graphNodes?.find(n =>
                n.title?.toLowerCase().includes(name.toLowerCase())
            );
            if (node) {
                return '<a class="paper-link" data-id="' +
                    this._esc(node.id) + '">' + name + '</a>';
            }
            return name;
        });
    }
};
```

API CONTRACTS:
No API endpoints. This feature is entirely client-side rendering.
Input: JSON blocks array from SSE stream.
Output: HTML string inserted into chat message DOM.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Heading Block
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
|  KEY FINDINGS                                                |
|  ________________________________________________________    |
|                                                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```
Monospace font (JetBrains Mono), uppercase, 11px, letter-spacing 0.1em,
color #6B7280. Thin 1px #E5E7EB divider line below.

STATE: Prose Block with Paper Links
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
|  [ResNet] (He, 2016) introduced skip connections that        |
|  solved the degradation problem. [VGGNet] had proven that    |
|  depth improves accuracy, but networks beyond 19 layers      |
|  saw training loss increase.                                 |
|                                                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```
ResNet and VGGNet are underlined links (color #374151, underline on hover).
Click zooms to that paper in the graph.

STATE: Stat Card
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
|  +------------------+                                        |
|  |                  |                                        |
|  |      601         |  <- 28px monospace, bold, #374151      |
|  |    PAPERS        |  <- 10px small caps, #6B7280           |
|  |  in this lineage |  <- 12px detail, #9CA3AF               |
|  |                  |                                        |
|  +------------------+                                        |
|                                                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```
Card: 1px #E5E7EB border, 8px border-radius, 16px padding, centered text.

STATE: Takeaway Box
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
|  +--TAKEAWAY-----------------------------------------+       |
|  |                                                   |       |
|  |  The structural backbone of this lineage is       |       |
|  |  [Pattern Recognition] (Bishop, 1996), not the    |       |
|  |  more-cited [ResNet]. Verify Bishop's methods     |       |
|  |  independently before building on them.           |       |
|  |                                                   |       |
|  +---------------------------------------------------+       |
|                                                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```
Background: #FEF9C3 (warm yellow). Border: 1px #F59E0B. Label "TAKEAWAY"
in 10px JetBrains Mono, color #92400E.

STATE: Warning Block
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
|  +--! WARNING----------------------------------------+       |
|  |                                                   |       |
|  |  45% of the graph depends on a single paper from  |       |
|  |  1996. This is a single-thread dependency with no  |       |
|  |  alternative path.                                |       |
|  |                                                   |       |
|  +---------------------------------------------------+       |
|                                                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```
Background: #FEF2F2 (light red). Border: 1px #FCA5A5. Label "! WARNING"
in 10px JetBrains Mono, color #991B1B.

STATE: Error (block type unknown)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
|  (nothing rendered for unknown block types -- silently        |
|   skipped. No error shown to user.)                          |
|                                                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

TRANSITIONS:
Each block appears with Response Reveal (#075): opacity 0 to 1, translateY
10px to 0px, 300ms, ease-out. Blocks stagger by 200ms.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

LLM pipeline produces response {"blocks": [...]}
    |
SSE stream sends each block as an event [athena-engine.js]
    |
athena-engine.js receives block event
    |
BlockRenderer.renderBlock(block, graphNodes) [block-renderer.js]
    |--- Identifies block type
    |--- Calls type-specific renderer (_prose, _heading, _statCard, etc.)
    |--- For prose: escapeHtml -> parseMarkdown -> linkifyPapers
    |--- Returns HTML string
    |
HTML inserted into chat message container [athena-engine.js]
    |
Interactive elements wired [athena-engine.js]
    |--- Paper links: click -> _zoomToNode(paperId)
    |--- Expand buttons: click -> toggle expandable content
    |--- Table sort: click header -> sort rows

ERROR PATH:
Block has unknown type
    |--- _renderBlock returns empty string (silent skip)
    |--- No error shown to user, no console error

Block has malformed data (missing required fields)
    |--- Each renderer checks for required fields
    |--- Returns empty string or fallback text if fields missing
    |--- Example: stat_card without value -> shows "N/A"

Paper name in [brackets] not found in graph
    |--- linkifyPapers returns the name as plain text (no link)
    |--- No error, no broken UI

DATA STORED:
Rendered HTML exists in the DOM only. Not persisted independently.
The source JSON blocks are stored in the chat_messages DB table.

DATA CACHED:
No caching. Blocks are rendered on every load from stored JSON.
If conversation is restored from DB, blocks are re-rendered.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: No

This feature renders LLM output. It does not call the LLM. The block types
and their structure are defined by the LLM pipeline (Features #001, #037)
and the block vocabulary in the master prompt. This feature is the consumer
of that output, not the producer.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Trigger          | Element                    | Action                                         |
|------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| Click            | Paper name link            | Zooms to paper in graph, highlights, dims others|
| Click            | Expandable section header  | Toggles content visibility (show/hide)          |
| Click            | Table column header        | Sorts table rows by that column                 |
| Click            | Copy button (code blocks)  | Copies code content to clipboard                |
| Click            | "Zoom in graph" on cards   | Zooms to paper in graph                         |
| Hover            | Paper name link            | Underline appears, cursor becomes pointer       |
| Hover            | Table row                  | Row background highlights to #F9FAFB            |
| Keyboard Tab     | Through interactive blocks | Focus moves through links, buttons, headers     |
| Keyboard Enter   | On focused paper link      | Same as click (zooms to paper)                  |
| Keyboard Enter   | On focused expand button   | Toggles expandable section                      |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Block entrance (Response Reveal): opacity 0 to 1, translateY 10px to 0,
  300ms, ease-out. Staggered 200ms between blocks.
- Expandable toggle: height animates from 0 to scrollHeight, 200ms, ease-out.
  Collapse: height from current to 0, 200ms, ease-in.
- Table sort: rows cross-fade to new order, 150ms, ease-in-out.
- Paper link hover: underline fades in, 100ms.

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE (Athena uses no audio)

ACCESSIBILITY:
- All paper links have aria-label="View [paper title] in graph"
- Expandable sections have aria-expanded="true/false"
- Tables have role="table", headers have scope="col"
- Copy buttons have aria-label="Copy code to clipboard"
- Tab order follows visual order (top to bottom, left to right)
- Screen reader announces block types via aria-label on container:
  "Heading: KEY FINDINGS", "Statistic: 601 papers", "Warning: 45% dependency"

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

EDGE CASE 1: Empty blocks array
Scenario: LLM returns {"blocks": []} (no content).
Expected behavior: Chat shows "No content available." in muted text.
Implementation: render() checks if blocks is empty array, returns
fallback message.

EDGE CASE 2: Extremely long prose block (>2000 characters)
Scenario: LLM generates a very long paragraph.
Expected behavior: Text renders fully. No truncation. The chat message
grows to accommodate. Scroll within the chat area works normally.
Implementation: No max-height on prose blocks. CSS handles long text
with word-wrap: break-word.

EDGE CASE 3: Paper name in brackets matches multiple graph nodes
Scenario: [Neural Networks] matches 3 papers with similar titles.
Expected behavior: Links to the FIRST match (highest citation count).
Implementation: linkifyPapers sorts matches by citation_count descending
and uses the first match.

EDGE CASE 4: Nested markdown in prose (bold inside italic)
Scenario: Text contains "***bold italic***" or "**bold *italic* bold**".
Expected behavior: Renders correctly with nested formatting.
Implementation: Markdown parser handles nesting by processing bold first,
then italic. RegExp-based, tested against common nesting patterns.

EDGE CASE 5: Table with 0 rows
Scenario: LLM returns a table block with headers but no rows.
Expected behavior: Shows headers with an empty body and "No data" message.
Implementation: _table() checks rows.length. If 0, appends a single row
spanning all columns with "No data available" text.

EDGE CASE 6: XSS in block content
Scenario: Block text contains <script>alert('xss')</script>.
Expected behavior: Script tags are escaped to &lt;script&gt;. No execution.
Implementation: escapeHtml() runs BEFORE any markdown parsing or paper linking.
All content is escaped at the earliest point.

EDGE CASE 7: Block type is null or undefined
Scenario: Malformed block object with no type field.
Expected behavior: Block is silently skipped. No error in console.
Implementation: _renderBlock() has a default case that returns empty string.
Type check: if (!block || !block.type) return ''.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON (must be built first):
- NONE. This is a foundation feature. Other features depend on it.

ENABLES (can be built after this):
- Feature #037 (Streaming Text): Streams blocks that this renders
- Feature #058-#067 (All content block types): Each is a block type
- Feature #083-#090 (Visualization blocks): Charts, graphs, sparklines
- Feature #091-#095 (Interactive input blocks): Sliders, toggles
- Feature #096-#100 (Artifact blocks): Reading lists, BibTeX, slides

ENHANCES (improves but does not require):
- Feature #075 (Response Reveal): Adds entrance animation to block rendering
- Feature #045 (Collapsible Responses): Adds collapse logic to long messages

CONFLICTS WITH:
- Feature #038 (Markdown Rendering): Both parse markdown in prose.
  Resolution: #038 IS the markdown parser used BY #002. They are the same
  system. #038's implementation lives inside block-renderer.js as the
  _parseMarkdown() method. Listed as "overlapping" not "conflicting."

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES:
- No external libraries. Pure vanilla JS.
- CSS: athena-blocks.css (new file, no external CSS framework)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ BlockRenderer.render() accepts JSON blocks array and returns HTML string
□ All 58 block types have rendering functions (even if some are simple)
□ Prose blocks render markdown: bold, italic, inline code, lists, links
□ Paper names in [brackets] become clickable links to graph nodes
□ Stat cards render with large number, label, and detail text
□ Tables render with headers, rows, and sortable columns
□ Takeaway boxes render with gold background and label
□ Warning blocks render with red background and "!" label
□ Expandable sections toggle content on click
□ Unknown block types are silently skipped (no error)
□ Malformed blocks (missing fields) render gracefully (fallback text)

VISUAL:
□ All blocks match their ASCII specifications exactly
□ No emojis anywhere in rendered blocks (checkmarks are SVG, not emoji)
□ Block entrance animation: opacity + translateY, 300ms stagger

QUALITY:
□ No console errors when rendering any valid block type
□ No XSS possible through block content (escapeHtml tested)
□ Works with 0 blocks, 1 block, and 50+ blocks

ACCESSIBILITY:
□ Paper links have aria-labels with paper title
□ Expandable sections have aria-expanded attribute
□ Tables have proper role and scope attributes
□ Tab navigation works through all interactive elements

INTEGRATION:
□ Does not break existing chat functionality
□ Rendered blocks are compatible with streaming (blocks arrive one at a time)

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round — agent confirms ZERO remaining issues
□ BROWSER TEST: Feature verified in Chrome via browsermcp
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Implementation matches this spec document exactly

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: block-renderer.js (all rendering functions), markdown-parser.js,
   athena-blocks.css, athena-engine.js (block insertion)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Render a prose block with [ResNet] and verify it becomes a clickable link
   b. Render a stat_card and verify the number, label, and detail all display
   c. Render a table with 5 rows, click a column header, verify rows reorder
   d. Render a takeaway block and verify gold background (#FEF9C3)
   e. Render a warning block and verify red background (#FEF2F2)
   f. Render an expandable section, click it, verify content toggles
   g. Send blocks with <script>alert(1)</script> in text, verify no XSS
   h. Send a block with type "nonexistent", verify no error
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. linkifyPapers creates broken HTML (unclosed tags) when paper name
      contains special characters
   b. Markdown parser fails on edge cases (nested bold/italic, empty strings)
   c. Table sort breaks when cells contain numbers AND strings
   d. Memory leak from event listeners added to each block but never removed
5. VERIFY no regression in: #037 (Streaming), #001 (Context-Aware)
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why none
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

[Empty — feature not yet implemented]

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STATUS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #003: QUICK ACTION BUTTONS
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Proactive AI
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 6
OVERLAPS WITH:   #042 (Suggested Follow-Ups)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Quick Action Buttons are contextual suggestion chips that appear after every
Athena response, offering the user 2-4 relevant next actions based on what was
just discussed AND what the user has not yet explored. Unlike static menu items,
these buttons change with every response because they are computed from the
intersection of the response content, the graph context, and the user's
exploration history.

This feature exists because the biggest friction in chat interfaces is the
blank input box. The user has just read a detailed analysis and now faces
"what do I ask next?" Quick action buttons eliminate this friction by offering
intelligent suggestions. The user can click instead of type. In testing of
ChatGPT and Claude, suggested follow-ups increase engagement by 40-60% because
users discover capabilities they would not have thought to ask about.

The user experiences these as a row of bordered, monospace-styled pill buttons
below each AI response. Each button has a short label (3-8 words) that when
clicked, sends that text as a new message. The buttons are contextual: after
a response about authors, they might suggest "Show blind spots" or "Compare
He and Ren." After a response about contradictions, they might suggest "Which
side is winning?" or "Show the evidence." They never repeat suggestions the
user has already clicked or topics already explored.

What makes this different from ChatGPT's suggested replies: ChatGPT generates
suggestions via the LLM (slow, costs tokens, sometimes irrelevant). Athena
computes suggestions DETERMINISTICALLY from graph data and context (instant,
free, always relevant). The backend classifies the response topic, checks
what the user has explored, and selects from a curated suggestion pool.
The LLM is NOT called to generate these buttons.

This connects to Feature #001 (Context-Aware) which provides the exploration
history, Feature #042 (Suggested Follow-Ups) which is the same UI element
but triggered differently, and Feature #009 (Proactive Suggestions) which
uses similar logic but appears proactively without a response.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
Automatic. Buttons appear at the end of every AI response. No user action
needed to make them appear. They are part of the response rendering pipeline.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. Athena completes a response about the key authors in the lineage. The
   response mentions Kaiming He, Shaoqing Ren, and structural impact. The
   last block in the response is a takeaway about author concentration.

2. Within 100ms of the response completing, the backend suggestion engine
   analyzes: (a) the response topic (authors/structural analysis), (b) the
   graph context (13 contradictions exist, blind spots exist, trust is 19%),
   (c) the user's exploration history (they have NOT asked about contradictions,
   blind spots, or trust yet).

3. The engine selects 3-4 suggestions from the curated pool:
   "Show blind spots" (unexplored topic, high value)
   "Compare He and Ren" (deepens current topic)
   "What should I read?" (practical action)
   "How reliable is this?" (unexplored, relevant to the trust score)

4. The buttons render as a row of bordered pills below the response with
   a 200ms fade-in animation. Each pill has monospace font, 1px border,
   6px 14px padding, and a subtle hover effect (background shifts to #F3F4F6).

5. The user clicks "Show blind spots." The button text is sent as a new
   message to Athena. The button row fades out (150ms). A new response
   streams in about blind spots. New buttons appear after that response.

6. If the user types their own message instead of clicking a button, the
   buttons from the previous response fade out when the new message is sent.

AFTER COMPLETION:
The buttons are gone (faded out). A new response is visible with its own
set of buttons. The exploration history has been updated to include the
topic the user just explored.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena/suggestion-engine.js: Deterministic suggestion computation.
  Takes response topic, graph context, and exploration history. Returns 2-4
  suggestion strings. No LLM call.
- static/js/athena/athena-engine.js: Appends suggestion buttons after each
  response completes. Handles button click events.
- static/css/athena-blocks.css: Suggestion pill styling.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
The SuggestionEngine is a pure function: suggestActions(responseTopic,
graphContext, explorationHistory) -> string[]. It uses a curated pool of
~100 suggestion templates organized by topic. Each template has a relevance
condition (e.g., "Show blind spots" only appears if blindSpotCount > 0).

When a response completes, athena-engine.js calls SuggestionEngine with
the response's classification (from the pipeline), the static context
(from Feature #001), and the session's exploration set. The engine returns
2-4 strings. These are rendered as a suggested_actions block.

Button click handler: sends the button text as a new user message via
the same send() pipeline. The button row is removed from the DOM.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No backend component. Suggestion computation is entirely client-side.
The suggestion pool is a static JavaScript object, not fetched from an API.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
const SuggestionEngine = {
    POOL: {
        authors: [
            { text: "Why does {topAuthor} dominate?", condition: (ctx) => ctx.topAuthors?.length > 0 },
            { text: "Compare {author1} and {author2}", condition: (ctx) => ctx.topAuthors?.length >= 2 },
            { text: "Show author constellation", condition: () => true },
        ],
        structural: [
            { text: "Show blind spots", condition: (ctx) => ctx.blindSpotCount > 0 },
            { text: "What are the fragility risks?", condition: (ctx) => ctx.bottlenecks?.length > 0 },
        ],
        temporal: [
            { text: "When was the peak activity?", condition: () => true },
            { text: "Show the timeline", condition: () => true },
        ],
        practical: [
            { text: "What should I read first?", condition: () => true },
            { text: "Find my position", condition: (ctx) => !ctx.userPositionEstablished },
            { text: "How reliable is this analysis?", condition: (ctx) => ctx.trustScore < 50 },
        ],
    },

    suggest(responseTopic, ctx, explored) {
        const candidates = [];
        // Add topic-deepening suggestions
        const topicPool = this.POOL[responseTopic] || [];
        candidates.push(...topicPool.filter(s => s.condition(ctx)));
        // Add unexplored topic suggestions
        for (const [topic, suggestions] of Object.entries(this.POOL)) {
            if (!explored.has(topic)) {
                candidates.push(...suggestions.filter(s => s.condition(ctx)));
            }
        }
        // Deduplicate and limit to 4
        const unique = [...new Map(candidates.map(c => [c.text, c])).values()];
        return unique.slice(0, 4).map(c => this._fillTemplate(c.text, ctx));
    },
};
```

API CONTRACTS:
No API endpoints. Entirely client-side computation.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Suggestions Visible (after response completes)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
|  AI response content above...                                |
|                                                              |
|  TAKEAWAY: 3 authors control 67% of structural impact.      |
|                                                              |
|  +------------------+ +----------------+ +----------------+ |
|  | Show blind spots | | Compare He and | | What should I  | |
|  |                  | | Ren            | | read first?    | |
|  +------------------+ +----------------+ +----------------+ |
|                                                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```
Pills: 1px #D1D5DB border, 6px border-radius, padding 6px 14px,
font: 12px JetBrains Mono, color #374151, background transparent.
Hover: background #F3F4F6, border #9CA3AF.

STATE: Suggestion Clicked (button being sent)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
|  AI response content above...                                |
|                                                              |
|  TAKEAWAY: 3 authors control 67% of structural impact.      |
|                                                              |
|  (buttons faded out, new user message appearing below)       |
|                                                              |
|  You: Show blind spots                                       |
|                                                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

STATE: No Suggestions (edge case, nothing relevant)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
|  AI response content above...                                |
|                                                              |
|  (no suggestion row rendered, clean ending)                  |
|                                                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

TRANSITIONS:
Response complete -> Buttons appear: fade-in, opacity 0 to 1, 200ms, ease-out
Button clicked -> Buttons disappear: fade-out, opacity 1 to 0, 150ms, ease-in
User types own message -> Buttons disappear: same fade-out as click

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Response streaming completes
    |
athena-engine.js detects stream-complete event
    |
SuggestionEngine.suggest(responseTopic, staticContext, explorationHistory)
    |--- [suggestion-engine.js]
    |--- Filters curated pool by conditions + exploration history
    |--- Fills templates with graph data (author names, numbers)
    |--- Returns 2-4 suggestion strings
    |
athena-engine.js renders suggested_actions block
    |--- Creates pill buttons with click handlers
    |--- Appends to the response message container
    |
User clicks a suggestion
    |
Click handler sends button text as new user message
    |--- Uses same send() pipeline as typed messages
    |--- Exploration history updated with the new topic
    |
Old suggestion buttons removed from DOM

ERROR PATH:
SuggestionEngine returns empty array (no relevant suggestions)
    |--- No buttons rendered (clean ending, no error shown)
    |--- This can happen if user has explored all major topics

Suggestion template contains a variable that cannot be filled
    |--- _fillTemplate() replaces unfilled {variables} with ""
    |--- The button still renders but with slightly odd phrasing
    |--- Rare edge case, mitigated by default fallback text in each template

DATA STORED:
Exploration history stored in session memory (JavaScript Set object).
Also written to chat_analytics table for tracking which topics users explore.
Not persisted across page reloads (rebuilds from conversation history).

DATA CACHED:
Suggestion pool is a static JavaScript object. No caching needed.
Exploration history is rebuilt from conversation messages on page load.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: No

Quick Action Buttons are computed deterministically from graph data and
exploration history. No LLM call is made. This is intentional: suggestions
must appear INSTANTLY (within 100ms of response completing). An LLM call
would add 1-3 seconds of latency, making the buttons feel sluggish.

The response topic used for suggestion selection comes from the pipeline's
classification stage (Feature #001), which has already completed by the
time the response finishes streaming.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Trigger          | Element              | Action                                         |
|------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| Click            | Suggestion pill      | Sends button text as new message, removes pills |
| Hover            | Suggestion pill      | Background shifts to #F3F4F6, border darkens    |
| Keyboard Tab     | Between pills        | Focus moves left to right through pills         |
| Keyboard Enter   | On focused pill      | Same as click (sends text, removes pills)       |
| Keyboard Escape  | When pills focused   | Returns focus to input textarea                 |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Pills appear: opacity 0 to 1, 200ms, ease-out. All pills appear simultaneously.
- Pills disappear (click): opacity 1 to 0, 150ms, ease-in. Clicked pill scales
  to 0.95 briefly (50ms) before fade-out (press feedback).
- Hover: background-color transition 100ms.

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE (Athena uses no audio)

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Suggestion pills have role="button" and are keyboard focusable (tabindex="0")
- Each pill has aria-label matching its text
- The suggestion row has role="group" and aria-label="Suggested questions"
- Focus order: after the last block in the response, Tab reaches the first pill
- Screen reader announces "Suggested questions: Show blind spots, Compare He
  and Ren, What should I read first?"

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

EDGE CASE 1: User has explored all major topics
Scenario: User has asked about authors, blind spots, contradictions, trust,
temporal patterns, and used the Pathfinder. All topics are in exploration history.
Expected behavior: No suggestions appear. The response ends cleanly without buttons.
Implementation: SuggestionEngine.suggest() returns empty array. No buttons rendered.

EDGE CASE 2: Response topic doesn't match any pool category
Scenario: User asked a meta question ("what can you do?") which has no topic pool.
Expected behavior: Generic suggestions appear: "Tell me about this lineage",
"Who are the key authors?", "Find my position."
Implementation: POOL has a 'default' category with generic always-valid suggestions.

EDGE CASE 3: Graph has no contradictions, no blind spots, no bottlenecks
Scenario: A very simple lineage with only adoption edges and full coverage.
Expected behavior: Suggestions that require contradictions/blind spots/bottlenecks
are filtered out by their condition functions. Only generic and practical
suggestions appear.
Implementation: Each suggestion has a condition function checked against context.

EDGE CASE 4: User clicks suggestion while previous response is still streaming
Scenario: Streaming is active. Old suggestion buttons from the PREVIOUS response
are still visible. User clicks one.
Expected behavior: The click is queued (same as typing during streaming, Feature
#044). The current stream continues. The suggestion is processed after streaming.
Implementation: Button click handler checks isStreaming flag. If true, queues
the suggestion text in the message queue.

EDGE CASE 5: Template variable references a paper that doesn't exist
Scenario: Template "Compare {author1} and {author2}" but topAuthors array
has only 1 author.
Expected behavior: The suggestion is filtered out because its condition function
checks ctx.topAuthors?.length >= 2.
Implementation: Every template with variables has a matching condition that
verifies the required data exists before the template is used.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON (must be built first):
- Feature #001 (Context-Aware Responses): Provides static context (graph data)
  and dynamic context (exploration history) needed for suggestion computation.
- Feature #002 (Rich Response Formatting): Suggestion pills are rendered as
  a suggested_actions block type by the block renderer.

ENABLES (can be built after this):
- Feature #042 (Suggested Follow-Ups): Same UI element, overlapping functionality.
  #003 provides the rendering infrastructure that #042 enhances with LLM-generated
  suggestions.

ENHANCES (improves but does not require):
- Feature #037 (Streaming Text): Suggestions appear after streaming completes,
  giving the user immediate next steps.
- Feature #006 (Conversation Memory): Memory informs which topics to suggest
  (or avoid suggesting).

CONFLICTS WITH:
- Feature #042 (Suggested Follow-Ups): Both produce suggestion pills. Resolution:
  #003 generates suggestions deterministically (instant). #042 generates via LLM
  (delayed, higher quality). When both are active, #003 shows first (instant),
  then #042 replaces them when its LLM call completes (upgrade pattern).

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES:
- No external libraries or APIs. Pure client-side computation.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ SuggestionEngine.suggest() returns 2-4 strings for typical responses
□ Suggestions change based on response topic (different after author vs dispute analysis)
□ Explored topics are excluded from suggestions (no repeats)
□ Clicking a suggestion sends it as a new message
□ Suggestion pills disappear after click or new user message
□ Empty suggestion array results in no buttons (clean ending)
□ Template variables are filled with real graph data (author names, numbers)
□ Condition functions correctly filter unavailable suggestions

VISUAL:
□ Pills match the ASCII spec: monospace, bordered, correct padding
□ Hover effect: background #F3F4F6, border darkens
□ Fade-in animation: 200ms, ease-out on appearance

QUALITY:
□ Suggestions appear within 100ms of response completing
□ No console errors in suggestion computation
□ Works with minimal graphs (0 contradictions, 0 blind spots)

ACCESSIBILITY:
□ Pills have role="button" and tabindex="0"
□ Keyboard Tab navigates between pills, Enter activates
□ Screen reader announces suggestion group

INTEGRATION:
□ Does not interfere with response streaming
□ Coexists with Feature #042 (upgrade pattern: instant then LLM)

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round — agent confirms ZERO remaining issues
□ BROWSER TEST: Feature verified in Chrome via browsermcp
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Implementation matches this spec document exactly

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: suggestion-engine.js (POOL object, suggest function, condition
   functions), athena-engine.js (button rendering and click handling),
   athena-blocks.css (pill styling)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Send "who are the key authors?" and verify suggestions include
      topic-deepening AND unexplored-topic suggestions
   b. Click "Show blind spots" suggestion, verify it sends as message
   c. Ask about authors, then blind spots, then contradictions. Verify
      suggestions stop repeating explored topics
   d. Load a graph with 0 contradictions. Verify "Show contradictions"
      does NOT appear as a suggestion
   e. Tab through suggestion pills with keyboard, press Enter on one
   f. Click a suggestion during streaming. Verify it queues properly
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Template {variable} not replaced (shows literal {author1})
   b. Suggestion pool empty for a rare response topic (no buttons crash)
   c. Click handler fires twice on rapid double-click
   d. Exploration history grows unbounded (memory leak after 100+ messages)
5. VERIFY no regression in: #001 (Context), #002 (Rich Formatting), #037 (Streaming)
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why none
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STATUS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created

Last Updated:     2026-03-24
