════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #004: DISCUSS INTEGRATION
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           C
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #016 (Paper Deep Dive), #026 (Paper Relationship Explainer)
BLOCKS:          #012 (AI Annotations on Graph), #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation)
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Discuss Integration is the bridge between Arivu's visual graph exploration and
Athena's conversational intelligence. When a user right-clicks any node or edge
in the ancestry graph, a context menu includes "Discuss with Athena" as a primary
action. Selecting it opens the Athena chat panel (if not already open) and
automatically seeds a conversation about that specific paper or relationship,
pre-populated with computed graph data about the selected element.

This feature solves the fundamental disconnect between visual and textual analysis.
Without it, a user sees a node glowing red in the graph (high structural impact)
but has to manually type "tell me about paper X" in the chat. With Discuss
Integration, the transition is seamless: right-click the red node, select Discuss,
and Athena immediately responds with a data-rich analysis of WHY that node is
structurally critical, which papers depend on it, and what would collapse if it
were removed.

For edge discussions, the seeded conversation includes the mutation type, confidence
tier, citation intent, the exact citing and cited sentences, and a contextual
explanation. The user sees something like "This edge represents a methodological
adoption where Paper A (2019) generalized the batch normalization technique from
Paper B (2015). Confidence: HIGH (0.87). The citing sentence is: 'Following the
normalization approach of [B], we extend...'"

What makes this unique to Athena versus generic discuss buttons: the seeded context
includes COMPUTED graph metrics. The initial message is not "Tell me about ResNet"
but includes PageRank position (0.043, 3rd highest), pruning impact (removing it
collapses 31% of the graph), cluster membership (belongs to the "Deep Residual
Learning" DNA strand), and 4 key relationships. This is information no generic
chatbot can provide because it requires the live graph state.

The feature integrates with the existing right-click context menu system in
graph.js. The context menu already has entries for "Show details," "Prune from
here," and "Highlight path." Discuss Integration adds a new entry that bridges
to Athena. It also works with multi-select: if 2-3 nodes are selected, the
discuss action triggers a comparison discussion rather than a single-paper deep
dive, seamlessly connecting to Feature #014 (Comparison Mode).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User right-clicks any node or edge in the ancestry graph. A context menu appears
with "Discuss with Athena" as the second entry (after "Show Details"). Alternatively,
user can double-click a node while holding Shift key. A third trigger: the user
can type "/discuss [paper title fragment]" in the chat input.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User right-clicks the "ResNet" node in the graph. Context menu appears within
   100ms. The menu has a dot matrix bullet (●) next to "Discuss with Athena."

2. User clicks "Discuss with Athena." If the chat panel is collapsed, it slides
   open over 300ms with a spring animation. The chat input area shows a ghost
   message: "Preparing context for ResNet (He et al., 2016)..."

3. Within 500ms, a system message appears in the chat: "Starting discussion about
   Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition (He et al., 2016). I have access
   to this paper's full graph context including 23 descendants, 8 ancestors, and
   its structural role as the 3rd most impactful node in this lineage."

4. Immediately after the system message, Athena begins streaming a structured
   response. The response includes: a Paper Citation Card block (#058) with full
   metadata, a Stat Dashboard block (#063) showing PageRank, pruning impact,
   cluster membership, and descendant count, then 2-3 paragraphs of prose analysis
   explaining the paper's structural role in the lineage.

5. After the initial analysis streams complete (3-6 seconds), a set of Quick
   Action Buttons (#003) appears specific to this paper: "Show what depends on
   this," "Compare with VGGNet," "What would collapse without this?", "Show its
   DNA strand."

6. The graph simultaneously highlights the discussed node with a pulsing gold
   ring animation (2s period, infinite loop) so the user can see which node is
   being discussed even if they scroll.

AFTER COMPLETION:
The chat is now in a "paper-focused" conversation state. Subsequent messages from
the user are interpreted in the context of this specific paper. The highlighted
node continues pulsing until the user starts a new topic or clicks elsewhere.
The conversation thread is tagged with the paper_id for session history.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/graph.js: Adds "Discuss with Athena" to the existing right-click
  context menu. Dispatches a custom event 'athena:discuss' with node/edge data.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Listens for 'athena:discuss' events. Builds the
  context payload. Sends to backend. Manages the chat panel open/focus.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Renders the Paper Citation Card and Stat Dashboard
  blocks that appear in the seeded response.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Styles for the discuss-initiated messages, including
  the system message bar and the pulsing node highlight ring.
- app.py: /api/athena/discuss endpoint that accepts a paper_id or edge_id and
  returns a pre-structured context payload with graph metrics.
- backend/graph_engine.py: get_node_context() method that computes PageRank
  position, pruning impact, cluster membership, and key relationships for a
  single node.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
The context menu addition hooks into graph.js's existing contextMenuHandler. When
"Discuss with Athena" is clicked, it dispatches:

  document.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('athena:discuss', {
    detail: { type: 'node', paperId: node.paper_id, graphId: currentGraphId }
  }));

The athena-engine.js listener catches this, checks if the chat panel is open (opens
it if not), and constructs a discuss request:

  async function handleDiscuss(event) {
    const { type, paperId, edgeId, graphId } = event.detail;
    if (!chatPanelOpen) openChatPanel();

    // Show preparing state
    appendSystemMessage(`Preparing context for ${getPaperTitle(paperId)}...`);

    // Fetch rich context from backend
    const context = await fetch(`/api/athena/discuss`, {
      method: 'POST',
      body: JSON.stringify({ type, paper_id: paperId, edge_id: edgeId, graph_id: graphId })
    }).then(r => r.json());

    // Send as a structured prompt to the LLM pipeline
    sendToAthena({
      type: 'discuss',
      context: context,
      userVisible: false  // The initial discuss is system-initiated
    });
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
The /api/athena/discuss endpoint computes node context without an LLM call. It
returns pre-computed metrics from the cached graph:

  @app.route('/api/athena/discuss', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_discuss():
      data = request.json
      graph_data = load_graph(data['graph_id'])

      if data['type'] == 'node':
          context = compute_node_context(graph_data, data['paper_id'])
      elif data['type'] == 'edge':
          context = compute_edge_context(graph_data, data['edge_id'])

      return jsonify(context)

compute_node_context returns: paper metadata, pagerank_position, pagerank_score,
pruning_impact_percentage, cluster_name, ancestor_count, descendant_count,
top_3_relationships (with mutation types), and text_tier.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
// Context menu addition in graph.js
function buildContextMenu(target, type) {
  const menu = [
    { label: 'Show Details', icon: '●', action: () => showDetails(target) },
    { label: 'Discuss with Athena', icon: '◐', action: () => {
      document.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('athena:discuss', {
        detail: { type, paperId: target.paper_id, graphId: graphState.graphId }
      }));
    }},
    { divider: true },
    { label: 'Prune from here', icon: '○', action: () => startPrune(target) },
  ];
  if (selectedNodes.length > 1) {
    menu[1].label = `Compare ${selectedNodes.length} papers with Athena`;
    menu[1].action = () => {
      document.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('athena:discuss', {
        detail: { type: 'compare', paperIds: selectedNodes.map(n => n.paper_id) }
      }));
    };
  }
  return menu;
}
```

API CONTRACTS:

POST /api/athena/discuss
Request:
{
  "type": "node" | "edge" | "compare",
  "paper_id": "2c03df8b48bf3fa39054345bafabfeff15bfd11d",  // for node
  "edge_id": "abc123:def456",  // for edge
  "paper_ids": ["abc...", "def..."],  // for compare (2-3 max)
  "graph_id": "a1b2c3d4..."
}

Response (node):
{
  "paper": { "title": "...", "authors": [...], "year": 2016, ... },
  "metrics": {
    "pagerank_position": 3,
    "pagerank_score": 0.043,
    "pruning_impact": 31.2,
    "cluster_name": "Deep Residual Learning",
    "ancestor_count": 8,
    "descendant_count": 23,
    "text_tier": 2
  },
  "top_relationships": [
    { "paper_id": "...", "title": "VGGNet", "mutation_type": "generalization", "direction": "ancestor" },
    ...
  ],
  "suggested_prompts": [
    "What makes this paper structurally critical?",
    "What would collapse if this paper never existed?",
    "Compare this with its closest ancestor"
  ]
}

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Context Menu Open (Node)

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              [Ancestry Graph Area]                    │
  │                                                      │
  │         ○──────○          ┌─────────────────────┐    │
  │        /        \         │ ● Show Details       │    │
  │  ○────●[ResNet]──○        │ ◐ Discuss with Athena│    │
  │        \   ↑     /        │ ─────────────────── │    │
  │         ○──┘────○         │ ○ Prune from here    │    │
  │                           │ ○ Highlight path     │    │
  │                           └─────────────────────┘    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Chat Panel Opening with Discuss Context

  ┌──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┐
  │  [Graph Area]        │  ATHENA                       │
  │                      │                               │
  │    ●[ResNet]         │  ┌─ SYSTEM ──────────────────┐│
  │    (pulsing gold)    │  │ Starting discussion about  ││
  │                      │  │ Deep Residual Learning for ││
  │                      │  │ Image Recognition (2016)   ││
  │                      │  └───────────────────────────┘│
  │                      │                               │
  │                      │  ┌─ PAPER CARD ──────────────┐│
  │                      │  │ Deep Residual Learning...  ││
  │                      │  │ He, Zhang, Ren, Sun (2016) ││
  │                      │  │ Citations: 182,431         ││
  │                      │  └───────────────────────────┘│
  │                      │                               │
  │                      │  ┌─ STATS ──────────────────┐ │
  │                      │  │ PageRank: #3 (0.043)     │ │
  │                      │  │ Impact: 31.2% collapses  │ │
  │                      │  │ Cluster: Deep Residual    │ │
  │                      │  │ Descendants: 23 papers    │ │
  │                      │  └──────────────────────────┘ │
  │                      │                               │
  │                      │  This paper occupies a        │
  │                      │  critical structural position █│
  └──────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Multi-Select Compare Trigger

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ●[ResNet] ●[VGGNet]    ┌──────────────────────────┐ │
  │  (both selected, blue)  │ ◐ Compare 2 papers with  │ │
  │                         │   Athena                  │ │
  │                         │ ○ Show common ancestors   │ │
  │                         └──────────────────────────┘ │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
Context Menu → Chat Opening: Menu fades out (150ms), chat panel slides in from
  right (300ms, ease-out-back spring). Node begins pulsing gold ring.
System Message → Streaming Response: System message bar appears with slide-down
  (200ms). Paper card renders instantly. Stats dashboard renders instantly. Prose
  begins streaming after cards.
Discuss → Normal Chat: When user sends a new unrelated message, the pulsing gold
  ring fades out (500ms) and conversation continues in general context mode.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User right-clicks node
  → graph.js captures click target + coordinates
  → Context menu renders at click position
  → User selects "Discuss with Athena"
  → graph.js dispatches CustomEvent('athena:discuss')
  → athena-engine.js catches event
  → Opens chat panel if needed
  → Shows "Preparing context..." system message
  → POST /api/athena/discuss { type: 'node', paper_id, graph_id }
  → Backend loads cached graph JSON from DB/R2
  → Computes node_context: pagerank, pruning impact, cluster, relationships
  → Returns context JSON (no LLM call yet)
  → Frontend receives context
  → Constructs structured prompt with context injected
  → Sends to /api/athena/stream (SSE) with discuss context
  → LLM generates prose analysis using injected data
  → Response streams to chat with Paper Card + Stats blocks

ERROR PATH:
  → Graph not loaded yet → Show toast "Load a graph first to discuss papers"
  → Node not in current graph → Show error "This paper is not in the current lineage"
  → Backend timeout → Show retry button in chat "Could not load context. Retry?"
  → LLM stream fails mid-response → Partial response preserved + error message appended

DATA STORED:
  → chat_history table: discussion context stored as system message with paper_id tag
  → action_log table: { action: 'discuss', paper_id, source: 'context_menu' }

DATA CACHED:
  → Node context response cached in sessionStorage keyed by paper_id + graph_id
  → TTL: duration of browser session (cleared on page reload)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant) for initial classification,
       config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) for prose generation

STAGE: 3-stage pipeline from Pathfinder system
  Stage 1 (Groq fast): Classify discuss type (single-paper / edge / compare)
  Stage 2 (Groq fast): Select response blocks (Paper Card, Stats, Prose)
  Stage 3 (Groq 70b): Generate prose analysis using injected context

PROMPT (Stage 3 — prose generation for single paper discuss):
```
You are Athena, a research intelligence assistant analyzing a citation graph.
The user wants to discuss this paper:

Title: {paper.title}
Authors: {paper.authors}
Year: {paper.year}
PageRank Position: #{metrics.pagerank_position} out of {total_nodes} nodes
PageRank Score: {metrics.pagerank_score}
Pruning Impact: Removing this paper collapses {metrics.pruning_impact}% of the lineage
Cluster: {metrics.cluster_name}
Ancestors: {metrics.ancestor_count} | Descendants: {metrics.descendant_count}

Key relationships:
{for rel in top_relationships}
- {rel.direction}: {rel.title} ({rel.mutation_type})
{endfor}

Write 2-3 paragraphs analyzing this paper's structural role in the lineage.
Focus on COMPUTED FACTS from the data above, not general knowledge.
Use specific numbers. Explain what the PageRank and pruning impact mean
for this paper's importance. Do not use em dashes. Do not use emojis.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Plain text prose (2-3 paragraphs). The structured blocks (Paper Card, Stats)
are rendered by the frontend from the context JSON, NOT from LLM output.
The LLM only generates the prose analysis paragraphs.

FALLBACK:
If LLM is unavailable (no GROQ_API_KEY or rate limited), display only the
structured blocks (Paper Card + Stats Dashboard) without prose. Add a note:
"Detailed analysis unavailable. Showing computed metrics only."

TOKEN BUDGET:
- Context injection: ~300 tokens (paper metadata + metrics)
- System prompt: ~150 tokens
- Generated output: ~400-600 tokens (2-3 paragraphs)
- Total per discuss: ~900-1050 tokens

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Context menu item    | Click       | Dispatches athena:discuss event              |
| Context menu item    | Hover       | Gold highlight, cursor pointer               |
| Node (after discuss) | Visual      | Pulsing gold ring, 2s period, infinite       |
| Paper Card block     | Click title | Opens paper URL in new tab                   |
| Stats dashboard      | Hover stat  | Tooltip with explanation (e.g., "PageRank    |
|                      |             | measures structural importance in the graph") |
| Quick Action button  | Click       | Sends as new message in discuss context      |
| System message bar   | -           | Non-interactive, informational only          |
| Chat panel           | Drag edge   | Panel resizes (existing behavior)            |
| Pulsing ring         | Click away  | Ring fades out after 500ms                   |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Context menu: appears instantly at click position, no animation
- Chat panel open: translateX(100%) to translateX(0), 300ms, ease-out-back
- System message: slideDown 200ms, background: var(--bg-surface) with left gold border
- Paper Card: opacity 0 to 1, 200ms ease-in, no translate
- Stats Dashboard: opacity 0 to 1, 200ms ease-in, 100ms delay after Paper Card
- Prose streaming: standard token-by-token streaming animation
- Pulsing gold ring: SVG circle, stroke: var(--accent-gold), stroke-opacity
  oscillates 0.3 to 1.0, 2s period, ease-in-out
- Ring fadeout: opacity 1 to 0, 500ms ease-out

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE: Arivu does not use sound effects. All feedback is visual.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Context menu items have role="menuitem" and aria-label descriptions
- "Discuss with Athena" context menu item is keyboard accessible (Tab + Enter)
- Screen reader announces "Opening discussion about [paper title] with Athena"
- The pulsing gold ring has aria-hidden="true" (decorative)
- Stats dashboard values are exposed as aria-label on each stat element
- All structured blocks have proper heading hierarchy (h3 for card title, etc.)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User right-clicks a node but the graph data has not finished loading
   Expected: Context menu shows "Discuss with Athena" grayed out with tooltip
   "Graph still loading. Please wait."
   Implementation: Check graphState.isLoaded before enabling the menu item.

2. Scenario: User rapidly right-clicks 5 different nodes within 2 seconds
   Expected: Only the most recent discuss request is processed. Previous
   pending requests are cancelled via AbortController.
   Implementation: Store active AbortController, abort on new discuss trigger.

3. Scenario: User discusses a node, then prunes it while the response is streaming
   Expected: Streaming continues but a warning toast appears: "Note: This paper
   was just pruned from the graph. Analysis reflects its pre-prune state."
   Implementation: Listen for prune events during active discuss, append warning.

4. Scenario: User discusses a paper with text_tier=4 (title only, no abstract)
   Expected: Stats dashboard includes a "Limited Data" indicator. Prose analysis
   acknowledges: "Analysis is based on structural position only, as no abstract
   or full text is available for this paper."
   Implementation: Check text_tier in context, inject data-quality caveat.

5. Scenario: User multi-selects 4+ nodes and tries "Compare with Athena"
   Expected: Toast message "Comparison supports up to 3 papers. Please select
   fewer papers." The context menu item is disabled.
   Implementation: Check selectedNodes.length, disable menu item if > 3.

6. Scenario: Edge discuss where mutation_confidence is SPECULATIVE (< 0.35)
   Expected: The edge analysis includes a prominent warning: "This relationship
   classification has low confidence. The identified mutation type may not be
   accurate." Warning rendered as a Warning/Alert block (#066).
   Implementation: Check confidence_tier in edge context, prepend warning block.

7. Scenario: User opens discuss on a node that was already discussed earlier
   Expected: New discuss starts fresh but the conversation history sidebar (#048)
   shows the previous discussion is still accessible. No duplicate system messages.
   Implementation: Check chat_history for existing discuss on same paper_id,
   start new thread but preserve old one.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON:
  #001 — Context-Aware Responses (provides the context injection system)
  #002 — Rich Response Formatting (renders Paper Card, Stats blocks)
  #037 — Streaming Text (streams the prose analysis)
  #058 — Paper Citation Card Block (renders paper metadata card)
  #063 — Stat Dashboard Block (renders metrics dashboard)

ENABLES:
  #012 — AI Annotations on Graph (uses discuss infrastructure to annotate nodes)
  #034 — Chat-Driven Graph Navigation (uses discuss context for navigation commands)
  #105 — Click-Aware Chat (extends discuss to single-click awareness)

ENHANCES:
  #003 — Quick Action Buttons (generates paper-specific suggestions after discuss)
  #014 — Comparison Mode (multi-select discuss triggers comparison)
  #016 — Paper Deep Dive (discuss is the entry point for deep dives)

CONFLICTS:
  NONE — this feature adds new behavior without modifying existing features.

EXTERNAL:
  Depends on cached graph data being available (graph must have been built).
  Uses existing right-click context menu system in graph.js.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ Right-click on any node shows "Discuss with Athena" in context menu
□ Right-click on any edge shows "Discuss with Athena" in context menu
□ Clicking "Discuss" opens chat panel if collapsed
□ System message appears with paper title and context summary
□ Paper Citation Card block renders with correct metadata
□ Stat Dashboard block renders with PageRank, pruning impact, cluster, descendant count
□ LLM generates prose analysis using injected graph data (not generic knowledge)
□ Quick Action Buttons appear after discuss response completes
□ Multi-select (2-3 nodes) triggers comparison discuss

VISUAL:
□ Context menu renders at click position with dot matrix bullet (●/◐)
□ Discussed node shows pulsing gold ring animation
□ Chat panel slides in with spring animation if previously collapsed
□ System message has gold left border and surface background
□ Paper Card and Stats blocks render before prose begins streaming

PERFORMANCE:
□ Context menu appears within 100ms of right-click
□ /api/athena/discuss responds within 500ms (no LLM, only graph computation)
□ Full discuss response (including LLM prose) completes within 8 seconds
□ Node context cached in sessionStorage (no re-fetch for same node)

ACCESSIBILITY:
□ Context menu items are keyboard navigable (Tab + Enter)
□ Screen reader announces paper title on discuss activation
□ All stat values have aria-labels
□ Pulsing ring is aria-hidden (decorative)

EDGE CASES:
□ Graceful handling when graph is not yet loaded
□ AbortController cancels previous request on rapid re-trigger
□ text_tier=4 papers show "Limited Data" caveat
□ SPECULATIVE confidence edges show warning block
□ 4+ multi-select shows "max 3" toast and disables menu item

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: graph.js (context menu handler, CustomEvent dispatch), athena-engine.js
   (discuss event listener, context fetch, panel open logic), app.py
   (/api/athena/discuss endpoint, compute_node_context, compute_edge_context)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes above
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Right-click a high-impact node (ResNet). Verify Paper Card shows correct
      PageRank position and pruning impact percentage
   b. Right-click an edge with SPECULATIVE confidence. Verify warning block appears
   c. Multi-select 2 nodes, right-click. Verify "Compare 2 papers with Athena" text
   d. Multi-select 4 nodes, right-click. Verify "Discuss" is disabled with toast
   e. Discuss a node, then discuss a different node. Verify first gold ring fades
   f. Discuss a text_tier=4 paper. Verify "Limited Data" caveat in response
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Race condition: discuss request returns after user has navigated away
   b. Context menu positioned off-screen (right edge, bottom edge of viewport)
   c. Discuss fires on nodes not in the current graph (stale references)
   d. sessionStorage cache grows unbounded (verify key format includes graph_id)
   e. LLM prose mentions facts NOT in the injected context (hallucination check)
5. VERIFY no regression in: existing context menu behavior, graph click/hover,
   chat panel open/close, streaming responses
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why none
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #005: GRAPH-AWARE COMMANDS
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           C
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #056 (Slash Commands), #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation)
BLOCKS:          #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation), #108 (Prune-Aware Chat)
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #004 (Discuss Integration), #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Graph-Aware Commands transforms Athena from a passive Q&A system into an active
graph manipulation tool. Users can type natural language commands that directly
affect the ancestry graph visualization. Instead of manually finding the prune
button, the user types "prune ResNet from the graph" and Athena executes the
pruning simulation, narrates what collapses, and renders the animation. Instead
of hunting for a filter control, the user types "show only the machine learning
cluster" and the graph filters accordingly.

This feature exists because researchers think in questions, not in button clicks.
When a researcher wonders "what happens if we remove the attention mechanism
papers?", they should be able to ask that question directly rather than learning
which UI element corresponds to that operation. The chat interface becomes a
command line for graph exploration, where natural language maps to graph operations.

The command recognition system uses a combination of keyword detection and LLM
classification. Simple commands like "prune X" or "highlight X" are detected via
keyword patterns (no LLM call needed, saving tokens). Complex commands like
"show me the path between transformers and the earliest NLP paper" require LLM
classification to determine intent (pathfinding) and extract entities (transformers,
earliest NLP paper). The LLM classification uses the fast model (Groq 8b) for
speed, keeping total command execution under 3 seconds.

What makes this unique to Athena: graph-aware commands are not just text filters.
They are OPERATIONS on a live citation graph. "Prune ResNet" triggers the actual
BFS cascading pruning animation. "Find the bottleneck" computes PageRank and
highlights the highest-impact node with a gold ring. "Trace the path from BERT
to Word2Vec" computes and animates the shortest path through the graph. These
operations produce real-time visual changes in the graph area while Athena narrates
what is happening in the chat panel.

The command system supports 12 distinct graph operations: prune, highlight, filter,
trace path, find bottleneck, show cluster, compare, zoom to node, reset view,
show descendants, show ancestors, and annotate. Each operation has its own
confirmation flow, animation, and narration template. The system never modifies
the graph state silently: every command produces visible feedback in both the graph
and the chat.

Integration with existing features is deep. Commands invoke the same underlying
functions as the UI buttons (prune uses pruning.js, highlight uses graph.js
highlight system, filter uses the existing filter controls). This means graph-aware
commands are not a parallel system but a natural language interface to the existing
graph manipulation toolkit.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User types a message in the Athena chat input that contains a graph operation
intent. Examples: "prune VGGNet", "show me the attention cluster", "what's the
bottleneck?", "trace the path from ResNet to AlexNet", "zoom into BERT."
The system detects the command intent automatically; no special prefix required.
Alternatively, user can prefix with /graph for explicit command mode.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types "prune ResNet from the graph" in the Athena chat input and presses
   Enter. The message appears in the chat as a normal user bubble.

2. Within 500ms, Athena recognizes this as a graph command (keyword "prune" +
   entity resolution matches "ResNet" to paper_id). A confirmation message appears:
   "I'll simulate removing Deep Residual Learning (He, 2016) from the lineage.
   This will show which papers would collapse without it. Proceed?" with two
   buttons: [Execute Prune] and [Cancel].

3. User clicks [Execute Prune]. Athena sends the prune command to the graph
   system. The chat shows a progress indicator: "Running pruning simulation..."

4. The graph area begins the cascading pruning animation: ResNet turns red,
   dependent papers collapse in BFS waves (each wave takes 400ms), surviving
   papers glow green. The stats counter runs in the bottom bar.

5. As the animation plays, Athena narrates in real time: "Removing ResNet
   collapses 47 of 152 papers (31%). Wave 1: 8 direct dependents collapse.
   Wave 2: 15 indirect dependents collapse. Wave 3: 24 more collapse. 105
   papers survive through alternative ancestry paths."

6. After the animation completes (3-6 seconds), Athena shows a Stat Dashboard
   block with final metrics and suggests follow-up actions: "Compare with
   pruning VGGNet," "Show surviving paths," "Reset graph view."

AFTER COMPLETION:
The graph shows the pruned state (collapsed nodes faded/red, survivors green).
The chat has a complete narration of what happened. The user can type "reset"
to restore the graph to its original state, or chain another command.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-commands.js: Command recognition engine. Pattern matching for
  12 operations. Entity resolution (fuzzy paper title match). LLM fallback for
  ambiguous commands. Confirmation flow UI. Dispatch to graph operations.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes recognized commands to athena-commands.js
  before sending to LLM. Intercepts responses that need graph side effects.
- static/js/graph.js: Exposes programmatic API for graph operations that commands
  can invoke: pruneNode(paperId), highlightNode(paperId), filterCluster(name),
  tracePath(fromId, toId), zoomToNode(paperId), resetView().
- static/js/pruning.js: Existing pruning system, invoked by prune command.
- app.py: /api/athena/resolve-entity endpoint for fuzzy paper title matching
  when the user types partial names like "ResNet" or "the attention paper."
- backend/graph_engine.py: compute_shortest_path() for trace-path commands.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
The command recognition pipeline runs entirely client-side for keyword commands:

  function recognizeCommand(userMessage) {
    const patterns = {
      prune:     /\b(prune|remove|delete|eliminate|what.?if.+never existed)\b/i,
      highlight: /\b(highlight|show|find|locate|where is)\b/i,
      filter:    /\b(filter|only show|hide|show only)\b/i,
      trace:     /\b(trace|path|connect|route|how.+relate)\b/i,
      bottleneck:/\b(bottleneck|most important|critical|key paper)\b/i,
      cluster:   /\b(cluster|group|strand|dna|family)\b/i,
      compare:   /\b(compare|versus|vs|difference between)\b/i,
      zoom:      /\b(zoom|focus|center on|go to)\b/i,
      reset:     /\b(reset|restore|undo|original|clear)\b/i,
      ancestors: /\b(ancestors?|parents?|predecessors?|built on)\b/i,
      descendants:/\b(descendants?|children|influenced|built from)\b/i,
      annotate:  /\b(annotate|mark|label|tag|note)\b/i,
    };
    for (const [cmd, regex] of Object.entries(patterns)) {
      if (regex.test(userMessage)) return { command: cmd, raw: userMessage };
    }
    return null; // No command detected, pass to regular LLM
  }

Entity resolution uses fuzzy matching against the current graph's node titles:

  function resolveEntity(text, graphNodes) {
    const candidates = graphNodes.map(n => ({
      id: n.paper_id,
      title: n.title,
      score: fuzzyScore(text, n.title) // Levenshtein + substring match
    }));
    candidates.sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score);
    if (candidates[0].score > 0.6) return candidates[0];
    // Ambiguous: ask user to clarify
    return { ambiguous: true, top3: candidates.slice(0, 3) };
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
The /api/athena/resolve-entity endpoint handles cases where client-side fuzzy
matching fails or is ambiguous:

  @app.route('/api/athena/resolve-entity', methods=['POST'])
  def resolve_entity():
      query = request.json['query']
      graph_id = request.json['graph_id']
      graph_data = load_graph(graph_id)
      matches = fuzzy_match_papers(query, graph_data['nodes'])
      return jsonify({'matches': matches[:5], 'exact': len(matches) == 1})

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
// Command execution with confirmation flow
async function executeGraphCommand(command, entities) {
  // Show confirmation
  const confirmed = await showCommandConfirmation(command, entities);
  if (!confirmed) {
    appendAssistantMessage("Command cancelled.");
    return;
  }

  // Execute the graph operation
  switch (command) {
    case 'prune':
      appendAssistantMessage("Running pruning simulation...");
      const result = await window.pruningSystem.executePrune(entities.paperId);
      narratePruneResult(result);
      break;
    case 'trace':
      appendAssistantMessage("Computing path...");
      const path = await window.graphInstance.tracePath(entities.fromId, entities.toId);
      narrateTracePath(path);
      break;
    case 'bottleneck':
      const top = window.graphInstance.getTopPageRankNodes(3);
      narrateBottleneck(top);
      window.graphInstance.highlightNode(top[0].paper_id);
      break;
    // ... other commands
  }
}
```

API CONTRACTS:

POST /api/athena/resolve-entity
Request:  { "query": "ResNet", "graph_id": "a1b2c3..." }
Response: {
  "matches": [
    { "paper_id": "2c03df8b...", "title": "Deep Residual Learning...", "score": 0.95 },
    { "paper_id": "abc123...", "title": "Residual Networks for...", "score": 0.72 }
  ],
  "exact": false
}

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Command Detected (Confirmation)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ You: prune ResNet from the graph          │
  │                                           │
  │  ┌─ ATHENA ────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ I'll simulate removing Deep         │  │
  │  │ Residual Learning (He, 2016) from   │  │
  │  │ the lineage. This will show which   │  │
  │  │ papers collapse without it.         │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │  ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────┐     │  │
  │  │  │ Execute Prune │ │  Cancel  │     │  │
  │  │  └──────────────┘ └──────────┘     │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Ambiguous Entity (Clarification)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ You: prune the residual paper             │
  │                                           │
  │  ┌─ ATHENA ────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ I found multiple matches. Which     │  │
  │  │ paper did you mean?                 │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │  ○ Deep Residual Learning (2016)    │  │
  │  │  ○ Residual Networks Behave (2018)  │  │
  │  │  ○ Identity Mappings in Deep (2016) │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Command Executing (Narration)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌─ ATHENA ────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ Running pruning simulation...       │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │ Removing ResNet collapses 47 of     │  │
  │  │ 152 papers (31%).                   │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │ Wave 1: 8 direct dependents         │  │
  │  │ Wave 2: 15 indirect dependents      │  │
  │  │ Wave 3: 24 more collapse            │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │ ┌─ STATS ─────────────────────────┐ │  │
  │  │ │ Collapsed: 47  Survived: 105    │ │  │
  │  │ │ Impact: 31%    Waves: 3         │ │  │
  │  │ └─────────────────────────────────┘ │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │ ● Compare with VGGNet prune         │  │
  │  │ ● Show surviving paths              │  │
  │  │ ● Reset graph view                  │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
User message → Confirmation: 200ms fade-in for confirmation block
Confirmation → Executing: Buttons fade out (150ms), progress text appears
Executing → Narration: Text streams token-by-token synchronized with graph animation
Narration → Complete: Stats block slides in (200ms), quick actions appear (200ms delay)
Cancel → Reset: Confirmation fades out, "Command cancelled" message appears

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User types command message
  → athena-engine.js intercepts before LLM send
  → athena-commands.js recognizeCommand() runs pattern matching
  → If command detected: extract entities from message text
  → fuzzyMatch entities against graphState.nodes titles
  → If exact match: show confirmation in chat
  → If ambiguous: show clarification radio buttons
  → If no match: POST /api/athena/resolve-entity for backend fuzzy search
  → User confirms → dispatch to graph operation function
  → Graph operation executes (prune, highlight, filter, etc.)
  → Operation results returned to athena-commands.js
  → narrateResult() constructs structured chat response
  → Response appended to chat with relevant blocks

ERROR PATH:
  → No entity found in graph → "I could not find a paper matching '[query]'
    in this lineage. Try using the full title or paper ID."
  → Graph operation fails → "The operation could not be completed. [error detail]"
  → Command ambiguous (no keyword match) → Fall through to regular LLM processing
  → User cancels → "Command cancelled." No graph state change.

DATA STORED:
  → action_log: { action: 'graph_command', command: 'prune', entity: paper_id }
  → chat_history: Command + confirmation + narration stored as conversation

DATA CACHED:
  → Entity resolution results cached per session (sessionStorage)
  → Command patterns are static (no caching needed)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant) for ambiguous command
       classification only. Most commands are detected via keyword patterns
       without any LLM call.

STAGE: Pre-pipeline intercept. Commands are detected BEFORE the 3-stage LLM
       pipeline. Only messages that do not match any command pattern are passed
       to the regular LLM pipeline.

PROMPT (for ambiguous command classification):
```
Classify this user message as one of these graph commands or NONE:
- PRUNE: user wants to remove a paper and see what collapses
- HIGHLIGHT: user wants to visually emphasize specific nodes
- FILTER: user wants to show/hide nodes by criteria
- TRACE: user wants to see the path between two papers
- BOTTLENECK: user wants to find the most critical paper
- CLUSTER: user wants to see a specific research cluster
- COMPARE: user wants to compare two papers
- ZOOM: user wants to focus on a specific paper
- RESET: user wants to undo graph changes
- ANCESTORS: user wants to see a paper's ancestors
- DESCENDANTS: user wants to see a paper's descendants
- ANNOTATE: user wants to add a note to a node
- NONE: this is a regular question, not a graph command

User message: "{userMessage}"

Respond with ONLY the command name (e.g., PRUNE) or NONE.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: Single word (command name or NONE). No prose.

FALLBACK: If LLM is unavailable, rely solely on keyword pattern matching.
Commands that require LLM classification will fall through to regular chat.

TOKEN BUDGET:
- Classification prompt: ~200 tokens
- Response: ~5 tokens
- Only called for ambiguous messages (estimated 20% of graph-related queries)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element                | Action     | Response                                  |
|------------------------|------------|-------------------------------------------|
| [Execute] button       | Click      | Command executes, graph animation starts  |
| [Cancel] button        | Click      | "Command cancelled" message               |
| Clarification radio    | Click      | Selects entity, proceeds to confirmation  |
| Narration text         | Scroll     | Auto-scrolls during narration, stops if   |
|                        |            | user manually scrolls up                  |
| Quick Action (follow)  | Click      | Sends as new command (e.g., "reset")      |
| Command input          | Enter      | Triggers command recognition pipeline     |
| /graph prefix          | Type       | Forces command mode (bypasses regular LLM)|

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Confirmation block: fadeIn 200ms, background slightly elevated (--bg-elevated)
- Execute button: gold background, on hover darkens 10%, click ripple effect
- Cancel button: outline style, text-secondary color
- Narration text: streams synchronized with graph animation events
- Stats block: slides up from 20px below, 200ms ease-out
- Clarification radio buttons: appear sequentially (100ms stagger per option)

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE: Arivu does not use sound effects.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Confirmation buttons are keyboard focusable (Tab order: Execute first, then Cancel)
- Clarification radio buttons use native radio group with aria-label
- Narration text has role="log" and aria-live="polite" for screen readers
- All graph state changes announced via aria-live region
- Command execution progress announced: "Pruning simulation in progress"

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User types "remove the attention paper" (ambiguous: multiple attention papers)
   Expected: Clarification radio buttons show top 3 matches sorted by fuzzy score.
   User selects one, then confirmation flow proceeds normally.
   Implementation: fuzzyMatch returns ambiguous=true, top3 candidates rendered.

2. Scenario: User types "prune everything" (no specific entity)
   Expected: Athena responds "Which specific paper would you like to prune? You can
   type a paper title or click a node in the graph."
   Implementation: Entity extraction returns empty, show help message.

3. Scenario: User types "prune ResNet" while a previous prune animation is running
   Expected: Queue the new command. Show "A pruning simulation is already running.
   I'll execute this after it completes, or you can cancel the current one."
   Implementation: Check pruningSystem.isAnimating, queue if true.

4. Scenario: User types something that matches a command pattern but is actually a question
   (e.g., "What does pruning ResNet show us?" matches "prune" + "ResNet")
   Expected: LLM classification identifies this as a QUESTION, not a PRUNE command.
   Falls through to regular chat with the prune data in context.
   Implementation: Question patterns ("what does", "how", "why") override command detection.

5. Scenario: User types "compare ResNet and VGGNet and AlexNet and BERT" (4 papers)
   Expected: "Comparison supports up to 3 papers. I'll compare ResNet, VGGNet, and
   AlexNet. Would you like to include BERT in a separate comparison afterward?"
   Implementation: Take first 3 entities, suggest the rest as follow-up.

6. Scenario: "trace path" between two papers with no path (disconnected components)
   Expected: "There is no citation path between Paper A and Paper B in this lineage.
   They exist in separate components of the graph."
   Implementation: graph.tracePath returns null, show informational message.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON:
  #001 — Context-Aware Responses (graph data injection for narrations)
  #004 — Discuss Integration (entity resolution system, discuss infrastructure)
  #037 — Streaming Text (narration streaming)
  #002 — Rich Response Formatting (Stat blocks in narration)

ENABLES:
  #034 — Chat-Driven Graph Navigation (extends graph commands to navigation)
  #108 — Prune-Aware Chat (prune command feeds into prune awareness)

ENHANCES:
  #003 — Quick Action Buttons (command follow-ups as quick actions)
  #014 — Comparison Mode (compare command triggers comparison)
  #056 — Slash Commands (/graph prefix integrates with slash command system)

CONFLICTS:
  NONE — commands are an additive layer on existing graph operations.

EXTERNAL:
  Uses existing graph manipulation functions (pruning.js, graph.js).
  Entity resolution may call backend if client-side matching fails.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ All 12 command types recognized via keyword patterns
□ Entity resolution matches partial paper titles to full paper_id
□ Ambiguous entities show clarification radio buttons (top 3 matches)
□ Confirmation flow for destructive commands (prune, filter, reset)
□ Prune command triggers actual cascading pruning animation
□ Highlight command adds gold ring to matched node
□ Filter command shows/hides nodes by cluster name
□ Trace command highlights shortest path between two papers
□ Bottleneck command highlights top PageRank node
□ Reset command restores graph to original state
□ Narration text streams synchronized with graph animation
□ Quick Action buttons appear after command completes

VISUAL:
□ Confirmation block uses elevated background with dot matrix bullets
□ Execute button is gold, Cancel is outline
□ Clarification radios use dot matrix style (● selected, ○ unselected)
□ Narration text appears in chat while graph animates simultaneously

PERFORMANCE:
□ Keyword command recognition completes within 50ms (no network call)
□ LLM classification for ambiguous commands completes within 2 seconds
□ Entity resolution completes within 300ms client-side
□ Full command execution (confirmation to animation complete) under 8 seconds

ACCESSIBILITY:
□ Confirmation buttons keyboard navigable
□ Radio buttons use native radio group semantics
□ Graph state changes announced via aria-live
□ Narration text has role="log"

EDGE CASES:
□ Question-pattern overrides false-positive command detection
□ Handles 4+ entity compare gracefully (first 3 + suggestion)
□ Disconnected path returns informational message
□ Queues commands when animation already running

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round — agent confirms ZERO remaining issues
□ BROWSER TEST: Feature verified in Chrome via browsermcp
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Implementation matches this spec document exactly

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-commands.js (pattern matching, entity resolution, confirmation
   flow, command dispatch), graph.js (programmatic API exposure), pruning.js
   (prune invocation from command)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Type "prune ResNet" and verify confirmation appears with correct paper title
   b. Type "what is pruning?" and verify it is NOT treated as a prune command
   c. Type "highlight the attention paper" with multiple matches, verify clarification
   d. Type "trace path from BERT to Word2Vec" and verify path highlights in graph
   e. Type "find the bottleneck" and verify highest PageRank node highlights
   f. Type "reset" after pruning, verify graph returns to original state
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Regex pattern matches inside unrelated words (e.g., "pruned" in past tense context)
   b. Entity resolution returns wrong paper (fuzzy match false positive)
   c. Command fires without confirmation for destructive operations
   d. Narration and animation out of sync (narration finishes before animation)
5. VERIFY no regression in: normal chat messages, existing graph interactions
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why none
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #006: CONVERSATION MEMORY
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Core Infrastructure
PRIORITY:        P0
PHASE:           A
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #044 (Multi-Turn Context), #109 (Session History)
BLOCKS:          #010 (Conversation Threads), #022 (Multi-Graph Chat), #048 (Conversation History Sidebar)
BLOCKED BY:      #037 (Streaming Text), #002 (Rich Response Formatting)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Conversation Memory is the persistence layer that stores every message exchanged
between the user and Athena across the entire session, including structured
context about what was discussed, which papers were referenced, which graph
operations were executed, and what insights were generated. Without this feature,
every message exists in a vacuum. The user asks about ResNet, then asks "what
about its ancestors?" and Athena has no idea what "its" refers to because the
previous exchange was not stored or referenced.

This feature solves the fundamental statelessness problem of HTTP-based chat.
Each Athena request to the backend must include sufficient conversation history
for the LLM to maintain coherent multi-turn dialogue. The memory system manages
this history: it stores messages in the PostgreSQL chat_history table (server-side,
never from client payload for security), maintains a sliding window of recent
messages for LLM context, and provides semantic retrieval of older messages
when the conversation references something from many turns ago.

The memory system operates at three levels. Level 1 is the immediate context
window: the last 10 messages (user + assistant) are included verbatim in every
LLM prompt. This handles "what about its ancestors?" type references. Level 2
is the session summary: after 10 messages, a compact summary of the conversation
so far is generated and prepended to the context. This allows the LLM to reference
earlier topics without consuming the full token budget. Level 3 is the semantic
retrieval layer: when a user references something from 30+ messages ago ("remember
when we discussed the bottleneck?"), the system searches chat_history using
keyword matching to find and inject the relevant earlier exchange.

What makes this unique to Athena: conversation memory includes not just text
messages but STRUCTURED DATA. When the user discussed ResNet via Feature #004,
the memory stores the paper_id, the computed metrics (PageRank, pruning impact),
and the graph state at that moment. When the user later asks "how does that
compare to the other critical paper?", the memory system knows "that" refers
to ResNet (paper_id stored) and "other critical paper" can be resolved by
looking at the stored PageRank rankings.

The chat_history table schema (from existing Phase 1 migration) stores: session_id,
role (user/assistant/system), content (text), metadata (JSONB with paper_ids,
graph_id, command_type, block_types), created_at. The JSONB metadata field is
critical for structured recall. Security rule from CLAUDE.md Part 13: conversation
history is ALWAYS loaded from the database, NEVER accepted from client payloads.
This prevents history injection attacks where a malicious client sends fabricated
conversation context.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
Automatic. Every message the user sends and every response Athena generates is
automatically stored in conversation memory. The user never explicitly activates
this feature. Memory is invisible infrastructure that makes the chat feel coherent.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User sends first message: "Tell me about the key papers in this lineage."
   The message is stored in chat_history with metadata { graph_id, turn: 1 }.
   Athena responds with a list of key papers. Response stored with metadata
   { paper_ids: [...], block_types: ['paper_card', 'prose'] }.

2. User sends second message: "What about the third one?" The backend loads
   the last 10 messages from chat_history for this session_id. The LLM sees
   both messages and knows "the third one" refers to the third paper in the
   previous response. Athena responds about that specific paper.

3. After 12 messages, the system generates a conversation summary:
   "User explored the ResNet lineage (601 nodes). Discussed key papers including
   ResNet, VGGNet, AlexNet. Ran pruning simulation on ResNet (31% collapse).
   Currently interested in the attention mechanism cluster."
   This summary is stored as a system message in chat_history.

4. At message 25, user says "remember when we talked about the bottleneck?"
   The last 10 messages do not contain the bottleneck discussion (it was at
   message 8). The semantic retrieval system searches chat_history for messages
   containing "bottleneck" and injects message 8 and its response into the
   LLM context as "Earlier in this conversation: [...]"

5. User navigates to a different graph (new lineage). Memory creates a new
   conversation thread tagged with the new graph_id. Previous conversation
   is preserved and accessible via the history sidebar (#048).

AFTER COMPLETION:
The entire conversation is persisted in chat_history. If the user refreshes
the page, the conversation reloads from the database (up to the last 50
messages displayed, with infinite scroll for older ones). Session-based memory
persists for the session TTL (24 hours for anonymous, indefinite for logged-in).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-memory.js: Client-side memory manager. Maintains local
  message cache. Syncs with server on each message. Handles context window
  construction for LLM prompts. Manages summary generation triggers.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Calls memory manager before each LLM request
  to construct the context payload. Stores responses after receiving them.
- app.py: /api/athena/history endpoint (GET: load conversation, POST: store
  message). /api/athena/summary endpoint (POST: trigger summary generation).
- backend/chat_guide.py: Existing ChatGuide class updated to use structured
  memory retrieval instead of flat message list.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
The memory manager maintains a local cache of the conversation:

  class AthenaMemory {
    constructor(sessionId, graphId) {
      this.sessionId = sessionId;
      this.graphId = graphId;
      this.messages = [];        // Full local cache
      this.summaries = [];       // Generated summaries
      this.turnCount = 0;
    }

    async storeMessage(role, content, metadata = {}) {
      const msg = { role, content, metadata: { ...metadata, graph_id: this.graphId } };
      this.messages.push(msg);
      this.turnCount++;
      // Persist to server
      await fetch('/api/athena/history', {
        method: 'POST',
        body: JSON.stringify({ session_id: this.sessionId, ...msg })
      });
      // Trigger summary if needed
      if (this.turnCount % 10 === 0) await this.generateSummary();
    }

    buildContextWindow() {
      const recent = this.messages.slice(-10);
      const summaryBlock = this.summaries.length > 0
        ? `[Conversation summary: ${this.summaries.at(-1).content}]`
        : '';
      return { recent, summary: summaryBlock };
    }

    async semanticSearch(query) {
      const res = await fetch(`/api/athena/history/search`, {
        method: 'POST',
        body: JSON.stringify({ session_id: this.sessionId, query })
      });
      return res.json(); // Returns matching messages with context
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
Server-side history management enforces the security rule (history from DB only):

  @app.route('/api/athena/history', methods=['GET'])
  def get_athena_history():
      session_id = get_session_id()
      rows = db.fetchall(
          "SELECT role, content, metadata, created_at FROM chat_history "
          "WHERE session_id = %s ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50",
          (session_id,)
      )
      return jsonify({'messages': rows[::-1]})  # Reverse to chronological

  @app.route('/api/athena/history', methods=['POST'])
  def store_athena_message():
      session_id = get_session_id()
      data = request.json
      db.execute(
          "INSERT INTO chat_history (session_id, role, content, metadata) "
          "VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s)",
          (session_id, data['role'], data['content'],
           json.dumps(data.get('metadata', {})))
      )
      return jsonify({'stored': True})

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
// Context window construction for LLM prompt
function buildLLMContext(memory, currentMessage) {
  const { recent, summary } = memory.buildContextWindow();

  const contextMessages = [];

  // 1. System prompt (always first)
  contextMessages.push({ role: 'system', content: ATHENA_SYSTEM_PROMPT });

  // 2. Summary of earlier conversation (if exists)
  if (summary) {
    contextMessages.push({ role: 'system', content: summary });
  }

  // 3. Recent messages (last 10)
  for (const msg of recent) {
    contextMessages.push({ role: msg.role, content: msg.content });
  }

  // 4. Current user message
  contextMessages.push({ role: 'user', content: currentMessage });

  return contextMessages;
}
```

API CONTRACTS:

GET /api/athena/history?limit=50
Response: {
  "messages": [
    { "role": "user", "content": "Tell me about key papers",
      "metadata": { "graph_id": "abc..." }, "created_at": "2026-03-24T10:00:00Z" },
    { "role": "assistant", "content": "The key papers in this lineage...",
      "metadata": { "paper_ids": ["abc...", "def..."], "block_types": ["paper_card"] },
      "created_at": "2026-03-24T10:00:05Z" }
  ]
}

POST /api/athena/history
Request: { "role": "user", "content": "...", "metadata": { "graph_id": "..." } }
Response: { "stored": true }

POST /api/athena/history/search
Request: { "session_id": "...", "query": "bottleneck" }
Response: { "matches": [ { "content": "...", "created_at": "...", "score": 0.9 } ] }

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Normal (Memory is invisible infrastructure)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Conversation flows naturally. User sees   │
  │ no memory UI elements. Memory operates    │
  │ entirely behind the scenes.               │
  │                                           │
  │ You: Tell me about key papers             │
  │                                           │
  │ Athena: The key papers in this lineage... │
  │                                           │
  │ You: What about the third one?            │
  │                                           │
  │ Athena: The third paper, AlexNet (2012),  │
  │ is notable because... [contextual reply]  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Page Reload (History Restored)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ┌─ SYSTEM ───────────────────────────────┐│
  │ │ ○ Previous conversation restored       ││
  │ │   (12 messages from earlier today)     ││
  │ └────────────────────────────────────────┘│
  │                                           │
  │ You: Tell me about key papers             │
  │                                           │
  │ Athena: The key papers in this lineage... │
  │                                           │
  │ [... earlier messages ...]                │
  │                                           │
  │ You: What about the third one?            │
  │                                           │
  │ Athena: AlexNet (2012) is notable...      │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Summary Generated (Debug only, not shown to user)

  Internal state after 10 messages:
  summary = "User explored ResNet lineage (601 nodes). Discussed key
  papers: ResNet, VGGNet, AlexNet. Interested in pruning impact."

TRANSITIONS:
Page Load → History Restored: Messages fade in sequentially (50ms stagger per
  message) from oldest to newest. System bar slides down (200ms).
Normal → Summary: No visual change. Summary generated silently in background.
  Console.debug logs "Conversation summary generated at turn 10."

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User sends message
  → athena-engine.js calls memory.storeMessage('user', message, metadata)
  → POST /api/athena/history stores in chat_history table
  → memory.buildContextWindow() constructs LLM context
  → Recent 10 messages + summary injected into prompt
  → LLM generates response
  → athena-engine.js calls memory.storeMessage('assistant', response, metadata)
  → POST /api/athena/history stores assistant message
  → Every 10 turns: memory.generateSummary() triggers
  → Summary stored as system message in chat_history

Page reload:
  → athena-engine.js calls memory.loadHistory()
  → GET /api/athena/history returns last 50 messages
  → Messages rendered in chat panel
  → System bar shows "Previous conversation restored"

ERROR PATH:
  → Database write fails → Message still in local cache, retry on next send
  → History load fails → Start fresh conversation, log warning
  → Summary generation fails → Continue without summary, next trigger at turn 20

DATA STORED:
  → chat_history table: every message with role, content, metadata JSONB, timestamp
  → Metadata includes: graph_id, paper_ids referenced, command_type, block_types

DATA CACHED:
  → Local message cache in AthenaMemory instance (JS runtime)
  → Summary cached locally until replaced by next summary
  → No cross-session caching (each session starts fresh or loads from DB)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL for summary generation

STAGE: Post-conversation (summary generated after every 10 turns)

PROMPT (Summary generation):
```
Summarize this research conversation in 2-3 sentences. Focus on:
- Which lineage/graph is being explored
- Which specific papers were discussed
- What operations were performed (pruning, comparison, etc.)
- What the user seems most interested in

Conversation:
{last_10_messages}

Write a concise summary. No em dashes. No emojis.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: 2-3 sentence plain text summary

FALLBACK: If LLM is unavailable, construct a rule-based summary from metadata:
"Discussed papers: [paper titles from metadata]. Operations: [command types].
Graph: [graph_id]."

TOKEN BUDGET:
- Summary prompt: ~500 tokens (includes 10 messages)
- Summary output: ~100 tokens
- Called once every 10 turns
- Cost: ~60 tokens per turn amortized

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element                | Action        | Response                               |
|------------------------|---------------|----------------------------------------|
| Chat messages          | Send/Receive  | Automatically stored (no user action)  |
| Page reload            | Refresh       | History restored from DB, system bar   |
| "Previous conversation"| Visible       | Informational, non-interactive         |
| Older messages         | Scroll up     | Infinite scroll loads older messages   |
| New graph navigation   | Switch graph  | New conversation thread, old preserved |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- History restoration: messages fade in with 50ms stagger, opacity 0 to 1
- System bar (restored): slideDown 200ms, background var(--bg-surface), left
  border 3px solid var(--accent-blue)
- Infinite scroll: loading spinner (dot matrix rotating dots) at top while
  fetching older messages, 300ms minimum display

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE: Arivu does not use sound effects.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Restored conversation announced via aria-live: "Previous conversation restored,
  12 messages loaded"
- Infinite scroll loading state announced: "Loading older messages"
- All message content accessible to screen readers in order

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User sends 100+ messages in one session
   Expected: Context window still only includes last 10 messages + summary.
   Summary is regenerated every 10 turns. Older messages accessible via scroll.
   Implementation: buildContextWindow() always takes slice(-10). Summary replaces
   previous summary at each 10-turn checkpoint.

2. Scenario: Database is temporarily unavailable during message store
   Expected: Message appears in chat normally. Local cache retains it. On next
   successful DB write, retry failed stores (queued in memory).
   Implementation: storeMessage catches fetch errors, queues for retry.

3. Scenario: User refreshes page mid-stream (assistant message was not complete)
   Expected: Partial message is NOT stored (incomplete). On reload, conversation
   shows up to the last COMPLETE message pair.
   Implementation: Assistant messages only stored after stream completes. Partial
   messages discarded on page unload.

4. Scenario: Semantic search for "the paper we discussed" (vague reference)
   Expected: Falls back to most recently discussed paper (highest paper_id frequency
   in recent metadata). System does not error on vague queries.
   Implementation: Semantic search returns most recent matches if no strong keyword hit.

5. Scenario: Session expires (anonymous 24-hour TTL) while conversation in progress
   Expected: On next message send, backend returns 401/session expired. Frontend
   creates new session, shows toast "Session expired. Starting fresh conversation."
   Old history is no longer accessible.
   Implementation: Session middleware checks TTL, returns error on expired sessions.

6. Scenario: User has two browser tabs open to same graph
   Expected: Both tabs share the same session_id and conversation. Messages from
   tab A appear in tab B on next scroll or manual refresh (not real-time sync).
   Implementation: Both tabs POST to same session_id. No WebSocket sync (too complex).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON:
  #037 — Streaming Text (messages must complete streaming before storing)
  #002 — Rich Response Formatting (response content format for storage)

ENABLES:
  #010 — Conversation Threads (threading requires persistent message history)
  #022 — Multi-Graph Chat (memory separated by graph_id)
  #044 — Multi-Turn Context (multi-turn requires memory of previous turns)
  #048 — Conversation History Sidebar (sidebar reads from stored history)
  #109 — Session History Awareness (session history is stored memory)

ENHANCES:
  #001 — Context-Aware Responses (context includes conversation memory)
  #003 — Quick Action Buttons (suggestions reference what was already discussed)

CONFLICTS:
  NONE

EXTERNAL:
  Depends on chat_history table (Phase 1 migration).
  Session management via backend/session_manager.py.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ Every user message stored in chat_history with correct metadata
□ Every assistant message stored after stream completes
□ Context window includes last 10 messages for LLM prompt
□ Summary generated every 10 turns
□ Page reload restores conversation from database
□ Semantic search finds relevant earlier messages by keyword
□ New graph creates new conversation thread
□ History never accepted from client payload (security rule)

VISUAL:
□ "Previous conversation restored" system bar on page reload
□ Messages fade in with stagger animation on restore
□ Infinite scroll loads older messages with loading spinner

PERFORMANCE:
□ Message store completes within 200ms (non-blocking to chat UX)
□ History load (50 messages) completes within 500ms
□ Context window construction (local) completes within 10ms
□ Summary generation (LLM call) completes within 3 seconds

ACCESSIBILITY:
□ Screen reader announces conversation restoration
□ Loading states announced for infinite scroll

EDGE CASES:
□ DB write failure queues for retry without breaking chat
□ Partial messages on page refresh are NOT stored
□ 100+ message sessions do not grow context window beyond 10+summary
□ Expired session handled gracefully with new session creation
□ Two-tab scenario does not corrupt conversation history

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round — agent confirms ZERO remaining issues
□ BROWSER TEST: Feature verified in Chrome via browsermcp
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Implementation matches this spec document exactly

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-memory.js (AthenaMemory class, storeMessage, buildContextWindow,
   semanticSearch), athena-engine.js (memory integration points), app.py
   (/api/athena/history endpoints, security enforcement)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Send 3 messages, verify all stored in chat_history table
   b. Send "what about its ancestors?" after discussing a paper, verify pronoun
      resolution works (LLM receives previous context)
   c. Refresh the page, verify conversation restores with system bar
   d. Send 12 messages, verify summary generated at turn 10
   e. At message 25, reference something from message 5, verify semantic search
      retrieves it
   f. Verify POST /api/athena/history does NOT accept a full messages array
      (security: only single message per call)
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Memory leak: messages array grows unbounded (verify cleanup strategy)
   b. Race condition: two messages sent rapidly, both try to store simultaneously
   c. Summary generation fails, next 10 turns have no summary context
   d. sessionStorage vs DB mismatch (local cache diverges from server)
5. VERIFY no regression in: chat panel behavior, streaming responses
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why none
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below
