════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #010: CONVERSATION THREADS
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Conversation Mgmt
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #048 (Conversation History Sidebar), #068 (Message Grouping)
BLOCKS:          #133 (Conversation Branching Visualization)
BLOCKED BY:      #006 (Conversation Memory), #048 (Conversation History Sidebar)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Conversation Threads allows users to branch their Athena conversation into
parallel discussion tracks without losing the main conversation context. When
a user is deep in a discussion about ResNet's pruning impact and suddenly wants
to ask about a completely different topic (the attention mechanism cluster), they
can fork the conversation into a new thread. The main thread preserves the
ResNet discussion exactly where it was. The new thread inherits the conversation
summary but starts fresh on the new topic. The user can switch between threads
at any time.

This feature exists because research exploration is inherently non-linear.
Researchers frequently follow tangents, explore side questions, and return to
their main line of inquiry. A linear chat forces them to either abandon their
current train of thought or muddle both topics into one confusing conversation.
Threads give them the cognitive workspace to explore freely without losing their
place.

The threading model is simple and flat: no nested threads (threads of threads).
Each thread has a name (auto-generated from the first message or user-specified),
a parent thread ID (the thread it was forked from), and an independent message
history. When creating a thread, the user chooses whether to carry over the
full conversation context (all previous messages visible in the new thread) or
start clean with just the session summary.

The UI shows threads as tabs in a thin bar above the chat messages area. Each
tab shows the thread name (truncated to 20 characters) and a message count.
The active thread tab is highlighted with the gold accent. Clicking a tab
switches the message view to that thread's history. A "+" button creates a
new thread from the current position. A maximum of 5 threads per session
prevents cognitive overload.

What makes this unique to Athena: each thread is tagged with the graph context
at the time of creation. If a user creates a thread while viewing a specific
cluster filter, the new thread inherits that cluster context. Thread names
auto-generate from graph context: "Thread: Attention Cluster Analysis" or
"Thread: ResNet Pruning Impact." This contextual naming eliminates the need
for the user to manually organize their threads.

Threads persist in the database via the chat_history table. Each message has
a thread_id column (added to the existing schema). The default thread is
"main" (thread_id = 'main'). New threads get UUIDs. When loading conversation
history, threads are reconstructed from the thread_id grouping.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User clicks the "+" button in the thread tab bar to create a new thread. Or
user long-presses (500ms) on a message and selects "Start thread from here."
Or user types /thread [name] in the chat input.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User is in the main thread, deep in a discussion about ResNet's structural
   role. The thread bar shows: [● Main (8 msgs)] [+]

2. User wants to explore a tangent about the attention mechanism. They click
   the "+" button. A small dialog appears: "New thread from current position.
   Name: [Attention Analysis] [Carry context ○] [Clean start ●]"
   The name auto-fills from the user's most recent interaction or they can edit.

3. User accepts the default name and selects "Clean start." A new tab appears:
   [○ Main (8)] [● Attention Analysis (0)] [+]
   The chat area clears and shows a system message: "New thread: Attention
   Analysis. Starting with session context summary."

4. User sends messages in the new thread. The thread tab updates its count:
   [○ Main (8)] [● Attention Analysis (3)] [+]

5. User clicks the "Main" tab. The chat view switches back to the main thread,
   showing all 8 original messages exactly where they left off. The scroll
   position is restored.

6. User returns to "Attention Analysis" tab. That thread resumes at its last
   position (3 messages). Scroll position restored.

7. After exploration, the user can close a non-main thread by clicking the X
   on its tab. A confirmation: "Close this thread? Messages will be preserved
   in history but the tab will be removed."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Threads persist for the session duration. On page reload, threads are restored
from chat_history (grouped by thread_id). Closed threads do not reappear but
their messages are accessible via the history sidebar (#048).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-threads.js: Thread manager. Tab bar UI. Thread creation,
  switching, closing. Scroll position persistence. Thread naming.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Updated to route messages to the active thread.
  Thread context in LLM prompts. Thread ID in all API calls.
- static/js/athena-memory.js: Updated to store/retrieve messages by thread_id.
  Summary generation per thread.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Thread tab bar styles, active/inactive states,
  new thread dialog, transition animations.
- app.py: /api/athena/history updated to accept thread_id parameter.
  Messages filtered by thread_id on retrieval.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
Thread manager maintains thread state:

  class AthenaThreads {
    constructor() {
      this.threads = [{ id: 'main', name: 'Main', messageCount: 0 }];
      this.activeThreadId = 'main';
      this.scrollPositions = {}; // { threadId: scrollTop }
    }

    createThread(name, carryContext = false) {
      if (this.threads.length >= 5) {
        showToast('Maximum 5 threads. Close one to create another.');
        return;
      }
      const id = crypto.randomUUID();
      this.threads.push({ id, name, messageCount: 0, parentThread: this.activeThreadId });
      this.switchTo(id);
      if (!carryContext) {
        showSystemMessage(`New thread: ${name}. Starting with session summary.`);
      }
      this.renderTabs();
    }

    switchTo(threadId) {
      // Save current scroll position
      this.scrollPositions[this.activeThreadId] = getMessageScrollTop();
      this.activeThreadId = threadId;
      // Load messages for this thread
      loadThreadMessages(threadId);
      // Restore scroll position
      setMessageScrollTop(this.scrollPositions[threadId] || 0);
      this.renderTabs();
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
The chat_history table needs a thread_id column. This is added via ALTER TABLE:

  ALTER TABLE chat_history ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS thread_id TEXT DEFAULT 'main';

All history queries are updated:
  SELECT * FROM chat_history WHERE session_id=%s AND thread_id=%s ORDER BY created_at

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
// Thread tab bar rendering
renderTabs() {
  const tabBar = document.querySelector('.athena-thread-tabs');
  tabBar.innerHTML = '';
  for (const thread of this.threads) {
    const tab = document.createElement('button');
    tab.className = `thread-tab ${thread.id === this.activeThreadId ? 'active' : ''}`;
    tab.innerHTML = `
      <span class="thread-dot">${thread.id === this.activeThreadId ? '●' : '○'}</span>
      <span class="thread-name">${thread.name.slice(0, 20)}</span>
      <span class="thread-count">(${thread.messageCount})</span>
      ${thread.id !== 'main' ? '<span class="thread-close" data-id="' + thread.id + '">x</span>' : ''}
    `;
    tab.onclick = () => this.switchTo(thread.id);
    tabBar.appendChild(tab);
  }
  // Add "+" button
  const addBtn = document.createElement('button');
  addBtn.className = 'thread-add';
  addBtn.textContent = '+';
  addBtn.onclick = () => this.showNewThreadDialog();
  tabBar.appendChild(addBtn);
}
```

API CONTRACTS:

GET /api/athena/history?thread_id=main&limit=50
Response: { "messages": [...], "thread": { "id": "main", "name": "Main" } }

POST /api/athena/history
Request: { "role": "user", "content": "...", "thread_id": "abc-123", "metadata": {...} }

GET /api/athena/threads
Response: {
  "threads": [
    { "id": "main", "name": "Main", "message_count": 8, "last_active": "..." },
    { "id": "abc-123", "name": "Attention Analysis", "message_count": 3, "last_active": "..." }
  ]
}

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Single Thread (Default)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ● ATHENA                            [≡]  │
  │ [● Main (8)]  [+]                        │
  │───────────────────────────────────────────│
  │  [Messages...]                            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Multiple Threads

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ● ATHENA                            [≡]  │
  │ [○ Main (8)] [● Attention (3)] [○ Gap..  │
  │───────────────────────────────────────────│
  │  [Attention Analysis thread messages]     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: New Thread Dialog

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ┌─ New Thread ─────────────────────────┐  │
  │ │ Name: [Attention Analysis       ]    │  │
  │ │                                      │  │
  │ │ Context:                             │  │
  │ │ ○ Carry full conversation            │  │
  │ │ ● Clean start (summary only)         │  │
  │ │                                      │  │
  │ │         [Cancel]  [Create Thread]    │  │
  │ └──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Thread Close Confirmation

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ┌─ Close Thread ───────────────────────┐  │
  │ │ Close "Attention Analysis"?          │  │
  │ │ Messages preserved in history.       │  │
  │ │         [Cancel]  [Close Thread]     │  │
  │ └──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
Tab click → Thread switch: active tab highlights gold (100ms), message area
  crossfades (200ms opacity transition), scroll position restored instantly
"+" click → Dialog: dialog slides down from tab bar (200ms ease-out)
Create → New tab: new tab slides in from right (200ms), messages area clears
Close → Tab remove: tab slides left and collapses width (200ms), remaining
  tabs shift to fill the space

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User clicks "+"
  → showNewThreadDialog()
  → User enters name, selects context option
  → createThread(name, carryContext)
  → New UUID generated for thread_id
  → Tab added to thread bar
  → switchTo(newThreadId)
  → Message area clears
  → System message shows thread start

User sends message in thread:
  → athena-engine.js includes activeThreadId in all requests
  → POST /api/athena/history with thread_id
  → LLM context includes only THIS thread's messages (not other threads)
  → Response stored with same thread_id

Page reload:
  → GET /api/athena/threads returns all thread metadata
  → Tabs reconstructed
  → Active thread messages loaded
  → Other thread messages loaded on demand (when tab clicked)

ERROR PATH:
  → Thread creation fails (DB error) → "Could not create thread. Try again."
  → Thread messages fail to load → Show retry button in message area
  → More than 5 threads → Toast "Maximum reached. Close one first."

DATA STORED:
  → chat_history.thread_id: thread assignment for every message
  → New column on chat_history table (ALTER TABLE)
  → Thread metadata derived from messages (name from first system message)

DATA CACHED:
  → Thread messages cached in AthenaMemory per thread_id
  → Scroll positions cached in AthenaThreads.scrollPositions (runtime only)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (for auto-generating thread names)

STAGE: Post-creation (thread name generated from first message context)

PROMPT (Thread name auto-generation):
```
Generate a 2-4 word thread name for this research conversation topic.
The user is exploring: {graph_name}
Their first message in this thread: {first_message}
Recent graph context: {recent_clicks_summary}

Respond with ONLY the thread name. No quotes. No punctuation. Example: "Attention Mechanism Analysis"
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: Single short string (2-4 words)

FALLBACK: If LLM unavailable, use rule-based naming:
"Thread [N]" or extract first noun phrase from user message.

TOKEN BUDGET:
- Name generation: ~100 tokens per thread creation
- At most 4 thread creations per session
- Total: ~400 tokens maximum

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Thread tab           | Click       | Switches to that thread's messages           |
| Thread tab           | Hover       | Subtle gold underline, shows full name tooltip|
| Active tab           | Visual      | Gold text + filled dot (●)                   |
| Inactive tab         | Visual      | Muted text + empty dot (○)                   |
| "+" button           | Click       | Opens new thread dialog                      |
| Close (x) on tab     | Click       | Opens close confirmation dialog               |
| Dialog Cancel        | Click       | Closes dialog, no action                     |
| Dialog Create        | Click       | Creates thread, switches to it               |
| Thread name input    | Type        | Live preview in tab bar (grayed out)         |
| /thread command      | Type+Enter  | Creates thread with specified name            |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Tab switch: active color transition 100ms, message crossfade 200ms
- New tab appear: width grows from 0 to auto over 200ms
- Tab close: width shrinks to 0 over 200ms, siblings shift
- Dialog: translateY(-20px) to translateY(0) with opacity 0 to 1, 200ms
- Thread bar overflow: horizontal scroll with subtle gradient fade at edges

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE: Arivu does not use sound effects.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Thread tabs use role="tablist" with role="tab" on each
- Active thread has aria-selected="true"
- Tab switch announced: "Switched to [thread name] thread"
- Close confirmation is a dialog with role="alertdialog"
- Keyboard: Arrow keys navigate tabs, Enter activates, Delete opens close dialog
- Screen reader: "Thread bar: Main, 8 messages, active. Attention Analysis, 3 messages."

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User creates 5 threads, tries to create a 6th
   Expected: "+" button is disabled (grayed out). Tooltip: "Maximum 5 threads.
   Close one to create another."
   Implementation: createThread() checks length before proceeding.

2. Scenario: User tries to close the "Main" thread
   Expected: Main thread cannot be closed. The X button is not shown on the Main
   tab. Main thread is permanent.
   Implementation: renderTabs() only adds X to non-main thread tabs.

3. Scenario: Thread bar overflows horizontally (5 threads with long names)
   Expected: Horizontal scrolling with gradient fade indicators at edges showing
   more tabs are available. Arrow buttons appear at edges on hover.
   Implementation: overflow-x: auto with gradient mask pseudo-elements.

4. Scenario: User is in Thread B, receives a proactive suggestion (#009)
   Expected: Suggestion appears in the current thread (Thread B), not Main.
   Suggestions are thread-aware and generate in the active thread context.
   Implementation: Suggestion engine reads activeThreadId from AthenaThreads.

5. Scenario: Two threads discuss the same paper from different angles
   Expected: Each thread has independent memory. Discussing ResNet in Thread A
   (pruning focus) does not contaminate Thread B's context (cluster focus).
   Implementation: buildContextWindow() filters by thread_id.

6. Scenario: User creates a thread with "Carry full conversation" option
   Expected: All messages from the parent thread appear in the new thread as
   read-only context (grayed out, non-interactive). New messages start below
   the carried context with a divider.
   Implementation: Carried messages stored with is_carried=true metadata flag.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON:
  #006 — Conversation Memory (message persistence, context window)
  #048 — Conversation History Sidebar (thread list in sidebar)

ENABLES:
  #133 — Conversation Branching Visualization (visual thread tree)

ENHANCES:
  #009 — Proactive Suggestions (thread-aware suggestions)
  #022 — Multi-Graph Chat (threads per graph)
  #068 — Message Grouping (threads provide macro-grouping)

CONFLICTS:
  NONE

EXTERNAL:
  Requires ALTER TABLE on chat_history (thread_id column).
  Compatible with existing session management.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ Thread tab bar visible with Main thread by default
□ "+" button creates new thread with name dialog
□ Thread names auto-generated from context (LLM or rule-based)
□ Switching threads loads correct message history
□ Scroll positions preserved per thread
□ Messages stored with thread_id in chat_history
□ Maximum 5 threads enforced
□ Main thread cannot be closed
□ Closing non-main threads removes tab but preserves messages
□ /thread command creates thread from chat input
□ "Carry context" option copies parent thread messages

VISUAL:
□ Tab bar uses dot matrix style (● active, ○ inactive)
□ Active tab highlighted with gold accent
□ Tab overflow handled with horizontal scroll + fade gradient
□ New thread dialog matches Athena visual system
□ Close confirmation dialog is clean and non-intrusive

PERFORMANCE:
□ Thread switch completes within 200ms (message crossfade)
□ Thread messages load from cache (instant) or DB (< 500ms)
□ Tab bar renders without layout shift on thread add/remove

ACCESSIBILITY:
□ role="tablist" and role="tab" semantics
□ Keyboard navigation (arrows, Enter, Delete)
□ Screen reader announces thread switches
□ Close confirmation is a proper dialog with focus trap

EDGE CASES:
□ 5-thread limit enforced with disabled "+" button
□ Main thread permanent (no close button)
□ Tab overflow scrollable with gradient indicators
□ Thread-aware suggestions (from #009)
□ Independent memory per thread

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round — ZERO issues
□ BROWSER TEST: Verified in Chrome
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-threads.js (thread manager, tab rendering, dialog), athena-engine.js
   (thread_id routing), athena-memory.js (per-thread message storage),
   app.py (history endpoints with thread_id)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Create a thread, send 3 messages, switch to Main, verify Main messages intact
   b. Switch back to new thread, verify 3 messages + correct scroll position
   c. Create 5 threads, verify "+" button disabled
   d. Close a non-main thread, verify tab removed and messages accessible via history
   e. Refresh page, verify all threads restore with correct messages
   f. Type /thread "Test" and verify thread created with that name
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Thread switch loses unsent message in input area
   b. Thread_id not included in all API calls (messages end up in wrong thread)
   c. Tab bar layout breaks with 5 long-named threads
   d. Memory leak: closed thread data not garbage collected
5. VERIFY no regression in: main chat, conversation memory, streaming
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STATUS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #011: ARTIFACT CARDS
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Artifact Outputs
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           G
ESTIMATED HOURS: 18
OVERLAPS WITH:   #082 (Export as Document), #096 (Research Note Artifact)
BLOCKS:          #096, #097, #098, #099, #100
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #037 (Streaming Text), #082 (Export as Document)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Artifact Cards are reusable, exportable containers that hold structured research
outputs generated by Athena during a conversation. Unlike ordinary prose paragraphs
that exist as static text in the message feed, Artifact Cards are distinct visual
components with their own identity, lifecycle, and set of actions. Each card has a
title bar showing the artifact type and name, a content area rendering the actual
research output, and an action bar with Copy, Export, Edit, and Pin buttons. The
card visually separates itself from surrounding prose through a subtle border and
background contrast, using the Arivu dot matrix aesthetic with monospace headings.

Artifact Cards exist because researchers do not merely consume analysis — they
produce deliverables. A researcher using Athena to explore the ResNet lineage will
at some point need a BibTeX file for their citation manager, a reading list for
their advisor, a paragraph for their literature review draft. Without Artifact Cards,
these outputs live inside the conversation as undifferentiated text. Once the
conversation grows to 30 messages the output is buried and irretrievable. Artifact
Cards solve the retrieval problem by surfacing research outputs as persistent objects
that can be collected, organized, and exported independently of the conversation.

The user experiences this as a shift in register: when Athena produces an artifact,
the response does not just stream text into the chat. A distinct card element
materializes below the accompanying prose, with a title bar announcing what it is
("RESEARCH NOTE -- VGGNet Bottleneck Analysis"), a formatted content area, and
action buttons. The card persists in the conversation even as the user continues
chatting, and a small Artifacts panel on the side of the chat shows a running
collection of all cards generated in the session. The user can pin important cards
so they float to the top of the collection.

What makes this unique to Arivu is that artifacts are not generic documents: they are
always grounded in citation graph data. A BibTeX artifact contains exactly the papers
visible in the current lineage, not papers recalled from LLM training data. A reading
list artifact ranks papers by their computed bottleneck impact score from the pruning
engine. A literature review draft cites specific mutation types from edge_analysis
records. Every artifact inherits the precision of Arivu's computed graph intelligence.

This feature is the prerequisite for the entire Artifact Output category (#096 through
#100). Without the card container, frame, and action infrastructure established here,
features like Research Note Artifact (#096), BibTeX Output Block (#097), Reading List
Artifact (#098), Literature Review Draft Artifact (#099), and Presentation Slide
Artifact (#100) cannot be implemented. Those features are content types; this feature
is the container they all live inside.

The implementation requires three coordinated layers: a frontend ArtifactCard class
that renders and manages card state, an ArtifactsPanel class that tracks all cards
in the session and provides the side panel, and a backend route that signals to the
frontend when a response contains artifact content rather than pure prose. Artifact
content is identified by a JSON wrapper in the LLM response: when the LLM returns a
block tagged as type "artifact", the frontend instantiates a card rather than flowing
text into the standard message bubble.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
Athena produces an artifact-type response. This can happen in two ways: the user
explicitly requests an artifact ("Generate a BibTeX file for the top 10 papers"),
or Athena proactively offers one ("I have prepared a reading list based on the
bottleneck papers. Would you like to save it?"). The card appears at the bottom of
the Athena response message, below any prose explanation.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. You type "Create a reading list of the 8 most critical papers in this lineage,
   ordered by their bottleneck impact score." Athena acknowledges and begins
   streaming an explanation of its approach.

2. As the explanation prose finishes streaming, a new element materializes below
   it inside the same message bubble. The artifact card renders with a translucent
   background distinct from the prose area. The title bar reads "READING LIST --
   Critical Papers by Bottleneck Impact" with a small document icon in monospace
   style. The card enters via a 300ms translateY(12px) to translateY(0) fade-in.

3. The content area of the card fills with the reading list: 8 paper entries, each
   showing title, author, year, and bottleneck impact percentage. Impact percentages
   come from the precomputed pruning data in the graph JSON -- these are computed
   numbers, not LLM-generated estimates. Papers are ranked: ResNet (He, 2016, 38.2%
   impact) at the top, down through VGGNet (Simonyan, 2014, 14.7% impact).

4. Below the content area, an action bar appears: [Copy] [Export] [Edit] [Pin].
   You click Copy. The reading list text (formatted as markdown) is placed on the
   clipboard. A small confirmation indicator "Copied" appears in the title bar for
   1.5 seconds, then fades. No modal. No toast. Just an inline confirmation.

5. You click Pin. A small filled circle appears in the title bar to indicate pinned
   state. The card appears at the top of the Artifacts panel on the right side of
   the chat interface. The panel header updates from "ARTIFACTS (2)" to "ARTIFACTS (3)"
   to reflect the new entry.

6. Later in the conversation you click Export. A small dropdown menu appears below
   the button: "Markdown", "PDF", "Plain Text". You select Markdown. The browser
   downloads a file named "reading-list-[session-id].md" immediately.

7. You click Edit. The content area transitions from rendered view to an editable
   textarea pre-populated with the artifact content. You can modify the text,
   reorder entries, or add annotations. Clicking "Save" re-renders the content and
   updates the pinned panel entry. Clicking "Discard" reverts to the pre-edit state.

AFTER COMPLETION:
The artifact card persists in the conversation message feed at the position it was
created. It also appears in the Artifacts panel if pinned. The user can scroll past
it, collapse it using the title bar, or expand it again. Refreshing the page loses
the artifact unless the session is persisted (Phase 6 auth feature). Within a session
the artifact survives conversation scrolling and is always accessible.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena/athena-artifacts.js: ArtifactCard class (render, pin, copy,
  export, edit lifecycle), ArtifactsPanel class (side panel collection manager),
  ArtifactRegistry (session-scoped store mapping artifact_id to card instance).
- static/js/athena/athena-engine.js: Modified to detect artifact blocks in LLM
  response stream, instantiate ArtifactCard when artifact block completes, inject
  card into message DOM after prose section.
- static/css/athena/athena-chat.css: .athena-artifact-card, .artifact-title-bar,
  .artifact-content, .artifact-actions, .artifact-panel styles. Card border, hover
  states, pinned indicator.
- app.py: No new route required. The /api/athena/message response format is extended
  with an optional artifact block. The LLM is prompted to return artifacts as a JSON
  block tagged with type="artifact". The backend passes this block through the SSE
  stream as an event type "artifact_block".

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
ArtifactCard: Constructed with {artifact_id, title, artifact_type, content, source_papers}.
render() returns a DOM element with three child sections: title bar (type badge +
title text + pin indicator + collapse toggle), content area (rendered based on
artifact_type -- markdown for notes and reading lists, code block for BibTeX, table
for comparison outputs), action bar (Copy button, Export dropdown, Edit button, Pin
toggle). The card registers itself in ArtifactRegistry on construction.

ArtifactsPanel: A collapsible panel rendered in the chat sidebar. Listens for
'artifact:created' and 'artifact:pinned' custom events. Shows pinned artifacts at
the top, all artifacts sorted by creation time below. Each panel entry is a compact
row with artifact type badge and title. Clicking a row scrolls the conversation to
the originating card.

athena-engine.js modifications: The SSE stream parser already handles event types
"token", "block_start", "block_end". Add handling for event type "artifact_block".
When this event is received, the payload contains the full artifact JSON. The engine
calls ArtifactCard.create(payload) and injects the resulting DOM element at the end
of the current message container, after any prose content.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class ArtifactCard {
    constructor({ artifact_id, title, artifact_type, content, source_papers }) {
        this.id = artifact_id;
        this.title = title;
        this.type = artifact_type;
        this.content = content;
        this.sourcePapers = source_papers || [];
        this.pinned = false;
        this.element = null;
    }

    render() {
        const card = document.createElement('div');
        card.className = 'athena-artifact-card';
        card.dataset.artifactId = this.id;
        card.innerHTML = `
            <div class="artifact-title-bar">
                <span class="artifact-type-badge">${this.type.toUpperCase()}</span>
                <span class="artifact-title">${this.title}</span>
                <button class="artifact-pin" aria-label="Pin artifact">o</button>
                <button class="artifact-collapse" aria-label="Collapse">-</button>
            </div>
            <div class="artifact-content">${this._renderContent()}</div>
            <div class="artifact-actions">
                <button class="artifact-copy">Copy</button>
                <div class="artifact-export-wrap">
                    <button class="artifact-export">Export</button>
                    <div class="artifact-export-menu hidden">
                        <button data-fmt="markdown">Markdown</button>
                        <button data-fmt="pdf">PDF</button>
                        <button data-fmt="text">Plain Text</button>
                    </div>
                </div>
                <button class="artifact-edit">Edit</button>
            </div>`;
        this.element = card;
        this._attachListeners();
        return card;
    }

    _renderContent() {
        if (this.type === 'bibtex') {
            return `<pre class="artifact-code">${this._escape(this.content)}</pre>`;
        }
        return window.AthenaMarkdown.render(this.content);
    }
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
```
SSE event type: "artifact_block"
Payload (JSON):
{
    "artifact_id": "art_a1b2c3d4",
    "artifact_type": "reading_list",
    "title": "Critical Papers by Bottleneck Impact",
    "content": "## Critical Papers...\n1. **ResNet** (He et al., 2016)...",
    "source_papers": [
        {"paper_id": "2c03df8b48bf3fa39054345bafabfeff15bfd11d", "title": "Deep Residual Learning", "impact_pct": 38.2}
    ]
}
```

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Card Newly Rendered (default state after creation)
```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  ATHENA                                                  |
|                                                          |
|  Here are the 8 most critical papers, ranked by their   |
|  computed bottleneck impact from the pruning analysis.   |
|                                                          |
|  +-- [READING LIST] Critical Papers by Impact ---------+ |
|  |  1. Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition    | |
|  |     He et al., 2016 -- Impact: 38.2%                | |
|  |  2. ImageNet Classification with Deep Convnets      | |
|  |     Krizhevsky et al., 2012 -- Impact: 22.1%        | |
|  |  3. Very Deep Convolutional Networks (VGGNet)        | |
|  |     Simonyan & Zisserman, 2014 -- Impact: 14.7%     | |
|  |  ... (5 more entries)                               | |
|  |                                                      | |
|  |  [Copy]  [Export v]  [Edit]  [o Pin]                | |
|  +------------------------------------------------------+ |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```
[READING LIST] = type badge in accent-gold. "o Pin" uses dot matrix circle.

STATE: Card Pinned
```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  +-- [READING LIST] Critical Papers by Impact -- [*] --+ |
|  |  ... (content same as above) ...                    | |
|  |  [Copy]  [Export v]  [Edit]  [* Pinned]             | |
|  +------------------------------------------------------+ |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```
[*] = filled dot matrix circle indicating pinned state. Title bar background
shifts to a slightly warmer tint (--bg-elevated with accent-gold border-left).

STATE: Card in Edit Mode
```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  +-- [READING LIST] Critical Papers by Impact -- EDIT -+ |
|  |  +------------------------------------------------+ | |
|  |  | 1. Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition| | |
|  |  | He et al., 2016 -- Impact: 38.2%               | | |
|  |  | ...                                            | | |
|  |  +------------------------------------------------+ | |
|  |                                                      | |
|  |  [Save Changes]  [Discard]                          | |
|  +------------------------------------------------------+ |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```
Edit mode replaces rendered content with a textarea. "EDIT" badge appears
in title bar in --warning color. Save/Discard replace the normal action bar.

STATE: Artifacts Panel (sidebar, collapsed)
```
+--------------------+
|  ARTIFACTS (3)  [+]|
+--------------------+
```

STATE: Artifacts Panel (sidebar, expanded, showing 3 artifacts)
```
+------------------------------------+
|  ARTIFACTS (3)              [-]    |
|  [*] READING LIST  Critical Papers |
|  [ ] BIBTEX        8 papers        |
|  [ ] NOTE          VGGNet analysis |
+------------------------------------+
```
[*] = pinned, [ ] = unpinned. Clicking a row scrolls to that card in chat.

TRANSITIONS:
Card creation: translateY(12px) + opacity 0 to translateY(0) + opacity 1, 300ms, ease-out.
Pin toggle: title bar background transitions 200ms ease. Panel entry added with same 300ms.
Edit mode: content area height animates with max-height transition, 250ms ease-in-out.
Export menu: translateY(-4px) to translateY(0), opacity 0 to 1, 150ms ease-out.
Copy confirmation: "Copied" text cross-fades with "Copy" label, 150ms. Reverts after 1.5s.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User requests artifact ("Create a reading list...")
    |
POST /api/athena/message [app.py]
    |--- System prompt instructs LLM to return artifact block when appropriate
    |--- LLM response includes "artifact_block" SSE event after prose tokens
    |
SSE stream received by athena-engine.js
    |--- "token" events -> stream into prose section of message bubble
    |--- "artifact_block" event -> parse JSON payload
    |
ArtifactCard.create(payload) [athena-artifacts.js]
    |--- Instantiate card with artifact_id, title, type, content, source_papers
    |--- card.render() returns DOM element
    |--- Element injected into message container after prose section
    |--- 'artifact:created' custom event dispatched
    |
ArtifactsPanel listens for 'artifact:created' [athena-artifacts.js]
    |--- Adds compact entry to panel list
    |--- Updates panel header count
    |
User clicks Pin
    |--- card.pin() sets this.pinned = true
    |--- Title bar updates with filled-circle indicator
    |--- 'artifact:pinned' event dispatched
    |--- ArtifactsPanel moves entry to pinned section
    |
User clicks Export -> Markdown
    |--- card.export('markdown') constructs Blob from this.content
    |--- Triggers browser download: URL.createObjectURL(blob)
    |--- No backend call for markdown export
    |--- PDF export: POST /api/athena/artifact/export {artifact_id, format: "pdf"}
    |       -> returns presigned R2 URL or inline PDF bytes
    |
User clicks Edit -> modifies -> Save
    |--- card.enterEditMode() swaps rendered content for textarea
    |--- card.saveEdit(newContent) updates this.content
    |--- card.render() called again to refresh rendered view
    |--- If card was pinned, panel entry title updates if title changed

ERROR PATH:
LLM returns malformed artifact_block JSON
    |
athena-engine.js catches JSON.parse error
    |--- Emits error into message bubble: "Could not render artifact. Raw output below."
    |--- Renders content as plain preformatted text
    |--- No card instantiated

DATA STORED:
Artifact content: In-memory only (ArtifactRegistry, a Map keyed by artifact_id).
Not persisted to DB in Phase G. Phase 6 auth adds DB persistence to user accounts.

DATA CACHED:
Source paper data (title, impact_pct) comes from graphData already in memory.
No additional cache layer needed. ArtifactRegistry itself is the session cache.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: Yes

MODEL: Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile
STAGE: Generation (artifact content produced during the main generation stage)

PROMPT SECTION: "ARTIFACT GENERATION RULES" appended to system prompt when
the user message contains artifact-triggering intent signals (keywords: "create",
"generate", "list", "write", "give me", "make", "produce" + output-type words:
"bibtex", "reading list", "notes", "summary", "literature review").

ADDITIONAL PROMPT (literal text):
```
ARTIFACT GENERATION RULES:
When the user requests a structured output (reading list, BibTeX, research notes,
literature review draft), you must produce TWO sections in your response:
1. A brief prose explanation (1-3 sentences) of what you are creating and why.
2. An artifact block formatted as a JSON object on its own line, after the prose:

<ARTIFACT>
{
  "artifact_type": "reading_list" | "bibtex" | "research_note" | "lit_review_draft",
  "title": "[Descriptive title, max 60 chars]",
  "content": "[Full artifact content as markdown string. Escape newlines as \\n.]",
  "source_papers": [
    {"paper_id": "[40-char S2 ID from context]", "title": "[exact title]", "reason": "[why included]"}
  ]
}
</ARTIFACT>

CRITICAL RULES FOR ARTIFACT CONTENT:
- Reading lists MUST use bottleneck impact percentages from the lineage context.
  Do not invent impact numbers. Use the values in the LINEAGE CONTEXT block.
- BibTeX entries must use paper metadata from the lineage context (title, authors,
  year, doi if available). Do not fabricate DOIs or journal names.
- Research notes must cite specific papers by their exact titles from context.
- Literature review drafts must reference specific mutation_type relationships
  from the edge analysis data in context (e.g., "adoption", "generalization").
- source_papers must only include papers present in the lineage context.
  Do not include papers not in the current graph.
```

EXPECTED OUTPUT FORMAT:
Prose text streams normally via "token" SSE events. The <ARTIFACT>...</ARTIFACT>
block is detected by the backend SSE emitter, which parses it and emits a single
"artifact_block" event with the parsed JSON as payload. The closing </ARTIFACT>
tag triggers the event emission.

FALLBACK IF LLM FAILS:
If the LLM returns an artifact block with malformed JSON: render content as
plaintext code block with label "RAW ARTIFACT OUTPUT (rendering failed)".
If the LLM ignores artifact format instructions and returns only prose: the
engine does not instantiate a card. The prose is displayed normally. A small
"Save as note" button appears at the bottom of the message.

TOKEN BUDGET:
Input: artifact generation adds ~200 tokens to the system prompt (the rules above).
Output: artifact content varies. Reading list: 300-600 tokens. BibTeX 10 papers:
400-700 tokens. Literature review draft: 600-1200 tokens. All within Groq 70b's
32,768 token context window.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Trigger                     | Element                    | Action                                              |
|-----------------------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| Click Copy button           | .artifact-copy             | Copy content to clipboard, show "Copied" for 1.5s  |
| Click Export button         | .artifact-export           | Show export format dropdown menu                    |
| Click Markdown in dropdown  | .artifact-export-menu      | Download .md file, close dropdown                   |
| Click PDF in dropdown       | .artifact-export-menu      | POST to /api/athena/artifact/export, download PDF   |
| Click Plain Text in dropdown| .artifact-export-menu      | Download .txt file, close dropdown                  |
| Click outside export menu  | document                   | Close export dropdown, 150ms fade-out               |
| Click Pin button (unpinned) | .artifact-pin              | Pin card, update panel, fill dot indicator          |
| Click Pin button (pinned)   | .artifact-pin              | Unpin card, remove from pinned section in panel     |
| Click Edit button           | .artifact-edit             | Enter edit mode, show textarea, show Save/Discard   |
| Click Save Changes          | .artifact-save             | Update content, exit edit mode, re-render           |
| Click Discard               | .artifact-discard          | Revert content, exit edit mode                      |
| Click collapse toggle (-)   | .artifact-collapse         | Collapse content area, 250ms height animation       |
| Click expand toggle (+)     | .artifact-collapse         | Expand content area, 250ms height animation         |
| Click panel entry           | ArtifactsPanel row         | Scroll conversation to that artifact's position     |
| Click paper link in content | [data-paper-id] link       | Dispatch arivu:focus-paper event to graph.js        |

KEYBOARD SHORTCUTS:
- While in edit mode: Ctrl+Enter saves, Escape discards.
- Tab moves focus between action bar buttons in correct order.
- Enter on Pin button toggles pin state.

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE (Athena uses no audio)

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Card has role="region" and aria-label="Artifact: [title]"
- Copy button: aria-label="Copy artifact content to clipboard"
- Pin button: aria-pressed="true|false" (toggled on pin/unpin)
- Edit mode textarea: aria-label="Edit artifact content"
- Export dropdown: role="menu", items role="menuitem"
- Collapsed state: aria-expanded="false" on collapse toggle
- ArtifactsPanel: role="complementary", aria-label="Saved artifacts"

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

EDGE CASE 1: LLM returns artifact with papers not in current lineage
Scenario: The LLM hallucinates a paper_id not present in graphData.nodes.
Expected behavior: The card renders but the paper link for that entry renders
as plain text (not a clickable graph link). A small warning indicator "(not in
graph)" appears next to the paper title in the card content.
Implementation: During card render, source_papers are cross-referenced against
AthenaEngine.currentGraphData.nodes. Unmatched entries get class "artifact-paper-unverified".

EDGE CASE 2: User edits artifact and removes all content
Scenario: User enters edit mode, selects all text, deletes it, clicks Save.
Expected behavior: Save is disabled when content area is empty. An inline warning
"Artifact content cannot be empty" appears below the textarea. Discard button
remains active to exit without saving.
Implementation: .artifact-save button has disabled attribute toggled by textarea
input event. If textarea.value.trim().length === 0, save button disabled.

EDGE CASE 3: Multiple artifacts in a single response
Scenario: User asks "Give me a BibTeX file AND a reading list."
Expected behavior: Two artifact blocks are returned in sequence. Two cards render
in the message, stacked vertically with 12px gap. Both appear in the Artifacts panel.
Implementation: SSE parser accumulates multiple artifact_block events. Each
triggers its own ArtifactCard.create() call. Both are appended to message container.

EDGE CASE 4: Session ends and user returns to page
Scenario: User refreshes the browser. The conversation is lost (pre-Phase 6).
Expected behavior: All artifact cards are lost along with conversation history.
The Artifacts panel is empty. No stale data persists.
Implementation: ArtifactRegistry is a module-level Map, not persisted to
sessionStorage or localStorage in Phase G. Full persistence is a Phase 6 feature.
No data recovery is attempted.

EDGE CASE 5: Very long artifact content (literature review draft, 2000+ words)
Scenario: User requests a full literature review draft. LLM returns 2000 words.
Expected behavior: Card content area shows the first ~300 words with a
"Show full content" button below. Clicking the button expands the content area
to show all text with a max-height plus overflow-y: auto scrollable region.
Implementation: If rendered content height exceeds 400px, apply max-height: 400px
and overflow: hidden. Add a "Show more" button that removes the max-height constraint.

EDGE CASE 6: Export fails (PDF generation error on backend)
Scenario: User clicks Export PDF. The backend /api/athena/artifact/export returns
a 500 error.
Expected behavior: The export dropdown shows an inline error message "PDF export
failed. Try Markdown instead." The dropdown stays open so the user can choose
a different format. No unhandled promise rejection.
Implementation: fetch() call inside export handler catches non-OK responses.
Error message injected into dropdown as a text row with --danger color.

EDGE CASE 7: Artifact generated while previous card is in edit mode
Scenario: User is editing an artifact card and sends a new message that triggers
another artifact.
Expected behavior: The new card renders in the new message. The previously open
edit session is NOT interrupted. Both cards exist independently. The user finishes
editing the first card normally.
Implementation: Each ArtifactCard instance manages its own edit state. Cards
are independent DOM elements. No global edit lock exists.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON (must be built first):
- Feature #002 (Rich Response Formatting): Artifact card content (markdown section)
  uses the AthenaMarkdown renderer from the rich formatting system. Without #002
  the content area can only render plaintext.
- Feature #037 (Streaming Text): Artifact blocks are delivered via the SSE stream.
  The streaming infrastructure must exist before artifact_block events can be parsed.
- Feature #082 (Export as Document): The PDF export path for artifacts delegates
  to the export generator infrastructure established by #082. The markdown and
  plaintext paths are self-contained; PDF is not.

ENABLES (can be built after this):
- Feature #096 (Research Note Artifact): Adds the "research_note" artifact type
  and its specific rendering template.
- Feature #097 (BibTeX Output Block): Adds the "bibtex" artifact type with syntax
  highlighted code block rendering and bibtex-specific copy formatting.
- Feature #098 (Reading List Artifact): Adds the "reading_list" artifact type
  with ranked paper list rendering and bottleneck impact bar visualization.
- Feature #099 (Literature Review Draft Artifact): Adds the "lit_review_draft"
  type with section-aware rendering and citation linking.
- Feature #100 (Presentation Slide Artifact): Adds the "slide_deck" type.

ENHANCES (improves but does not require):
- Feature #001 (Context-Aware Responses): Context ensures artifact content uses
  real graph data rather than LLM-hallucinated numbers and paper details.
- Feature #069 (Pinned Messages): Pinned artifacts share visual language with
  pinned messages. Both use filled-circle indicators. No code sharing required.

CONFLICTS WITH:
None. Artifact cards are additive to the message feed. They do not replace or
modify existing response rendering paths.

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES:
- Groq API (groq==0.8.0): Generation of artifact content. Without Groq, artifacts
  fall back to the "Save as note" button which captures raw user-initiated content.
- R2 Client (backend/r2_client.py): PDF export may store the generated PDF in R2
  and return a presigned URL. If R2 is unavailable, PDF export fails gracefully.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
[] ArtifactCard renders for all four artifact_types without errors
[] Copy button places artifact content on clipboard in correct format
[] Export Markdown downloads a valid .md file without backend call
[] Export PDF posts to /api/athena/artifact/export and downloads result
[] Pin button toggles pinned state and updates ArtifactsPanel
[] Edit mode replaces rendered content with pre-populated textarea
[] Save Changes updates content and re-renders card
[] Discard reverts to pre-edit content
[] Collapse toggle animates content area height correctly
[] ArtifactsPanel count increments with each new artifact
[] Panel entry click scrolls conversation to correct card position
[] Paper links in content dispatch arivu:focus-paper to graph.js

VISUAL:
[] Card has distinct background from surrounding prose (--bg-elevated)
[] Type badge uses --accent-gold color
[] Pinned indicator uses filled dot matrix circle
[] Edit mode textarea fills card width with monospace font
[] Export dropdown appears with translateY animation, closes on outside click
[] Card creation uses 300ms translateY(12px) entrance animation

QUALITY:
[] No console errors when LLM returns malformed artifact JSON
[] Empty edit content disables Save button (no empty artifact save)
[] Long content (2000+ words) renders with Show More truncation
[] Multiple artifacts in one response render independently without overlap

ACCESSIBILITY:
[] Card has role="region" with aria-label
[] Pin button has aria-pressed reflecting current state
[] Export dropdown items have role="menuitem"
[] Edit textarea has descriptive aria-label
[] Keyboard navigation: Tab through action bar, Enter/Space activates buttons

INTEGRATION:
[] Does not break existing message rendering in athena-engine.js
[] Does not conflict with #082 Export as Document (separate code paths)
[] SSE stream parser handles artifact_block events alongside existing event types

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed -- all findings addressed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed -- all findings addressed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round -- agent confirms ZERO remaining issues
[] BROWSER TEST: Feature verified in Chrome via browsermcp
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Implementation matches this spec document exactly

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-artifacts.js (ArtifactCard, ArtifactsPanel, ArtifactRegistry),
   athena-engine.js (artifact_block event handling), athena-chat.css (artifact styles),
   app.py (artifact export route if implemented)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes above
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Request a reading list: verify card renders with real impact percentages from
      graph data, not LLM-fabricated numbers
   b. Request BibTeX for 10 papers: verify each entry has real author/year from graph
   c. Pin and unpin a card: verify ArtifactsPanel count and ordering update correctly
   d. Enter edit mode, delete all text: verify Save button is disabled
   e. Export to Markdown: verify the downloaded file contains the artifact content
   f. Generate two artifacts in one message: verify both cards render and both appear
      in the panel
   g. Click a paper link inside a card: verify arivu:focus-paper fires with correct ID
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. ArtifactRegistry retains references to removed DOM elements (memory leak)
   b. LLM sometimes forgets to close </ARTIFACT> tag -- incomplete block handling
   c. Impact percentages in reading list artifacts come from LLM generation rather
      than actual pruning data (hallucinated numbers)
   d. Export PDF route does not exist in app.py (undefined endpoint returns 404)
   e. Edit mode textarea does not escape HTML in content (XSS risk)
5. VERIFY no regression in: #037 (Streaming), #002 (Rich Formatting), #082 (Export)
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why none
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below
