════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #014: COMPARISON MODE
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 22
OVERLAPS WITH:   #061 (Comparison Card Block), #004 (Discuss Integration), #005 (Graph-Aware Commands)
BLOCKS:          #116 (Before/After Comparison — depends on comparison infrastructure)
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #004 (Discuss Integration), #005 (Graph-Aware Commands), #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Mode is a dedicated conversational mode where Athena systematically
analyzes and compares two or three papers across multiple analytical dimensions
simultaneously. The comparison is not a casual prose answer of the form "Paper A
is about X while Paper B is about Y." It is a structured, data-driven contrast
that surfaces concrete differences in methodology, structural role within the
lineage, citation patterns, mutation type distribution, temporal positioning,
and influence reach, producing side-by-side cards, sortable metric tables,
a prose synthesis section, and a visual diff overlay on the live graph.

This feature exists because every researcher doing a literature review eventually
needs to decide between competing approaches. Which technique should I build on?
Which paper represents the mainstream versus the contrarian view? When did these
two research threads diverge? Athena's answer to these questions is quantified and
sourced from the actual citation graph, not from general knowledge. When Athena
says VGGNet has 31% higher structural dependency than ResNet, that number comes
from PageRank and pruning simulation, not from training data.

Comparison Mode activates through three entry points: natural language ("compare
ResNet and VGGNet"), the /compare slash command, or multi-selecting two or three
nodes in the graph and choosing "Compare with Athena" from the selection menu.
All three paths produce identical output. The natural language path includes an
entity extraction step that resolves paper names to graph node IDs before the
comparison pipeline starts.

The comparison pipeline has four stages. Stage 1 resolves paper identities and
loads all graph data for each paper: node attributes, incident edges, pruning
impact, PageRank, cluster membership, NLP confidence tier distribution, and
publication year. Stage 2 computes sixteen comparison metrics and builds a
structured diff object. Stage 3 calls the 70b LLM to generate a prose synthesis
that explains WHAT the differences mean for a researcher's decisions, not just
WHAT the differences are. Stage 4 triggers the graph overlay that dims all
unrelated nodes and highlights comparison targets with distinct ring animations.

The output renders as a multi-block response: a mode header card, side-by-side
paper cards for each subject, a scrollable metric table with sixteen rows
sortable by gap size, a prose synthesis section of three to five paragraphs with
section headers, a recommendation box if differences are decisive, and a footer
showing which data fields drove the comparison. The visual diff overlay persists
on the graph until dismissed or until the user navigates away.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User types "compare ResNet and VGGNet" in Athena, or types /compare followed by
two paper names, or multi-selects two graph nodes and clicks "Compare with Athena"
in the selection action bar. All three paths converge on the same pipeline.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types "compare ResNet and VGGNet" and sends the message. The input is
   classified as a comparison request within 200ms. Athena responds with a
   system message: "Comparison mode activated. Extracting paper identities..."
   A thin gold border appears on the left side of the Athena panel.

2. Entity extraction resolves "ResNet" and "VGGNet" to their graph node IDs
   within 300ms using fuzzy title matching against graphData.nodes. If either
   name is ambiguous, Athena presents a disambiguation card showing two or three
   candidate papers with year, citation count, and author. User selects one.

3. Stage 1 data loading completes in under 500ms. The comparison subjects are
   confirmed with a brief card: paper title, year, and citation count for each.
   The graph dims to 20% opacity on all non-subject nodes. The two subject nodes
   pulse with distinct ring animations (blue ring for the first, gold for second).

4. Stage 2 metric computation runs locally in the frontend from graph data.
   Sixteen metrics computed: citation count, PageRank score, pruning impact,
   cluster membership, incoming edge count, outgoing edge count, median confidence
   tier of edges, year, author overlap percentage, mutation type distribution,
   BFS depth from seed, field overlap, retraction status, orphan status, NLP
   text tier, and number of papers it directly enables.

5. Stage 3 sends the computed metrics and paper summaries to the 70b LLM with a
   structured prompt requesting synthesis. The response streams into the chat.
   The loading indicator shows "Generating synthesis (70b model)..." with a
   dot-matrix progress animation.

6. The full comparison response renders in sections: mode header, side-by-side
   paper cards, the sixteen-row metric table with color-coded gap indicators,
   three to five paragraphs of prose synthesis with section headers, and an
   optional recommendation box. Total render time: 8 to 12 seconds.

7. Quick action chips appear below the response: "Compare with AlexNet," "Show
   influence paths between them," "Which should I cite first?", and "Dismiss
   overlay." The graph overlay remains active until dismissed.

AFTER COMPLETION:
The comparison result is stored in conversation memory as a single structured
block. Future messages can reference "the comparison" and memory resolves it.
The user can ask follow-up questions ("why does ResNet have higher PageRank?")
and Athena treats the comparison context as the working reference. The graph
overlay persists across follow-up messages until the user dismisses it.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena/athena-compare.js: ComparisonMode class. Handles activation,
  entity extraction, metric computation, overlay triggering, and result rendering.
  Contains computeMetrics(paperA, paperB, graphData) and resolveEntities(names).
- static/js/athena/athena-blocks.js: renderComparisonCard(), renderMetricTable(),
  renderComparisonHeader() rendering functions for comparison output blocks.
- static/js/athena/athena-engine.js: Detection and routing of comparison queries
  to ComparisonMode. Handles slash command and multi-select paths.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Comparison mode styles — gold left border, overlay
  styles, metric table row colors, recommendation box, ring animation keyframes.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/compare for 70b LLM synthesis generation.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
ComparisonMode is a class instantiated once per Athena session. It maintains
state: active comparison subjects, current overlay, and metric cache. The
resolveEntities(names, graphNodes) method performs fuzzy matching: exact title
match first, then case-insensitive partial match, then Levenshtein distance for
typo tolerance. If resolution is ambiguous (two nodes match equally), it returns
a DisambiguationResult requiring user selection before proceeding.

computeMetrics(nodeA, nodeB, graphData) is a pure function. It reads from
graphData.nodes, graphData.edges, and graphData.metadata to produce a metrics
object with sixteen named fields. All computation is local and runs in under
100ms for graphs of up to 600 nodes. No backend call is needed for metrics.

The graph overlay is triggered by dispatching an arivu:compare-overlay-activate
event with the two paper IDs. The graph.js handler dims non-subject nodes by
setting opacity to 0.15 and applies ring animations using CSS keyframes on the
subject nodes' SVG circle elements.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
POST /api/athena/compare receives the computed metrics object, paper summaries,
and session context. It calls config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile)
with a structured comparison synthesis prompt. The response streams back as SSE
blocks: heading blocks, prose blocks, and a recommendation block if the metric
differences exceed a synthesis_threshold of 0.4. LLM cache key includes both
paper IDs and a fingerprint of the metrics to avoid stale synthesis.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class ComparisonMode {
    constructor(graphData, engine) {
        this.graphData = graphData;
        this.engine = engine;
        this.active = false;
        this.subjects = [];
        this.overlayActive = false;
    }

    async activate(names) {
        this.active = true;
        this.engine.showSystemMessage('Comparison mode activated. Resolving papers...');
        this._applyGoldBorder();

        const resolved = await this.resolveEntities(names);
        if (resolved.ambiguous) {
            return this.engine.renderDisambiguationCard(resolved.candidates);
        }

        this.subjects = resolved.papers;
        this._triggerGraphOverlay();
        const metrics = this.computeMetrics(resolved.papers[0], resolved.papers[1]);
        await this._requestSynthesis(metrics);
    }

    computeMetrics(nodeA, nodeB) {
        const edges = this.graphData.edges || [];
        const incA = edges.filter(e => e.target === nodeA.id).length;
        const incB = edges.filter(e => e.target === nodeB.id).length;
        return {
            citationCount:    [nodeA.citation_count || 0, nodeB.citation_count || 0],
            pageRank:         [nodeA.pagerank || 0, nodeB.pagerank || 0],
            pruningImpact:    [nodeA.pruning_impact || 0, nodeB.pruning_impact || 0],
            incomingEdges:    [incA, incB],
            year:             [nodeA.year, nodeB.year],
            bfsDepth:         [nodeA.depth || 0, nodeB.depth || 0],
            isBottleneck:     [!!nodeA.is_bottleneck, !!nodeB.is_bottleneck],
            textTier:         [nodeA.text_tier || 4, nodeB.text_tier || 4],
            isRetracted:      [!!nodeA.is_retracted, !!nodeB.is_retracted],
        };
    }
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
```
POST /api/athena/compare
Request: {
    "session_id": "...",
    "paper_a": {
        "id": "204e3073870fae3d05bcbc2f6a8e263d9b72e776",
        "title": "Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition",
        "year": 2016,
        "citation_count": 180000
    },
    "paper_b": {
        "id": "abd1c342495432171beb7ca8fd9551ef13cbd0ff",
        "title": "ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks",
        "year": 2012,
        "citation_count": 120000
    },
    "metrics": { "citationCount": [180000, 120000], "pageRank": [0.042, 0.031], ... },
    "graph_summary": { "totalPapers": 601, "seedPaper": "..." }
}
Response: SSE stream
event: block
data: {"type": "heading", "text": "COMPARISON: RESNET vs ALEXNET"}

event: block
data: {"type": "prose", "text": "The two papers diverge fundamentally..."}

event: block
data: {"type": "recommendation", "text": "For methodology adoption, ResNet..."}

event: done
data: {}
```

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Comparison Mode Active
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ┃  COMPARISON MODE                                          │
│  ┃  ResNet (2016)  vs  VGGNet (2014)                         │
│  ┃  ● 2 papers selected  ○ Overlay active on graph           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────┐         │
│  │ RESNET               │  │ VGGNET               │         │
│  │ He et al., 2016      │  │ Simonyan et al., 2014│         │
│  │ 180,247 citations    │  │ 96,882 citations     │         │
│  │ PageRank: 0.042      │  │ PageRank: 0.031      │         │
│  │ Cluster: residual    │  │ Cluster: depth-scale │         │
│  │ [View in graph]      │  │ [View in graph]      │         │
│  └──────────────────────┘  └──────────────────────┘         │
│                                                              │
│  METRICS COMPARISON           ResNet   VGGNet   Gap          │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────          │
│  Citation count            180,247   96,882   +86%           │
│  PageRank score              0.042    0.031   +35%           │
│  Pruning impact               41%      28%   +46%           │
│  Incoming edges                87       62   +40%           │
│  Publication year             2016     2014    +2yr          │
│  BFS depth from seed             1        2    -1            │
│  ...                                                         │

│  |  adoption in this lineage.                         |     │
│  +────────────────────────────────────────────────────+     │
│                                                              │

```
Left border: 3px solid #D4A843 (gold). Header background: #1E293B.
Paper cards: 1px #334155 border, 8px radius. Blue ring on first subject,
gold ring on second subject (graph overlay). Metric gap: green for >0, muted for 0.
Recommendation box: background #FEF9C3, border 1px #F59E0B.

STATE: Entity Disambiguation Required
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ┃  COMPARISON MODE — DISAMBIGUATION NEEDED                  │
│  ┃  "ResNet" matched multiple papers. Select one:            │
│                                                              │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │ ○  Deep Residual Learning...    He et al.  2016    │     │
│  │ ○  Residual Networks of Residual...  Zagoruyko 2016│     │
│  │ ○  Identity Mappings in Deep...  He et al.  2016   │     │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
Radio buttons styled as dot matrix circles (○ unselected, ● selected).

TRANSITIONS:
Idle -> Active: Gold left border slides in from top, 250ms, ease-out.
Active -> Disambiguation: Paper cards fade in, 200ms stagger per card.
Disambiguation -> Computing: Selected card highlights, others fade, 150ms.
Computing -> Complete: Metric table rows reveal top-to-bottom, 40ms per row.
Complete -> Dismissed: Overlay fades to normal graph opacity, 400ms ease-out.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User sends comparison query or uses /compare or multi-selects nodes
    |
ComparisonMode.activate(names) [athena-compare.js]
    |--- resolveEntities() fuzzy-matches names to graphData.nodes
    |--- If ambiguous: render disambiguation card, wait for selection
    |--- If resolved: proceed to metric computation
    |
computeMetrics(nodeA, nodeB, graphData) [athena-compare.js]
    |--- Pure local computation from graph JSON
    |--- Produces 16-field metrics object in < 100ms
    |--- No backend call at this stage
    |
arivu:compare-overlay-activate dispatched [athena-compare.js -> graph.js]
    |--- graph.js dims non-subject nodes to opacity 0.15
    |--- Applies CSS ring animation to subject nodes
    |
POST /api/athena/compare [app.py]
    |--- Receives metrics + paper summaries + session context
    |--- Checks llm_cache (key: SHA256 of both paper IDs + metrics fingerprint)
    |--- Cache hit: stream cached synthesis blocks immediately
    |--- Cache miss: call GROQ_SMART_MODEL, stream response, cache result
    |
SSE block stream -> athena-blocks.js renders each block
    |--- heading block -> renderComparisonHeader()
    |--- prose block -> renderComparisionProse() with paper link injection
    |--- recommendation block -> renderRecommendationBox()
    |
Quick action chips rendered below completed response
    |--- arivu:compare-complete event dispatched for memory storage

ERROR PATH:
Entity resolution fails (paper not in graph)
    |--- Show inline error card: "Paper not found in this lineage."
    |--- Offer suggestion: "Did you mean [closest title match]?"

LLM synthesis call fails
    |--- Fall back to template synthesis from metric object only
    |--- Show notice: "Deep synthesis unavailable. Showing metric summary."
    |--- Metric table and paper cards still render fully

DATA STORED:
Comparison result stored in chat_history as a single structured entry.
Metrics object stored in session memory for follow-up resolution.
LLM synthesis cached in llm_cache (30-day TTL, key = paper IDs + metrics fingerprint).

DATA CACHED:
Metrics: in-memory per comparison activation, discarded on new comparison.
Synthesis: DB llm_cache, 30 days. Entity resolution results: in-memory per session.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: Yes

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) for synthesis
STAGE: Stage 3 — Synthesis generation after metric computation is complete

PROMPT (literal text, variables replaced at runtime):
```
You are Athena, a research intelligence system analyzing a citation graph of
[totalPapers] papers about [seedTitle].

Compare these two papers for a researcher deciding which to prioritize:

PAPER A: [titleA] ([yearA]) - [citationCountA] citations
  PageRank: [pageRankA], Pruning impact: [pruningImpactA]%
  Cluster: [clusterA], BFS depth from seed: [depthA]
  Incoming edges: [incA], Mutation type distribution: [mutTypesA]

PAPER B: [titleB] ([yearB]) - [citationCountB] citations
  PageRank: [pageRankB], Pruning impact: [pruningImpactB]%
  Cluster: [clusterB], BFS depth from seed: [depthB]
  Incoming edges: [incB], Mutation type distribution: [mutTypesB]

Write a structured synthesis with these sections:
1. STRUCTURAL ROLE: What role does each paper play in the lineage architecture?
2. INFLUENCE PATTERN: How do their citation influences differ in reach and type?
3. TEMPORAL POSITIONING: What does the time gap between them reveal?
4. RESEARCHER DECISION: If a researcher must prioritize one, which and why?

Use specific numbers from the data above. Do not fabricate statistics.
If differences are minor (< 10% gap on all metrics), say so explicitly.
```

EXPECTED OUTPUT FORMAT:
Streaming SSE blocks:
- heading block: "STRUCTURAL ROLE"
- prose block: 2-3 sentences with specific numbers
- heading block: "INFLUENCE PATTERN"
- prose block: 2-3 sentences
- heading block: "TEMPORAL POSITIONING"
- prose block: 1-2 sentences
- heading block: "RESEARCHER DECISION"
- recommendation block: 3-5 sentences

FALLBACK IF LLM FAILS:
Generate template synthesis from metric data directly without LLM. Template
format: "Paper A has [N]% higher citation count and [M]% higher pruning impact.
Paper B was published [Y] years earlier and operates at BFS depth [D]. Metric
differences are [small/moderate/significant] across the [K] dimensions compared."
No LLM call, no cache write, clearly labeled as "Metric Summary (LLM unavailable)."

TOKEN BUDGET:
Input: ~800 tokens (prompt + metrics + paper summaries + session context)
Output: ~600 tokens (synthesis across 4 sections)
Cache TTL: 30 days
Budget alert: If session has < 5 remaining Pathfinder energy calls, show warning
before executing synthesis.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Trigger                        | Element                    | Action                                              |
|--------------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|
| "compare X and Y" typed        | athena-engine.js           | Activates ComparisonMode, extracts entities         |
| /compare slash command         | athena-engine.js           | Same path as natural language activation            |
| Multi-select 2 nodes + action  | athena-compare.js          | Skips entity extraction, uses selected node IDs     |
| Disambiguation card shown      | DisambiguationCard         | User clicks a radio option to resolve ambiguity     |
| Metric table header clicked    | MetricTable                | Sorts rows by gap size (largest first by default)   |
| "View in graph" clicked        | PaperCard                  | Fires arivu:node-focus event, camera pans to node   |
| "Compare with AlexNet" chip    | QuickActionChip            | Adds third paper to comparison, reruns pipeline     |
| "Dismiss overlay" chip         | ComparisonMode             | Dispatches arivu:compare-overlay-dismiss            |
| Navigate away from lineage     | ComparisonMode             | Auto-dismisses overlay, clears active state         |
| Third paper added              | ComparisonMode             | Extends metric table with third column              |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Gold left border entry: translateX(-3px) to translateX(0), 250ms ease-out
- Graph overlay fade: non-subject nodes opacity 1.0 -> 0.15, 300ms ease-out
- Blue ring on first subject: SVG circle stroke-dashoffset animation, 800ms loop
- Gold ring on second subject: same, offset by 400ms phase shift
- Metric table row reveal: translateY(8px) opacity 0 -> natural, 40ms stagger per row
- Paper cards stagger: first card at 0ms, second at 120ms, both 200ms duration
- Recommendation box: background fades in over 400ms after prose complete

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE (Athena uses no audio)

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Disambiguation card: role="radiogroup", each option role="radio", keyboard nav
- Metric table: role="table" with proper th/td semantics, sortable headers have
  aria-sort="ascending/descending/none"
- Graph overlay: aria-hidden nodes not in comparison, overlay dismissible via
  Escape key
- Paper cards: focusable, Enter activates "View in graph" action
- Mode active banner: role="status" with aria-live="polite" for screen readers
- All comparison metrics readable by screen reader in table format

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

EDGE CASE 1: Paper not in current lineage
Scenario: User types "compare BERT and GPT-3" but the current lineage is ResNet
and neither BERT nor GPT-3 appear in graphData.nodes.
Expected behavior: Athena shows an error card: "Neither paper appears in this
lineage. Comparison Mode works on papers within the loaded graph only." Suggests
loading a BERT or GPT lineage instead.
Implementation: resolveEntities() returns an error result when neither name
matches any node. ComparisonMode renders the error card and does not activate
the overlay or call the backend.

EDGE CASE 2: Same paper compared to itself
Scenario: User types "compare ResNet and ResNet" or selects the same node twice.
Expected behavior: Athena catches the duplicate and responds: "Both references
resolve to the same paper. Please specify two different papers."
Implementation: resolveEntities() checks if both resolved IDs are identical.
Returns an error result before any pipeline stage runs.

EDGE CASE 3: Three papers instead of two
Scenario: User types "compare ResNet, VGGNet, and AlexNet" (three names).
Expected behavior: ComparisonMode extends to three-column layout. Metric table
has three data columns. Prose synthesis covers all three. Graph overlay adds a
third ring (teal color) on the third subject node.
Implementation: The pipeline supports up to three subjects. computeMetrics()
accepts an array of nodes. The 70b prompt template has a three-paper variant.
A fourth paper causes the mode to reject with "Maximum 3 papers per comparison."

EDGE CASE 4: One or both papers have no abstract or thin text tier
Scenario: Comparing two papers that only have title-level data (text_tier = 4).
Expected behavior: Metric computation still works (uses graph structural data
only). Synthesis prompt notes "Limited text data for [paper name]. Structural
metrics only." LLM is instructed not to make methodology claims without text.
Implementation: Before calling backend, check text_tier for both papers. If
either is 4, add a data quality notice to the prompt and to the rendered output.

EDGE CASE 5: Comparison during active pruning animation
Scenario: User initiates comparison while the cascading pruning animation is
running on the graph.
Expected behavior: Comparison activation is queued and waits until the
pruning animation completes (arivu:prune-complete event). A brief loading
indicator says "Waiting for animation to complete..." during the wait.
Implementation: ComparisonMode checks for an active pruning state on activation.
If PruningSystem.isAnimating is true, it queues the activation and listens for
arivu:prune-complete before proceeding.

EDGE CASE 6: Very similar papers (all metrics within 10%)
Scenario: Comparing two papers from the same cluster with nearly identical
structural metrics.
Expected behavior: The metric table still renders but gap column shows small
values. The LLM synthesis explicitly states "These papers are structurally
similar in this lineage" and focuses the analysis on content differences
(methodology, temporal gap, and mutation type differences instead).
Implementation: Metrics computation detects low-variance case (max gap < 10%).
Adds a low_variance flag to the synthesis request. The prompt template has
a low-variance variant that shifts analysis toward qualitative differentiation.

EDGE CASE 7: Network failure during synthesis request
Scenario: The POST /api/athena/compare call times out or returns an error.
Expected behavior: Metric table and paper cards are already rendered (local
computation). Only the prose synthesis fails. A retry button appears in the
synthesis section: "Synthesis failed. [Retry]". The comparison result is still
useful for the metric data even without prose.
Implementation: The synthesis streaming is separate from the metric render.
If the fetch call fails or the SSE stream emits an error event, the metric
section stays rendered and a RetryableSection component appears for synthesis.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON (must be built first):
- Feature #001 (Context-Aware Responses): Comparison uses graphData from context.
  Without context loading, paper entity resolution has no node list to match.
- Feature #002 (Rich Response Formatting): Comparison output uses comparison_card,
  metric_table, and recommendation block types from the block renderer.
- Feature #004 (Discuss Integration): Multi-select entry path requires the node
  selection action bar built by Discuss Integration.
- Feature #005 (Graph-Aware Commands): The comparison activation detection in
  athena-engine.js runs at the same priority level as graph commands. Command
  detection infrastructure must exist before comparison detection is added.
- Feature #037 (Streaming Text): Synthesis response streams as SSE blocks.
  Streaming infrastructure must exist before the backend call is made.

ENABLES (can be built after this):
- Feature #116 (Before/After Comparison): Uses the same side-by-side card
  infrastructure and overlay system. Before/After is a temporal variant of
  Comparison Mode applied across time slices.

ENHANCES (improves but does not require):
- Feature #061 (Comparison Card Block): Comparison Mode is the primary consumer
  of the comparison_card block type. Both features are stronger together.
- Feature #009 (Proactive Suggestions): Proactive suggestions can offer "Compare
  this paper with [top competitor]" when structural similarity is detected.

CONFLICTS WITH:
- Feature #015 (Timeline Narration): Both take control of the graph overlay.
  They cannot be simultaneously active. Resolution: both modes check for the
  other's active state and refuse to start if the other is running. A conflict
  banner shows: "Exit Timeline Mode first."
- Feature #027 (Counter-History Mode): Same overlay conflict as Timeline. Same
  resolution pattern applies.

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES:
- Groq API (llama-3.3-70b-versatile): Required for prose synthesis. Metric table
  and paper cards render without it, but synthesis falls back to template text.
- graphData from AncestryGraph.export_to_json(): All metric computation uses the
  nodes array with pagerank, pruning_impact, is_bottleneck, depth, text_tier fields.
  If graph JSON missing these fields, metric rows show "N/A."

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ "compare X and Y" activates Comparison Mode within 200ms of send
□ /compare slash command activates same pipeline
□ Multi-select two nodes and choose Compare activates without entity extraction
□ resolveEntities() returns disambiguation card for ambiguous names
□ computeMetrics() returns all 16 fields from local graph data without backend call
□ computeMetrics() runs in < 100ms on 600-node graph
□ POST /api/athena/compare calls llama-3.3-70b-versatile
□ LLM cache hit bypasses Groq call and serves cached synthesis blocks
□ Metric table sorts by gap size on column header click
□ Graph overlay dims non-subject nodes to 0.15 opacity on activation
□ Blue ring animation on first subject, gold ring on second subject
□ Three-paper comparison works with extended metric table and third ring (teal)
□ "Dismiss overlay" restores full graph opacity
□ Comparison context retained in memory for follow-up questions
□ Template synthesis fallback renders when Groq unavailable

VISUAL:
□ Gold left border on Athena panel when Comparison Mode is active
□ Side-by-side paper cards render with correct data
□ Metric table uses color-coded gap column (green positive, muted zero/negative)
□ Recommendation box has #FEF9C3 background and gold border
□ Disambiguation card uses dot matrix radio circles (○ / ●)
□ No emojis anywhere in the Comparison Mode output

PERFORMANCE:
□ Entity extraction completes within 300ms
□ Metric computation completes within 100ms for 600-node graph
□ Full comparison response (including LLM) complete within 12 seconds
□ Graph overlay animation completes within 400ms

ACCESSIBILITY:
□ Disambiguation card has proper radiogroup semantics
□ Metric table has correct ARIA table markup with sortable headers
□ Overlay dismissible via Escape key
□ Screen reader announces mode activation via aria-live region

EDGE CASES:
□ Paper not in lineage: error card shown, no pipeline run
□ Same paper twice: caught before pipeline, clear error message
□ Three papers: extended layout, no crash
□ Text tier 4 papers: data quality notice in output, no hallucinated content
□ Comparison during pruning animation: queued, runs after animation completes

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round — agent confirms ZERO remaining issues
□ BROWSER TEST: Feature verified in Chrome via browsermcp
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Implementation matches this spec document exactly

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-compare.js (ComparisonMode class, resolveEntities, computeMetrics),
   athena-blocks.js (renderComparisonCard, renderMetricTable, renderRecommendationBox),
   athena-engine.js (comparison detection and routing),
   app.py (POST /api/athena/compare route)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Type "compare ResNet and VGGNet" on a loaded 601-paper graph. Verify gold border
      appears, overlay activates, all 16 metric rows render, synthesis streams.
   b. Type "compare ResNet and ResNet" — verify duplicate detection, error card shown.
   c. Multi-select two nodes via graph selection, choose Compare — verify entity
      extraction step is skipped and pipeline starts immediately.
   d. Type "compare ResNet and GPT-3" when GPT-3 is not in graph — verify error card.
   e. Kill the Groq API mid-comparison (network tab block) — verify metric table
      still renders and template synthesis fallback appears with retry button.
   f. Activate comparison, then attempt to activate Timeline Narration — verify
      conflict banner appears and timeline does not start.
   g. Click metric table "Citation count" header — verify rows sort by gap size.
   h. Press Escape key with overlay active — verify overlay dismisses, graph returns
      to full opacity.
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Entity resolution with unicode paper names (accented characters in author names)
   b. computeMetrics() returning NaN for papers missing pagerank or pruning_impact
   c. LLM synthesis prompt exceeding Groq token limit for three-paper comparison
   d. Graph overlay not clearing after navigate-away (stale overlay on new lineage)
5. VERIFY no regression in: #001 (context), #002 (block rendering), #005 (commands)
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why none
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #015: TIMELINE NARRATION
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 26
OVERLAPS WITH:   #062 (Timeline Block), #005 (Graph-Aware Commands)
BLOCKS:          #027 (Counter-History Mode — uses timeline sequencing infrastructure)
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #005 (Graph-Aware Commands), #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Timeline Narration is a cinematic mode where Athena takes control of the graph
visualization and narrates the complete chronological evolution of the research
lineage as a structured story. The user activates it and Athena assumes the role
of a documentary narrator guiding a visual journey through time: "In 1998, LeCun
published the foundational work on convolutional networks. Five years passed
before the field returned to this approach. Then, in 2012, Krizhevsky applied
these ideas to a much larger dataset..." The graph camera pans and zooms to each
paper as it is narrated, highlighting the active paper with a spotlight effect
while the rest of the graph dims.

This feature exists because understanding a research lineage as a STORY is
fundamentally different from understanding it as a static graph. A researcher
new to a field does not need a node map; they need the narrative arc: what problem
was unsolved, who proposed what, which ideas competed, which won and why, what
dead ends were explored and abandoned, and how the current state of the art
emerged from that history. Timeline Narration transforms Arivu's graph data into
exactly this format: a temporally ordered story grounded in computed graph metrics.

The narration is generated by the 70b LLM using a structured prompt that receives
the papers sorted by year with their structural metrics, mutation types, and cluster
memberships. The LLM is instructed to produce narration segments: one or two
sentences per paper or per meaningful cluster transition. The segments are then
played back in sync with graph camera animations. The user has playback controls:
pause, resume, rewind to previous paper, skip to next, and adjust speed (0.5x,
1x, 2x).

The mode has three phases. In the preparation phase (0.5 to 2 seconds), the
narration segments are generated and buffered. The generation happens entirely
before playback starts, not sentence by sentence during playback, to ensure
smooth animation synchronization. In the playback phase, each paper's narration
segment is displayed in the Athena panel as text that appears word by word at the
natural speech rate of the selected speed. The graph camera smoothly transitions
to the active paper using the existing graph.js animation API. In the completion
phase, the full narration is displayed as a scrollable transcript and the user
can explore any paper by clicking its transcript entry.

The narration is context-sensitive. Papers that are structural bottlenecks receive
longer narration segments. Papers in the same cluster during the same year may be
grouped into a single segment to avoid repetition. Mutation types drive the
narrative language: an "adoption" edge gets "built on this work," a "contradiction"
edge gets "challenged the assumption that," a "generalization" edge gets "extended
beyond its original domain." This grounding in graph data ensures the narrative
is not generic historical prose but a story specifically about THIS lineage.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User types "narrate this lineage" or "walk me through the history," uses the
/narrate slash command, or clicks the "Timeline Narration" option in the Athena
mode selector. All three paths are equivalent.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types "/narrate" and sends. Athena responds with a preparation card:
   "Preparing timeline narration... Sorting [N] papers by year, generating
   story segments." A dot-matrix progress bar shows generation progress.
   The graph dims slightly (background opacity 0.7) to signal mode activation.

2. Narration generation completes within 2 to 4 seconds for graphs up to 200
   papers. For larger graphs, Athena narrates the top 60 most structurally
   important papers (by PageRank) and mentions the remainder in aggregate.
   The preparation card updates: "Ready. [N] chapters. Est. [T] minutes at 1x."

3. Playback begins. The Athena panel transitions to the narration view: a large
   text area where narration words appear, playback controls below (pause, rewind,
   skip, speed selector), and a progress indicator showing "Chapter 3 of 47:
   AlexNet (2012)."

4. The graph camera animates to the first paper (earliest year) over 800ms using
   smooth ease-in-out. The paper node gains a spotlight effect: its circle
   enlarges to 1.4x size, all other nodes fade to 15% opacity, and a soft glow
   ring appears. The narration text types out at the natural speech pace.

5. When the narration segment for paper 1 completes, the camera transitions to
   paper 2. Transition duration scales with the year gap: same year = 400ms,
   1 to 5 year gap = 600ms, more than 5 years = 900ms with a brief screen
   "time passing" fade. This temporal rhythm conveys the pace of the field.

6. The user presses pause at paper 12. Playback stops. The paper stays highlighted.
   The user can click "What made this paper pivotal?" in the quick actions below
   the narration. Athena answers in the narration context. The user resumes.

7. Playback completes. The narration view shows "END OF TIMELINE" and the full
   narration transcript renders as a scrollable section with each paper as a
   clickable anchor. The user can click any entry to jump to that paper in the
   graph and read its narration segment.

AFTER COMPLETION:
The full narration transcript is available in conversation history. Future
messages can reference "the timeline" and Athena knows the narration content.
The graph returns to full opacity but the last narrated paper remains lightly
highlighted. The user can export the narration transcript via the Export panel.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena/athena-timeline.js: TimelineNarration class. Manages the full
  mode lifecycle: preparation, segment generation coordination, playback engine,
  camera control API calls, transcript rendering, and playback controls.
- static/js/graph.js: Exposes animation API (animateToNode, setNodeSpotlight,
  setGlobalOpacity, clearSpotlight) used by athena-timeline.js. These methods
  must be added to graph.js as part of this feature's implementation.
- static/js/athena/athena-engine.js: Detection and activation of narration queries
  and /narrate command. Handoff to TimelineNarration after detection.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Narration view styles — full-text area, playback
  controls bar, progress indicator, transcript section, spotlight CSS keyframes.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/narrate-prepare for LLM narration segment generation.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
TimelineNarration maintains a segments array populated during preparation. Each
segment is an object: { paperId, title, year, text, durationMs }. The durationMs
is computed as: word_count * (60000 / words_per_minute) where words_per_minute
defaults to 150 at 1x speed.

The playback engine uses a queue-based approach. A currentIndex pointer tracks
the active segment. An interval (or requestAnimationFrame loop) advances the
word-by-word text display. On each segment transition, the engine calls
graph.animateToNode(paperId, transitionDuration) and graph.setNodeSpotlight(paperId).

Camera animation is delegated entirely to graph.js. The TimelineNarration class
calls graph.animateToNode(paperId, durationMs) and expects graph.js to handle
the D3 zoom transition. The graph.js implementation computes the target translation
and scale from the paper's SVG coordinates and applies a D3 zoom transition.

Speed multiplier (0.5x, 1x, 2x) affects both word display rate and transition
durations. At 2x, segment durations halve but minimum transition duration is
capped at 200ms to keep camera motion readable.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
POST /api/athena/narrate-prepare receives the sorted papers array (title, year,
citation_count, pagerank, pruning_impact, is_bottleneck, cluster, mutation_types
of incident edges) and returns the narration segments array. The 70b LLM generates
all segments in a single call using a batch generation prompt. Segments cap at
150 words each. The total output is capped at 4000 tokens (about 60 papers worth
of narration). Papers beyond the cap are summarized in an aggregate segment.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class TimelineNarration {
    constructor(graphData, atlasEngine) {
        this.graphData = graphData;
        this.engine = atlasEngine;
        this.segments = [];
        this.currentIndex = 0;
        this.playing = false;
        this.speed = 1.0;
        this._wordTimer = null;
    }

    async prepare() {
        const sortedPapers = this._sortByYear();
        const topPapers = this._selectTopPapers(sortedPapers, 60);
        this.engine.showPreparationCard(topPapers.length);

        const response = await fetch('/api/athena/narrate-prepare', {
            method: 'POST',
            headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
            body: JSON.stringify({ papers: topPapers, seed_title: this.graphData.metadata?.seed_paper_title })
        });
        const data = await response.json();
        this.segments = data.segments;
        this.engine.showReadyCard(this.segments.length, this._estimateDuration());
    }

    play() {
        this.playing = true;
        this._playSegment(this.currentIndex);
    }

    _playSegment(index) {
        if (index >= this.segments.length) { this._complete(); return; }
        const seg = this.segments[index];
        graph.animateToNode(seg.paperId, this._transitionDuration(index));
        graph.setNodeSpotlight(seg.paperId);
        this._typeWords(seg.text, seg.durationMs / this.speed, () => {
            this.currentIndex++;
            if (this.playing) this._playSegment(this.currentIndex);
        });
    }

    _transitionDuration(index) {
        if (index === 0) return 800;
        const yearGap = this.segments[index].year - this.segments[index - 1].year;
        const base = yearGap > 5 ? 900 : yearGap > 0 ? 600 : 400;
        return Math.max(200, base / this.speed);
    }
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
```
POST /api/athena/narrate-prepare
Request: {
    "session_id": "...",
    "seed_title": "Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition",
    "papers": [
        {
            "id": "204e3073...",
            "title": "ImageNet Classification with DCNNs",
            "year": 2012,
            "citation_count": 120000,
            "pagerank": 0.031,
            "pruning_impact": 28,
            "is_bottleneck": true,
            "cluster": "depth-scaling",
            "mutation_types": ["adoption", "generalization"]
        },
        ...
    ]
}
Response (JSON, not SSE): {
    "segments": [
        {
            "paperId": "abd1c342...",
            "title": "ImageNet Classification with DCNNs",
            "year": 2012,
            "text": "In 2012, Krizhevsky, Sutskever, and Hinton published...",
            "word_count": 42
        },
        ...
    ],
    "total_papers": 47,
    "aggregate_note": "An additional 153 papers in this lineage are summarized below."
}
```

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Preparation
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ATHENA                                                      │
│                                                              │
│  ┌── PREPARING TIMELINE NARRATION ───────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                        │  │
│  │  ● Sorting 601 papers chronologically...               │  │
│  │  ● Selecting top 60 structural papers...               │  │
│  │  ○ Generating narration segments...                    │  │
│  │  ○ Building playback timeline...                       │  │
│  │                                                        │  │
│  │  ████████████░░░░░░░░  60%                             │  │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
● = dot matrix filled circle (completed step), ○ = empty (pending).
Progress bar uses ASCII block characters. No spinner emoji.

STATE: Playback Active
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CHAPTER 7 OF 47 · AlexNet (2012)                           │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
│                                                              │
│  In 2012, Krizhevsky, Sutskever, and Hinton demonstrated    │
│  that a deep convolutional network trained on GPUs could     │
│  achieve dramatic improvements on large-scale image          │
│  classification. This was not merely an incremental gain:    │
│  the error rate dropped by nearly 10 percentage points,      │
│  a margin that forced the field to reconsider its            │
│  foundational assumptions about feature engineering.         │
│                                                              │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
│  [||]  [<<]  [>>]  [0.5x] [1x] [2x]          7 / 47        │
│                                                              │
│  [What made this pivotal?]  [Skip to ResNet]                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
Word-by-word typing effect for active narration text. Chapter indicator in
monospace. Playback controls in a fixed bottom bar. No emojis in controls.
[||] = pause, [<<] = rewind to previous, [>>] = skip to next.

STATE: Transcript View (after completion)
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TIMELINE COMPLETE · 47 papers · ~8 minutes                  │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
│                                                              │
│  1998 · [LeCun (1998)] · Gradient-based learning applied...  │
│  2001 · [Hochreiter (2001)] · Long Short-Term Memory...      │
│  2012 · [AlexNet (2012)] · In 2012, Krizhevsky, Sutskever.. │
│  ...                                                         │
│                                                              │
│  [Export transcript]  [Restart narration]                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
Paper names in [brackets] are clickable links. Clicking jumps graph camera to
that paper. Transcript is a scrollable div with max-height and overflow-y scroll.

GRAPH STATES during playback:
- Non-active papers: opacity 0.15
- Active paper node: scale 1.4x, soft glow ring (box-shadow equivalent in SVG)
- Visited papers: opacity 0.4 (slightly brighter than non-active)
- Unvisited future papers: opacity 0.15

TRANSITIONS:
Idle -> Preparation: Smooth fade-in of preparation card, 200ms.
Preparation -> Ready: Progress bar fills to 100%, then card transitions to ready card.
Ready -> Playback: Athena panel layout shifts to narration view, 300ms.
Segment to segment: Camera D3 zoom transition (duration varies by year gap).
Active paper spotlight: scale + glow animate in over 400ms ease-out.
Playback -> Transcript: Active paper spotlight fades, all opacities restore, 500ms.
Transcript -> Idle: User clicks exit, all states clear, 200ms.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User activates narration via command or mode selector
    |
TimelineNarration.prepare() [athena-timeline.js]
    |--- Sort graphData.nodes by year ascending
    |--- Select top 60 by PageRank if > 60 nodes
    |--- POST /api/athena/narrate-prepare with sorted papers array
    |
app.py [POST /api/athena/narrate-prepare]
    |--- Check llm_cache (key: SHA256 of graph_id + sorted paper IDs)
    |--- Cache hit: return cached segments immediately (< 100ms)
    |--- Cache miss: call GROQ_SMART_MODEL with batch generation prompt
    |--- Parse LLM output into segments array, validate each segment
    |--- Store in llm_cache (TTL: 30 days), return JSON
    |
TimelineNarration receives segments array
    |--- Computes durationMs for each segment from word_count
    |--- Renders ReadyCard: chapter count + estimated duration
    |--- Waits for user to click Play
    |
User clicks Play
    |
TimelineNarration._playSegment(index) loop
    |--- graph.animateToNode(paperId, transitionDuration) [graph.js]
    |--- graph.setNodeSpotlight(paperId) [graph.js]
    |--- _typeWords(text, duration, callback) [athena-timeline.js]
    |--- On completion: index++, loop continues
    |
Playback complete
    |--- graph.clearSpotlight() [graph.js]
    |--- Render full transcript with anchors
    |--- Dispatch arivu:narration-complete event

ERROR PATH:
narrate-prepare API call fails
    |--- Show error card: "Narration generation failed. [Retry]"
    |--- Retry re-sends the same request
    |--- After 3 failures: "Narration unavailable. You can explore the timeline
         manually using the Time Machine (Feature #007 in Arivu)."

graph.animateToNode(paperId) fails (paperId not in graph)
    |--- Log warning to console
    |--- Skip spotlight for this segment
    |--- Narration text still plays for the segment

DATA STORED:
Narration segments stored in llm_cache (DB, 30-day TTL).
Full transcript stored in chat_history as a single conversation entry.
No session-specific persistence (segments are deterministic from graph data).

DATA CACHED:
Segments: llm_cache DB table, key = SHA256(graph_id + sorted_paper_ids), 30 days.
On same graph reload: identical segments served from cache, preparation takes < 1s.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: Yes

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile)
STAGE: Preparation — all segments generated in a single batch call before playback

PROMPT (literal text, variables replaced at runtime):
```
You are a documentary narrator for a research intelligence system. You are
narrating the chronological evolution of the citation lineage of "[seedTitle]."

Below are the [N] most structurally important papers in this lineage, sorted
by year. For each paper, write a narration segment of 2 to 4 sentences.

RULES:
- Use past tense. Write as if telling a story to an expert researcher.
- Reference specific years: "In 2012..." / "Three years later..."
- For bottleneck papers (is_bottleneck=true): write 4 sentences, emphasize structural role.
- Use mutation type language: adoption="built directly on", contradiction="challenged",
  generalization="extended beyond", specialization="narrowed to".
- For cluster transitions (cluster changes from previous paper): add one sentence
  about the shift in research direction.
- Do NOT fabricate methodology details. Only state what the data tells you.
- Keep each segment under 150 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (JSON array, no markdown wrapper):
[
  {"paperId": "...", "text": "In 1998..."},
  {"paperId": "...", "text": "Building on this foundation..."}
]

PAPERS:
[{id, title, year, citation_count, pagerank, is_bottleneck, cluster, mutation_types}, ...]
```

EXPECTED OUTPUT FORMAT:
JSON array of segment objects. Each object has paperId (string) and text (string).
Validated against the input paper IDs. Any segment with an unknown paperId is
discarded. Segments shorter than 10 words are padded with a generic continuation
or discarded and the paper is noted in the aggregate section.

FALLBACK IF LLM FAILS:
Generate template narration from graph data: "In [year], [title] was published
with [citation_count] citations. [If is_bottleneck: This paper became a structural
bottleneck in the lineage.] [mutation_type_sentence]." No prose, purely template.
Labeled as "Template Narration (LLM unavailable)." Functional but less engaging.

TOKEN BUDGET:
Input: ~2000 tokens (60 papers with metadata)
Output: ~3600 tokens (60 segments at ~60 words each)
Total: ~5600 tokens per narration generation
Cache hit rate expected at > 80% (same graph rarely changes between sessions).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Trigger                          | Element                     | Action                                                |
|----------------------------------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| /narrate command                 | athena-engine.js            | Activates TimelineNarration.prepare()                 |
| "narrate" or "walk me through"   | athena-engine.js            | Same as /narrate                                      |
| Preparation complete             | TimelineNarration           | Shows ReadyCard, waits for Play                       |
| [Play] button click              | PlaybackControls            | Starts _playSegment(0)                                |
| [||] Pause button                | PlaybackControls            | Suspends word timer and camera queue                  |
| [<<] Rewind button               | PlaybackControls            | Decrements currentIndex, replays segment              |
| [>>] Skip button                 | PlaybackControls            | Increments currentIndex, skips to next segment        |
| Speed selector [0.5x/1x/2x]     | PlaybackControls            | Updates this.speed, applies to next segment boundary  |
| "What made this pivotal?" chip   | QuickAction                 | Pauses playback, sends paper context to Athena engine |
| "Skip to ResNet" chip            | QuickAction                 | Jump to segment matching that paper title             |
| Transcript entry clicked         | TranscriptAnchor            | Camera animates to that paper, segment text shown     |
| [Export transcript] button       | TranscriptFooter            | Copies plain text transcript to clipboard             |
| [Restart narration] button       | TranscriptFooter            | Resets to index 0, clears transcript, plays again     |
| Escape key (any point)           | KeyboardHandler             | Pauses playback, shows exit confirmation              |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Word-by-word typing: each word appends to text node, 150ms per word at 1x speed
- Camera transition: D3 zoom.transform with ease-in-out, duration per year gap
- Node spotlight entry: SVG circle r scales from 1.0 to 1.4, glow ring fades in, 400ms
- Node spotlight exit: reverse of entry, 300ms
- Visited node opacity: fades from 0.15 to 0.4 as spotlight moves past, 200ms
- Time-gap fade (> 5 year gap): entire graph fades to black for 300ms before camera moves
- Transcript reveal: each entry slides in from left, 30ms stagger

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE (Athena uses no audio)

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Narration text container: role="log", aria-live="polite" for screen reader
- Playback controls: proper button roles, aria-label="Pause narration" etc.
- Progress indicator: aria-valuenow, aria-valuemax, aria-label="Narration progress"
- Transcript: each entry is a list item (role="list" / role="listitem")
- Speed selector: role="radiogroup" with three options
- Escape exits narration mode: announced via aria-live region

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

EDGE CASE 1: Graph with papers missing year data
Scenario: Some nodes have year = null or year = 0 due to incomplete API data.
Expected behavior: Papers with no year are placed at the END of the narration
sequence, after all dated papers, with a segment: "The publication year of
[title] is not recorded in the available data." They receive no year-based
narration language ("In [year]..." is omitted).
Implementation: _sortByYear() places null-year papers last with a sentinel
value. The LLM prompt marks them as "year: unknown." Template narration
generation also handles this case with a null check before year formatting.

EDGE CASE 2: Very large graph (> 200 papers) with slow LLM
Scenario: A 601-paper graph where narration generation takes > 10 seconds.
Expected behavior: Preparation card shows a real-time countdown: "Generating...
this may take up to 15 seconds for large lineages." No timeout is thrown for
preparation. The user sees progress and can cancel if they wish.
Implementation: The POST /api/athena/narrate-prepare uses a 60-second timeout
(not the standard 30s). The preparation card polls a /api/athena/narrate-status
endpoint every 2 seconds to show progress during generation.

EDGE CASE 3: User interacts with graph during narration
Scenario: While narration is playing, user drags the graph, zooms manually,
or clicks a node.
Expected behavior: Manual interaction overrides the narration camera. Narration
continues (text still types, timer still runs) but the camera does not animate
until the user releases interaction. The next segment transition resumes camera
control.
Implementation: graph.js sets a userInteracting flag on mousedown/touchstart.
TimelineNarration._playSegment checks this flag before calling animateToNode.
If true, the camera call is deferred until the flag clears (mouseup/touchend).

EDGE CASE 4: Narration activated on a graph with only 3-5 papers
Scenario: A shallow or minimally resolved lineage has only 4 papers.
Expected behavior: Narration still works but completes in under 30 seconds.
The preparation card says "Short narration: 4 chapters. Est. 1 minute." No
minimum paper count required. The LLM generates richer segments to compensate
for the small number.
Implementation: No minimum paper count check. _selectTopPapers returns all
papers if count <= 60. The LLM prompt note "This is a small lineage of [N]
papers. Write richer segments, up to 100 words each."

EDGE CASE 5: User pauses and leaves tab for > 5 minutes
Scenario: Narration is paused, user switches tabs and returns 10 minutes later.
Expected behavior: Narration remains paused at the same segment. No timeout
or auto-exit. The graph overlay remains (subject node still highlighted). User
can resume where they left off.
Implementation: The playback engine uses a simple playing boolean, not a
timeout-based scheduler. Pausing sets playing = false and clears the word timer.
State is purely in-memory. Tab visibility changes do not affect state.

EDGE CASE 6: Conflict with Comparison Mode overlay
Scenario: Comparison Mode overlay is active when user tries to activate narration.
Expected behavior: Athena shows a conflict banner: "Comparison Mode is active.
Exit comparison first to start narration." Narration does not activate.
Implementation: TimelineNarration.prepare() checks ComparisonMode.active before
proceeding. If true, dispatches a conflict warning via the engine and returns
early without calling the backend.

EDGE CASE 7: LLM produces malformed JSON in response
Scenario: The 70b LLM returns a partial JSON array or markdown-wrapped output.
Expected behavior: The backend attempts to extract and repair the JSON using a
regex extraction pass. If extraction yields at least 50% of expected segments,
the partial result is used and the missing papers use template narration for
their segments. If extraction fails entirely, full template fallback activates.
Implementation: app.py wraps the LLM output in a try/except JSON parse block.
On failure, runs a regex r'\{"paperId":\s*"[^"]+",\s*"text":\s*"[^"]+"\}' to
extract valid segment objects individually from the raw string.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON (must be built first):
- Feature #001 (Context-Aware Responses): graphData including node metadata
  (year, pagerank, is_bottleneck, cluster) is provided by context infrastructure.
- Feature #002 (Rich Response Formatting): Preparation card, ready card, and
  transcript view use heading, prose, and list block types from the block renderer.
- Feature #005 (Graph-Aware Commands): The /narrate slash command routes through
  the same command detection infrastructure as all other slash commands.
- Feature #037 (Streaming Text): While narration generation returns JSON (not SSE),
  the engine's infrastructure for handling backend responses is shared.

ENABLES (can be built after this):
- Feature #027 (Counter-History Mode): Uses timeline sequencing infrastructure
  (sorted papers array, segment generation pipeline) as its base. Counter-History
  Mode replays the same sequence with hypothetical alternative narration.

ENHANCES (improves but does not require):
- Feature #062 (Timeline Block): The transcript view is a static version of the
  Timeline Block. Both features benefit from the same chronological data ordering.
- Feature #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation): The graph camera animation API
  built for narration (animateToNode) is reused by chat-driven navigation.

CONFLICTS WITH:
- Feature #014 (Comparison Mode): Both control the graph overlay. Cannot be
  simultaneously active. Resolution: each mode checks the other's active state
  before activation and shows a conflict warning if the other is running.
- Feature #027 (Counter-History Mode): Both control playback sequence. Cannot
  be simultaneously active. Same conflict resolution pattern.

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES:
- Groq API (llama-3.3-70b-versatile): Required for prose narration. Template
  fallback available if unavailable. Narration quality is significantly lower
  with template fallback.
- graph.js animation API: animateToNode, setNodeSpotlight, clearSpotlight,
  setGlobalOpacity must be implemented as part of this feature. These methods
  do not exist in the current graph.js and must be added.
- graphData.nodes with year, pagerank, is_bottleneck, cluster attributes:
  These fields are produced by AncestryGraph.export_to_json() from Phase 2/3.
  If graph was built before Phase 3 upgrades, some fields may be null.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ /narrate activates narration preparation within 200ms
□ Preparation card shows real-time progress during segment generation
□ POST /api/athena/narrate-prepare returns valid segments array
□ Segments are LLM-cached; second activation on same graph returns in < 1s
□ Playback starts from paper with earliest year
□ Camera animates to each paper using graph.animateToNode (new method)
□ Node spotlight (scale 1.4x + glow ring) activates for current paper
□ Non-active nodes at 0.15 opacity during playback
□ Visited nodes at 0.4 opacity after spotlight passes
□ Pause/resume works correctly, word timer suspends and resumes cleanly
□ Rewind returns to previous segment correctly
□ Skip advances to next segment correctly
□ Speed selector (0.5x, 1x, 2x) changes word display rate and transition durations
□ Full transcript renders after completion with clickable paper anchors
□ Clicking transcript anchor fires graph camera to that paper
□ Escape key pauses playback and shows exit confirmation
□ Template fallback activates when Groq unavailable

VISUAL:
□ Preparation card uses dot matrix circles (● / ○) for step status
□ Playback view uses monospace chapter indicator
□ Playback controls use text labels, no emojis
□ Transcript entries use [Paper Name] link format
□ Year gap > 5 triggers brief fade-to-dark transition between segments

PERFORMANCE:
□ Segment generation completes within 4s for 60-paper set
□ Camera transition completes within specified duration per year gap
□ Word-by-word display is smooth (no frame drops at 1x speed)
□ Preparation uses LLM cache; repeat activation < 1s

ACCESSIBILITY:
□ Narration text container has aria-live="polite"
□ Playback controls have proper aria-labels
□ Progress indicator has aria-valuenow and aria-valuemax
□ Transcript is a proper list structure
□ Speed selector is a radiogroup

EDGE CASES:
□ Null-year papers placed at end, no year formatting crash
□ Large graph (> 200 papers) preparation does not time out in 60 seconds
□ Manual graph interaction during playback defers camera without breaking word timer
□ Conflict with Comparison Mode shows warning and blocks narration start

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round — agent confirms ZERO remaining issues
□ BROWSER TEST: Feature verified in Chrome via browsermcp
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Implementation matches this spec document exactly

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-timeline.js (TimelineNarration class, playback engine, transcript),
   graph.js (animateToNode, setNodeSpotlight, setGlobalOpacity, clearSpotlight methods),
   athena-engine.js (narrate command detection and routing),
   app.py (POST /api/athena/narrate-prepare route, LLM call, cache logic)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Activate /narrate on a 60-paper graph. Verify all segments load, playback
      starts from the earliest year, camera moves to each paper in sequence.
   b. Pause at chapter 5, wait 30 seconds, resume. Verify narration continues from
      chapter 5, not from chapter 1.
   c. Activate /narrate on the same graph twice in the same session. Verify second
      activation uses cache (preparation takes < 1s).
   d. Activate /narrate while Comparison Mode overlay is active. Verify conflict
      banner appears and narration does not start.
   e. Kill Groq API during preparation (network block). Verify template fallback
      activates and narration plays with template segments.
   f. Click transcript entry for chapter 20 after playback completes. Verify camera
      animates to that paper in the graph.
   g. Set speed to 2x. Verify word display is faster and camera transitions are
      faster (but still at least 200ms minimum).
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Memory leak: word timer interval not cleared on pause or completion
   b. Spotlight not cleared when narration exits mid-sequence (graph stays dim)
   c. Camera animation queue not drained on rewind (double animation fires)
   d. LLM JSON output with escaped quotes breaking the segment parser
5. VERIFY no regression in: #001 (context), #005 (graph commands), graph.js zoom
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why none
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below
