════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #016: PAPER DEEP DIVE
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           C
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #004 (Discuss Integration), #008 (Pathfinder Integration)
BLOCKS:          #032 (Paper Recommendation Engine — uses deep dive output as structured data source)
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #004 (Discuss Integration), #008 (Pathfinder Integration), #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Paper Deep Dive is an extended single-paper analysis mode that goes substantially
further than the initial paper discussion provided by Feature #004 (Discuss
Integration). Where Discuss Integration gives a two to three paragraph orientation
("this paper introduced X, uses Y methodology, is cited Z times"), Paper Deep Dive
produces a comprehensive, multi-section structured analysis of a single paper
within its lineage context. The analysis covers six distinct analytical dimensions
rendered as separate labeled sections within a single scrollable chat message.

The six sections are: methodology extraction, key contributions, structural
position analysis, influence map, vulnerability assessment, and related work
positioning. Methodology extraction summarizes the technical approach and, when
full text is available (text_tier 1 or 2), quotes specific sentences from the
methods section. Key contributions enumerates the paper's specific advances over
prior work based on its incoming mutation type edges. Structural position analysis
describes the paper's role in the lineage using PageRank, BFS depth, bottleneck
status, and cluster membership as quantitative anchors. Influence map identifies
all papers that cite this paper (its direct children in the graph) and groups them
by the mutation type of their citation, explaining HOW the paper's ideas were used.
Vulnerability assessment identifies papers whose sole structural path to the seed
runs through this paper, quantifying what would collapse if this paper had not
been published. Related work positioning situates the paper relative to its
cluster, explaining what makes it distinct from its nearest structural neighbors.

This feature is the analytical centerpiece for a researcher who wants to deeply
understand a single paper rather than the lineage as a whole. It serves three
distinct use cases: evaluating a paper for citation ("is this the right paper to
base my methods on?"), understanding a paper's intellectual context ("what was
this paper responding to and what did it enable?"), and assessing risk ("how
dependent is this lineage on this single paper?"). Each of these use cases is
served by one or more of the six sections.

The feature uses the 70b LLM exclusively. The 8b model is insufficient for the
depth and nuance required. Full text access (text_tier 1 or 2) significantly
improves the methodology extraction section by enabling actual quotation of
methods text. When only abstract or title data is available (text_tier 3 or 4),
the analysis notes the data quality limitation explicitly rather than fabricating
methodology claims.

The output renders as a multi-block scrollable single message with a sticky
section navigation bar. The sections are labeled with monospace headers and
separated by dividers. Each section uses the appropriate block types: prose for
analysis, stat cards for key numbers, paper cards for related papers, and a
custom vulnerability block for the collapse visualization. The total message
length is expected to be 600 to 1200 words across the six sections.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User right-clicks a node in the graph and selects "Deep Dive with Athena" from
the context menu, or activates a node's info panel and clicks the "Deep Dive"
button, or types "deep dive on [paper name]" or "/deepdive [paper name]" in
Athena's chat input.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User right-clicks on the AlexNet node in the graph. The context menu shows
   three options: "Discuss with Athena," "Deep Dive with Athena," and "Add to
   Comparison." User selects "Deep Dive with Athena."

2. Athena receives the paper ID and title. A system message appears in the chat:
   "Starting deep dive on AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012)..." A loading
   indicator shows "Preparing analysis: 6 sections..." with a dot matrix
   progress representation.

3. Within 300ms, section 1 (Methodology) begins streaming. The heading block
   "1 - METHODOLOGY" appears, followed by the prose analysis streaming word by
   word. If full text is available, a quoted sentence from the methods section
   appears in a quote block ("We trained on two GPUs with..."  directly quoted
   from the paper's methods section).

4. Sections 2 through 6 stream sequentially as each LLM output chunk arrives.
   The user sees the analysis building in real time. A sticky navigation bar
   at the top of the message shows: "1 METHOD | 2 CONTRIB | 3 POSITION |
   4 INFLUENCE | 5 VULNERABILITY | 6 RELATED" with the active section highlighted.

5. The Structural Position section (3) includes three stat cards in a horizontal
   row: PageRank score, BFS depth from seed, and pruning impact percentage.
   These are computed locally from graph data and rendered immediately, before
   the LLM prose for section 3 arrives.

6. The Influence Map section (4) renders as a mini-list of the paper's direct
   children in the graph, grouped by mutation type. Each group has a count and
   a one-sentence LLM explanation. "Adoption (23 papers): 23 papers adopted
   AlexNet's convolutional architecture directly for image classification tasks."

7. The Vulnerability Assessment section (5) renders a single large stat: "47
   papers exclusively depend on this paper." Below it, the LLM explains what
   this means for the field. A button: "Show vulnerable papers" dispatches a
   filter event to the graph highlighting those 47 nodes.

8. After all six sections stream, quick action chips appear: "Compare with VGGNet,"
   "Show all papers that adopted this," "What would I lose without this paper?",
   "Export this analysis."

AFTER COMPLETION:
The deep dive is stored as a single structured memory entry. Future messages can
reference "the AlexNet analysis" and Athena resolves it. The user can ask follow-up
questions about any section. The "/deepdive" command is available for other papers
without needing to interact with the graph directly.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena/athena-deepdive.js: PaperDeepDive class. Manages activation,
  local data assembly, section navigation bar, and post-render interaction handlers
  (Show vulnerable papers button, quick action chips).
- static/js/athena/athena-blocks.js: renderDeepDiveHeader(), renderSectionNav(),
  renderInfluenceMap(), renderVulnerabilityBlock() rendering functions added to
  the existing block renderer.
- static/js/athena/athena-engine.js: Detection and routing of deep dive queries
  and /deepdive command. Receives paper ID from graph context menu event.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Sticky section nav bar styles, vulnerability block
  styles, influence map list styles, deep dive header color treatment.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/deep-dive for 70b analysis generation.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
PaperDeepDive is a class that assembles the local data package for a paper before
making the backend call. The data package includes: node attributes from graphData,
all incident edges (both incoming and outgoing) with their mutation types and
confidence tiers, cluster membership, PageRank score, BFS depth, pruning impact,
the list of papers solely dependent on this paper (computed from the pruning data),
and any cached full text excerpt (from the graph's precomputed full_text field if
available).

The section navigation bar is a sticky div rendered at the top of the deep dive
message with six anchor links. As the user scrolls through the long message, the
active anchor highlights based on scroll position using an IntersectionObserver.
Each anchor smoothly scrolls to its section when clicked.

The "Show vulnerable papers" button in section 5 dispatches an
arivu:filter-apply event with the list of vulnerable paper IDs. The graph.js
handler applies a highlight filter to those nodes, making them visually distinct.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
POST /api/athena/deep-dive receives the assembled data package and calls
GROQ_SMART_MODEL with a structured six-section prompt. The response is requested
in a specific JSON format: an array of section objects, each with a type, heading,
and blocks array. The backend streams each section as it arrives from the LLM
using SSE events. Section boundaries are signaled with section_start events.

LLM cache key: SHA256 of paper_id + graph_id + text_tier (allows cache
invalidation when the graph is rebuilt with better text data).

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class PaperDeepDive {
    constructor(graphData, engine) {
        this.graphData = graphData;
        this.engine = engine;
    }

    async activate(paperId) {
        const node = this.graphData.nodes.find(n => n.id === paperId);
        if (!node) { this.engine.showError('Paper not found in graph.'); return; }

        const dataPackage = this._assembleDataPackage(node);
        this.engine.showSystemMessage(
            `Starting deep dive on ${node.title} (${node.year || 'year unknown'})...`
        );

        const response = await fetch('/api/athena/deep-dive', {
            method: 'POST',
            headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
            body: JSON.stringify({ paper_id: paperId, data_package: dataPackage,
                                   session_id: this.engine.sessionId })
        });
        await this._streamSections(response.body);
    }

    _assembleDataPackage(node) {
        const edges = this.graphData.edges || [];
        const incoming = edges.filter(e => e.target === node.id);
        const outgoing = edges.filter(e => e.source === node.id);
        const pruning = this.graphData.precomputed_pruning?.[node.id] || null;

        return {
            paper: { id: node.id, title: node.title, year: node.year,
                     citation_count: node.citation_count, authors: node.authors,
                     text_tier: node.text_tier, cluster: node.cluster,
                     pagerank: node.pagerank, depth: node.depth,
                     is_bottleneck: node.is_bottleneck,
                     pruning_impact: node.pruning_impact },
            incoming_edges: incoming.map(e => ({ source: e.source, mutation_type: e.mutation_type,
                                                  confidence_tier: e.confidence_tier })),
            outgoing_edges: outgoing.map(e => ({ target: e.target, mutation_type: e.mutation_type,
                                                  confidence_tier: e.confidence_tier })),
            sole_dependents_count: pruning?.collapsed_count || 0,
            sole_dependents: (pruning?.collapsed_nodes || []).slice(0, 20).map(n => n.paper_id),
            full_text_excerpt: node.abstract || null,
        };
    }
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
```
POST /api/athena/deep-dive
Request: {
    "session_id": "...",
    "paper_id": "abd1c342495432171beb7ca8fd9551ef13cbd0ff",
    "data_package": {
        "paper": {
            "id": "abd1c342...",
            "title": "ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks",
            "year": 2012,
            "citation_count": 120000,
            "text_tier": 2,
            "cluster": "depth-scaling",
            "pagerank": 0.031,
            "depth": 1,
            "is_bottleneck": true,
            "pruning_impact": 28
        },
        "incoming_edges": [
            {"source": "...", "mutation_type": "adoption", "confidence_tier": "HIGH"},
            ...
        ],
        "outgoing_edges": [
            {"target": "...", "mutation_type": "generalization", "confidence_tier": "MEDIUM"},
            ...
        ],
        "sole_dependents_count": 47,
        "sole_dependents": ["...", "..."],
        "full_text_excerpt": "We trained a large, deep convolutional neural network..."
    }
}
Response: SSE stream
event: section_start
data: {"section": 1, "title": "METHODOLOGY"}

event: block
data: {"type": "prose", "text": "AlexNet employed a deep convolutional architecture..."}

event: block
data: {"type": "quote", "text": "We used two GTX 580 GPUs with 3GB of memory each."}

event: section_start
data: {"section": 2, "title": "KEY CONTRIBUTIONS"}

event: block
data: {"type": "prose", "text": "AlexNet made three primary contributions..."}

event: section_start
data: {"section": 3, "title": "STRUCTURAL POSITION"}

event: block
data: {"type": "stat_row", "stats": [
    {"label": "PageRank", "value": "0.031"},
    {"label": "BFS Depth", "value": "1"},
    {"label": "Pruning Impact", "value": "28%"}
]}

event: done
data: {}
```

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Deep Dive Active (message rendering)
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DEEP DIVE: ALEXNET (2012)                                   │
│  Krizhevsky, Sutskever, Hinton                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  [1 METHOD] [2 CONTRIB] [3 POSITION] [4 INFLUENCE]          │
│  [5 VULN] [6 RELATED]                               <- nav  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  1 - METHODOLOGY                                             │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────                      │
│  AlexNet used a deep convolutional network with ReLU         │
│  activations, dropout regularization, and data augmentation. │
│  The network was trained on two GPUs in parallel.            │
│                                                              │
│  ┌─ QUOTED FROM PAPER ─────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  "We used two GTX 580 3GB GPUs which took five to   │    │
│  │   six days to train."                                │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                              │
│  2 - KEY CONTRIBUTIONS                                       │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────                      │
│  (streams here...)                                           │
│                                                              │
│  3 - STRUCTURAL POSITION                                     │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────                      │
│  +─────────────+  +─────────────+  +─────────────+          │
│  |    0.031    |  |      1      |  |     28%     |          │
│  |   PAGERANK  |  |  BFS DEPTH  |  |  PRUNING    |          │
│  +─────────────+  +─────────────+  +─────────────+          │
│                                                              │
│  4 - INFLUENCE MAP                                           │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────                      │
│  Adoption   (23): 23 papers used AlexNet's architecture...  │
│  Generalize ( 8): 8 papers extended beyond image tasks...   │
│  Contradict ( 2): 2 papers challenged the dropout...        │
│                                                              │
│  5 - VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT                                │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────                      │
│  +──────────────────────────────────────────────────────+   │
│  |  47 papers exclusively depend on AlexNet's path      |   │
│  |  [Show vulnerable papers in graph]                   |   │
│  +──────────────────────────────────────────────────────+   │
│                                                              │
│  6 - RELATED WORK POSITIONING                                │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────                      │
│  (streams here...)                                           │
│                                                              │
│  [Compare with VGGNet]  [Show adoption papers]              │
│  [What if this was never published?]  [Export analysis]     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
Section nav: sticky monospace bar, active section highlighted with gold underline.
Section headers: 11px monospace uppercase, color #6B7280. Divider: 1px #E5E7EB.
Quote block: left border 3px #3B82F6, background #EFF6FF, italic text.
Stat row: three inline stat cards, same style as standard stat_card blocks.
Vulnerability block: border 2px #EF4444 (danger color), background #FFF1F2.

STATE: Loading (before first section streams)
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STARTING DEEP DIVE: AlexNet (2012)                          │
│                                                              │
│  ○ Assembling paper data...          (complete)              │
│  ● Sending to analysis pipeline...   (in progress)          │
│  ○ Section 1: Methodology            (pending)              │
│  ○ Section 2: Contributions          (pending)              │
│  ○ Section 3: Structural Position    (pending)              │
│  ○ Section 4: Influence Map          (pending)              │
│  ○ Section 5: Vulnerability          (pending)              │
│  ○ Section 6: Related Work           (pending)              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
Steps update as each section_start event arrives. ● = active, ○ = pending,
filled ● = complete. Using dot matrix circles, no emojis.

TRANSITIONS:
Idle -> Loading: System message fades in, 200ms.
Loading -> Streaming: First section_start event triggers section 1 reveal.
Section to section: Section heading fades in, prose streams word by word.
Streaming -> Complete: All sections rendered, quick action chips fade in, 300ms stagger.
"Show vulnerable papers" click: graph filter applies, 400ms overlay fade.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User activates deep dive (context menu, info panel button, or /deepdive command)
    |
PaperDeepDive.activate(paperId) [athena-deepdive.js]
    |--- Find node in graphData.nodes by paperId
    |--- Assemble dataPackage: node attributes, incident edges, pruning data
    |--- Show loading indicator card
    |
POST /api/athena/deep-dive [app.py]
    |--- Check llm_cache (key: SHA256(paper_id + graph_id + text_tier))
    |--- Cache hit: replay cached SSE blocks (< 500ms total)
    |--- Cache miss: call GROQ_SMART_MODEL with six-section prompt
    |
Groq streams structured sections
    |--- app.py parses LLM output stream
    |--- Emits section_start SSE event on each new section
    |--- Emits block SSE events for each content block within section
    |--- After all six sections: emits done event, writes to llm_cache
    |
Frontend processes SSE stream [athena-deepdive.js]
    |--- section_start -> update loading step indicator, render section header
    |--- block -> pass to BlockRenderer, append to active section div
    |--- done -> render quick action chips, store in conversation memory
    |
User clicks "Show vulnerable papers"
    |--- Dispatches arivu:filter-apply event with sole_dependents array
    |--- graph.js highlights those nodes

ERROR PATH:
Paper not in graphData.nodes
    |--- Error card: "Paper not found in the loaded graph."
    |--- No backend call made

Groq API failure during streaming
    |--- If mid-stream (some sections complete): render completed sections
    |--- Show partial result notice: "Analysis interrupted after section [N]."
    |--- Retry button to resume from next section (stateful retry)
    |--- If no sections complete: full error card with retry

DATA STORED:
Deep dive result stored in llm_cache (DB): key = SHA256(paper_id + graph_id + text_tier).
Full analysis stored in chat_history as a single entry with paper_id metadata.
Sole dependents list stored in session memory for follow-up queries.

DATA CACHED:
llm_cache: 30-day TTL. Same paper on same graph returns cached result immediately.
Cache invalidation: if graph is rebuilt (new graph_id) or text_tier improves,
cache key changes and fresh analysis is generated.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: Yes

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) exclusively
STAGE: Single six-section generation call, streams each section sequentially

PROMPT (literal text, variables replaced at runtime):
```
You are Athena, a research intelligence system. Produce a six-section deep dive
analysis of the paper described below. Write for an expert researcher.

PAPER: [title] ([year])
Authors: [authors]
Citations: [citation_count]
Text quality: [text_tier_description]  ("Full text available" / "Abstract only" / "Title only")
Cluster: [cluster_name]
PageRank in lineage: [pagerank] (higher = more structurally central)
BFS depth from seed: [depth]
Pruning impact: [pruning_impact]% of lineage depends on this paper's path

INCOMING EDGES ([incoming_count] papers citing this paper):
[List of mutation_type: count pairs]

OUTGOING EDGES ([outgoing_count] papers this paper cites in lineage):
[List of mutation_type: count pairs]

SOLE DEPENDENTS: [sole_dependents_count] papers have no path to the seed
without this paper.

[If text_tier <= 2: FULL TEXT EXCERPT: "[abstract_or_intro_text]"]

Write exactly 6 sections with these headings:
1 - METHODOLOGY: Technical approach and methods. [If full text available: quote
    one specific sentence.] [If no full text: state data limitation explicitly.]
2 - KEY CONTRIBUTIONS: What specific advances over prior work does this paper make?
    Base claims on mutation_type data (adoption = built on, contradiction = challenged).
3 - STRUCTURAL POSITION: What role does this paper play in the lineage architecture?
    Reference the PageRank, depth, and bottleneck status with specific numbers.
4 - INFLUENCE MAP: How did subsequent papers use this work? Group by mutation type.
    Be specific about the groups shown in the edge data.
5 - VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT: What is at risk without this paper? Reference
    sole_dependents_count specifically. What does this mean for the field?
6 - RELATED WORK POSITIONING: How does this paper relate to papers in the same
    cluster? What makes it distinct from its structural neighbors?

Do not fabricate statistics. Use only the numbers provided above.
If data is limited, acknowledge it rather than speculating.
Each section: 80 to 200 words. Total: 600 to 1200 words.
```

EXPECTED OUTPUT FORMAT:
Six sections streaming sequentially. The backend parses the LLM output and emits
SSE events with block types appropriate for each section. Section 3 always includes
a stat_row block with PageRank, BFS depth, and pruning impact computed locally
from graph data (not from LLM output) to ensure accuracy. Section 5 always
includes a vulnerability block with the sole_dependents_count from the data package.

FALLBACK IF LLM FAILS:
Section-level fallback: each section has a template version computed from the
data package without LLM. If a section fails mid-stream, the template version
fills that section. If all sections fail, a full-template analysis renders:
plain data summary without prose narrative. Labeled "Data Summary (LLM unavailable)."

TOKEN BUDGET:
Input: ~1200 tokens (prompt + data package + text excerpt)
Output: ~900 tokens (six sections)
Total per deep dive: ~2100 tokens
Cache saves the full output token cost on repeat access.
70b model used exclusively — no downgrade to 8b for this feature.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Trigger                           | Element                   | Action                                                   |
|-----------------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------|
| Right-click node -> Deep Dive     | Graph context menu        | PaperDeepDive.activate(paperId)                         |
| Info panel Deep Dive button       | Node info panel           | Same as above                                           |
| /deepdive [paper name] command    | athena-engine.js          | Entity resolve then PaperDeepDive.activate()            |
| "deep dive on X" typed            | athena-engine.js          | Same as /deepdive                                       |
| Section nav item clicked          | SectionNav anchor         | Smooth scroll to that section within message            |
| "Show vulnerable papers" button   | VulnerabilityBlock        | arivu:filter-apply with sole_dependents array           |
| "Compare with VGGNet" chip        | QuickActionChip           | Passes both paper IDs to ComparisonMode.activate()      |
| "Show adoption papers" chip       | QuickActionChip           | arivu:filter-apply filtering to adoption-edge children  |
| "Export analysis" chip            | QuickActionChip           | Copies plain text deep dive to clipboard or opens export|
| Scroll within message             | IntersectionObserver      | Updates active section in sticky nav bar                |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Loading step indicator: each step dot transitions ○ -> ● as section_start arrives, 150ms
- Section header reveal: fade in from opacity 0, translateY 4px, 200ms ease-out
- Prose streaming: same word-by-word streaming as standard Athena responses
- Stat row reveal: three cards animate in with 60ms stagger, translateY 8px to 0
- Quote block: left border grows from height 0 to full height, 300ms ease-out
- Influence map rows: each row slides in from left, 40ms stagger
- Vulnerability block: border pulses once (opacity 1.0 -> 0.6 -> 1.0) on first render, 600ms
- Quick action chips: stagger fade-in from bottom, 80ms per chip, after done event

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE (Athena uses no audio)

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Section nav: role="navigation", aria-label="Deep dive sections"
- Each nav item: role="link", aria-current="true" for active section
- Loading steps: role="list" with aria-live="polite" for step updates
- Vulnerability block: role="region", aria-label="Vulnerability assessment"
- "Show vulnerable papers" button: aria-haspopup is false, triggers graph filter
- All stat cards: aria-label="[metric name]: [value]"
- Quote block: blockquote element with cite attribute when source URL available
- Message scrollable container: tabindex="0", keyboard-scrollable

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

EDGE CASE 1: Paper with text_tier = 4 (title only)
Scenario: User requests deep dive on a paper that has only title data and no
abstract or methods text. The methodology section cannot be grounded in actual text.
Expected behavior: Section 1 (Methodology) explicitly states: "Only title-level
data is available for this paper. Methodology analysis cannot be grounded in
the paper's actual text. The following is inferred from citation pattern analysis
only." The LLM is instructed not to fabricate methods claims. The section is
shorter than others but still analyzes what the citation patterns suggest.
Implementation: data_package includes text_tier field. The LLM prompt includes
the text_tier_description string. The backend flags low-text-quality papers with
a data_quality_notice block inserted before section 1.

EDGE CASE 2: Paper with zero outgoing edges in the graph
Scenario: Deep diving on the seed paper itself or a leaf node with no edges into
the lineage.
Expected behavior: Section 4 (Influence Map) states "This paper has no citation
children in the loaded lineage. It appears as a leaf node in this graph." Section
5 (Vulnerability) shows sole_dependents_count = 0. Both are handled gracefully
without zero-division errors.
Implementation: _assembleDataPackage() returns empty arrays for incoming/outgoing
edges when none exist. The LLM prompt includes empty edge arrays. The vulnerability
section renders a zero-count block with an explanatory note.

EDGE CASE 3: Paper is flagged as retracted
Scenario: User requests deep dive on a paper in the retraction_watch table.
Expected behavior: A prominent warning block appears at the TOP of the deep dive,
before section 1: "Warning: This paper has been retracted. Analysis proceeds but
results should be interpreted with this in mind." The warning uses danger color
styling (red border). The LLM is informed of the retraction in the prompt.
Implementation: _assembleDataPackage() checks node.is_retracted. If true, a
retraction_warning block is prepended. The LLM prompt includes "NOTE: This paper
has been retracted. Acknowledge this prominently in your analysis."

EDGE CASE 4: User requests deep dive on a paper not in the graph
Scenario: User types "/deepdive GPT-4" but the current graph is a ResNet lineage
that does not contain GPT-4.
Expected behavior: Entity resolution fails. Athena responds: "GPT-4 is not in
this lineage. Deep dive works on papers within the loaded graph. Did you mean one
of these?" with up to three fuzzy-matched candidates from graphData.nodes.
Implementation: PaperDeepDive.activate() calls resolveEntities(name, graphData.nodes).
If no match above the fuzzy threshold, returns a disambiguation card with closest
matches by Levenshtein distance.

EDGE CASE 5: Very highly connected paper (200+ incoming edges)
Scenario: Deep diving on the seed paper or a highly cited hub with 200 incoming edges.
The influence map section would list hundreds of papers.
Expected behavior: Influence map groups edges by mutation type and shows top 5
groups with counts. Individual paper listings are capped at 10 per group. A
summary row says "and [N] more adoption papers." The total influence map section
stays within 300 words.
Implementation: _assembleDataPackage() includes incoming_edges limited to top 50
by confidence tier. The LLM prompt says "incoming_count is [total] but only top
[N] shown." The influence map block renderer shows grouped data, not individual papers.

EDGE CASE 6: LLM streams out of order or skips a section
Scenario: The 70b LLM produces sections out of numeric order or omits section 4.
Expected behavior: The backend detects section headers in the LLM output stream
and routes blocks to the correct section regardless of order. If a section is
missing, a placeholder renders: "Section [N] — Analysis not available for this
paper." The other five sections render normally.
Implementation: app.py parses the LLM stream for section marker patterns
(e.g., "1 - METHODOLOGY", "METHODOLOGY:", "## METHODOLOGY"). The section router
maps content to the correct section div. Missing sections detected at done event
time and filled with placeholder blocks.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON (must be built first):
- Feature #001 (Context-Aware Responses): graphData with full node attributes
  (pagerank, is_bottleneck, pruning_impact, text_tier, cluster) must be available.
  These are populated by the StaticContextBuilder from the graph JSON.
- Feature #002 (Rich Response Formatting): Deep dive uses stat_row, quote, prose,
  heading, and the new vulnerability block type. Block renderer must exist.
- Feature #004 (Discuss Integration): The graph context menu "Deep Dive" option
  and the node info panel "Deep Dive" button are built as extensions of the
  infrastructure created by Discuss Integration.
- Feature #008 (Pathfinder Integration): The deep dive uses the same 70b LLM
  pipeline routing established by Pathfinder Integration. Energy consumption
  is tracked the same way (one deep dive = one Pathfinder energy unit).
- Feature #037 (Streaming Text): The six-section response streams via SSE.

ENABLES (can be built after this):
- Feature #032 (Paper Recommendation Engine): Uses deep dive structural data
  (cluster membership, PageRank, influence pattern) as features for recommendation
  scoring. Without deep dive data, recommendations are shallower.

ENHANCES (improves but does not require):
- Feature #009 (Proactive Suggestions): Proactive suggestions can offer "Deep dive
  on [bottleneck paper]" when a bottleneck node is clicked.
- Feature #014 (Comparison Mode): "Compare with [X]" chip at the end of deep dive
  directly feeds into Comparison Mode for a two-paper comparison.

CONFLICTS WITH:
- NONE. Deep dive is an additive mode that does not take control of graph overlays
  or block other Athena functions. Multiple deep dives can coexist in conversation
  history without conflict.

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES:
- Groq API (llama-3.3-70b-versatile): Required for prose generation. Template
  fallback available but significantly less useful. No 8b model substitution.
- graphData.precomputed_pruning[paperId]: Used for sole_dependents computation.
  If missing (paper not precomputed), sole_dependents_count = 0 and vulnerability
  section notes "Vulnerability data not precomputed for this paper."
- retraction_watch table: Checked for is_retracted status via node.is_retracted
  field (populated from retraction_watch during graph build in Phase 5).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ Right-click context menu on graph node includes "Deep Dive with Athena" option

□ Six section_start SSE events emitted, one per section

□ Section 3 stat_row uses locally computed values (not LLM output)
□ Section 5 vulnerability block uses sole_dependents_count from data package
□ Section nav sticky bar renders, active section updates on scroll
□ "Show vulnerable papers" button dispatches arivu:filter-apply event
□ LLM cache hit serves cached result in < 500ms
□ Retracted paper: warning block prepended before section 1
□ Template fallback activates for each section if that section's LLM output fails
□ Result stored in llm_cache with correct 30-day TTL

VISUAL:
□ Section headers use 11px monospace uppercase style
□ Quote blocks use 3px blue left border and light blue background
□ Vulnerability block uses red border styling
□ Stat row has three inline cards with PageRank, BFS depth, pruning impact
□ Loading step indicator uses dot matrix circles (○ / ●), no emojis
□ Quick action chips appear after done event with stagger animation

PERFORMANCE:
□ Data package assembly completes in < 50ms
□ First section starts streaming within 2 seconds of activation
□ All six sections complete within 15 seconds
□ Cache hit response completes within 500ms

ACCESSIBILITY:

□ Active nav item has aria-current="true"
□ Loading steps container has aria-live="polite"
□ All stat cards have aria-label with metric name and value

EDGE CASES:
□ text_tier=4 paper: data quality notice shown, no fabricated methodology claims
□ Zero outgoing edges: influence map shows "no citation children" message
□ Retracted paper: warning block prominent, LLM informed

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed — all findings addressed
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed — all findings addressed

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-deepdive.js (PaperDeepDive class, _assembleDataPackage, _streamSections),
   athena-blocks.js (renderDeepDiveHeader, renderSectionNav, renderInfluenceMap,
   renderVulnerabilityBlock),
   athena-engine.js (deep dive command detection and routing),
   app.py (POST /api/athena/deep-dive route, LLM call, section parsing, cache logic)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Right-click AlexNet on a 601-paper graph. Verify context menu shows Deep Dive
      option, activation proceeds, all six sections render with correct headings.
   b. Verify section 3 stat row shows values matching graphData node attributes
      (not values from LLM output — these must come from local computation).
   c. Click "Show vulnerable papers" button. Verify graph applies filter and
      those specific nodes are highlighted.
   d. Request deep dive on a retracted paper. Verify warning block is the first
      element, before section 1 content.
   e. Request deep dive on a paper with text_tier=4. Verify section 1 states
      data limitation explicitly and does not make specific methodology claims.
   f. Request deep dive on same paper twice. Verify second request returns from
      cache (< 500ms, no new Groq call in network tab).
   g. Type /deepdive on a paper name not in the graph. Verify disambiguation
      card shows closest matches, no backend call is made.
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. _assembleDataPackage() throws if node.pruning_impact is undefined (use || 0)
   b. Section nav IntersectionObserver not disconnected on message removal (memory leak)
   c. LLM outputs "## METHODOLOGY" but parser expects "1 - METHODOLOGY" format
   d. vulnerability block rendering when sole_dependents_count is 0 (empty state)
5. VERIFY no regression in: #001 (context), #004 (discuss integration), #008 (pathfinder)
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why none
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below

================================================================================
FEATURE #032: PAPER RECOMMENDATION ENGINE
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           F
ESTIMATED HOURS: 18
OVERLAPS WITH:   #009 (Proactive Suggestions), #013 (Research Question Generator)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #006 (Conversation Memory),
                 #009 (Proactive Suggestions), #016 (Gap Finder Integration)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Paper Recommendation Engine gives Athena the ability to surface research
papers that are NOT already in the current lineage graph but that the user
should know about. This transforms Athena from a tool that only explains what
is already visible into one that actively extends the user's knowledge horizon.
The feature operates at the intersection of semantic similarity (via the
existing gap_finder.py pgvector infrastructure), user exploration history
(tracked across the session), and structural analysis of the current graph.

The core insight is that a user exploring the attention mechanism lineage has
already revealed their research interests through every click, hover, and query.
Athena watches what papers they dwell on, what questions they ask, what threads
they pull, and builds a latent interest profile. When the user asks for
recommendations or when Athena detects a gap in the visible lineage, it queries
the paper_embeddings table for semantically adjacent papers using pgvector cosine
similarity. Results are filtered to exclude papers already in the graph,
re-ranked by recency and citation velocity, and returned with tailored
justifications.

Each recommendation is presented in a dedicated block format (athena-blocks.js
recommendation card) with three elements: the paper title and authors, a
one-sentence justification anchored to the user's observed interest ("You spent
4 minutes on the multi-head attention cluster and this paper extends that work
into cross-modal settings"), and a connection indicator showing which node in
the current graph the recommendation is most semantically adjacent to. Users
can click to load the recommended paper as a new graph or add it to a watch
list for later.

What makes this feature distinct from generic recommendation systems is its
grounding in the visible graph structure. Recommendations are not pulled from
a generic popularity list. They are gap-fillers. If the lineage shows strong
coverage of transformer architecture papers through 2020 but sparse coverage
after that, Athena notices and surfaces post-2020 work. If the graph has deep
coverage of English NLP but the user's query history suggests cross-lingual
interest, Athena surfaces multilingual papers not yet in the graph. The system
understands not just what the user is looking at but what is missing from
what they are looking at.

The feature connects to #009 (Proactive Suggestions) by triggering
recommendation offers after specific user behaviors: dwelling on a leaf node
for more than 30 seconds, asking three questions about the same cluster, or
requesting a gap analysis. It connects to #016 (Gap Finder Integration) by
using the same pgvector search infrastructure rather than building a second
search path.

Technically, the backend route accepts the current graph ID, the user's session
exploration summary, and optional explicit interest signals (paper IDs the user
flagged). It calls gap_finder.py GapFinder.find_gaps() with the seed embeddings
derived from papers the user dwelt on longest, filters results by a minimum
similarity threshold of 0.60 (higher than the gap finder default to ensure
relevance), and returns the top 3 candidates with structured justification
metadata generated by the GROQ_FAST_MODEL.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User types "What should I read next?" or "Are there related papers I'm missing?"
or "Recommend something." Alternatively, Athena proactively offers
recommendations after the user has dwelt on leaf nodes for a cumulative 60
seconds without finding follow-up papers in the graph.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User has been exploring the attention mechanism lineage for 8 minutes. They
   have clicked through the BERT cluster, spent time on the multi-head attention
   paper, and asked two questions about cross-attention variants. Athena has been
   tracking this exploration silently.

2. User types: "Are there papers I should read that aren't in this lineage?"
   Athena responds immediately with a thinking indicator: "Scanning 47 adjacent
   research threads..." The indicator pulses for 2-3 seconds while the backend
   queries pgvector.

3. Athena returns a recommendation block. Three papers appear in a styled card:

   [Paper #1] "FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention"
   Dao et al., 2022 -- 3,847 citations
   Why: You explored multi-head attention mechanics for 4 minutes. This paper
   optimizes the exact operation you were studying. Not in your lineage.
   Adjacent to: "Attention Is All You Need" (92% similarity)

   [Paper #2] "Longformer: The Long-Document Transformer"
   Beltagy et al., 2020 -- 2,103 citations
   Why: Your questions about scalability match this paper's core contribution.
   Not in your lineage.
   Adjacent to: "BERT" (87% similarity)

   [Paper #3] "Cross-Attention in Vision Transformers"
   Chen et al., 2021 -- 891 citations
   Why: Three of your queries mentioned cross-modal attention.
   Not in your lineage.
   Adjacent to: Multi-head attention cluster (84% similarity)

4. Each card has two action buttons: "Load Graph" (builds the selected paper's
   lineage as a new graph) and "Add to Watchlist" (saves for later without
   navigating away).

5. User clicks "Load Graph" on Paper #1. A confirmation dialog appears: "This
   will replace your current graph. Your exploration history will be saved." User
   confirms. The graph transitions to FlashAttention's lineage.

AFTER COMPLETION:
The recommendation is logged to action_log with the papers suggested and which
(if any) the user acted on. This feedback loop improves future recommendation
relevance over longer sessions. The watchlist (Phase H feature) accumulates
papers the user saved without navigating away.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-recommend.js: Recommendation block renderer. Handles
  recommendation request, card layout with paper metadata, action buttons
  (Load Graph, Add to Watchlist), loading state, and empty-state messaging.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Updated to include recommendation card block type.
  The recommendation card is a new block variant alongside existing analysis
  and comparison blocks.
- app.py: New route POST /api/athena/recommend. Accepts graph_id, session_id,
  interest_signals (list of paper_ids user dwelt on). Returns ranked
  recommendation list with justification metadata.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
The /api/athena/recommend route:

  1. Load session exploration summary from action_log (papers viewed > 20s,
     questions asked, clusters visited).
  2. Derive interest embeddings: fetch paper_embeddings for the top-5 most-
     dwelt-upon papers. Average their vectors to form a composite interest
     vector (numpy mean over 384-dim arrays).
  3. Call GapFinder.find_gaps(seed_embedding, exclude_ids=graph_paper_ids,
     threshold=0.60, limit=10) to get candidate papers from DB.
  4. Re-rank candidates: multiply similarity score by log(citation_count + 1)
     and recency bonus (papers from last 3 years get 1.15x multiplier).
  5. Take top 3. For each, call GROQ_FAST_MODEL with a compact prompt to
     generate a one-sentence personalized justification anchored to the user's
     observed behavior.
  6. Return structured JSON: list of {paper_id, title, authors, year,
     citation_count, similarity_score, adjacent_to_paper_id,
     adjacent_to_title, justification}.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
- Interest vector derivation: numpy.mean(embeddings_array, axis=0)
- pgvector query: GapFinder.find_gaps() from backend/gap_finder.py
- Justification generation: llm_client.py ArivuLLMClient with caching
  (cache key includes paper_id + session_interest_hash to avoid redundant calls)
- Exploration tracking: action_log entries with action_type='paper_dwell',
  action_data={paper_id, dwell_ms}

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/recommend
  Request:  { graph_id: str, session_id: str, interest_signals: [str] }
  Response: { recommendations: [ { paper_id, title, authors, year,
              citation_count, similarity_score, adjacent_to_paper_id,
              adjacent_to_title, justification } ], generated_at: int }
  Errors:   422 if graph_id missing, 503 if NLP worker unavailable,
            200 with empty list if no papers meet 0.60 threshold

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: IDLE
  Recommendation block not visible. No indicator in chat.

STATE: LOADING
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ [ATHENA]                                                    │
  │ Scanning adjacent research threads...                       │
  │ ● ○ ○  (pulsing dot animation, 400ms interval)              │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: RESULTS (3 recommendation cards)
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ [ATHENA] Based on your exploration, 3 papers you should     │
  │ read that are not in this lineage:                          │
  │                                                             │
  │ ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │ │ 01  FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022)  3,847 cites   │   │
  │ │     Adjacent to: Attention Is All You Need (92%)      │   │
  │ │     "You studied multi-head attention for 4 min..."   │   │
  │ │     [Load Graph]  [Add to Watchlist]                  │   │
  │ └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │ ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │ │ 02  Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020)  2,103 cites   │   │
  │ │     Adjacent to: BERT (87%)                           │   │
  │ │     "Your scalability questions match this..."        │   │
  │ │     [Load Graph]  [Add to Watchlist]                  │   │
  │ └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │ ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │ │ 03  Cross-Attention in Vision (Chen et al., 2021)     │   │
  │ │     Adjacent to: Multi-head cluster (84%)             │   │
  │ │     "Three queries mentioned cross-modal..."          │   │
  │ │     [Load Graph]  [Add to Watchlist]                  │   │
  │ └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: EMPTY
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ [ATHENA] This lineage appears comprehensive within the      │
  │ database. No strongly adjacent papers found outside it      │
  │ with similarity > 60%. Try exploring a subfield cluster     │
  │ for more targeted recommendations.                          │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
  IDLE -> LOADING: User sends recommendation request. Instant.
  LOADING -> RESULTS: pgvector query + LLM completes (2-5s). Cards fade in
    staggered: card 1 at 0ms, card 2 at 150ms, card 3 at 300ms.
  LOADING -> EMPTY: Query returns < 1 result above threshold.
  RESULTS -> [Graph loads]: User clicks "Load Graph". Confirmation dialog.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User request -> athena-recommend.js -> POST /api/athena/recommend
  -> action_log query (dwell times, cluster visits) in db.py
  -> paper_embeddings query for top-5 dwelt papers
  -> numpy.mean() composite interest vector
  -> GapFinder.find_gaps() (pgvector cosine similarity search)
  -> Re-ranking (citation count + recency multiplier)
  -> ArivuLLMClient.generate() x3 (one per recommendation, cached)
  -> JSON response
  -> athena-recommend.js card renderer
  -> athena-blocks.js recommendation block displayed in chat

ERROR PATH:
  - NLP worker unavailable: return 503, Athena displays "Recommendation
    requires the NLP worker to be online. Try again in a moment."
  - No dwell history: use seed paper embedding as fallback interest vector.
  - All results filtered (already in graph): return empty list, show
    "This lineage is comprehensive" message.
  - LLM quota exceeded: return recommendations without justification text;
    display "Based on semantic similarity" as fallback justification.

DATA STORED:
  - Recommendations offered: logged to action_log
    (action_type='recommendation_offered', action_data={paper_ids, timestamp})
  - User actions on cards: logged to action_log
    (action_type='recommendation_acted', action_data={paper_id, action_type})

DATA CACHED:
  - LLM justifications: llm_cache (30-day TTL)
    Key: SHA256("recommend:" + paper_id + ":" + interest_hash)
  - pgvector results: NOT cached (interest vectors change with exploration)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL:    config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant)
STAGE:    Stage 2 equivalent (classification/generation, not graph building)
PROMPT:
  System: "You are Athena, a research intelligence guide. Generate a single
  sentence (max 20 words) explaining why a researcher who showed interest in
  [interest_summary] should read [paper_title] by [authors]. Be specific and
  direct. No preamble."
  User:   "Interest: {interest_summary}. Paper: {title} ({year}). Abstract
  excerpt: {abstract_150_chars}. Adjacent to user's graph via: {adjacent_title}
  ({similarity_pct}% similarity). Write the justification."
OUTPUT:   Single sentence, max 20 words. Example: "You explored multi-head
  attention mechanics for 4 minutes; this paper optimizes that exact operation."
FALLBACK: If LLM call fails or quota exceeded, use template:
  "Semantically adjacent to {adjacent_title} at {similarity_pct}% similarity."
TOKEN ESTIMATE: ~200 tokens per justification. 3 calls per recommendation
  request. Total: ~600 tokens per user request. Cached after first generation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Behavior              | Trigger                    | Response                       |
|-----------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Load Graph button     | Click                      | Confirmation dialog + graph load|
| Add to Watchlist      | Click                      | Toast "Saved to watchlist"      |
| Card hover            | Mouse over                 | Shadow lift (2px), bg lighten   |
| Adjacent node link    | Click adjacent paper name  | Graph pans to that node         |
| Refresh recommendations | Click "Refresh" in block | New request with updated context|

ANIMATION DETAILS:
  Card entry: opacity 0 -> 1, translateY(8px) -> 0, duration 300ms, ease-out.
  Stagger: 150ms between each of the 3 cards.
  Loading dots: three dots, each 400ms pulse, offset by 133ms each.
  "Load Graph" confirmation: modal slides up from bottom (200ms, ease-out).

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
  Cards use role="article" with aria-label="Recommendation [N]: [title]".
  "Load Graph" button has aria-label="Load [title] as new graph".
  "Add to Watchlist" has aria-label="Add [title] to watchlist".
  Loading state announces "Finding recommendations" to screen readers.
  Keyboard: Tab navigates cards, Enter activates focused button.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: No papers in database beyond current graph
   Expected: Empty state shown. "This lineage appears comprehensive..."
   Implementation: Return empty recommendations array, frontend shows
   empty state block instead of card list.

2. Scenario: User has zero dwell history (loaded graph but never clicked)
   Expected: Fallback to seed paper embedding as interest vector.
   Implementation: interest_signals defaults to [seed_paper_id] if
   action_log shows no dwell events for the session.

3. Scenario: NLP worker offline during recommendation request
   Expected: Athena shows "Recommendations require NLP worker." No crash.
   Implementation: Route returns 503 with error_code NLP_WORKER_UNAVAILABLE.
   Frontend displays friendly error message in place of loading indicator.

4. Scenario: User requests recommendations on a precomputed gallery graph
   Expected: Works normally. Precomputed graphs have paper_ids in the DB.
   Implementation: graph_id resolves to paper list from graphs table.
   Exclusion filter built from node list in the precomputed graph JSON.

5. Scenario: All top recommendations are retracted papers
   Expected: Retracted papers are marked with a retraction badge but
   still shown. User is informed before they can load the graph.
   Implementation: Cross-reference recommendation paper_ids against
   retraction_watch table. Add is_retracted flag to response. Frontend
   shows "[RETRACTED]" badge in red on the card.

6. Scenario: User clicks "Refresh" 10 times in rapid succession
   Expected: Rate limiter prevents hammering. After 3 requests in 60s,
   button is disabled for 30 seconds with countdown.
   Implementation: Client-side rate limit on the refresh button.
   Backend also rate-limited via ArivuRateLimiter.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON:
  #001 -- Context-Aware Responses (graph context awareness)
  #006 -- Conversation Memory (exploration history access)
  #009 -- Proactive Suggestions (trigger mechanism for proactive offers)
  #016 -- Gap Finder Integration (pgvector search infrastructure)

ENABLES:
  Phase H watchlist feature (papers saved via "Add to Watchlist")

ENHANCES:
  #013 -- Research Question Generator (questions about recommended papers)
  #009 -- Proactive Suggestions (recommendations as a suggestion type)

CONFLICTS:
  NONE

EXTERNAL:
  Requires paper_embeddings table to be populated (Phase 2+).
  Requires GapFinder from backend/gap_finder.py (Phase 3+).
  Requires ArivuLLMClient from backend/llm_client.py (Phase 3+).
  pgvector extension and cosine similarity index must be active.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] POST /api/athena/recommend returns 3 ranked recommendations
[] Recommendations exclude papers already in current graph
[] Each recommendation includes title, authors, year, citation count
[] Each recommendation includes adjacent_to_paper_id and similarity score
[] Each recommendation includes LLM-generated justification sentence
[] "Load Graph" button triggers graph navigation flow
[] "Add to Watchlist" button shows success toast
[] Empty state shown when no papers exceed 0.60 threshold
[] Fallback to seed paper embedding when no dwell history exists
[] Retracted papers marked with badge in recommendation cards

VISUAL:

[] Cards stagger in 150ms apart (300ms each, ease-out)
[] Recommendation cards match dot matrix aesthetic (not rounded/modern)
[] Adjacent paper link is clickable (graph pans to node)
[] Empty state message is informative and non-alarming

PERFORMANCE:
[] Total recommendation request completes within 5 seconds (including LLM)
[] LLM justifications cached -- repeat requests for same paper instantaneous
[] pgvector query completes within 500ms for a 100k-embedding database
[] Card animations run at 60fps (no layout thrash)

ACCESSIBILITY:
[] role="article" on each recommendation card
[] All buttons have descriptive aria-label attributes
[] Loading state announced via aria-live region
[] Keyboard navigation works (Tab, Enter)
[] Retraction badge conveyed via text, not color alone

EDGE CASES:

[] Zero dwell history fallback works
[] NLP worker offline returns friendly error (not 500)
[] Retracted papers flagged in results
[] Client-side rate limit on Refresh button

VERIFICATION:

[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round -- ZERO issues

[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-recommend.js (card renderer, request handler, loading state),
   athena-blocks.js (recommendation block type), app.py (/api/athena/recommend
   route), backend/gap_finder.py (GapFinder.find_gaps signature and return
   shape), backend/llm_client.py (caching behavior)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Fresh session with no dwell history -- verify seed paper fallback
   b. Session with 3 dwelt papers -- verify composite embedding is mean
   c. All results already in graph -- verify empty state renders
   d. NLP worker offline -- verify 503 handled, no crash
   e. LLM quota exceeded -- verify fallback justification appears
   f. Click "Refresh" 4 times in 30s -- verify rate limit activates
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Graph exclusion filter misses papers (recommendations already in graph)
   b. LLM cache key collision (same paper, different interest context)
   c. Retraction check skipped when LLM call fails
   d. "Load Graph" triggers without confirmation dialog
5. VERIFY no regression in: gap_finder.py behavior, llm_client caching
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below

================================================================================
FEATURE #033: LIVE ANNOTATION PAD
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 22
OVERLAPS WITH:   #012 (AI Annotations on Graph), #021 (Export Chat as Report)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #012 (AI Annotations on Graph), #021 (Export Chat as Report),
                 #006 (Conversation Memory)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Live Annotation Pad is a floating, semi-transparent notepad that users can
open alongside the Athena chat and the citation graph. It gives researchers the
ability to capture free-form thoughts, observations, and hypotheses in real time
while they explore the lineage without context-switching to an external tool.
Unlike the Athena chat (which is a back-and-forth dialogue), the annotation pad
is a personal scratchpad: unstructured, non-conversational, and entirely user-
controlled. It is the digital equivalent of a researcher's notebook sitting next
to their monitor.

The notepad floats over the graph panel at a configurable position. It is semi-
transparent (80% opacity) by default so the underlying graph remains legible
through it. The user can resize it (from 200x150 to 600x400 pixels), drag it
to any corner, and toggle it open or closed via a keyboard shortcut (Ctrl+Shift+N
or a button in the Athena toolbar). When closed, it collapses to a small tab
on the edge of the graph panel showing the note count.

Notes are created as individual blocks within the pad. Each note block is linked
to whatever is in the current graph viewport at the time of creation: the
visible paper cluster, the selected node, or the current zoom level. This
linking is automatic and invisible to the user -- they just type and click
"Add." When they later hover near the annotated graph area, the note icon
appears as a floating label on the graph, indicating that a note exists for
that area. Clicking the icon jumps to that note in the pad.

Athena can participate in the annotation workflow in two ways. First, the user
can right-click any note and select "Ask Athena about this" to send the note
text as a chat message with graph context pre-loaded. Second, Athena can
optionally auto-suggest an annotation when it detects the user has lingered
on a specific paper cluster for more than 45 seconds without asking a question.
The suggestion appears as a subtle prompt below the last chat message: "Want
to make a note about the attention cluster? [Add note]." The auto-suggestion
is dismissible and defaults to off (enabled via settings).

Notes persist in the browser via localStorage (for immediate use) and are
optionally synced to the database as part of the session state. This is a
Phase H feature, so database persistence is via the sessions.graph_memory
JSONB column (already exists in the schema). Notes are part of the
graph_memory payload under the key "annotations".

The link between graph regions and notes is implemented via viewport hash:
a compact string encoding the visible node IDs and current zoom transform.
When the user creates a note, the current viewport hash is stored. When the
user hovers over a graph region, the current viewport hash is computed and
compared against stored note hashes. Matches within a configurable similarity
threshold (>= 70% node overlap) trigger the floating note indicator.

The export integration (#021) treats annotation pad notes as a first-class
source of user-generated content. When exporting as a report, the notes appear
as a "Researcher Notes" appendix section.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User opens the annotation pad via Ctrl+Shift+N, or by clicking the notepad
icon in the Athena panel toolbar. Or Athena offers a note suggestion after
extended cluster dwell time.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User has been studying the ResNet cluster for several minutes. They notice
   an interesting pattern: all the post-2015 residual connection papers cite
   the same intermediate paper. They want to record this observation.

2. User presses Ctrl+Shift+N. A semi-transparent floating pad appears over
   the bottom-right of the graph. It shows: "ANNOTATION PAD [0 notes] [x]"
   at the top. Below is an empty text area with placeholder: "Write an
   observation about what you're seeing..."

3. User types: "All post-2015 ResNet variants cite 'Identity Mappings' paper
   as the critical bridge. This wasn't visible in the main lineage view."
   They click "Add Note." The note appears as a numbered block below the
   input area.

4. The note block shows:
   [01] All post-2015 ResNet variants cite 'Identity Mappings' paper...
        Linked to: ResNet cluster (7 nodes visible) | 14:32:07
        [Ask Athena] [Delete]

5. User navigates away to explore the attention cluster. The annotation pad
   collapses to a tab showing "1 note." Later they return to the ResNet area.
   As they zoom into that region, a small note icon (pencil) appears near
   the Identity Mappings node. Hovering shows a tooltip: "You noted: All
   post-2015 ResNet variants..."

6. User clicks "Ask Athena" on the note. Athena receives a prefilled message:
   "I noted: 'All post-2015 ResNet variants cite Identity Mappings as the
   critical bridge.' Is this structurally significant?" Athena responds with
   a graph-aware analysis.

7. On export (#021), the annotation appears in a "Researcher Notes" section
   of the generated report with timestamp and graph context preserved.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Notes are saved to localStorage immediately. On session persist events (graph
save, export, page unload), notes are written to sessions.graph_memory under
the "annotations" key. Notes survive page refreshes within the same session.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-notepad.js: Core annotation pad module. Handles pad open/
  close/resize/drag. Note creation, deletion, listing. Viewport hash computation.
  Graph overlay indicator rendering. LocalStorage persistence. Session sync.
- static/js/graph.js: Updated to render note indicators on the graph canvas
  (floating SVG pencil icons near annotated regions). Viewport hash export API.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Updated with annotation pad styles: floating
  container, semi-transparent background, resize handle, note block layout,
  graph overlay indicator styles.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaNotePad {
    constructor(graphRef) {
      this.notes = [];                  // { id, text, viewportHash, timestamp }
      this.graphRef = graphRef;         // ArivuGraph instance for viewport hash
      this.position = { x: 'right', y: 'bottom' };
      this.size = { w: 320, h: 240 };
      this.isOpen = false;
    }

    computeViewportHash() {
      // Get visible node IDs from graph, encode as sorted comma-joined string
      // Hash with a 16-char hex digest for compact storage
      const visibleNodes = this.graphRef.getVisibleNodeIds();
      const sorted = [...visibleNodes].sort().join(',');
      return simpleHash(sorted); // 16-char hex
    }

    addNote(text) {
      const note = {
        id: crypto.randomUUID(),
        text,
        viewportHash: this.computeViewportHash(),
        timestamp: Date.now()
      };
      this.notes.push(note);
      this.persist();
      this.graphRef.renderNoteIndicators(this.notes);
    }

    matchNoteToViewport(hash, threshold = 0.70) {
      // Compare current hash against stored hashes by node overlap ratio
      // Return notes whose stored hash overlaps >= threshold with current
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  No new backend route required. Notes persist via existing session update
  mechanism: PUT /api/session/memory updates sessions.graph_memory JSONB.
  The frontend calls this route with the annotations array on session persist
  events (graph save, page unload via navigator.sendBeacon).

  Note sync payload (merged into graph_memory):
  { "annotations": [ { id, text, viewportHash, timestamp }, ... ] }

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
- Drag/resize: CSS resize property + mousedown/mousemove listeners
- Semi-transparency: backdrop-filter: blur(2px); background: rgba(30,41,59,0.85)
- Graph overlay icons: SVG <g> elements positioned via D3 projection of the
  annotated node's coordinates. ArivuGraph.renderNoteIndicators() adds these.
- LocalStorage key: "arivu_annotations_{session_id}"
- Viewport hash similarity: Jaccard index on visible node sets

API CONTRACTS:
  Note sync via: PUT /api/session/memory
  Request: { session_id: str, updates: { annotations: [...] } }
  (Existing endpoint from backend/utils.py update_graph_memory)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: CLOSED (tab visible)
  ┌──────┐
  │ [/] 1│  <- collapsed tab on right edge of graph panel
  └──────┘
  Shows pencil icon and note count.

STATE: OPEN (floating pad)
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ANNOTATION PAD      2 notes  [ x ] │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │ │ Write an observation...         │ │
  │ └─────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │ [Add Note]                          │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ [01] All post-2015 ResNet cite...   │
  │      ResNet cluster | 14:32:07      │
  │      [Ask Athena]  [Delete]         │
  ├──── ────────────────────────────────┤
  │ [02] Attention papers cluster...    │
  │      Attention cluster | 14:45:22   │
  │      [Ask Athena]  [Delete]         │
  └─────────────────────────────────── ┘
                                    [=] <- resize handle

GRAPH OVERLAY (note indicator):
  Graph canvas shows small pencil icon (16x16px) near annotated region:
    ┌──────────────┐
    │     [/]      │  <- SVG pencil icon, gold color (#D4A843)
    │   Identity   │     appears on hover of annotated region
    │   Mappings   │
    └──────────────┘

STATE: ATHENA AUTO-SUGGESTION
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ [ATHENA] You have been exploring the attention cluster for  │
  │ 45 seconds. Want to add a note? [Add Note] [Dismiss]        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
  CLOSED -> OPEN: Pad slides in from right edge (250ms, ease-out).
  OPEN -> CLOSED: Pad slides out to right edge (200ms, ease-in).
  Note added: New note block fades in (200ms) below input area.
  Graph indicator: Appears on viewport match (100ms fade-in).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User types note -> athena-notepad.js addNote()
  -> computeViewportHash() (reads visible node IDs from graph.js)
  -> Note stored in this.notes[]
  -> persist() -> localStorage["arivu_annotations_{session_id}"]
  -> graphRef.renderNoteIndicators() -> SVG pencil icons on graph

Session persist events -> PUT /api/session/memory
  -> update_graph_memory(session_id, {annotations: [...]}) in db.py
  -> sessions.graph_memory JSONB updated

On page load:
  -> Load from localStorage first (instant)
  -> Verify against DB via GET /api/session (if session active)
  -> Merge, deduplicating by note ID

ERROR PATH:
  - LocalStorage full: Show toast "Note storage full. Export your notes
    to free space." Notes not lost (already in DB from last sync).
  - DB sync fails: Notes retained in localStorage. No error shown to user.
    Silent retry on next persist event.
  - Graph reference unavailable: Viewport hash defaults to "unknown".
    Note still created without graph linkage.

DATA STORED:
  - Notes: sessions.graph_memory JSONB under "annotations" key
  - LocalStorage: "arivu_annotations_{session_id}" (immediate cache)

DATA CACHED:
  - Note list: In-memory in AthenaNotePad.notes[]
  - Viewport hash: Recomputed on each note creation (cheap operation)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

NOT APPLICABLE for note creation itself. The "Ask Athena" button sends the
note text as a standard chat message (routes through normal Athena pipeline).
The auto-suggestion trigger is rule-based (45s dwell timer), not LLM-generated.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Behavior                | Trigger               | Response                         |
|-------------------------|-----------------------|----------------------------------|
| Open/close pad          | Ctrl+Shift+N          | Slide in/out animation (250ms)   |
| Open/close pad          | Toolbar icon click    | Same animation                   |
| Add note                | Click "Add Note"      | Note block appears (fade 200ms)  |
| Add note                | Ctrl+Enter in textarea| Same as button                   |
| Ask Athena about note   | Click "Ask Athena"    | Prefilled chat message sent      |
| Delete note             | Click "Delete"        | Confirm then remove (fade out)   |
| Drag pad                | Mousedown on header   | Pad follows cursor               |
| Resize pad              | Drag resize handle    | Pad resizes (min 200x150)        |
| Graph indicator hover   | Mouse over pencil icon| Tooltip shows note text preview  |
| Graph indicator click   | Click pencil icon     | Pad opens, scrolls to that note  |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
  Pad open: translateX(320px) -> 0, opacity 0 -> 1, 250ms ease-out.
  Pad close: 0 -> translateX(320px), opacity 1 -> 0, 200ms ease-in.
  Note entry: opacity 0 -> 1, 200ms ease-out.
  Note delete: opacity 1 -> 0, height -> 0, 200ms ease-in.

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
  Annotation pad is role="dialog" with aria-label="Annotation Pad".
  Ctrl+Shift+N announced via aria-live "Annotation pad opened/closed".
  Textarea has aria-label="Write a note about what you're seeing".
  Each note block is role="article" with aria-label="Note [N]: [text preview]".
  Delete button has confirmation to prevent accidental deletion.
  Graph indicators have aria-label="Note near [region]: [text preview]".

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User creates 50 notes in one session
   Expected: All notes persist. Pad scrolls internally. No limit enforced.
   Implementation: Pad content area is scrollable. LocalStorage can hold
   ~5MB; 50 short notes well within limit. DB JSONB has no practical limit.

2. Scenario: User notes something while graph is mid-animation (node moving)
   Expected: Viewport hash computed from stable node positions only.
   Implementation: computeViewportHash() reads from D3 data binding
   (stable paper_ids), not from SVG transform positions. Animation state
   does not affect the hash.

3. Scenario: Two sessions open in separate tabs
   Expected: Each tab has its own annotation pad. Notes do not cross-contaminate.
   Implementation: LocalStorage key includes session_id which is tab-specific.
   DB writes include session_id as PK. No collision.

4. Scenario: User deletes a note that has a graph indicator visible
   Expected: Graph indicator disappears immediately.
   Implementation: deleteNote() calls graphRef.renderNoteIndicators() after
   removing the note from this.notes[]. D3 update removes the SVG element.

5. Scenario: Page navigates to a different graph while pad has notes
   Expected: Notes are saved before navigation. On new graph load, the pad
   shows "0 notes" (no notes for the new graph context).
   Implementation: Before navigation, trigger persist(). On new graph load,
   load notes for the new graph_id from localStorage/DB. Notes are namespaced
   by graph_id in addition to session_id.

6. Scenario: Auto-suggestion fires while user is actively typing in the pad
   Expected: Auto-suggestion is suppressed when pad is open and focused.
   Implementation: Auto-suggestion check includes: if (notepad.isOpen &&
   notepad.isFocused) return; before showing suggestion.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON:
  #012 -- AI Annotations on Graph (graph overlay infrastructure for indicators)
  #021 -- Export Chat as Report (annotations appear in exported reports)
  #006 -- Conversation Memory (session persistence infrastructure)

ENABLES:
  Phase H watchlist / research notes export features

ENHANCES:
  #021 -- Export Chat as Report (user notes as first-class report content)
  #009 -- Proactive Suggestions (auto-suggest annotation after dwell time)

CONFLICTS:
  NONE

EXTERNAL:
  Requires graph.js ArivuGraph to expose getVisibleNodeIds() and
  renderNoteIndicators() methods.
  Requires sessions.graph_memory JSONB column (Phase 1+).
  Uses navigator.sendBeacon for reliable unload sync.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[] Pad opens/closes via Ctrl+Shift+N and toolbar button
[] Notes created with text and linked to current viewport hash
[] Notes displayed in numbered list with timestamp and graph context
[] Notes persist in localStorage immediately on creation
[] Notes synced to sessions.graph_memory on persist events

[] Clicking graph indicator opens pad and scrolls to that note
