[] Delete note removes from list and removes graph indicator
[] Pad resizable (drag handle, min 200x150, max 600x400)
[] Pad draggable (header drag, stays within viewport)
[] Auto-suggestion fires after 45s dwell when pad is closed

VISUAL:

[] Pad matches dot matrix aesthetic (monospace elements, border styling)
[] Graph indicators are gold pencil icons (16x16 SVG)
[] Collapsed tab shows note count
[] Note blocks show truncated text preview (first 60 chars)

PERFORMANCE:
[] Viewport hash computation under 10ms
[] Pad open/close animation is 60fps
[] Graph indicator re-render after note add/delete under 50ms
[] LocalStorage write under 5ms for 50 notes

ACCESSIBILITY:
[] role="dialog" on pad container
[] aria-live announcement for open/close
[] Notes are role="article" with aria-labels
[] Graph indicators have descriptive aria-labels
[] Delete requires confirmation (no accidental data loss)

EDGE CASES:
[] 50 notes: pad scrolls, no limit errors
[] Mid-animation viewport hash stable
[] Multi-tab note isolation working
[] Deleted note removes graph indicator
[] Graph navigation clears and reloads notes per graph

VERIFICATION:

[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round -- ZERO issues
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified in Chrome
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-notepad.js (note creation, viewport hash, persistence, indicators),
   graph.js (getVisibleNodeIds, renderNoteIndicators), athena-chat.css (pad
   styles, indicator styles)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Create a note, refresh page -- verify note persists via localStorage
   b. Create a note, verify pencil icon appears on graph near correct region
   c. Click graph icon -- verify pad opens and scrolls to that note
   d. Click "Ask Athena" -- verify note text appears as prefilled chat message
   e. Navigate to a different graph -- verify notes scoped to previous graph
   f. Open pad, type, auto-suggestion fires -- verify suggestion suppressed
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Viewport hash computed from animated positions (should be from data)
   b. Notes leak between sessions (localStorage key collision)
   c. Graph indicator persists after note deletion
   d. Pad drag escapes viewport bounds
   e. sendBeacon fails silently -- notes lost on tab close
5. VERIFY no regression in: graph.js rendering, session memory, Athena chat
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below

================================================================================
FEATURE #034: CHAT-DRIVEN GRAPH NAVIGATION
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           C
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #005 (Graph-Aware Commands), #008 (Pathfinder Integration)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #005 (Graph-Aware Commands), #001 (Context-Aware Responses),
                 #037 (Graph State Sync)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Chat-Driven Graph Navigation makes the citation graph fully controllable through
natural language. Rather than requiring users to manually scroll, zoom, click,
and pan to navigate the graph, they can simply tell Athena where they want to go
and the graph animates there. "Show me the oldest papers" zooms to the pre-1990
nodes in a smooth BFS sweep. "Take me to the attention cluster" pans the viewport
and zooms into that semantic cluster. "Follow the path from BERT backward" runs
an animated reverse traversal, node by node, along the ancestry path.

This feature extends #005 (Graph-Aware Commands) significantly. Where #005
handles discrete operations (highlight a node, filter by field), this feature
handles fluid animated navigation. The difference is the same as between
clicking a button and being guided. Navigation here produces smooth camera
movement, sequential node highlighting in traversal order, and contextual
narration from Athena describing what is being shown as the animation plays.
The user feels like they are being given a tour of the graph, not just executing
commands.

The navigation system understands four classes of navigation intent parsed from
natural language: temporal ("oldest papers," "before 2000," "latest additions"),
structural ("the attention cluster," "the bridge nodes," "the most cited paper"),
path-based ("from BERT backward," "follow the chain from ResNet to AlexNet"),
and field-based ("show me the biology papers," "find the economics outliers").
These intents map to distinct animation sequences in graph.js.

Temporal navigation uses paper year data: the graph pans to the centroid of
papers matching the year filter and applies a highlight pulse to those nodes.
Structural navigation uses cluster labels from the DNA profiler and graph metrics
(PageRank, betweenness) to locate the target. Path navigation uses the existing
BFS infrastructure to trace a chain, then animates the viewport following each
edge in sequence at 600ms per hop. Field-based navigation filters nodes by
fields_of_study and zooms to the bounding box of matching nodes.

The implementation requires a navigation command API from graph.js: a set of
functions that the Athena engine can call to trigger specific animations. These
functions are: navigateTo(nodeId), navigateToCluster(clusterLabel),
animatePath(nodeIdList, directionForward), zoomToBoundingBox(nodeIdList), and
highlightSet(nodeIdList, color, pulseCount). Athena's backend parses the intent
and returns a structured navigation command. The frontend executes the command
animation sequence.

What makes this Athena-specific is the narration layer. As the animation plays,
Athena types a running commentary alongside it: "Following the path backward
from BERT... here is Transformer (2017)... the core architecture paper...
now jumping to the attention mechanism papers from 2015... this cluster is where
the foundational ideas originate." The narration is pre-generated during the
navigation command resolution (not streamed per-hop for latency reasons) and
displayed in synchrony with the animation.

The backend route returns both the animation command sequence and the narration
text. The frontend plays them in coordinated timing: each narration sentence
appears when its corresponding animation step begins. This coordination uses
a timeline array where each entry has a delay_ms and a message index.

Connection to #037 (Graph State Sync): navigation commands require knowledge
of the current graph state (which nodes exist, their positions, their cluster
assignments). #037 establishes the sync mechanism that gives the backend
reliable access to this state.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User types a navigation intent into the Athena chat. Examples: "Show me the
oldest papers," "Take me to the ResNet cluster," "Follow the path from BERT
backward to its earliest ancestor," "Find the bridge nodes."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types: "Take me to the foundational attention papers, oldest first."
   The graph is currently zoomed out showing all 120 nodes at once.

2. Athena responds immediately: "Navigating to the attention cluster,
   ordered by year..." The graph begins animating:
   -- 500ms: Viewport pans to the left side of the graph (where older papers
      cluster due to D3 layout) smoothly (D3 zoom transition, 800ms).
   -- The 8 pre-2016 attention papers pulse gold simultaneously.

3. The viewport settles on a zoomed view showing the 8 highlighted papers.
   Athena's narration begins typing: "These 8 papers form the theoretical
   foundation. The oldest is Bahdanau et al. (2015), which introduced
   the alignment mechanism that Vaswani et al. directly built on."

4. The Bahdanau node pulses once more (blue, distinct from gold) as it is
   mentioned. Athena continues: "From here, two paths diverge: the NLP
   track leading to BERT, and the multi-modal track leading to ViT."

5. Two path traces animate: one toward the BERT side of the graph, one
   toward the vision transformer side. Both animate simultaneously, each
   edge lighting up in sequence over 2 seconds.

6. Athena: "Which path would you like to explore?" Quick action buttons
   appear: [NLP Path] [Vision Path] [Show Both] [Done]

7. User clicks [NLP Path]. The graph pans to the BERT cluster and zooms in.
   Narration continues. The tour is now user-directed.

AFTER COMPLETION:
The graph remains in its navigated state (zoomed to the last destination).
Navigation history is logged to action_log. The user can pan/zoom freely
after the animation completes. A "Reset View" button in the graph controls
returns to the default overview.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-navigate.js: Navigation command executor. Receives navigation
  command objects from the Athena engine. Executes animation sequences using
  graph.js animation API. Manages narration timing coordination. Handles
  user interruption (click to skip animation).
- static/js/graph.js: Navigation API extensions. New methods: navigateTo(),
  navigateToCluster(), animatePath(), zoomToBoundingBox(), highlightSet(),
  getClusterBounds(), getNodesByYear(), getPathBetween(). These are called
  by athena-navigate.js.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Updated to recognize navigation intents and
  route them to the /api/athena/navigate endpoint instead of the standard
  /api/athena/chat endpoint.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
New route POST /api/athena/navigate:

  1. Accept: session_id, graph_id, navigation_query (user text),
     graph_state (node list with positions, cluster labels, years).
  2. Parse intent via GROQ_FAST_MODEL. Classify into:
     TEMPORAL | STRUCTURAL | PATH | FIELD
     Extract parameters: year_range, cluster_name, start_node_id,
     end_node_id, field_name, direction.
  3. Resolve parameters against graph_state:
     - TEMPORAL: filter nodes by year range, compute centroid
     - STRUCTURAL: match cluster_name to graph_state.clusters,
       get bounding box
     - PATH: call BFS/DFS to trace path between nodes
     - FIELD: filter nodes by fields_of_study
  4. Build animation command sequence:
     [{ action: 'zoom', target_bbox, duration_ms },
      { action: 'highlight', node_ids, color, delay_ms },
      { action: 'animate_path', node_sequence, ms_per_hop, delay_ms },
      ...]
  5. Generate narration via GROQ_SMART_MODEL (quality matters here):
     Full tour narration pre-generated, split into sentences, each
     sentence assigned a delay_ms matching its animation step.
  6. Return: { commands: [...], narration: [{text, delay_ms}, ...],
     duration_total_ms, post_nav_suggestions: [...] }

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
  D3 zoom transition:
    svg.transition().duration(800).call(
      zoom.transform,
      d3.zoomIdentity.translate(tx, ty).scale(scale)
    );
  Highlight pulse:
    node.classed('pulse-gold', true);
    setTimeout(() => node.classed('pulse-gold', false), 1200);
  Narration sync:
    commands.forEach(({ text, delay_ms }) => {
      setTimeout(() => athena.appendNarrationToken(text), delay_ms);
    });

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/navigate
  Request:  { session_id: str, graph_id: str, navigation_query: str,
              graph_state: { nodes: [{paper_id, x, y, year, cluster,
              fields}], edges: [{source, target}] } }
  Response: { commands: [NavigationCommand], narration: [{text, delay_ms}],
              duration_total_ms: int, post_nav_suggestions: [str] }
  Errors:   422 if graph_id missing, 404 if cluster not found,
            200 with empty commands if intent unresolvable (Athena explains)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: IDLE
  Normal graph view. No navigation in progress.

STATE: INTENT PARSING (< 500ms)
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ [ATHENA] Navigating to attention cluster...                 │
  │ ● (single pulsing dot)                                      │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  Graph: No change yet.

STATE: ANIMATING
  Graph viewport smoothly zooms/pans to target.
  Highlighted nodes pulse in sequence.
  Athena narration types below the "Navigating..." message.
  Skip button appears: [Skip Animation]

STATE: NARRATING (animation complete, narration still playing)
  Graph settled at destination. Nodes highlighted.
  Athena continues typing narration text at 40 chars/sec.

STATE: COMPLETE
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ [ATHENA] These 8 papers form the theoretical foundation.   │
  │ The oldest is Bahdanau et al. (2015)...                     │
  │                                                             │
  │ Where would you like to go next?                            │
  │ [NLP Path] [Vision Path] [Show Both] [Reset View]           │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

PATH ANIMATION VISUAL:
  Edges animate in sequence: each edge lights up gold for 200ms then dims
  to a persistent light gold (indicating traversed path). Nodes at each hop
  pulse once when the path reaches them.

  ○ -- ○ -- ● (current) --> ○ --> ○
             ^ animated gold pulse, 200ms

TRANSITIONS:
  IDLE -> INTENT PARSING: User sends navigation message.
  INTENT PARSING -> ANIMATING: API returns (< 1s).
  ANIMATING -> NARRATING: Animation sequence completes.
  NARRATING -> COMPLETE: Narration text fully typed.
  Any state -> IDLE: User clicks "Skip Animation" or "Reset View".

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User message -> athena-engine.js (intent classified as navigation)
  -> POST /api/athena/navigate with graph_state snapshot
  -> GROQ_FAST_MODEL intent classification (< 300ms, cached)
  -> Graph state resolution (BFS/cluster lookup, in-memory)
  -> GROQ_SMART_MODEL narration generation (1-3s, cached by query+graph_id)
  -> NavigationCommand array + narration timeline
  -> athena-navigate.js executeSequence()
  -> graph.js animation API calls in timed sequence
  -> Narration text appears synchronized with animation steps

ERROR PATH:
  - Cluster not found in graph_state: Return 200 with empty commands,
    Athena explains "I don't see a cluster labeled 'X' in this graph.
    Available clusters: [list]."
  - Path between nodes does not exist (disconnected graph): Athena explains
    "No direct path found from BERT to AlexNet in this lineage. They belong
    to separate subgraphs."
  - LLM unavailable: Rule-based fallback for simple navigation (temporal,
    field-based). PATH and STRUCTURAL intents fall back to a zoom-to-centroid.

DATA STORED:
  - Navigation actions: action_log (action_type='graph_navigation',
    action_data={query, intent_type, destination})
  - Nothing persisted to graphs table.

DATA CACHED:
  - Navigation narration: llm_cache (30-day TTL, key includes graph_id + query)
  - Graph state is NOT cached on backend (sent fresh per request)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL 1: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant)
STAGE:   Intent parsing (Step 2 of backend route)
PROMPT:  System: "Parse a graph navigation query into a structured intent.
         Return JSON only: {intent_type, parameters}. Intent types:
         TEMPORAL (params: year_min, year_max), STRUCTURAL (params:
         cluster_name, metric), PATH (params: start_node_id, end_node_id,
         direction), FIELD (params: field_name)."
         User: "{navigation_query}. Available clusters: {cluster_list}.
         Available fields: {field_list}. Node IDs: {node_sample}."
OUTPUT:  JSON intent object. Example: {"intent_type": "PATH",
         "parameters": {"start_node_id": "abc123", "direction": "backward"}}
FALLBACK: Regex-based parsing for common patterns ("oldest", "newest",
          year mentions, cluster names from available list).

MODEL 2: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile)
STAGE:   Narration generation (Step 5 of backend route)
PROMPT:  System: "You are Athena, guiding a researcher through a citation
         graph. Write a narration for the animated tour. Each sentence
         corresponds to one animation step. Be precise and insightful.
         Reference specific papers by name and year."
         User: "Animation steps: {step_descriptions}. Papers at each step:
         {paper_summaries}. User's navigation intent: {query}."
OUTPUT:  Array of sentences, one per animation step. JSON format.
FALLBACK: Generic template narration: "Moving to {cluster}. This area
         contains {N} papers from {year_range}."
TOKEN ESTIMATE: ~800 tokens for intent parse + narration. Cached aggressively.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Behavior               | Trigger               | Response                        |
|------------------------|-----------------------|---------------------------------|
| Start navigation       | Navigation message    | Animation begins (< 500ms)      |
| Skip animation         | Click [Skip Animation]| Jump to final state instantly   |
| Interrupt navigation   | Click any graph node  | Animation stops, node selected  |
| Post-nav suggestion    | Click suggestion btn  | New navigation command sent     |
| Reset view             | Click [Reset View]    | Smooth zoom-out to full graph   |
| Hover navigated path   | Mouse over path edges | Edge tooltip shows hop sequence |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
  Viewport pan/zoom: D3 zoom.transform transition, 800ms, ease cubic-in-out.
  Node highlight pulse: 1200ms total, opacity 1.0 -> 0.6 -> 1.0 (2 cycles).
  Path edge animation: each edge 200ms, left-to-right stroke animation via
    SVG stroke-dasharray/stroke-dashoffset technique.
  Narration type speed: 40 chars/second, standard Athena typewriter effect.
  Skip: instant transition (no animation), narration appears fully typed.

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
  Navigation narration is the primary text description of what the graph
  is showing. Screen readers receive the full narration text via aria-live.
  Skip button is keyboard-accessible (Tab-focusable, Enter-activates).
  After navigation, focus moves to the Athena chat input.
  All highlighted node sets are described in text ("8 pre-2016 attention
  papers highlighted") via aria-live announcement.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Navigation query references a paper not in the graph
   Expected: Athena says "I don't see [paper name] in this graph. The
   closest match is [paper X] -- did you mean that?"
   Implementation: Fuzzy title matching (pg_trgm) against graph nodes.
   If no match, offer available cluster names.

2. Scenario: User sends a second navigation command during animation
   Expected: Current animation stops cleanly. New navigation starts.
   Implementation: athena-navigate.js tracks a cancelToken. New command
   cancels pending timeouts and starts fresh.

3. Scenario: Graph has only 3 nodes (very small lineage)
   Expected: Navigation still works but narration acknowledges limited graph.
   "This lineage is small. Showing all 3 papers." No crash, no empty state.
   Implementation: Route handles minimal graph_state gracefully.

4. Scenario: PATH navigation requested between nodes with no connecting path
   Expected: Athena explains: "No path found. These nodes are in separate
   connected components." Offers alternative: "Show both clusters side by side."
   Implementation: BFS returns None if no path. Backend returns
   intent_type='PATH_NOT_FOUND' response. Frontend renders Athena message.

5. Scenario: Navigation animation is 8 seconds long, user loses interest
   Expected: Skip button visible from start. One click ends animation
   and shows final state immediately.
   Implementation: Skip sets a flag that immediately applies the final
   D3 zoom state and marks all path edges as traversed.

6. Scenario: Graph state sent to backend is stale (graph was rebuilt mid-session)
   Expected: Backend detects mismatch between graph_id in request and
   current graph_id in DB. Returns 409 with "Graph has been updated.
   Please reload." Frontend triggers graph reload.
   Implementation: graph_id version check in route handler.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON:
  #005 -- Graph-Aware Commands (foundation for graph command parsing)
  #001 -- Context-Aware Responses (graph context in prompts)
  #037 -- Graph State Sync (reliable graph state available to backend)

ENABLES:
  Phase H guided tour feature (automated navigation sequences)

ENHANCES:
  #008 -- Pathfinder Integration (path navigation is a natural extension)
  #015 -- Timeline Narration (navigation enables temporal tours)

CONFLICTS:
  NONE with #005 -- this feature extends #005, does not replace it

EXTERNAL:
  Requires graph.js to expose navigation API (new methods added to ArivuGraph).
  Requires D3.js zoom transition capability (already present).
  Requires GROQ_SMART_MODEL availability for quality narration.
  Graceful degradation when LLM unavailable (rule-based fallback).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] POST /api/athena/navigate parses TEMPORAL, STRUCTURAL, PATH, FIELD intents
[] Animation commands returned and executed by athena-navigate.js
[] Viewport pan/zoom animations play smoothly for each intent type
[] Path animation highlights edges in sequence (600ms per hop default)
[] Narration text appears synchronized with animation steps
[] Skip button terminates animation and shows final state
[] Second navigation command cancels first cleanly
[] Post-navigation quick action buttons appear after tour completes
[] PATH_NOT_FOUND handled with helpful Athena message
[] Navigation actions logged to action_log

VISUAL:
[] D3 zoom transition is 800ms cubic-in-out (not jarring)
[] Node highlight pulse: gold, 1200ms, 2 cycles
[] Path edges animate left-to-right with stroke-dashoffset
[] Narration types at 40 chars/second (matches standard Athena speed)
[] Skip button visible from start of animation

PERFORMANCE:
[] Intent parsing complete within 500ms
[] Total time from message to animation start under 1 second
[] Animation runs at 60fps (no dropped frames on 120-node graph)
[] Narration pre-generated (not streamed per-hop)

ACCESSIBILITY:
[] Full narration text available to screen readers via aria-live
[] Skip button keyboard-accessible
[] Highlighted node sets announced in text
[] Focus moves to chat input after navigation completes

EDGE CASES:
[] Paper not in graph: fuzzy match fallback with suggestion
[] Concurrent navigation commands: clean cancellation
[] 3-node graph: works without crash
[] PATH between disconnected nodes: informative error
[] Stale graph state: 409 response + reload prompt

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round -- ZERO issues
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified in Chrome
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-navigate.js (command executor, timing, skip logic),
   graph.js (navigation API methods: navigateTo, animatePath, etc.),
   athena-engine.js (intent routing to /navigate endpoint),
   app.py (/api/athena/navigate route, intent parser, narration generator)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. "Show me the oldest papers" -- verify TEMPORAL intent, correct nodes
   b. "Take me to the attention cluster" -- verify STRUCTURAL, smooth pan
   c. "Follow the path from BERT backward" -- verify PATH animation, hop timing
   d. Send second nav command during animation -- verify clean cancellation
   e. Click Skip during animation -- verify instant jump to final state
   f. Reference a paper not in graph -- verify fuzzy match or clear error
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Graph state snapshot sent with stale positions (nodes moved after send)
   b. Narration timing desynchronized from animation (delay_ms mismatch)
   c. Skip button not visible until after animation starts (should be immediate)
   d. D3 zoom transition conflicts with user pan/zoom during animation
5. VERIFY no regression in: graph.js existing pan/zoom, #005 commands
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase C feature. Requires graph.js navigation API extensions.
                  Backend requires GROQ_SMART_MODEL for quality narration.
                  Depends on #037 (Graph State Sync) for reliable state access.


================================================================================
FEATURE #035: PEER DISCUSSION SIMULATOR
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 28
OVERLAPS WITH:   #018 (Debate Mode), #002 (Rich Response Formatting)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
                 #018 (Debate Mode), #037 (Graph State Sync)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Peer Discussion Simulator allows users to generate and observe a simulated
academic discussion between two or more hypothetical researcher personas with
distinct, opposing, or complementary perspectives on the research lineage. The
user defines the setup: "Simulate a discussion between a deep learning skeptic
and an enthusiast about whether the attention mechanism was truly novel."
Athena generates a multi-turn dialogue where each persona argues from evidence
grounded in the papers visible in the current graph. The discussion is rendered
as a multi-speaker chat with distinct visual identities for each persona.

This feature serves a specific research need: stress-testing an interpretation.
When a researcher forms a view about a field's direction, the best way to
validate it is to argue against it. The simulator forces that confrontation
without requiring a human interlocutor. It generates the strongest version of
the opposing view using the same evidence base (the graph) and makes the
researcher defend their interpretation against it. This is not roleplay for
entertainment -- it is Socratic pressure for epistemic rigor.

Each simulated researcher has a name, a perspective label, an avatar color,
and a role in the discussion. Personas are either drawn from predefined
archetypes (skeptic, enthusiast, empiricist, theorist, historian, critic) or
described by the user in free text ("a researcher who believes unsupervised
learning is undervalued"). Athena assigns a perspective to each persona that
is coherent, grounded in real academic debate positions, and defensible using
evidence in the graph.

The discussion format is a thread of alternating messages. Each message
references specific papers from the graph by paper_id, quoted as "[Vaswani
et al. 2017, Attention Is All You Need]" inline. When the user hovers over
a paper citation in the discussion, the corresponding node in the graph
highlights. The discussion can be 3 to 10 turns per persona (user-specified,
default 5). At the end, Athena writes a brief synthesis: "The key tension
in this discussion was X. Both perspectives agree on Y. The evidence
most cited was Z."

The feature connects to #018 (Debate Mode) but is distinct: Debate Mode
is Athena arguing against the user. The Peer Discussion Simulator is Athena
generating a discussion between two third-party personas the user observes.
The user is the audience, not a participant, unless they choose to inject
a message ("I want to ask the skeptic a question...").

Technically, the simulation is generated in one LLM call (not streamed turn-
by-turn) using GROQ_SMART_MODEL with a structured prompt. The output is a
JSON array of messages: [{persona_name, text, cited_paper_ids, turn_number}].
This structured output is then rendered by athena-peers.js into the multi-
speaker chat UI. The single-call architecture avoids latency accumulation from
multi-turn streaming and allows the full discussion to be re-read and referenced
cohesively.

Each persona is visually distinct. Avatar colors cycle through: teal (#06B6D4),
gold (#D4A843), blue (#3B82F6), purple (#9333EA) for up to 4 personas. The
persona name appears above each message bubble in the persona's color. Messages
use a slightly different background from standard Athena chat to visually
separate the simulation context from the main conversation.

After the simulation, quick action buttons offer: [Save as Report Section]
[Ask the Skeptic a Question] [Ask the Enthusiast a Question] [Run Another
Round]. "Run Another Round" generates 5 more turns from where the discussion
left off, continuing the argument. Users can generate up to 3 rounds (15 turns
per persona maximum) before Athena offers a forced synthesis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User types "Simulate a discussion between..." or uses the Peer Discussion
option from the Athena special modes menu. Mode can also be triggered by
Athena proactively: "I notice this lineage has papers with contradictory
conclusions. Want me to simulate the researchers debating them?"

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types: "Simulate a discussion between a deep learning skeptic and
   an attention mechanism advocate. 5 turns each. Focus on whether the
   Transformer was truly novel or just engineering."

2. Athena responds: "Setting up discussion. Two researchers: Dr. Carter
   (Skeptic) and Dr. Patel (Advocate). Generating 5-turn debate..."
   A progress bar pulses for 3-5 seconds (single LLM call generating
   full discussion).

3. The discussion renders as a distinct visual block below the Athena message.
   The block has a header: "PEER DISCUSSION: Novelty of the Transformer
   Architecture | Dr. Carter (Skeptic) vs. Dr. Patel (Advocate)"

4. Messages appear in a staggered animation (300ms between each):

   [DR. CARTER -- teal] Turn 1
   "The Transformer's success isn't architectural novelty -- it's scale.
   The attention mechanism existed in [Bahdanau et al., 2015] and [Luong
   et al., 2015]. Vaswani's contribution was removing the RNN bottleneck
   and scaling the training, not inventing attention itself."

   [DR. PATEL -- gold] Turn 1
   "Scale alone doesn't explain the Transformer's generalization capability.
   The parallelization architecture in [Vaswani et al., 2017] enabled an
   entirely new training paradigm. Without the architectural choice to
   remove sequential dependency entirely, you cannot achieve the pretrain-
   finetune paradigm that makes [BERT, 2018] and [GPT-2, 2019] possible."

   [... 4 more turns each ...]

5. Synthesis block appears at the bottom:
   "SYNTHESIS: The core tension is attribution vs. enablement. Carter argues
   prior work contains all components; Patel argues architectural integration
   was the enabling leap. Most cited paper in this debate: Vaswani 2017 (8
   references). Only agreed point: scale was necessary."

6. Quick action buttons: [Save as Report Section] [Ask Dr. Carter]
   [Ask Dr. Patel] [Another Round] [End Discussion]

7. User clicks "Ask Dr. Carter." The Athena input prefills: "@Dr. Carter:
   [your question]". User completes the question. Athena generates a single
   in-character response from Dr. Carter and appends it to the discussion.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Discussion can be saved as a report section (via #021). The discussion is
stored in the session's chat_history with a thread_id of "sim_{uuid}" so
it does not pollute the main conversation thread.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-peers.js: Peer discussion renderer. Handles persona setup,
  discussion block rendering, staggered message animation, paper citation
  hover-to-highlight integration, synthesis block, follow-up question routing.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Updated to include peer_discussion block type.
  Renders the discussion container within a chat message.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Updated to recognize peer simulation intents
  and route them to /api/athena/simulate-peers.
- app.py: New route POST /api/athena/simulate-peers. Accepts persona setup,
  graph context, topic, turn count. Returns structured discussion JSON.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
POST /api/athena/simulate-peers:

  1. Parse persona setup from user query:
     - Extract persona descriptions (or map to archetypes)
     - Extract topic, turn count (default 5, max 10)
  2. Load graph summary via get_graph_summary_for_chat() from utils.py.
     (Top 15 papers, key clusters, edge mutations -- compact context.)
  3. Single GROQ_SMART_MODEL call with structured output prompt.
     Request JSON array of messages, each with:
     { persona_name, perspective_label, text, cited_paper_ids, turn_number }
  4. Validate output: ensure cited_paper_ids are valid (in the graph),
     turn counts are balanced between personas, synthesis present.
  5. Generate synthesis (in same call as a final element in the array).
  6. Return: { personas: [{name, color, perspective}],
               turns: [{persona_name, text, cited_paper_ids, turn_number}],
               synthesis: str, topic: str, total_turns: int }

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
  Structured LLM output using JSON mode:
    response = groq_client.chat.completions.create(
      model=config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL,
      response_format={"type": "json_object"},
      messages=[...]
    )
  Paper citation hover integration:
    element.querySelectorAll('[data-paper-id]').forEach(el => {
      el.addEventListener('mouseenter', () => {
        graphRef.highlightNode(el.dataset.paperId, 'temp');
      });
    });

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/simulate-peers
  Request:  { session_id: str, graph_id: str, topic: str,
              personas: [{description: str}], turns_per_persona: int }
  Response: { personas: [{name, color, perspective}],
              turns: [{persona_name, text, cited_paper_ids, turn_number}],
              synthesis: str, topic: str, duration_ms: int }
  Errors:   422 if fewer than 2 personas, 400 if turns > 10,
            503 if GROQ unavailable

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DISCUSSION BLOCK HEADER:
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ PEER DISCUSSION                                             │
  │ Novelty of the Transformer Architecture                     │
  │ Dr. Carter (Skeptic) vs. Dr. Patel (Advocate)               │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

PERSONA MESSAGE (Dr. Carter -- teal avatar):
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ● DR. CARTER  [SKEPTIC]                     Turn 1 of 5    │
  │ ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │ │ "The Transformer's success is scale, not novelty.     │   │
  │ │ [Bahdanau et al., 2015] introduced attention first."  │   │
  │ └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  (Paper citations underlined, hoverable)

PERSONA MESSAGE (Dr. Patel -- gold avatar):
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │               ● DR. PATEL  [ADVOCATE]       Turn 1 of 5    │
  │         ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │         │ "Parallelization enabled a new paradigm.    │     │
  │         │ [Vaswani et al., 2017] changed training." │     │
  │         └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  (Right-aligned, distinct background shade)

SYNTHESIS BLOCK:
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ SYNTHESIS                                                   │
  │ Core tension: attribution vs. enablement.                   │
  │ Most cited: Vaswani 2017 (8 references).                    │
  │ Only agreed point: scale was necessary.                     │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

ACTION BUTTONS:
  [Save as Report Section] [Ask Dr. Carter] [Ask Dr. Patel]
  [Another Round]          [End Discussion]

TRANSITIONS:
  Setup response -> loading bar (3-5s LLM call).
  Loading -> Discussion: messages fade in staggered, 300ms between each.
  Paper citation hover -> graph node highlights (gold pulse, 500ms).
  "Another Round" -> new turns appended (same stagger animation).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User request -> athena-engine.js (simulation intent detected)
  -> POST /api/athena/simulate-peers
  -> get_graph_summary_for_chat() (top 15 papers, edges, clusters)
  -> GROQ_SMART_MODEL single call (structured JSON output, 2-5s)
  -> Validation (cited paper IDs checked against graph node list)
  -> Response JSON
  -> athena-peers.js discussion block renderer
  -> Staggered message animation
  -> Paper citation hover events linked to graph.js highlightNode()

ERROR PATH:
  - GROQ unavailable: Athena says "Peer simulation requires the AI service.
    Try again in a moment." No partial output shown.
  - LLM returns invalid JSON: Retry once with explicit JSON format reminder.
    If second attempt fails, Athena describes the discussion in prose format
    (non-structured fallback).
  - Cited paper_ids not in graph: Filter them out silently. If > 50% of
    citations are invalid, trigger retry with stricter prompt.

DATA STORED:
  - Discussion thread: chat_history with thread_id="sim_{uuid}" and
    role="simulation", not mixed with user/assistant messages.
  - Action: action_log (action_type='simulation_run', action_data={topic,
    personas, turn_count})

DATA CACHED:
  - LLM output: llm_cache (30-day TTL, key: SHA256(graph_id + topic +
    personas_description + turns_count))

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL:   config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile)
STAGE:   Single call, structured JSON output
PROMPT:
  System: "You are generating a simulated academic discussion between
  researchers for the Arivu research platform. Generate a structured JSON
  discussion grounded in provided paper evidence. Each speaker argues from
  specific papers only. Use academic language. Be fair to both sides.
  Output format: { personas, turns, synthesis } as defined in schema."
  User:   "Topic: {topic}. Personas: {persona_descriptions}.
  Turns per persona: {turns}. Graph summary: {graph_summary}.
  Paper list with abstracts: {paper_list}. Cite papers as
  [Author et al., Year, Short Title] using only papers from the list.
  Generate the full {total_turns}-turn discussion plus synthesis."
OUTPUT:  JSON with personas array, turns array, synthesis string.
         Validated server-side for paper ID accuracy.
FALLBACK: Prose-format discussion if JSON parsing fails (2 retries before
          fallback). Prose version cannot support paper hover interactions.
TOKEN ESTIMATE: ~3000-4000 tokens per 5-turn discussion. Cached after first
  generation. Expensive but a deliberate P3 feature.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Behavior                   | Trigger               | Response                      |
|----------------------------|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
| Paper citation hover       | Hover over [Author Y] | Graph node highlights gold    |
| Ask Dr. X button           | Click                 | Chat input prefilled @Dr. X:  |
| Another Round button       | Click                 | 5 more turns generated+appended|
| Save as Report Section     | Click                 | Sent to export module         |
| End Discussion             | Click                 | Discussion collapsed to summary|
| Persona name hover         | Hover                 | Tooltip: perspective summary  |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
  Loading: single pulsing bar (3-5s, width oscillates 20%-80%).
  Message stagger: each message fades in (opacity 0->1, 300ms) with 300ms
    delay between consecutive messages.
  Paper citation hover: graph node scale 1.0 -> 1.3 (200ms), gold pulse.
  Synthesis block: slides in from bottom (300ms, ease-out) after last turn.

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
  Discussion block is role="region" with aria-label="Peer Discussion: [topic]".
  Each persona message is role="article" with aria-label="[Name], Turn [N]".
  Paper citations have aria-label="Paper: [title], [year]".
  Loading state announced via aria-live "Generating peer discussion..."
  "Ask Dr. X" buttons have descriptive aria-labels.
  Discussion readable linearly by screen reader (left-to-right, not positional).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User requests 3 personas (not just 2)
   Expected: Three-way discussion works. Color cycle: teal, gold, blue.
   Layout shifts: messages from persona 3 appear centered.
   Implementation: Backend supports up to 4 personas (hard cap).
   athena-peers.js assigns colors from a fixed 4-color cycle.

2. Scenario: LLM cites a paper not in the graph
   Expected: The citation is rendered without the hover-to-highlight
   interaction (no data-paper-id attribute). A "(not in graph)" note
   appears in parentheses after the citation.
   Implementation: Server-side validation marks each cited_paper_id as
   valid or invalid. Frontend renders invalid citations as plain text.

3. Scenario: User asks follow-up question to a persona mid-discussion
   Expected: In-character response appended as a new turn.
   Implementation: POST /api/athena/simulate-peers with the existing
   turns in context and a new instruction: "Dr. Carter is asked:
   {question}. Generate Dr. Carter's response only."

4. Scenario: Topic is highly sensitive (funding, replication crisis, fraud)
   Expected: Athena generates factual, evidence-based discussion without
   making accusations. Safety prompt included in system message.
   Implementation: System prompt includes "Discuss only on the basis of
   published papers in the graph. Do not make personal accusations."

5. Scenario: Graph has fewer than 5 papers (too small for meaningful debate)
   Expected: Athena warns: "This graph has only 3 papers -- the discussion
   may be limited. Proceeding with available evidence."
   Implementation: Route checks len(graph_summary['nodes']) < 5 and
   adds a warning field to the response. Frontend displays the warning.

6. Scenario: User triggers "Another Round" after 3 rounds (15 turns per
   persona)
   Expected: Button is disabled after Round 3. Forced synthesis message:
   "Maximum discussion length reached. Here is the final synthesis."
   Implementation: Turn counter tracked client-side. Button disabled
   at max_rounds=3. Server enforces same limit.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON:
  #001 -- Context-Aware Responses (graph context for persona grounding)
  #002 -- Rich Response Formatting (multi-speaker block rendering)

ENABLES:

  a starting point for human-annotated debates)

ENHANCES:
  #021 -- Export Chat as Report (discussion as a saveable report section)
  #002 -- Rich Response Formatting (new block type: peer_discussion)

CONFLICTS:
  NONE with #018 -- Debate Mode is user-vs-Athena; this is observer mode

EXTERNAL:
  Requires GROQ_SMART_MODEL (expensive: ~4000 tokens per discussion).
  Requires get_graph_summary_for_chat() from backend/utils.py.
  Requires JSON mode support in Groq API (available in llama-3.3-70b).

PERFORMANCE:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-peers.js (renderer, hover integration, action buttons),
   athena-blocks.js (peer_discussion block type), app.py
   (/api/athena/simulate-peers route), backend/llm_client.py (JSON mode
   usage, caching), backend/utils.py (get_graph_summary_for_chat)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. 2-persona debate, 5 turns -- verify full render, synthesis present
   b. Hover paper citation -- verify graph node highlights
   c. Click "Ask Dr. X" -- verify in-character response generated
   d. Click "Another Round" 3 times -- verify 3rd round disables button
   e. LLM returns invalid JSON -- verify retry then prose fallback
   f. 3-persona discussion -- verify 3 colors, centered third persona
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. All citations invalid (graph summary mismatch) -- silent failure
   b. Discussion stored in main thread instead of sim_ thread
   c. "Another Round" context truncation (turns too long for context window)
   d. Persona colors hardcoded and fail for 5+ personas
5. VERIFY no regression in: Athena chat, Debate Mode (#018), export (#021)
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below

================================================================================
FEATURE #036: RESEARCH IMPACT CALCULATOR
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           F
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #032 (Recommendation Engine)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
                 #037 (Graph State Sync)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Research Impact Calculator gives researchers a data-grounded estimate of
the structural impact a hypothetical paper would have if published in a specific
area of the current lineage. When a user asks "If I publish a paper extending
ResNet's skip connections to transformers, what would its predicted structural
impact be?", Athena runs a simulation using the graph's existing topology,
PageRank scores, citation velocity data, and cluster growth patterns to return
a quantified impact assessment with confidence intervals and a breakdown of
which graph structures would be most affected.

This feature addresses a specific failure mode in research planning: researchers
often choose problems based on personal interest or advisor guidance, without
systematic analysis of whether the contribution would be structurally significant
or structurally marginal. A paper that fills a true structural gap in the lineage
will accrue more citations, generate more follow-on work, and have more lasting
impact than one that adds another incremental node to an already saturated
cluster. The Impact Calculator makes this invisible structural calculus explicit.

The calculation proceeds in three analytical layers. First, structural analysis:
the system identifies which existing nodes the hypothetical paper would most
likely cite (based on semantic similarity to the user's description) and
simulates adding the hypothetical paper to the graph. It runs a modified PageRank
computation on the augmented graph and measures the delta in centrality scores
for affected nodes. The sum of absolute PageRank deltas gives the structural
footprint score.

Second, velocity analysis: the system examines citation velocity data for the
cluster the hypothetical paper would enter. Clusters growing at > 15% per year
represent active research fronts where new papers gain traction quickly. Clusters
that have plateaued (< 3% growth over 3 years) are saturated. The velocity
score reflects how receptive the field is to new contributions in this area.

Third, gap analysis: cross-reference the hypothetical paper's description
against the gap_finder.py output for the current graph. If the described paper
fills a known gap (high similarity to gap_finder results but low similarity to
existing nodes), it scores high on originality. If it overlaps heavily with
existing nodes, it scores low on originality and will likely be perceived as
incremental.

The final impact score is a weighted composite: structural footprint (40%),
velocity (35%), originality (25%). Each component is scored 0-100. The composite
is presented with a qualitative label: "TRANSFORMATIVE" (80-100), "SIGNIFICANT"
(60-79), "INCREMENTAL" (40-59), "MARGINAL" (< 40). Each label has a definition
shown on hover.

The result is rendered as a specialized response block: a score card with a
breakdown bar chart (ASCII art within Athena's dot matrix style), the qualitative
label, and a bulleted list of "what would change" if this paper were added to
the lineage. The "what would change" list identifies the top 3 nodes whose
PageRank would increase most significantly.

Confidence intervals are derived from the variance in the hypothetical paper's
estimated position. Because the user provides a description rather than a
specific paper, the system cannot know exactly which papers it would cite.
The calculation runs the simulation for three scenarios: conservative (cites
only the 3 most similar papers), central (cites the top 5 by similarity score),
and aggressive (cites the top 8 including tangential connections). The three
PageRank delta values form the interval.

The backend does not require an LLM call for the core calculation (it is
deterministic graph math). The LLM is used only to generate the qualitative
interpretation text ("what would change") which requires natural language
output. This keeps latency low (< 3 seconds for the graph math, + ~1 second
for the interpretation).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User asks a "what if I publish..." question directly in chat. Or user clicks
the "Calculate Impact" button on any node in the graph (which pre-fills the
chat with "What would be the impact of extending [node title]?").

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types: "If I write a paper combining ResNet's skip connections with
   transformer attention heads for image classification, what impact would
   it have on this lineage?"

2. Athena responds: "Calculating structural impact for your hypothetical
   paper..." A progress bar animates through three visible phases:
   "Finding position in graph... (1s)" -> "Computing PageRank delta... (1s)"
   -> "Assessing research velocity... (1s)"

3. The impact result block appears:

   IMPACT ASSESSMENT: ResNet-Transformer Hybrid (Image Classification)
   ========================================
   OVERALL IMPACT SCORE: 74 / 100
   Classification: SIGNIFICANT

   COMPONENT BREAKDOWN:
   Structural Footprint:  [██████████░░░░] 68/100
   Research Velocity:     [███████████░░░] 82/100
   Originality Gap:       [████████░░░░░░] 67/100

   CONFIDENCE: 74 ± 9  (Conservative: 65, Central: 74, Aggressive: 83)

   WHAT WOULD CHANGE:
   - ResNet (He, 2016) gains 12% PageRank -- your paper would be a
     primary bridge to the transformer cluster.
   - Vision Transformer (Dosovitskiy, 2020) gains 8% PageRank --
     your paper creates a backward link that increases its influence.
   - 3 current leaf nodes would gain a new common successor, ending
     their isolated status.

   RECOMMENDED CLUSTER ENTRY POINT: Vision-Language bridge subgraph
   (currently 4 papers, growing at 22% per year)

4. Quick action buttons: [Explore This Area] [Compare Two Approaches]
   [Ask About Risks] [Export Assessment]

5. User clicks "Compare Two Approaches." Athena prompts for a second
   hypothesis. User enters it. A side-by-side comparison renders.

6. User clicks "Explore This Area." The graph navigates (#034) to the
   Vision-Language bridge subgraph.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Assessment is stored in chat_history. The user can reference it later.
If the user exports a report (#021), the impact assessment appears as
a "Research Positioning Analysis" section.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-impact.js: Impact calculator frontend. Renders impact
  score card with breakdown bar, confidence interval, "what would change"
  list, action buttons. Handles the three-phase progress animation.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Updated to include impact_assessment block type.
- app.py: New route POST /api/athena/impact-calc. Accepts description of
  hypothetical paper, graph_id, session_id. Returns impact score breakdown.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
POST /api/athena/impact-calc:

  1. Accept hypothesis_description (user's paper description, max 500 chars),
     graph_id, session_id.
  2. Fetch current graph from DB (graphs table, full JSON via r2_client or
     DB JSON column).
  3. Build NetworkX graph from graph JSON. Compute baseline PageRank:
     baseline_pr = nx.pagerank(G, alpha=0.85)
  4. Encode hypothesis_description via NLP worker /encode_batch (1 text).
     Compute cosine similarity against paper_embeddings for all nodes
     in graph. Sort by similarity.
  5. Three scenarios:
     - conservative: add hypothetical node citing top 3 similar nodes
     - central:      add hypothetical node citing top 5 similar nodes
     - aggressive:   add hypothetical node citing top 8 similar nodes
     For each scenario: add node to graph copy, recompute PageRank,
     measure delta = new_pr - baseline_pr for each affected node.
  6. Compute scores:
     - structural_footprint = sum(abs(delta)) * 1000 (normalized to 0-100)
     - velocity: fetch year distribution for candidate cluster from DB.
       Compute CAGR over last 3 years. Map to 0-100.
     - originality: compare hypothesis embedding to GapFinder results.
       High gap score = high originality.
  7. Composite = 0.4*structural + 0.35*velocity + 0.25*originality
     Clip to [0, 100]. Round to integer.
  8. Top 3 affected nodes by absolute PageRank delta.
  9. Call GROQ_FAST_MODEL to generate "what would change" narrative
     from the top 3 node deltas and their paper metadata.
  10. Return full assessment JSON.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
  PageRank simulation:
    G_hyp = G.copy()
    G_hyp.add_node('__hypothesis__')
    for paper_id in top_k_similar:
        G_hyp.add_edge('__hypothesis__', paper_id)
    hyp_pr = nx.pagerank(G_hyp, alpha=0.85)
    delta = {k: hyp_pr.get(k, 0) - baseline_pr.get(k, 0)
             for k in G.nodes()}

  Citation velocity:
    SELECT year, COUNT(*) as n FROM papers
    WHERE paper_id = ANY(%s) GROUP BY year ORDER BY year DESC LIMIT 5;
    # CAGR = (n_last / n_first)^(1/years) - 1

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/impact-calc
  Request:  { session_id: str, graph_id: str,
              hypothesis_description: str (max 500 chars) }
  Response: { overall_score: int, classification: str,
              components: { structural: int, velocity: int, originality: int },
              confidence: { score: int, low: int, high: int,
                            conservative: int, central: int, aggressive: int },
              what_would_change: [ { paper_id, title, pagerank_delta_pct,
                                     explanation } ],
              recommended_entry_cluster: str, cluster_growth_rate_pct: float,
              generated_at: int }
  Errors:   422 if hypothesis empty, 503 if NLP worker unavailable,
            404 if graph_id not found

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  [Explore This Area] [Compare Approaches] [Ask Risks] [Export]

CLASSIFICATION LABELS (hover for definition):
  TRANSFORMATIVE (80-100): "Would restructure the field's citation topology"
  SIGNIFICANT    (60-79):  "Would become a bridge node within 2-3 years"
  INCREMENTAL    (40-59):  "Would add a node to an existing cluster"
  MARGINAL       (<40):    "Would likely remain a leaf node"

TRANSITIONS:
  Loading phases: each phase bar fills in 1s, then next phase activates.
  Results appear: score counter animates 0 -> final (800ms, ease-out).
  Bar components: each bar fills left-to-right simultaneously (600ms).
  "What would change" items: staggered fade-in, 150ms each.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User hypothesis -> athena-engine.js (impact intent detected)
  -> POST /api/athena/impact-calc
  -> DB: fetch graph JSON (graphs table)
  -> NetworkX: build graph, baseline PageRank (in-memory)
  -> NLP worker /encode_batch (1 text: hypothesis description)
  -> pgvector: cosine similarity against graph node embeddings
  -> 3x PageRank simulation (conservative, central, aggressive)
  -> Citation velocity query (papers table)
  -> GapFinder.find_gaps() for originality score
  -> GROQ_FAST_MODEL: "what would change" narrative
  -> JSON response
  -> athena-impact.js score card renderer

ERROR PATH:
  - NLP worker offline: Cannot compute similarity -> 503 with explanation.
    "Impact calculation requires the NLP worker to be online."
  - Graph not found: 404. Athena: "This graph is not available for analysis."
  - Hypothesis too short (< 20 chars): 422. Athena: "Please describe your
    hypothetical paper in more detail (at least 20 characters)."
  - LLM unavailable: Return score data without "what would change" text.
    Frontend shows score card with "Narrative unavailable" placeholder.

DATA STORED:
  - Assessment query: action_log (action_type='impact_calc_run',
    action_data={hypothesis_preview, overall_score, classification})
  - Nothing persisted to graphs or papers tables.

DATA CACHED:
  - LLM narrative: llm_cache (30-day TTL)
    Key: SHA256("impact:" + graph_id + ":" + hypothesis_hash)
  - PageRank computation: NOT cached (graph may change between sessions)
  - NLP embedding of hypothesis: NOT cached (one-time-use vector)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL:   config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant)
STAGE:   Post-calculation narrative generation only
PROMPT:
  System: "You are Athena, a research intelligence guide. Given a structural
  impact analysis of a hypothetical paper, generate 3 bullet points describing
  what would change in the research field if this paper were published.
  Each bullet: max 20 words. Reference specific papers by name. Be precise."
  User:   "Hypothesis: {description}. Top 3 affected papers and their
  PageRank changes: {affected_papers_json}. Graph context: {graph_summary}.
  Cluster entry point: {cluster}. Growth rate: {growth_rate}%/year."
OUTPUT:  3 bullet points (strings). JSON array.
FALLBACK: If LLM fails, use template bullets:
  "- {paper_title} gains {delta}% structural influence."
  (one per top-3 affected node)
TOKEN ESTIMATE: ~300 tokens. Cached after first call per hypothesis+graph pair.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Behavior                  | Trigger               | Response                       |
|---------------------------|-----------------------|--------------------------------|
| "Explore This Area" click | Click                 | Graph navigates to entry cluster|
| "Compare Approaches"      | Click                 | Prompts for second hypothesis  |
| "Ask About Risks"         | Click                 | Prefills chat with risk query  |
| "Export" button           | Click                 | Adds to export queue           |
| Classification label hover| Hover                 | Tooltip: definition of label   |
| Bar component hover       | Hover                 | Tooltip: what this score means  |
| Affected paper click      | Click                 | Graph zooms to that node        |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
  Loading phases: phase 1 bar fills 0->100% over 1s, then phase 2 activates.
  Score counter: 0 -> final value, 800ms, ease-out (integer increment per frame).
  Bar fill: 0 -> score%, 600ms, ease-out, all 3 bars simultaneously.
  "What would change" items: opacity 0->1, translateY(4px)->0, 200ms each,
    150ms stagger between items.
  Classification label: appears with a brief flash of its color (200ms).

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
  Impact block is role="region" with aria-label="Research Impact Assessment".
  Score announced via aria-live: "Impact score: 74 out of 100, Significant."
  Bars have role="meter" with aria-valuenow, aria-valuemin=0, aria-valuemax=100.
  Classification label tooltip available via aria-describedby.
  Affected papers list items are role="listitem" with descriptive aria-labels.
  "Explore This Area" button has aria-label="Navigate graph to [cluster name]".

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Hypothesis description is very vague ("I want to write about AI")
   Expected: NLP embedding is generic, similarity scores are diffuse.
   Low originality score. Athena suggests: "Your description is broad.
   A more specific hypothesis would yield a more precise impact estimate."
   Implementation: If max similarity score across all graph nodes < 0.40,
   include a specificity_warning in response. Frontend shows the warning.

2. Scenario: Hypothesis describes something almost identical to an existing
   paper in the graph (similarity > 0.90)
   Expected: Athena flags this. "Your described paper is 91% similar to
   [existing paper title]. This may already exist in the field."
   Implementation: Check max cosine similarity in the top result. If > 0.90,
   add a near_duplicate_warning with the matching paper's ID and title.

3. Scenario: Graph has no citation velocity data (all papers from same year)
   Expected: Velocity component defaults to 50 (neutral) with a note
   "Velocity data unavailable (single-year graph)."
   Implementation: If year range < 2 years, set velocity=50, add caveat.

4. Scenario: PageRank delta is effectively zero (hypothetical paper enters
   an isolated subgraph with no structural connections to main component)
   Expected: Structural footprint score near 0. Classification: MARGINAL.
   Athena: "This area is structurally isolated. A paper here would have
   limited propagation through the wider lineage."
   Implementation: sum(abs(delta)) < 0.001 triggers isolated_subgraph
   warning in response.

5. Scenario: User compares two hypotheses back-to-back ("Compare Approaches")
   Expected: Side-by-side display of two impact score cards with a
   "Recommendation: Hypothesis A scores 74 vs Hypothesis B at 52.
   Hypothesis A has higher structural impact."
   Implementation: Frontend stores first hypothesis result. On second
   result receipt, renders both in a two-column layout with delta comparison.

6. Scenario: NLP worker times out during hypothesis embedding
   Expected: 503 response after NLP_WORKER_TIMEOUT. Athena: "Impact
   calculation is taking longer than expected. Try again in a moment."
   No partial results shown.
   Implementation: asyncio.wait_for with NLP_WORKER_TIMEOUT limit.
   NLPTimeoutError caught and returned as 503.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON:
  #001 -- Context-Aware Responses (graph context for calculation)
  #002 -- Rich Response Formatting (score card block rendering)
  #037 -- Graph State Sync (access to current graph topology)

ENABLES:
  Phase H research planning features (publication strategy tools)

ENHANCES:
  #032 -- Paper Recommendation Engine (high-impact areas for reading)
  #013 -- Research Question Generator (questions in high-impact areas)

CONFLICTS:
  NONE

EXTERNAL:
  Requires networkx for in-memory PageRank simulation (already in requirements.txt).
  Requires NLP worker /encode_batch (1 text per request).
  Requires paper_embeddings table to be populated for graph nodes.
  Requires GapFinder from backend/gap_finder.py (Phase 3+).
  Requires ArivuLLMClient for narrative generation (Phase 3+).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] POST /api/athena/impact-calc returns overall_score, components, confidence
[] Three-scenario simulation (conservative, central, aggressive) runs correctly
[] Structural footprint computed via PageRank delta simulation
[] Velocity score computed from citation year distribution CAGR
[] Originality score computed via GapFinder comparison
[] Weighted composite (0.4/0.35/0.25) computed and returned
[] Confidence interval (low, central, high) returned
[] Top 3 affected nodes identified by absolute PageRank delta
[] LLM narrative generated for "what would change" (3 bullets)
[] Specificity warning returned when max similarity < 0.40
[] Near-duplicate warning returned when max similarity > 0.90

VISUAL:
[] Three-phase loading progress bar animates sequentially
[] Score counter animates from 0 to final value (800ms)
[] Component bars animate simultaneously (600ms, ease-out)
[] Classification label (TRANSFORMATIVE/SIGNIFICANT/etc.) displayed
[] Confidence interval shown as "score +/- N (low-high)"
[] "What would change" items stagger in 150ms apart

PERFORMANCE:
[] Graph load + PageRank computation under 2 seconds
[] NLP embedding call under 3 seconds (NLP worker)
[] Total calculation time under 6 seconds end-to-end
[] LLM narrative cached -- repeat calls instant

ACCESSIBILITY:
[] role="region" on impact block
[] Score announced via aria-live on render
[] Bars have role="meter" semantics
[] All buttons have descriptive aria-labels
[] Classification tooltip available via keyboard

EDGE CASES:
[] Vague hypothesis triggers specificity warning
[] Near-duplicate detection working (> 0.90 similarity threshold)
[] Single-year graph uses velocity fallback (score=50 + caveat)
[] Isolated subgraph gives near-zero structural score + warning
[] Side-by-side comparison renders correctly for 2 hypotheses
[] NLP timeout returns 503 with friendly message

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round -- ZERO issues
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified in Chrome
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-impact.js (score card renderer, loading phases, action buttons),
   athena-blocks.js (impact_assessment block type), app.py
   (/api/athena/impact-calc route), backend computation logic
   (PageRank delta, velocity CAGR, originality via gap_finder)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Specific hypothesis on large graph -- verify score breakdown
   b. Vague hypothesis -- verify specificity warning appears
   c. Near-duplicate hypothesis (> 0.90 similarity) -- verify warning
   d. NLP worker offline -- verify 503 with friendly message, no crash
   e. "Compare Approaches" -- verify side-by-side display
   f. Click "Explore This Area" -- verify graph navigates to cluster
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. NetworkX graph built from stale DB data (not current graph state)
   b. PageRank normalization error (scores don't sum to 1.0 after adding node)
   c. Velocity CAGR divides by zero (all papers same year)
   d. Confidence interval inverted (conservative > aggressive)
   e. LLM narrative returned as single string instead of array of 3 bullets
5. VERIFY no regression in: gap_finder.py, llm_client caching, NLP worker calls
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below

PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
