STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase F feature. Core calculation is deterministic graph math
                  (no LLM). LLM used only for narrative. NetworkX required.
                  NLP worker required for hypothesis embedding.
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Session Summary generates a comprehensive, structured recap of everything the
user explored during their Athena session. When a user has spent 30 minutes
exploring a lineage, discussing papers, running pruning simulations, and asking
questions, they should not have to mentally reconstruct what they learned. Athena
compiles it automatically: which papers were discussed, what insights were
discovered, what graph operations were performed, what contradictions were found,
and what questions remain unanswered.

This feature exists because research sessions produce knowledge that is easily
lost. A researcher explores a lineage, has several insights, then closes the
browser. Without a summary, those insights exist only in the chat history, which
is difficult to scan. Session Summary distills the entire session into a
structured document that can be exported, shared, or referenced later.

The summary is generated on demand (user clicks "Summarize session" or types
/summary) or automatically when the user has been idle for 5+ minutes after
at least 10 messages. It uses the GROQ_SMART_MODEL (70b) because summary quality
matters more than speed here. The prompt includes the conversation summary from
memory (#006), the list of papers discussed (from metadata), graph operations
performed (from action_log), and key insights generated.

The output is a structured document with sections: Overview (1-2 sentences),
Papers Explored (list with one-line descriptions of why each was discussed),
Key Insights (numbered list of discoveries), Graph Operations (pruning results,
comparisons), Open Questions (topics raised but not fully explored), and
Suggested Next Steps (based on gaps in the exploration).

What makes this unique to Athena: the summary references COMPUTED DATA, not
just conversation topics. "You discovered that removing VGGNet collapses 18%
of the lineage, compared to 31% for ResNet" is a data-driven summary point
that no generic chatbot can produce. The summary is grounded in the actual
graph analysis, not LLM memory of the conversation.

The summary renders as a special full-width message block with a distinct header
("SESSION SUMMARY") and a subtle gold top border. It can be collapsed/expanded.
An export button allows saving it as Markdown, PDF, or copying to clipboard.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User types /summary in chat, or clicks "Summarize" in the Athena header menu,
or Athena auto-suggests after 5+ minutes of idle with 10+ messages in session.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types /summary. A system message appears: "Compiling session summary..."
   with a progress indicator showing sections being generated.

2. Within 5-8 seconds, the summary renders as a structured block:
   SESSION SUMMARY header with gold border, then Overview, Papers Explored,
   Key Insights, Graph Operations, Open Questions, Suggested Next Steps.

3. Each section is collapsible. Papers Explored shows paper titles as clickable
   links that highlight the node in the graph. Key Insights are numbered.

4. An action bar at the bottom offers: [Copy to Clipboard] [Export Markdown]
   [Export PDF] [Share Link].

5. The summary stays in the chat as a permanent message. If the user continues
   the conversation and runs /summary again later, a new updated summary is
   generated reflecting the additional exploration.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Summary is visible in chat. User can export or continue chatting. The summary
message is stored in chat_history with type='summary' metadata.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-summary.js: Summary generation trigger, rendering, export.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: /summary command recognition, routing.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: SummaryBlock renderer with collapsible sections.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Summary block styles, gold header, section toggles.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/summary endpoint that compiles context and calls LLM.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
Summary rendering uses a dedicated block type:

  class SummaryBlock {
    render(data) {
      return `
        <div class="block block-summary">
          <div class="summary-header">● SESSION SUMMARY</div>
          <div class="summary-section" data-section="overview">
            <h4 class="section-toggle">Overview</h4>
            <div class="section-content">${data.overview}</div>
          </div>
          <!-- More sections... -->
          <div class="summary-actions">
            <button class="btn-copy">Copy to Clipboard</button>
            <button class="btn-export" data-format="md">Export Markdown</button>
          </div>
        </div>
      `;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/summary', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_summary():
      session_id = get_session_id()
      messages = db.fetchall(
          "SELECT role, content, metadata FROM chat_history WHERE session_id=%s",
          (session_id,))
      actions = db.fetchall(
          "SELECT action_type, action_data FROM action_log WHERE session_id=%s",
          (session_id,))
      # Compile context
      context = compile_summary_context(messages, actions)
      # Generate with 70b model
      summary = llm_client.generate(config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL, SUMMARY_PROMPT, context)
      return jsonify(summary)

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```python
SUMMARY_PROMPT = """Generate a structured research session summary.
Context: {context}
Output JSON with keys: overview, papers_explored (array of {title, why}),
key_insights (array of strings), graph_operations (array of {type, result}),
open_questions (array of strings), next_steps (array of strings).
Use specific numbers and paper names. No em dashes. No emojis."""
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/summary
Request: { "session_id": "..." }
Response: {
  "overview": "You explored the ResNet lineage...",
  "papers_explored": [{"title": "ResNet", "why": "Highest structural impact"}],
  "key_insights": ["Removing ResNet collapses 31% of the lineage"],
  "graph_operations": [{"type": "prune", "result": "47/152 collapsed"}],
  "open_questions": ["How does the attention cluster connect to CNNs?"],
  "next_steps": ["Explore the bridging papers between clusters"]
}

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Summary Rendered

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌─ ● SESSION SUMMARY ────────────────┐  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │ ▼ Overview                          │  │
  │  │   You explored the ResNet lineage   │  │
  │  │   (601 nodes) over 12 exchanges...  │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │ ▼ Papers Explored (5)               │  │
  │  │   1. ResNet - highest impact (31%)  │  │
  │  │   2. VGGNet - comparison target     │  │
  │  │   3. AlexNet - foundational CNN     │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │ ▼ Key Insights (3)                  │  │
  │  │   1. ResNet collapse: 31%           │  │
  │  │   2. VGGNet collapse: 18%           │  │
  │  │   3. Gap in attention+CNN bridge    │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │ ▶ Graph Operations (collapsed)      │  │
  │  │ ▶ Open Questions (collapsed)        │  │
  │  │ ▶ Suggested Next Steps (collapsed)  │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │ [Copy] [Export MD] [Export PDF]      │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
/summary typed → "Compiling..." message (200ms fadeIn)
Compiling → Summary block: sections render sequentially (100ms stagger)
Section toggle click: content slides open/closed (200ms ease-out)
Export click: toast "Copied to clipboard" or download initiated

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User types /summary
  → athena-engine.js intercepts as command
  → POST /api/athena/summary with session_id
  → Backend loads chat_history + action_log for session
  → Compiles context (papers discussed, operations, topics)
  → Sends to GROQ_SMART_MODEL with structured output prompt
  → Returns JSON with 6 sections
  → Frontend renders SummaryBlock
  → Summary stored in chat_history with metadata {type: 'summary'}

ERROR PATH:
  → No messages in session → "No conversation to summarize yet."
  → LLM timeout → "Summary generation timed out. Try again."
  → Partial LLM output → Render available sections, note missing ones

DATA STORED:
  → chat_history: summary as assistant message with type='summary' metadata
  → action_log: { action: 'summary_generated' }

DATA CACHED:
  → Summary response cached per session (invalidated on new messages)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile)
STAGE: On-demand generation (not part of regular chat pipeline)
PROMPT: Structured JSON output with 6 sections (see KEY CODE PATTERNS above)
OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with overview, papers_explored, key_insights, graph_operations, open_questions, next_steps
FALLBACK: If LLM unavailable, generate rule-based summary from metadata only (list of papers discussed, operations performed, no prose)
TOKEN BUDGET: ~800 context + ~600 output = ~1400 tokens per summary

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Section header (▼/▶) | Click      | Toggle section collapse/expand               |
| Paper title link     | Click       | Highlights paper node in graph               |
| [Copy to Clipboard]  | Click       | Copies Markdown summary to clipboard, toast  |
| [Export Markdown]    | Click       | Downloads .md file                           |
| [Export PDF]         | Click       | Downloads .pdf file                          |
| Insight number       | Hover       | Tooltip with source message reference        |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Section toggle: maxHeight transition 200ms ease-out
- Summary block entrance: fadeIn + slideUp 300ms
- Action buttons: standard hover state (darken 10%)

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Section toggles use aria-expanded and aria-controls
- Summary has role="region" with aria-label="Session Summary"
- Export buttons have descriptive aria-labels
- Collapsible sections keyboard accessible (Enter/Space to toggle)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User runs /summary with only 2 messages exchanged
   Expected: Minimal summary: "Brief session with 2 exchanges about [topic]."
   No empty sections. Sections with no data are omitted.
   Implementation: Check message count, simplify prompt for short sessions.

2. Scenario: User runs /summary twice in the same session
   Expected: Second summary includes everything from the first plus new activity.
   Previous summary message stays in chat (not replaced).
   Implementation: Each /summary generates fresh from full session data.

3. Scenario: LLM returns malformed JSON
   Expected: Parse what is available, render partial summary with note.
   Implementation: Try/catch JSON parse, fallback to regex extraction.

4. Scenario: Session has 200+ messages (very long session)
   Expected: Summary context is truncated to fit LLM context window.
   Use conversation summaries from memory (#006) instead of raw messages.
   Implementation: Use memory summaries + metadata, not full message text.

5. Scenario: Export PDF when summary contains special characters
   Expected: PDF renders correctly with UTF-8 support.
   Implementation: Use existing ExportGenerator PDF pipeline.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON:
  #006 — Conversation Memory (message history, summaries)
  #001 — Context-Aware Responses (graph data for summary context)
  #037 — Streaming Text (summary can stream if long)

ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #135 (Evolving Summary Bar), #082 (Export as Document)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: action_log table, chat_history table, GROQ_SMART_MODEL

PERFORMANCE:

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #018: DEBATE MODE
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 22
OVERLAPS WITH:   #035 (Peer Discussion Simulator), #029 (Socratic Mode)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Formatting), #006 (Memory), #037 (Streaming), #049 (Mode Selector)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Debate Mode transforms Athena into a structured academic debate simulator where
two opposing perspectives argue about a research question using evidence from the
actual citation graph. The user poses a debate topic (e.g., "Are residual
connections fundamentally necessary for deep learning?") and Athena generates two
AI personas: one arguing FOR and one arguing AGAINST. Each persona cites specific
papers from the lineage, references computed metrics, and builds arguments grounded
in the graph data.

This feature exists because academic understanding deepens through adversarial
reasoning. A researcher who only reads supporting evidence develops blind spots.
Debate Mode forces confrontation with counter-arguments, using the lineage data
to ensure both sides are grounded in real citations rather than hypothetical
reasoning. When the FOR side says "ResNet's 182K citations prove residual
connections are essential," the AGAINST side counters with "But 67% of those
citations are architectural, not conceptual, and the attention mechanism cluster
achieved comparable depth without residual connections."

The debate follows a structured format: Opening Statement (FOR), Opening Statement
(AGAINST), Rebuttal (FOR), Rebuttal (AGAINST), Closing Summary. Each round is
generated as a separate LLM call with the previous rounds as context, creating a
genuinely evolving argument. The user can interject at any point to steer the
debate or ask a persona to elaborate on a specific claim.

Debate personas are visually distinct: FOR uses a blue left border, AGAINST uses
a red left border. Each claim that references a paper shows the paper as a
clickable inline citation. Computed metrics (PageRank, pruning impact, citation
count) are rendered as inline stat badges rather than raw numbers. The debate
concludes with a "Verdict" section that summarizes the strongest arguments from
each side without declaring a winner (the researcher decides).

What makes this unique: both debate sides use REAL DATA from the graph. When FOR
claims "ResNet is the most structurally critical paper," it cites PageRank #3
position and 31% pruning impact. When AGAINST counters, it uses actual alternative
ancestry paths and survival rates from the pruning simulation. This is not generic
argumentation; it is data-driven dialectic.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User activates via mode selector (#049) choosing "Debate Mode," types /debate
[topic], or Athena detects a debatable claim and suggests "Want to see both sides?"

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types /debate Are residual connections necessary for deep learning?
   System message appears: "Setting up debate: FOR vs AGAINST residual connections."

2. FOR persona opens (blue border): "Residual connections represent a fundamental
   architectural innovation. In this lineage, ResNet (PageRank #3, 31% structural
   impact) anchors 23 descendant papers..." (with inline paper citations)

3. AGAINST persona responds (red border): "While impactful, residual connections
   are one approach among many. The attention mechanism cluster (12 papers) achieved
   comparable network depth through a fundamentally different mechanism..."

4. FOR rebuttal: "The attention cluster still relies on skip connections, which
   ARE residual connections by another name. 8 of those 12 papers cite ResNet..."

5. AGAINST rebuttal with counter-data from the graph.

6. Verdict section (gold border): Summarizes strongest arguments from each side.

7. User can interject at any point: "Can the FOR side address VGGNet specifically?"

AFTER COMPLETION:
Debate stored in conversation history. User can export as document or continue
with follow-up questions about specific claims.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-debate.js: Debate orchestrator. Round management. Persona
  rendering. Interjection handling. Verdict generation.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: /debate command recognition, mode activation.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: DebateRound block with persona styling.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: FOR (blue) and AGAINST (red) border styles,
  verdict (gold) styling, inline citation badges.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/debate endpoint with round-by-round generation.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
Debate orchestrator manages round sequence:

  class AthenaDebate {
    constructor(topic, graphContext) {
      this.topic = topic;
      this.rounds = [];
      this.currentRound = 0;
      this.sequence = ['for_open', 'against_open', 'for_rebuttal', 'against_rebuttal', 'verdict'];
    }
    async runNextRound() {
      const roundType = this.sequence[this.currentRound];
      const response = await fetch('/api/athena/debate', {
        method: 'POST',
        body: JSON.stringify({ topic: this.topic, round: roundType, history: this.rounds })
      });
      const data = await response.json();
      this.rounds.push(data);
      this.currentRound++;
      renderDebateRound(data);
      if (this.currentRound < this.sequence.length) {
        showContinueButton(); // "Continue debate" or "Interject"
      }
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
Each round is a separate LLM call with accumulated debate history:

  @app.route('/api/athena/debate', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_debate():
      data = request.json
      graph_context = get_graph_summary_for_chat(load_graph(data['graph_id']))
      prompt = build_debate_prompt(data['topic'], data['round'], data['history'], graph_context)
      response = llm_client.generate(config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL, prompt)
      return jsonify(parse_debate_response(response))

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```python
def build_debate_prompt(topic, round_type, history, context):
    persona = "FOR" if "for" in round_type else "AGAINST" if "against" in round_type else "NEUTRAL"
    return f"""You are debating: {topic}
Your position: {persona}
Graph context: {context}
Previous rounds: {json.dumps(history)}
Write a {round_type} argument using specific papers and metrics from the graph.
Cite papers by title. Reference PageRank positions and pruning impacts.
No em dashes. No emojis. 150-250 words."""
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/debate
Request: { "topic": "...", "round": "for_open", "history": [...], "graph_id": "..." }
Response: { "persona": "FOR", "content": "...", "citations": [{"paper_id": "...", "title": "..."}], "metrics_used": ["pagerank", "pruning_impact"] }

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Debate In Progress

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ○ DEBATE: Are residual connections       │
  │    necessary for deep learning?           │
  │                                           │
  │  ┌─ FOR (blue border) ────────────────┐   │
  │  │ Residual connections represent a   │   │
  │  │ fundamental innovation. ResNet     │   │
  │  │ [PageRank #3] anchors 23 papers... │   │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                           │
  │  ┌─ AGAINST (red border) ─────────────┐   │
  │  │ While impactful, the attention     │   │
  │  │ cluster (12 papers) achieved       │   │
  │  │ comparable depth differently...    │   │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                           │
  │  [Continue Debate] [Interject] [End]      │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
Each round: fadeIn 300ms with left border color establishing persona
Between rounds: 500ms pause, "Continue" button or auto-advance option
Verdict: gold border, wider block, conclusive tone

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User types /debate [topic]
  → Debate orchestrator initializes with topic + graph context
  → POST /api/athena/debate { round: 'for_open' }
  → LLM generates FOR opening with graph citations
  → Rendered with blue border, inline paper links
  → User clicks "Continue" or debate auto-advances
  → POST /api/athena/debate { round: 'against_open', history: [round1] }
  → Continues through all 5 rounds
  → Verdict rendered with gold border
  → All rounds stored in chat_history

ERROR PATH: LLM timeout on any round → "Debate paused. Click to retry this round."
DATA STORED: chat_history with metadata { type: 'debate', round: '...', persona: '...' }
DATA CACHED: Each round cached to avoid re-generation on page issues

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (70b) for all rounds (quality matters)
STAGE: Round-by-round generation (5 sequential LLM calls)
PROMPT: Position-aware with accumulated history (see KEY CODE PATTERNS)
OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with persona, content, citations array, metrics_used
FALLBACK: If 70b unavailable, fall back to 8b with quality warning
TOKEN BUDGET: ~500 context + ~300 output per round x 5 rounds = ~4000 total

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| [Continue Debate]    | Click       | Generates next round                        |
| [Interject]          | Click       | Opens input for user to steer debate         |
| [End Debate]         | Click       | Jumps to verdict round                      |
| Inline paper citation| Click       | Highlights paper in graph                   |
| Stat badge           | Hover       | Tooltip with metric explanation              |
| Round block          | Hover       | Subtle elevation increase                   |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Round blocks fadeIn 300ms, blue/red border animates width 0 to 3px over 200ms
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Persona labels as aria-label, citations as links with paper titles, debate rounds numbered for screen readers

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Topic has no clear FOR/AGAINST framing
   Expected: Athena reformulates: "I'll frame this as [specific claim]. FOR: [position]. AGAINST: [position]. Does this framing work?"
   Implementation: Pre-debate framing step with user confirmation.

2. Scenario: Graph has only 5 papers (insufficient data for rich debate)
   Expected: Debate proceeds but with a note: "Limited data (5 papers). Arguments may be less evidence-rich."
   Implementation: Check node count, adjust prompt expectations.

3. Scenario: User interjects with new evidence mid-debate
   Expected: Next round incorporates user's point. "The user raises a valid point about [X]. Addressing this..."
   Implementation: User interjection appended to debate history before next round.

4. Scenario: LLM generates same argument for both sides
   Expected: Deduplication check. If >60% similarity between FOR and AGAINST, re-prompt AGAINST with explicit differentiation instruction.
   Implementation: Cosine similarity check on output text.

5. Scenario: User requests debate on non-research topic
   Expected: "Debate Mode works best with research questions related to the current lineage. Try asking about a specific methodology, paper, or finding."
   Implementation: Topic validation against graph context.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #001, #002, #006, #037, #049 (Mode Selector)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #029 (Socratic Mode), #035 (Peer Discussion Simulator)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: GROQ_SMART_MODEL, graph data

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ /debate command initiates structured debate
□ 5 rounds generated sequentially (FOR open, AGAINST open, FOR rebuttal, AGAINST rebuttal, Verdict)
□ Each round cites specific papers from the graph
□ Each round references computed metrics (PageRank, pruning impact)
□ User can interject between rounds
□ User can end debate early (jumps to verdict)
□ Inline paper citations clickable (highlight in graph)
□ Debate stored in conversation history

VISUAL:
□ FOR rounds have blue left border, AGAINST have red, Verdict has gold
□ Inline stat badges for metrics
□ Clear persona labels on each round

PERFORMANCE:
□ Each round generates within 5 seconds
□ Full debate completes within 30 seconds

ACCESSIBILITY:
□ Persona labels announced by screen reader
□ Round numbers for sequential navigation
□ Citations are accessible links

EDGE CASES:
□ Non-research topics rejected gracefully
□ Small graphs produce reduced-evidence debate with note
□ Duplicate arguments detected and re-prompted

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
□ BROWSER TEST: Verified
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. READ all implementation files 2. COMPARE against Done Criteria 3. TEST with research and non-research topics 4. CHECK for duplicate arguments, citation accuracy 5. VERIFY no regression 6. Assumes BROKEN 7. 1+ issue per round 8. Records below

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #019: HYPOTHESIS TESTER
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           F
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #013 (Research Question Generator), #145 (What-If Calculator)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Formatting), #005 (Graph Commands), #037 (Streaming)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Hypothesis Tester allows users to propose a research hypothesis and have Athena
evaluate it against the evidence in the citation graph. The user states something
like "I hypothesize that attention mechanisms made RNNs obsolete" and Athena
systematically checks this claim against the graph data: Are there papers that
use both attention and RNNs? Does the citation velocity of RNN papers decline
after the attention mechanism papers appeared? Do RNN papers still receive
citations from recent (post-2017) papers? What percentage of the lineage uses
attention versus RNNs?

This feature exists because hypotheses should be tested against data, not just
debated philosophically. Arivu has the unique advantage of having computed
structural metrics for the entire lineage. A hypothesis about paper importance
can be tested against PageRank and pruning impact. A hypothesis about methodology
trends can be tested against cluster composition and temporal citation patterns.
A hypothesis about a paper's influence can be tested against descendant counts
and mutation type distributions.

The evaluation produces a structured "Evidence Report" with three sections:
Supporting Evidence (data points that support the hypothesis), Contradicting
Evidence (data points that contradict it), and Confidence Assessment (overall
evaluation from STRONG SUPPORT through INCONCLUSIVE to STRONG CONTRADICTION,
with a numerical score 0-100). Each evidence point links to the specific paper
or metric it references.

What makes this unique to Athena: the hypothesis test uses COMPUTED GRAPH METRICS,
not LLM world knowledge. When testing "attention mechanisms made RNNs obsolete,"
Athena counts actual papers in each cluster, computes citation velocity trends
from year-over-year data in the graph, and checks for recent RNN citations. The
LLM interprets and narrates these computed facts but does not invent them.

The LLM (70b) receives the hypothesis plus pre-computed evidence data and generates
the narrative evaluation. The evidence data is assembled server-side from graph
analysis: cluster membership counts, temporal citation distributions, mutation
type frequencies, and structural metrics. This ensures the evaluation is grounded.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User types /hypothesis [claim] or "test this hypothesis: [claim]" in chat.
Also accessible via Research Question Generator (#013) "Test" button.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types: /hypothesis Attention mechanisms made RNNs obsolete in NLP
2. System message: "Testing hypothesis against lineage data..."
3. Backend computes evidence: cluster analysis, temporal patterns, citation data
4. Athena streams the Evidence Report as a structured block:
   - HYPOTHESIS: (restated clearly)
   - SUPPORTING EVIDENCE: (3-5 data points with paper citations)
   - CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE: (3-5 data points)
   - CONFIDENCE: [MODERATE SUPPORT - 62/100]
5. Each evidence point has a clickable paper reference
6. Quick actions: "Refine hypothesis," "Explore supporting papers," "Test opposite"

AFTER COMPLETION:
Evidence Report stored in chat. User can refine and re-test.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-hypothesis.js: Hypothesis input, evidence report rendering.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: /hypothesis command routing.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: EvidenceReportBlock renderer.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Evidence report styles, confidence meter.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/hypothesis endpoint.
- backend/graph_engine.py: compute_hypothesis_evidence() method.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/hypothesis', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_hypothesis():
      hypothesis = request.json['hypothesis']
      graph_data = load_graph(request.json['graph_id'])
      # Compute evidence from graph data (no LLM here)
      evidence = compute_hypothesis_evidence(graph_data, hypothesis)
      # LLM interprets and narrates the evidence
      report = llm_client.generate(config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL,
          HYPOTHESIS_PROMPT.format(hypothesis=hypothesis, evidence=json.dumps(evidence)))
      return jsonify(report)

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```python
def compute_hypothesis_evidence(graph_data, hypothesis):
    nodes = graph_data['nodes']
    edges = graph_data['edges']
    # Cluster analysis
    clusters = group_by_cluster(nodes)
    # Temporal citation patterns
    yearly_counts = count_by_year(nodes)
    # Mutation type distribution
    mutation_dist = count_mutation_types(edges)
    # PageRank top 10
    top_nodes = sorted(nodes, key=lambda n: n.get('pagerank', 0), reverse=True)[:10]
    return {
        'cluster_sizes': {k: len(v) for k, v in clusters.items()},
        'yearly_counts': yearly_counts,
        'mutation_distribution': mutation_dist,
        'top_nodes': [{'title': n['title'], 'pagerank': n.get('pagerank')} for n in top_nodes],
        'total_nodes': len(nodes), 'total_edges': len(edges)
    }
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/hypothesis
Request: { "hypothesis": "...", "graph_id": "..." }
Response: {
  "hypothesis_restated": "...",
  "supporting": [{"point": "...", "paper_id": "...", "metric": "..."}],
  "contradicting": [{"point": "...", "paper_id": "...", "metric": "..."}],
  "confidence": { "label": "MODERATE SUPPORT", "score": 62, "explanation": "..." }
}

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Evidence Report

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌─ EVIDENCE REPORT ──────────────────┐   │
  │  │ HYPOTHESIS: Attention mechanisms    │   │
  │  │ made RNNs obsolete in NLP           │   │
  │  │                                     │   │
  │  │ ● SUPPORTING (green left)           │   │
  │  │   1. Attention cluster: 12 papers   │   │
  │  │      vs RNN cluster: 4 papers       │   │
  │  │   2. Post-2017 papers: 89% use      │   │
  │  │      attention, 11% use RNNs        │   │
  │  │                                     │   │
  │  │ ○ CONTRADICTING (red left)          │   │
  │  │   1. RNN papers still cited: 3 in   │   │
  │  │      last 2 years of lineage        │   │
  │  │   2. Hybrid attention+RNN papers    │   │
  │  │      exist (2 papers)               │   │
  │  │                                     │   │
  │  │ CONFIDENCE: ████████░░ 62/100       │   │
  │  │ MODERATE SUPPORT                    │   │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Report sections fade in sequentially (150ms stagger). Confidence bar fills from 0 to score over 1s.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User types /hypothesis [claim]
  → athena-engine.js routes to hypothesis handler
  → POST /api/athena/hypothesis { hypothesis, graph_id }
  → Backend: compute_hypothesis_evidence() extracts graph metrics
  → Backend: LLM interprets evidence, generates narrative report
  → Response: structured JSON with supporting, contradicting, confidence
  → Frontend: EvidenceReportBlock renders with paper links and confidence bar
  → Stored in chat_history with type='hypothesis'

ERROR PATH: Non-testable hypothesis → "This hypothesis is too broad for graph analysis. Try narrowing to specific papers or methods."
DATA STORED: chat_history with hypothesis metadata
DATA CACHED: Evidence computation cached per graph_id + hypothesis hash

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (70b)
STAGE: Post-evidence computation (LLM narrates pre-computed data)
PROMPT: "Evaluate this hypothesis against the evidence data. Classify supporting and contradicting points. Assign confidence 0-100. Output JSON."
OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with supporting array, contradicting array, confidence object
FALLBACK: If LLM unavailable, show raw evidence data without narrative
TOKEN BUDGET: ~600 context + ~500 output = ~1100 tokens

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Evidence point       | Click paper | Highlights paper in graph                   |
| Confidence bar       | Hover       | Tooltip with detailed explanation            |
| [Refine hypothesis]  | Click       | Opens input pre-filled with current hypothesis|
| [Test opposite]      | Click       | Tests negation of current hypothesis         |
| [Explore supporting] | Click       | Discusses supporting papers in detail        |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Confidence bar fill animation 1s ease-out, sections stagger 150ms
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Confidence score as aria-valuenow, evidence points numbered, paper links as standard anchors

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Hypothesis references papers not in the lineage
   Expected: "I can only test hypotheses against papers in the current lineage. [paper] is not in this graph."
   Implementation: Validate paper references against graph nodes.

2. Scenario: No supporting OR contradicting evidence found
   Expected: Confidence = INCONCLUSIVE. "The current lineage does not contain sufficient data to evaluate this hypothesis."
   Implementation: Check evidence array lengths, use INCONCLUSIVE when both empty.

3. Scenario: Hypothesis is a tautology ("papers with more citations are more cited")
   Expected: Athena detects trivially true claims: "This hypothesis is definitionally true and not testable."
   Implementation: LLM classification step before evidence computation.

4. Scenario: Very long hypothesis (500+ characters)
   Expected: Athena reformulates: "Let me restate this more concisely: [reformulation]. Is this correct?"
   Implementation: If hypothesis > 300 chars, reformulate first.

5. Scenario: User tests same hypothesis twice
   Expected: Cached result returned instantly with note "Same hypothesis tested earlier."
   Implementation: Cache key = hash(hypothesis + graph_id).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #001, #002, #005, #037
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #013 (Research Question Generator), #145 (What-If Calculator)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Graph data, GROQ_SMART_MODEL

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ /hypothesis command triggers evidence-based evaluation
□ Evidence computed from actual graph data (clusters, temporal, metrics)
□ LLM narrates pre-computed evidence (does not invent data)
□ Supporting and contradicting evidence sections populated
□ Confidence score 0-100 with label (STRONG SUPPORT to STRONG CONTRADICTION)
□ Paper citations clickable and linked to graph
□ Quick actions for refinement and follow-up

VISUAL:
□ Green left border for supporting, red for contradicting
□ Confidence bar with fill animation
□ Structured report with clear sections

PERFORMANCE: □ Evidence computation < 2s, LLM narration < 5s
ACCESSIBILITY: □ Confidence as aria-valuenow, evidence numbered
EDGE CASES: □ Non-testable hypotheses rejected, tautologies detected

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
□ BROWSER TEST: Verified
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. READ all files 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST various hypotheses 4. CHECK evidence accuracy 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN until proven 7. 1+ issue/round 8. Log below

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #020: CITATION SENTENCE VIEWER
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           C
ESTIMATED HOURS: 12
OVERLAPS WITH:   #026 (Paper Relationship Explainer), #004 (Discuss Integration)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Formatting), #037 (Streaming)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Citation Sentence Viewer displays the exact sentences where one paper cites
another, directly within the Athena chat interface. When discussing an edge in
the graph, Athena shows the citing sentence and cited sentence that form the
basis of the mutation type classification. This transforms abstract edge labels
("methodological adoption") into concrete textual evidence that the user can
read and evaluate.

This feature exists because trust in AI classifications requires evidence.
When Athena says "Paper A adopted Paper B's methodology," the researcher wants
to see the actual sentence that supports this claim. The citation sentences are
already computed and stored in the edge_analysis table (citing_sentence and
cited_sentence fields from the NLP pipeline). This feature surfaces them in
context.

The viewer renders as an inline block within Athena messages whenever an edge
is discussed. It shows: the citing sentence (with the citation reference
highlighted), the cited sentence (the passage being referenced), the mutation
type badge, the confidence tier badge, and the text sources (which section each
sentence came from: methods, abstract, title). The two sentences are displayed
in a side-by-side layout in Split/Full mode or stacked in Panel mode.

What makes this unique to Athena: these are not generic citation contexts pulled
from a database. These are the SPECIFIC sentence pairs that the NLP pipeline
identified as the highest-similarity match between the two papers. The similarity
score, mutation type, and confidence tier are computed values that the user can
interrogate. If they disagree, they can flag the classification (connecting to
the existing edge_feedback system).

The viewer also integrates with the edge hover system in graph.js. When a user
hovers over an edge in the graph for 2+ seconds, Athena can proactively show
the citation sentences as a suggestion (#009). When discussing edges via Feature
#004 (Discuss Integration), citation sentences are automatically included in the
seeded conversation.

The text_tier of each paper determines what is available: tier 1 (full text)
provides method-section sentences, tier 2 (intro) provides introduction sentences,
tier 3 (abstract) provides abstract sentences, tier 4 (title only) provides no
sentences. When sentences are unavailable (tier 4), the viewer shows "Citation
sentence unavailable (title-only data)" with a muted style.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User asks about an edge ("how does ResNet relate to VGGNet?"), clicks an edge in
the graph and selects "Discuss with Athena," or Athena includes citation sentences
in a response about paper relationships. Also via /citation [paper A] [paper B].

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "How does ResNet cite VGGNet?"
2. Athena identifies the edge between ResNet and VGGNet in the graph.
3. Response includes a CitationSentenceBlock:

   CITING (ResNet, methods section):
   "Following the deep architecture of Simonyan and Zisserman [VGGNet],
    we introduce shortcut connections that..."

   CITED (VGGNet, abstract):
   "We investigate the effect of convolutional network depth on accuracy
    in large-scale image recognition..."

   Classification: GENERALIZATION | Confidence: HIGH (0.87)
   [Flag if incorrect]

4. Below the citation block, Athena provides prose analysis of what this
   specific citation relationship means for the lineage.

5. User can click "Flag if incorrect" to submit disagreement feedback to the
   edge_feedback table, connecting to the existing trust system.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Citation sentences visible in chat. User can discuss further or flag for review.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: CitationSentenceBlock renderer with side-by-side
  or stacked layout depending on panel width.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Edge detection in user messages, citation data fetch.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Citation block styles, source labels, confidence badges.
- app.py: GET /api/edge/<edge_id>/citation endpoint returning sentence data.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/edge/<edge_id>/citation')
  def get_edge_citation(edge_id):
      row = db.fetchone(
          "SELECT citing_sentence, cited_sentence, citing_text_source, "
          "cited_text_source, mutation_type, mutation_confidence, citation_intent "
          "FROM edge_analysis WHERE edge_id=%s", (edge_id,))
      if not row: return jsonify({'error': 'Edge not found'}), 404
      return jsonify({
          'citing_sentence': row['citing_sentence'],
          'cited_sentence': row['cited_sentence'],
          'citing_source': row['citing_text_source'],
          'cited_source': row['cited_text_source'],
          'mutation_type': row['mutation_type'],
          'confidence': row['mutation_confidence'],
          'confidence_tier': get_confidence_tier(row['mutation_confidence']),
          'citation_intent': row['citation_intent']
      })

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class CitationSentenceBlock {
  render(data) {
    const isWide = document.querySelector('.athena-panel-container').offsetWidth > 500;
    return `
      <div class="block block-citation ${isWide ? 'side-by-side' : 'stacked'}">
        <div class="citation-citing">
          <div class="citation-label">CITING (${data.citing_paper}, ${data.citing_source})</div>
          <blockquote>${data.citing_sentence || 'Unavailable (title-only data)'}</blockquote>
        </div>
        <div class="citation-cited">
          <div class="citation-label">CITED (${data.cited_paper}, ${data.cited_source})</div>
          <blockquote>${data.cited_sentence || 'Unavailable (title-only data)'}</blockquote>
        </div>
        <div class="citation-meta">
          <span class="badge badge-mutation">${data.mutation_type}</span>
          <span class="badge badge-confidence badge-${data.confidence_tier.toLowerCase()}">${data.confidence_tier}</span>
          <button class="btn-flag" data-edge="${data.edge_id}">Flag if incorrect</button>
        </div>
      </div>`;
  }
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
GET /api/edge/{edge_id}/citation
Response: { "citing_sentence": "...", "cited_sentence": "...", "citing_source": "methods", "cited_source": "abstract", "mutation_type": "generalization", "confidence": 0.87, "confidence_tier": "HIGH", "citation_intent": "methodological_adoption" }

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Citation Block (Wide/Side-by-Side)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌─ CITATION ───────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  CITING (ResNet, methods) │ CITED (VGGNet,   │ │
  │  │  "Following the deep     │ abstract)         │ │
  │  │  architecture of          │ "We investigate   │ │
  │  │  Simonyan and Zisserman  │ the effect of     │ │
  │  │  [VGGNet], we introduce  │ convolutional     │ │
  │  │  shortcut connections..."│ network depth..." │ │
  │  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────── │ │
  │  │  [GENERALIZATION] [HIGH 0.87] [Flag]         │ │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Citation Block (Narrow/Stacked)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌─ CITATION ─────────────────┐   │
  │  │ CITING (ResNet, methods)   │   │
  │  │ "Following the deep arch-  │   │
  │  │ itecture of Simonyan..."   │   │
  │  │                            │   │
  │  │ CITED (VGGNet, abstract)   │   │
  │  │ "We investigate the effect │   │
  │  │ of convolutional network..." │  │
  │  │                            │   │
  │  │ [GENERALIZATION] [HIGH]    │   │
  │  │ [Flag if incorrect]        │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────┘   │
  └───────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Block fadeIn 200ms. Flag button → confirmation dialog → toast "Feedback submitted."

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User asks about edge relationship
  → athena-engine.js detects edge reference in message
  → Resolves edge_id from paper names
  → GET /api/edge/{edge_id}/citation
  → Backend reads from edge_analysis table
  → Returns sentence pair + classification data
  → CitationSentenceBlock renders in Athena chat
  → User optionally flags → POST /api/edge/flag (existing endpoint)

ERROR PATH: Edge not found → "I could not find a direct citation link between those papers."
  No sentences available (tier 4) → Shows "Unavailable" with muted style
DATA STORED: Feedback stored in edge_feedback table
DATA CACHED: Citation data cached in sessionStorage per edge_id

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL for edge identification from user message (when not
explicit). No LLM needed for citation display itself (data from DB).
STAGE: Pre-fetch (identify which edge) then direct DB lookup (no LLM for content)
PROMPT: Only if user's reference is ambiguous: "Which edge is the user asking about? Papers: [list]"
OUTPUT: Edge identification only
FALLBACK: If edge cannot be resolved, ask user to click the edge in graph
TOKEN BUDGET: ~100 tokens for edge identification (only when ambiguous)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Citation block       | Visible     | Renders inline in Athena response            |
| Paper name in label  | Click       | Highlights paper in graph                   |
| Mutation badge       | Hover       | Tooltip explaining mutation type             |
| Confidence badge     | Hover       | Tooltip: "HIGH: 75%+ confidence"            |
| [Flag if incorrect]  | Click       | Opens flag dialog with reason field          |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Block fadeIn 200ms, badges slide in 100ms stagger
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Blockquotes with proper cite attributes, badges as aria-labels, flag button descriptive label

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Both papers are tier 4 (no sentences available)
   Expected: Block shows "Citation sentences unavailable for both papers (title-only data). Classification based on structural analysis only."
   Implementation: Check both sentence fields for null/empty.

2. Scenario: Edge has been flagged by user previously
   Expected: Block shows "You flagged this classification" badge next to Flag button.
   Implementation: Check edge_feedback for session_id + edge_id.

3. Scenario: User asks about papers with no direct edge
   Expected: "These papers are not directly connected. The shortest path between them passes through [intermediary papers]."
   Implementation: If edge_id not found, compute shortest path.

4. Scenario: Citing sentence is very long (200+ characters)
   Expected: Truncated with "Show more" toggle. Full sentence on expand.
   Implementation: CSS text-overflow with JS toggle.

5. Scenario: Multiple edges between papers (rare but possible with deduplication)
   Expected: Show the primary edge (highest confidence). Note: "Multiple citation contexts found. Showing highest confidence."
   Implementation: ORDER BY mutation_confidence DESC LIMIT 1.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #001, #002, #037
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #004 (Discuss Integration), #026 (Relationship Explainer), #108 (Prune-Aware Chat)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: edge_analysis table, edge_feedback table

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

□ Citation sentences displayed for any discussed edge
□ Citing and cited sentences shown with source labels
□ Mutation type and confidence badges displayed
□ Flag button submits to edge_feedback
□ Side-by-side layout in wide mode, stacked in narrow
□ Tier 4 papers show "unavailable" message
□ /citation command works for explicit edge lookup

VISUAL:
□ Clean blockquote style for sentences
□ Source labels (methods, abstract, etc.) visible
□ Mutation and confidence badges use color coding
□ Responsive layout (side-by-side vs stacked)

PERFORMANCE: □ Citation fetch < 200ms (direct DB query)
ACCESSIBILITY: □ Blockquotes with cite attributes, badges as aria-labels
EDGE CASES: □ Both tier 4 handled, long sentences truncated, previously flagged shown

□ BROWSER TEST: Verified

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #021: COLLABORATIVE NOTES
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 18
OVERLAPS WITH:   #033 (Live Annotation Pad), #129 (Sticky Notes)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #006 (Conversation Memory), #011 (Artifact Cards), #037 (Streaming)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Collaborative Notes allows users to create, edit, and organize research notes
directly within the Athena chat interface. Unlike the Live Annotation Pad (#033)
which attaches notes to graph viewport positions, Collaborative Notes is a
structured note-taking system that lives alongside the conversation. Users can
pin important insights from Athena's responses as notes, write their own
observations, tag notes with paper references, and export the collection as a
structured research document.

This feature exists because research insights are perishable. A user discovers
that "the attention mechanism cluster has 3 bridging papers that connect to the
CNN lineage" during an Athena conversation. Without a note-taking system, this
insight exists only in the chat scroll. With Collaborative Notes, the user clicks
"Save as note" on Athena's response, optionally edits it, tags it with the
relevant papers, and it becomes a permanent, organized research artifact.

The notes system uses a sidebar panel (accessible via a "Notes" tab in the Athena
interface) that shows all notes for the current session. Notes are organized
chronologically by default, with options to sort by paper reference, tag, or
creation time. Each note has a title (auto-generated from first line), body
(rich text with Markdown support), paper tags (clickable references to graph
nodes), and a source link (which Athena message it came from, if pinned).

What makes this unique to Athena: notes can be AI-assisted. When saving a note,
the user can click "Enhance with Athena" to have Athena expand a rough note into
a polished research observation, adding computed context from the graph. A note
like "ResNet important" becomes "ResNet (He 2016) is the 3rd most structurally
critical paper in this lineage (PageRank 0.043). Removing it would collapse 31%
of descendant papers. It anchors the Deep Residual Learning cluster (23 papers)."

Notes are stored in the sessions table graph_memory JSONB field, making them
session-scoped for anonymous users. For authenticated users (Phase 6+), notes
persist across sessions. The notes collection can be exported as Markdown,
included in session summaries (#017), and referenced in literature reviews.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User clicks "Save as note" on any Athena message, clicks the "Notes" tab in
Athena sidebar, types /note [text] in chat, or clicks the + button in the Notes
panel to create a new note from scratch.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User reads an Athena response about ResNet's structural importance. They hover
   over the message and click the "Save as note" icon (a small bookmark/pin icon
   that appears in the message action bar).

2. A note editor slides open in the Notes panel (right side of Athena). The note
   is pre-populated with the Athena response text. The title auto-generates from
   the first line. Paper tags are auto-detected from the message metadata.

3. User edits the note, adding their own observations. They click "Enhance with
   Athena" to have the AI expand their additions with computed data.

4. User saves the note. It appears in the Notes panel with: title, first 2 lines
   of body as preview, paper tags as clickable pills, source message link, and
   timestamp.

5. Later, user creates a note from scratch via the + button. Types freely in the
   editor. Tags papers by typing @ followed by paper name (auto-complete from
   graph nodes).

6. At session end, user exports all notes as Markdown via "Export Notes" button.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Notes persist in Notes panel for session duration. Accessible from any point in
the conversation. Exportable as Markdown or included in session summary.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-notes.js: Notes manager, editor, panel rendering, export.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: "Save as note" action on messages, /note command.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Notes panel styles, editor, note cards.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/notes (save), GET /api/athena/notes (list),
  DELETE /api/athena/notes/<id> (remove).

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaNotes {
    constructor(sessionId) {
      this.notes = [];
      this.sessionId = sessionId;
    }
    async saveNote(note) {
      const res = await fetch('/api/athena/notes', {
        method: 'POST',
        body: JSON.stringify({ title: note.title, body: note.body,
          paper_tags: note.paperTags, source_message_id: note.sourceId })
      });
      const saved = await res.json();
      this.notes.push(saved);
      this.render();
    }
    async enhance(noteId) {
      const note = this.notes.find(n => n.id === noteId);
      const enhanced = await fetch('/api/athena/notes/enhance', {
        method: 'POST',
        body: JSON.stringify({ body: note.body, graph_id: currentGraphId })
      }).then(r => r.json());
      note.body = enhanced.body;
      this.render();
    }
    exportMarkdown() {
      let md = '# Research Notes\n\n';
      for (const note of this.notes) {
        md += `## ${note.title}\n${note.body}\n\n`;
        if (note.paperTags.length) md += `Papers: ${note.paperTags.join(', ')}\n\n`;
      }
      downloadFile('research-notes.md', md);
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
Notes stored in sessions.graph_memory JSONB under 'notes' key:
  @app.route('/api/athena/notes', methods=['POST'])
  def save_note():
      session_id = get_session_id()
      note = request.json
      note['id'] = str(uuid.uuid4())[:8]
      note['created_at'] = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
      update_graph_memory(session_id, {'notes': {note['id']: note}})
      return jsonify(note)

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
// Paper tag auto-complete
function setupPaperTagging(editor, graphNodes) {
  editor.addEventListener('input', (e) => {
    const text = e.target.value;
    const atMatch = text.match(/@(\w+)$/);
    if (atMatch) {
      const query = atMatch[1];
      const matches = graphNodes.filter(n =>
        n.title.toLowerCase().includes(query.toLowerCase())).slice(0, 5);
      showAutoComplete(matches, (selected) => {
        e.target.value = text.replace(/@\w+$/, `@[${selected.title}](${selected.paper_id})`);
      });
    }
  });
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/notes
Request: { "title": "...", "body": "...", "paper_tags": ["paper_id1"], "source_message_id": "..." }
Response: { "id": "abc123", "title": "...", "body": "...", "paper_tags": [...], "created_at": "..." }

GET /api/athena/notes
Response: { "notes": [...] }

POST /api/athena/notes/enhance
Request: { "body": "ResNet important", "graph_id": "..." }
Response: { "body": "ResNet (He 2016) is the 3rd most structurally critical..." }

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Notes Panel Open

  │───────────────────────────────────────────│
  │  [+ New Note]          [Export Notes]     │
  │                                           │

  │  │ the RNN cluster directly...        │   │

  │  ┌─ Edit Note ─────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ Title: [ResNet Structural Role   ]  │  │
  │  │                                     │  │
  │  │ ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │  │
  │  │ │ ResNet is important because... │ │  │
  │  │ │ @ResNet has PageRank #3        │ │  │
  │  │ │                                │ │  │

TRANSITIONS: Panel tab switch 200ms crossfade. Note card hover: elevation increase. Editor: slideDown 200ms.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User clicks "Save as note" on message
  → Note pre-populated with message text + auto-detected paper tags
  → Editor opens in Notes panel
  → User edits, optionally enhances via LLM
  → POST /api/athena/notes saves to graph_memory JSONB
  → Note card appears in Notes panel

Export:
  → User clicks "Export Notes"
  → All notes compiled into Markdown
  → Downloaded as .md file

ERROR PATH: Save fails → "Could not save note. Your text is preserved. Try again."
DATA STORED: sessions.graph_memory.notes JSONB
DATA CACHED: Notes cached in AthenaNotes.notes array (runtime)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (for "Enhance" feature only)
STAGE: On-demand (only when user clicks "Enhance with Athena")
PROMPT: "Expand this research note with computed data from the graph: {note_body}. Graph context: {graph_summary}. Add specific numbers, paper titles, and metrics. No em dashes. No emojis."
OUTPUT FORMAT: Enhanced note text (plain text with Markdown)
FALLBACK: If LLM unavailable, "Enhancement unavailable. Note saved as-is."
TOKEN BUDGET: ~300 context + ~200 output = ~500 per enhancement

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| [Chat] / [Notes] tab | Click      | Switches between chat and notes view        |
| [+ New Note]         | Click       | Opens empty note editor                     |
| Note card            | Click       | Opens note in editor                        |
| Note card            | Hover       | Subtle elevation, edit icon appears          |
| Paper tag pill       | Click       | Highlights paper in graph                   |
| @ in editor          | Type        | Paper auto-complete dropdown                |
| [Enhance with Athena]| Click       | LLM expands note content                    |
| [Export Notes]       | Click       | Downloads Markdown file                     |
| "Save as note" icon  | Click       | Pins message as note                        |
| [Delete] on note     | Click       | Confirmation, then remove                   |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Tab switch 200ms crossfade. Editor slideDown 200ms. Auto-complete dropdown fadeIn 150ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Tab panel uses role="tabpanel", notes list as role="list", editor has aria-label, paper tags as buttons with aria-label

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User saves 50+ notes in one session
   Expected: Notes panel becomes scrollable. Performance remains smooth.
   Implementation: Virtual scrolling for notes list if count > 30.

2. Scenario: "Enhance with Athena" on a note that has no paper references
   Expected: Enhancement adds general lineage context without specific paper data.
   Implementation: If no paper tags, use overall graph summary for enhancement.

3. Scenario: User edits a note that was originally from an Athena message
   Expected: Source link preserved. Edited indicator shown. Original text NOT modified.
   Implementation: Note stores source_message_id, original preserved in metadata.

4. Scenario: Graph changes (new build) after notes were created
   Expected: Paper tag links may become stale. Show warning on stale tags.
   Implementation: Validate paper_ids against current graph on load.

5. Scenario: Export includes notes with special characters or code blocks
   Expected: Markdown export handles escaping correctly.
   Implementation: Proper Markdown escaping for special characters.
