════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #022: MULTI-GRAPH CHAT
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Contextual Awareness
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #010 (Conversation Threads)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #006 (Conversation Memory), #001 (Context-Aware), #010 (Threads)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Multi-Graph Chat enables Athena to maintain context across multiple lineage
graphs within the same session. Currently, when a user builds a new graph,
the conversation resets and previous graph context is lost. Multi-Graph Chat
preserves all conversations, allowing the user to reference insights from a
previous lineage while exploring a new one. "How does this ResNet lineage
compare to the BERT lineage I explored earlier?" becomes a valid question.

This feature exists because researchers rarely study a single lineage in
isolation. They explore 2-3 related lineages to understand a broader research
landscape. The ability to cross-reference insights ("the attention cluster in
the BERT graph has 3x more papers than in the ResNet graph") is uniquely
powerful and impossible in any existing tool.

Multi-Graph Chat works by extending conversation memory (#006) with graph-scoped
contexts. Each graph has its own conversation thread (using the threading system
from #010). When the user switches graphs, Athena creates a new thread tagged
with the new graph_id. Cross-graph questions are detected by references to
papers or concepts from non-active graphs. When detected, Athena loads the
relevant graph context from the cached graph data.

The LLM prompt for cross-graph queries includes a compact summary of BOTH graphs:
the current graph's full context (as usual) plus a condensed summary of the
referenced graph (top 10 papers, cluster names, key metrics). This keeps within
token limits while providing enough context for meaningful comparison.

What makes this unique: Athena can compare COMPUTED METRICS across lineages.
"The ResNet lineage has 601 nodes with 31% concentration in the CNN cluster,
while the BERT lineage has 423 nodes with 45% in the transformer cluster" is
a computed cross-graph comparison no other tool can provide.

A graph selector dropdown appears in the Athena header showing all graphs
explored in the current session. Switching between graphs switches the
conversation thread and loads the corresponding graph in the visualization.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User builds a second graph in the same session. The graph selector dropdown
auto-appears in the Athena header. Cross-graph questions are auto-detected.
User can also type /compare-graphs to explicitly trigger cross-graph analysis.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User explores the ResNet lineage, has a conversation with Athena (8 messages).
2. User builds a new graph for BERT. Athena header shows: [Graph: BERT v] with
   a dropdown arrow. Previous graph shown as option.
3. Athena: "I see you have been exploring ResNet. Now looking at BERT. I have
   context from both lineages."
4. User asks: "How does the structural concentration compare between these two?"
5. Athena responds with a cross-graph comparison using data from both cached graphs.
6. User clicks dropdown, selects "ResNet." Graph visualization switches, Athena
   thread switches to the ResNet conversation (thread from #010).

AFTER COMPLETION:
Both graph contexts preserved. User can switch freely. Cross-references work
in either direction.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-multigraph.js: Graph selector, cross-graph detection,
  context merging for cross-graph queries.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routing cross-graph queries, graph context switching.
- static/js/athena-memory.js: Per-graph conversation threads.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Graph selector dropdown styles.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/cross-graph endpoint for cross-lineage comparisons.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/cross-graph', methods=['POST'])
  def cross_graph_comparison():
      graph_ids = request.json['graph_ids']
      contexts = []
      for gid in graph_ids:
          graph_data = load_graph(gid)
          contexts.append(get_graph_summary_for_chat(graph_data))
      prompt = build_cross_graph_prompt(request.json['question'], contexts)
      response = llm_client.generate(config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL, prompt)
      return jsonify(response)

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class AthenaMultiGraph {
  constructor() {
    this.graphs = []; // {id, title, seed_paper, node_count}
    this.activeGraphId = null;
  }
  addGraph(graphData) {
    this.graphs.push({
      id: graphData.metadata.graph_id,
      title: graphData.metadata.seed_paper_title,
      seed_paper: graphData.metadata.seed_paper_id,
      node_count: graphData.metadata.total_nodes
    });
    this.renderSelector();
  }
  detectCrossGraphReference(message) {
    for (const g of this.graphs) {
      if (g.id === this.activeGraphId) continue;
      if (message.toLowerCase().includes(g.title.toLowerCase().split(' ')[0])) {
        return g.id;
      }
    }
    return null;
  }
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/cross-graph
Request: { "graph_ids": ["abc...", "def..."], "question": "How do they compare?" }
Response: { "comparison": "...", "graph_summaries": [...] }

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Multi-Graph Selector Active

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ● ATHENA  [Graph: BERT ▼]          [≡]   │
  │           ┌────────────────────┐          │
  │           │ ● BERT (423 nodes) │          │
  │           │ ○ ResNet (601)     │          │
  │           └────────────────────┘          │
  │───────────────────────────────────────────│
  │  Cross-graph comparison response...       │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Dropdown fadeIn 150ms. Graph switch: 300ms crossfade on messages, graph viz reloads.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User builds second graph
  → AthenaMultiGraph.addGraph() registers new graph
  → Selector dropdown appears
  → New conversation thread created for new graph (#010)
  → User asks cross-graph question
  → detectCrossGraphReference() finds referenced graph
  → POST /api/athena/cross-graph with both graph_ids
  → Backend loads both graphs, creates merged context
  → LLM generates cross-graph comparison
  → Response rendered in current thread

ERROR PATH: Referenced graph expired from cache → "The [graph name] graph is no longer cached. Rebuild it to compare."
DATA STORED: session_graphs table links session to multiple graphs
DATA CACHED: Graph summaries cached per graph_id in sessionStorage

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (cross-graph comparisons need quality)
STAGE: On-demand for cross-graph questions
PROMPT: "Compare these two research lineages: Graph A: {summary_a}. Graph B: {summary_b}. Question: {question}. Use specific numbers from both graphs."
OUTPUT FORMAT: Prose comparison with inline metrics from both graphs
FALLBACK: Show side-by-side metric tables without LLM narrative
TOKEN BUDGET: ~800 (two summaries) + ~500 output = ~1300 per cross-graph query

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Graph selector       | Click       | Opens dropdown with available graphs         |
| Graph option         | Click       | Switches active graph + conversation thread  |
| Cross-graph reference| Auto-detect | Loads referenced graph context for LLM       |
| /compare-graphs      | Type        | Explicit cross-graph analysis                |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Selector dropdown 150ms fadeIn. Graph switch 300ms crossfade.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Dropdown as aria-haspopup, graph options as radio group, active graph announced

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User has explored 5+ graphs in one session
   Expected: Dropdown shows all graphs with scroll if > 4. Cross-graph queries
   reference at most 2 graphs at once (token limit).
   Implementation: Dropdown scrollable. Cross-graph limited to pairwise comparison.

2. Scenario: Referenced graph was built hours ago and cache expired
   Expected: "The ResNet graph is no longer cached. Would you like to rebuild it?"
   Implementation: Check graph cache before loading context.

3. Scenario: User asks about a paper that exists in both graphs
   Expected: Athena clarifies: "ResNet appears in both lineages. I'll use its
   context from the currently active graph (BERT lineage)."
   Implementation: Disambiguate using active graph priority.

4. Scenario: Cross-graph query while one graph is still building
   Expected: "The BERT graph is still building. Cross-graph comparison will be
   available once the build completes."
   Implementation: Check build job status before cross-graph.

5. Scenario: Graphs have no overlapping papers
   Expected: Comparison focuses on structural differences rather than shared papers.
   Implementation: Detect overlap count, adjust comparison prompt.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #006 (Memory), #001 (Context-Aware), #010 (Threads for per-graph conversations)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #014 (Comparison Mode can cross graphs), #017 (Summary includes all graphs)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: session_graphs table, graph cache (DB/R2)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ Graph selector appears after second graph built
□ Switching graphs switches conversation thread
□ Cross-graph references auto-detected
□ Cross-graph comparison uses data from both lineages
□ /compare-graphs command for explicit comparison
□ Per-graph conversation threads preserved

VISUAL: □ Dropdown selector with dot matrix style, active graph highlighted
PERFORMANCE: □ Graph switch < 500ms, cross-graph query < 8s
ACCESSIBILITY: □ Dropdown as aria-haspopup, active graph announced
EDGE CASES: □ Expired graphs handled, disambiguation for shared papers

VERIFICATION:
□ PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
□ BROWSER TEST: Verified
□ CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #023: AI DISAGREEMENT BUTTON
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Message Actions
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 8
OVERLAPS WITH:   #052 (Reactions/Bookmarks)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Formatting), #037 (Streaming), #006 (Memory)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The AI Disagreement Button allows users to formally disagree with any claim
Athena makes, triggering a re-evaluation of that specific statement. When Athena
says "ResNet is the most critical paper in this lineage," the user can click a
subtle "Challenge" button on that message. Athena then re-examines the claim
against the graph data: "You are right to question this. While ResNet has the
highest pruning impact (31%), VGGNet has higher citation count and the Attention
paper has higher PageRank. Let me revise: ResNet is the most structurally
critical by pruning impact, but not by all metrics."

This feature exists because AI should be challengeable. Researchers need to
trust Athena's outputs, and trust comes from the ability to verify and push
back. The disagreement button creates a formal channel for skepticism that
improves both the immediate response quality and (through the edge_feedback
system) the long-term accuracy of classifications.

When the user clicks "Challenge," a small input field appears asking "What
specifically do you disagree with?" with three quick options: "Inaccurate data,"
"Oversimplification," "Missing context." The user can also type a custom reason.
Athena then generates a re-evaluation that addresses the specific concern, using
the same graph data but with an explicitly critical lens.

The disagreement is logged in the action_log table and, if it concerns an edge
classification, also in the edge_feedback table. Over time, this feedback data
improves the system. Frequently challenged claims about specific edges can
trigger automatic re-classification in future graph builds.

What makes this unique: the re-evaluation uses THE SAME DATA but with a
different prompt. Instead of "analyze this paper's role," the prompt becomes
"the user disagrees that [claim]. Re-examine using these metrics: [data].
Identify where the original claim may have been too strong, too simplified,
or missing nuance." This produces a genuinely different perspective, not just
a rephrasing.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User hovers over any Athena message. A subtle action bar appears with icons:
[Copy] [Save as Note] [Challenge]. User clicks the "Challenge" icon (a small
dot-matrix question mark ◐).

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. Athena says: "ResNet is the most critical paper in this lineage."
2. User hovers, clicks Challenge (◐). A mini-form slides open below the message:
   "What's wrong? [Inaccurate] [Oversimplified] [Missing context] [Custom: ___]"
3. User clicks "Oversimplified."
4. Athena generates a re-evaluation: "You are right that this was oversimplified.
   While ResNet has the highest pruning impact (31%), the claim 'most critical'
   depends on the metric. By PageRank, the Attention paper ranks higher. By
   citation count, VGGNet leads. A more precise statement: ResNet is the most
   structurally critical by cascading dependency, but not by all importance
   metrics."
5. The re-evaluation appears as a new message with a subtle "Revised" badge.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Original message remains (not modified). Re-evaluation follows as a new message.
Disagreement logged for quality improvement.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Challenge action handler, re-evaluation trigger.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Challenge form rendering, "Revised" badge.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Challenge form styles, message action bar.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/challenge endpoint for re-evaluation.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/challenge', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_challenge():
      original_message = request.json['original_content']
      reason = request.json['reason']
      graph_context = get_graph_summary_for_chat(load_graph(request.json['graph_id']))
      prompt = f"""The user disagrees with this claim: "{original_message}"
      Reason: {reason}
      Graph data: {graph_context}
      Re-examine this claim critically. Where is it too strong, too simple, or
      missing nuance? Provide a revised, more precise statement. Use specific
      metrics. No em dashes. No emojis."""
      response = llm_client.generate(config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL, prompt)
      log_action(get_session_id(), 'challenge', {'reason': reason})
      return jsonify({'revised': response})

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
function showChallengeForm(messageElement) {
  const form = document.createElement('div');
  form.className = 'challenge-form';
  form.innerHTML = `
    <div class="challenge-label">What's wrong?</div>
    <div class="challenge-options">
      <button data-reason="inaccurate">Inaccurate data</button>
      <button data-reason="oversimplified">Oversimplified</button>
      <button data-reason="missing">Missing context</button>
      <input type="text" placeholder="Custom reason..." class="challenge-custom">
    </div>`;
  form.querySelectorAll('button').forEach(btn => {
    btn.onclick = () => submitChallenge(messageElement.dataset.content, btn.dataset.reason);
  });
  messageElement.after(form);
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/challenge
Request: { "original_content": "...", "reason": "oversimplified", "graph_id": "..." }
Response: { "revised": "You are right that this was oversimplified..." }

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Message Action Bar (on hover)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Athena: ResNet is the most critical      │
  │  paper in this lineage.                   │
  │                          [□] [📌] [◐]    │
  │                          copy save challenge│
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Challenge Form Open

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Athena: ResNet is the most critical...   │
  │  ┌─ Challenge ─────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ What's wrong?                       │  │
  │  │ [Inaccurate] [Oversimplified]       │  │
  │  │ [Missing context] [Custom: ___]     │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Revised Response

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Athena: ResNet is the most critical...   │
  │                                           │
  │  ┌─ REVISED ──────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ You are right that this was over-  │   │
  │  │ simplified. While ResNet has the   │   │
  │  │ highest pruning impact (31%), the  │   │
  │  │ claim depends on the metric...     │   │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Action bar fadeIn 150ms on hover. Challenge form slideDown 200ms. Revised message fadeIn 300ms with gold "REVISED" label.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User hovers message → action bar appears
  → Clicks Challenge → form slides open
  → Selects reason → POST /api/athena/challenge
  → Backend re-evaluates with critical prompt + same graph data
  → Revised response returned
  → Rendered as new message with "REVISED" badge
  → Logged in action_log and optionally edge_feedback

ERROR PATH: LLM timeout → "Could not re-evaluate. Try again."
DATA STORED: action_log: { action: 'challenge', reason, original_snippet }
DATA CACHED: None (re-evaluation always fresh)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (re-evaluation needs quality)
STAGE: On-demand (only when user challenges)
PROMPT: Critical re-examination with explicit instruction to find weaknesses in original claim
OUTPUT FORMAT: Revised prose with specific metrics
FALLBACK: If LLM unavailable, show raw graph data that relates to the challenged claim
TOKEN BUDGET: ~500 context + ~400 output = ~900 per challenge

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Message hover        | Hover       | Action bar fades in (copy, save, challenge)  |
| Challenge icon (◐)   | Click       | Challenge form slides open                  |
| Reason button        | Click       | Submits challenge, form closes               |
| Custom input         | Enter       | Submits custom reason                        |
| Revised badge        | Visible     | Gold "REVISED" label on re-evaluation        |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Action bar 150ms fadeIn. Form 200ms slideDown. Revised 300ms fadeIn.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Action bar buttons with aria-labels, challenge form fieldset with legend, reason buttons as radio alternatives

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User challenges a message that contains no factual claims (greeting)
   Expected: "This message does not contain claims to challenge. Try challenging
   a response with specific data or analysis."
   Implementation: Check message metadata for claim_type before showing challenge option.

2. Scenario: User challenges the same message twice
   Expected: Second challenge generates new re-evaluation (may differ with different reason).
   Both re-evaluations visible in sequence.
   Implementation: Allow multiple challenges, each produces separate revised message.

3. Scenario: Re-evaluation agrees with original claim
   Expected: "After re-examination, the original statement holds. Here is the supporting evidence: [data]."
   Implementation: LLM prompt allows for "the original was correct" as valid output.

4. Scenario: Challenge during streaming (message still generating)
   Expected: Challenge button not available until streaming completes.
   Implementation: Action bar only appears on fully rendered messages.

5. Scenario: User challenges an edge classification specifically
   Expected: Challenge creates entry in edge_feedback table in addition to action_log.
   Implementation: Detect edge references in original message, write to edge_feedback.

DEPENDS ON: #002, #037, #006
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Trust system (edge_feedback), #052 (Reactions/Bookmarks)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: action_log, edge_feedback tables

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

□ Challenge icon appears on message hover
□ Challenge form with 3 preset reasons + custom input
□ Re-evaluation generated with critical lens
□ Revised response appears with "REVISED" badge
□ Disagreement logged in action_log
□ Edge-specific challenges also logged in edge_feedback
□ Multiple challenges on same message supported

VISUAL: □ Action bar fadeIn on hover, challenge form slideDown, "REVISED" gold badge
PERFORMANCE: □ Re-evaluation within 5 seconds
ACCESSIBILITY: □ Action buttons labeled, form accessible
EDGE CASES: □ Non-claim messages excluded, streaming messages protected

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #024: TEACH ME MODE
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #029 (Socratic Mode), #111 (Tutorial Mode), #031 (Explain for Another Field)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Formatting), #006 (Memory), #037 (Streaming), #049 (Mode Selector)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Teach Me Mode transforms Athena into a patient academic tutor that explains the
research lineage at the user's level of understanding. Instead of assuming expert
knowledge, Athena adapts its explanations based on the user's declared expertise
level (beginner, intermediate, advanced) and the field they come from. A biology
PhD student exploring a computer science lineage gets fundamentally different
explanations than a senior CS researcher.

This feature exists because Arivu serves researchers across disciplines and
career stages. A first-year PhD student needs "ResNet introduced shortcut
connections that let gradients flow through very deep networks without
vanishing" while a senior ML researcher needs "ResNet's identity mappings
enable gradient highways that decouple depth from optimization difficulty,
as shown by the 152-layer configuration outperforming shallower baselines
on ImageNet." Same concept, radically different depth.

When Teach Me Mode is active, every Athena response includes: (1) the main
explanation at the chosen level, (2) a "Go deeper" link that provides a more
technical version, (3) a "Simplify" link that provides a more accessible version,
(4) key terms defined in tooltips (hover over bolded terms to see definitions),
and (5) analogies drawn from the user's declared field when applicable.

The mode uses a dedicated system prompt that instructs the LLM to explain at
the specified level, define jargon, use analogies, and structure explanations
with clear progression from simple to complex. The level can be changed mid-
conversation, and Athena adapts immediately.

What makes this unique: Teach Me Mode uses the COMPUTED GRAPH DATA to construct
explanations. Instead of "ResNet is important," it says "In this lineage of 601
papers, ResNet is the 3rd most structurally important paper. If it had never
existed, 31% of the research that followed would not exist in its current form.
Think of it like a major highway interchange: rerouting is possible but adds
enormous distance." The analogy is dynamic, the numbers are computed.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User activates via mode selector (#049), types /teach, or Athena detects novice-
level questions and suggests: "Would you like me to explain this lineage at a
more introductory level?"

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User activates Teach Me Mode. A brief setup asks:
   "What is your expertise level? [Beginner] [Intermediate] [Advanced]"
   "What field are you from? [CS/ML] [Biology] [Physics] [Other: ___]"

2. User selects "Intermediate" and "Biology." Athena acknowledges: "I'll explain
   this computer science lineage using biology-friendly language. Ask me anything."

3. User asks: "What is ResNet and why does it matter?"

4. Athena responds at intermediate level with biology analogies: "ResNet (2016)
   introduced 'residual connections' in neural networks. Think of it like gene
   regulatory networks where feedback loops allow signals to bypass intermediate
   nodes. In this lineage of 601 papers, ResNet is the 3rd most structurally
   critical node. If you removed it (like a knockout experiment), 31% of
   downstream papers would lose their methodological foundation."

5. Below the response: [Go deeper] [Simplify] links. Bolded terms ("residual
   connections," "neural networks") have hover tooltips with definitions.

6. User clicks "Go deeper." A more technical expansion appears: "Specifically,
   residual connections implement F(x) + x identity shortcuts that solve the
   degradation problem: networks deeper than ~20 layers actually performed
   worse before this innovation..."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Teach Me Mode stays active until deactivated. All subsequent responses use the
configured level and field context.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-teach.js: Mode state, level/field configuration, tooltip
  system for term definitions, "Go deeper"/"Simplify" handlers.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Teach Mode system prompt injection.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: TeachBlock with expandable levels.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Tooltip styles, level indicators, Go deeper/Simplify links.
- app.py: Teach Mode prompt prefix added to /api/athena/stream when active.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No new endpoint. The teach mode configuration is passed as metadata with each
chat request. The system prompt is modified:

  TEACH_PROMPT = """You are explaining research to a {level} audience from {field}.
  Adapt your language:
  - Beginner: No jargon. Use everyday analogies. Define all technical terms.
  - Intermediate: Some jargon OK. Use field-specific analogies ({field}).
  - Advanced: Full technical depth. Assume domain knowledge.
  When using technical terms, wrap them in **bold** for tooltip activation.
  Include specific computed data from the graph. No em dashes. No emojis."""

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class AthenaTeachMode {
  constructor() {
    this.active = false;
    this.level = 'intermediate';
    this.field = 'general';
  }
  getSystemPromptPrefix() {
    if (!this.active) return '';
    return TEACH_PROMPT
      .replace('{level}', this.level)
      .replace('{field}', this.field);
  }
  setupTooltips(messageElement) {
    messageElement.querySelectorAll('strong').forEach(term => {
      term.classList.add('teach-term');
      term.setAttribute('data-tooltip', this.getDefinition(term.textContent));
    });
  }
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
No new endpoints. Teach mode state sent as metadata in existing /api/athena/stream:
{ "message": "...", "teach_mode": { "active": true, "level": "intermediate", "field": "biology" } }

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User activates Teach Me Mode
  → Level + field stored in session state
  → System prompt modified with teach prefix
  → All subsequent messages include teach context
  → LLM adapts explanation level
  → Bold terms processed for tooltips
  → "Go deeper"/"Simplify" generate follow-up at adjusted level

ERROR PATH: LLM ignores teach level → Re-prompt with explicit level instruction
DATA STORED: Session: teach_mode settings. action_log: mode activation.
DATA CACHED: Term definitions cached per session (reused across messages)

LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (regular chat with modified system prompt)
STAGE: System prompt modification (prepended to every LLM call in teach mode)
PROMPT: TEACH_PROMPT prefix with level and field (see above)
OUTPUT FORMAT: Standard prose with **bold** terms for tooltip activation
FALLBACK: If LLM ignores level instructions, standard response (no degradation)
TOKEN BUDGET: ~100 extra tokens for teach prefix per message

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Mode selector        | Activate    | Setup dialog for level + field               |
| Level selector       | Click       | Sets explanation level                       |
| Field selector       | Click/Type  | Sets user's field for analogies              |

| [Simplify]           | Click       | Simpler re-explanation below                 |
| Header indicator     | Visible     | "Teach: Intermediate/Bio" badge              |

1. Scenario: User from "Biology" asks about a biology-adjacent paper in a CS lineage
   Expected: Athena recognizes overlap and adjusts: "Since you are from biology,
   you will find this bioinformatics paper particularly relevant..."
   Implementation: Field-aware paper recommendations in teach context.

2. Scenario: "Go deeper" already at Advanced level
   Expected: Shows raw data and equations if available. Note: "This is the most
   technical depth available."
   Implementation: Cap at "advanced" level, offer raw metrics as final depth.

3. Scenario: User changes level mid-conversation
   Expected: Next response uses new level. No re-generation of previous messages.
   Implementation: Level change updates system prompt prefix for future messages.

4. Scenario: Bold term has no definition in the system
   Expected: Tooltip shows "Definition not available. Ask me about this term."
   Implementation: Fallback for undefined terms with clickable ask action.

5. Scenario: Teach mode with Pathfinder queries (#008)
   Expected: Pathfinder responses also adapt to teach level. Stat blocks include
   tooltips explaining each metric.
   Implementation: Teach prefix added to Pathfinder pipeline prompts too.

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #025: SENTIMENT HEATMAP
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #149 (Heatmap Overlay)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Formatting), #037 (Streaming), #085 (Heatmap Block)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Sentiment Heatmap generates a visual heatmap showing how the citation sentiment
varies across the ancestry graph. Each edge in the graph carries a citation
intent (methodological_adoption, theoretical_foundation, direct_contradiction,
negative_citation, etc.) which encodes the sentiment of the citation. The
Sentiment Heatmap aggregates these into a visual overlay: warm colors (green/blue)
for positive citations (adoption, foundation), cool colors (red/orange) for
negative citations (contradiction, negative), and neutral (gray) for incidental.

This feature exists because understanding citation sentiment reveals the
intellectual dynamics of a field. A cluster where most edges are contradictions
represents an active debate. A cluster where all edges are adoptions represents
consensus. A paper that receives both strong adoptions AND contradictions is
contentious. These patterns are invisible in a standard graph view.

The heatmap is generated as an interactive block in Athena's chat response.
When a user asks "show me the sentiment landscape" or activates the heatmap
via /sentiment, Athena renders a miniature graph visualization within the chat
where nodes are colored by their aggregate sentiment score. The score is computed
as: (positive_edge_count - negative_edge_count) / total_edge_count, producing
a value from -1.0 (all negative) to +1.0 (all positive).

Below the heatmap, Athena provides a narrative summary: "This lineage is
predominantly positive (78% adoption/foundation), with a notable controversy
cluster around [papers X, Y, Z] where 4 contradiction edges exist. The attention
mechanism papers are the most cited positively, while [Paper Q] receives the
most negative citations."

What makes this unique: the sentiment data comes from the NLP pipeline's
computed citation_intent classifications, not from LLM sentiment analysis.
These are pre-computed, evidence-based classifications stored in edge_analysis.
The heatmap visualizes real data, not LLM opinions.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User types /sentiment, asks "show me the sentiment landscape," or Athena
suggests it when contradictions are detected in the lineage.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types /sentiment.
2. Athena: "Analyzing citation sentiment across 203 edges..."
3. A Heatmap Block renders in the chat: a miniature graph (SVG, 300x200px) where
   nodes are colored from green (positive) through gray (neutral) to red (negative).
   Edge colors also reflect sentiment.
4. Below the heatmap, a legend: Green = adoption/foundation, Gray = incidental,
   Red = contradiction/negative.
5. Below the legend, a narrative: "This lineage is 78% positive. The attention
   cluster has the highest positive sentiment (92%). A controversy zone exists
   around [papers] with 4 contradiction edges."
6. Hovering over a node in the heatmap shows a tooltip with sentiment score and
   edge breakdown.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Heatmap visible in chat. User can discuss specific controversy areas.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: SentimentHeatmapBlock renderer with SVG mini-graph.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: /sentiment command routing.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Heatmap block styles, legend, tooltip.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/sentiment endpoint computing sentiment aggregates.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/sentiment', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_sentiment():
      graph_data = load_graph(request.json['graph_id'])
      edges = graph_data['edges']
      # Classify sentiment per edge
      positive_intents = {'methodological_adoption', 'theoretical_foundation',
                          'empirical_baseline', 'conceptual_inspiration'}
      negative_intents = {'direct_contradiction', 'negative_citation'}
      # Compute per-node sentiment scores
      node_scores = {}
      for edge in edges:
          target = edge['cited_paper_id']
          intent = edge.get('citation_intent', 'incidental_mention')
          if target not in node_scores:
              node_scores[target] = {'pos': 0, 'neg': 0, 'total': 0}
          node_scores[target]['total'] += 1
          if intent in positive_intents: node_scores[target]['pos'] += 1
          elif intent in negative_intents: node_scores[target]['neg'] += 1
      # Compute normalized scores (-1 to +1)
      for nid, s in node_scores.items():
          s['score'] = (s['pos'] - s['neg']) / max(s['total'], 1)
      return jsonify({'node_scores': node_scores, 'edges': edges})

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class SentimentHeatmapBlock {
  render(data) {
    const svg = d3.create('svg').attr('viewBox', '0 0 300 200');
    // Mini force layout
    const sim = d3.forceSimulation(data.nodes)
      .force('charge', d3.forceManyBody().strength(-5))
      .force('center', d3.forceCenter(150, 100));
    // Color scale: red (-1) to gray (0) to green (+1)
    const color = d3.scaleLinear()
      .domain([-1, 0, 1])
      .range(['#EF4444', '#94A3B8', '#22C55E']);
    // Render nodes with sentiment colors
    svg.selectAll('circle').data(data.nodes).join('circle')
      .attr('r', 4).attr('fill', d => color(d.sentiment_score || 0));
    return svg.node();
  }
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/sentiment
Request: { "graph_id": "..." }
Response: { "node_scores": { "paper_id": { "pos": 5, "neg": 1, "total": 8, "score": 0.5 } }, "summary": { "overall_positive_pct": 78, "controversy_papers": [...] } }

VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  │  │  ┌──────────────────────────────┐  │   │
  │  │  │   ●green  ●green   ●gray    │  │   │
  │  │  │  / \      |       / \       │  │   │
  │  │  │ ●   ●red  ●green ●   ●gray │  │   │
  │  │  │      \   /        \  /      │  │   │
  │  │  │       ●red         ●green   │  │   │
  │  │  └──────────────────────────────┘  │   │
  │  │                                     │   │
  │  │  ● Positive  ○ Neutral  ◐ Negative │   │
  │  │                                     │   │
  │  │  This lineage is 78% positive.     │   │
  │  │  Controversy zone: [papers] with   │   │
  │  │  4 contradiction edges.            │   │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Heatmap SVG draws nodes sequentially (20ms stagger). Legend fadeIn after graph.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User types /sentiment → POST /api/athena/sentiment
  → Backend loads graph, iterates edges, classifies intents
  → Computes per-node sentiment scores
  → Returns scores + summary statistics
  → Frontend renders SVG mini-graph with color mapping
  → Narrative summary rendered below

ERROR PATH: Graph has no edge classifications → "Sentiment analysis requires edge classifications. This graph may not have been fully analyzed."
DATA STORED: action_log: { action: 'sentiment_view' }
DATA CACHED: Sentiment scores cached per graph_id in sessionStorage

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (for narrative summary only)
STAGE: Post-computation (narrates pre-computed sentiment data)
PROMPT: "Summarize the sentiment landscape: {scores_summary}. Identify controversy areas and dominant sentiment patterns. 2-3 sentences."
OUTPUT FORMAT: Short prose summary
FALLBACK: Rule-based summary: "X% positive, Y% negative. Top controversy: [papers]."
TOKEN BUDGET: ~200 context + ~100 output = ~300 per sentiment view

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Heatmap node         | Hover       | Tooltip: paper title, sentiment score, edge breakdown |
| Heatmap node         | Click       | Discusses that paper's citation sentiment    |
| Legend item          | Hover       | Highlights all nodes of that sentiment type  |
| Controversy paper    | Click       | Discusses the controversy in detail          |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Nodes draw with 20ms stagger. Tooltip 150ms fadeIn. Click highlight 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: SVG has role="img" with aria-label describing overall sentiment. Tooltip content in aria-live region.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: All edges are "incidental_mention" (no strong sentiment)
   Expected: Heatmap is all gray. Narrative: "This lineage has minimal sentiment variation. Most citations are incidental mentions."
   Implementation: Detect uniform sentiment, adjust narrative.

2. Scenario: Graph has 600+ nodes (too many for readable mini-graph)
   Expected: Heatmap shows cluster-level aggregation rather than individual nodes.
   Group nodes by cluster, show cluster sentiment averages.
   Implementation: If nodes > 100, aggregate by cluster.

3. Scenario: Edge has no citation_intent classification (missing data)
   Expected: Treat as "neutral" in scoring. Count in total but not pos/neg.
   Implementation: Default to neutral for missing/null citation_intent.

4. Scenario: User asks about sentiment for a specific paper
   Expected: Focus on that paper's incoming edge sentiment breakdown.
   Implementation: Filter scores by paper_id if specified.

5. Scenario: SVG mini-graph renders too small in Panel mode (380px)
   Expected: Heatmap uses full available width. In Panel mode, graph is simpler
   (fewer force iterations, larger nodes).
   Implementation: Responsive SVG with panel-width-aware rendering.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #149 (Heatmap Overlay on main graph)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: edge_analysis.citation_intent data

□ Color scale: green (positive) to gray (neutral) to red (negative)
□ SVG mini-graph renders in chat with interactive nodes
□ Narrative summary describes overall and notable patterns
□ Controversy areas identified and named

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #026: PAPER RELATIONSHIP EXPLAINER
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           C
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #020 (Citation Sentence Viewer), #004 (Discuss Integration)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Formatting), #020 (Citation Sentence Viewer), #037 (Streaming)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Paper Relationship Explainer generates a detailed narrative explanation of how
any two papers in the lineage are connected, going beyond the simple edge label
to tell the complete story of their intellectual relationship. When a user asks
"how are ResNet and VGGNet related?", Athena does not just say "generalization."
It explains: "ResNet generalized VGGNet's insight that deeper networks can learn
richer features. While VGGNet proved depth matters (going from 8 to 19 layers),
it hit the degradation problem: deeper networks performed WORSE. ResNet solved
this with identity shortcuts, enabling 152-layer networks. In the graph data,
ResNet inherits 3 citation links from VGGNet and has 1.4x higher PageRank."

This feature exists because edge labels are compressed abstractions. Researchers
need the full story of how ideas evolved between papers. The mutation type
("generalization") is the classification; the relationship explainer is the
narrative that makes that classification meaningful and verifiable.

The explainer combines multiple data sources: the edge_analysis record (mutation
type, confidence, citation sentences), both papers' metadata (year, citations,
fields), structural metrics (PageRank comparison, shared descendants), and the
broader graph context (are they in the same cluster? do they share ancestors?).
This multi-source compilation creates a genuinely rich explanation.

The LLM receives all this pre-computed data and generates a 3-4 paragraph
narrative. Paragraph 1: the intellectual relationship. Paragraph 2: the
structural relationship in the graph (shared paths, relative importance).
Paragraph 3: what this relationship means for the field (were subsequent
papers more influenced by one or the other?). The LLM narrates; it does not
invent the data.

What makes this unique: the explanation references the SPECIFIC citation
sentence (#020) and COMPUTED structural metrics. "ResNet cites VGGNet in its
methods section: 'Following the deep architecture of [VGGNet]...' This is
classified as a methodological adoption (confidence: HIGH, 0.87). Structurally,
ResNet outranks VGGNet in PageRank (0.043 vs 0.031) and has 53% more descendants
(23 vs 15)." Every claim is verifiable.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User asks "how are [paper A] and [paper B] related?", clicks an edge in the
graph and selects "Explain relationship," or types /explain [paper A] [paper B].

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "How are ResNet and VGGNet related?"
2. Athena identifies both papers in the graph and the edge between them.
3. Backend compiles relationship data: edge_analysis record, paper metadata for
   both, PageRank comparison, shared descendants, cluster membership.
4. LLM generates narrative using the compiled data.
5. Response renders with: Citation Sentence Block (#020) showing the actual
   sentences, a mini comparison table (PageRank, citations, year, cluster),
   and 3-4 paragraphs of narrative explanation.
6. Quick actions: "Show their shared descendants," "Compare in detail (#014),"
   "What if VGGNet never existed?"

AFTER COMPLETION:
Explanation in chat. User can follow up on specific aspects.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Relationship question detection, paper pair resolution.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: RelationshipBlock renderer with comparison table.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Relationship block styles.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/relationship endpoint.
- backend/graph_engine.py: compute_relationship_context() method.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/relationship', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_relationship():
      paper_a = request.json['paper_a_id']
      paper_b = request.json['paper_b_id']
      graph_data = load_graph(request.json['graph_id'])
      # Get edge data
      edge_id = f"{paper_a}:{paper_b}"
      edge = db.fetchone("SELECT * FROM edge_analysis WHERE edge_id IN (%s, %s)",
                          (edge_id, f"{paper_b}:{paper_a}"))
      # Compute relationship context
      context = compute_relationship_context(graph_data, paper_a, paper_b, edge)
      # Generate narrative
      narrative = llm_client.generate(config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL,
          RELATIONSHIP_PROMPT.format(**context))
      return jsonify({**context, 'narrative': narrative})

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```python
def compute_relationship_context(graph_data, paper_a_id, paper_b_id, edge):
    nodes = {n['paper_id']: n for n in graph_data['nodes']}
    a, b = nodes.get(paper_a_id, {}), nodes.get(paper_b_id, {})
    # Shared descendants
    a_desc = get_descendants(graph_data, paper_a_id)
    b_desc = get_descendants(graph_data, paper_b_id)
    shared = a_desc & b_desc
    return {
        'paper_a': a, 'paper_b': b,
        'edge': edge,
        'a_pagerank': a.get('pagerank', 0), 'b_pagerank': b.get('pagerank', 0),
        'a_descendants': len(a_desc), 'b_descendants': len(b_desc),
        'shared_descendants': len(shared),
        'same_cluster': a.get('cluster') == b.get('cluster'),
        'year_gap': abs((a.get('year') or 0) - (b.get('year') or 0))
    }
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/relationship
Request: { "paper_a_id": "...", "paper_b_id": "...", "graph_id": "..." }
Response: {
  "paper_a": { "title": "...", "year": 2016, "pagerank": 0.043 },
  "paper_b": { "title": "...", "year": 2014, "pagerank": 0.031 },
  "edge": { "mutation_type": "generalization", "confidence": 0.87, "citing_sentence": "...", "cited_sentence": "..." },
  "shared_descendants": 12,
  "same_cluster": true,
  "narrative": "ResNet generalized VGGNet's insight..."
}

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Relationship Explanation

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌─ RELATIONSHIP ─────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  ResNet (2016)    ──→  VGGNet (2014)│  │
  │  │  PageRank: #3     │    PageRank: #5 │  │
  │  │  Descendants: 23  │    Descendants: 15│ │
  │  │  Same cluster: Yes│                 │  │
  │  │  Shared desc: 12  │                 │  │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                           │
  │  ┌─ CITATION ────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ "Following the deep architecture  │   │
  │  │ of [VGGNet]..."                   │   │
  │  │ GENERALIZATION | HIGH (0.87)      │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                           │
  │  ResNet generalized VGGNet's insight     │
  │  that deeper networks learn richer       │
  │  features. While VGGNet proved depth     │
  │  matters, it hit the degradation problem.│
  │  ResNet solved this with identity        │
  │  shortcuts...                            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Comparison table fadeIn 200ms. Citation block 200ms delay. Prose streams after blocks.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User asks about relationship
  → athena-engine.js detects relationship question pattern
  → Resolves both paper_ids from names
  → POST /api/athena/relationship { paper_a_id, paper_b_id, graph_id }
  → Backend: loads edge_analysis + computes relationship context
  → Backend: LLM generates narrative from context
  → Response: context data + narrative
  → Frontend: renders comparison table + citation block + streamed narrative

ERROR PATH: No direct edge → "These papers are not directly connected. The shortest path goes through [papers]."
  One paper not found → "I could not find [paper] in this lineage."
DATA STORED: chat_history with type='relationship'
DATA CACHED: Relationship context per paper pair per graph (sessionStorage)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (70b for narrative quality)
STAGE: Post-computation (narrates pre-computed relationship data)
PROMPT: "Explain the intellectual relationship between {paper_a.title} and {paper_b.title}. Data: {context}. Write 3-4 paragraphs. Paragraph 1: intellectual connection. Paragraph 2: structural position. Paragraph 3: field impact. Use specific numbers."
OUTPUT FORMAT: 3-4 paragraphs of prose
FALLBACK: Show raw data table + citation sentences without narrative
TOKEN BUDGET: ~500 context + ~500 output = ~1000 per explanation

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Paper name in table  | Click       | Highlights paper in graph                   |
| Citation block       | Click paper | Navigates to paper details                  |
| Mutation badge       | Hover       | Tooltip explaining mutation type             |
| [Show shared desc]   | Click       | Lists shared descendant papers               |
| [Compare in detail]  | Click       | Triggers Comparison Mode (#014)              |
| Arrow (→)            | Visual      | Shows direction of citation                 |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Table 200ms fadeIn. Citation block 200ms delay. Prose streams.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Table as proper HTML table with headers, citation as blockquote, badges as aria-labels

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Papers are indirectly connected (no direct edge, path length > 1)
   Expected: Show the shortest path with explanations for each hop.
   Implementation: Compute shortest path, explain each edge in sequence.

2. Scenario: Papers are in different disconnected components
   Expected: "These papers are in separate components of the graph with no citation path between them."
   Implementation: Check connectivity before computing path.

3. Scenario: Edge exists in both directions (A cites B AND B cites A)
   Expected: Show both edges with their respective sentences and classifications.
   Implementation: Query both edge_id directions.

4. Scenario: Edge has SPECULATIVE confidence
   Expected: Warning: "This relationship classification has low confidence."
   Implementation: Prepend warning block for SPECULATIVE tier.

5. Scenario: User asks about relationship between papers from different graphs
   Expected: "These papers are from different lineages. Use Multi-Graph Chat (#022) for cross-lineage comparison."
   Implementation: Check both paper_ids against current graph nodes.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #001, #002, #020 (Citation Sentence Viewer for inline citations), #037
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #004 (Discuss), #014 (Comparison Mode), #108 (Prune-Aware Chat)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: edge_analysis table, graph cache

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ Relationship question detection ("how are X and Y related?")
□ Paper pair resolution from names
□ Comparison table with PageRank, descendants, cluster membership
□ Citation Sentence Block embedded
□ 3-4 paragraph narrative generated using computed data
□ Indirect path explanation for non-adjacent papers
□ Quick actions for follow-up

VISUAL: □ Comparison table, citation block, streaming prose
PERFORMANCE: □ Data compilation < 1s, narrative < 5s
ACCESSIBILITY: □ Table headers, blockquotes, badges labeled
EDGE CASES: □ Indirect paths, disconnected components, bidirectional edges

VERIFICATION: □ PESSIMISTIC AGENT complete □ BROWSER TEST verified □ CROSS-REFERENCE matches

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. READ files 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST various paper pairs 4. CHECK indirect paths 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #027: COUNTER-HISTORY MODE
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #018 (Debate Mode), #145 (What-If Calculator)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001, #002, #005, #006, #037, #049 (Mode Selector)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Counter-History Mode invites the user to explore "what if" scenarios by removing
foundational papers from history and narrating the alternate timeline that unfolds.
This extends the visual pruning animation into a full narrative experience. When
the user selects a paper to remove, Athena does not just show which nodes
collapse. It tells the STORY of the alternate history: "Without VGGNet (2014),
the idea that deeper networks improve accuracy would not have been empirically
validated at scale until 2016 when GoogLeNet independently discovered this.
This 2-year delay means ResNet (which built directly on VGGNet's depth insight)
would have emerged 1-2 years later, potentially missing the ImageNet competition
window that gave it its current influence."

This feature exists because the pruning animation shows WHAT happens when a paper
is removed, but not WHY or WHAT IT MEANS. A 31% collapse is a number. Counter-
History Mode transforms that number into a narrative that reveals the intellectual
dependencies and contingencies in the research lineage. It is the difference
between knowing "this paper is important" and understanding "this paper is
important because without it, the field would have taken this different path."

The mode works in phases: (1) User selects a paper to remove. (2) Pruning
simulation runs and computes the collapse metrics. (3) Athena generates an
alternate history narrative using the collapse data, survival paths, and temporal
information. (4) The narrative plays alongside a slowed-down version of the
pruning animation, with Athena narrating each wave.

The narrative uses the GROQ_SMART_MODEL (70b) because quality is critical for
speculative reasoning. The prompt includes: the removed paper's metadata,
the BFS collapse waves (which papers collapse in which order), the survival
paths (which papers survive and through what alternative ancestry), temporal
gaps (years between the removed paper and the next similar contribution), and
the cluster impact (which research strands are most affected).

What makes this unique: Athena's counter-history is GROUNDED in computed
pruning data. When it says "47 papers collapse," that is a verified simulation
result. When it says "the Deep Residual Learning strand would be most affected,"
that is computed from cluster membership of collapsed nodes. The speculation is
about the NARRATIVE connecting these facts, not the facts themselves.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User activates via mode selector (#049) choosing "Counter-History," types
/whatif [paper], or clicks "What if this never existed?" on a pruning result.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types: /whatif ResNet never existed
2. Athena: "Constructing alternate timeline without ResNet (2016)..."
3. Pruning simulation runs (reuses existing pruning system).
4. Athena generates narrative in 3 phases, synchronized with a slowed pruning animation:
   - Phase 1 (immediate impact): "Without ResNet, 8 direct dependents lose their
     foundational method. These include DenseNet, ResNeXt, and SENet..."
   - Phase 2 (cascading effects): "The loss cascades. 15 papers in Wave 2 lose
     their path to residual learning. The attention mechanism cluster survives
     through an independent ancestry path via..."
   - Phase 3 (alternate history): "In this timeline, the depth problem would
     likely have been solved by GoogLeNet's Inception approach, which does not
     use residual connections. This would have led to a fundamentally different
     architecture landscape..."
5. Final stats: "31% of the lineage collapses. The surviving 69% reorganize
   around [alternate anchor papers]."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Graph shows pruned state. Narrative in chat. User can try different papers.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-counterhistory.js: Counter-history orchestrator, phase management.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: /whatif command routing, mode activation.
- static/js/pruning.js: Existing pruning system (reused for simulation data).
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Counter-history phase styling (phase indicators).
- app.py: POST /api/athena/counter-history endpoint.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/counter-history', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_counter_history():
      paper_id = request.json['paper_id']
      graph_data = load_graph(request.json['graph_id'])
      # Run pruning simulation
      prune_result = compute_pruning_result(graph_data, [paper_id])
      # Compute temporal context
      removed_paper = next(n for n in graph_data['nodes'] if n['paper_id'] == paper_id)
      temporal = compute_temporal_gaps(graph_data, prune_result, removed_paper)
      # Generate narrative per phase
      narrative = llm_client.generate(config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL,
          COUNTER_HISTORY_PROMPT.format(
              paper=removed_paper, prune=prune_result, temporal=temporal))
      return jsonify({'prune_result': prune_result, 'narrative': narrative, 'temporal': temporal})

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```python
COUNTER_HISTORY_PROMPT = """You are narrating an alternate history of research.
Paper removed: {paper[title]} ({paper[year]})
Collapse: {prune[collapsed_count]} of {prune[total]} papers ({prune[impact_percentage]}%)
Wave 1 ({prune[wave_1_count]} papers): {prune[wave_1_titles]}
Wave 2 ({prune[wave_2_count]} papers): {prune[wave_2_titles]}
Surviving papers: {prune[surviving_count]} through alternative paths
Temporal gaps: {temporal[next_similar_paper]} fills the gap in {temporal[gap_years]} years
Most affected cluster: {temporal[most_affected_cluster]}

Write a 3-phase narrative:
Phase 1: Immediate impact (which direct dependents lose their foundation)
Phase 2: Cascading effects (how the loss propagates, what survives and why)
Phase 3: Alternate timeline (how the field would have evolved differently)

Use specific paper names and numbers. Be speculative but grounded in the data.
No em dashes. No emojis. 200-300 words total."""
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/counter-history
Request: { "paper_id": "...", "graph_id": "..." }
Response: {
  "prune_result": { "collapsed_count": 47, "impact_percentage": 31, "waves": [...] },
  "narrative": { "phase_1": "...", "phase_2": "...", "phase_3": "..." },
  "temporal": { "gap_years": 2, "next_similar_paper": "GoogLeNet" }
}

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Counter-History Narrative

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ○ COUNTER-HISTORY: What if ResNet never  │
  │    existed?                               │
  │                                           │
  │  ┌─ PHASE 1: Immediate Impact ────────┐   │
  │  │ Without ResNet, 8 direct dependents│   │
  │  │ lose their foundational method.    │   │
  │  │ DenseNet, ResNeXt, SENet...        │   │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                           │
  │  ┌─ PHASE 2: Cascading Effects ───────┐   │
  │  │ 15 papers in Wave 2 collapse. The  │   │
  │  │ attention cluster survives via...  │   │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                           │
  │  ┌─ PHASE 3: Alternate Timeline ──────┐   │
  │  │ GoogLeNet's approach would have    │   │
  │  │ filled the gap 2 years later...    │   │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                           │
  │  ┌─ FINAL STATS ──────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ Collapsed: 47 | Survived: 105      │   │
  │  │ Impact: 31% | Gap: 2 years         │   │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                           │
  │  ● Try removing VGGNet instead            │
  │  ● Show surviving paths                   │
  │  ● Reset and explore                      │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Each phase renders sequentially (500ms delay between phases). Phase headers have subtle gold accent. Graph animation synchronized with phase narration.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User types /whatif [paper]
  → Paper resolved from name
  → POST /api/athena/counter-history { paper_id, graph_id }
  → Backend runs compute_pruning_result (reuses existing pruning)
  → Computes temporal gaps and cluster impact
  → LLM generates 3-phase narrative from computed data
  → Response streams with phase-by-phase rendering
  → Graph animation plays (slowed) synchronized with narrative

ERROR PATH: Paper not found → "I could not find [paper] in this lineage."
  Paper has 0% impact → "Removing this paper does not collapse any others. It is a leaf node."
DATA STORED: chat_history with type='counter_history', action_log
DATA CACHED: Prune result cached per paper_id + graph_id

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (70b for speculative narrative quality)
STAGE: Post-computation (narrates computed pruning data)
PROMPT: 3-phase narrative with specific papers, waves, temporal gaps (see above)
OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with phase_1, phase_2, phase_3 narrative strings
FALLBACK: Show raw pruning stats without narrative
TOKEN BUDGET: ~600 context + ~400 output = ~1000 per counter-history

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Phase header         | Click       | Collapse/expand phase content                |
| Paper name in text   | Click       | Highlights paper in graph                   |
| [Try removing X]     | Click       | Runs counter-history for suggested paper     |
| [Show surviving]     | Click       | Highlights survival paths in graph           |
| [Reset]              | Click       | Restores graph to original state             |
| Graph animation      | Watch       | Synchronized with phase narration            |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Phase blocks render with 500ms stagger. Gold left border on phase headers. Graph animation at 0.5x speed.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Phase blocks as aria-expanded sections, paper names as links, stats as aria-labels

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Paper is a leaf node (0% impact)
   Expected: "Removing this paper has no cascading effect. It is a terminal node
   with no dependents. This suggests it represents a recent or specialized contribution."
   Implementation: Check collapsed_count, use simplified narrative for 0.

2. Scenario: Paper is THE root/seed paper
   Expected: Special handling: "Removing the seed paper would collapse the ENTIRE
   lineage. Every paper in this graph traces ancestry to this paper."
   Implementation: Detect seed paper, use dramatic root-removal narrative.

3. Scenario: Removed paper has surviving alternatives (low impact despite importance)
   Expected: Narrative emphasizes resilience: "Despite being the 3rd most cited,
   ResNet's removal only collapses 5% because alternative paths through [papers]
   provide redundant ancestry."
   Implementation: Include survival path analysis in narrative prompt.

4. Scenario: LLM generates historically inaccurate speculation
   Expected: All speculative claims are clearly marked as speculative. The narrative
   uses hedging language: "would likely have," "might have," "could have."
   Implementation: Prompt explicitly requires hedging language for Phase 3.

5. Scenario: User runs counter-history on 5 different papers rapidly
   Expected: Each generates independently. Previous graph animations reset before
   new one starts. No queuing issues.
   Implementation: Cancel previous animation before starting new one.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #001, #002, #005, #006, #037, #049
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Existing pruning system (adds narrative layer)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: pruning.py compute_pruning_result, GROQ_SMART_MODEL

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
□ /whatif command triggers counter-history for specified paper
□ Pruning simulation runs and computes collapse metrics
□ 3-phase narrative generated (immediate, cascading, alternate timeline)
□ Graph animation synchronized with narrative phases
□ Temporal gap analysis (next similar paper, years delayed)
□ Quick actions for follow-up explorations
□ Leaf node (0% impact) handled with appropriate narrative

VISUAL: □ Phase blocks with gold left borders, staggered rendering
PERFORMANCE: □ Narrative generation < 8 seconds
ACCESSIBILITY: □ Phase sections collapsible, paper links accessible
EDGE CASES: □ Root paper handled, leaf node handled, hedging language enforced

VERIFICATION: □ PESSIMISTIC AGENT complete □ BROWSER TEST verified □ CROSS-REFERENCE matches

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. READ files 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST high/low/zero impact papers 4. CHECK narrative grounding 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below
