  Entry cards: var(--bg-surface) with 1px border var(--bg-elevated)
  Backdrop: rgba(10, 14, 23, 0.6)

CONVERSATION ENTRY CARD:

  ┌───────────────────────────────┐
  │ ● What is the pruning impact  │    ● = has graph context (filled)
  │   of BERT?                    │    ○ = no graph context (open)
  │                               │
  │   15 turns  ○  Attention      │    ○ = separator dot
  │   10:00 AM                    │
  └───────────────────────────────┘

  Hover: border-left 3px solid var(--accent-gold), slight bg lighten
  Active (current): border-left 3px solid var(--accent-teal)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  User clicks history icon
       |
       v
  HistorySidebar.open()
       |
       v
  fetchConversations()
       |
       v
  GET /api/chat/conversations?session_id=X
       |
       v
  Backend: SELECT DISTINCT conversation groups from chat_history
           WHERE session_id = ? ORDER BY last_message_at DESC
       |
       v
  renderConversationList(conversations)
       |
       ├── groupByTimePeriod() -- Today, Yesterday, This Week, Earlier
       |
       └── Render entry cards with metadata
       |
       v
  User clicks an entry
       |
       v
  onConversationClick(conversationId)
       |
       ├── (if current conversation exists) -> archive confirmation (#047 flow)
       |
       v
  loadConversation(conversationId)
       |
       v
  GET /api/chat/conversation/:id
       |
       v
  Replace chat panel messages with loaded conversation
       |
       v
  contextManager.restoreContext(conversationId, graphId)
       |
       v
  HistorySidebar.close()

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROVIDER: Groq (indirectly - via title generation)
MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL for auto-generating conversation titles

TITLE GENERATION:
  When a conversation is archived (#047), a quick LLM call generates a concise
  title from the first user message. Prompt: "Generate a 6-word title for a
  conversation that starts with: [first message]". Cached in llm_cache.

INPUT: First user message of each conversation
OUTPUT: Short conversation title (max 50 chars)
FALLBACK: If title generation fails, use truncated first message as title
CACHE: Title cached in llm_cache (key = SHA256 of first message)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

 Trigger               Action                           Visual Feedback
 ───────────────────── ──────────────────────────────── ─────────────────────────
 Click history icon     Toggle sidebar                   Slide in/out from left
 Search typed           Filter conversations             Entries filter in real-time
 Click entry            Load conversation                Sidebar closes, chat updates
 Swipe left on entry    Show delete option               Red delete button revealed
 Click delete           Delete conversation              Entry slides out
 Click backdrop         Close sidebar                    Sidebar slides out
 Escape key             Close sidebar                    Sidebar slides out
 Empty history          Show empty state                 "No conversations yet"
 Loading conversations  Show skeleton cards              Pulse animation

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Session has 100+ conversations (heavy user)
   Expected: Conversations paginated. First 20 loaded, "Load more" at bottom.
   Implementation: API returns paginated results, sidebar lazy-loads.

2. Scenario: Conversation references a graph that no longer exists
   Expected: Entry shows graph title from metadata but marks it as "(graph
   expired)". Loading the conversation works but graph context is empty.
   Implementation: Conversation metadata stored with graph title at archive time.

3. Scenario: User opens sidebar while AI is streaming
   Expected: Sidebar opens normally. Streaming continues behind the backdrop.
   Implementation: Sidebar is an overlay, does not interrupt stream.

4. Scenario: Search returns no results
   Expected: "No conversations match your search" empty state.
   Implementation: Client-side filter shows empty state message.

5. Scenario: Delete the currently active conversation
   Expected: Not possible. Current conversation entry has no delete option.
   Use #047 New Chat/Clear instead.
   Implementation: Delete button hidden on active conversation entry.

6. Scenario: Anonymous user clears browser cookies
   Expected: Session lost. Conversation history inaccessible. Empty sidebar.
   Implementation: Server returns empty list for unknown session_id.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #033 (Chat Panel), #044 (Multi-Turn Context), #047 (New Chat)
ENABLES: Long-term research continuity across sessions
ENHANCES: #044 (Multi-Turn Context), exploration workflow
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: NONE

VERIFICATION:

================================================================================
================================================================================
==  FEATURE #049 — MODEL/MODE SELECTOR                                        ==
================================================================================
================================================================================

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
METADATA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Feature ID:       049
Feature Name:     Model/Mode Selector
Category:         Chat Controls
Priority:         P1
Phase:            B
Complexity:       Medium
Estimated Effort: 10 hours
Dependencies:     #033 (Chat Panel), #037 (Streaming Text), #044 (Multi-Turn Context)
Introduced:       2026-03-24
Status:           NOT_STARTED
Author:           Athena Core Team

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Model/Mode Selector allows the researcher to switch between different AI
analysis modes in the Athena chat panel. These modes are not different LLM
models exposed directly (the researcher never sees "llama-3.1-8b" vs "llama-
3.3-70b" as a choice). Instead, they are curated analysis personas that change
how the AI interprets questions and formulates responses about the citation
ancestry graph.

Arivu defines three core analysis modes that map to the existing persona system
(sessions.persona column) and the Groq model selection:

  1. EXPLORER mode (default) - Uses GROQ_FAST_MODEL. Responds quickly with
     concise overviews. Best for browsing the graph, getting quick summaries,
     asking about individual papers. The AI acts as a knowledgeable guide who
     gives direct answers about PageRank scores, mutation types, and graph
     topology.

  2. ANALYST mode - Uses GROQ_SMART_MODEL. Responds with deeper, more nuanced
     analysis. Best for understanding complex relationships, comparing multiple
     papers, tracing intellectual lineages across generations. The AI produces
     longer responses with evidence from edge_analysis data, confidence tiers,
     and structural patterns.

  3. STORYTELLER mode - Uses GROQ_SMART_MODEL with a different system prompt
     focused on narrative. Best for understanding the "story" of a research
     field - how ideas evolved, why certain paradigms won, what the turning
     points were. The AI produces engaging narrative-style responses that
     connect the graph data to the broader arc of scientific progress.

The mode selector appears as a segmented control in the chat panel header,
showing the three mode names. The active mode is indicated by a filled dot
(●) and gold underline. Inactive modes show open dots (○). Switching modes
does not clear the conversation history - the AI retains the same context
but changes its response style and depth.

Each mode has a distinct visual accent. Explorer uses the default gold accent.
Analyst uses the blue accent (var(--accent-blue)). Storyteller uses the teal
accent (var(--accent-teal)). When a mode is active, the chat panel header
border-bottom color subtly shifts to match the mode accent, providing a
persistent visual cue about the current analysis depth.

The mode selection is stored in the sessions table (persona column) and
persists across page reloads. It is also sent with each /api/chat request
so the backend can select the appropriate Groq model and system prompt.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER: Researcher wants to change the AI's analysis depth and style.

STEP 1: The researcher is in EXPLORER mode (default). They ask "What are the
most important papers in this graph?" and receive a concise list of top 5
papers by PageRank with brief descriptions.

STEP 2: The researcher wants deeper analysis. They click "ANALYST" in the
mode selector. The selector animates: the gold underline slides from Explorer
to Analyst, the header border-bottom shifts to blue.

STEP 3: The researcher asks the same question: "What are the most important
papers in this graph?" This time, the AI responds with a detailed multi-
paragraph analysis covering PageRank methodology, structural bottleneck
analysis, pruning impact for each top paper, confidence tiers of the
supporting edge classifications, and a comparison of importance metrics.

STEP 4: The researcher switches to STORYTELLER mode. They ask "Tell me the
story of how attention mechanisms evolved." The AI responds with a narrative
arc: the precursors, the breakthrough moment of Vaswani 2017, the adoption
wave, the generalization into new domains, the contradiction papers that
challenged the paradigm, and the current frontier.

STEP 5: The researcher switches back to EXPLORER for quick questions. The
conversation history from all three modes is preserved and the AI can
reference insights from earlier exchanges regardless of which mode generated
them.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:

  NEW:
    static/js/athena-mode-selector.js   Mode selector UI, state management
    static/css/athena-mode-selector.css Segmented control, mode accents, transitions

  MODIFIED:
    static/js/athena-chat.js            Include mode in message payload
    app.py                              /api/chat reads mode, selects model + prompt
    backend/chat_guide.py               Mode-specific system prompts and model selection
    backend/session_manager.py          Persist mode in sessions.persona

FRONTEND ARCHITECTURE:

  ModeSelector class renders a segmented control with three buttons:
  Explorer, Analyst, Storyteller. The active mode is stored in local state
  and synced to the backend via a PATCH /api/session/mode endpoint.

  When mode changes:
  1. Update visual state (slide underline, shift accent color)
  2. PATCH /api/session/mode to persist selection
  3. Dispatch "athena:mode:changed" event for other components to react
  4. The next chat message will use the new mode's model and system prompt

  The segmented control uses CSS transitions for smooth underline sliding.
  The underline width matches the active segment's text width. Mode accent
  colors are applied via CSS custom properties set on the chat panel container.

BACKEND ARCHITECTURE:

  The /api/chat endpoint reads the mode from the request payload and uses it
  to select:
  1. The Groq model: Explorer -> GROQ_FAST_MODEL, Analyst/Storyteller -> GROQ_SMART_MODEL
  2. The system prompt: each mode has a distinct prompt in chat_guide.py

  Mode-specific system prompts:

  EXPLORER: "You are a concise research guide. Give direct, factual answers
  about the citation graph. Use specific numbers (PageRank scores, pruning
  percentages, confidence tiers). Keep responses under 200 words."

  ANALYST: "You are a thorough research analyst. Provide detailed, evidence-
  based analysis of the citation graph. Cross-reference multiple data sources:
  edge_analysis classifications, structural metrics, temporal patterns. Support
  every claim with specific data from the graph."

  STORYTELLER: "You are a research storyteller. Weave the data from the citation
  graph into a compelling narrative about how ideas evolved. Connect the dots
  between papers, explain why certain innovations mattered, and make the
  intellectual history come alive. Use the graph data as evidence but present
  it as narrative, not statistics."

CODE PATTERNS:

  class ModeSelector {
    constructor(chatPanel, sessionId) { ... }
    render() { ... }
    setMode(mode) { ... }
    getMode() { ... }
    onModeChange(mode) { ... }
    updateVisuals(mode) { ... }
    persistMode(mode) { ... }  // PATCH /api/session/mode
  }

  MODE_CONFIG = {
    explorer: { model: 'fast', accent: '--accent-gold', icon: '○' },
    analyst:  { model: 'smart', accent: '--accent-blue', icon: '◐' },
    storyteller: { model: 'smart', accent: '--accent-teal', icon: '●' }
  }

API:

  PATCH /api/session/mode
    Request: { session_id, mode: "explorer"|"analyst"|"storyteller" }
    Response: { updated: true, mode: "analyst" }

  POST /api/chat (modified)
    Request: { session_id, message, graph_id, mode }
    (mode parameter added to existing endpoint)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODE SELECTOR IN CHAT HEADER:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Athena Research Guide                                              │
  │                                                                     │
  │  ┌──────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────────┐                │
  │  │ ● Explorer   │ ◐ Analyst    │ ○ Storyteller    │                │
  │  └──────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────────┘                │
  │  ═══════════                                           [+] [H] [...│
  │  (gold underline under active mode)                                │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

MODE ACCENT STATES:

  Explorer active:     Header border-bottom: var(--accent-gold)
                       Underline: var(--accent-gold)
                       Mode icon: ● (filled)

  Analyst active:      Header border-bottom: var(--accent-blue)
                       Underline: var(--accent-blue)
                       Mode icon: ◐ (half-filled)

  Storyteller active:  Header border-bottom: var(--accent-teal)
                       Underline: var(--accent-teal)
                       Mode icon: ● (filled, teal)

SEGMENTED CONTROL DETAILS:

  Background: var(--bg-surface)
  Active segment: text var(--text-primary), underline 2px solid accent
  Inactive segment: text var(--text-muted), no underline
  Hover: text var(--text-secondary)
  Transition: underline slides via transform translateX (200ms ease)
  Height: 32px
  Font: 13px Inter, font-weight 500

UNDERLINE SLIDE ANIMATION:

  Explorer -> Analyst:
    Underline translateX: 0 -> segmentWidth (200ms)
    Border-bottom color: gold -> blue (200ms)

  Analyst -> Storyteller:
    Underline translateX: segmentWidth -> 2*segmentWidth (200ms)
    Border-bottom color: blue -> teal (200ms)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  User clicks mode segment (e.g., "Analyst")
       |
       v
  ModeSelector.setMode("analyst")
       |
       ├── updateVisuals("analyst")
       |        |
       |        ├── Slide underline to Analyst segment
       |        ├── Shift header border-bottom to blue
       |        └── Update icon states
       |
       ├── persistMode("analyst")
       |        |
       |        └── PATCH /api/session/mode { session_id, mode: "analyst" }
       |                  |
       |                  v
       |             Backend: UPDATE sessions SET persona = 'analyst'
       |
       └── dispatch("athena:mode:changed", { mode: "analyst" })
       |
       v
  Next message sent includes mode: "analyst"
       |
       v
  /api/chat handler:
       |
       ├── mode == "analyst" -> model = config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL
       |
       └── chat_guide.py uses ANALYST system prompt
       |
       v
  Deeper, more analytical response streams back

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROVIDER: Groq
MODEL: Varies by mode
  Explorer:    config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant)
  Analyst:     config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile)
  Storyteller: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile)

SYSTEM PROMPTS: Three distinct prompts (see Backend Architecture above)
INPUT: Same context assembly as #044, but model and prompt vary by mode
OUTPUT: Response style and depth varies by mode
FALLBACK: If GROQ_SMART_MODEL is unavailable, all modes fall back to GROQ_FAST_MODEL
CACHE: Responses cached per normal llm_cache key (model included in hash key)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

 Trigger               Action                           Visual Feedback
 ───────────────────── ──────────────────────────────── ─────────────────────────
 Click mode segment     Switch active mode               Underline slide + color shift
 Mode changed           Persist to session               No visible feedback
 Send message           Mode included in payload         Response reflects mode
 Page reload            Mode restored from session       Correct segment highlighted
 During streaming       Mode switch disabled             Segments grayed out
 GROQ_SMART unavailable Analyst/Storyteller fall back    Toast: "Using standard model"
 Hover inactive mode    Highlight segment                Text brightens

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User switches mode mid-conversation
   Expected: Conversation history preserved. Only future responses use new mode.
   Previous responses retain their original style.
   Implementation: Mode is per-request, not retroactively applied.

2. Scenario: GROQ_SMART_MODEL rate limited or unavailable
   Expected: Analyst and Storyteller modes fall back to GROQ_FAST_MODEL.
   User warned via toast notification.
   Implementation: chat_guide.py catches Groq errors and falls back.

3. Scenario: User switches mode while AI is streaming
   Expected: Mode switch is disabled during streaming. Segments are visually
   dimmed and non-clickable until stream completes.
   Implementation: ModeSelector checks streaming state, disables interactions.

4. Scenario: Session persona column has unexpected value (e.g., from Phase 7 persona engine)
   Expected: Unknown persona values map to "explorer" mode as default.
   Implementation: ModeSelector validates against known modes, defaults to explorer.

5. Scenario: GROQ_API_KEY not set (no LLM available)
   Expected: Mode selector hidden entirely. Chat panel shows "AI features require
   configuration" message.
   Implementation: ModeSelector checks config.GROQ_ENABLED before rendering.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #033 (Chat Panel), #037 (Streaming Text), #044 (Multi-Turn Context)
ENABLES: Differentiated analysis workflows, Phase 7 persona system integration
ENHANCES: AI response quality and relevance
CONFLICTS: NONE (complements Phase 7 PersonaEngine)
EXTERNAL: Groq API (FAST + SMART models)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Three modes selectable: Explorer, Analyst, Storyteller
[ ] Mode persisted in sessions.persona column
[ ] Explorer uses GROQ_FAST_MODEL
[ ] Analyst and Storyteller use GROQ_SMART_MODEL
[ ] Each mode has distinct system prompt
[ ] Conversation history preserved across mode switches
[ ] Mode restored on page reload
[ ] Disabled during streaming

VISUAL:
[ ] Segmented control with sliding underline
[ ] Mode-specific accent colors (gold, blue, teal)
[ ] Header border-bottom shifts with mode
[ ] Dot matrix icons (filled, half, open) per mode
[ ] Smooth 200ms slide transitions

PERFORMANCE: [ ] Mode switch under 100ms (visual), persist under 500ms (API)
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Segmented control is a radio group with aria-labels
EDGE CASES: [ ] GROQ_SMART fallback, unknown persona default, no API key handled

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: All three modes tested with same question
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Compatible with sessions.persona, chat_guide.py

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. READ athena-mode-selector.js, chat_guide.py mode prompts
2. COMPARE criteria against implementation
3. TEST: Switch each mode, send same question, verify response style differs
4. TEST: Reload page, verify mode persisted
5. TEST: Switch during streaming, verify disabled
6. CHECK: Correct Groq model used per mode (inspect API logs)
7. ASSUME BROKEN until all criteria green
8. Minimum 1 fix per round. Log below.

================================================================================
================================================================================
==  FEATURE #050 — RICH INPUT AREA                                            ==
================================================================================
================================================================================

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
METADATA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Feature ID:       050
Feature Name:     Rich Input Area
Category:         Core Infrastructure
Priority:         P0
Phase:            A
Complexity:       Medium
Estimated Effort: 12 hours
Dependencies:     #033 (Chat Panel)
Introduced:       2026-03-24
Status:           NOT_STARTED
Author:           Athena Core Team

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Rich Input Area is the primary text entry component in the Athena chat
panel where the researcher composes messages to the AI. It goes beyond a
simple single-line text input by providing a multi-line, auto-expanding
textarea with intelligent features: keyboard shortcuts, character counting,
slash command support, mention autocomplete for paper titles, and visual
integration with the Arivu dot matrix aesthetic.

The input area uses a contenteditable div (not a <textarea>) to enable future
inline formatting, @-mentions with rich previews, and embedded graph node
references. However, for Phase A, the input remains plain text only - the
contenteditable foundation is laid but rich formatting is deferred to later
phases. The submitted text is always extracted as plain text via innerText
for processing.

Auto-expansion is the most visible feature. The input area starts at a single
line height (44px) and grows as the researcher types, up to a maximum of 6
lines (approximately 132px). After 6 lines, a scrollbar appears within the
input area. The expansion is smooth, animated with CSS transitions, and does
not cause the chat message history above to jump or re-layout unpredictably.

The send button sits to the right of the input area. It is enabled only when
the input contains non-whitespace text. The send action is triggered by
clicking the button or pressing Enter (without Shift). Shift+Enter inserts
a newline. This is the standard convention researchers expect from chat
interfaces. The send button uses the Arivu gold accent with a right-pointing
arrow icon rendered in the dot matrix style.

The input area includes a character counter that appears when the text
approaches the maximum length (2000 characters, matching the ChatRequest
Pydantic schema validation). The counter appears at 80% of the limit (1600
chars) and changes color as it approaches the max: text-secondary at 80%,
warning color at 95%, danger color at 100%. At 2000 characters, further
input is blocked.

A subtle placeholder text reads "Ask about the ancestry graph..." when the
input is empty. The placeholder fades out as the researcher begins typing and
fades back in when the input is cleared. The placeholder text is context-aware:
if a graph is loaded, it says "Ask about [graph title]...". If no graph is
loaded, it says "Search for a paper to begin...".

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER: Researcher focuses the input area to compose a message.

STEP 1: The researcher clicks into the input area. The placeholder text
"Ask about Attention Is All You Need..." fades out. The input border subtly
brightens from muted to the secondary text color, indicating focus.

STEP 2: The researcher types a multi-line question:
  "Can you compare the pruning impact of these three papers:
   1. Vaswani 2017 (Attention)
   2. Devlin 2018 (BERT)
   3. He 2016 (ResNet)"
The input area auto-expands to accommodate all 4 lines. Each line causes a
smooth height increase.

STEP 3: As the researcher types, the send button transitions from disabled
(dim, non-interactive) to enabled (gold, interactive). The character counter
is not visible yet because the text is well under 1600 characters.

STEP 4: The researcher presses Enter (without Shift). The message sends, the
input area collapses back to single-line height, the placeholder reappears,
and the send button returns to its disabled state.

STEP 5: For their next message, the researcher presses Shift+Enter to insert
a deliberate newline within their message, then continues typing on the new
line without triggering a send.

STEP 6: The researcher pastes a long text block from a paper abstract (1800
characters). The character counter appears: "1800 / 2000" in the text-secondary
color. They add more text and at 1900 characters the counter shifts to warning
color. At 2000, the input stops accepting characters and the counter turns red.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:

  NEW:
    static/js/athena-input.js           Input area logic, auto-expand, shortcuts
    static/css/athena-input.css         Input styling, expansion, counter, button

  MODIFIED:
    static/js/athena-chat.js            Wire input to message send pipeline
    static/css/athena-chat.css          Layout integration for input area

FRONTEND ARCHITECTURE:

  InputArea class manages the contenteditable div and associated controls:

  1. The main input element is a <div contenteditable="true"> with a
     placeholder overlay positioned behind it
  2. An input event listener triggers auto-expansion by comparing scrollHeight
     to clientHeight. If scrollHeight exceeds clientHeight, height is increased
     up to max-height (132px for ~6 lines)
  3. A keydown event listener handles:
     - Enter (no modifier): prevent default, call send()
     - Shift+Enter: allow default (insert newline)
     - Escape: blur input (if no dialog open)
     - Ctrl+A / Cmd+A: select all text in input
  4. An input event listener updates the character counter and enforces the
     2000-character limit by truncating excess text
  5. The send button listens for clicks and calls the same send() function

  The input area dispatches custom events:
  - "athena:input:send" with { text } when send is triggered
  - "athena:input:change" with { text, length } on every input change
  - "athena:input:focus" and "athena:input:blur" for focus tracking

BACKEND ARCHITECTURE:

  No backend changes. The input area is a pure frontend component. The
  text is sent to the existing POST /api/chat endpoint as the message field.
  Server-side validation via ChatRequest schema enforces the same 2000-char
  limit.

CODE PATTERNS:

  class InputArea {
    constructor(container, options = {}) { ... }
    render() { ... }
    getText() { ... }          // Returns plain text from contenteditable
    setText(text) { ... }      // Sets text programmatically (for #042 pill injection)
    clear() { ... }            // Clears input and resets height
    focus() { ... }
    blur() { ... }
    send() { ... }             // Extract text, validate, dispatch send event, clear
    onInput(event) { ... }     // Auto-expand + char counter update
    onKeyDown(event) { ... }   // Enter/Shift+Enter/Escape handling
    updateCharCounter() { ... }
    updatePlaceholder() { ... }
    setEnabled(enabled) { ... } // Disable during streaming
    autoExpand() { ... }
    collapse() { ... }
  }

API:

  No new API endpoints. Input text is sent via existing POST /api/chat.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT AREA - EMPTY STATE:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                      │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ ┌─┐ │
  │  │  Ask about Attention Is All You Need...                    │ │○│ │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ └─┘ │
  │                                                                  ^   │
  │                                                            Send btn  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Input: bg var(--bg-surface), border 1px solid var(--text-muted)
  Placeholder: color var(--text-muted), italic
  Send button: 36x36px, bg transparent, border none, color var(--text-muted)

INPUT AREA - FOCUSED WITH TEXT:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                      │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ ┌─┐ │
  │  │  Can you compare the pruning impact of these three papers:│ │●│ │
  │  │  1. Vaswani 2017 (Attention)                              │ │ │ │
  │  │  2. Devlin 2018 (BERT)                                    │ │ │ │
  │  │  3. He 2016 (ResNet)                                      │ └─┘ │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  │                                                                      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Input: border 1px solid var(--text-secondary) (brightened on focus)
  Send button: bg var(--accent-gold), color var(--bg-primary), ● filled

INPUT AREA - NEAR CHARACTER LIMIT:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                      │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ ┌─┐ │
  │  │  (long text content, 6 lines max, scrollbar inside)       │ │●│ │
  │  │  ...                                                      │ │ │ │
  │  │  ...                                                      │ │ │ │
  │  │  ...                                                      │ │ │ │
  │  │  ...                                                      │ │ │ │
  │  │  ...                                                      │ └─┘ │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  │                                              1850 / 2000           │
  │                                              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^          │
  │                                              warning color          │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

SEND BUTTON STATES:

  Disabled (no text):   ○  color: var(--text-muted)
                           background: transparent
                           cursor: not-allowed

  Enabled (has text):   ●  color: var(--bg-primary)
                           background: var(--accent-gold)
                           cursor: pointer
                           border-radius: 50%

  Hover:                ●  background: lighten(var(--accent-gold), 10%)
                           transform: scale(1.05)

  Active (pressed):     ●  transform: scale(0.95)

CHARACTER COUNTER THRESHOLDS:

  < 80% (< 1600):      Hidden
  80-95% (1600-1900):   color: var(--text-secondary)    "1750 / 2000"
  95-100% (1900-2000):  color: var(--warning)            "1950 / 2000"
  100% (2000):          color: var(--danger)             "2000 / 2000"

AUTO-EXPAND HEIGHTS:

  Min height:   44px (1 line)
  Per line:     ~22px (at 16px font, 1.375 line-height)
  Max height:   132px (6 lines)
  Transition:   height 100ms ease

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  User types in input area
       |
       v
  onInput(event)
       |
       ├── autoExpand() -- compare scrollHeight vs clientHeight, grow if needed
       ├── updateCharCounter() -- show/hide counter, update color
       ├── updatePlaceholder() -- hide if text present, show if empty
       └── dispatch("athena:input:change", { text, length })
       |
       v
  User presses Enter
       |
       v
  onKeyDown(event)
       |
       ├── Shift held? -> allow default (newline), return
       |
       └── No shift -> prevent default
              |
              v
         send()
              |
              ├── getText() -- extract plain text from contenteditable
              ├── Validate: non-empty, <= 2000 chars
              ├── dispatch("athena:input:send", { text })
              ├── clear() -- reset input to empty state
              └── collapse() -- return to min height
              |
              v
         athena-chat.js receives "athena:input:send" event
              |
              v
         Constructs payload { session_id, message: text, graph_id, mode }
              |
              v
         POST /api/chat

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROVIDER: None directly. Input text is passed to LLM via existing chat pipeline.
MODEL: N/A (determined by mode selector #049)
INPUT: User-composed plain text (max 2000 chars)
OUTPUT: N/A (input component only)
FALLBACK: N/A
CACHE: N/A

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

 Trigger               Action                           Visual Feedback
 ───────────────────── ──────────────────────────────── ─────────────────────────
 Focus input            Brighten border                  Border color shift
 Type text              Auto-expand height               Smooth height growth
 Enter (no shift)       Send message                     Input clears, collapses
 Shift+Enter            Insert newline                   Height grows
 Escape                 Blur input                       Border dims
 Text > 1600 chars      Show character counter           Counter appears
 Text > 1900 chars      Counter turns warning            Yellow counter
 Text = 2000 chars      Block further input              Red counter
 Paste long text        Truncate to 2000 chars           Counter shows immediately
 Clear input            Show placeholder                 Placeholder fades in
 Click send button      Send message (same as Enter)     Button depresses
 During streaming       Input disabled                   Dimmed, non-editable

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User pastes text exceeding 2000 characters
   Expected: Text truncated to 2000 characters. Character counter shows
   "2000 / 2000" in red. User notified that text was truncated.
   Implementation: onInput handler checks length, truncates if over limit.

2. Scenario: User types only whitespace and presses Enter
   Expected: Send does not fire. Input treated as empty.
   Implementation: getText().trim() check before dispatching send event.

3. Scenario: ContentEditable produces HTML on paste (e.g., from a webpage)
   Expected: HTML stripped. Only plain text preserved.
   Implementation: "paste" event handler strips HTML via clipboard API or
   div.innerText extraction.

4. Scenario: User types very quickly (fast typist, 120 WPM)
   Expected: Auto-expand does not lag or cause visual jitter.
   Implementation: Auto-expand uses requestAnimationFrame debounce.

5. Scenario: Input contains special characters (angle brackets, ampersands)
   Expected: Characters preserved as-is. No HTML interpretation.
   Implementation: getText() uses innerText, not innerHTML.

6. Scenario: Mobile browser with virtual keyboard
   Expected: Input area visible above keyboard. Chat panel adjusts viewport.
   Implementation: CSS uses position: sticky for input, or viewport height
   calculation accounts for keyboard.

7. Scenario: Screen reader user navigates to input area
   Expected: Input announced as "Message input, ask about [graph title]".
   Role="textbox", aria-label set, aria-describedby points to counter.
   Implementation: Proper ARIA attributes on contenteditable div.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #033 (Chat Panel)
ENABLES: #042 (Suggestions - pill text injection), #043 (Attachments - paperclip button), #049 (Mode Selector - layout integration)
ENHANCES: Every chat interaction in Athena
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: NONE (pure HTML/CSS/JS)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL

1. READ athena-input.js, athena-input.css
2. COMPARE criteria against implementation
3. TEST: Type multi-line text, verify auto-expand
4. TEST: Enter sends, Shift+Enter newline
5. TEST: Paste HTML, verify plain text extraction

8. ASSUME BROKEN until all criteria green

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

[Empty - feature not yet implemented]

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STATUS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            P0 Phase A. Foundation for all chat input. 12 hours estimated.
The graph contains PageRank scores, mutation_type classifications, pruning
impact percentages, and citation_intent labels. When Athena references any of
these, it can point to the exact data source rather than asking the user to
trust a language model's assertion.

Citations come in three flavors. Paper citations reference a specific paper
node in the graph and display as numbered markers like [1] or [2] that expand
to show title, year, and citation count. Edge citations reference a specific
relationship between two papers and display the mutation_type and confidence
tier. Data citations reference computed metrics (PageRank score, pruning
impact percentage, DNA cluster membership) and display the raw number with
its source formula.

The citation index appears at the bottom of the response, following academic
convention. Each entry in the index shows the full reference: paper title,
authors, year, venue, and a link to open the paper in the graph. Edge
references show both papers and the classified relationship. Data references
show the metric name, value, and computation source.

The LLM is instructed to produce citation markers in its output using a
structured format: {cite:paper_id} for paper citations, {cite:edge_id}
for edge citations, and {cite:metric:name} for data citations. The frontend
parser detects these markers during streaming and replaces them with
interactive citation chips. If the LLM produces a citation marker that does
not match any known entity in the graph, the marker is rendered as plain text
with no link, preventing dead references.

The dot matrix aesthetic of Arivu extends to citations. Citation markers
render as small gold dots with the number inside, matching the accent-gold
palette. The citation index at the bottom uses the JetBrains Mono font for
reference numbers and Inter for the text, creating a clear visual hierarchy
between the marker and the reference content.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User asks Athena a question about the graph. Athena's response includes
claims that can be backed by specific papers, edges, or metrics in the
current lineage.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "What are the most influential papers in this lineage?" Athena
   begins streaming a response with inline citation markers.
2. Text streams: "The most influential paper is [1] with a PageRank of
   0.0847 [d1], contributing to 31% of downstream work [d2]."
3. Citation chips render inline as small gold dots. [1] shows paper info on
   hover. [d1] and [d2] show metric sources on hover.
4. As the response continues, more citations appear. Each is numbered
   sequentially within the response.
5. When streaming completes, a citation index appears at the bottom of the
   response, listing all references with full details.
6. User clicks [1]. The graph pans to highlight that paper node. The right
   panel shows paper details.
7. User hovers [d2]. A tooltip shows: "Pruning impact: 31.0% -- computed
   by removing node and counting collapsed dependents via BFS."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Response with interactive inline citations visible. Citation index at bottom.
Hovering any citation shows source details. Clicking navigates to graph node
or edge.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-citations.js: Citation parser, hover preview, click nav.
- static/js/athena-stream.js: Citation marker detection during streaming.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Citation index block renderer.
- static/css/athena-citations.css: Citation chip styles, index layout.
- app.py: Citation data lookup endpoint.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class CitationParser {
    constructor(graphData) {
      this.graphData = graphData;
      this.citations = [];
      this.counter = 0;
    }

    parse(text) {
      return text.replace(
        /\{\{cite:(paper|edge|metric):([^}]+)\}\}/g,
        (match, type, id) => this.renderCitation(type, id)
      );
    }

    renderCitation(type, id) {
      this.counter++;
      const data = this.resolve(type, id);
      if (!data) return `[${this.counter}]`;
      this.citations.push({ num: this.counter, type, id, data });
      return `<span class="cite-chip" data-cite="${this.counter}"
        data-type="${type}" data-id="${id}"
        aria-label="Citation ${this.counter}: ${data.label}"
        role="button" tabindex="0">[${this.counter}]</span>`;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  # In app.py -- citation data resolution
  @app.route('/api/athena/citation/<cite_type>/<cite_id>')
  def get_citation_data(cite_type, cite_id):
      if cite_type == 'paper':
          row = db.fetchone("SELECT * FROM papers WHERE paper_id=%s", [cite_id])
          if not row: return jsonify({"error": "not_found"}), 404
          return jsonify({"title": row["title"], "year": row["year"],
                         "citation_count": row["citation_count"]})
      elif cite_type == 'edge':
          row = db.fetchone("SELECT * FROM edge_analysis WHERE edge_id=%s", [cite_id])
          if not row: return jsonify({"error": "not_found"}), 404
          return jsonify({"mutation_type": row["mutation_type"],
                         "confidence_tier": get_confidence_tier(row["mutation_confidence"])})

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```css
.cite-chip {
  display: inline-flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
  min-width: 20px; height: 20px; border-radius: 10px;
  background: var(--accent-gold); color: var(--bg-primary);
  font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: 11px;
  cursor: pointer; vertical-align: super; margin: 0 2px;
  transition: background 150ms ease;
}
.cite-chip:hover { background: var(--accent-blue); }
.cite-chip[data-type="metric"] { background: var(--accent-teal); }
```

API CONTRACTS:
  GET /api/athena/citation/<cite_type>/<cite_id>
  Response 200: { "title": "...", "year": 2017, ... }
  Response 404: { "error": "not_found" }

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Response with Inline Citations

  +-------------------------------------------+
  |  Athena:                                  |
  |  The most influential paper is            |
  |  Attention Is All You Need (2017) [1]     |
  |  with a PageRank of 0.0847 [d1],         |
  |  contributing to 31% of downstream        |
  |  work [d2]. Its core innovation was       |
  |  generalized by BERT [2] and adopted      |
  |  by GPT-2 [3].                            |
  |                                           |
  |  [1] = gold chip, [d1][d2] = teal chips  |
  |                                           |
  |  --- References ---                       |
  |  [1] Vaswani et al. (2017) "Attention     |
  |      Is All You Need" -- 216,247 cites    |
  |  [2] Devlin et al. (2018) "BERT"          |
  |  [3] Radford et al. (2019) "GPT-2"       |
  |  [d1] PageRank: 0.0847 (graph engine)    |
  |  [d2] Pruning impact: 31.0% (BFS)        |
  +-------------------------------------------+

STATE: Hover on Citation Chip

  +-------------------------------------------+
  |  ... contributing to 31% [d2] ...         |
  |                    +--------------------+ |
  |                    | Pruning Impact     | |
  |                    | Value: 31.0%       | |
  |                    | Method: BFS cascade| |
  |                    | Seed: Vaswani 2017 | |
  |                    +--------------------+ |
  +-------------------------------------------+

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

LLM generates text with {{cite:paper:abc123}} markers
  -> Streaming parser detects marker pattern
  -> CitationParser.renderCitation() resolves against graphData
  -> If match found: render interactive chip with number
  -> If no match: render plain text number (no link)
  -> All citations collected in this.citations array
  -> On stream complete: render citation index block at bottom
  -> User hover on chip -> tooltip with source details
  -> User click on chip -> graph.focusNode(paper_id) or graph.focusEdge(edge_id)

DATA STORED: Citations array per response in chat_history metadata
DATA CACHED: Citation resolution results in memory (same graph session)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: Yes

MODEL: Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile (needs structured output with markers)
STAGE: Generation (citation markers embedded in response text)

PROMPT SECTION: "CITATION FORMAT RULES" appended to system prompt

ADDITIONAL PROMPT:
```
CITATION FORMAT RULES:
When referencing a paper in the graph, use: {{cite:paper:<paper_id>}}
When referencing a relationship, use: {{cite:edge:<citing_id>:<cited_id>}}
When referencing a metric, use: {{cite:metric:<metric_name>}}

Available metrics: pagerank, pruning_impact, citation_count, mutation_confidence
Always cite specific data points. Never state numbers without citation markers.
Use exact values from the graph data, never approximate or fabricate.
```

EXPECTED OUTPUT FORMAT:
Text with embedded citation markers, e.g.:
"The transformer architecture {{cite:paper:204e3073870fae3d05bcbc2f6a8e263d9b72e776}}
introduced self-attention, achieving a PageRank of 0.0847 {{cite:metric:pagerank}}."

FALLBACK: If LLM fails to include citation markers, response renders as plain
text with no citations. No error shown to user.

TOKEN BUDGET: ~50 extra tokens per response for citation markers (negligible)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Citation chip [N]    | Hover       | Tooltip with source details                 |
| Citation chip [N]    | Click       | Navigate to paper/edge in graph             |
| Citation chip [N]    | Keyboard    | Enter/Space activates click behavior        |
| Citation index entry | Click       | Same as chip click for that reference       |
| Dead citation        | Hover       | No tooltip (plain text, no interaction)     |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Chip hover scales 1.1x over 100ms. Tooltip fades in 150ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Each chip has aria-label with citation details. Index is a list with role="list".

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: LLM generates citation marker for paper not in current graph
   Expected: Marker renders as plain text [N] with no link. No tooltip.
   Implementation: CitationParser.resolve() returns null for unknown IDs.

2. Scenario: LLM generates malformed citation marker (missing closing braces)
   Expected: Raw text shown, not parsed as citation.
   Implementation: Regex requires exact {{cite:type:id}} format.

3. Scenario: Response has 20+ citations (very detailed response)
   Expected: All render correctly. Index at bottom is scrollable if long.
   Implementation: No artificial limit on citation count.

4. Scenario: Same paper cited multiple times in one response
   Expected: Same number used each time. One entry in index.
   Implementation: CitationParser deduplicates by paper_id.

5. Scenario: User is on mobile (small screen)
   Expected: Citation chips are touch-friendly (min 44px tap target).
   Implementation: Chips have padding for touch. Tooltips appear on tap.

6. Scenario: Graph data not loaded yet when citations are parsed
   Expected: Citations render as plain numbers. Re-resolve when graph loads.
   Implementation: Deferred resolution queue.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #037 (Streaming Text), #002 (Rich Response Formatting)
ENABLES: #058 (Paper Citation Card Block -- uses same citation resolution)
ENHANCES: #001 (Context-Aware Responses -- citations ground the context)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Groq API (for structured citation output)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] LLM produces citation markers in responses
[ ] Paper citations resolve to correct graph nodes
[ ] Edge citations resolve to correct relationships
[ ] Data citations resolve to correct metrics
[ ] Citation index appears at response bottom
[ ] Hover shows source preview tooltip
[ ] Click navigates to graph node/edge
[ ] Dead citations render as plain text

VISUAL:
[ ] Gold chips for paper citations
[ ] Teal chips for metric citations
[ ] JetBrains Mono for reference numbers
[ ] Tooltip matches Arivu design system

PERFORMANCE: [ ] Citation resolution < 5ms per marker (in-memory graph lookup)
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Chips have aria-labels, keyboard navigable, index is list
EDGE CASES: [ ] Malformed markers, unknown IDs, duplicate citations, mobile touch

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. READ athena-citations.js, athena-stream.js, CSS. 2. COMPARE citation markers in LLM output against graph data. 3. TEST hover/click on each citation type (paper, edge, metric). 4. CHECK dead citation handling for unknown IDs. 5. VERIFY citation index matches inline markers. 6. BROKEN until all citation types resolve correctly. 7. At least 1 fix per round. 8. Log below.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FEATURE #052: REACTIONS/BOOKMARKS
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Message Actions
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 10
OVERLAPS WITH:   #023 (AI Disagreement Button)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #006 (Conversation Memory)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Reactions and Bookmarks let researchers mark individual Athena messages with
lightweight signals that persist across the session. A researcher exploring a
lineage graph with 152 nodes and 203 edges will receive dozens of Athena
responses during a session. Some are immediately useful ("The pruning impact
of removing Vaswani 2017 is 31%"). Others are interesting but not urgent
("The mutation_type distribution shows 42% adoption, 23% generalization").
Others are wrong or unhelpful. Without a way to mark these, the researcher
must remember which responses mattered, scroll back to find them, or lose
them entirely.

Reactions are quick emotional signals: a thumbs-up (useful), thumbs-down (not
useful), lightbulb (insightful), and a question mark (confusing). They appear
as a floating action bar when the user hovers over any Athena response.
Clicking a reaction stores the signal in the action_log table, tagged to the
specific message ID within the chat_history. Reactions also feed into Athena's
learning loop: messages with thumbs-down are flagged for the Pessimistic Agent
to review, while lightbulb reactions signal high-value response patterns.

Bookmarks are a separate mechanism. Clicking the bookmark icon on any Athena
response saves that message to a persistent bookmarks panel accessible from
the sidebar. Bookmarked messages are stored with their full context: the user
question that prompted them, the graph state at the time, and the response
content. This means a researcher can return to a bookmarked insight days later
and still understand the context.

The bookmark panel uses the dot matrix aesthetic. Each bookmark entry shows a
truncated preview of the response (first 100 characters), the timestamp, and
the graph seed paper title. Clicking a bookmark restores the chat context and
scrolls to the original message. If the graph session has expired, the bookmark
still shows the response text but the graph navigation link is grayed out.

Reactions integrate with the edge_feedback system. If a user reacts with
thumbs-down to a response that contained specific mutation_type claims, the
system logs this as potential evidence of misclassification. This feeds into
the confidence layer: edges referenced in thumbs-down responses get their
flagged_by_users count incremented, which affects the displayed confidence
tier over time.

The design follows Arivu's minimal aesthetic. Reaction icons are 16px, drawn
in text-muted color until selected, when they shift to their semantic color
(thumbs-up: success green, thumbs-down: danger red, lightbulb: accent-gold,
question: info blue). Bookmarks use the accent-gold color when active.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User hovers over any Athena response message. The reaction/bookmark bar
appears at the top-right corner of the message bubble.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User hovers over an Athena response about DNA cluster composition. A small
   floating bar appears: [thumbs-up] [thumbs-down] [lightbulb] [?] [bookmark].
2. User clicks the lightbulb icon. It fills with accent-gold color and a subtle
   pulse animation plays (200ms).
3. The reaction is stored immediately. The bar remains visible for 2 seconds,
   then fades unless the user is still hovering.
4. Later, user hovers a different response that contains a wrong claim about
   mutation_type. They click thumbs-down. The icon fills red.
5. User clicks the bookmark icon on a third response. A small toast appears:
   "Bookmarked." The icon stays gold.
6. User opens the bookmarks panel from the sidebar. They see all bookmarked
   messages with previews and timestamps.
7. User clicks a bookmark. The chat scrolls to that message with a brief
   highlight animation (gold border flash, 300ms).

AFTER COMPLETION:
Reactions stored in action_log. Bookmarks accessible from sidebar. Thumbs-down
reactions linked to edge_feedback for confidence tracking.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-reactions.js: Reaction bar component, click handlers, API calls.
- static/js/athena-bookmarks.js: Bookmark storage, panel rendering, restore logic.
- static/css/athena-reactions.css: Reaction bar styles, bookmark panel styles.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/reaction, GET /api/athena/bookmarks endpoints.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class ReactionBar {
    constructor(messageEl, messageId) {
      this.messageEl = messageEl;
      this.messageId = messageId;
      this.reactions = ['thumbsup', 'thumbsdown', 'lightbulb', 'question'];
      this.active = null;
    }

    render() {
      const bar = document.createElement('div');
      bar.className = 'reaction-bar';
      bar.setAttribute('role', 'toolbar');
      bar.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Message reactions');
      this.reactions.forEach(r => {
        const btn = document.createElement('button');
        btn.className = `reaction-btn reaction-${r}`;
        btn.setAttribute('aria-label', r);
        btn.onclick = () => this.toggle(r);
        bar.appendChild(btn);
      });
      return bar;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/reaction', methods=['POST'])
  def add_reaction():
      data = request.json
      session_id = session.get('session_id')
      log_action(session_id, 'message_reaction', {
          'message_id': data['message_id'],
          'reaction': data['reaction'],
          'graph_id': data.get('graph_id')
      })
      if data['reaction'] == 'thumbsdown' and data.get('edge_ids'):
          for eid in data['edge_ids']:
              db.execute(
                  "UPDATE edge_feedback SET flagged_by_users = flagged_by_users + 1 "
                  "WHERE edge_id = %s", [eid]
              )
      return jsonify({"status": "ok"})

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```css
.reaction-bar {
  position: absolute; top: -28px; right: 8px;
  display: flex; gap: 4px; padding: 4px 8px;
  background: var(--bg-elevated); border-radius: 16px;
  opacity: 0; transition: opacity 150ms ease;
  box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);
}
.athena-message:hover .reaction-bar { opacity: 1; }
.reaction-btn { width: 24px; height: 24px; border: none; background: none;
  color: var(--text-muted); cursor: pointer; border-radius: 50%; }
.reaction-btn.active.reaction-thumbsup { color: var(--success); }
.reaction-btn.active.reaction-thumbsdown { color: var(--danger); }
.reaction-btn.active.reaction-lightbulb { color: var(--accent-gold); }
.reaction-btn.active.reaction-question { color: var(--info); }
```

API CONTRACTS:
  POST /api/athena/reaction
  Body: { "message_id": "...", "reaction": "thumbsup|thumbsdown|lightbulb|question", "edge_ids": [...] }
  Response 200: { "status": "ok" }

  GET /api/athena/bookmarks?session_id=...
  Response 200: { "bookmarks": [{ "message_id": "...", "preview": "...", "timestamp": "...", "graph_seed": "..." }] }

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Hover on Athena Message (Reaction Bar Visible)

  +-------------------------------------------+
  |                 [thumbsup][thumbsdown]     |
  |                 [lightbulb][?][bookmark]   |
  |  Athena: The DNA cluster analysis shows   |
  |  three dominant strands: Attention (42%),  |
  |  Representation Learning (31%), and       |
  |  Sequence Modeling (27%).                 |
  +-------------------------------------------+

STATE: Bookmark Panel Open

  +-------------------------------------------+
  | BOOKMARKS                            [x]  |
  | ----------------------------------------- |
  | ● "The DNA cluster analysis shows three   |
  |   dominant strands: Attention (42%)..."    |
  |   -- Vaswani 2017 lineage -- 2m ago       |
  |                                           |
  | ● "Pruning Vaswani removes 31% of the    |
  |   graph, including all transformer..."    |
  |   -- Vaswani 2017 lineage -- 5m ago       |
  |                                           |
  | ○ No more bookmarks                       |
  +-------------------------------------------+

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User hovers message -> reaction bar appears (CSS only, no JS needed for show/hide)
  -> User clicks reaction icon
  -> POST /api/athena/reaction with message_id and reaction type
  -> Backend stores in action_log table
  -> If thumbs-down: edge_ids extracted from message citations
  -> edge_feedback.flagged_by_users incremented for each edge
  -> Frontend: icon color changes to active state
  -> Reaction persisted for session duration in local memory

Bookmark flow:
User clicks bookmark icon -> message data saved to localStorage + backend
  -> Toast "Bookmarked" appears (1.5s auto-dismiss)
  -> Sidebar bookmark count updates
  -> User opens bookmark panel -> GET /api/athena/bookmarks
  -> Click bookmark -> scrollToMessage(message_id) with highlight animation

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: No (reactions are user input, not LLM output)

MODEL: NOT APPLICABLE
STAGE: NOT APPLICABLE

PROMPT SECTION: NOT APPLICABLE

However, reaction data feeds INTO future LLM calls. When constructing the
context for Athena's system prompt, messages with lightbulb reactions are
weighted higher in the conversation summary. Messages with thumbs-down are
excluded from context to prevent reinforcing bad patterns.

FALLBACK: NOT APPLICABLE
TOKEN BUDGET: NOT APPLICABLE

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Athena message       | Hover       | Reaction bar fades in at top-right          |
| Reaction icon        | Click       | Toggle reaction on/off, color change        |
| Reaction icon        | Hover       | Tooltip with reaction name                  |
| Bookmark icon        | Click       | Save to bookmarks, toast confirmation       |
| Bookmark panel entry | Click       | Scroll to original message with highlight   |
| Bookmark panel entry | Hover       | Full preview tooltip                        |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Reaction bar fade-in 150ms. Active reaction pulse 200ms. Bookmark toast 1.5s.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Toolbar role on reaction bar, aria-pressed on toggle buttons, bookmark list navigable.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User reacts to a message, then the session expires
   Expected: Reaction stored in action_log persists. Bookmark in localStorage survives.
   Implementation: Reactions sent to backend immediately on click.

2. Scenario: User clicks thumbs-up then thumbs-down on same message
   Expected: Previous reaction is replaced. Only one reaction per message.
   Implementation: Toggle logic replaces active reaction.

3. Scenario: Bookmarked message references a graph that has been evicted from cache
   Expected: Bookmark text still visible. Graph link grayed out with "(expired)" label.
   Implementation: Check graph existence on bookmark panel open.

4. Scenario: User bookmarks 50+ messages in one session
   Expected: All stored. Panel is scrollable. No performance degradation.
   Implementation: Virtual scrolling if bookmark count exceeds 20.

5. Scenario: Screen reader user navigating reactions
   Expected: Toolbar announced. Each button has label. Active state announced.
   Implementation: role="toolbar", aria-pressed, aria-label on each button.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #006 (Conversation Memory)
ENABLES: Confidence layer feedback loop (edge_feedback integration)
ENHANCES: #023 (AI Disagreement Button -- shares thumbs-down signal)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: NONE

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

[ ] Reaction bar appears on hover over Athena messages
[ ] Four reaction types work (toggle on/off)
[ ] Only one reaction per message at a time
[ ] Reactions stored in action_log
[ ] Thumbs-down increments edge_feedback.flagged_by_users
[ ] Bookmark saves message with context
[ ] Bookmark panel shows all bookmarks
[ ] Click bookmark scrolls to original message

VISUAL:

[ ] Bookmark panel matches dot matrix aesthetic
[ ] Toast notification on bookmark action
[ ] Highlight animation on bookmark navigation

PERFORMANCE: [ ] Reaction POST completes < 100ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Toolbar role, aria-pressed, keyboard navigable

FEATURE #053: SHARE INDIVIDUAL MESSAGES
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Export & Sharing
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           G
ESTIMATED HOURS: 12
OVERLAPS WITH:   #011 (Artifact Cards)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #051 (Inline Citations)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Share Individual Messages lets a researcher export a single Athena response as
a standalone artifact. When Athena produces a particularly insightful analysis
of a paper lineage, the researcher should be able to share that specific
message with a colleague, embed it in a presentation, or save it as a
reference. This feature extracts a single message from the conversation,
packages it with its context (the question that prompted it, the graph seed
paper, the citations), and produces a shareable output.

Three share formats are supported. Copy to clipboard produces a rich text
version with formatted citations and a header showing the Arivu source.
Export as image generates a PNG screenshot of the message as rendered in the
Athena UI, including the dot matrix background and citation chips. Share as
link generates a unique URL that renders the message in a read-only view with
the citation index visible.

The share link is the most complex format. It creates a row in the
shared_graphs table (reusing the Phase 7 infrastructure) with the message
content, citation data, and a snapshot of the relevant graph metadata. The
shared link renders the message using the same CSS and block renderers as the
live chat, but in a static read-only mode. Citations in the shared view link
to Semantic Scholar paper pages rather than the Arivu graph (since the viewer
may not have access to the original session).

The image export uses html2canvas to capture the message DOM element as a PNG.
The capture includes the Athena avatar, the message content, citation chips,
any embedded blocks (Paper Cards, Data Tables), and a footer with "Generated
by Arivu -- arivu.app" branding. The image uses the dark background
(bg-primary) and produces a clean, presentation-ready screenshot.

For the clipboard format, the system converts the HTML message content to
rich text with academic formatting. Citation chips become inline references
like "(Vaswani et al., 2017)". Metric citations become plain values.
Markdown headers become bold text. The clipboard output is suitable for
pasting into Google Docs, Notion, or email.

The share button appears in the reaction bar (Feature #052) as an additional
icon. Clicking it opens a small dropdown with the three format options.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User clicks the share icon on any Athena response message. A dropdown appears
with three options: Copy, Image, Link.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User hovers over an Athena response about PageRank distribution in the
   lineage. The reaction bar appears with the share icon.
2. User clicks the share icon. A dropdown appears: "Copy to clipboard,"
   "Save as image," "Create share link."
3. User clicks "Copy to clipboard." The message content is converted to rich
   text with academic citations and copied. Toast: "Copied to clipboard."
4. User pastes into Google Docs. The formatted text appears with proper
   citations like "(Vaswani et al., 2017)" instead of chip markers.
5. Alternatively, user clicks "Save as image." A brief capture animation plays
   (100ms flash), then a PNG downloads with the message rendered on the dark
   Arivu background.
6. Or user clicks "Create share link." A unique URL is generated and copied
   to clipboard. Toast: "Share link copied." The link opens a read-only view.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Message shared in chosen format. No change to the chat state. Share action
logged in action_log.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-share.js: Share dropdown, clipboard logic, image capture, link generation.
- static/css/athena-share.css: Dropdown styles, image capture overrides.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/share (create share link), GET /shared/message/<id> (render shared).
- templates/shared_message.html: Read-only shared message template.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class MessageShareHandler {
    async copyToClipboard(messageEl) {
      const html = messageEl.innerHTML;
      const text = this.htmlToAcademic(html);
      await navigator.clipboard.write([
        new ClipboardItem({
          'text/html': new Blob([html], { type: 'text/html' }),
          'text/plain': new Blob([text], { type: 'text/plain' })
        })
      ]);
    }

    async saveAsImage(messageEl) {
      const canvas = await html2canvas(messageEl, {
        backgroundColor: '#0a0e17',
        scale: 2,
        useCORS: true
      });
      const link = document.createElement('a');
      link.download = `arivu-insight-${Date.now()}.png`;
      link.href = canvas.toDataURL('image/png');
      link.click();
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/share', methods=['POST'])
  def create_share_link():
      data = request.json
      share_id = secrets.token_urlsafe(16)
      db.execute(
          "INSERT INTO shared_graphs (share_id, graph_data, created_at) "
          "VALUES (%s, %s, NOW())",
          [share_id, json.dumps({
              'type': 'message',
              'content': data['content'],
              'citations': data['citations'],
              'seed_paper': data['seed_paper'],
              'timestamp': data['timestamp']
          })]
      )
      return jsonify({"share_url": f"/shared/message/{share_id}"})

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
// Convert citation chips to academic format
htmlToAcademic(html) {
  const div = document.createElement('div');
  div.innerHTML = html;
  div.querySelectorAll('.cite-chip').forEach(chip => {
    const data = this.citations[chip.dataset.cite];
    if (data && data.type === 'paper') {
      chip.replaceWith(`(${data.authors[0]} et al., ${data.year})`);
    } else if (data && data.type === 'metric') {
      chip.replaceWith(`${data.value}`);
    }
  });
  return div.textContent;
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
  POST /api/athena/share
  Body: { "content": "...", "citations": [...], "seed_paper": "...", "timestamp": "..." }
  Response 200: { "share_url": "/shared/message/abc123" }

  GET /shared/message/<share_id>
  Response 200: HTML page with rendered message

DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User clicks share icon on message
  -> Share dropdown appears with 3 options
  -> Option 1 (Copy): htmlToAcademic() converts citations -> clipboard.write()
  -> Option 2 (Image): html2canvas captures message DOM -> PNG download
  -> Option 3 (Link): POST /api/athena/share with message content
  -> Backend generates share_id, stores in shared_graphs
  -> Share URL returned, copied to clipboard
  -> GET /shared/message/<id> renders read-only view
  -> All share actions logged in action_log

DATA CACHED: NONE

LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: NOT APPLICABLE
STAGE: NOT APPLICABLE
PROMPT SECTION: NOT APPLICABLE
FALLBACK: NOT APPLICABLE
TOKEN BUDGET: NOT APPLICABLE

INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Share icon           | Click       | Opens share dropdown                        |
| Copy to clipboard    | Click       | Copies rich text, shows toast               |
| Save as image        | Click       | Captures PNG, triggers download             |
| Create share link    | Click       | Generates URL, copies to clipboard          |
| Dropdown             | Click away  | Closes dropdown                             |
| Share link (viewer)  | Load        | Renders read-only message with citations    |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Dropdown fade-in 100ms. Image capture flash 100ms. Toast 1.5s.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Dropdown role="menu", menu items role="menuitem", keyboard navigable.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Message contains embedded blocks (Paper Card, Data Table)
   Expected: Clipboard strips blocks to text. Image captures visual. Link renders blocks.
   Implementation: Each format has its own serializer.

2. Scenario: Clipboard API not available (older browser)
   Expected: Fallback to document.execCommand('copy'). Toast still shows.
   Implementation: Feature detection with fallback.

3. Scenario: html2canvas fails on complex message (SVG graphs)
   Expected: Fallback to simpler text-only capture. Error logged.
   Implementation: Try/catch with degraded capture mode.

4. Scenario: Share link accessed after shared_graphs TTL expires
   Expected: "This shared insight has expired" page with Arivu branding.
   Implementation: Check expiry on GET /shared/message/<id>.

5. Scenario: User shares a message that contains sensitive session data
   Expected: Share link strips session_id and user data. Only content and citations included.
   Implementation: Sanitize shared data before storage.

DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #051 (Inline Citations)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #052 (Reactions/Bookmarks -- share is adjacent to reaction bar)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: html2canvas library, Clipboard API
