FEATURE #064: EQUATION/FORMULA BLOCK
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #038 (Markdown Rendering)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #038 (Markdown Rendering), #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Equation/Formula Block renders mathematical notation inside Athena
responses whenever the conversation involves quantitative methods,
statistical formulas, algorithmic complexity, or the mathematical
underpinnings of research papers. Academic research is dense with equations
and Athena must be able to display them natively rather than falling back
to ASCII approximations that are hard to read and imprecise.

This feature integrates KaTeX (the fast, server-side-renderable subset of
LaTeX math) into the AthenaRenderer pipeline. When the LLM emits inline
math delimited by single dollar signs ($...$) or display math delimited
by double dollar signs ($$...$$), the renderer intercepts these tokens
during the Markdown pass and replaces them with KaTeX-rendered HTML. This
is done client-side because KaTeX is lightweight (< 100KB gzipped) and
renders in sub-millisecond time per expression.

The block supports both inline formulas (flowing within a paragraph, such
as "the PageRank formula $PR(v) = (1-d) + d \sum_{u \in B(v)} PR(u)/L(u)$
is central to Arivu's graph analysis") and display formulas (centered on
their own line, used for multi-line derivations or prominent equations).
Display formulas receive a numbered label on the right margin (Eq. 1,
Eq. 2, etc.) that resets per message, allowing the user and Athena to
reference specific equations in follow-up conversation.

The visual style follows the Arivu dot matrix aesthetic: display equations
sit inside a subtle bg-surface card with a 1px left border in accent-teal.
The equation number label uses JetBrains Mono in text-muted. Inline
equations inherit the surrounding text color and size, blending seamlessly
into prose. On hover, a display equation shows a "Copy LaTeX" button
in the top-right corner of the card.

This block is critical for Athena's role as a research comprehension
assistant. When explaining how pruning_impact is calculated, how DNA
cluster distances are derived, how the diversity radar scoring works,
or how the NLP similarity threshold maps to confidence tiers, Athena
needs real math notation. Without it, explanations of quantitative methods
devolve into ambiguous prose or ugly ASCII hacks that no researcher would
accept.

The implementation is pure frontend with no backend changes. KaTeX is
loaded as a static asset (CDN with local fallback). The AthenaRenderer
integrates KaTeX via a post-processing pass after Markdown rendering:
scan the rendered HTML for unprocessed dollar-sign-delimited regions,
call katex.renderToString() on each, and inject the result. This approach
avoids conflicts between Markdown's handling of special characters and
LaTeX's use of underscores and braces.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User asks about a mathematical concept, formula, or quantitative method
used in the research graph. For example: "Explain how PageRank works in
this context" or "Show me the pruning impact formula."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks about a formula. Athena begins streaming a response that
   includes LaTeX notation.
2. As tokens stream in, inline math ($...$) is detected by the renderer.
   Each complete inline expression is rendered via KaTeX in real time as
   the closing $ arrives.
3. When a display math block ($$...$$) begins, the renderer enters buffer
   mode. A skeleton equation placeholder (pulsing horizontal lines) appears.
4. When the closing $$ arrives, KaTeX renders the full expression. The
   skeleton crossfades to the rendered equation over 150ms. An equation
   number label (Eq. 1) appears on the right margin.
5. User hovers over the display equation card. A "Copy LaTeX" button fades
   in at the top-right corner.
6. User clicks "Copy LaTeX" to copy the raw LaTeX source to clipboard.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Equations persist in the chat as rendered KaTeX HTML. Equation numbers
are stable within each message (Eq. 1, Eq. 2, etc.). On page refresh,
equations re-render from the stored LaTeX source in chat_history.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-renderer.js: KaTeX integration, dollar-sign detection.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: EquationBlock class for display math.
- static/css/athena-blocks.css: Equation card styles, number labels.
- static/vendor/katex.min.js: KaTeX library (v0.16.x).
- static/vendor/katex.min.css: KaTeX stylesheet.
- templates/base.html: KaTeX asset loading (CDN + local fallback).

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // In athena-renderer.js
  function renderMathInElement(container) {
    const text = container.innerHTML;
    // Display math first (greedy match on $$...$$)
    const displayProcessed = text.replace(
      /\$\$([\s\S]+?)\$\$/g,
      (match, latex) => renderDisplayMath(latex)
    );
    // Then inline math ($...$, not preceded by \)
    const inlineProcessed = displayProcessed.replace(
      /(?<![\\])\$([^\$\n]+?)\$/g,
      (match, latex) => renderInlineMath(latex)
    );
    container.innerHTML = inlineProcessed;
  }

  function renderDisplayMath(latex) {
    try {
      const html = katex.renderToString(latex, {
        displayMode: true,
        throwOnError: false,
        trust: false
      });
      return `<div class="equation-block">${html}<span class="eq-num"></span></div>`;
    } catch (e) {
      return `<code class="equation-error">${latex}</code>`;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
NOT APPLICABLE. All rendering is client-side via KaTeX.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```css
.equation-block {
  position: relative;
  padding: 16px 48px 16px 20px;
  margin: 12px 0;
  background: var(--bg-surface);
  border-left: 3px solid var(--accent-teal);
  border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;
  overflow-x: auto;
}
.eq-num {
  position: absolute;
  right: 12px;
  top: 50%;
  transform: translateY(-50%);
  font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
  font-size: 12px;
  color: var(--text-muted);
}
.equation-block:hover .eq-copy-btn {
  opacity: 1;
}
.eq-copy-btn {
  position: absolute;
  top: 4px;
  right: 4px;
  opacity: 0;
  transition: opacity 150ms;
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
No dedicated API. Math notation is emitted inline within the Markdown
stream using standard LaTeX delimiters: $...$ for inline, $$...$$ for
display mode.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Display Equation Rendered

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Athena: The PageRank formula used in Arivu's graph       │
  │  engine computes node importance as follows:              │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┃                                                (Eq. 1) │
  │  ┃  PR(v) = (1-d) + d * SUM[ PR(u) / L(u) ]             │
  │  ┃          for all u in B(v)                             │
  │  ┃                                              [Copy]    │
  │                                                           │
  │  where d = 0.85 (damping factor), B(v) is the set of     │
  │  nodes linking to v, and L(u) is the outgoing edge count  │
  │  of node u.                                               │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Inline Math Within Prose

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Athena: Each edge with similarity above the threshold    │
  │  (s > 0.25) is classified. The confidence tier maps as    │
  │  c >= 0.75 to HIGH, c >= 0.55 to MEDIUM, and c >= 0.35   │
  │  to LOW.                                                  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Skeleton During Buffering

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┃  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░              │
  │  ┃  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░                               │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
Skeleton to rendered: crossfade 150ms.
Copy button on hover: opacity 0 to 1, 150ms.
Inline math: renders instantly as closing $ detected.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

LLM emits tokens containing LaTeX delimiters within Markdown stream
  → AthenaStream passes tokens to AthenaRenderer
  → Renderer detects $ or $$ delimiter in accumulated text
  → Inline ($...$): render immediately via katex.renderToString()
  → Display ($$...$$): buffer until closing $$, show skeleton
  → KaTeX renders LaTeX to HTML (sub-millisecond per expression)
  → HTML injected into message container, replacing raw LaTeX
  → Equation numbers assigned sequentially per message
  → Copy button listeners attached to display equation cards
  → Raw LaTeX source stored in data-latex attribute for copy

ERROR PATH: Invalid LaTeX syntax
  → KaTeX throwOnError=false, renders error message inline
  → Raw LaTeX displayed in monospace code font as fallback
  → Console warning: "KaTeX parse error in equation"

DATA STORED: Raw LaTeX source in chat_history message content
DATA CACHED: None (KaTeX re-renders on page load from stored LaTeX)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROMPT INJECTION:
The system prompt instructs the LLM to use standard LaTeX math notation
whenever formulas, equations, or mathematical expressions are part of the
answer. The LLM is told: "Use $...$ for inline math and $$...$$ for
display equations. Use standard LaTeX commands. Do not use Unicode math
symbols as replacements for LaTeX notation."

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant) for standard queries.
config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) for complex multi-step
derivations.

TOKEN BUDGET: LaTeX notation is generally more token-efficient than
describing equations in prose. A typical equation adds 20-50 tokens.

CONTEXT WINDOW USAGE: The LaTeX instruction in the system prompt consumes
approximately 50 tokens.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action    | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-----------|---------------------------------------------|
| Display equation     | Hover     | Copy LaTeX button fades in                  |
| Copy LaTeX button    | Click     | Raw LaTeX copied to clipboard, toast shown  |
| Inline equation      | Hover     | Subtle underline highlight                  |
| Equation number      | Click     | Scrolls to that equation (within message)   |
| Equation card        | Swipe-R   | Collapses to single-line summary (mobile)   |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Skeleton crossfade 150ms. Copy button opacity 150ms. Inline hover underline 100ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: KaTeX generates MathML for screen readers. aria-label on equation blocks with plain-text description.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: LaTeX contains syntax errors (missing brace, unknown command)
   Expected: KaTeX renders partial result with red error marker, not crash.
   Implementation: throwOnError: false in KaTeX options.

2. Scenario: Dollar signs in non-math context (e.g., "$100 million")
   Expected: Not parsed as math. Heuristic: single $ followed by digit
   without a closing $ within 50 chars is treated as literal.
   Implementation: Regex excludes $[0-9] patterns unless matching pair found.

3. Scenario: Very long equation overflows container width
   Expected: Horizontal scroll within the equation card. No layout break.
   Implementation: overflow-x: auto on .equation-block.

4. Scenario: Equation references across messages ("See Eq. 1 from above")
   Expected: Equation numbers reset per message. Cross-message references
   use the original message context, not a global counter.
   Implementation: Counter resets on each new message render.

5. Scenario: KaTeX library fails to load (CDN down, no local fallback)
   Expected: Raw LaTeX displayed in monospace code blocks. Functional but
   not pretty.
   Implementation: Guard katex availability before calling renderToString().

6. Scenario: Nested dollar signs in LaTeX (e.g., \text{cost is $5})
   Expected: Proper handling via KaTeX's internal parser. Outer delimiters
   correctly identified.
   Implementation: KaTeX handles internal $ in \text{} contexts natively.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #038 (Markdown Rendering), #037 (Streaming Text)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #016 (Paper Deep Dive)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: KaTeX v0.16.x (MIT license, CDN + local fallback)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Inline math ($...$) renders correctly within prose
[ ] Display math ($$...$$) renders in dedicated equation card
[ ] Equation numbers (Eq. 1, Eq. 2) assigned per message
[ ] Copy LaTeX button copies raw source to clipboard
[ ] Invalid LaTeX degrades gracefully (no crash, fallback display)
[ ] Dollar signs in non-math context not parsed as equations
[ ] Equations persist after page refresh (re-rendered from stored LaTeX)

VISUAL:

[ ] Equation number in JetBrains Mono, text-muted, right-aligned
[ ] Skeleton placeholder during display math buffering
[ ] Copy button visible on hover only

ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] MathML generated for screen readers, aria-labels on blocks

FEATURE #065: QUOTE BLOCK
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 6
OVERLAPS WITH:   #038 (Markdown Rendering)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #038 (Markdown Rendering), #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Quote Block renders attributed quotations from research papers inside
Athena responses. When Athena cites a specific sentence or passage from a
paper in the ancestry graph, the Quote Block displays it as a visually
distinct, attributed block rather than inline text. This is essential for
a research intelligence platform: users need to see exact language from
source material, clearly separated from Athena's own synthesis, to verify
claims and understand the original author's intent.

In Arivu's context, the Quote Block draws from three sources of text:
(a) the citing_sentence and cited_sentence fields from edge_analysis,
which capture the exact sentence pairs that establish inheritance
relationships, (b) extracted full-text sections stored in the full_text
table via the full_text_fetcher, and (c) abstracts from the papers table.
Athena identifies which source provides the most relevant excerpt and
presents it in the Quote Block with full attribution.

The visual design features a left border in accent-gold (the Arivu
signature color) to distinguish quotes from Athena's own text. The
attribution line below the quote includes the paper title (truncated to
60 characters with ellipsis), first author last name, publication year,
and a clickable link icon that highlights the source node in the graph
when available. This integration between the chat and the graph is a
core Arivu differentiator.

The Quote Block also supports a confidence annotation. Since Arivu tracks
text_tier (1=full text+methods, 2=intro, 3=abstract, 4=title only), the
Quote Block shows a small badge indicating the source quality: a green
dot for tier 1-2 (verified full text), an amber dot for tier 3 (abstract
only), and a gray dot for tier 4 (title-derived, lowest confidence). This
tells the researcher how much to trust the quoted passage.

Athena emits quote blocks using the standard Markdown blockquote syntax
(> prefix) augmented with a metadata line. The AthenaRenderer detects
this pattern and promotes the standard blockquote to a full Quote Block
with attribution, confidence badge, and graph link. If the metadata line
is absent, the blockquote renders as a standard blockquote without
attribution (for non-paper quotes such as definitions or general
statements).

The implementation layers on top of the existing Markdown rendering
pipeline. After the Markdown pass converts > lines to <blockquote> HTML,
a post-processor scans for blockquotes whose first line matches the
attribution pattern. Matching blockquotes are upgraded to Quote Blocks;
non-matching ones are left as standard blockquotes.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User asks about a specific paper's contribution, methodology, or claims.
Athena includes a direct quote from the paper's text in its response.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "What did Vaswani et al. actually say about attention
   replacing recurrence?" Athena begins streaming a response.
2. The response includes Athena's summary, then a blockquote with the
   relevant passage. As the > lines stream in, the renderer detects the
   blockquote start and begins accumulating.
3. The attribution metadata line arrives (format: > -- Paper Title,
   Author (Year) [paper_id]). The renderer upgrades the blockquote to a
   full Quote Block.
4. The Quote Block renders with the gold left border, the quoted text in
   slightly italic text-primary, and the attribution line below in
   text-secondary with the text_tier confidence badge.
5. User clicks the link icon next to the attribution. The graph view
   pans to highlight the cited paper node, pulsing it twice.
6. User hovers over the confidence badge. A tooltip explains: "Source:
   Full text (methods section) -- highest confidence."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Quote Block persists in chat. Attribution link remains active for graph
navigation. Multiple quote blocks can appear in a single response.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-renderer.js: Blockquote upgrade logic.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: QuoteBlock class.
- static/css/athena-blocks.css: Quote block styles.
- static/js/graph.js: highlightNode() method for graph navigation.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // In athena-renderer.js (post-Markdown pass)
  function upgradeBlockquotes(container) {
    container.querySelectorAll('blockquote').forEach(bq => {
      const lines = bq.innerHTML.split('\n');
      const lastLine = lines[lines.length - 1].trim();
      const attrMatch = lastLine.match(
        /^--\s*(.+?),\s*(.+?)\s*\((\d{4})\)\s*\[([a-f0-9]+)\]$/
      );
      if (attrMatch) {
        const [_, title, author, year, paperId] = attrMatch;
        const quoteBlock = new QuoteBlock({
          text: lines.slice(0, -1).join('\n'),
          title, author, year, paperId
        });
        bq.replaceWith(quoteBlock.render());
      }
    });
  }

  // In athena-blocks.js
  class QuoteBlock {
    constructor({ text, title, author, year, paperId }) {
      this.text = text;
      this.title = title.length > 60 ? title.slice(0, 57) + '...' : title;
      this.author = author;
      this.year = year;
      this.paperId = paperId;
    }
    render() {
      const block = document.createElement('div');
      block.className = 'quote-block';
      block.innerHTML = `
        <div class="quote-block__text">${this.text}</div>
        <div class="quote-block__attr">
          <span class="quote-block__confidence" data-tier=""></span>
          <span class="quote-block__source">${this.title}, ${this.author} (${this.year})</span>
          <button class="quote-block__link" data-paper="${this.paperId}">&#9679;</button>
        </div>`;
      return block;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
NOT APPLICABLE. Quote blocks are rendered entirely client-side from
Markdown blockquote syntax emitted by the LLM.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```css
.quote-block {
  margin: 8px 0;
  padding: 12px 16px;
  border-left: 3px solid var(--accent-gold);
  background: var(--bg-surface);
  border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;
}
.quote-block__text {
  font-style: italic;
  color: var(--text-primary);
  line-height: 1.6;
}
.quote-block__attr {
  margin-top: 8px;
  font-size: 12px;
  color: var(--text-secondary);
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  gap: 6px;
}
.quote-block__confidence[data-tier="1"],
.quote-block__confidence[data-tier="2"] { color: var(--conf-high); }
.quote-block__confidence[data-tier="3"] { color: var(--conf-low); }
.quote-block__confidence[data-tier="4"] { color: var(--text-muted); }
.quote-block__link {
  cursor: pointer;
  background: none;
  border: none;
  color: var(--accent-blue);
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
No dedicated API. LLM emits standard Markdown blockquote with
attribution metadata as the last line:

> The key insight is that attention mechanisms can entirely replace
> recurrence for sequence transduction tasks.
> -- Attention Is All You Need, Vaswani (2017) [204e3073870fae3d05bcbc2f6a8e263d9b72e776]

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Quote Block With Attribution

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Athena: The original authors described the attention     │
  │  mechanism in clear terms:                                │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┃  "The key insight is that attention mechanisms can     │
  │  ┃   entirely replace recurrence for sequence             │
  │  ┃   transduction tasks."                                 │
  │  ┃                                                        │
  │  ┃  ● Attention Is All You Need, Vaswani (2017)  [->]    │
  │                                                           │
  │  This was a radical departure from the dominant LSTM      │
  │  paradigm at the time.                                    │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Multiple Quotes in Single Response

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┃  "We propose a new simple network architecture..."     │
  │  ┃  ● Attention Is All You Need, Vaswani (2017)  [->]    │
  │                                                           │
  │  In contrast, the BERT paper built on this by stating:    │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┃  "We introduce a new language representation model..." │
  │  ┃  ○ BERT: Pre-training of Deep..., Devlin (2018) [->]  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

LEGEND:
● = full text source (tier 1-2, green dot)
○ = abstract-only source (tier 3-4, amber/gray dot)
[->] = clickable graph navigation link

TRANSITIONS:
Quote block entrance: slide-in from left 200ms, border animates from 0 to 3px.
Graph link click: node pulses twice over 600ms in the graph view.
Hover on confidence dot: tooltip with text_tier explanation.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

LLM emits Markdown blockquote with attribution line
  → AthenaStream passes rendered Markdown to AthenaRenderer
  → Markdown parser converts > lines to <blockquote> HTML
  → upgradeBlockquotes() scans for attribution pattern
  → Matching blockquotes upgraded to QuoteBlock instances
  → QuoteBlock.render() builds DOM with attribution, badge, link
  → Confidence badge data-tier set from paper lookup (text_tier)
  → Graph link button wired to ArivuGraph.highlightNode(paperId)
  → Non-matching blockquotes left as standard <blockquote>

ERROR PATH: paper_id not found in current graph
  → Graph link button hidden (paper not in loaded graph)
  → Attribution still displayed without navigation link

DATA STORED: Quote block persisted as Markdown in chat_history
DATA CACHED: Paper text_tier values cached in session graph data

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROMPT INJECTION:
The system prompt instructs the LLM to use blockquote syntax with
attribution whenever directly quoting from a paper. The format
specification is: "> [quoted text]\n> -- [title], [author] ([year])
[paper_id]". The LLM is told to only quote text that exists in the
graph context (citing_sentence, cited_sentence, or abstract) and to
never fabricate quotes.

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant) for standard queries.
TOKEN BUDGET: Each quote block adds approximately 50-100 tokens.
CONTEXT WINDOW USAGE: Format instruction adds approximately 80 tokens
to the system prompt.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action    | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-----------|---------------------------------------------|
| Graph link [->]      | Click     | Pans graph to node, pulses twice            |
| Confidence badge     | Hover     | Tooltip: "Source: [tier description]"       |
| Quote text           | Select    | Standard text selection for copy            |
| Quote block          | Long-press| "Copy quote with attribution" option        |
| Attribution line     | Click     | Opens paper URL in new tab (if available)   |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Quote entrance slide-in 200ms. Node pulse 600ms. Badge tooltip 150ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: blockquote role preserved, aria-label includes attribution, graph link described

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Attribution line has no paper_id (paper not in graph)
   Expected: Quote block renders without graph navigation link. Attribution
   text only.
   Implementation: Regex allows optional [paper_id] capture group.

2. Scenario: Quoted text contains Markdown formatting (bold, italic, links)
   Expected: Inner Markdown rendered correctly within the quote block.
   Implementation: Quote text goes through Markdown pass before insertion.

3. Scenario: Very long quote (> 500 characters)
   Expected: Quote block expands vertically. No truncation by default.
   After 300 characters, a "Show more" toggle collapses excess text.
   Implementation: CSS max-height with overflow hidden, toggle button.

4. Scenario: LLM fabricates a quote not found in the source paper
   Expected: Athena should not fabricate quotes (system prompt instructs
   against it). If detected, confidence badge shows "unverified" state.
   Implementation: Frontend cannot verify content, relies on LLM fidelity.

5. Scenario: Paper title in attribution contains special characters
   Expected: Title rendered correctly. HTML entities escaped.
   Implementation: textContent assignment (not innerHTML) for title.

ENHANCES: #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #004 (Discuss Integration)

FEATURE #066: WARNING/ALERT BLOCK
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 6
OVERLAPS WITH:   NONE
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #038 (Markdown Rendering), #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Warning/Alert Block is a structured response element that Athena uses
to communicate critical information that demands the user's immediate
attention. Unlike normal prose which flows past at reading speed, an alert
block is visually loud: a colored left border, a distinct background tint,
and an icon prefix that forces the eye to stop and read. This block exists
because Arivu surfaces information with real consequences for research
integrity and the user must not miss it.

There are four severity levels, each with its own color and icon:

DANGER (red): Retracted papers detected in the ancestry graph. A paper
flagged in the retraction_watch table has is_retracted=true, and Athena
must immediately warn the user that part of the intellectual lineage rests
on retracted work. This is the highest-severity alert and renders with a
solid danger-red left border and a bg tinted toward red.

WARNING (amber): Low-confidence classifications or SPECULATIVE tier edges
dominate a critical path. When Athena detects that a key inheritance
chain relies on edge_analysis rows where mutation_confidence < 0.35
(SPECULATIVE tier), the user needs to know that the analysis is uncertain.
Amber left border, amber-tinted background.

INFO (blue): Contextual information that enriches understanding but does
not indicate a problem. For example, "This paper has 3 independent
discovery parallels detected by the serendipity engine" or "The DNA
profiler identified this cluster as primarily methodological_adoption."
Blue left border, blue-tinted background.

SUCCESS (green): Positive verification results. For example, "All 12
edges in this path have HIGH confidence tier classifications" or "No
retracted papers found in this lineage." Green left border, green-tinted
background.

The LLM emits alert blocks using a fenced directive syntax:
```athena-alert followed by a JSON payload containing severity, title,
and body. The streaming parser buffers the directive and inflates it
into the styled block when the closing fence arrives.

The alert block is not optional decoration. In a system that analyzes
research integrity (retraction status, confidence tiers, citation
shadows), failing to visually distinguish critical warnings from routine
text is a safety issue. A researcher who misses a retraction warning
because it was buried in a paragraph may build their work on flawed
foundations. The alert block prevents this by making critical information
impossible to scroll past without seeing.

Each alert block supports a "Dismiss" button that collapses the block
to a single-line summary, reducing visual clutter once the user has
acknowledged the information. Dismissed state is stored in session
memory so the same alert does not re-expand if the chat is scrolled.

The implementation integrates with the existing block infrastructure
(shared with #063 Stat Dashboard Block). The AthenaRenderer recognizes
the ```athena-alert fence and dispatches to the AlertBlock class which
builds the DOM structure: icon container, title row, body, and dismiss
button.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
Athena detects a condition that warrants an alert during response
generation: retracted paper in graph, low-confidence path, positive
verification result, or contextual enrichment.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks "Analyze the reliability of this paper's lineage." Athena
   begins streaming the analysis.
2. During analysis, Athena discovers that one ancestor paper has
   is_retracted=true in the retraction_watch table.
3. Athena emits a ```athena-alert block with severity "danger". The
   streaming parser begins buffering.
4. A skeleton alert placeholder (pulsing red-tinted bar) appears in the
   chat while the block buffers.
5. The closing fence arrives. The AlertBlock renders: red left border,
   danger icon, title "Retracted Paper Detected", body with details.
   The block enters with a subtle shake animation (2px horizontal
   oscillation over 300ms) to draw attention.
6. User reads the alert, clicks "Dismiss." The block collapses to a
   single line: "[!] Retracted Paper Detected (click to expand)."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Alert block persists in chat. Dismissed state stored in session memory.
User can click the collapsed line to re-expand at any time.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AlertBlock class.
- static/js/athena-renderer.js: Detects ```athena-alert fence.
- static/css/athena-blocks.css: Alert severity styles.
- static/js/athena-stream.js: Buffer logic for fenced directives.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // In athena-blocks.js
  class AlertBlock {
    static SEVERITIES = {
      danger:  { icon: '!', color: 'var(--danger)',  bg: 'rgba(239,68,68,0.08)' },
      warning: { icon: '!', color: 'var(--warning)', bg: 'rgba(245,158,11,0.08)' },
      info:    { icon: 'i', color: 'var(--info)',    bg: 'rgba(96,165,250,0.08)' },
      success: { icon: '+', color: 'var(--success)', bg: 'rgba(34,197,94,0.08)' }
    };

    constructor(payload) {
      this.severity = payload.severity || 'info';
      this.title = payload.title;
      this.body = payload.body;
      this.dismissible = payload.dismissible !== false;
    }

    render() {
      const config = AlertBlock.SEVERITIES[this.severity];
      const block = document.createElement('div');
      block.className = `alert-block alert-block--${this.severity}`;
      block.style.borderLeftColor = config.color;
      block.style.background = config.bg;
      block.innerHTML = `
        <div class="alert-block__icon">${config.icon}</div>
        <div class="alert-block__content">
          <div class="alert-block__title">${this.title}</div>
          <div class="alert-block__body">${this.body}</div>
        </div>
        ${this.dismissible ? '<button class="alert-block__dismiss">Dismiss</button>' : ''}`;
      if (this.severity === 'danger') {
        block.classList.add('alert-block--shake');
      }
      return block;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
NOT APPLICABLE. Alert blocks are rendered entirely client-side from
directives emitted by the LLM.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```css
.alert-block {
  display: flex;
  gap: 12px;
  padding: 12px 16px;
  margin: 8px 0;
  border-left: 3px solid;
  border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;
}
.alert-block__icon {
  width: 24px;
  height: 24px;
  border-radius: 50%;
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
  font-weight: 700;
  font-size: 14px;
  flex-shrink: 0;
}
.alert-block--danger .alert-block__icon {
  background: var(--danger);
  color: white;
}
.alert-block--warning .alert-block__icon {
  background: var(--warning);
  color: var(--bg-primary);
}
.alert-block--shake {
  animation: alertShake 300ms ease-out;
}
@keyframes alertShake {
  0%, 100% { transform: translateX(0); }
  25% { transform: translateX(-2px); }
  75% { transform: translateX(2px); }
}
.alert-block--dismissed {
  padding: 6px 16px;
  cursor: pointer;
}
.alert-block--dismissed .alert-block__body,
.alert-block--dismissed .alert-block__dismiss { display: none; }
```

API CONTRACTS:
No dedicated API. LLM emits the alert inside the streaming Markdown
response as a fenced directive:

```athena-alert
{
  "severity": "danger",
  "title": "Retracted Paper Detected",
  "body": "Paper X (2015) has been retracted. 3 edges in the current graph depend on its findings.",
  "dismissible": true
}
```

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Danger Alert (Retraction Warning)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┃ (!) Retracted Paper Detected                          │
  │  ┃                                                       │
  │  ┃  Paper "On the Properties of Neural..." (2015) has    │
  │  ┃  been retracted. 3 edges in the current graph         │
  │  ┃  depend on its findings. Review pruning_impact to     │
  │  ┃  assess lineage damage.                               │
  │  ┃                                          [Dismiss]    │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Warning Alert (Low Confidence Path)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┃ (!) SPECULATIVE Confidence Path                       │
  │  ┃                                                       │
  │  ┃  The inheritance chain from node A to node D relies   │
  │  ┃  on 2 edges with mutation_confidence below 0.35.      │
  │  ┃  Treat this path as uncertain.                        │
  │  ┃                                          [Dismiss]    │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Dismissed Alert

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┃ (!) Retracted Paper Detected   (click to expand)      │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
Block entrance: slide-in from left 200ms. Danger blocks add shake 300ms.
Dismiss: collapse animation 200ms (height transitions to single line).
Expand: reverse collapse 200ms.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

LLM emits ```athena-alert fenced block during streaming
  → AthenaStream detects opening fence, enters buffer mode
  → Skeleton alert placeholder with severity-tinted bar appears
  → Closing fence detected, JSON parsed
  → AlertBlock class instantiated with severity, title, body
  → DOM rendered with severity-specific colors and icon
  → Danger alerts receive shake animation
  → Dismiss button wired: click collapses block, stores state
  → Dismissed state saved to sessionStorage by block ID
  → On re-render, dismissed blocks start collapsed

ERROR PATH: Invalid severity in JSON
  → Defaults to "info" severity
  → Console warning: "Unknown alert severity, defaulting to info"

DATA STORED: Alert content in chat_history. Dismiss state in sessionStorage.
DATA CACHED: None

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROMPT INJECTION:
The system prompt instructs the LLM to emit ```athena-alert blocks when:
(a) a retracted paper is detected (severity=danger), (b) SPECULATIVE tier
edges dominate a path (severity=warning), (c) enrichment context enhances
understanding (severity=info), (d) verification passes (severity=success).
The LLM is told to always include severity, title, and body fields.

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant).
TOKEN BUDGET: Alert blocks add approximately 100-200 tokens each.
CONTEXT WINDOW USAGE: Alert format specification adds approximately
120 tokens to the system prompt.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action    | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-----------|---------------------------------------------|
| Dismiss button       | Click     | Collapses alert to single-line summary      |
| Collapsed alert      | Click     | Re-expands to full alert content            |
| Alert body links     | Click     | Navigation to referenced paper/node         |
| Alert icon           | Hover     | Tooltip with severity level name            |
| Entire block         | Long-press| "Copy alert text" option                    |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Entrance slide-in 200ms. Danger shake 300ms. Dismiss collapse 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: role="alert" for danger/warning, role="status" for info/success. aria-live="polite".

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Multiple danger alerts in a single response
   Expected: Each renders independently. No merging or deduplication.
   Implementation: Each fenced block creates its own AlertBlock instance.

2. Scenario: Alert body contains Markdown formatting
   Expected: Body text passes through Markdown renderer before display.
   Implementation: AlertBlock.body is processed by marked.parse().

3. Scenario: User dismisses alert, then scrolls up to see it again
   Expected: Alert remains collapsed (state persisted in sessionStorage).
   Implementation: On re-render, check sessionStorage for dismissed IDs.

4. Scenario: Unknown severity value in JSON (e.g., "critical")
   Expected: Falls back to "info" styling. No crash.
   Implementation: Default case in SEVERITIES lookup.

5. Scenario: Alert emitted during code block streaming (nested fences)
   Expected: Inner fences do not confuse the parser. Block detection
   tracks nesting depth.
   Implementation: Fence counter increments on ``` open, decrements on close.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #038 (Markdown Rendering), #037 (Streaming Text)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #005 (Graph-Aware Commands)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: None (pure frontend rendering)

[ ] Collapsed alerts re-expand on click

FEATURE #067: EXPANDABLE SECTION BLOCK
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 8
OVERLAPS WITH:   #038 (Markdown Rendering)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #038 (Markdown Rendering), #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Expandable Section Block allows Athena to deliver layered responses
where the top level provides a concise summary and the details are hidden
behind expandable sections that the user opens on demand. This addresses
one of the most common UX failures in LLM chat interfaces: responses that
are too long. A 2,000-word response about a research lineage overwhelms
the user even when every word is relevant. The Expandable Section Block
solves this by structuring the response hierarchically.

In Arivu's context, this block is used extensively. When Athena explains
the mutation_type distribution across a graph, it provides a one-line
summary ("6 adoption, 3 generalization, 2 hybridization, 1 contradiction")
and then hides the per-edge details inside expandable sections labeled by
mutation type. When Athena reports DNA profiler cluster results, the top
level shows the cluster count and dominant theme, while each cluster's
paper list and theme description lives in an expandable section. When
Athena describes pruning_impact results, the summary shows the percentage
and collapsed count, while the per-level BFS breakdown is expandable.

The block uses the HTML <details>/<summary> pattern under the hood,
enhanced with Arivu-specific styling. The LLM emits expandable sections
using a fenced directive syntax: ```athena-details with a JSON payload
containing a summary string and a body string (which may itself contain
Markdown). The streaming parser buffers the directive, and the
AthenaRenderer inflates it into a styled <details> element.

Multiple expandable sections can be nested. A top-level section titled
"Mutation Type Analysis" can contain three nested sections: "Adoption
Edges (6)", "Generalization Edges (3)", "Contradiction Edges (1)". Nesting
depth is capped at 3 levels to prevent UX chaos.

The expand/collapse state is animated with a smooth height transition.
The toggle indicator is a small triangle (right-pointing when collapsed,
down-pointing when expanded) in accent-teal. The summary line is displayed
in text-primary at normal weight, and the body content is displayed in a
slightly indented region below with a subtle left border in bg-elevated.

Expandable sections can optionally declare a "default open" state. When
the LLM determines that a section is critical (e.g., a retraction path
detail), it can set defaultOpen: true in the directive, causing the
section to render expanded. All other sections start collapsed.

The block integrates with Athena's print and export flows. When the user
exports a chat, all expandable sections are force-expanded so the export
contains the complete content. The "Copy response" action also includes
all section content regardless of expand state.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
Athena generates a response with enough structured detail that collapsing
subsections improves readability. Typically triggered by analysis commands
like "Analyze this graph" or "Explain the mutation types."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "Break down the mutation types in this graph." Athena begins
   streaming a response.
2. The response starts with a summary paragraph. Then the LLM emits a
   ```athena-details block for each mutation type.
3. The streaming parser buffers each block. Skeleton placeholders (pulsing
   summary lines) appear in sequence.
4. As each closing fence arrives, the AthenaRenderer inflates the section.
   The summary line appears with a right-pointing triangle. The body is
   hidden.
5. User clicks "Adoption Edges (6)". The triangle rotates 90 degrees
   clockwise over 150ms. The body slides open from 0 to auto height over
   200ms, revealing the 6 edge descriptions.
6. User clicks again to collapse. Triangle rotates back. Body slides
   closed over 200ms.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Expandable sections persist in chat. Expand/collapse state is ephemeral
(not stored in session). On scroll-away and back, sections revert to
their initial state (collapsed unless defaultOpen was true).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: ExpandableBlock class.
- static/js/athena-renderer.js: Detects ```athena-details fence.
- static/css/athena-blocks.css: Details/summary animation styles.
- static/js/athena-stream.js: Buffer logic for fenced directives.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // In athena-blocks.js
  class ExpandableBlock {
    constructor(payload) {
      this.summary = payload.summary;
      this.body = payload.body;
      this.defaultOpen = payload.defaultOpen || false;
      this.nested = payload.nested || [];
    }

    render() {
      const details = document.createElement('details');
      details.className = 'expandable-block';
      if (this.defaultOpen) details.setAttribute('open', '');

      const summary = document.createElement('summary');
      summary.className = 'expandable-block__summary';
      summary.innerHTML = `<span class="expandable-block__arrow"></span>${this.summary}`;
      details.appendChild(summary);

      const content = document.createElement('div');
      content.className = 'expandable-block__content';
      content.innerHTML = marked.parse(this.body);
      details.appendChild(content);

      // Render nested sections
      this.nested.forEach(child => {
        const childBlock = new ExpandableBlock(child);
        content.appendChild(childBlock.render());
      });

      return details;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
NOT APPLICABLE. Expandable sections are rendered entirely client-side.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```css
.expandable-block {
  margin: 6px 0;
  border-radius: 6px;
  overflow: hidden;
}
.expandable-block__summary {
  padding: 8px 12px;
  cursor: pointer;
  color: var(--text-primary);
  font-size: 14px;
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  gap: 8px;
  background: var(--bg-surface);
  border-radius: 6px;
  list-style: none;
}
.expandable-block__summary::-webkit-details-marker { display: none; }
.expandable-block__arrow {
  width: 0; height: 0;
  border-left: 5px solid var(--accent-teal);
  border-top: 4px solid transparent;
  border-bottom: 4px solid transparent;
  transition: transform 150ms ease-out;
  flex-shrink: 0;
}
.expandable-block[open] > .expandable-block__summary .expandable-block__arrow {
  transform: rotate(90deg);
}
.expandable-block__content {
  padding: 8px 12px 8px 28px;
  border-left: 2px solid var(--bg-elevated);
  margin-left: 10px;
  color: var(--text-primary);
  font-size: 14px;
  line-height: 1.6;
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
No dedicated API. LLM emits the expandable section inside the streaming
Markdown response as a fenced directive:

```athena-details
{
  "summary": "Adoption Edges (6)",
  "body": "- Paper A adopted Method X from Paper B\n- Paper C adopted...",
  "defaultOpen": false,
  "nested": []
}
```

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Collapsed Sections

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Athena: The graph contains 12 edges with the following  │
  │  mutation type distribution:                              │
  │                                                           │
  │  > Adoption Edges (6)                                     │
  │  > Generalization Edges (3)                               │
  │  > Hybridization Edges (2)                                │
  │  > Contradiction Edges (1)                                │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: One Section Expanded

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  v Adoption Edges (6)                                     │
  │  │  ● Paper A adopted the attention mechanism from        │
  │  │    Paper B (confidence: HIGH)                          │
  │  │  ● Paper C adopted the training procedure from         │
  │  │    Paper D (confidence: MEDIUM)                        │
  │  │  ● Paper E adopted the tokenization from               │
  │  │    Paper F (confidence: HIGH)                          │
  │  │  ... (3 more)                                          │
  │                                                           │
  │  > Generalization Edges (3)                               │
  │  > Hybridization Edges (2)                                │
  │  > Contradiction Edges (1)                                │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

LEGEND:
> = collapsed (right-pointing triangle)
v = expanded (down-pointing triangle)
│ = content indentation border

TRANSITIONS:
Arrow rotation: 150ms ease-out.
Content reveal: height 0 to auto, 200ms ease-out.
Content collapse: height auto to 0, 200ms ease-in.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

LLM emits ```athena-details fenced block during streaming
  → AthenaStream detects opening fence, enters buffer mode
  → Skeleton summary placeholder (pulsing line) appears
  → Closing fence detected, JSON parsed
  → ExpandableBlock instantiated with summary, body, nested
  → <details>/<summary> DOM built, content Markdown-rendered
  → Nested sections recursively rendered (max 3 depth)
  → Arrow and toggle animations wired via CSS
  → defaultOpen sections start with [open] attribute
  → On export/copy: all sections force-expanded

ERROR PATH: Malformed JSON in block
  → Falls back to rendering summary as bold text, body as plain paragraph
  → Console warning: "Invalid expandable block payload"

DATA STORED: Full content (expanded) in chat_history
DATA CACHED: None (expand state is ephemeral)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROMPT INJECTION:
The system prompt instructs the LLM to use expandable sections when a
response contains 3 or more parallel subsections that would each be 3
or more sentences long. The LLM is told to write a concise summary for
each section header and to put the detailed content in the body. It is
instructed to use nested sections for hierarchical analysis (e.g.,
mutation types containing individual edge descriptions).

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant).
TOKEN BUDGET: Expandable blocks add approximately 50 tokens overhead
per section (JSON structure). The body content tokens are the same as
if written inline.
CONTEXT WINDOW USAGE: Format specification adds approximately 100 tokens
to the system prompt.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action    | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-----------|---------------------------------------------|
| Summary line         | Click     | Toggles expand/collapse with animation      |
| Arrow indicator      | Visual    | Rotates 90 degrees on expand                |
| Nested summary       | Click     | Toggles nested section independently        |
| "Expand All" (menu)  | Click     | Opens all sections in the message           |
| "Collapse All" (menu)| Click     | Closes all sections in the message          |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Arrow rotation 150ms. Content height transition 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: <details>/<summary> is natively accessible. Arrow is decorative (aria-hidden).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Nesting depth exceeds 3 levels
   Expected: Fourth level rendered as flat content (no further nesting).
   Implementation: ExpandableBlock tracks depth, stops nesting at 3.

2. Scenario: Body content is empty (summary-only section)
   Expected: Section renders as a non-interactive bold line (no toggle).
   Implementation: If body is empty string, render as <p> not <details>.

3. Scenario: All sections set to defaultOpen
   Expected: All render expanded. User can collapse individually.
   Implementation: Each section's defaultOpen is independent.

4. Scenario: Very long body content (> 2000 characters)
   Expected: Content scrolls within the section. No max-height by default.
   Implementation: Content flows naturally. Consider "Show less" if extreme.

5. Scenario: Expandable section inside a code block or quote block
   Expected: Nested blocks render correctly. Block types do not conflict.
   Implementation: Fenced directives are parsed before Markdown blocks.

6. Scenario: Print or export while sections are collapsed
   Expected: All sections force-expanded in print/export output.
   Implementation: Print CSS: details { open } or JS opens all before export.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #038 (Markdown Rendering), #037 (Streaming Text)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: None (uses native HTML <details>/<summary>)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

[ ] Expandable sections render from ```athena-details directives
[ ] Click summary to toggle expand/collapse
[ ] Arrow indicator rotates on toggle
[ ] Nested sections work up to 3 levels deep
[ ] defaultOpen sections start expanded
[ ] "Expand All" / "Collapse All" work across sections in a message
[ ] Export and copy include all section content regardless of state
[ ] Empty body renders as non-interactive text

[ ] Content indented with bg-elevated left border

FEATURE #068: MESSAGE GROUPING
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Conversation Mgmt
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #010 (Conversation Threads)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #006 (Conversation Memory), #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Message Grouping organizes the chat timeline into collapsible topical
clusters so that long research conversations remain navigable. Without
grouping, a 50-message conversation about a paper's ancestry graph becomes
an undifferentiated scroll of messages where finding "that part where we
discussed the retraction issue" requires reading through everything. With
grouping, the chat is segmented into labeled sections that the user can
expand and collapse independently.

Groups are created automatically by Athena based on topic detection. When
the conversation shifts from discussing mutation types to discussing
pruning impact, Athena detects the topic change and inserts a group
boundary. Each group gets a generated label (e.g., "Mutation Type
Analysis", "Pruning Impact Discussion", "Retraction Concerns") and a
message count badge. The user can also manually create groups by
right-clicking a message and selecting "Start new group here."

The grouping algorithm works by analyzing the semantic similarity between
consecutive messages. When the cosine similarity between a new message's
embedding and the running group centroid drops below a threshold (0.4),
a new group is started. The centroid is the mean embedding of all messages
in the current group. Embeddings are computed by the NLP worker via the
/encode_batch endpoint, reusing the same infrastructure that powers the
edge_analysis similarity scoring.

Each group displays as a horizontal divider in the chat with the group
label centered and a message count badge. Clicking the label toggles
collapse of all messages in that group. When collapsed, only the label
line is visible, reducing 15 messages to a single line. This is
particularly valuable for repeat analysis sessions where the user
returns to a conversation and wants to jump directly to a specific
topic without scrolling through resolved discussions.

Groups are stored in the chat_history table as metadata annotations.
Each message row in chat_history gets a group_id field (nullable) that
links it to a group. Groups have their own lightweight table
(chat_groups) with columns: group_id, session_id, label, start_index,
end_index, created_at. The group boundaries are computed lazily: when
the user opens a conversation, the frontend requests group boundaries
from the backend, which computes them if not already cached.

The visual design follows the Arivu dot matrix aesthetic. Group dividers
use a thin horizontal rule with the label in a bg-surface pill centered
on the line. The message count badge sits to the right of the label in a
small rounded rectangle using text-muted. Collapsed groups show a subtle
down-arrow indicator and a preview of the first message (truncated to 60
characters) to help the user identify the group without expanding.

Manual grouping overrides auto-grouping. If the user manually splits or
merges groups, the manual boundaries take precedence and the auto-grouper
skips those ranges in future recalculations. This ensures user
customization is never lost.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
Automatic: conversation reaches 8+ messages. The grouping algorithm runs
after each new message and inserts boundaries when topic shifts are
detected. Manual: user right-clicks a message and selects "Start new
group here."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User has a 20-message conversation spanning multiple topics. After the
   8th message, the auto-grouper activates and segments the conversation
   into 3 groups based on topic similarity.
2. Group dividers appear between message clusters. The first group is
   labeled "Graph Structure Overview (5 messages)." The second is labeled
   "Mutation Type Deep Dive (8 messages)." The third is labeled "Pruning
   Impact Analysis (7 messages)."
3. User clicks the "Mutation Type Deep Dive" label. All 8 messages in that
   group collapse to the single divider line with a preview: "You asked:
   What are the mutation types..."
4. User clicks the collapsed group to expand it. Messages slide back into
   view over 200ms.
5. User right-clicks a message in the third group and selects "Start new
   group here." The third group splits into two. The user is prompted to
   name the new group (or accept the auto-generated label).

AFTER COMPLETION:
Groups persist in the conversation. Collapse states are ephemeral (reset
on page reload). Group boundaries and labels stored in backend.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-groups.js: GroupManager class, auto-grouping algorithm.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Integration with message rendering pipeline.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Group divider, collapse, badge styles.
- backend/chat_guide.py: Group computation endpoint.
- app.py: /api/chat/groups GET and PUT endpoints.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // In athena-groups.js
  class GroupManager {
    constructor(chatContainer) {
      this.container = chatContainer;
      this.groups = [];
      this.manualOverrides = new Set();
    }

    async computeGroups(messages) {
      if (messages.length < 8) return [];
      const response = await fetch('/api/chat/groups', {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: JSON.stringify({
          session_id: currentSessionId,
          messages: messages.map(m => ({ index: m.index, text: m.text }))
        })
      });
      this.groups = await response.json();
      this.renderGroupDividers();
    }

    renderGroupDividers() {
      this.groups.forEach(group => {
        const divider = document.createElement('div');
        divider.className = 'group-divider';
        divider.innerHTML = `
          <hr class="group-divider__line" />
          <span class="group-divider__label">${group.label}</span>
          <span class="group-divider__count">${group.count}</span>`;
        // Insert before the first message of the group
        const firstMsg = this.container.querySelector(`[data-index="${group.startIndex}"]`);
        if (firstMsg) firstMsg.before(divider);
      });
    }

    toggleGroup(groupId) {
      const group = this.groups.find(g => g.id === groupId);
      if (!group) return;
      group.collapsed = !group.collapsed;
      // Toggle visibility of messages in range
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  # In chat_guide.py or a dedicated grouping module
  async def compute_message_groups(session_id, messages):
      texts = [m['text'] for m in messages]
      embeddings = await encode_batch(texts)  # NLP worker call
      groups = []
      current_group = { 'start': 0, 'centroid': embeddings[0] }
      for i in range(1, len(embeddings)):
          sim = cosine_similarity(embeddings[i], current_group['centroid'])
          if sim < 0.4:
              groups.append(finalize_group(current_group, i - 1, messages))
              current_group = { 'start': i, 'centroid': embeddings[i] }
          else:
              current_group['centroid'] = update_centroid(
                  current_group['centroid'], embeddings[i], i - current_group['start']
              )
      groups.append(finalize_group(current_group, len(messages) - 1, messages))
      return groups

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```css
.group-divider {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  gap: 12px;
  margin: 16px 0 8px 0;
  position: relative;
}
.group-divider__line {
  flex: 1;
  border: none;
  border-top: 1px solid var(--bg-elevated);
}
.group-divider__label {
  background: var(--bg-surface);
  padding: 4px 12px;
  border-radius: 12px;
  font-size: 12px;
  color: var(--text-secondary);
  white-space: nowrap;
  cursor: pointer;
}
.group-divider__count {
  background: var(--bg-elevated);
  padding: 2px 8px;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-size: 11px;
  color: var(--text-muted);
  font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
}
.group-collapsed .chat-message { display: none; }
.group-collapsed .group-divider__preview { display: block; }
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/chat/groups
  Request: { session_id, messages: [{ index, text }] }
  Response: [{ id, label, startIndex, endIndex, count }]
  Calls NLP worker /encode_batch for embeddings.

PUT /api/chat/groups
  Request: { session_id, group_id, action: "split"|"merge"|"rename", params }
  Response: { updated_groups: [...] }

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Groups Expanded (Normal View)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ────── Graph Structure Overview (5) ──────               │
  │                                                           │
  │  You: Show me the graph for Attention Is All You Need     │
  │  Athena: Building ancestry graph...                       │
  │  ... (3 more messages)                                    │
  │                                                           │
  │  ────── Mutation Type Deep Dive (8) ──────                │
  │                                                           │
  │  You: What mutation types are present?                    │
  │  Athena: The graph contains 12 edges...                   │
  │  ... (6 more messages)                                    │
  │                                                           │
  │  ────── Pruning Impact Analysis (7) ──────                │
  │                                                           │
  │  You: What happens if we remove the LSTM paper?           │
  │  Athena: Removing this paper would collapse...            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Second Group Collapsed

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ────── Graph Structure Overview (5) ──────               │
  │  ... (messages visible)                                   │
  │                                                           │
  │  ────── Mutation Type Deep Dive (8) ────── v              │
  │         "You: What mutation types are..."                 │
  │                                                           │
  │  ────── Pruning Impact Analysis (7) ──────                │
  │  ... (messages visible)                                   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

LEGEND:
(N) = message count badge
v = collapsed indicator (click to expand)
"..." = truncated preview of first message in group

TRANSITIONS:
Group collapse: messages slide up and fade, 200ms.
Group expand: messages slide down and fade in, 200ms.
Divider insertion: fade-in 150ms when auto-grouper activates.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Message count reaches 8
  → GroupManager.computeGroups() called
  → Messages sent to POST /api/chat/groups
  → Backend calls NLP worker /encode_batch for message embeddings
  → Cosine similarity computed between consecutive embeddings
  → Topic boundaries detected where similarity < 0.4
  → Groups labeled via LLM (GROQ_FAST_MODEL summarizes group content)
  → Group metadata returned to frontend
  → GroupManager.renderGroupDividers() inserts dividers in DOM
  → Click handlers attached for collapse/expand
  → Manual overrides stored in manualOverrides set

ERROR PATH: NLP worker unavailable for embeddings
  → Fallback to simple heuristic: group every 5 messages
  → Label groups numerically: "Messages 1-5", "Messages 6-10"

DATA STORED: chat_groups table (group_id, session_id, label, start_index, end_index)
DATA CACHED: Group boundaries cached per session until new messages arrive

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROMPT INJECTION:
The LLM (GROQ_FAST_MODEL) is called to generate concise group labels.
Input: first and last message of each group. Output: 2-5 word label.
Example prompt: "Given these messages from a research conversation,
generate a 2-5 word topic label: [messages]"

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant) for label generation.
TOKEN BUDGET: Label generation costs approximately 50 tokens per group.
CONTEXT WINDOW USAGE: Label prompt adds approximately 200 tokens per
group (message excerpts + instruction).

CACHE: Group labels cached in llm_cache with key=SHA256("group_label:" +
session_id + ":" + start_index + ":" + end_index).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action       | Response                                 |
|----------------------|--------------|------------------------------------------|
| Group label          | Click        | Toggle collapse/expand for group         |
| Group label          | Double-click | Enter rename mode (editable text)        |
| Message              | Right-click  | Context menu: "Start new group here"     |
| Collapse indicator   | Click        | Same as group label click                |
| "Expand All" button  | Click        | Expands all collapsed groups             |
| "Collapse All" button| Click        | Collapses all expanded groups            |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Collapse/expand 200ms ease-out. Divider insertion 150ms fade-in.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Group dividers use role="separator". Collapsed groups announce count to screen readers.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Conversation has fewer than 8 messages
   Expected: No grouping applied. Messages display as a flat list.
   Implementation: GroupManager.computeGroups() returns early if count < 8.

2. Scenario: All messages are on the same topic (high similarity throughout)
   Expected: Single group containing all messages. No dividers shown.
   Implementation: If only one group is produced, skip divider rendering.

3. Scenario: User manually splits a group, then the auto-grouper runs again
   Expected: Manual split boundaries are preserved. Auto-grouper skips
   ranges covered by manual overrides.
   Implementation: manualOverrides set filters auto-grouper output.

4. Scenario: Message is deleted after grouping (via edit/delete feature)
   Expected: Group boundaries recalculated. Counts updated.
   Implementation: GroupManager listens for message deletion events.

5. Scenario: Rapid messages (user sends 5 messages in 10 seconds)
   Expected: Grouping recalculation debounced to avoid excessive NLP calls.
   Implementation: Debounce computeGroups() with 2-second delay.

6. Scenario: NLP worker is down when grouping is requested
   Expected: Fallback to simple 5-message chunking with numeric labels.
   Implementation: try/catch around NLP worker call, fallback heuristic.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #006 (Conversation Memory), #037 (Streaming Text)
ENABLES: #069 (Pinned Messages benefits from group context)
ENHANCES: #010 (Conversation Threads), #001 (Context-Aware Responses)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: NLP worker /encode_batch endpoint

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Auto-grouping activates at 8+ messages
[ ] Groups labeled by LLM-generated topic labels
[ ] Click group label to collapse/expand
[ ] Collapsed groups show message count and preview
[ ] Manual group creation via right-click context menu
[ ] Manual overrides persist across auto-grouper recalculations
[ ] Group rename via double-click on label
[ ] "Expand All" / "Collapse All" buttons work
[ ] Fallback to numeric grouping when NLP worker unavailable

VISUAL:
[ ] Group dividers centered with pill label
[ ] Message count badge in JetBrains Mono
[ ] Collapse/expand animations (200ms)
[ ] Divider insertion fade-in (150ms)

PERFORMANCE: [ ] Grouping computation < 2s for 50 messages
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Group dividers use role="separator", counts announced
EDGE CASES: [ ] < 8 messages, single topic, manual splits, rapid messages

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec
