================================================================================
FEATURE #133: CONVERSATION BRANCHING VISUALIZATION
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Conversation Mgmt
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 18
OVERLAPS WITH:   #010 (Conversation Threads), #127 (Layered Chat)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #006 (Conversation Memory), #007 (Visual Redesign)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Conversation Branching Visualization renders the conversation structure as an
interactive tree diagram in a sidebar panel. Each message is a node, and
branches occur when the user asks follow-up questions that diverge from the
main thread, when layered chats (#127) create sub-conversations, or when the
user edits a previous message (#040) creating an alternate timeline. The tree
shows which branch is currently active, which branches have been explored, and
how many messages each branch contains. Users can click any branch node to
jump to that point in the conversation and continue from there. This provides
a visual map of the entire conversation's exploration path, making it easy to
navigate complex research sessions.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User opens the branching panel via the sidebar or /branches command.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User has explored 30 messages with 3 branch points.
2. User clicks the branch icon in the sidebar.
3. A tree diagram appears: Main trunk (20 messages), Branch A (5 messages from
   message #12), Branch B (5 messages from message #18).
4. Active branch highlighted in gold. Explored branches in regular color.
5. User clicks Branch A's root node. Chat scrolls to message #12 and shows
   Branch A's continuation.
6. User clicks back to main trunk.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Branch tree visible in sidebar. Navigation between branches instant.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-branches.js: BranchVisualizer. Tree construction from
  chat_history, SVG rendering, branch navigation.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Branch detection on message edit, layer creation.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Branch tree styles, active highlight, node sizes.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  Branch metadata stored in sessions.graph_memory as conversation_branches JSONB.

API CONTRACTS:
NOT APPLICABLE. Branch tree computed client-side from chat history structure.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Branch tree in sidebar

  ┌──────────────────────────────┐
  │  CONVERSATION BRANCHES       │
  │                              │
  │  ○ Start                     │
  │  │                           │
  │  ○ msg 1                     │
  │  │                           │
  │  ○ ...                       │
  │  │                           │
  │  ○ msg 12                    │
  │  ├──── ○ Branch A (5 msgs)   │
  │  │                           │
  │  ○ ...                       │
  │  │                           │
  │  ○ msg 18                    │
  │  ├──── ○ Branch B (5 msgs)   │
  │  │                           │
  │  ● msg 30 (current)         │
  │                              │
  │  ● = active   ○ = explored  │
  └──────────────────────────────┘

  Active node: var(--accent-gold) filled circle
  Explored node: var(--text-secondary) outlined circle
  Branch lines: var(--bg-elevated) 2px

TRANSITIONS: Branch tree draws with 20ms node stagger. Click jump 200ms scroll.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Chat messages accumulated --> Branch points detected:
  - Message edit creates alternate timeline branch
  - Layer creation (#127) creates depth branch
  - Explicit "branch" command creates manual branch
  --> Tree structure built from parent-child message relationships
  --> SVG tree rendered in sidebar panel
  --> Click on node navigates to that message in chat

ERROR PATH: Linear conversation (no branches) --> simple vertical line with "No branches yet"
DATA STORED: Branch structure in sessions.graph_memory.conversation_branches
DATA CACHED: Tree DOM in sidebar

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

NOT APPLICABLE. Branch visualization is a UI navigation feature.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Branch node          | Click       | Jump to that message in conversation        |
| Branch node          | Hover       | Show message preview tooltip                |
| Branch label         | Click       | Switch active branch                        |
| "Merge" button       | Click       | Merge branch back into main trunk           |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Tree draw 20ms stagger. Jump scroll 200ms smooth.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Tree has role="tree". Nodes are role="treeitem". Navigation via arrow keys.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: No branches (linear conversation)
   Expected: Simple vertical line. "All messages are in the main thread."

2. Scenario: 10+ branches creating complex tree
   Expected: Tree scrollable. Zoom out to fit. Branch labels for identification.

3. Scenario: User navigates to old branch and continues typing
   Expected: New messages added to that branch. Branch becomes active.

4. Scenario: Branch has only 1 message (created and abandoned)
   Expected: Shown as stub branch. Can be deleted via right-click.

5. Scenario: Layered chat (#127) layers shown as depth branches
   Expected: Layers rendered as vertical sub-trees descending from their parent.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001, #006, #007
ENABLES: Visual navigation of complex conversations
ENHANCES: #010 (Threads), #127 (Layered Chat), #040 (Message Editing)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: chat_history structure

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[] Click navigates to message in conversation
[] Active branch highlighted
[] Branch labels and message counts

VISUAL: [] SVG tree with nodes, edges, active highlight
PERFORMANCE: [] Tree render < 200ms for 100 messages
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Tree role, treeitem roles, arrow key navigation
EDGE CASES: [] No branches handled, complex trees scrollable, stub branches shown

================================================================================
FEATURE #134: NOTION/OBSIDIAN EXPORT
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Export & Sharing
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           G
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #119 (Share Chat Snapshot), #011 (Artifact Cards)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #006 (Conversation Memory)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Notion/Obsidian Export enables users to export their Athena conversation,
notes, and research insights into formats compatible with Notion and Obsidian
knowledge management tools. For Obsidian, the export produces a folder of
interlinked Markdown files: one file per discussed paper, one file per major
conversation topic, and a master index file. Internal links use Obsidian's
[[wikilink]] syntax so papers reference each other. YAML frontmatter includes
paper metadata (title, year, authors, DOI, citation_count). For Notion, the
export produces a structured Markdown file with toggles (using Notion's
supported syntax), database-compatible CSV for paper properties, and a
structured outline. Both formats preserve the intellectual structure of the
conversation, not just the raw text.

The export is generated server-side to access full chat history, graph data,
and paper metadata. The output is a ZIP file containing all export files.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User clicks the export button in the Athena toolbar and selects "Obsidian" or
"Notion" from the format dropdown, or types /export obsidian or /export notion.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User has explored the Attention lineage for 30 minutes.
2. User types /export obsidian.
3. Athena: "Preparing your Obsidian vault export... This includes 12 papers
   discussed, 3 topic notes, and your 5 sticky notes."
4. Export generates. Download link appears: "research-session-2026-03-24.zip"
5. User downloads and extracts into their Obsidian vault.
6. Obsidian shows: index.md linking to paper-attention-is-all-you-need.md,
   paper-bert.md, topic-mutation-patterns.md, etc. All interlinked.

AFTER COMPLETION:
ZIP file downloaded. Research session preserved in knowledge management tool.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-export.js: ExportManager. Format selection, download trigger.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/export endpoint generating export ZIP.
- backend/athena_export.py: ObsidianExporter and NotionExporter classes.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/export', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_export():
      session_id = request.json['session_id']
      format = request.json['format']  # 'obsidian' or 'notion'
      history = load_chat_history(session_id)
      graph_data = load_graph(session_id)
      notes = load_sticky_notes(session_id)
      if format == 'obsidian':
          zip_buffer = ObsidianExporter(history, graph_data, notes).export()
      elif format == 'notion':
          zip_buffer = NotionExporter(history, graph_data, notes).export()
      return send_file(zip_buffer, mimetype='application/zip',
                       download_name=f'athena-{format}-{session_id[:8]}.zip')

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```python
class ObsidianExporter:
    def export(self):
        files = {}
        # Index file
        files['index.md'] = self.build_index()
        # Per-paper files
        for paper in self.discussed_papers:
            slug = self.slugify(paper['title'])
            files[f'papers/{slug}.md'] = self.build_paper_note(paper)
        # Topic files
        for topic in self.extract_topics():
            files[f'topics/{topic["slug"]}.md'] = self.build_topic_note(topic)
        # Sticky notes
        for note in self.notes:
            files[f'notes/{note["id"]}.md'] = self.build_sticky_export(note)
        return self.zip_files(files)

    def build_paper_note(self, paper):
        frontmatter = f---
title: {paper['title']}
year: {paper.get('year', 'unknown')}
authors: {', '.join(paper.get('authors', []))}
doi: {paper.get('doi', '')}
citations: {paper.get('citation_count', 0)}
tags: [{', '.join(paper.get('fields_of_study', []))}]
---
        body = f"# {paper['title']}\n\n"
        # Add mutation relationships as wikilinks
        for edge in self.get_edges_for(paper['paper_id']):
            target = self.get_paper(edge['cited_paper_id'])
            body += f"- [[{self.slugify(target['title'])}|{target['title']}]]: {edge['mutation_type']}\n"
        return frontmatter + '\n\n' + body
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/export
Request: { "session_id": "...", "format": "obsidian" | "notion" }
Response: ZIP file download (application/zip)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Export dialog

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌─ EXPORT ──────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │                                                    │   │
  │  │  Export your research session to:                  │   │
  │  │                                                    │   │
  │  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐                │   │
  │  │  │  Obsidian   │  │   Notion    │                │   │
  │  │  │  Vault      │  │   Page      │                │   │
  │  │  │  (.md)      │  │   (.md+csv) │                │   │
  │  │  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘                │   │
  │  │                                                    │   │
  │  │  Includes: 12 papers, 3 topics, 5 notes           │   │
  │  │                                                    │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Dialog fadeIn 150ms. Download progress bar for generation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User selects format --> POST /api/athena/export
  --> Backend loads chat_history, graph_data, sticky_notes
  --> Exporter class builds file structure:
      - Obsidian: Markdown files with YAML frontmatter and [[wikilinks]]
      - Notion: Markdown with toggle syntax and properties CSV
  --> Files zipped in memory
  --> ZIP streamed as download response
  --> Frontend triggers download

ERROR PATH: No chat history --> "Nothing to export yet. Start a conversation first."
DATA STORED: action_log: { action: 'export', format: 'obsidian' }
DATA CACHED: NONE (generated fresh each export)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (optional, for topic extraction)
PROMPT: "Summarize the main topics discussed in this conversation for note organization: {messages_summary}. Return as JSON array of {topic, slug, summary}."
FALLBACK: Keyword-based topic extraction without LLM.
TOKEN BUDGET: ~500 context + ~200 output

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Export button        | Click       | Opens format selection dialog                |
| Format card          | Click       | Starts export generation                    |
| Download link        | Click       | Downloads ZIP file                          |
| Cancel button        | Click       | Closes dialog                               |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Dialog fadeIn 150ms. Progress bar during generation.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Dialog is role="dialog" with aria-label. Format cards are buttons. Download link announced.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: No graph loaded (text-only conversation)
   Expected: Export contains conversation transcript only. No paper files.

2. Scenario: Session has 0 sticky notes
   Expected: Notes folder omitted from ZIP.

3. Scenario: Paper title contains special characters
   Expected: Filename slugified (spaces to hyphens, special chars removed).
   Title preserved in frontmatter.

4. Scenario: Very large session (100+ messages, 50+ papers)
   Expected: Export generation may take 5-10s. Progress bar shown.

5. Scenario: Obsidian vault already has a file with same name
   Expected: User responsibility. Export files include session date in names
   to reduce collision likelihood.

6. Scenario: Export requested while conversation is still streaming
   Expected: Export includes all completed messages. Streaming message excluded.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001, #002, #006
ENABLES: Research session preservation in knowledge tools
ENHANCES: #119 (Share Chat Snapshot), #129 (Sticky Notes)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Obsidian Markdown format, Notion import format

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] Obsidian export produces interlinked Markdown files with YAML frontmatter
[] Notion export produces structured Markdown with properties CSV
[] Per-paper files with mutation relationships as wikilinks
[] Topic notes extracted from conversation
[] Sticky notes included in export
[] ZIP download with organized folder structure

================================================================================
FEATURE #135: EVOLVING SUMMARY BAR
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Chat Controls
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 12
OVERLAPS WITH:   #131 (Chat About the Chat), #135 (itself -- unique)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #006 (Conversation Memory), #037 (Streaming)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Evolving Summary Bar is a persistent thin bar at the top of the Athena chat
panel that displays a continuously updated one-line summary of the conversation
so far. As the conversation progresses, the summary evolves to reflect the
current focus. After 3 messages it might read: "Exploring the attention
mechanism lineage." After 15 messages: "Analyzing mutation patterns between
Vaswani 2017 and BERT, with focus on generalization dominance." After 30
messages: "Deep dive into generalization mutations post-2015, contradictions
in ResNet branch, and pruning impact of foundational papers."

The summary updates after every assistant response. It is computed by sending
the last 5 messages plus the previous summary to the LLM with a compression
prompt. The result replaces the bar content. The bar is always visible, never
scrolls with the chat, and acts as a persistent orientation anchor for the
user.

Clicking the summary bar expands it to show 3-5 key topics as clickable pills
that scroll to the relevant conversation segment. This makes the bar both an
orientation tool and a navigation aid.

The summary computation is lightweight: it piggybacks on the Athena response
pipeline, adding a small secondary LLM call (50 tokens in, 30 tokens out)
after each response. The bar update is non-blocking and asynchronous.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
Automatically appears after the 3rd message exchange in a conversation.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User has 3 exchanges about the attention mechanism.
2. Summary bar appears at top: "Exploring the attention mechanism lineage."
3. After 10 more exchanges focusing on BERT, bar updates: "Mutation analysis
   of Vaswani-to-BERT generalization in attention lineage."
4. User clicks the bar. It expands to show pills: [Attention mechanism]
   [BERT mutations] [Generalization pattern].
5. User clicks [BERT mutations]. Chat scrolls to message #8 where BERT
   discussion started.
6. Bar collapses back to one-line summary.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Summary bar always visible, always current. Navigation via topic pills.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-summary-bar.js: EvolvingSummaryBar class. Summary display,
  topic pills, navigation, LLM summary trigger.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Triggers summary update after each response.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Summary bar styles (fixed top, pill buttons).
- app.py: POST /api/athena/summarize endpoint for summary compression.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/summarize', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_summarize():
      previous_summary = request.json.get('previous_summary', '')
      recent_messages = request.json['recent_messages']  # last 5
      prompt = f"Previous summary: {previous_summary}\nRecent messages: {recent_messages}\nUpdate the summary in one sentence (max 100 chars). Also extract 3-5 key topics."
      result = llm_client.generate(prompt, model=config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL)
      return jsonify({'summary': result['summary'], 'topics': result['topics']})

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/summarize
Request: { "session_id": "...", "previous_summary": "...", "recent_messages": [...] }
Response: { "summary": "...", "topics": [{"label": "...", "message_index": 8}] }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Summary bar (collapsed)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ▾ Analyzing mutation patterns between Vaswani and BERT... │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                           │
  │  (chat messages below)                                    │

STATE: Summary bar (expanded)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ▴ Analyzing mutation patterns between Vaswani and BERT    │
  │                                                           │
  │  [Attention mechanism] [BERT mutations] [Generalization]  │
  │  [Pruning impact]                                         │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                           │
  │  (chat messages below)                                    │

  Bar: var(--bg-surface) background, var(--text-primary) text, 14px
  Pills: var(--bg-elevated) background, var(--accent-gold) text, 12px, rounded
  Height: 36px collapsed, ~80px expanded

TRANSITIONS: Bar text crossfade 300ms on update. Expand/collapse 150ms.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Athena response completes --> trigger summary update (async, non-blocking)
  --> POST /api/athena/summarize with previous summary + last 5 messages
  --> LLM compresses into one-line summary + topic list
  --> Frontend updates bar text with crossfade transition
  --> Topic pills stored for expand view

ERROR PATH: Summary call fails --> bar keeps previous summary. No error shown.
DATA STORED: Current summary in sessions.graph_memory.conversation_summary
DATA CACHED: Summary and topics in memory

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL
PROMPT: "Update this conversation summary. Previous: '{previous_summary}'. Recent messages: {messages}. Return JSON: {summary: '...', topics: [{label, message_index}]}. Summary must be under 100 characters."
TOKEN BUDGET: ~200 context + ~80 output = ~280 per summary update
FALLBACK: Keyword extraction from last 5 messages joined as summary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Summary bar          | Click       | Expand to show topic pills                  |
| Topic pill           | Click       | Scroll to relevant conversation segment     |
| Summary bar (expanded)| Click header| Collapse back to one-line                  |
| Summary text         | Hover       | Show full text if truncated                 |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Text crossfade 300ms. Expand 150ms. Pill fadeIn stagger 50ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Bar is role="status" with aria-live="polite". Topic pills are buttons with aria-label. Expand/collapse announced.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Conversation shorter than 3 messages
   Expected: Bar not shown. Appears after 3rd exchange.

2. Scenario: Summary text exceeds bar width
   Expected: Truncated with ellipsis. Full text on hover.

3. Scenario: LLM returns summary over 100 characters
   Expected: Truncated at 100 characters with ellipsis.

4. Scenario: Rapid messages (5 in 10 seconds)
   Expected: Summary update debounced. Only updates after last response.

5. Scenario: User switches to a different graph mid-conversation
   Expected: Summary resets. "Switched to new graph: [title]."

6. Scenario: Summary update takes too long (> 3s)
   Expected: Previous summary stays. Update silently discarded. Next response
   triggers a fresh attempt.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001, #006, #037
ENABLES: Persistent conversation orientation
ENHANCES: #131 (Chat About the Chat)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: NONE

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] Summary bar appears after 3rd message exchange
[] Summary updates after each assistant response
[] One-line summary under 100 characters
[] Expand shows 3-5 topic pills
[] Topic pill click scrolls to conversation segment
[] Summary persisted in session

VISUAL:
[] Fixed top bar with crossfade text updates
[] Topic pills with gold text on elevated background
[] Expand/collapse animation

PERFORMANCE: [] Summary update < 2s, bar render < 10ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [] role="status" aria-live, pills as buttons, expand announced
EDGE CASES: [] Short conversations, truncation, rapid messages, graph switch

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-summary-bar.js, app.py endpoint, CSS 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST with various conversation lengths 4. CHECK summary accuracy 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24


================================================================================
FEATURE #136: FOOTNOTE SYSTEM
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 10
OVERLAPS WITH:   #137 (Marginalia), #002 (Rich Response Formatting)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #037 (Streaming Text)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Footnote System adds inline numbered footnote markers to Athena responses that
link to detailed annotations at the bottom of the message bubble. When Athena
makes a factual claim about a paper relationship, mutation classification, or
statistical observation, it inserts a superscript number that the user can
click to see the supporting evidence. The footnote content includes the data
source (which paper, which edge_analysis entry), the confidence tier, and a
brief explanation of how the claim was derived.

This feature directly supports Arivu's trust philosophy by making every claim
traceable. Instead of Athena saying "BERT generalized Vaswani's attention
mechanism," it says "BERT generalized Vaswani's attention mechanism[1]" where
[1] links to: "Source: edge_analysis 204e3073...:df2b0e26..., mutation_type:
generalization, confidence: HIGH (0.82). Evidence: Both papers implement
multi-head attention; BERT applies it to bidirectional context."

Footnotes are generated server-side as part of the response. The LLM is
prompted to include [N] markers in its output text. The backend attaches
footnote content as structured metadata. The frontend renders superscript
numbers and a collapsible footnote section at the bottom of each message.

The footnote section is collapsed by default, showing only "N footnotes" as a
clickable count. Expanding reveals all footnotes. Individual footnote markers
in the text highlight their corresponding footnote when hovered, and vice
versa.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
Athena response contains factual claims about graph data.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "How does BERT relate to the original attention paper?"
2. Athena responds: "BERT (Devlin 2018) generalized the self-attention
   mechanism[1] from Vaswani et al.'s Transformer architecture[2], applying
   it to bidirectional language understanding[3]."
3. At the bottom of the message: "3 footnotes [expand]"
4. User clicks [expand]. Footnote section reveals:
   [1] edge_analysis: generalization mutation, confidence HIGH (0.82)
   [2] Paper: "Attention Is All You Need" (2017), citations: 95,000+
   [3] LLM inference based on abstract comparison, confidence MEDIUM (0.61)
5. User hovers over [1] in the text. Footnote [1] below highlights with
   gold background.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Footnoted response visible. User can trace every claim to its source.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-footnotes.js: FootnoteSystem. Marker rendering, footnote
  section, hover linking, expand/collapse.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Integrates footnotes into response rendering.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Superscript markers, footnote section, hover
  highlight, expand/collapse styles.
- app.py: Footnote generation in Athena chat response.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  LLM prompted to include [N] markers. Post-processing extracts markers and
  generates footnote content from graph data:
  def generate_footnotes(response_text, graph_data, referenced_papers, referenced_edges):
      markers = re.findall(r'\[(\d+)\]', response_text)
      footnotes = []
      for i, marker in enumerate(markers):
          edge = find_referenced_edge(response_text, marker, referenced_edges)
          if edge:
              footnotes.append({
                  'index': int(marker),
                  'type': 'edge_analysis',
                  'source': edge['edge_id'],
                  'mutation_type': edge['mutation_type'],
                  'confidence': edge['mutation_confidence'],
                  'tier': get_confidence_tier(edge['mutation_confidence']),
                  'evidence': edge.get('mutation_evidence', '')
              })
          else:
              footnotes.append({
                  'index': int(marker),
                  'type': 'llm_inference',
                  'confidence': 0.5,
                  'tier': 'MEDIUM',
                  'note': 'Based on LLM analysis of paper content'
              })
      return footnotes

API CONTRACTS:
Response metadata in SSE stream:
{ "type": "footnotes", "data": [{ "index": 1, "type": "edge_analysis", "source": "...", "mutation_type": "generalization", "confidence": 0.82, "tier": "HIGH", "evidence": "..." }] }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Response with footnotes

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌─ Athena ──────────────────────────────────────┐       │
  │  │ BERT (Devlin 2018) generalized the self-      │       │
  │  │ attention mechanism[1] from Vaswani et al.'s   │       │
  │  │ Transformer architecture[2], applying it to    │       │
  │  │ bidirectional language understanding[3].       │       │
  │  │                                                │       │
  │  │ ──────────────────────────────────             │       │
  │  │ 3 footnotes                         [expand]   │       │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Footnotes expanded

  │  │ ──────────────────────────────────             │
  │  │ [1] edge_analysis: generalization              │
  │  │     mutation, confidence HIGH (0.82)           │
  │  │     Evidence: Both papers implement multi-     │
  │  │     head attention; BERT applies it to...      │
  │  │                                                │
  │  │ [2] Paper: Attention Is All You Need (2017)    │
  │  │     95,000+ citations                          │
  │  │                                                │
  │  │ [3] LLM inference, confidence MEDIUM (0.61)    │
  │  │                                    [collapse]  │

  Superscript: var(--accent-gold), 10px, vertical-align super, cursor pointer
  Footnote: var(--text-secondary), 12px, indented
  Highlight: var(--accent-gold) 10% opacity background on hover-linked pair

TRANSITIONS: Expand/collapse 200ms. Hover highlight 100ms.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Athena response generated with [N] markers in text
  --> Backend resolves markers to graph data sources
  --> Footnote metadata attached to response
  --> Frontend renders superscript markers in message text
  --> Footnote section rendered below message (collapsed by default)
  --> Hover on marker highlights corresponding footnote and vice versa
  --> Click on marker scrolls to footnote

ERROR PATH: No markers in response --> no footnote section shown
DATA STORED: Footnotes in chat_history message metadata
DATA CACHED: Footnote DOM elements linked to markers

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL
PROMPT ADDITION: "Include [N] footnote markers after factual claims about papers, mutations, or statistics. Number sequentially starting from [1]."
TOKEN BUDGET: Minimal overhead (~10 tokens for markers in output)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Superscript [N]      | Hover       | Highlights corresponding footnote below     |
| Superscript [N]      | Click       | Scrolls to and expands footnote section     |
| Footnote [N]         | Hover       | Highlights corresponding marker in text     |
| "expand" button      | Click       | Shows all footnotes                         |
| "collapse" button    | Click       | Hides footnote section                      |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Expand 200ms. Highlight 100ms background.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Superscripts are links with aria-label "Footnote N". Footnotes have id="fn-N". Link relationships via aria-describedby. Footnote section role="complementary".

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Response has no factual claims (general discussion)
   Expected: No footnote markers. No footnote section.

2. Scenario: LLM inserts markers but backend cannot resolve sources
   Expected: Footnotes marked as "LLM inference" with MEDIUM confidence.

3. Scenario: 20+ footnotes in a single response
   Expected: Footnote section scrollable. All markers functional.

4. Scenario: Footnote references a retracted paper
   Expected: Footnote includes retraction warning: "Note: This paper has been retracted."

5. Scenario: Streaming response with footnotes arriving incrementally
   Expected: Markers render during streaming. Footnote section updates when
   footnote metadata event arrives after text.

6. Scenario: User copies response text
   Expected: Footnote markers copied as [N] text. Footnote content not included
   in text copy (separate section).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #002, #037
ENABLES: Evidence-traceable responses
ENHANCES: #137 (Marginalia), #132 (Confidence Thermometer)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: edge_analysis data, paper metadata

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] Superscript [N] markers in response text
[] Footnote section at bottom of message (collapsed by default)
[] Footnotes sourced from edge_analysis and paper metadata
[] Hover linking between markers and footnotes
[] Click scrolls to footnote
[] Confidence tier shown per footnote

VISUAL:
[] Gold superscripts, indented footnotes, hover highlights
[] Expand/collapse animation

PERFORMANCE: [] Footnote resolution < 100ms, render < 50ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Markers as links, footnotes with ids, aria-describedby linking
EDGE CASES: [] No markers, unresolvable sources, streaming, retracted papers

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-footnotes.js, app.py footnote generation, CSS 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST with various response types 4. CHECK footnote accuracy against graph data 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

================================================================================
FEATURE #137: MARGINALIA
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #136 (Footnote System), #129 (Sticky Notes)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #007 (Visual Redesign), #136 (Footnote System)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Marginalia adds a slim annotation column beside Athena's response bubbles
where the system and the user can place contextual notes, mini-charts, and
quick references that relate to specific paragraphs. System-generated
marginalia includes citation counts beside paper names, mini confidence badges
beside claims, field-color dots beside paper references, and year markers
beside historical discussions. User-generated marginalia lets users add their
own annotations (like Sticky Notes #129 but inline beside specific paragraphs
rather than pinned to whole messages). The column is 80px wide, positioned to
the left of assistant messages, and only visible on screens wider than 1024px.
On narrower screens, marginalia collapses into inline annotations.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
Automatically generated on every Athena response. User can add custom marginalia.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. Athena responds with a paragraph about Vaswani 2017's impact.
2. In the margin beside the paragraph: a small "95K" citation count badge
   beside the Vaswani reference, a green HIGH confidence dot beside the
   mutation claim, and "2017" year marker.
3. User double-clicks in the margin. A small annotation input appears.
4. User types: "Cross-reference with biology domain?" and presses Enter.
5. Custom marginalia note appears in the margin beside that paragraph.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Marginalia visible beside response. System and user annotations coexist.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-marginalia.js: MarginaliaRenderer. Auto-generation from
  response metadata, user annotation input, responsive collapse.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Integration for marginalia data passing.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Margin column layout, badges, annotations.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  Marginalia data generated alongside response. Paper references in response
  text annotated with metadata: { paper_id, citation_count, field, year }.
  Claim confidence annotated with mutation_confidence tier.

API CONTRACTS:
Response metadata in SSE stream:
{ "type": "marginalia", "data": [{ "paragraph_index": 0, "items": [{ "type": "citation_count", "value": "95K", "paper_id": "..." }, { "type": "confidence", "tier": "HIGH" }] }] }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Marginalia beside response

  ┌──────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ MARGIN   │  Athena response                               │
  │          │                                                 │
  │  95K ●   │  The Attention Is All You Need paper (Vaswani   │
  │  2017    │  2017) introduced the self-attention mechanism  │
  │  ●HIGH   │  that generalized[1] to BERT's bidirectional   │
  │          │  approach...                                    │
  │          │                                                 │
  │  "Check  │  The generalization mutation is the dominant    │
  │  bio?"   │  pattern in this lineage, accounting for 60%   │
  │  [user]  │  of all edge classifications...                │
  │          │                                                 │
  └──────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Margin width: 80px. Background: transparent.
  Badges: 10px font, rounded pill, field-appropriate colors.
  User notes: 11px italic, var(--accent-gold) border-left.

TRANSITIONS: Badges fadeIn 100ms stagger. User note 150ms slideIn.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Athena response generated --> Backend attaches marginalia metadata per paragraph
  --> Frontend renders margin column beside response
  --> Auto-generated badges placed at paragraph level
  --> User can add custom annotations via double-click
  --> User annotations saved to session
  --> Responsive: collapses to inline on screens < 1024px

ERROR PATH: No metadata --> margin column hidden for that response
DATA STORED: User marginalia in sessions.graph_memory.user_marginalia JSONB
DATA CACHED: Marginalia DOM elements per message

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

NOT APPLICABLE. Marginalia is derived from existing metadata, not LLM output.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action         | Response                              |
|----------------------|----------------|---------------------------------------|
| Margin column        | Double-click   | Opens annotation input at that point  |
| Citation badge       | Hover          | Shows paper full title and details    |
| Confidence dot       | Hover          | Shows confidence tier explanation     |
| User annotation      | Click          | Edits the annotation text             |
| User annotation      | Right-click    | Delete annotation option              |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Badge fadeIn 100ms. Note slideIn 150ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Margin items have aria-label descriptions. Not essential (decorative supplement). Screen readers can skip margin via aria-hidden on decorative items.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Screen narrower than 1024px
   Expected: Margin column hidden. Badges render inline within text.

2. Scenario: Paragraph has 10+ marginalia items
   Expected: Items stack vertically. Overflow scrolls within margin cell.

3. Scenario: User annotation is very long
   Expected: Truncated to 50 chars in margin. Full text on hover/click.

4. Scenario: Response has no paper references or claims
   Expected: Margin column hidden (no auto-generated items). User can still
   add custom annotations.

5. Scenario: Print or export
   Expected: Marginalia included as inline annotations in export format.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #002, #007, #136
ENABLES: Inline contextual annotations
ENHANCES: #129 (Sticky Notes), #132 (Confidence Thermometer)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Paper metadata, edge_analysis data

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] Auto-generated marginalia beside responses (citations, confidence, years)
[] User can add custom annotations via double-click
[] Responsive collapse on screens < 1024px
[] User annotations persisted in session

VISUAL: [] 80px margin column, badge styles, user note styles
PERFORMANCE: [] Marginalia render < 50ms per response
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Decorative items aria-hidden, user notes labeled
EDGE CASES: [] Narrow screens, many items, long annotations, no metadata

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

================================================================================
FEATURE #138: REDLINE MODE
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #014 (Comparison Mode), #018 (Debate Mode)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Formatting), #037 (Streaming), #014 (Comparison Mode)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Redline Mode turns Athena into a critical reviewer that challenges the user's
interpretation of the ancestry graph. When activated via /redline, Athena
shifts from a helpful assistant to an adversarial reviewer: it identifies
weaknesses in the user's stated hypotheses, flags potential misinterpretations
of mutation classifications, points out papers the user has overlooked, and
questions whether the user's conclusions are supported by the graph data.
This mode draws from the adversarial_reviewer.py module (Phase 7) and the
edge_analysis confidence tiers to construct evidence-based critiques. Every
challenge includes a specific reference to graph data. The visual treatment
uses red accent colors throughout: response bubbles have a red left border,
challenge markers use red badges, and the mode indicator shows "REDLINE ACTIVE"
in the header. The mode is deliberately uncomfortable to use, forcing the
researcher to defend their thinking.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User types /redline or activates from the mode menu.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types /redline. Header shows "REDLINE ACTIVE" in red.
2. User states: "I think the generalization mutation is the most important
   pattern in this lineage."
3. Athena responds in redline mode: "Challenge: Your claim assumes generalization
   is dominant, but 34% of edges are classified as adoption, not generalization.
   Additionally, 3 of your 5 'generalization' edges have SPECULATIVE confidence.
   Paper [X] which you have not examined contradicts this pattern with a direct
   contradiction edge."
4. User responds with a defense or revised hypothesis.
5. Athena continues challenging until user types /redline off.

AFTER COMPLETION:
User has stress-tested their hypothesis against graph data.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-redline.js: RedlineMode. Mode state, visual treatment,
  challenge formatting.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Mode activation, prompt modification.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Redline styles (red borders, badges, header).
- app.py: Redline prompt injection in /api/athena/chat when mode active.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  When redline mode is active (stored in session persona), the system prompt
  for LLM calls is modified:
  "You are a critical academic reviewer. Challenge every claim the user makes.
  Find weaknesses in their interpretation. Reference specific graph data:
  mutation types, confidence tiers, overlooked papers. Be constructive but
  rigorous. Never agree without evidence."

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat with session persona='redline' triggers modified prompt.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Redline mode active

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌ REDLINE ACTIVE ──────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │ (red header bar)                                  │    │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┌─ User ────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
  │  │ I think generalization is the most important   │       │
  │  │ pattern in this lineage.                       │       │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┌─ Athena [CHALLENGE] ─────────────────────────┐        │
  │  ┃ Challenge: Your claim assumes generalization   │        │
  │  ┃ is dominant, but 34% of edges are adoption.    │        │
  │  ┃ 3 of 5 "generalization" edges have SPECULATIVE │        │
  │  ┃ confidence. Paper [X] contradicts this...      │        │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
  │                                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Red border-left: 3px var(--danger) on assistant messages
  Challenge badge: var(--danger) background, white text, 10px
  Header: var(--danger) background, white text, pulsing glow

TRANSITIONS: Mode activation 300ms red wash overlay. Deactivation fade 200ms.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User activates /redline --> session persona set to 'redline'
  --> All subsequent /api/athena/chat calls include redline system prompt
  --> Backend loads graph data for evidence-based challenges
  --> LLM generates adversarial responses with specific data references
  --> Frontend renders with red styling treatment
  --> /redline off restores normal mode

ERROR PATH: No graph loaded --> "Redline mode needs graph data to challenge your claims. Load a graph first."
DATA STORED: Session persona in sessions table. Redline history in chat_history.
DATA CACHED: Mode state in frontend

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (for nuanced critique)
SYSTEM PROMPT: "You are a critical academic reviewer analyzing the user's interpretation of a research ancestry graph. Challenge every claim with specific data: mutation type distributions, confidence tiers, overlooked papers, alternative interpretations. Be constructive but rigorous. Reference exact percentages and paper titles from the graph data."
CONTEXT: Full graph summary + user's recent claims
TOKEN BUDGET: ~1500 context (graph + conversation) + ~500 output

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| /redline command     | Enter       | Activates redline mode                      |
| /redline off         | Enter       | Deactivates redline mode                    |
| Challenge badge      | Hover       | Shows challenge category (data, logic, gap) |
| Data reference       | Click       | Highlights referenced edge/node in graph    |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Mode activation 300ms. Red pulse on header. Challenge badge bounce 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Mode change announced. Challenge badges have aria-label. Red styling supplemented with [CHALLENGE] text prefix (not color-only).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User asks a factual question (not making a claim)
   Expected: Athena answers factually but adds: "If you are asserting X,
   consider that..."

2. Scenario: User's claim is well-supported by graph data
   Expected: Athena acknowledges the strength but probes further: "Your claim
   holds for the direct edges, but have you considered second-order effects?"

3. Scenario: Redline mode with no graph loaded
   Expected: Mode activation blocked. "Load a graph first."

4. Scenario: User forgets to deactivate and asks unrelated question
   Expected: Athena still applies critical lens. User can type /redline off.

5. Scenario: User gets frustrated and wants to stop
   Expected: /redline off immediately restores normal mode. No judgment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001, #002, #037, #014
ENABLES: Hypothesis stress-testing
ENHANCES: #018 (Debate Mode), #029 (Socratic Mode)
CONFLICTS: Cannot be active simultaneously with Teach Me Mode (#024)
EXTERNAL: edge_analysis mutation distributions, graph node data

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] /redline activates adversarial review mode
[] Challenges reference specific graph data
[] Red visual treatment on responses
[] /redline off deactivates mode
[] Mode state persisted in session

VISUAL: [] Red border, header bar, challenge badges, pulse animation
PERFORMANCE: [] Challenge generation same as normal response time
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Mode change announced, [CHALLENGE] text prefix, badges labeled
EDGE CASES: [] No graph blocked, factual questions handled, well-supported claims probed

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-redline.js, app.py prompt modification, CSS 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST challenges against graph data 4. CHECK data reference accuracy 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24


================================================================================
FEATURE #139: LIVE DIAGRAM BUILDER
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 22
OVERLAPS WITH:   #122 (Handwriting/Sketch), #125 (Canvas Chat)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Formatting), #007 (Visual Redesign), #037 (Streaming)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Live Diagram Builder lets users collaboratively construct diagrams with Athena
during conversation. The user describes a relationship ("draw a box for
Vaswani 2017 connected to a box for BERT with a 'generalization' arrow") and
Athena renders an editable diagram in a block within the chat. The user can
then modify the diagram by voice command ("make the arrow thicker," "add
ResNet as a third node") or by direct manipulation (dragging, resizing). The
diagram is SVG-based and can reference actual graph nodes from the loaded
ancestry graph. This is distinct from the sketch input (#122) because the
output is structured vector graphics, not freehand drawing, and Athena
actively participates in building the diagram based on natural language
instructions.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User types /diagram or asks "draw me a diagram of..."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User: "Draw a diagram showing how Vaswani 2017 connects to BERT and GPT-2."
2. Athena renders an SVG diagram block in chat: three boxes with labeled
   arrows (Vaswani -> BERT: "generalization", Vaswani -> GPT-2: "adoption").
3. User: "Add ResNet to the diagram with a dotted line to Vaswani."
4. Athena updates the diagram: adds ResNet box, dotted arrow to Vaswani.
5. User drags the GPT-2 box to the right for better layout.
6. User: "Color the generalization arrow green and the adoption arrow blue."
7. Athena updates arrow colors.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Interactive diagram visible in chat. Exportable as SVG.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-diagram.js: DiagramBuilder class. SVG generation, natural
  language instruction parsing, direct manipulation, export.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: DiagramBlock renderer.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: /diagram command routing.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Diagram block styles, node/edge rendering.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/diagram for LLM-driven diagram generation.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/diagram', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_diagram():
      instruction = request.json['instruction']
      current_diagram = request.json.get('current_diagram')
      graph_data = load_graph(session_id)
      # LLM generates diagram JSON from instruction + graph context
      diagram_json = llm_generate_diagram(instruction, current_diagram, graph_data)
      return jsonify(diagram_json)

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/diagram
Request: { "session_id": "...", "instruction": "...", "current_diagram": {...} or null }
Response: { "nodes": [{"id": "...", "label": "...", "x": 0, "y": 0, "style": {...}}], "edges": [{"from": "...", "to": "...", "label": "...", "style": {...}}] }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Diagram in chat

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌─ DIAGRAM ──────────────────────────────────────┐      │
  │  │                                                  │      │
  │  │  ┌──────────────┐    generalization             │      │
  │  │  │ Vaswani 2017 │ ─────────────────→ ┌────────┐│      │
  │  │  └──────┬───────┘                     │ BERT   ││      │
  │  │         │                             └────────┘│      │
  │  │         │ adoption                              │      │
  │  │         └─────────────────────→ ┌─────────────┐│      │
  │  │                                 │ GPT-2       ││      │
  │  │                                 └─────────────┘│      │
  │  │  ┌──────────┐                                   │      │
  │  │  │ ResNet   │ ............> Vaswani 2017       │      │
  │  │  └──────────┘  (dotted)                         │      │
  │  │                                                  │      │
  │  │  [Export SVG] [Edit] [Reset]                    │      │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Nodes appear 150ms fade. Edge draws 200ms path animation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User describes diagram --> POST /api/athena/diagram
  --> LLM generates structured diagram JSON from instruction
  --> Cross-reference with graph data for real paper metadata
  --> Frontend renders SVG diagram from JSON
  --> User modifies via text ("add X") or direct manipulation (drag)
  --> Modifications sent back to backend for LLM update
  --> Diagram state maintained per conversation

ERROR PATH: Ambiguous instruction --> "I am not sure what you mean. Can you specify which papers?"
DATA STORED: Diagram JSON in chat_history as block metadata
DATA CACHED: Diagram state in memory per conversation

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (for diagram generation from natural language)
PROMPT: "Generate a diagram JSON from this instruction: {instruction}. Current diagram: {current_diagram_or_null}. Available graph nodes: {node_summary}. Return JSON: {nodes: [{id, label, x, y, style}], edges: [{from, to, label, style}]}"
TOKEN BUDGET: ~800 context + ~400 output

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Diagram node         | Drag        | Reposition node in SVG                      |
| Diagram edge         | Click       | Show edge label and mutation details        |
| "Export SVG" button  | Click       | Download diagram as SVG file                |
| "Edit" button        | Click       | Opens text input for diagram modifications  |
| "Reset" button       | Click       | Clears diagram back to initial state        |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Node fade 150ms. Edge path draw 200ms. Drag at 60fps.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: SVG has role="img" with aria-label describing diagram content. Nodes are focusable. Tab navigates between nodes.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User references papers not in the graph
   Expected: Create generic nodes with "(not in graph)" label.

2. Scenario: Diagram becomes very complex (20+ nodes)
   Expected: Auto-layout using force-directed positioning. Zoom controls.

3. Scenario: User asks to modify a diagram from many messages ago
   Expected: Most recent diagram in conversation used. If ambiguous, ask.

4. Scenario: LLM generates invalid diagram JSON
   Expected: Fallback to previous diagram state. Error: "Could not update diagram."

5. Scenario: Export SVG with embedded graph data
   Expected: SVG is standalone (no external dependencies). Paper IDs in data attributes.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001, #002, #007, #037
ENABLES: Collaborative visual thinking
ENHANCES: #122 (Handwriting), #125 (Canvas Chat)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Graph node data, SVG rendering

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] Natural language diagram generation (/diagram command)
[] SVG diagram rendered in chat block
[] Iterative modification via text instructions
[] Direct manipulation (drag nodes)
[] Export as SVG file
[] Graph data cross-referenced for real paper metadata

VISUAL: [] Clean SVG nodes, labeled edges, proper layout
PERFORMANCE: [] Diagram generation < 3s, drag 60fps
ACCESSIBILITY: [] SVG labeled, nodes focusable, tab navigation
EDGE CASES: [] Unknown papers handled, complex diagrams zoomable, invalid JSON safe

VERIFICATION:

[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
