================================================================================
FEATURE #110: INTERVIEW MODE
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #029 (Socratic Mode), #024 (Teach Me Mode)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #006 (Conversation Memory), #037 (Streaming Text), #049 (Mode Selector)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Interview Mode transforms Athena into a structured interviewer that asks the
user targeted questions about their research to help them articulate gaps,
assumptions, and opportunities. Instead of the user driving the conversation
with questions, Athena takes the lead, asking probing questions one at a time,
building on previous answers to dig deeper.

This mode serves researchers preparing for thesis defenses, qualifying exams,
paper revisions, or simply trying to stress-test their understanding of a
research lineage. The interview follows a structured progression: it begins
with broad framing questions ("What is the central question this lineage
addresses?"), narrows to methodology ("Which mutation types dominate and why?"),
probes assumptions ("You mentioned adoption is the most common mutation. What
would change if that were wrong?"), and concludes with synthesis ("Based on
our discussion, what is the most underexplored direction?").

The interview engine maintains a state machine with five phases:
FRAMING (2-3 questions), EXPLORATION (3-4 questions), PROBING (2-3 questions),
SYNTHESIS (1-2 questions), and DEBRIEF (summary). Each phase has a template
set of questions that are dynamically populated with graph data (paper titles,
mutation types, PageRank scores, cluster labels).

The LLM generates follow-up questions based on the user's answers, but it is
constrained to the current interview phase. It cannot skip ahead or loop back.
The interview progress is shown as a vertical timeline in the chat panel,
with completed phases checked off and the current phase highlighted.

The user can exit Interview Mode at any time by clicking "End Interview" or
switching modes. Upon exit, a summary card is generated showing all questions
asked, the user's key answers, and Athena's assessment of the user's
understanding depth.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User selects "Interview Mode" from the mode selector (#049) or types
/interview in the chat.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User activates Interview Mode. A banner appears: "Interview Mode Active.
   Athena will ask you questions about this research lineage."
2. Athena begins with a framing question: "Looking at this lineage rooted in
   'Attention Is All You Need,' what do you think is the single most important
   idea that propagated through the field?"
3. User responds with their analysis.
4. Athena acknowledges the answer, adds a brief observation from the graph
   data, and asks a follow-up: "Interesting. The graph shows that 'self-
   attention' was adopted by 34 papers but generalized by only 7. Why do you
   think adoption dominated over generalization?"
5. The interview progress timeline shows FRAMING complete, EXPLORATION active.
6. After 8-10 questions across all phases, Athena enters DEBRIEF: "Let me
   summarize what we covered..." A summary card appears with key insights.
7. User clicks "End Interview." Mode returns to default chat.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Summary card saved to conversation history. Interview transcript available
for export. Mode returns to default.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-modes.js: Interview mode state machine, phase progression,
  progress timeline UI.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes messages through interview mode when active.
- static/css/athena-modes.css: Interview banner, progress timeline, summary card.
- app.py: /api/athena/stream handles interview mode via system prompt switching.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class InterviewMode {
    constructor() {
      this.phases = ['FRAMING', 'EXPLORATION', 'PROBING', 'SYNTHESIS', 'DEBRIEF'];
      this.currentPhaseIndex = 0;
      this.questionsAsked = 0;
      this.questionsPerPhase = { FRAMING: 3, EXPLORATION: 4, PROBING: 3, SYNTHESIS: 2, DEBRIEF: 1 };
      this.phaseQuestionCount = 0;
      this.transcript = [];
    }
    getCurrentPhase() { return this.phases[this.currentPhaseIndex]; }
    advancePhase() {
      if (this.currentPhaseIndex < this.phases.length - 1) {
        this.currentPhaseIndex++;
        this.phaseQuestionCount = 0;
        this.updateTimeline();
      }
    }
    recordExchange(question, answer) {
      this.transcript.push({ phase: this.getCurrentPhase(), question, answer });
      this.phaseQuestionCount++;
      this.questionsAsked++;
      if (this.phaseQuestionCount >= this.questionsPerPhase[this.getCurrentPhase()]) {
        this.advancePhase();
      }
    }
    generateSummary() { /* produces summary card from transcript */ }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No new endpoints. Interview mode is handled via system prompt modification
on the existing /api/athena/stream endpoint. The system prompt includes the
current phase, phase-specific question templates, and the graph summary.

API CONTRACTS:
No new endpoints. Mode state is managed client-side.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Interview In Progress

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  [Interview Mode]                    [End]   │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────── │
  │  Progress:                                    │
  │    ● FRAMING          (2/3 questions)         │
  │    ○ EXPLORATION       ───────────            │
  │    ○ PROBING           ───────────            │
  │    ○ SYNTHESIS         ───────────            │
  │    ○ DEBRIEF           ───────────            │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────── │
  │                                               │
  │  Athena: Looking at this lineage, what do    │
  │  you think is the central question it        │
  │  addresses?                                   │
  │                                               │
  │  You: The lineage explores how attention     │
  │  mechanisms replaced recurrence...            │
  │                                               │
  │  Athena: The graph confirms that. 34 papers  │
  │  adopted self-attention while only 7          │
  │  generalized it. Why do you think...          │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
- Phase change: current phase dot fills (○ to ●), 300ms
- Banner appear: slide-down 200ms
- Summary card: fade-in 300ms on debrief

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User activates Interview Mode
  -> InterviewMode state machine initialized
  -> System prompt set to interview-specific prompt with phase context
  -> Athena generates first question using graph data
  -> User responds
  -> Response + answer recorded in transcript
  -> Phase question count incremented
  -> If phase complete, advance to next phase
  -> System prompt updated with new phase context
  -> Repeat until DEBRIEF
  -> Summary generated from transcript

ERROR PATH:
- User exits mid-interview: partial summary generated from collected transcript
- LLM fails to generate question: fallback to template question for phase
DATA STORED: Interview transcript stored in chat_history
DATA CACHED: No caching

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) for interview
questions (requires nuanced reasoning about user answers).
TEMPERATURE: 0.5
MAX TOKENS: 800

SYSTEM PROMPT (varies by phase):
FRAMING: "You are interviewing the user about this research lineage. Ask broad
questions about the field's central question, most important ideas, and overall
trajectory. Reference specific graph data when forming questions."
EXPLORATION: "Ask focused questions about specific mutation patterns, key
papers, and methodological evolution in the lineage."
PROBING: "Challenge the user's assumptions. Ask 'what if' questions about
alternative paths, contradictions, and unexamined connections."
SYNTHESIS: "Ask the user to synthesize their understanding. What is the most
important takeaway? What is the biggest gap?"
DEBRIEF: "Summarize the interview. Highlight the user's strongest insights
and areas where their understanding could deepen."

RESPONSE FORMAT: One question per turn. Brief acknowledgment of previous
answer (1-2 sentences) before new question.
CACHING: NOT cached
FALLBACK: Template questions per phase if LLM fails.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element          | Action      | Response                                      |
|------------------|-------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| End Interview    | Click       | Generates summary, exits mode                 |
| Progress dot     | Hover       | Tooltip shows phase description                |
| Mode selector    | Switch      | Exits interview, generates partial summary    |
| Chat input       | Enter       | Submits answer, triggers next question         |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Phase dot fill: scale(0.8) to scale(1) with color fill, 300ms
- Question appear: standard streaming animation
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Progress timeline has role="progressbar" with aria-valuenow.
Phase names announced on transition.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User gives one-word answers
   Expected: Athena asks a more specific follow-up to draw out detail.
   Implementation: System prompt instructs to probe deeper on short answers.

2. Scenario: User asks Athena a question during the interview
   Expected: Athena briefly answers, then redirects to the next interview
   question to maintain structure.
   Implementation: System prompt instructs to stay in interviewer role.

3. Scenario: User exits during FRAMING phase (only 1 question answered)
   Expected: Minimal summary generated: "Interview ended early. One question
   was discussed..."
   Implementation: Summary handles any transcript length.

4. Scenario: Graph has very few nodes (< 5 papers)
   Expected: Interview is shorter (fewer questions per phase). Some phases
   may be skipped.
   Implementation: questionsPerPhase adjusted based on graph size.

5. Scenario: User switches to Interview Mode from another active mode
   Expected: Previous mode deactivated cleanly. Interview starts fresh.
   Implementation: Mode switching handled by mode selector (#049).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context-Aware), #006 (Memory), #037 (Streaming), #049 (Mode Selector)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Research comprehension and thesis preparation
CONFLICTS: Other active modes (only one mode at a time)
EXTERNAL: NONE

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] Interview state machine progresses through 5 phases
[] Questions grounded in actual graph data
[] Follow-up questions adapt to user answers
[] Progress timeline shows current phase
[] End Interview generates summary card
[] Partial exit produces partial summary
[] Mode switch exits interview cleanly

VISUAL:
[] Interview banner with mode label and End button
[] Progress timeline with phase dots
[] Summary card styled as artifact card

PERFORMANCE: [] Question generation < 3s
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Progress bar labeled, phases announced
EDGE CASES: [] Short answers, user questions, early exit, small graph

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-modes.js interview section, mode selector integration 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST full interview flow, early exit, phase transitions 4. CHECK summary card content 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24

================================================================================
FEATURE #111: TUTORIAL MODE
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #024 (Teach Me Mode), #029 (Socratic Mode)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #006 (Conversation Memory), #037 (Streaming Text), #049 (Mode Selector), #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Tutorial Mode transforms Athena into a guided tour conductor that walks new
users through the Arivu interface step by step, teaching them how to read the
ancestry graph, understand mutation types, interpret the DNA profile, use
pruning, and navigate the tool's advanced features. Unlike Teach Me Mode
(which teaches about the research content), Tutorial Mode teaches about the
tool itself.

The tutorial follows a fixed curriculum of 7 lessons, each focusing on one
aspect of the Arivu interface:

Lesson 1: Reading the Graph (nodes, edges, colors, shapes, layout)
Lesson 2: Understanding Mutation Types (7 types with examples from the graph)
Lesson 3: Confidence Tiers (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/SPECULATIVE, what they mean)
Lesson 4: The DNA Profile (what the donut chart shows, cluster labels)
Lesson 5: Pruning (how to remove papers, reading the cascade result)
Lesson 6: The Leaderboard (PageRank, impact percentage, bottleneck nodes)
Lesson 7: Advanced Features (filters, search, export, chat guide)

Each lesson consists of: an explanation card, a highlight action (the relevant
UI element is highlighted with a pulsing border), a "try it" prompt (the user
is asked to interact with the highlighted element), and a confirmation check
(Athena verifies the user completed the action).

The tutorial uses the Chat-Driven Graph Navigation feature (#034) to
programmatically focus the graph view on relevant elements during each lesson.
For example, during Lesson 2, Athena highlights an edge with a "generalization"
mutation and zooms the graph to center on it.

Progress is persisted in the sessions.graph_memory JSONB field so the user can
resume the tutorial if they close the browser. The tutorial can be restarted
or skipped at any point.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User selects "Tutorial Mode" from the mode selector (#049), or Athena
proactively suggests it for first-time users.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User activates Tutorial Mode. A welcome card appears: "Welcome to Arivu!
   I will walk you through the key features in 7 short lessons. Ready?"
2. User clicks "Start Tutorial" or types "yes."
3. Lesson 1 begins. Athena explains what nodes represent (papers). The graph
   highlights a specific node with a pulsing gold border. A tooltip appears
   explaining the node's components.
4. Athena says: "Try clicking on the highlighted node to see its details."
5. User clicks the node. Athena confirms: "You clicked on 'BERT (2018).' The
   right panel now shows its metadata. Notice the citation count and mutation
   types listed below."
6. Lesson 1 completes. Progress bar advances: "1/7 complete." Athena proceeds
   to Lesson 2.
7. Lessons continue through all 7 topics. User can skip any lesson or end
   the tutorial at any point.
8. After Lesson 7, a completion card appears: "Tutorial complete! You now
   know how to use Arivu's core features."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Tutorial completion flag set in graph_memory. Tutorial Mode deactivates.
Regular chat resumes. User can re-enter Tutorial Mode anytime.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-modes.js: Tutorial mode state machine, lesson definitions,
  highlight controller, progress tracking.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes messages through tutorial mode.
- static/js/graph.js: Accepts highlight commands from tutorial system.
- static/css/athena-modes.css: Tutorial highlight pulse, progress bar, cards.
- app.py: No new endpoints. Tutorial state managed client-side with
  graph_memory persistence via existing /api/session/memory endpoint.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class TutorialMode {
    constructor(graphInstance) {
      this.graph = graphInstance;
      this.lessons = [
        { id: 'graph-reading', title: 'Reading the Graph', steps: 3 },
        { id: 'mutation-types', title: 'Mutation Types', steps: 4 },
        { id: 'confidence', title: 'Confidence Tiers', steps: 2 },
        { id: 'dna-profile', title: 'DNA Profile', steps: 2 },
        { id: 'pruning', title: 'Pruning', steps: 3 },
        { id: 'leaderboard', title: 'Leaderboard', steps: 2 },
        { id: 'advanced', title: 'Advanced Features', steps: 3 },
      ];
      this.currentLesson = 0;
      this.currentStep = 0;
    }
    startLesson(index) {
      this.currentLesson = index;
      this.currentStep = 0;
      this.highlightTarget(this.lessons[index].id);
      this.showLessonCard(this.lessons[index]);
    }
    highlightTarget(lessonId) {
      // Programmatically highlight the relevant UI element
      // Uses graph.js API to pulse-highlight nodes/edges/panels
    }
    checkCompletion(action) {
      // Verify user performed the requested action
      // Advance step on success
    }
    saveProgress() {
      // Persist to sessions.graph_memory via API
      fetch('/api/session/memory', {
        method: 'POST',
        body: JSON.stringify({ tutorial_lesson: this.currentLesson })
      });
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No new endpoints. Uses existing /api/session/memory (update_graph_memory).

API CONTRACTS:
No new endpoints.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Tutorial Lesson Active

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  [Tutorial Mode]   Lesson 2/7        [Skip]  │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────── │
  │  ● ● ○ ○ ○ ○ ○                              │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────── │
  │                                               │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │  Lesson 2: Mutation Types            │     │
  │  │                                      │     │
  │  │  Edges between papers show how ideas │     │
  │  │  changed. The 7 mutation types are:  │     │
  │  │                                      │     │
  │  │  ● adoption: Used as-is              │     │
  │  │  ● generalization: Extended scope    │     │
  │  │  ● specialization: Narrowed focus    │     │
  │  │  ● hybridization: Combined ideas     │     │
  │  │  ● contradiction: Opposed claim      │     │
  │  │  ● revival: Brought back old idea    │     │
  │  │  ● incidental: Passing mention       │     │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  │                                               │
  │  Try it: Click the highlighted edge to see   │
  │  its mutation type.                           │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
- Lesson card: slide-in from right 200ms
- Highlight pulse: 2s repeating gold/blue border pulse on target element
- Progress dots: fill animation on lesson complete

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User activates Tutorial Mode
  -> TutorialMode state machine initialized
  -> Check graph_memory for saved progress (resume if exists)
  -> Start current lesson
  -> Highlight target UI element
  -> Show lesson card with explanation
  -> User performs requested action
  -> TutorialMode.checkCompletion() verifies action
  -> Advance step/lesson
  -> Save progress to graph_memory

ERROR PATH:
- User skips lesson: progress saved, advance to next lesson
- User exits tutorial: progress saved, can resume later
- Graph not loaded: Tutorial deferred until graph ready
DATA STORED: Tutorial progress in sessions.graph_memory JSONB
DATA CACHED: Lesson content static (no caching needed)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant)
TEMPERATURE: 0.3
MAX TOKENS: 600

SYSTEM PROMPT:
"You are a tutorial guide for the Arivu research tool. You are currently on
Lesson N: [Topic]. Explain the concept clearly using the actual graph data.
After explaining, ask the user to interact with a specific element. Keep
explanations under 100 words per step. Use encouraging, friendly language."

RESPONSE FORMAT: Short explanations + action prompts. One step at a time.
CACHING: Tutorial explanations can be cached (same graph, same lesson).
FALLBACK: Pre-written lesson content if LLM unavailable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element          | Action      | Response                                      |
|------------------|-------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| Skip button      | Click       | Advances to next lesson, saves progress       |
| Restart button   | Click       | Resets to Lesson 1                            |
| Highlighted el.  | Click       | Tutorial detects action, advances step         |
| End Tutorial     | Click       | Exits mode, saves progress                    |
| Progress dots    | Click       | Jump to specific lesson                       |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Highlight pulse: box-shadow alternating gold/transparent, 2s ease-in-out
- Step advance: lesson card slides left (out), new card slides right (in)
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Highlight has aria-describedby linking to tutorial instruction.
Progress dots have aria-label for each lesson.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User clicks wrong element during "try it" step
   Expected: Athena says "Not quite. Try clicking the highlighted element."
   Implementation: checkCompletion() validates target, provides feedback.

2. Scenario: User closes browser mid-tutorial, returns later
   Expected: Tutorial resumes at last saved lesson.
   Implementation: graph_memory persistence via API.

3. Scenario: Graph changes (new paper analyzed) during tutorial
   Expected: Tutorial resets to Lesson 1 (graph context changed).
   Implementation: Listen for graph:rebuild, reset tutorial state.

4. Scenario: User activates another mode during tutorial
   Expected: Tutorial paused, progress saved. Can resume later.
   Implementation: Mode switch saves tutorial state.

5. Scenario: Graph has very few nodes (< 5), some lessons not applicable
   Expected: Lessons that need many nodes (clustering, pruning) are
   simplified or skipped with a note.
   Implementation: Lesson prerequisites checked against graph size.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context-Aware), #006 (Memory), #037 (Streaming), #049 (Mode Selector), #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: New user onboarding experience
CONFLICTS: Other active modes (only one mode at a time)
EXTERNAL: NONE

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] 7 lessons covering all major Arivu features
[] Each lesson has explanation, highlight, try-it, and confirmation
[] Progress persisted in graph_memory (survives page reload)
[] Skip and restart buttons work correctly
[] Tutorial detects user actions on highlighted elements
[] Completion card generated after Lesson 7

VISUAL:
[] Highlight pulse animation on target elements
[] Progress dots show lesson completion
[] Lesson cards styled consistently

PERFORMANCE: [] Lesson transitions < 200ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Highlights described, progress labeled, lessons navigable
EDGE CASES: [] Resume after close, wrong element click, small graph

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified

FEATURE #113: QUIZ MODE
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #029 (Socratic Mode), #110 (Interview Mode)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #006 (Conversation Memory), #037 (Streaming Text), #049 (Mode Selector)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Quiz Mode transforms Athena into an adaptive quiz master that tests the user's
understanding of the research lineage by asking multiple-choice, true/false,
and short-answer questions generated from the actual graph data. Each question
is grounded in real paper relationships, mutation types, and structural
properties visible in the ancestry graph. The quiz adapts difficulty based on
the user's performance, asking harder questions when they answer correctly and
easier questions when they struggle.

The quiz draws from five question categories:
1. FACTUAL: "Which paper introduced the concept of self-attention?" (tests
   knowledge of specific papers in the lineage)
2. STRUCTURAL: "How many papers directly adopted the method from 'Attention Is
   All You Need'?" (tests understanding of graph structure)
3. MUTATION: "The relationship between Word2Vec and GloVe is best described
   as: (a) adoption, (b) generalization, (c) specialization, (d) hybridization"
   (tests mutation type understanding)
4. COUNTERFACTUAL: "If LSTM were removed from history, approximately what
   percentage of the lineage would collapse?" (tests pruning intuition)
5. SYNTHESIS: "What is the common thread connecting the 3 most-cited papers
   in this lineage?" (tests holistic understanding)

The quiz engine generates questions by reading the graph JSON data, including
node metadata (title, year, citation_count, fields_of_study), edge metadata
(mutation_type, citation_intent, confidence_tier), precomputed_pruning data,
and DNA profile clusters. This ensures every question has a verifiable correct
answer derived from the actual data.

Questions are generated in batches of 5 and presented one at a time. After
each answer, Athena shows whether the answer was correct, explains why, and
references the specific graph data that supports the correct answer. A running
score is displayed: "4/7 correct (57%)."

The difficulty adapts via a simple ELO-like system: 3 consecutive correct
answers increase difficulty; 2 consecutive wrong answers decrease it.
Difficulty levels affect question complexity: EASY (factual, obvious answers),
MEDIUM (structural, requires graph reading), HARD (counterfactual/synthesis,
requires deep understanding).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User selects "Quiz Mode" from the mode selector (#049) or types /quiz.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User activates Quiz Mode. Athena displays: "Quiz Mode! I will test your
   knowledge of this research lineage. Let us begin."
2. First question (EASY): "True or False: 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017)
   has more citations than 'BERT' (2018) in this lineage."
3. User types "True."
4. Athena responds: "Correct! 'Attention' has 12,000 citations vs BERT's
   9,800 in Semantic Scholar data. Score: 1/1."
5. Next question (MEDIUM): "Which mutation type best describes the
   relationship between Word2Vec and GloVe? (a) adoption (b) generalization
   (c) specialization (d) hybridization"
6. User types "b."
7. Athena: "Correct! The edge shows 'generalization' with HIGH confidence.
   GloVe generalized Word2Vec's word embedding approach from local to global
   co-occurrence statistics. Score: 2/2."
8. After 10 questions, Athena shows a results card: "Quiz complete! Score:
   7/10 (70%). Strongest area: Structural questions. Weakest: Counterfactual
   reasoning."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Results card saved to chat history. User can start a new quiz or exit mode.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-modes.js: Quiz mode state machine, question generator,
  difficulty adapter, score tracker, results card.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes messages through quiz mode when active.
- static/css/athena-modes.css: Quiz answer buttons, score display, results card.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class QuizMode {
    constructor(graphData) {
      this.graphData = graphData;
      this.categories = ['FACTUAL', 'STRUCTURAL', 'MUTATION', 'COUNTERFACTUAL', 'SYNTHESIS'];
      this.difficulty = 'MEDIUM';
      this.score = 0;
      this.total = 0;
      this.streak = 0;
      this.questions = [];
      this.currentQuestion = null;
    }
    generateQuestion() {
      const category = this.categories[Math.floor(Math.random() * this.categories.length)];
      // Generate question from graphData based on category and difficulty
      // Returns { question, options, correctAnswer, explanation, category, difficulty }
    }
    checkAnswer(userAnswer) {
      this.total++;
      const correct = userAnswer === this.currentQuestion.correctAnswer;
      if (correct) {
        this.score++;
        this.streak++;
        if (this.streak >= 3) this.increaseDifficulty();
      } else {
        this.streak = Math.min(this.streak, 0) - 1;
        if (this.streak <= -2) this.decreaseDifficulty();
      }
      return { correct, explanation: this.currentQuestion.explanation };
    }
    increaseDifficulty() {
      if (this.difficulty === 'EASY') this.difficulty = 'MEDIUM';
      else if (this.difficulty === 'MEDIUM') this.difficulty = 'HARD';
      this.streak = 0;
    }
    decreaseDifficulty() {
      if (this.difficulty === 'HARD') this.difficulty = 'MEDIUM';
      else if (this.difficulty === 'MEDIUM') this.difficulty = 'EASY';
      this.streak = 0;
    }
    generateResults() {
      return {
        score: this.score,
        total: this.total,
        percentage: Math.round((this.score / this.total) * 100),
        byCategory: this.getCategoryBreakdown()
      };
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No new endpoints. Quiz questions generated client-side from graph JSON data.
LLM used only for generating explanations of correct answers and for
SYNTHESIS-type questions that require natural language generation.

API CONTRACTS:
No new endpoints. Uses existing /api/athena/stream for explanation generation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Quiz Question Display

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  [Quiz Mode]   Score: 4/6 (67%)     [End]   │
  │  Difficulty: MEDIUM                           │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────── │
  │                                               │
  │  Q7: Which mutation type describes the       │
  │  relationship between Word2Vec and GloVe?     │
  │                                               │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │  (a) adoption                        │     │
  │  │  (b) generalization                  │     │
  │  │  (c) specialization                  │     │
  │  │  (d) hybridization                   │     │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  │                                               │
  │  Type a, b, c, or d...                 [>]    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Quiz Results

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │  Quiz Complete!                      │     │
  │  │                                      │     │
  │  │  Score: 7/10 (70%)                   │     │
  │  │                                      │     │
  │  │  By Category:                        │     │
  │  │    FACTUAL:        3/3 ●●●           │     │
  │  │    STRUCTURAL:     2/3 ●●○           │     │
  │  │    MUTATION:       1/2 ●○            │     │
  │  │    COUNTERFACTUAL: 1/1 ●             │     │
  │  │    SYNTHESIS:      0/1 ○             │     │
  │  │                                      │     │
  │  │  Strongest: Factual questions        │     │
  │  │  Weakest: Synthesis questions        │     │

  │  [New Quiz]              [Exit Quiz Mode]     │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
- Correct answer: green flash on selected option, 300ms
- Wrong answer: red flash on selected, green highlight on correct, 300ms
- Results card: fade-in 300ms

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User activates Quiz Mode
  -> QuizMode initialized with graphData
  -> Question generated from graph data + difficulty level
  -> Question displayed with options
  -> User submits answer
  -> checkAnswer() evaluates, adjusts difficulty
  -> Explanation shown (LLM-generated for complex questions)
  -> Score updated
  -> Next question generated
  -> After 10 questions (or user ends): results card generated

ERROR PATH:
- Graph data insufficient for question type: skip category, try another
- LLM fails to generate explanation: use pre-written template explanation
DATA STORED: Quiz results saved to chat_history
DATA CACHED: Generated questions cached during the quiz session

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant)
TEMPERATURE: 0.3
MAX TOKENS: 400

SYSTEM PROMPT:
"Generate a quiz question about this research ancestry graph. Category:
[CATEGORY]. Difficulty: [DIFFICULTY]. Use the actual paper data provided.
The question must have exactly one correct answer derivable from the graph
data. Format: { question, options (for MC), correctAnswer, explanation }."

RESPONSE FORMAT: JSON for question generation. Prose for explanations.
CACHING: Questions cached per graph (same graph, same questions possible).
FALLBACK: Client-side question generation from graph data templates.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element          | Action      | Response                                      |
|------------------|-------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| Option button    | Click       | Submits answer, shows result                  |
| End Quiz         | Click       | Shows results card, exits after review        |
| New Quiz         | Click       | Resets score, generates fresh questions        |
| Hint button      | Click       | Eliminates 2 wrong options (MC only, -50% pts)|
| Chat input       | Type answer | Accepted for short-answer questions            |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Correct: option background flashes var(--success) 300ms
- Wrong: selected flashes var(--danger), correct flashes var(--success) 300ms
- Score update: counter animation
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Options navigable with arrow keys. Enter submits. Results
table has proper th/td structure.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Graph has < 5 nodes, insufficient for diverse questions
   Expected: Quiz uses available data, reduces question count, skips
   categories that need more nodes (COUNTERFACTUAL needs pruning data).
   Implementation: Question generation validates minimum data per category.

2. Scenario: User answers incorrectly 5+ times in a row
   Expected: Difficulty drops to EASY. Athena encourages: "These are tricky!
   Let us try some foundational questions."
   Implementation: Difficulty floor at EASY, encouragement message.

3. Scenario: Multiple-choice question with ambiguous options
   Expected: Only questions with unambiguous correct answers generated.
   Implementation: Question validation ensures single correct answer.

4. Scenario: User types free-text instead of option letter for MC question
   Expected: Fuzzy match attempted ("generalization" matches option b).
   Implementation: Compare user input against option text, not just letter.

5. Scenario: Quiz interrupted by mode switch
   Expected: Partial results saved. Score preserved if user returns to quiz.
   Implementation: Quiz state saved to session memory on mode switch.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context-Aware), #006 (Memory), #037 (Streaming), #049 (Mode Selector)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Research comprehension through active testing
CONFLICTS: Other active modes (only one mode at a time)
EXTERNAL: NONE

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] 5 question categories (FACTUAL, STRUCTURAL, MUTATION, COUNTERFACTUAL, SYNTHESIS)
[] Multiple-choice, true/false, and short-answer formats
[] Adaptive difficulty (EASY/MEDIUM/HARD) based on streak
[] Running score display updated after each answer
[] Results card with category breakdown after quiz end
[] Questions derived from actual graph data (verifiable answers)
[] Hint system (-50% points, eliminates 2 options)

VISUAL:
[] Answer options styled as clickable cards
[] Correct/wrong flash animations
[] Results card with visual score breakdown

PERFORMANCE: [] Question generation < 2s
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Options keyboard-navigable, results in table format
EDGE CASES: [] Small graph, wrong streaks, ambiguous input, mode switch

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-modes.js quiz section, question generation logic 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST all 5 categories, difficulty adaptation, hint system 4. CHECK answers verified against real graph data 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified

================================================================================
FEATURE #114: WRITING ASSISTANT MODE
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 18
OVERLAPS WITH:   #099 (Literature Review Draft Artifact), #082 (Export as Document)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #006 (Conversation Memory), #037 (Streaming Text), #049 (Mode Selector), #038 (Markdown Rendering)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Writing Assistant Mode transforms Athena into a collaborative writing partner
that helps researchers draft sections of academic papers grounded in the
ancestry graph data. The user specifies what they want to write (literature
review, related work section, methodology comparison, introduction paragraph)
and Athena generates draft text that references actual papers from the graph,
uses proper academic tone, and includes inline citations.

This mode differs from the Literature Review Draft Artifact (#099) in scope
and interaction model. The artifact generates a complete document as a one-shot
output. Writing Assistant Mode is interactive: the user writes, Athena suggests,
the user revises, Athena refines. It is a back-and-forth collaborative process
where the user maintains control over every sentence.

The writing workspace appears as a split view within the chat panel: the left
side is the chat conversation, and the right side is the document draft area.
As the user and Athena discuss, text can be "sent to draft" with a click,
building up the document incrementally. The draft area supports basic editing
(the user can modify text directly) and tracks which sentences came from the
user vs. Athena (different background shading).

The mode supports four writing contexts:
LITERATURE_REVIEW: Surveys the lineage, organized by themes from DNA clusters.
RELATED_WORK: Positions a hypothetical paper within the lineage.
METHODOLOGY: Compares methods across papers using mutation_type data.
INTRODUCTION: Frames the research problem using the lineage's trajectory.

Each context configures the LLM system prompt differently and draws from
different graph data. The literature review uses DNA cluster labels and
mutation patterns. The methodology comparison uses edge mutation types and
confidence tiers. The introduction uses the chronological trajectory and
key bottleneck papers.

The generated text includes citation placeholders in the format [Author, Year]
that can be exported as BibTeX references using the export system (#082).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User selects "Writing Assistant" from the mode selector (#049) or types
/write in the chat.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User activates Writing Assistant Mode. A context selector appears:
   "What would you like to write? (a) Literature Review, (b) Related Work,
   (c) Methodology Comparison, (d) Introduction."
2. User selects "Literature Review."
3. The split view appears: chat on left, draft area on right.
4. Athena generates the first paragraph draft: "The evolution of attention
   mechanisms in neural networks can be traced through three distinct phases.
   The foundational work by Bahdanau et al. [Bahdanau, 2014] introduced
   additive attention for sequence-to-sequence models..."
5. A "Send to Draft" button appears below the generated text. User clicks it.
   The paragraph appears in the draft area.
6. User types "Can you make the opening more specific to transformer
   architectures?"
7. Athena generates a revised version. User can send it to draft (replacing
   the first paragraph) or keep the original.
8. Process continues paragraph by paragraph until the user is satisfied.
9. User clicks "Export Draft" to download as Markdown or PDF.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Draft saved in chat history. Exportable as document. Mode deactivates on exit.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-modes.js: Writing assistant state machine, split view
  controller, draft area, citation tracker.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes messages through writing mode.
- static/css/athena-modes.css: Split view layout, draft area styles,
  attribution shading, citation highlights.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class WritingAssistantMode {
    constructor(graphData) {
      this.graphData = graphData;
      this.writingContext = null; // LITERATURE_REVIEW, RELATED_WORK, etc.
      this.draft = [];
      this.citations = new Set();
    }
    setContext(context) {
      this.writingContext = context;
      this.showSplitView();
    }
    addToDraft(text, source) {
      this.draft.push({
        text: text,
        source: source, // 'user' or 'athena'
        citations: this.extractCitations(text),
        timestamp: Date.now()
      });
      this.renderDraft();
      this.updateCitationList();
    }
    extractCitations(text) {
      const regex = /\[([A-Za-z]+(?:\s+et\s+al\.?)?,\s*\d{4})\]/g;
      const matches = [];
      let match;
      while ((match = regex.exec(text)) !== null) {
        matches.push(match[1]);
        this.citations.add(match[1]);
      }
      return matches;
    }
    exportDraft(format) {
      const fullText = this.draft.map(p => p.text).join('\n\n');
      // Trigger export via ExportGenerator
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No new endpoints. Writing mode uses /api/athena/stream with writing-specific
system prompts. Citation data derived from graph JSON paper metadata.

API CONTRACTS:
No new endpoints.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Split View Writing

  ┌─────────────────────────┬────────────────────────┐
  │  Chat                   │  Draft                 │
  │  ─────────────────────  │  ──────────────────── │
  │                         │                        │
  │  Athena: Here is a      │  [Paragraph 1]         │
  │  draft paragraph about  │  The evolution of      │
  │  the attention lineage: │  attention mechanisms   │
  │                         │  can be traced through  │
  │  "The evolution of..."  │  three distinct phases. │
  │                         │  [Bahdanau, 2014]...    │
  │  [Send to Draft]        │                        │
  │                         │  [Paragraph 2]         │
  │  You: Make it more      │  (empty, awaiting)     │
  │  specific to            │                        │
  │  transformers.          │  ──────────────────── │
  │                         │  Citations: 4          │
  │  Athena: Revised...     │  [Export Draft]        │
  └─────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
- Split view open: draft area slides in from right, 300ms
- Send to Draft: text animates from chat to draft area, 200ms
- Citation highlight: gold underline pulse on citation references

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User selects writing context
  -> System prompt configured for context type
  -> LLM generates draft paragraph using graph data + DNA clusters
  -> User reviews, sends to draft or requests revision
  -> Draft area accumulates paragraphs
  -> Citations extracted and tracked
  -> User exports draft as Markdown/PDF with citations

ERROR PATH:
- LLM generates text without proper citations: frontend extracts and
  validates citations against known papers in graph
- Draft area text exceeds viewport: scroll within draft area
DATA STORED: Draft paragraphs stored in chat_history
DATA CACHED: No caching (writing is interactive and iterative)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) for academic writing.
TEMPERATURE: 0.4
MAX TOKENS: 1500

SYSTEM PROMPT (varies by writing context):
LITERATURE_REVIEW: "Help the user write a literature review. Use papers from
the ancestry graph. Organize by themes from the DNA profile clusters. Include
[Author, Year] citations. Academic tone. One paragraph at a time."
RELATED_WORK: "Help the user write a related work section positioning their
research within this lineage. Reference mutation types to explain relationships."
METHODOLOGY: "Help the user compare methodologies across papers in the lineage.
Use mutation_type data to explain how methods evolved."
INTRODUCTION: "Help the user write an introduction framing the research problem.
Use the lineage trajectory and key bottleneck papers."

RESPONSE FORMAT: Academic prose with [Author, Year] citations.
CACHING: NOT cached (interactive writing).
FALLBACK: Simplified writing without graph-specific citations if LLM fails.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element          | Action      | Response                                      |
|------------------|-------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| Send to Draft    | Click       | Moves text from chat to draft area            |
| Draft paragraph  | Click       | Selects for editing or replacement            |
| Citation         | Click       | Highlights corresponding node in graph         |
| Export Draft     | Click       | Opens export dialog (Markdown/PDF)            |
| Context selector | Change      | Resets system prompt for new writing context   |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Send to Draft: text slides from chat to draft area, 200ms ease-out
- Citation hover: connected paper node pulses in graph
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Draft area is contenteditable with aria-label. Citations
have role="link" pointing to paper data. Split view collapses to tabs on
narrow screens.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: LLM generates citation for paper not in graph
   Expected: Citation flagged with warning: "This paper is not in the
   current lineage." User can keep or remove it.
   Implementation: Citation validation against graphData nodes.

2. Scenario: User edits draft text directly (not via chat)
   Expected: Edits preserved. Source attribution changes to "user-edited."
   Implementation: contenteditable events update draft paragraph source.

3. Scenario: Draft exceeds 3000 words
   Expected: Warning shown: "Draft is getting long. Consider exporting and
   continuing in a dedicated editor."
   Implementation: Word count tracked, warning at threshold.

4. Scenario: User switches writing context mid-draft
   Expected: Draft preserved. New context applies to new paragraphs only.
   Implementation: Context switch does not clear draft.

5. Scenario: Graph has few papers (< 5), insufficient for literature review
   Expected: Athena notes limited scope and generates a shorter, focused
   review. No fabricated citations.
   Implementation: System prompt includes paper count constraint.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context-Aware), #006 (Memory), #037 (Streaming), #049 (Mode Selector), #038 (Markdown Rendering)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Research writing workflow
CONFLICTS: Other active modes (only one mode at a time)
EXTERNAL: NONE

[] 4 writing contexts (Literature Review, Related Work, Methodology, Introduction)

[] Send to Draft button moves text to draft area
[] Citations extracted and tracked in [Author, Year] format

[] Direct editing in draft area supported
[] Source attribution (user vs. Athena) visible

VISUAL:

[] Citation highlighting (gold underline)

PERFORMANCE: [] Draft paragraph generation < 5s
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Draft area editable, citations linked, split view accessible
EDGE CASES: [] Invalid citations flagged, long draft warned, small graph handled

================================================================================
FEATURE #115: ANNOTATED SCREENSHOTS
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #080 (Image Upload), #117 (Animated Explanation)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #037 (Streaming Text), #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Annotated Screenshots allows Athena to capture the current graph view, add
visual annotations (arrows, circles, labels, highlights), and embed the
annotated image directly in the chat response. When Athena explains a complex
structural relationship like "these three papers form a bottleneck cluster,"
it can show a screenshot of the graph with those three papers circled and
arrows pointing to the bottleneck edges.

This feature uses the HTML Canvas API to render a screenshot of the current
SVG graph (via SVG-to-Canvas conversion), then draws annotation overlays on
top. The annotated canvas is converted to a PNG data URL and displayed inline
in the chat message as an image block.

The annotation types are:
CIRCLE: Draws a colored circle around a specific node (used for highlighting)
ARROW: Draws a directional arrow between two points (used for flow explanation)
LABEL: Adds a text label at a specific position (used for naming regions)
HIGHLIGHT: Fills a rectangular region with semi-transparent color (cluster)
PATH: Draws a colored path along a sequence of edges (used for lineage trace)

Annotations are generated by the LLM as structured JSON embedded in the
response. When the stream encounters an `<ANNOTATED_SCREENSHOT>` block, it
captures the current graph state, parses the annotation instructions, renders
the annotated image, and displays it inline. The LLM specifies annotations
using node paper_ids (for circles/arrows targeting specific papers) and
coordinate hints (for labels and highlights).

The screenshot captures the graph at the current zoom level and viewport
position. If Athena needs to show a different view, it first uses the
Chat-Driven Graph Navigation feature (#034) to pan/zoom to the relevant
area, then captures the screenshot.

This is a P3 feature due to the complexity of SVG-to-Canvas conversion and
the need for reliable annotation positioning across different graph layouts.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
Athena decides to include an annotated screenshot when explaining structural
relationships. User can also request: "Show me what you mean on the graph."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "Where are the bottleneck papers in this lineage?"
2. Athena identifies the bottleneck papers using PageRank and degree centrality.
3. Athena's response includes an `<ANNOTATED_SCREENSHOT>` block with
   instructions: circle the 3 bottleneck papers, add arrows showing their
   critical edges, label each with its impact percentage.
4. The chat renders: first the text explanation, then an annotated screenshot
   showing the graph with red circles around the 3 papers, gold arrows along
   critical edges, and small labels showing "31% impact" next to each.
5. The screenshot appears inline in the chat, sized to fit the message width.
6. User can click the screenshot to view it full-size in a lightbox.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Annotated screenshot saved in chat history as a data URL image. Viewable
in conversation history. Exportable with the conversation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-annotator.js: Screenshot capture, annotation renderer,
  Canvas overlay drawing, image block generation.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Handles ANNOTATED_SCREENSHOT block type.
- static/js/graph.js: Exposes SVG element and node position mapping.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Annotated image block styles, lightbox.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaAnnotator {
    constructor(graphInstance) {
      this.graph = graphInstance;
    }
    async capture(annotations) {
      const svgEl = this.graph.getSvgElement();
      const canvas = await this.svgToCanvas(svgEl);
      const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d');
      for (const ann of annotations) {
        switch (ann.type) {
          case 'circle': this.drawCircle(ctx, ann); break;
          case 'arrow': this.drawArrow(ctx, ann); break;
          case 'label': this.drawLabel(ctx, ann); break;
          case 'highlight': this.drawHighlight(ctx, ann); break;
          case 'path': this.drawPath(ctx, ann); break;
        }
      }
      return canvas.toDataURL('image/png');
    }
    drawCircle(ctx, ann) {
      const pos = this.graph.getNodePosition(ann.paper_id);
      ctx.strokeStyle = ann.color || '#EF4444';
      ctx.lineWidth = 3;
      ctx.beginPath();
      ctx.arc(pos.x, pos.y, ann.radius || 30, 0, Math.PI * 2);
      ctx.stroke();
    }
    // drawArrow, drawLabel, drawHighlight, drawPath similarly
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No backend changes. Screenshots captured and annotated entirely client-side.

API CONTRACTS:
No new endpoints. Annotation instructions come from LLM response stream.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Annotated Screenshot in Chat

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Athena: The bottleneck papers in this       │
  │  lineage are:                                 │
  │                                               │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │                                      │     │
  │  │  [Graph screenshot with:             │     │
  │  │   - Red circles around 3 papers     │     │
  │  │   - Gold arrows on critical edges   │     │
  │  │   - "31%" label near ResNet         │     │
  │  │   - "18%" label near VGGNet         │     │
  │  │   - "12%" label near AlexNet]       │     │
  │  │                                      │     │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  │  Click to enlarge                             │
  │                                               │
  │  These three papers account for 61% of the   │
  │  total lineage impact...                      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
- Screenshot capture: brief flash overlay (50ms white flash) during capture
- Image appear: fade-in 300ms
- Lightbox: scale from thumbnail to full-size, 200ms

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

LLM response includes <ANNOTATED_SCREENSHOT> block
  -> AthenaBlocks parses annotation instructions (JSON)
  -> AthenaAnnotator.capture(annotations) called
  -> SVG graph converted to Canvas
  -> Annotations drawn on Canvas overlay
  -> Canvas exported as PNG data URL
  -> Image block inserted into chat message
  -> User can click to view full-size in lightbox

ERROR PATH:
- SVG-to-Canvas fails: show text-only explanation, no screenshot
- Node position not found (paper_id invalid): skip that annotation
- Canvas too large (> 4000px): scale down to 2000px max
DATA STORED: Data URL image stored in chat_history (can be large)
DATA CACHED: Screenshots not cached (graph state changes)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant)
TEMPERATURE: 0.3
MAX TOKENS: 2000

SYSTEM PROMPT ADDITION:
"When explaining structural relationships in the graph, you can include an
annotated screenshot. Output an <ANNOTATED_SCREENSHOT> block with JSON
instructions. Annotation types: circle (paper_id, color), arrow (from_id,
to_id, color), label (paper_id, text), highlight (paper_ids, color), path
(paper_ids sequence, color)."

RESPONSE FORMAT: Prose with embedded <ANNOTATED_SCREENSHOT> JSON block.
CACHING: NOT cached.
FALLBACK: Text-only explanation if screenshot capture fails.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Screenshot image     | Click       | Opens lightbox with full-size view          |
| Lightbox             | Click bg    | Closes lightbox                             |
| Lightbox             | Esc key     | Closes lightbox                             |
| Annotated node       | Hover       | Tooltip with paper info (in lightbox only)  |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Capture flash: white overlay opacity 0 to 0.3 to 0 over 100ms
- Image fade-in: opacity 0 to 1 over 300ms
- Lightbox open: transform scale(0.5) to scale(1), 200ms ease-out
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Screenshot image has alt text describing annotations.
Lightbox has role="dialog" with aria-label.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Graph is very large (600 nodes), screenshot would be huge
   Expected: Screenshot captures only the current viewport (not entire graph).
   Implementation: Capture visible SVG viewport, not full SVG extent.

2. Scenario: Multiple annotated screenshots in one response
   Expected: Each rendered independently. Max 3 per response.
   Implementation: Cap at 3 screenshots per message.

3. Scenario: LLM outputs invalid annotation JSON
   Expected: Screenshot captured without annotations. Warning logged.
   Implementation: Try/catch around annotation parsing.

4. Scenario: User is on mobile (small viewport)
   Expected: Screenshot scaled to fit. Lightbox still available.
   Implementation: Responsive image sizing, max-width: 100%.

5. Scenario: Graph in dark mode with annotations that blend into background
   Expected: Annotations use high-contrast colors (bright red, gold, white).
   Implementation: Annotation colors are fixed high-contrast, not theme-aware.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Formatting), #037 (Streaming), #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation)
ENABLES: #117 (Animated Explanation)
ENHANCES: Complex structural explanations
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: HTML Canvas API, SVG-to-Canvas conversion

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] SVG graph captured as Canvas screenshot
[] 5 annotation types rendered (circle, arrow, label, highlight, path)
[] Annotations positioned correctly using node positions
[] Screenshot appears inline in chat message
[] Lightbox view on click
[] Max 3 screenshots per response
[] Invalid annotations handled gracefully

VISUAL:
[] High-contrast annotation colors
[] Screenshot fits message width
[] Lightbox scales smoothly

PERFORMANCE: [] Screenshot capture < 500ms for 200-node graph
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Alt text on images, lightbox labeled
EDGE CASES: [] Large graph, mobile view, invalid JSON, dark mode

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-annotator.js, SVG capture logic, annotation rendering 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST all 5 annotation types, lightbox, multiple screenshots 4. CHECK positioning accuracy 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below
