================================================================================
FEATURE #116: BEFORE/AFTER COMPARISON
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           C
ESTIMATED HOURS: 10
OVERLAPS WITH:   #014 (Comparison Mode), #118 (Diff View)
BLOCKS:          #118 (Diff View)
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #037 (Streaming Text), #105 (Click-Aware Chat)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Before/After Comparison allows Athena to present side-by-side snapshots of
the graph state before and after a significant action, such as pruning,
filtering, or adding new papers. When a user prunes a foundational paper and
the graph collapses, Athena can show "Before: 152 nodes, 203 edges" next to
"After: 105 nodes, 147 edges" with a visual diff highlighting what changed.

This feature captures graph state snapshots at key moments and renders them
as a comparison block in the chat. The comparison block is a structured
response element (like a Paper Card or Stat Dashboard) that contains:
- Two mini-graph thumbnails (before and after)
- Key stat deltas: node count change, edge count change, cluster count change
- A highlight list of the most significant changes (papers that appeared,
  disappeared, or changed prominence)

The snapshot system works by serializing the graph's visible state (node list,
edge list, filter state, pruning state) at the moment of capture. When Athena
needs to show a comparison, it requests two snapshots and renders the diff.
Snapshots are lightweight (node IDs and positions only, not full data).

The before/after comparison is triggered in three scenarios:
1. PRUNE: Before pruning vs. after cascading collapse
2. FILTER: Before filter vs. after filter applied
3. EXPLORE: Before adding BFS depth vs. after (more nodes discovered)

The comparison block uses a slider UI: the user can drag a divider left/right
to reveal more of the "before" or "after" view, similar to image comparison
widgets. The slider defaults to center (50/50 split).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
Athena automatically generates a comparison after pruning or significant graph
changes. User can also request: "Show me what changed."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User prunes "Word2Vec" from the graph. Cascading animation completes.
2. Athena automatically offers: "Here is what changed when Word2Vec was
   removed from the lineage."
3. A Before/After comparison block appears in the chat showing:
   - Left side: Original graph thumbnail (152 nodes)
   - Right side: Post-prune graph thumbnail (125 nodes)
   - Slider divider in the center
   - Stats: "-27 nodes, -34 edges, 18% collapse"
   - Key changes: "Lost: GloVe, FastText, ELMo embedding chain"
4. User drags the slider to see more of the "before" view.
5. User types "Why did the GloVe chain collapse?"
6. Athena explains using the pruning data and the comparison context.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Comparison block saved in chat history. Viewable in conversation history.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-comparison.js: Snapshot capture, diff computation, slider
  comparison block rendering, mini-graph thumbnails.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Handles COMPARISON block type.
- static/js/graph.js: Exposes snapshot API for current graph state.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Comparison block styles, slider UI.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaComparison {
    constructor(graphInstance) {
      this.graph = graphInstance;
      this.snapshots = {};
    }
    captureSnapshot(label) {
      this.snapshots[label] = {
        nodes: this.graph.nodes.map(n => ({ id: n.paper_id, title: n.title, x: n.x, y: n.y })),
        edges: this.graph.edges.map(e => ({ source: e.source.paper_id, target: e.target.paper_id })),
        nodeCount: this.graph.nodes.length,
        edgeCount: this.graph.edges.length,
        timestamp: Date.now()
      };
    }
    computeDiff(beforeLabel, afterLabel) {
      const before = this.snapshots[beforeLabel];
      const after = this.snapshots[afterLabel];
      const removedNodes = before.nodes.filter(n =>
        !after.nodes.find(a => a.id === n.id));
      const addedNodes = after.nodes.filter(n =>
        !before.nodes.find(b => b.id === n.id));
      return {
        before, after,
        removedNodes, addedNodes,
        nodeDelta: after.nodeCount - before.nodeCount,
        edgeDelta: after.edgeCount - before.edgeCount,
      };
    }
    renderComparisonBlock(diff) {
      // Renders side-by-side thumbnails with slider
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No backend changes. Snapshots captured and diffed entirely client-side.

API CONTRACTS:
No new endpoints.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Before/After Comparison Block

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Before/After: Pruning Word2Vec              │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────── │
  │  ┌──────────────────┬──────────────────┐     │
  │  │                  │                  │     │
  │  │  [Before graph]  │  [After graph]   │     │
  │  │  152 nodes       │  125 nodes       │     │
  │  │  203 edges       │  169 edges       │     │
  │  │                  │                  │     │
  │  │        <<<< [slider] >>>>           │     │
  │  └──────────────────┴──────────────────┘     │
  │                                               │
  │  Changes:                                     │
  │    -27 nodes, -34 edges (18% collapse)       │
  │    Lost: GloVe, FastText, ELMo chain         │
  │    Survived: BERT (alternative path)         │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
- Comparison block appear: fade-in 300ms
- Slider drag: real-time clip-path update (smooth)
- Stats counter: animate from 0 to final value, 500ms

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Graph state change (prune/filter)
  -> captureSnapshot('before') called BEFORE action
  -> Action executes
  -> captureSnapshot('after') called AFTER action
  -> computeDiff('before', 'after') calculates changes
  -> Comparison block rendered in chat
  -> Diff data available for subsequent chat context

ERROR PATH:
- Before snapshot missing (action happened too fast): comparison unavailable
- Graph too large for thumbnail: render node-count-only comparison
DATA STORED: Snapshots stored in memory (not persisted)
DATA CACHED: Snapshots cached per session until graph rebuild

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant)
TEMPERATURE: 0.3
MAX TOKENS: 800

SYSTEM PROMPT ADDITION:
"The user has just performed an action that changed the graph. A before/after
comparison is available. Reference the specific changes (nodes added/removed,
impact percentage) when discussing what happened."

RESPONSE FORMAT: Prose accompanying the comparison block.
CACHING: NOT cached.
FALLBACK: Text-only diff summary if comparison block rendering fails.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Slider divider       | Drag        | Reveals more before/after view              |
| Before thumbnail     | Click       | Restores graph to before state (if possible)|
| After thumbnail      | Click       | Returns to current (after) state            |
| Lost paper name      | Click       | Highlights where that paper was in graph    |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Slider: clip-path transition, immediate response (no delay)
- Stats counter: easeOut animation from 0 to value, 500ms
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Comparison block has aria-label describing the diff. Slider
has role="slider" with aria-valuemin/max. Stats read by screen reader.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: No "before" snapshot available (page just loaded)
   Expected: Comparison unavailable. Athena uses text-only description.
   Implementation: Check snapshot existence before rendering block.

2. Scenario: Before and after are identical (filter had no effect)
   Expected: Athena notes "No visible change from this filter."
   Implementation: If nodeDelta=0 and edgeDelta=0, skip comparison block.

3. Scenario: Very large diff (> 100 nodes removed)
   Expected: Top 10 most significant changes shown. "...and 90 more" note.
   Implementation: Truncate change list with count of remaining.

4. Scenario: User wants to compare states from 10 minutes ago
   Expected: Only the most recent before/after pair available. Historical
   comparisons not supported.
   Implementation: Only one snapshot pair stored at a time.

5. Scenario: Thumbnail rendering for dense graph (200+ nodes)
   Expected: Thumbnail shows node positions as dots without labels.
   Implementation: Simplified rendering for thumbnails (dots only).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context-Aware), #002 (Rich Formatting), #037 (Streaming), #105 (Click-Aware Chat)
ENABLES: #118 (Diff View)
ENHANCES: Post-prune and post-filter understanding
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: HTML Canvas API for thumbnails

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] Before snapshot captured before graph action
[] After snapshot captured after graph action
[] Diff computed correctly (added/removed nodes and edges)
[] Comparison block rendered with slider UI
[] Stats show delta values with animation
[] Key changes listed (top removed/added papers)
[] Slider drag reveals before/after smoothly

VISUAL:
[] Side-by-side thumbnails clearly distinguishable
[] Slider responsive to drag
[] Stats counter animates

PERFORMANCE: [] Snapshot capture < 100ms, diff compute < 50ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Slider labeled, stats readable, diff described
EDGE CASES: [] Missing snapshot, no change, large diff, dense graph

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-comparison.js, snapshot logic, diff computation 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST prune comparison, filter comparison, slider interaction 4. CHECK snapshot accuracy 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            P2. Snapshot-based graph diffing. Slider UI for side-by-side comparison. Triggered after prune/filter actions.


================================================================================
FEATURE #117: ANIMATED EXPLANATION
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #115 (Annotated Screenshots), #015 (Timeline Narration)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #115 (Annotated Screenshots), #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation), #037 (Streaming Text), #001 (Context-Aware Responses)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Animated Explanation allows Athena to choreograph a sequence of graph
animations that visually walk the user through a complex concept step by step.
Instead of a static annotated screenshot, Athena produces a scripted animation
sequence: "First, watch the highlighted path from Paper A to Paper B (the
adoption chain). Now observe how Paper C branches off (generalization). Finally,
notice the contradiction edge from Paper D."

Each animation step consists of: a camera movement (pan/zoom to relevant area),
a visual highlight (node glow, edge pulse, region shade), and a narration text
that appears alongside the animation. The steps play sequentially like a
slideshow with animated transitions.

The animation script is a JSON array of steps generated by the LLM:
```json
[
  { "action": "zoom", "target": "paper_id_1", "narration": "Let us start here..." },
  { "action": "highlight_edge", "from": "id_1", "to": "id_2", "narration": "This adoption..." },
  { "action": "highlight_region", "paper_ids": ["id_3", "id_4"], "narration": "This cluster..." },
  { "action": "zoom_out", "narration": "Zooming out, we see..." }
]
```

The animation plays within the graph panel (not in the chat). A narration bar
appears at the bottom of the graph showing the current step's text. Play/pause
controls allow the user to control pacing. The chat panel shows a summary after
the animation completes.

This is a P3 feature because it requires tight synchronization between the
LLM's narrative output, the graph camera system, and the D3 animation engine.
It builds on the simpler Annotated Screenshots (#115) and Chat-Driven Graph
Navigation (#034) features.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User asks a complex structural question that benefits from animated
explanation. Examples: "Walk me through how attention mechanisms evolved in
this lineage" or "Show me the path from Perceptron to Transformer."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "Walk me through how transformers evolved from RNNs."
2. Athena generates an animation script with 5 steps tracing the lineage.
3. The graph panel dims slightly. A narration bar appears at the bottom.
4. Step 1: Graph zooms to "LSTM (1997)." Narration: "The journey begins with
   LSTM, which solved the vanishing gradient problem for RNNs."
5. Step 2: Camera pans to show the edge from LSTM to Seq2Seq. The edge
   pulses gold. Narration: "Seq2Seq adopted LSTM for machine translation."
6. Step 3: Camera pans to Bahdanau Attention. Narration: "Bahdanau added
   attention to Seq2Seq, allowing the model to focus on relevant inputs."
7. Step 4: Camera zooms to "Attention Is All You Need." All RNN edges dim.
   Narration: "Vaswani et al. contradicted the RNN approach entirely,
   replacing recurrence with self-attention."
8. Step 5: Camera zooms out to show the full post-transformer cluster.
   Narration: "This contradiction spawned 47 papers that adopted or
   generalized self-attention."
9. Animation completes. Narration bar fades. Chat shows a summary card.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Summary card in chat. Graph returns to previous view. User can replay.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-animator.js: Animation script parser, step sequencer,
  camera controller, highlight manager, narration bar.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Handles ANIMATED_EXPLANATION block type.
- static/js/graph.js: Exposes camera API (zoom, pan, highlight node/edge).
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Narration bar styles, play/pause controls.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaAnimator {
    constructor(graphInstance) {
      this.graph = graphInstance;
      this.steps = [];
      this.currentStep = 0;
      this.isPlaying = false;
    }
    loadScript(steps) {
      this.steps = steps;
      this.currentStep = 0;
    }
    async play() {
      this.isPlaying = true;
      this.showNarrationBar();
      for (let i = this.currentStep; i < this.steps.length; i++) {
        if (!this.isPlaying) break;
        this.currentStep = i;
        await this.executeStep(this.steps[i]);
        await this.wait(2000); // 2s between steps
      }
      this.onComplete();
    }
    async executeStep(step) {
      this.updateNarration(step.narration);
      switch (step.action) {
        case 'zoom':
          await this.graph.zoomToNode(step.target, 800);
          break;
        case 'highlight_edge':
          await this.graph.highlightEdge(step.from, step.to, 1000);
          break;
        case 'highlight_region':
          await this.graph.highlightNodes(step.paper_ids, 1000);
          break;
        case 'zoom_out':
          await this.graph.resetZoom(800);
          break;
      }
    }
    pause() { this.isPlaying = false; }
    resume() { this.play(); }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No backend changes. Animation scripts generated by LLM and executed client-side.

API CONTRACTS:
No new endpoints. Animation scripts come from LLM response stream.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Animation Playing

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                               │
  │         [Graph view with dim overlay]         │
  │         [Highlighted node: LSTM]              │
  │         [Pulsing edge: LSTM -> Seq2Seq]       │
  │                                               │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  Step 2/5   [<] [||] [>]                │ │
  │  │  "Seq2Seq adopted LSTM for machine      │ │
  │  │   translation, establishing the encoder-│ │
  │  │   decoder paradigm."                    │ │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
- Narration bar: slide-up from bottom, 200ms
- Step transition: cross-fade narration text, 150ms
- Camera moves: smooth D3 zoom transition, 800ms
- Highlight: node glow fade-in 300ms, edge pulse 1000ms

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

LLM response includes <ANIMATED_EXPLANATION> block with JSON script
  -> AthenaBlocks parses animation script
  -> AthenaAnimator.loadScript(steps) initializes
  -> AthenaAnimator.play() begins step sequence
  -> Each step: update narration + execute graph animation
  -> 2s pause between steps
  -> On complete: narration bar fades, summary card appears in chat

ERROR PATH:
- Invalid step (paper_id not in graph): skip step, show narration only
- Graph camera API unavailable: show annotated screenshot instead
- User navigates away during animation: animation paused, can resume
DATA STORED: Animation script not persisted. Summary card in chat_history.
DATA CACHED: No caching.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) for narrative
generation (requires understanding of lineage trajectory).
TEMPERATURE: 0.4
MAX TOKENS: 2000

SYSTEM PROMPT:
"Generate an animated explanation for the user's question. Output an
<ANIMATED_EXPLANATION> block with a JSON array of steps. Each step has:
action (zoom/highlight_edge/highlight_region/zoom_out), target/from/to/
paper_ids as appropriate, and narration text (1-2 sentences). Limit to
3-7 steps. Use paper_ids from the graph data."

RESPONSE FORMAT: JSON animation script in block delimiter.
CACHING: NOT cached.
FALLBACK: Annotated screenshot (#115) if animation fails.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Play/Pause button    | Click       | Toggles animation playback                  |
| Previous step        | Click       | Goes back one step                          |
| Next step            | Click       | Advances one step                           |
| Narration bar        | Click       | Pauses animation                            |
| Esc key              | Press       | Stops animation, returns to normal view     |
| Graph interaction    | Any click   | Pauses animation                            |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Camera: D3 zoom transition, 800ms ease-in-out
- Node glow: box-shadow animation, 300ms fade-in, 300ms fade-out
- Edge pulse: stroke-dashoffset animation, 1000ms
- Narration text: opacity transition 150ms
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Narration bar text readable by screen reader (aria-live).
Step count announced. Play/pause button labeled.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Animation references paper not visible at current zoom
   Expected: Camera zooms to that paper before highlighting.
   Implementation: Each step includes zoom action if target is off-screen.

2. Scenario: User interacts with graph during animation
   Expected: Animation pauses. User can resume or exit.
   Implementation: Graph click listener pauses animator.

3. Scenario: Animation script has > 10 steps (too long)
   Expected: Capped at 7 steps. LLM instructed to keep it concise.
   Implementation: Script truncated with "...continued in summary."

4. Scenario: Two animation requests overlap
   Expected: First animation stopped, second begins.
   Implementation: loadScript() stops any running animation first.

5. Scenario: Graph layout changes mid-animation (simulation not settled)
   Expected: Node positions may shift slightly. Animation adapts.
   Implementation: getNodePosition() called per step, not pre-computed.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #115 (Annotated Screenshots), #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation), #037 (Streaming), #001 (Context-Aware)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Complex lineage explanations with visual storytelling
CONFLICTS: #015 (Timeline Narration) when both active
EXTERNAL: D3.js zoom/transition API

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[] Animation script parsed from LLM response
[] Steps execute sequentially (zoom, highlight, narration)
[] Play/pause/next/previous controls work
[] Camera movements smooth (800ms transitions)
[] Narration bar shows current step text
[] Summary card generated on completion
[] Max 7 steps enforced

VISUAL:
[] Narration bar styled at bottom of graph panel
[] Highlights visible against dark graph background
[] Step transitions smooth

PERFORMANCE: [] Step transitions < 1s each, total animation < 30s
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Narration readable, controls labeled, step count announced
EDGE CASES: [] Missing paper, user interaction pause, long script, overlap

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-animator.js, graph camera API, narration bar 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST 5-step animation, play/pause, user interrupt, missing node 4. CHECK camera sync with narration 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            P3. Choreographed graph animations with narration. Builds on #115 and #034. 3-7 steps per animation.


================================================================================
FEATURE #118: DIFF VIEW
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           C
ESTIMATED HOURS: 10
OVERLAPS WITH:   #116 (Before/After Comparison), #014 (Comparison Mode)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #116 (Before/After Comparison), #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Diff View provides a detailed, text-based difference report between two graph
states, showing exactly what changed at the node and edge level. While the
Before/After Comparison (#116) provides a visual side-by-side view, Diff View
presents the changes as a structured list: added papers, removed papers,
changed edges, new mutation classifications, and shifted PageRank scores.

The Diff View is rendered as a structured response block in the chat. It uses
a familiar diff format with green (+) lines for additions and red (-) lines
for removals, similar to code diffs. Each line references a specific paper
or edge with its relevant metadata.

The Diff View is triggered in two scenarios:
1. POST-ACTION DIFF: After a prune, filter, or graph expansion, Athena can
   show the detailed diff of what changed.
2. COMPARISON DIFF: When the user asks to compare two specific aspects of the
   graph (e.g., "What is different between the CS papers and the Biology
   papers in this lineage?").

The diff is computed from the same snapshot system used by Before/After
Comparison (#116). The difference is in presentation: #116 shows visual
thumbnails with a slider, while #118 shows a text-based itemized list.

The diff report is organized into sections:
NODES ADDED: Papers that appeared (with title, year, field)
NODES REMOVED: Papers that disappeared (with title, year, field)
EDGES CHANGED: Connections that were added or removed
STATS DELTA: Aggregate changes (total nodes, edges, PageRank shifts)
SIGNIFICANCE: Athena's assessment of why these changes matter

Each section is collapsible, and the user can expand/collapse to focus on
the most relevant changes.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User requests "What changed?" or "Show me the diff" after a graph action.
Also triggered by /diff slash command or by Athena when a significant change
occurs.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User prunes "AlexNet" from the graph. Cascade completes.
2. User types "Show me exactly what changed."
3. Athena generates a Diff View block:

   DIFF: Pruning AlexNet (2012)
   ──────────────────────────────
   - ResNet (2016) [PageRank: 0.043]
   - VGGNet (2014) [PageRank: 0.031]
   - GoogLeNet (2014) [PageRank: 0.022]
   + (no nodes added)

   Edges Removed: 34
   Most Significant: AlexNet -> VGGNet (adoption, HIGH)

   Impact: 18% of lineage collapsed.
   Why it matters: AlexNet was the gateway paper for deep CNN
   architectures. Without it, the entire depth-scaling research
   line is severed.

4. User can collapse/expand each section.
5. User types "Why did GoogLeNet collapse?" referencing the diff.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Diff block saved in chat history. Sections remain collapsible.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-comparison.js: Diff computation (extends Before/After
  Comparison), diff block rendering, collapsible sections.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Handles DIFF_VIEW block type.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Diff styles (green/red lines, collapsible sections).

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaDiffView {
    constructor(comparison) {
      this.comparison = comparison; // AthenaComparison instance
    }
    generateDiffBlock(beforeLabel, afterLabel) {
      const diff = this.comparison.computeDiff(beforeLabel, afterLabel);
      return {
        type: 'DIFF_VIEW',
        sections: [
          { title: 'Nodes Removed', items: diff.removedNodes, style: 'removed' },
          { title: 'Nodes Added', items: diff.addedNodes, style: 'added' },
          { title: 'Edges Changed', items: this.computeEdgeDiff(diff), style: 'changed' },
          { title: 'Stats Delta', items: this.computeStatsDelta(diff), style: 'neutral' },
        ],
        summary: null // filled by LLM
      };
    }
    renderDiffBlock(block) {
      const container = document.createElement('div');
      container.className = 'athena-diff-view';
      for (const section of block.sections) {
        const sec = this.renderSection(section);
        container.appendChild(sec);
      }
      return container;
    }
    renderSection(section) {
      // Collapsible section with +/- line items
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No backend changes. Diff computed and rendered client-side.

API CONTRACTS:
No new endpoints.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Diff View Block

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DIFF: Pruning AlexNet (2012)                │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────── │
  │                                               │
  │  [-] Nodes Removed (12)           [collapse]  │
  │    - ResNet (2016)         CS, PageRank 0.043 │
  │    - VGGNet (2014)         CS, PageRank 0.031 │
  │    - GoogLeNet (2014)      CS, PageRank 0.022 │
  │    - ... 9 more                               │
  │                                               │
  │  [+] Nodes Added (0)             [collapse]   │
  │    (none)                                     │
  │                                               │
  │  [~] Edges Changed (34 removed)  [collapse]   │
  │    - AlexNet -> VGGNet (adoption, HIGH)       │
  │    - AlexNet -> GoogLeNet (adoption, HIGH)    │
  │    - ... 32 more                              │
  │                                               │
  │  Stats: -12 nodes, -34 edges, 18% collapse   │
  │                                               │
  │  Why it matters: AlexNet was the gateway...   │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
- Diff block appear: fade-in 300ms
- Section collapse: height animation 200ms
- Line items: slight slide-in stagger (20ms per item)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User requests diff or graph action triggers diff
  -> AthenaComparison.computeDiff() returns change data
  -> AthenaDiffView.generateDiffBlock() structures sections
  -> LLM generates significance/summary for the diff
  -> Diff block rendered in chat with collapsible sections

ERROR PATH:
- No snapshots available: text-only diff summary from current graph state
- Diff too large (> 100 items per section): truncated with "... N more"
DATA STORED: Diff block stored in chat_history
DATA CACHED: No caching.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant)
TEMPERATURE: 0.3
MAX TOKENS: 600

SYSTEM PROMPT ADDITION:
"A diff view is being generated showing changes in the research ancestry graph.
Generate a 2-3 sentence 'significance' summary explaining why these changes
matter to the research lineage. Reference the most important removed or added
papers and their structural roles."

RESPONSE FORMAT: Short prose for significance section.
CACHING: NOT cached.
FALLBACK: No significance section if LLM unavailable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Section header       | Click       | Toggle collapse/expand                      |
| Paper name in diff   | Click       | Highlights node in graph (if still present) |
| "N more" link        | Click       | Expands to show all items                   |
| Copy diff button     | Click       | Copies diff as plain text to clipboard      |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Collapse: max-height transition 200ms
- Line stagger: translateX(-5px) to 0, 20ms delay per item
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Sections have role="region" with aria-expanded. Lines have
proper list semantics. Color coding supplemented with +/- text prefixes.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Diff has 0 changes (filter had no effect)
   Expected: "No changes detected." Single-line block.
   Implementation: Check total delta, show empty state.

2. Scenario: Diff has > 200 changes (large prune)
   Expected: Sections show top 10 items each with "... N more" expandable.
   Implementation: Truncation with expand button.

3. Scenario: User requests diff without prior action
   Expected: "No graph changes to compare. Try pruning a paper or applying
   a filter first."
   Implementation: Check snapshot availability.

4. Scenario: Edge diff includes changed mutation types (re-classified edge)
   Expected: Shown as "~ edge: old_type -> new_type" with neutral styling.
   Implementation: Edge diff compares by source+target, detects type changes.

5. Scenario: Diff generated during streaming response
   Expected: Diff block renders atomically after computation completes.
   Implementation: Block buffered in stream handler, rendered on close tag.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Detailed understanding of graph changes
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: NONE

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[] Diff computed from before/after snapshots
[] Four sections: nodes removed, nodes added, edges changed, stats delta
[] Collapsible sections with expand/collapse toggle
[] Truncation with "... N more" for large diffs
[] Significance summary generated by LLM
[] Copy diff as plain text
[] Paper click highlights in graph

[] Collapsible section animation
[] Line item stagger animation

PERFORMANCE: [] Diff computation < 100ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [] Sections labeled, color not sole indicator, list semantics
EDGE CASES: [] Zero changes, large diff, no snapshots, type changes

================================================================================
FEATURE #119: SHARE CHAT SNAPSHOT
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Export & Sharing
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           G
ESTIMATED HOURS: 12
OVERLAPS WITH:   #053 (Share Individual Messages), #082 (Export as Document)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #006 (Conversation Memory), #037 (Streaming Text), #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #038 (Markdown Rendering)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Share Chat Snapshot captures a selected range of messages from the Athena
conversation and generates a shareable, self-contained HTML or image snapshot.
Unlike Share Individual Messages (#053) which shares a single message, Chat
Snapshot captures a contiguous thread of messages (e.g., a 5-message exchange
about pruning results) and packages them as a visually polished artifact that
can be shared via URL, downloaded as PNG, or copied as formatted text.

The snapshot preserves the full visual fidelity of the chat: message styling,
block elements (Paper Cards, Stat Dashboards, comparison blocks), user and
assistant message formatting, and inline citations. The output looks exactly
like the chat conversation but in a standalone format.

The sharing flow works in three steps:
1. SELECT: User enters selection mode and highlights a range of messages by
   clicking the first and last message. The selected range is highlighted with
   a blue border.
2. PREVIEW: A preview modal shows the snapshot as it will appear when shared.
   The user can adjust the range, toggle whether to include user messages
   (privacy option), and choose the export format.
3. SHARE: The snapshot is either (a) uploaded to R2 and a shareable URL
   generated, (b) exported as a PNG image, or (c) copied as rich text.

The shareable URL points to a lightweight read-only page that renders the
snapshot without requiring authentication or an active Arivu session. The
page includes the Arivu branding, a "Generated by Arivu" footer, and a link
to try Arivu. URLs expire after 30 days.

The snapshot includes metadata: which graph was being discussed (title, seed
paper), the date range of the conversation, and how many total messages were
in the full conversation (even though only a subset is shared).

Privacy controls: the user can choose to exclude their own messages (showing
only Athena's responses) or anonymize their messages (replacing their name
with "Researcher"). Athena's responses are always included since they are
AI-generated.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User clicks the "Share" icon in the chat toolbar, or selects messages and
chooses "Share Selection" from context menu.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User has a 10-message conversation about transformer lineage. They want
   to share messages 3-7 (the pruning discussion) with a colleague.
2. User clicks the "Share" icon in the chat toolbar. A "Select messages to
   share" prompt appears.
3. User clicks message 3 (start of range) then Shift+clicks message 7 (end).
   Messages 3-7 are highlighted with a blue border.
4. A preview modal appears showing the 5 selected messages exactly as they
   look in the chat. Options panel on the right:
   - [x] Include my messages
   - [ ] Anonymize my name
   - Format: [URL] [PNG] [Rich Text]
5. User selects URL format. Clicks "Generate Link."
6. Snapshot HTML uploaded to R2. A shareable URL is generated and copied to
   clipboard: "https://arivu.app/share/snap_abc123"
7. User pastes the link to their colleague. Colleague opens it and sees the
   conversation snippet in a clean, branded read-only view.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Link copied to clipboard. Toast confirmation: "Snapshot link copied!" Link
valid for 30 days.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-share.js: Message selection, snapshot capture, preview
  modal, format conversion, URL generation.
- static/css/athena-share.css: Selection highlight, preview modal, shared
  page styles.
- app.py: POST /api/athena/share-snapshot endpoint that uploads to R2 and
  returns URL. GET /share/<snapshot_id> serves the read-only page.
- templates/shared_snapshot.html: Read-only snapshot viewer page.
- backend/r2_client.py: Upload snapshot HTML to R2 (uses existing client).

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaChatShare {
    constructor() {
      this.selectedMessages = [];
      this.selectionMode = false;
    }
    enterSelectionMode() {
      this.selectionMode = true;
      this.selectedMessages = [];
      // Add click handlers to message elements
    }
    selectRange(startIndex, endIndex) {
      this.selectedMessages = this.getMessages(startIndex, endIndex);
      this.highlightSelection();
    }
    async generateSnapshot(options) {
      const html = this.renderSnapshotHTML(this.selectedMessages, options);
      if (options.format === 'url') {
        const resp = await fetch('/api/athena/share-snapshot', {
          method: 'POST',
          headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
          body: JSON.stringify({
            html: html,
            session_id: this.sessionId,
            message_count: this.selectedMessages.length,
            graph_title: this.graphTitle
          })
        });
        const data = await resp.json();
        await navigator.clipboard.writeText(data.share_url);
        return data.share_url;
      }
      if (options.format === 'png') {
        return this.htmlToPng(html);
      }
      if (options.format === 'richtext') {
        await navigator.clipboard.write([
          new ClipboardItem({ 'text/html': new Blob([html], { type: 'text/html' }) })
        ]);
      }
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
```python
@app.route('/api/athena/share-snapshot', methods=['POST'])
def share_snapshot():
    data = request.json
    snapshot_id = generate_snapshot_id()
    html_content = data['html']
    # Sanitize HTML (prevent XSS in shared snapshots)
    sanitized = sanitize_snapshot_html(html_content)
    # Upload to R2
    r2_key = f"snapshots/{snapshot_id}.html"
    r2_client.upload(r2_key, sanitized, content_type='text/html')
    share_url = f"{config.API_BASE_URL}/share/{snapshot_id}"
    # Store metadata
    db.execute(
        '''INSERT INTO action_log (session_id, action_type, action_data)
           VALUES (%s, 'share_snapshot', %s)''',
        (data['session_id'], json.dumps({
            'snapshot_id': snapshot_id,
            'message_count': data['message_count'],
            'graph_title': data.get('graph_title', ''),
            'expires_at': (datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(days=30)).isoformat()
        }))
    )
    return jsonify({"share_url": share_url, "snapshot_id": snapshot_id})

@app.route('/share/<snapshot_id>')
def view_shared_snapshot(snapshot_id):
    # Fetch from R2 and render in template
    html_content = r2_client.download(f"snapshots/{snapshot_id}.html")
    if not html_content:
        return render_template('404.html'), 404
    return render_template('shared_snapshot.html', content=html_content)
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/share-snapshot
  Body: { html: string, session_id: string, message_count: int, graph_title: string }
  Response: { share_url: string, snapshot_id: string }
  Max HTML size: 500KB

GET /share/<snapshot_id>
  Response: HTML page with snapshot content
  404 if snapshot not found or expired

================================================================================
FEATURE #120: COMMENT THREAD ON INSIGHT
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Conversation Mgmt
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #010 (Conversation Threads), #052 (Reactions/Bookmarks)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #006 (Conversation Memory), #037 (Streaming Text), #011 (Artifact Cards)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Comment Thread on Insight allows users to start a focused sub-conversation
on any specific insight, analysis, or claim that Athena generates. Instead of
the main chat continuing linearly, the user can "thread" a specific paragraph
or block from Athena's response, creating a nested conversation that discusses
that particular point in depth.

This mirrors how threaded discussions work in Slack or Google Docs comments.
When Athena generates a response with multiple insights (e.g., "Paper A is
a bottleneck. Cluster B is declining. The pruning result suggests..."), the
user might want to dive deeper into just one of those points. Without
threading, they would have to quote the specific insight and hope Athena
maintains context. With threading, they can attach a comment directly to that
insight, creating a focused sub-discussion.

The threading mechanism works at the block level. Each paragraph or structured
block in Athena's response is a threadable unit. A small thread icon appears
on hover next to each block. Clicking it opens a thread panel (similar to
Slack's thread sidebar) where the user can ask follow-up questions specific to
that block. The thread has its own conversation memory, separate from the main
chat, but inherits the parent message's context.

Thread context is powerful: when a user threads on a paragraph that says
"ResNet's PageRank of 0.043 makes it the highest-impact node," the thread
context includes that specific claim, the paper data, and the graph state at
the time of generation. The user can then ask "Is 0.043 typical for
bottleneck papers?" and Athena answers within the narrow context of that claim.

Threads are stored in the chat_history table as nested entries (parent_message_id
foreign key). Each thread has a maximum depth of 1 (no threads on threads).
Threads are displayed inline below their parent message when expanded, and
collapse to a "N replies" badge when collapsed.

This is a P3 feature because it requires significant UI complexity (thread
panel, inline expansion, nested message storage) and depends on multiple
foundational features being complete.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User hovers over a paragraph or block in Athena's response and clicks the
thread icon that appears.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. Athena generates a response with three paragraphs about the transformer
   lineage. Each paragraph has a subtle thread icon on the right margin.
2. User hovers over the second paragraph ("The adoption rate of self-attention
   is 2.3x higher than any previous mechanism..."). The thread icon highlights.
3. User clicks the thread icon. A thread panel slides in from the right
   (overlaying the graph panel partially).
4. The thread panel shows the quoted paragraph at the top (dimmed, for context)
   and an input area below.
5. User types: "What was the second highest adoption rate?"
6. Athena responds within the thread, focused on adoption rates, referencing
   the specific claim from the parent paragraph.
7. Thread conversation continues (3-4 exchanges).
8. User closes the thread panel. A "2 replies" badge appears below the
   original paragraph in the main chat.
9. User can click the badge to reopen the thread at any time.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Thread collapsed in main chat with reply count badge. Expandable. Thread
conversation persisted in chat_history with parent_message_id reference.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-threads.js: Thread creation, panel management, nested
  message handling, reply count badge.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes thread messages with parent context.
- static/css/athena-threads.css: Thread panel, inline thread badges, quoted
  block styling.
- app.py: /api/athena/stream accepts optional parent_message_id for thread
  context. /api/athena/thread/<message_id> returns thread messages.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaThreads {
    constructor() {
      this.activeThread = null;
      this.threadPanel = null;
    }
    openThread(messageId, blockIndex, blockContent) {
      this.activeThread = {
        parentMessageId: messageId,
        blockIndex: blockIndex,
        blockContent: blockContent,
        replies: []
      };
      this.showThreadPanel();
      this.loadExistingReplies(messageId, blockIndex);
    }
    async loadExistingReplies(messageId, blockIndex) {
      const resp = await fetch(
        `/api/athena/thread/${messageId}?block=${blockIndex}`
      );
      const data = await resp.json();
      this.activeThread.replies = data.replies;
      this.renderReplies();
    }
    async sendReply(message) {
      // Send message with parent context to stream endpoint
      const context = `[THREAD] User is commenting on this specific insight: `
        + `"${this.activeThread.blockContent}". Respond within this narrow context.`;
      // Route through AthenaEngine with thread context
    }
    closeThread() {
      this.updateReplyBadge();
      this.hideThreadPanel();
      this.activeThread = null;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
```python
@app.route('/api/athena/thread/<message_id>')
def get_thread(message_id):
    block_index = request.args.get('block', 0, type=int)
    replies = db.fetchall(
        '''SELECT * FROM chat_history
           WHERE parent_message_id = %s
           AND block_index = %s
           ORDER BY created_at ASC''',
        (message_id, block_index)
    )
    return jsonify({"replies": replies})
```

Note: The chat_history table requires two new columns for threading:
parent_message_id TEXT (nullable, references chat_history), block_index INT
(default 0). These would be added via ALTER TABLE in the appropriate migration.

API CONTRACTS:
GET /api/athena/thread/<message_id>
  Query params: block (int, default 0)
  Response: { replies: [{ role, content, created_at }] }

POST /api/athena/stream (extended)
  Additional query param: parent_message_id (optional), block_index (optional)
  When present, thread context is prepended to system prompt.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

- Reply badge: fade-in 200ms after thread closes
- Thread icon: opacity 0 to 1 on hover, 150ms

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User clicks thread icon on block
  -> AthenaThreads.openThread(messageId, blockIndex, content)
  -> Thread panel slides in
  -> Existing replies loaded from /api/athena/thread/<id>
  -> User types reply
  -> Reply sent to /api/athena/stream with parent_message_id context
  -> LLM generates thread-scoped response
  -> Reply stored in chat_history with parent_message_id
  -> Thread panel shows new reply
  -> On close: reply count badge updated

ERROR PATH:
- Thread panel fails to load: error message, main chat unaffected
- No existing replies: empty thread, ready for first comment
DATA STORED: Thread replies in chat_history with parent_message_id
DATA CACHED: Thread replies cached during panel open

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (llama-3.1-8b-instant)
TEMPERATURE: 0.4
MAX TOKENS: 800

SYSTEM PROMPT ADDITION:
"The user is commenting on a specific insight from your previous response.
The insight is: '[quoted block content]'. Keep your responses narrowly focused
on this specific point. Do not bring in unrelated topics from the main
conversation. If the user's question requires broader context, briefly note
the connection but stay focused on the threaded topic."

RESPONSE FORMAT: Focused prose addressing the specific insight.
CACHING: NOT cached.
FALLBACK: If thread context unavailable, respond normally.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Thread icon          | Hover       | Icon appears (150ms fade-in)                |
| Thread icon          | Click       | Opens thread panel                          |
| Reply badge          | Click       | Reopens thread panel with existing replies  |
| Thread panel close   | Click X     | Closes panel, shows badge                   |
| Thread panel         | Esc key     | Closes panel                                |
| Thread reply input   | Enter       | Sends reply in thread context               |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Panel slide-in: translateX(100%) to 0, 250ms ease-out
- Panel slide-out: translateX(0) to translateX(100%), 200ms
- Badge appear: scale(0) to scale(1), 200ms bounce
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE
ACCESSIBILITY: Thread panel has role="complementary" with aria-label.
Focus trapped in panel when open. Reply badge has aria-label with count.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User tries to thread on a thread reply (nested threading)
   Expected: Not allowed. Thread depth is 1. Thread icons do not appear on
   reply messages.
   Implementation: Block-level thread icons only on main chat messages.

2. Scenario: User opens thread, parent message scrolls off-screen
   Expected: Thread panel shows quoted block at top for context.
   Implementation: Quoted block always visible in thread panel header.

3. Scenario: Thread has > 20 replies
   Expected: Older replies collapsed with "Show earlier" button.
   Implementation: Pagination within thread panel.

4. Scenario: User opens multiple threads simultaneously
   Expected: Only one thread panel open at a time. Opening a new one closes
   the previous.
   Implementation: openThread() calls closeThread() if one is active.

5. Scenario: Original message deleted or edited after thread started
   Expected: Thread persists. Quoted block shows "[Original content may have
   changed]" if mismatch detected.
   Implementation: Compare stored block content on thread open.

FEATURE #141: TABLE BUILDER (INTERACTIVE)
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           F
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #146 (Pivot Table in Chat), #100 (Presentation Slides)
BLOCKS:          #146 (Pivot Table in Chat)
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
                 #011 (Artifact Cards)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Table Builder gives Athena the ability to generate fully interactive,
editable tables inside the chat panel. When a user asks a question whose answer
is naturally tabular ("compare these five papers by citation count, year, and
mutation type") Athena renders the result as a structured table rather than
a paragraph of text. The table is not a static image or markdown blob. It is
a live HTML table with sortable columns, editable cells, and row selection.

This feature exists because research data is inherently relational. A user
exploring the attention mechanism lineage frequently needs to compare papers
across multiple dimensions simultaneously: year, citation count, PageRank
centrality, dominant mutation type, cluster membership, and pruning impact.
Paragraphs bury these comparisons. Tables surface them.

The table builder operates in two modes. In auto mode, Athena detects when a
response is tabular (comparison queries, list queries, statistical summaries)
and renders the table block automatically via athena-blocks.js. In manual mode,
the user types /table or clicks the table icon in the input toolbar to request
an explicit table. In both modes, the backend returns structured JSON that the
frontend renders as an interactive table.

Each table has a toolbar row above it: [Sort] [Filter] [Export CSV] [Add Column]
[Add Row]. Sorting is client-side (click column header to toggle asc/desc).
Filtering shows a text input per column. Export CSV downloads the table
contents as a .csv file. Add Column prompts for a column name and optional
formula (e.g., "citation_count / year_span"). Add Row inserts a blank row for
manual data entry.

What makes this unique to Arivu: tables can reference live graph data. A column
can be bound to a graph metric. "PageRank" as a column header pulls the
PageRank centrality score for each paper row directly from the graph engine
state. "Pruning Impact %" pulls the precomputed pruning percentage. "Cluster"
pulls the DNA cluster assignment. The table is not a dead snapshot; it is a
live view into the graph's computed state.

Editable cells allow the user to annotate. They can add a "Notes" column and
type observations per paper. These annotations are stored in session memory
and persist across the session. If the user exports the table as CSV, their
annotations are included.

The backend produces table JSON in a canonical format:

  {
    "type": "table",
    "title": "Comparison: Top 5 Papers by Structural Impact",
    "columns": [
      { "key": "title", "label": "Paper", "sortable": true, "type": "text" },
      { "key": "year", "label": "Year", "sortable": true, "type": "number" },
      { "key": "pagerank", "label": "PageRank", "sortable": true, "type": "number" },
      { "key": "pruning_pct", "label": "Pruning %", "sortable": true, "type": "number" },
      { "key": "mutation", "label": "Dominant Mutation", "sortable": true, "type": "text" }
    ],
    "rows": [
      { "title": "Attention Is All You Need", "year": 2017, "pagerank": 0.087, "pruning_pct": 31.0, "mutation": "generalization" },
      ...
    ]
  }

The frontend table renderer (in athena-blocks.js) reads this JSON and builds
the interactive table DOM. Each cell is wrapped in a contenteditable span for
inline editing. Column headers are clickable for sorting. The table respects
the Arivu dark theme with alternating row colors using --bg-surface and
--bg-elevated.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User asks a comparison or list question: "Compare the top 5 papers by citation
count and pruning impact" or "List all papers in the attention cluster with
their mutation types." Alternatively, user types /table to request explicit
table mode. Also triggered by clicking the table icon in the input toolbar.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types: "Compare the 5 most-cited papers in this lineage by year,
   citations, PageRank, and cluster assignment."

2. Athena detects tabular intent. A thinking indicator appears: "Building
   comparison table..." The backend queries the graph engine for the top 5
   papers by citation_count, retrieves their computed PageRank scores, cluster
   assignments from dna_json, and pruning impacts from precomputed_pruning.

3. Athena renders the table block in the chat:

   ┌─ Comparison: Top 5 Most-Cited Papers ────────────────────────────┐
   │ [Sort] [Filter] [Export CSV] [Add Column] [Add Row]              │
   ├─────────────────────────┬──────┬───────┬──────────┬─────────────┤
   │ Paper                   │ Year │ Cites │ PageRank │ Cluster     │
   ├─────────────────────────┼──────┼───────┼──────────┼─────────────┤
   │ Attention Is All You... │ 2017 │ 98201 │ 0.087    │ Transformer │
   │ BERT                    │ 2018 │ 75443 │ 0.063    │ Pre-train   │
   │ ResNet                  │ 2016 │ 62187 │ 0.055    │ CNN Depth   │
   │ Word2Vec                │ 2013 │ 41892 │ 0.049    │ Embedding   │
   │ AlexNet                 │ 2012 │ 38104 │ 0.041    │ CNN Origin  │
   └─────────────────────────┴──────┴───────┴──────────┴─────────────┘

4. User clicks the "Year" column header. Rows re-sort ascending by year
   (AlexNet first). Click again for descending.

5. User clicks "Add Column." A dialog: "Column name: [Notes]". User adds a
   Notes column and types observations per row: "Foundational" for AlexNet,
   "Key breakthrough" for Attention.

6. User clicks "Export CSV." Browser downloads a CSV file containing all rows
   and columns including the Notes column.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Table persists in chat history as a structured block. If the user scrolls up,
the table is fully interactive again. Table state (sort order, added columns,
cell edits) is stored in session memory for the duration of the session.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Table block renderer. Handles column sorting,
  inline cell editing, row/column addition, CSV export, filter inputs.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Detects tabular intent in LLM responses.
  Routes table JSON to athena-blocks.js renderTable() method.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Table styles (dark theme, alternating rows,
  sortable header indicators, editable cell highlight).
- app.py: /api/athena/chat response includes table JSON when appropriate.
- backend/graph_engine.py: Method to extract tabular data from graph state
  (top-N papers by metric, comparison data).

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
Table renderer in athena-blocks.js:

  class AthenaTableBlock {
    constructor(tableData) {
      this.columns = tableData.columns;
      this.rows = tableData.rows;
      this.sortColumn = null;
      this.sortDirection = 'asc';
      this.addedColumns = [];
      this.cellEdits = {};
    }

    render() {
      const container = document.createElement('div');
      container.className = 'athena-table-block';
      container.innerHTML = this.buildToolbar() + this.buildTable();
      this.attachSortHandlers(container);
      this.attachEditHandlers(container);
      return container;
    }

    sortBy(columnKey) {
      if (this.sortColumn === columnKey) {
        this.sortDirection = this.sortDirection === 'asc' ? 'desc' : 'asc';
      } else {
        this.sortColumn = columnKey;
        this.sortDirection = 'asc';
      }
      this.rows.sort((a, b) => {
        const va = a[columnKey], vb = b[columnKey];
        const cmp = typeof va === 'number' ? va - vb : String(va).localeCompare(String(vb));
        return this.sortDirection === 'asc' ? cmp : -cmp;
      });
      this.reRender();
    }

    exportCSV() {
      const headers = this.columns.map(c => c.label).join(',');
      const rows = this.rows.map(r =>
        this.columns.map(c => JSON.stringify(String(r[c.key] || ''))).join(',')
      ).join('\n');
      const blob = new Blob([headers + '\n' + rows], { type: 'text/csv' });
      const a = document.createElement('a');
      a.href = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
      a.download = 'athena-table.csv';
      a.click();
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
Graph engine exposes a method for table extraction:

  def extract_table_data(self, metric='citation_count', top_n=10, columns=None):
      papers = sorted(self.papers.values(), key=lambda p: getattr(p, metric, 0), reverse=True)[:top_n]
      if columns is None:
          columns = ['title', 'year', 'citation_count']
      rows = []
      for p in papers:
          row = {}
          for col in columns:
              if col == 'pagerank':
                  row[col] = round(self.pagerank_scores.get(p.paper_id, 0), 4)
              elif col == 'pruning_pct':
                  pruning = self.precomputed_pruning.get(p.paper_id, {})
                  row[col] = pruning.get('impact_percentage', 0)
              elif col == 'cluster':
                  row[col] = self.dna_clusters.get(p.paper_id, 'Unassigned')
              else:
                  row[col] = getattr(p, col, '')
          rows.append(row)
      return rows

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response (when tabular):
{
  "response": "Here is the comparison table:",
  "blocks": [
    {
      "type": "table",
      "title": "...",
      "columns": [...],
      "rows": [...]
    }
  ]
}

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Table Rendered

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                        │
  │  Here is the comparison you requested:                           │
  │                                                                   │
  │  ┌─ Top 5 by Citations ──────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ [Sort] [Filter] [Export CSV] [+Col] [+Row]                │   │
  │  ├──────────────────┬──────┬───────┬──────────┬─────────────┤   │
  │  │ Paper         ▼  │ Year │ Cites │ PageRank │ Mutation    │   │
  │  ├──────────────────┼──────┼───────┼──────────┼─────────────┤   │
  │  │ Attention...     │ 2017 │ 98201 │  0.087   │ general.    │   │
  │  │ BERT             │ 2018 │ 75443 │  0.063   │ special.    │   │
  │  │ ResNet           │ 2016 │ 62187 │  0.055   │ adoption    │   │
  │  │ (alternating bg) │      │       │          │             │   │
  │  └──────────────────┴──────┴───────┴──────────┴─────────────┘   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Cell Editing

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ │ Attention...     │ 2017 │ 98201 │  0.087   │ [editing█] │ │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  Editing cell has gold border (1px solid --accent-gold), slightly
  elevated background (--bg-elevated).

TRANSITIONS:
Sort click: rows rearrange with 200ms translateY animation.
Column add: new column slides in from right (200ms).
Cell edit start: cell border transitions to gold (100ms).
Filter show: input row slides down from header (150ms).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User asks comparison question
  -> athena-engine.js sends to /api/athena/chat
  -> Backend detects tabular intent via LLM classification
  -> Backend queries graph_engine for requested metrics
  -> LLM formats column selection, title, and explanatory text
  -> Response JSON includes blocks[] with type:"table"
  -> Frontend athena-blocks.js renders AthenaTableBlock
  -> Table is interactive (sort, edit, export, filter)

User sorts column
  -> Client-side sort on rows array
  -> DOM rows rearranged with animation
  -> No server call

User edits cell
  -> contenteditable span updates
  -> cellEdits map tracks changes
  -> Changes stored in session memory (athena-memory.js)
  -> No server call (local annotations only)

User exports CSV
  -> Client-side Blob creation from current table state
  -> Download triggered via anchor element
  -> No server call

ERROR PATH:
  -> Graph data unavailable: "Could not retrieve graph metrics. Showing text
     comparison instead." Falls back to paragraph format.
  -> Table too large (>50 rows): Paginate with "Show more" button.

DATA STORED: Table JSON in chat_history (blocks field). Cell edits in session memory.
DATA CACHED: Table state (sort, edits) in AthenaTableBlock instance per message.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

STAGE: Response generation (when tabular intent detected)

PROMPT:
```
You are Athena, the research intelligence assistant for Arivu.
The user asked a question that requires a tabular comparison.
Graph context: {graph_summary}
Available columns: title, year, citation_count, pagerank, pruning_pct,
  cluster, mutation_type, text_tier, fields_of_study

User query: {user_message}

Return a JSON object with:
- "title": short table title (under 60 chars)
- "columns": array of column keys from the available list above
- "sort_by": which column to sort by initially
- "top_n": how many rows (default 10, max 25)
- "explanation": one sentence explaining the table

Return ONLY valid JSON. No markdown. No extra text.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON object defining table structure
FALLBACK: If LLM unavailable, use default columns [title, year, citation_count]
TOKEN BUDGET: ~200 tokens per table request

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Column header        | Click       | Sort rows by that column (toggle asc/desc)  |
| Column header        | Hover       | Subtle highlight, sort arrow indicator       |
| Table cell           | Double-click| Enter edit mode (contenteditable)            |
| Table cell           | Blur        | Save edit to cellEdits map                   |
| Sort button          | Click       | Opens column sort dropdown                   |
| Filter button        | Click       | Shows filter input row below headers         |
| Export CSV           | Click       | Downloads current table as .csv              |
| Add Column           | Click       | Dialog for column name and optional formula  |
| Add Row              | Click       | Appends blank row to table                   |
| Filter input         | Type        | Live-filters rows (case-insensitive match)   |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Sort: rows translateY from old position to new (200ms ease-out)
- Column add: new column width grows from 0 to auto (200ms)
- Cell edit: border color transition to --accent-gold (100ms)
- Filter row: height grows from 0, opacity 0 to 1 (150ms)

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE: Arivu does not use sound effects.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Table uses role="grid" with role="columnheader" and role="gridcell"
- Sort state announced: "Sorted by Year, ascending"
- Editable cells have aria-label="Editable: [column name]"
- Filter inputs labeled per column
- Keyboard: Tab moves between cells, Enter starts edit, Escape cancels edit
- Screen reader: "Table: Top 5 by Citations. 5 rows, 5 columns. Sorted by
  Citations descending."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User asks for a table with 100+ papers
   Expected: Table paginates at 25 rows. "Showing 25 of 102. [Show next 25]"
   button at bottom. Each page loads instantly (client-side pagination from
   full dataset).
   Implementation: AthenaTableBlock.pageSize = 25. Render slice.

2. Scenario: User adds a column with a formula referencing a non-existent field
   Expected: "Column formula error: 'impact_score' is not a recognized field.
   Available: title, year, citation_count, pagerank, pruning_pct, cluster,
   mutation_type." Column not added.
   Implementation: Validate formula fields against known column keys.

3. Scenario: Table rendered with no data (empty graph or no matching papers)
   Expected: Table shows header row with zero data rows and a message:
   "No papers match the criteria." in the table body area.
   Implementation: Empty state check before rendering rows.

4. Scenario: User sorts by a column containing mixed types (some null values)
   Expected: Null values sort to the bottom regardless of sort direction.
   Implementation: Sort comparator treats null/undefined as -Infinity for
   numbers, empty string for text.

5. Scenario: Export CSV while filter is active
   Expected: CSV exports only the filtered (visible) rows, not all rows.
   A note in the filename: "athena-table-filtered.csv" vs "athena-table.csv".
   Implementation: exportCSV() checks active filter state.

PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-blocks.js (table renderer), athena-engine.js (tabular intent detection), graph_engine.py (table data extraction), CSS
2. COMPARE against all Done Criteria checkboxes

4. CHECK: graph data columns populate correctly (PageRank, pruning, cluster)
5. VERIFY no regression in regular chat, block rendering, streaming
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase F. Core data presentation feature. 16 hours estimated.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #142 Mind Map Generator (P3, H -- shorter)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #142: MIND MAP GENERATOR
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #139 (Live Diagram Builder), #004 (Discuss Integration)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #011 (Artifact Cards),
                 #139 (Live Diagram Builder)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mind Map Generator allows Athena to produce interactive radial mind maps
from any research topic discussed in chat. When a user asks "map out the key
concepts in the attention mechanism lineage" Athena generates a hierarchical
mind map with the seed concept at center and branches radiating outward for
sub-topics, related papers, mutation pathways, and open questions. The map
is rendered as an SVG artifact inside the chat using a radial tree layout.

Nodes are colored by concept type: papers use --field-cs through --field-other
per field of study, mutation categories use --conf-high through --conf-specul,
and open questions use --warning. Branches are weighted by connection strength
derived from the graph's PageRank flow between concepts.

Users can expand/collapse branches by clicking nodes, drag nodes to reorganize,
and export the mind map as SVG or PNG. The mind map is not a copy of the
ancestry graph. It is a concept-level abstraction built by the LLM from the
graph data, organizing ideas hierarchically rather than by citation structure.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User types "mind map this topic" or /mindmap or asks "create a concept map of
the key ideas here." Athena detects mind-map intent.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User: "Create a mind map of the key concepts in this lineage."
2. Athena: "Building concept hierarchy..." (thinking indicator, 3-5 seconds).
3. Mind map renders as an SVG artifact block in the chat. Center node shows
   the seed paper. First ring: major concept branches (Attention, Embedding,
   Pre-training, Architecture). Second ring: sub-concepts. Third ring: specific
   papers or open questions.
4. User clicks "Embedding" branch to collapse it. Sub-nodes fold inward (300ms).
5. User clicks [Export SVG] in the artifact toolbar. SVG downloaded.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Mind map persists as an artifact block in chat. Interactive on revisit.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Mind map block renderer using SVG radial tree.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Mind map intent detection, concept extraction.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Mind map node styles, branch colors.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat returns mindmap block JSON when appropriate.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
SVG radial layout computed client-side. Node positions calculated using polar
coordinates from center. Branch angles distributed evenly per depth level.

  class AthenaMindMapBlock {
    constructor(data) { this.nodes = data.nodes; this.edges = data.edges; }
    render() { /* SVG radial tree layout */ }
    toggleBranch(nodeId) { /* collapse/expand children */ }
    exportSVG() { /* serialize SVG to file download */ }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
LLM extracts concept hierarchy from graph summary. Returns structured JSON
with nodes (concept, type, depth) and edges (parent-child relationships).

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response blocks[]: { "type": "mindmap", "nodes": [...], "edges": [...] }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Mind Map Rendered
