  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │  Here is the concept map for this lineage:                │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┌─ Mind Map ─────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │            ○ Sparse Attn                           │   │
  │  │           /                                        │   │
  │  │    ○ Efficiency ── ○ FlashAttn                     │   │
  │  │   /                                                │   │
  │  │  ● Attention ── ○ Multi-Head ── ○ Cross-Attn       │   │
  │  │   \                                                │   │
  │  │    ○ Pre-train ── ○ BERT ── ○ GPT                  │   │
  │  │                                                    │   │
  │  │  [Export SVG] [Export PNG] [Reset]                  │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
Branch collapse: children animate inward to parent (300ms).
Branch expand: children animate outward from parent (300ms).
Node hover: scale 1.1x, tooltip with concept description (150ms).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User requests mind map
  -> athena-engine.js routes to /api/athena/chat
  -> Backend extracts graph summary (get_graph_summary_for_chat)
  -> LLM generates concept hierarchy from graph data
  -> Response includes mindmap block with nodes[] and edges[]
  -> Frontend renders SVG radial tree
  -> User interactions are client-side only (expand/collapse/drag)

ERROR PATH: LLM fails -> "Could not build concept map. Try a simpler topic."
DATA STORED: Mind map JSON in chat_history blocks field.
DATA CACHED: SVG layout positions cached in block instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

PROMPT:
```
Extract a hierarchical concept map from this research lineage.
Graph summary: {graph_summary}
User topic: {user_message}

Return JSON: { "nodes": [{"id": "n1", "label": "...", "type": "concept|paper|question", "depth": 0}], "edges": [{"from": "n1", "to": "n2"}] }
Center node depth=0. Max depth=3. Max 30 nodes. No markdown.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with nodes and edges arrays
FALLBACK: Rule-based extraction using cluster labels from dna_json
TOKEN BUDGET: ~400 tokens per mind map request

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element        | Action      | Response                                    |
|----------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Node           | Click       | Toggle expand/collapse children              |
| Node           | Hover       | Scale 1.1x, show concept tooltip             |
| Node           | Drag        | Reposition node (and connected branches)     |
| Export SVG     | Click       | Download SVG file                            |
| Export PNG     | Click       | Canvas render then PNG download              |
| Reset          | Click       | Reset all positions and expand states        |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Collapse 300ms, expand 300ms, hover scale 150ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Nodes have role="treeitem", aria-expanded for collapsible.
  Keyboard: Tab between nodes, Enter toggles expand, arrow keys navigate tree.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Graph has only 3 papers (too few for meaningful mind map)
   Expected: "This lineage is too small for a concept map. Try a larger graph."
   Implementation: Check node count >= 5 before generating.

2. Scenario: User requests mind map with no graph loaded
   Expected: Generate a generic concept map from the chat topic without graph data.
   Implementation: Fallback to LLM-only concept extraction.

3. Scenario: Mind map has 30+ nodes (maximum)
   Expected: LLM caps at 30 nodes. Deepest branches pruned first.
   Implementation: Prompt enforces max 30 nodes.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context), #011 (Artifact Cards), #139 (Diagram Builder)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #004 (Discuss Integration), concept visualization
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: SVG rendering via browser native SVG support.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Mind map generated from graph data via LLM concept extraction
[ ] Radial SVG layout with center node and branching levels
[ ] Click to expand/collapse branches
[ ] Drag to reposition nodes
[ ] Export SVG and PNG

VISUAL: [ ] Field-of-study colors on nodes, weighted branches, dark theme
PERFORMANCE: [ ] Renders 30-node map within 500ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] role="treeitem", keyboard navigation, screen reader tree
EDGE CASES: [ ] Small graph fallback, no-graph fallback, 30-node cap

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-blocks.js (mindmap renderer), LLM prompt, CSS 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST generate, expand/collapse, export 4. CHECK concept accuracy vs graph data 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase H. Depends on diagram infrastructure from #139.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #143 Checklist Generator
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #143: CHECKLIST GENERATOR
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           F
ESTIMATED HOURS: 12
OVERLAPS WITH:   #011 (Artifact Cards), #021 (Collaborative Notes)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
                 #011 (Artifact Cards)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Checklist Generator gives Athena the ability to produce interactive,
checkable to-do lists grounded in the current research lineage. When a user
asks "What should I read to understand this field?" or "Give me a research
action plan," Athena produces a structured checklist where each item is tied
to a specific paper, concept, or action derived from the graph.

This feature transforms Athena from a Q&A system into a task planning
assistant. A researcher exploring the transformer lineage can ask Athena to
generate a reading order checklist, a methodology review checklist, a gap
analysis action plan, or a literature review preparation checklist. Each item
includes the paper title, why it matters (linked to graph structure), and a
checkbox that persists across the session.

The checklist format uses a structured block rendered by athena-blocks.js.
Each item has: a checkbox (stored in session memory), a title line, a
justification line (why this item matters in the lineage context), and an
optional link to the paper node in the graph. Items can be reordered via
drag-and-drop. Completed items move to the bottom with strikethrough text
and muted color.

The backend generates checklists by analyzing the graph structure. A "reading
order" checklist uses topological sort of the DAG to determine optimal reading
sequence. A "gap analysis" checklist pulls from gap_finder.py results. A
"methodology review" checklist extracts papers whose dominant mutation_type is
"methodological_adoption" and orders them by PageRank centrality.

Checklists can be exported as markdown (checkbox syntax: - [ ] / - [x]) or
as plain text. The export respects the current check state.

Each checklist has a progress indicator at the top showing "3/12 completed"
with a slim progress bar using --accent-gold fill.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User asks for a plan, reading order, or action list: "What should I read
first?" or "Create a research plan for understanding this field" or types
/checklist. Also triggered by Quick Action button "Reading Plan" after graph
load completes.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User: "Give me a reading order for understanding this attention lineage."

2. Athena: "Building reading plan from graph structure..." (2 seconds).

3. Athena renders a checklist block:

   ┌─ Reading Order: Attention Mechanism Lineage ──────────────┐
   │ Progress: 0/8 completed  [━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━] 0%       │
   ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
   │ [ ] 1. "Word Embeddings" (Mikolov 2013)                  │
   │     Foundation: embedding representations. PageRank: 0.049│
   │                                                           │
   │ [ ] 2. "Sequence to Sequence" (Sutskever 2014)            │
   │     Prerequisite: encoder-decoder architecture.           │
   │                                                           │
   │ [ ] 3. "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani 2017)         │
   │     Core: the transformer itself. PageRank: 0.087         │
   │     ... (5 more items)                                    │
   │                                                           │
   │ [Export MD] [Export Text] [Clear All]                      │
   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

4. User clicks the checkbox next to item 1. It checks with a subtle animation
   (check mark draws in, text gets slight muting). Progress updates to "1/8."

5. User drags item 5 above item 3 to customize their reading order. The item
   slides into position (200ms).

6. User clicks [Export MD]. Downloads a markdown file with current check states.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Checklist persists in chat. Check states stored in session memory. On page
reload within the same session, check states are restored.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Checklist block renderer. Checkbox persistence,
  drag-and-drop reorder, progress bar, export.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Checklist intent detection, LLM prompt routing.
- static/js/athena-memory.js: Checklist check state persistence per session.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Checklist styles, checkbox animation, progress bar.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat returns checklist block JSON.
- backend/graph_engine.py: Topological sort for reading order.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaChecklistBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.title = data.title;
      this.items = data.items; // [{id, text, detail, checked, paper_id}]
      this.checkStates = {};
    }

    toggleItem(itemId) {
      this.items.find(i => i.id === itemId).checked ^= true;
      this.updateProgress();
      this.saveToMemory();
      this.reRender();
    }

    exportMarkdown() {
      const lines = this.items.map(i =>
        `- [${i.checked ? 'x' : ' '}] ${i.text}\n  ${i.detail}`
      );
      const blob = new Blob([`# ${this.title}\n\n${lines.join('\n\n')}`],
        { type: 'text/markdown' });
      downloadBlob(blob, 'checklist.md');
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  def generate_reading_order(graph):
      topo = list(nx.topological_sort(graph.G))
      papers = [graph.papers[pid] for pid in topo if pid in graph.papers]
      return [{
          'id': f'item_{i}',
          'text': f'"{p.title}" ({p.authors[0] if p.authors else "Unknown"} {p.year})',
          'detail': f'PageRank: {graph.pagerank_scores.get(p.paper_id, 0):.3f}. '
                    f'Mutation role: {graph.dominant_mutations.get(p.paper_id, "N/A")}',
          'checked': False,
          'paper_id': p.paper_id
      } for i, p in enumerate(papers)]

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response blocks[]: { "type": "checklist", "title": "...", "items": [...] }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Checklist with Progress

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┌─ Reading Order ──────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ 2/8 completed [━━━━━○━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━] 25%       │     │
  │  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤     │
  │  │ [x] 1. "Word Embeddings" (Mikolov 2013)         │     │
  │  │     Foundation. PageRank: 0.049                  │     │
  │  │ [x] 2. "Seq2Seq" (Sutskever 2014)               │     │
  │  │     Prerequisite. PageRank: 0.038                │     │
  │  │ [ ] 3. "Attention" (Vaswani 2017)                │     │
  │  │     Core paper. PageRank: 0.087                  │     │
  │  │ [ ] 4. "BERT" (Devlin 2018)                      │     │
  │  │     Pre-training paradigm. PageRank: 0.063       │     │
  │  │ ... (4 more)                                     │     │
  │  │ [Export MD] [Export Text] [Clear All]             │     │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
Check item: checkmark draws in (200ms SVG path animation), text mutes (100ms).
Uncheck: reverse animation. Drag reorder: item slides (200ms). Progress bar
fills with --accent-gold (200ms width transition).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User asks for reading plan
  -> Backend runs topological sort on graph DAG
  -> LLM enriches each item with justification text
  -> Response includes checklist block with items[]
  -> Frontend renders AthenaChecklistBlock
  -> User checks items -> stored in athena-memory.js
  -> Export -> client-side markdown or text generation

ERROR PATH: Graph unavailable -> LLM generates generic checklist without graph data.
DATA STORED: Checklist JSON in chat_history blocks. Check states in session memory.
DATA CACHED: Check states in AthenaChecklistBlock instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

PROMPT:
```
Generate a research reading checklist from this graph.
Graph summary: {graph_summary}
Checklist type: {checklist_type} (reading_order|gap_analysis|methodology_review)
Papers in topological order: {topo_papers}

For each paper, write a one-line justification explaining its role in the
learning sequence. Reference PageRank centrality and mutation type where relevant.

Return JSON: { "title": "...", "items": [{"text": "...", "detail": "..."}] }
Max 15 items. No markdown wrapping.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with title and items array
FALLBACK: Use topological order without LLM enrichment
TOKEN BUDGET: ~300 tokens per checklist

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element           | Action      | Response                                    |
|-------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Checkbox          | Click       | Toggle check state, update progress         |
| Item row          | Drag        | Reorder items within the checklist          |
| Paper title       | Click       | Highlight corresponding node in graph       |
| Export MD         | Click       | Download markdown with check states         |
| Export Text       | Click       | Download plain text list                    |
| Clear All         | Click       | Uncheck all items, reset progress to 0%     |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Check 200ms, drag 200ms, progress bar 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: role="list" with role="listitem", checkbox has role="checkbox"
  with aria-checked. Keyboard: Space toggles check, arrow keys move between items.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Graph has 50+ papers (checklist too long)
   Expected: LLM selects top 15 most important papers for the checklist.
   Implementation: Prompt enforces max 15 items. Sort by PageRank for selection.

2. Scenario: User checks all items
   Expected: Progress shows "8/8 completed 100%". Confetti-free congratulation:
   "All items complete. Ready for the next step?"
   Implementation: 100% progress triggers completion message.

3. Scenario: Session expires with partial checklist progress
   Expected: Check states lost (session-scoped). User sees fresh checklist on new
   session. No cross-session persistence for anonymous users.
   Implementation: athena-memory.js scoped to session_id.

4. Scenario: User requests checklist with no graph loaded
   Expected: Generic research methodology checklist (not paper-specific).
   Implementation: LLM generates from topic alone.

5. Scenario: Drag-and-drop on mobile (touch)
   Expected: Long-press (500ms) activates drag mode. Touch-drag to reorder.
   Implementation: Touch event handlers with 500ms hold threshold.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context), #002 (Rich Formatting), #011 (Artifact Cards)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #032 (Recommendations as checklist), #021 (Collaborative Notes)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: NetworkX topological_sort for reading order generation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Checklists generated from graph structure (topological sort)
[ ] Checkbox persistence within session
[ ] Drag-and-drop reorder
[ ] Progress bar with completion percentage
[ ] Export as markdown and plain text
[ ] Paper title click highlights graph node
[ ] Clear All resets all checkboxes

VISUAL:
[ ] Checkmark SVG animation (200ms draw-in)
[ ] Progress bar with --accent-gold fill
[ ] Checked items have muted/strikethrough text
[ ] Dark theme with --bg-surface backgrounds

PERFORMANCE: [ ] Renders 15-item checklist within 200ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] role="checkbox" with aria-checked, keyboard Space toggle
EDGE CASES: [ ] 15-item cap, all-complete state, no-graph fallback, mobile drag

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-blocks.js (checklist), athena-memory.js (state), graph_engine.py (topo sort) 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST check, uncheck, reorder, export 4. CHECK persistence across scroll 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase F. Uses topological sort for intelligent ordering.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #144 Natural Language Queries
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #144: NATURAL LANGUAGE QUERIES
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           F
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #005 (Graph-Aware Commands)
BLOCKS:          #145 (What-If Calculator), #146 (Pivot Table in Chat)
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #006 (Conversation Memory)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Natural Language Queries transforms Athena from a conversational assistant into
a structured data query engine that accepts plain English questions and returns
precise, computed answers from the graph. Instead of relying solely on LLM
interpretation, this feature parses user questions into structured graph queries
that execute against the actual NetworkX graph and database, returning exact
numerical answers rather than LLM approximations.

The core problem this solves: when a user asks "How many papers in this lineage
were published after 2015?" the current system sends this to the LLM, which
may hallucinate a count. With Natural Language Queries, Athena parses the
question into a structured filter (year > 2015, count papers) and executes it
against the graph data directly. The answer is guaranteed accurate because it
comes from the data, not the model.

The system works through a two-stage pipeline. Stage 1: the LLM parses the
natural language question into a structured query DSL (a JSON object describing
the operation, filters, and aggregation). Stage 2: a query executor runs the
structured query against the graph engine and database, returning exact results.

Supported query types:
  - COUNT: "How many papers cite ResNet?" -> count(papers where cited_by contains ResNet)
  - FILTER: "Show me papers after 2018 in the CNN cluster" -> filter(year>2018, cluster="CNN")
  - RANK: "Top 5 papers by PageRank" -> sort(pagerank, desc, limit=5)
  - AGGREGATE: "Average citation count per cluster" -> group_by(cluster, avg(citation_count))
  - PATH: "What is the shortest path from Word2Vec to BERT?" -> shortest_path(word2vec, bert)
  - COMPARE: "Compare ResNet and AlexNet" -> compare(resnet, alexnet, all_metrics)
  - EXISTENCE: "Is there a paper about sparse attention?" -> search(title contains "sparse attention")

The query DSL is a simple JSON format:

  {
    "operation": "count",
    "target": "papers",
    "filters": [
      { "field": "year", "op": ">", "value": 2015 }
    ],
    "group_by": null,
    "sort_by": null,
    "limit": null
  }

The query executor maps each operation to a graph_engine or database method.
COUNT operations iterate the graph nodes with filters applied. FILTER returns
matching paper objects. RANK sorts by a computed metric. PATH uses
nx.shortest_path(). AGGREGATE groups nodes and computes statistics.

Results are returned with both the computed answer and the query interpretation,
so the user can verify Athena understood correctly: "Interpreting: count papers
where year > 2015. Found: 47 papers published after 2015 (out of 152 total)."

When the LLM cannot parse the question into the DSL (ambiguous or unsupported
query type), it falls back to standard conversational mode and notes: "I could
not parse this as a structured query. Here is my best interpretation..."

This feature is foundational because #145 (What-If Calculator) and #146 (Pivot
Table) both build on the structured query DSL. What-If extends it with
hypothetical filters. Pivot Table extends it with multi-dimensional grouping.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
Any question that implies a quantitative or structural answer about the graph.
The LLM detects "query intent" and routes through the structured pipeline
rather than generating a conversational response. Common patterns: "how many,"
"which papers," "top N by," "compare X and Y," "path from X to Y."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User: "How many papers in this lineage were published after 2015 and have
   more than 1000 citations?"

2. Athena shows a brief interpretation: "Query: count papers where year > 2015
   AND citation_count > 1000"

3. Athena executes the structured query against the graph. Result appears
   within 500ms:

   ┌─ Query Result ────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ Query: count papers where year > 2015 AND citations > 1000│
   │                                                            │
   │ Result: 23 papers                                         │
   │ (out of 152 total in this lineage)                        │
   │                                                            │
   │ Breakdown by cluster:                                      │
   │   Transformer: 11  Pre-training: 7  Vision: 5             │
   │                                                            │
   │ [Show Papers] [Refine Query] [Export]                      │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

4. User clicks [Show Papers]. A table block (#141) expands below with the 23
   matching papers, sortable by any column.

5. User follows up: "Of those, which ones have a pruning impact above 10%?"
   Athena adds the filter to the existing query context: "Refining: adding
   filter pruning_pct > 10%. Result: 8 papers."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Query results persist in chat. The structured query is stored in chat_history
metadata for audit and follow-up refinement.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Query intent detection, query result rendering,
  follow-up query chaining.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Query result block renderer.
- backend/query_executor.py: NEW FILE. Structured query executor. Parses DSL
  JSON and executes against graph_engine and database.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat routes query-intent messages through query_executor.
- backend/graph_engine.py: Exposes query-friendly methods (filter_papers,
  count_by, group_by, shortest_path_between).

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
Query results render as a dedicated block type:

  class AthenaQueryResultBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.query = data.query;
      this.result = data.result;
      this.breakdown = data.breakdown;
      this.matchingPapers = data.papers; // for "Show Papers" expansion
    }
    render() { /* query interpretation + result + breakdown + actions */ }
    showPapers() { /* expand into table block */ }
    refineQuery() { /* focus input with query context prefilled */ }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
Query executor class:

  class QueryExecutor:
      SUPPORTED_OPS = ['count', 'filter', 'rank', 'aggregate', 'path', 'compare', 'exists']

      def execute(self, query_dsl, graph):
          op = query_dsl['operation']
          if op == 'count':
              return self._count(query_dsl, graph)
          elif op == 'filter':
              return self._filter(query_dsl, graph)
          elif op == 'rank':
              return self._rank(query_dsl, graph)
          elif op == 'path':
              return self._shortest_path(query_dsl, graph)
          # ...

      def _count(self, dsl, graph):
          papers = self._apply_filters(graph.papers.values(), dsl.get('filters', []))
          return {'count': len(papers), 'total': len(graph.papers)}

      def _apply_filters(self, papers, filters):
          for f in filters:
              field, op, value = f['field'], f['op'], f['value']
              if op == '>': papers = [p for p in papers if getattr(p, field, 0) > value]
              elif op == '<': papers = [p for p in papers if getattr(p, field, 0) < value]
              elif op == '==': papers = [p for p in papers if getattr(p, field, '') == value]
              elif op == 'contains': papers = [p for p in papers if value.lower() in str(getattr(p, field, '')).lower()]
          return papers

      def _shortest_path(self, dsl, graph):
          source = dsl['source_paper_id']
          target = dsl['target_paper_id']
          try:
              path = nx.shortest_path(graph.G, source, target)
              return {'path': path, 'length': len(path) - 1, 'papers': [graph.papers[pid].title for pid in path]}
          except nx.NetworkXNoPath:
              return {'path': [], 'length': -1, 'message': 'No path exists between these papers.'}

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response (when query intent detected):
{
  "response": "Query: count papers where year > 2015...",
  "blocks": [{
    "type": "query_result",
    "query": { "operation": "count", "filters": [...] },
    "result": { "count": 23, "total": 152 },
    "breakdown": { "Transformer": 11, "Pre-training": 7, "Vision": 5 },
    "papers": [...]
  }]
}

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Query Result

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┌─ Query Result ───────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ ◐ count papers where year > 2015 AND cites > 1k  │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ Result: 23 papers (of 152)                        │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ By cluster:                                       │     │
  │  │   ● Transformer: 11                               │     │
  │  │   ● Pre-training: 7                               │     │
  │  │   ● Vision: 5                                     │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ [Show Papers] [Refine] [Export]                   │     │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Papers Expanded (after "Show Papers" click)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  (Query result above)                                     │
  │  ┌─ Matching Papers ──────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ Paper              │ Year │ Cites │ Cluster         │   │
  │  ├────────────────────┼──────┼───────┼─────────────────┤   │
  │  │ Attention...       │ 2017 │ 98201 │ Transformer     │   │
  │  │ BERT               │ 2018 │ 75443 │ Pre-training    │   │
  │  │ ... (21 more)      │      │       │                 │   │
  │  └────────────────────┴──────┴───────┴─────────────────┘   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS:
Query submitted: thinking indicator 500ms max. Result block slides in.
Show Papers: table expands below query result (200ms height animation).
Refine: input area receives focus with query prefix.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User asks quantitative question
  -> athena-engine.js sends to /api/athena/chat
  -> Backend LLM parses question into query DSL (Stage 1)
  -> query_executor.py executes DSL against graph_engine (Stage 2)
  -> Exact results returned with interpretation string
  -> Frontend renders query_result block
  -> User clicks "Show Papers" -> table block expands
  -> User clicks "Refine" -> new query builds on previous context

Follow-up query:
  -> Previous query DSL stored in chat context
  -> New filters appended to existing DSL
  -> Re-executed with combined filters

ERROR PATH:
  -> LLM cannot parse question -> fallback to conversational mode with note
  -> Query references non-existent paper -> "Paper not found in this lineage."
  -> Graph not loaded -> "Load a graph first to run structured queries."

DATA STORED: Query DSL + results in chat_history metadata.
DATA CACHED: Query results in block instance for "Show Papers" expansion.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

PROMPT (Query parsing):
```
Parse this research question into a structured query.
Graph context: {graph_summary} (includes {node_count} papers)

User question: {user_message}

Available fields: title, year, citation_count, pagerank, pruning_pct, cluster,
  mutation_type, text_tier, fields_of_study, authors

Available operations: count, filter, rank, aggregate, path, compare, exists

Return ONLY valid JSON:
{
  "operation": "count|filter|rank|aggregate|path|compare|exists",
  "target": "papers",
  "filters": [{"field": "...", "op": ">|<|==|>=|<=|contains", "value": ...}],
  "group_by": "field_name" or null,
  "sort_by": {"field": "...", "direction": "asc|desc"} or null,
  "limit": number or null,
  "source_paper_id": "..." (for path queries only),
  "target_paper_id": "..." (for path queries only)
}

If the question cannot be parsed as a structured query, return:
{"operation": "unparseable", "reason": "..."}
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: Query DSL JSON
FALLBACK: Conversational response with caveat about approximation
TOKEN BUDGET: ~250 tokens per query parse

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element           | Action      | Response                                    |
|-------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Show Papers       | Click       | Expand matching papers as sortable table    |
| Refine Query      | Click       | Focus input with query context for follow-up|
| Export            | Click       | Download query result as JSON or CSV        |
| Cluster name      | Click       | Filter graph to show only that cluster      |
| Paper row         | Click       | Highlight paper node in graph               |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Result block slide in 200ms. Table expand 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Query interpretation announced to screen reader. Result block
  has role="region" with aria-label. Table follows #141 accessibility patterns.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Ambiguous query ("tell me about the papers")
   Expected: LLM returns {"operation": "unparseable"}. Athena responds
   conversationally: "That is too broad for a structured query. Try something
   specific like 'how many papers after 2015?' or 'top 5 by PageRank.'"
   Implementation: Unparseable operation triggers fallback.

2. Scenario: Query references a paper not in the lineage
   Expected: "Paper 'XYZ' is not in this lineage. The query applies only to
   papers visible in the current graph."
   Implementation: query_executor validates paper_id existence.

3. Scenario: Path query between two disconnected components
   Expected: "No citation path exists between Word2Vec and [paper]. These
   papers are in separate branches of the lineage."
   Implementation: nx.shortest_path raises NetworkXNoPath, caught and reported.

4. Scenario: Aggregate query on empty group
   Expected: "No papers match the filter. The aggregation returned no results."
   Implementation: Empty result set check before aggregation.

5. Scenario: Follow-up query after session context lost
   Expected: Fresh query execution (no previous DSL context). Works as standalone.
   Implementation: If previous DSL not in context, treat as new query.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON:
  #001 -- Context-Aware Responses (graph context for query execution)
  #006 -- Conversation Memory (query chaining across messages)

ENABLES:
  #145 -- What-If Calculator (extends DSL with hypothetical filters)
  #146 -- Pivot Table (extends DSL with multi-dimensional grouping)

ENHANCES:
  #005 -- Graph-Aware Commands (structured queries complement slash commands)
  #141 -- Table Builder (query results expand into tables)

CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Requires backend/query_executor.py (new file).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] LLM parses natural language into structured query DSL
[ ] COUNT, FILTER, RANK, AGGREGATE, PATH, COMPARE, EXISTS operations work
[ ] Exact results from graph data (not LLM approximation)
[ ] Query interpretation shown to user for verification
[ ] "Show Papers" expands into sortable table
[ ] "Refine Query" chains with previous query context
[ ] Fallback to conversational mode for unparseable queries
[ ] Follow-up queries build on previous filter context

VISUAL:
[ ] Query result block with interpretation, result, breakdown
[ ] Cluster breakdown with dot-matrix indicators
[ ] Action buttons (Show Papers, Refine, Export)

PERFORMANCE:
[ ] Query execution within 500ms for graphs up to 600 nodes
[ ] LLM parsing within 2 seconds

ACCESSIBILITY:
[ ] Query interpretation announced to screen reader
[ ] Result block has proper ARIA roles
[ ] Table expansion follows #141 patterns

EDGE CASES:
[ ] Ambiguous query fallback
[ ] Non-existent paper reference
[ ] Disconnected path query
[ ] Empty aggregation results
[ ] Context-lost follow-up

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ query_executor.py, athena-engine.js, athena-blocks.js, graph_engine.py
2. COMPARE against all Done Criteria
3. TEST each operation type (count, filter, rank, aggregate, path, compare, exists)
4. CHECK exact results match graph data (not LLM hallucination)
5. VERIFY query chaining across follow-up messages
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase F. P1 priority. Foundation for #145 and #146. Creates new backend/query_executor.py.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #145 What-If Calculator
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #145: WHAT-IF CALCULATOR
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           F
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #144 (Natural Language Queries), #027 (Counter-History Mode)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #144 (Natural Language Queries), #001 (Context-Aware Responses)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The What-If Calculator extends the Natural Language Query system (#144) with
hypothetical reasoning. Users can ask counterfactual questions about the graph
and Athena computes the results by temporarily modifying the graph state,
running the query, and returning the hypothetical outcome alongside the
actual outcome for comparison.

The core value: a researcher exploring the transformer lineage wonders "What
if the attention mechanism had never been invented? How many downstream papers
would lose their foundation?" Instead of an LLM guess, the What-If Calculator
runs the actual pruning engine against the hypothetical scenario and returns
exact numbers: "If 'Attention Is All You Need' were removed, 47 papers (31%)
would lose their foundation. The most affected cluster is Pre-training (19
papers collapsed)."

This is different from Counter-History Mode (#027) which provides narrative
counterfactuals through LLM storytelling. The What-If Calculator is strictly
quantitative: it modifies the graph, computes metrics, and returns numbers.

Supported what-if operations:
  - REMOVE_PAPER: "What if [paper] never existed?" -> pruning simulation
  - REMOVE_CLUSTER: "What if the [cluster] field didn't exist?" -> bulk pruning
  - SHIFT_YEAR: "What if [paper] was published 5 years later?" -> recalculate dependencies
  - ADD_EDGE: "What if [paper A] had cited [paper B]?" -> add edge, recompute PageRank
  - REMOVE_EDGE: "What if [paper A] never cited [paper B]?" -> remove edge, recompute

Each what-if operation returns a comparison block showing the actual state
vs the hypothetical state side by side. Key metrics compared: total nodes,
total edges, average PageRank, cluster composition, top 5 papers by centrality.

The what-if engine operates on a copy of the graph (deep copy of the NetworkX
DiGraph). The original graph is never modified. All mutations are applied to
the copy, metrics recomputed on the copy, and the copy discarded after the
response is generated.

Results are presented as a structured comparison card with delta indicators:
green arrows for metrics that improved, red arrows for metrics that degraded,
and gray for unchanged. The pruning-based what-ifs reuse the existing
compute_pruning_result() function from backend/pruning.py.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User asks a "what if" question: "What if ResNet never existed?" or "What would
happen if BERT cited Attention directly?" or uses /whatif command.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User: "What if Attention Is All You Need had never been published?"

2. Athena: "Running hypothetical simulation..." (thinking indicator, 1-2 seconds).
   Backend deep-copies the graph, removes the Attention paper node and all its
   edges, runs pruning cascade via compute_pruning_result(), recomputes PageRank
   on the modified graph.

3. Athena renders a comparison block:

   ┌─ What-If: Remove "Attention Is All You Need" ──────────┐
   │                                                          │
   │            Actual          Hypothetical     Delta         │
   │ Nodes:     152             105              -47 (31%)    │
   │ Edges:     203             128              -75 (37%)    │
   │ Clusters:  7               4                -3           │
   │ Avg PR:    0.0066          0.0095           +0.0029      │
   │                                                          │
   │ Most affected cluster: Pre-training (19 papers lost)     │
   │ Surviving clusters: CNN Origin, Embedding, RNN           │
   │                                                          │
   │ [Show Collapsed Papers] [Show Survivors] [Try Another]   │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

4. User clicks [Show Collapsed Papers]. A list of 47 papers that would collapse,
   grouped by BFS level from the removed paper.

5. User: "What if it was published 5 years later, in 2022?" Athena shifts the
   year, recalculates which downstream papers could still cite it given their
   own publication years, and shows the cascading effect of delayed influence.

AFTER COMPLETION:
What-if results persist in chat. The hypothetical graph is discarded (not stored).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- backend/query_executor.py: Extended with what-if operations. Deep copy graph,
  apply mutation, recompute metrics, generate comparison.
- backend/pruning.py: compute_pruning_result() reused for REMOVE_PAPER scenarios.
- backend/graph_engine.py: Clone method for deep-copying graph state.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Comparison block renderer (actual vs hypothetical).
- static/js/athena-engine.js: What-if intent detection, routes to backend.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class WhatIfExecutor:
      def execute(self, operation, params, graph):
          graph_copy = graph.deep_clone()
          if operation == 'remove_paper':
              return self._remove_paper(params['paper_id'], graph, graph_copy)
          elif operation == 'shift_year':
              return self._shift_year(params['paper_id'], params['years'], graph, graph_copy)
          # ...

      def _remove_paper(self, paper_id, original, modified):
          modified.G.remove_node(paper_id)
          pruning = compute_pruning_result(original, paper_id)
          new_pagerank = nx.pagerank(modified.G, alpha=0.85)
          return {
              'actual': self._metrics(original),
              'hypothetical': self._metrics_from(modified, new_pagerank),
              'pruning': pruning,
              'delta': self._compute_deltas(original, modified)
          }

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response blocks[]: { "type": "whatif_result", "operation": "remove_paper",
  "actual": {...}, "hypothetical": {...}, "delta": {...}, "details": {...} }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: What-If Comparison

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┌─ What-If Result ─────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │            Actual     Hypothetical    Delta       │     │
  │  │ Nodes:     152        105             -47        │     │
  │  │ Edges:     203        128             -75        │     │
  │  │ Clusters:  7          4               -3         │     │
  │  │ Avg PR:    0.0066     0.0095          +0.0029    │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ [Show Collapsed] [Show Survivors] [Try Another]   │     │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Result block slides in 200ms. Delta values animate count-up 500ms.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User asks what-if question
  -> LLM parses into what-if operation + parameters
  -> Backend deep-clones graph
  -> Applies hypothetical mutation to clone
  -> Recomputes metrics (PageRank, pruning cascade)
  -> Returns actual vs hypothetical comparison
  -> Frontend renders comparison block
  -> Clone is discarded (no persistence)

ERROR PATH: Paper not found -> "Paper not in this lineage." Graph too large for clone -> "Graph too complex for what-if simulation (>600 nodes)."
DATA STORED: What-if result in chat_history. Clone NOT stored.
DATA CACHED: None (clone is ephemeral).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

PROMPT:
```
Parse this what-if question into a structured operation.
Graph: {node_count} papers. Seed: {seed_title}.

User question: {user_message}

Operations: remove_paper, remove_cluster, shift_year, add_edge, remove_edge.

Return JSON:
{ "operation": "...", "params": { "paper_id": "...", "years": 5, ... } }
If ambiguous, return { "operation": "unparseable", "reason": "..." }
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with operation and params
FALLBACK: Conversational response about hypothetical without exact numbers
TOKEN BUDGET: ~200 tokens per parse

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element            | Action | Response                                       |
|--------------------|--------|------------------------------------------------|
| Show Collapsed     | Click  | List of collapsed papers by BFS level          |
| Show Survivors     | Click  | List of papers that survive the hypothetical    |
| Try Another        | Click  | Focus input with "/whatif " prefix              |
| Delta values       | Render | Count-up animation from 0 to final value        |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Delta count-up 500ms, list expand 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Comparison table uses role="table" with row/cell headers. Delta
  values announced as "decreased by 47" or "increased by 0.003."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Remove the seed paper itself
   Expected: "Removing the seed paper would collapse the entire lineage."
   Show 100% collapse result.
   Implementation: Special case for seed paper removal.

2. Scenario: Add edge that creates a cycle
   Expected: "Adding this edge would create a citation cycle, which is not
   physically possible (a paper cannot cite a future paper)."
   Implementation: Year validation before adding hypothetical edge.

3. Scenario: Shift year makes paper published after papers that cite it
   Expected: Report temporal paradoxes: "3 citing papers were published before
   the shifted date. They could not have cited this paper."
   Implementation: Year comparison across all citing papers.

4. Scenario: What-if on a graph with 1 paper
   Expected: "The graph has only one paper. What-if analysis requires at least
   2 connected papers."
   Implementation: Minimum graph size check.

5. Scenario: Multiple what-ifs in sequence
   Expected: Each what-if runs on the ORIGINAL graph, not the previous
   hypothetical. What-ifs are independent unless user explicitly chains them.
   Implementation: Always deep-clone from original.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #144 (Natural Language Queries), #001 (Context-Aware Responses)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #027 (Counter-History Mode), pruning visualization
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: backend/pruning.py compute_pruning_result(), NetworkX deep copy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] REMOVE_PAPER what-if with exact pruning cascade numbers
[ ] REMOVE_CLUSTER what-if (bulk removal)
[ ] SHIFT_YEAR what-if with temporal analysis
[ ] ADD_EDGE and REMOVE_EDGE what-ifs with PageRank recomputation
[ ] Actual vs hypothetical comparison with deltas
[ ] Show Collapsed and Show Survivors detail views
[ ] Each what-if runs on fresh clone (independent)

VISUAL:
[ ] Comparison table with delta arrows (green/red/gray)
[ ] Delta count-up animation (500ms)

PERFORMANCE: [ ] What-if completes within 2 seconds for 600-node graphs
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Comparison table with proper ARIA, delta values announced
EDGE CASES: [ ] Seed removal, cycle prevention, temporal paradox, single-node graph

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ query_executor.py (what-if), pruning.py, graph_engine.py (clone) 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST each operation type 4. CHECK clone independence (original not modified) 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase F. Extends #144 DSL with hypothetical operations. Reuses pruning.py.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #146 Pivot Table in Chat (P3, H -- shorter)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #146: PIVOT TABLE IN CHAT
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 18
OVERLAPS WITH:   #141 (Table Builder), #144 (Natural Language Queries)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #141 (Table Builder), #144 (Natural Language Queries)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Pivot Table in Chat extends the Table Builder (#141) with multi-dimensional
grouping, aggregation, and cross-tabulation. Users can ask "break down papers
by cluster and mutation type" and Athena generates an interactive pivot table
where rows are clusters, columns are mutation types, and cells contain paper
counts, average PageRank, or summed citation counts.

The pivot engine builds on the query DSL from #144 by adding GROUP_BY with
two dimensions and an aggregation function (count, sum, avg, min, max). The
frontend extends AthenaTableBlock with pivot-specific controls: dimension
selectors (rows vs columns), aggregation function dropdown, and expandable
cells that show the underlying papers.

This gives researchers the ability to spot patterns in the lineage structure
that are invisible in the graph view. For example, a pivot of cluster (rows)
by decade (columns) with count aggregation instantly shows which research
directions peaked in which decades.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User asks for a cross-tabulation: "Break down papers by cluster and decade" or
"Show me mutation types per field of study" or types /pivot.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User: "Show me paper counts by cluster and mutation type."
2. Athena: "Building pivot table..." (1-2 seconds).
3. Pivot table renders with clusters as rows, mutation types as columns, and
   paper counts in cells. Totals shown in rightmost column and bottom row.
4. User clicks a cell (e.g., "Transformer x generalization: 8"). Cell expands
   to show the 8 matching papers.
5. User changes the aggregation from "count" to "avg citations" via dropdown.
   Table recalculates with average citation counts per cell.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Pivot table persists in chat. Aggregation changes are client-side.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: PivotTableBlock extending AthenaTableBlock.
- backend/query_executor.py: Pivot operation with dual GROUP_BY support.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Pivot styles, cell expansion, dimension controls.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaPivotBlock extends AthenaTableBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      super(data);
      this.rowDimension = data.row_dim;
      this.colDimension = data.col_dim;
      this.aggregation = data.aggregation;
      this.rawPapers = data.papers;
    }
    changeAggregation(fn) { /* recompute cells client-side */ }
    expandCell(row, col) { /* show matching papers list */ }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  def _pivot(self, dsl, graph):
      rows = set(); cols = set(); cells = {}
      for p in graph.papers.values():
          r = getattr(p, dsl['row_dim']); c = getattr(p, dsl['col_dim'])
          rows.add(r); cols.add(c)
          cells.setdefault((r,c), []).append(p)
      return {'rows': sorted(rows), 'cols': sorted(cols), 'cells': cells}

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response blocks[]: { "type": "pivot_table", "row_dim": "cluster",
  "col_dim": "mutation_type", "aggregation": "count", "data": {...} }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Pivot Table

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │  ┌─ Pivot: Cluster x Mutation ──────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ Agg: [count v]                                   │     │
  │  ├──────────┬────────┬────────┬────────┬────────────┤     │
  │  │          │ adopt. │ gener. │ spec.  │ Total      │     │
  │  ├──────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┼────────────┤     │
  │  │ Transf.  │   3    │   8    │   2    │  13        │     │
  │  │ Pre-train│   5    │   4    │   3    │  12        │     │
  │  │ CNN      │   7    │   1    │   6    │  14        │     │
  │  ├──────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┼────────────┤     │
  │  │ Total    │  15    │  13    │  11    │  39        │     │
  │  └──────────┴────────┴────────┴────────┴────────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Cell click expands to paper list (200ms). Aggregation change recalculates (100ms).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User asks for cross-tabulation
  -> LLM parses into pivot query DSL (row_dim, col_dim, aggregation)
  -> Backend groups papers by two dimensions
  -> Returns pivot data with raw papers per cell
  -> Frontend renders pivot table with aggregated values
  -> User changes aggregation -> client-side recompute from raw papers

ERROR PATH: Missing dimension data -> "Some papers lack cluster assignments."
DATA STORED: Pivot data in chat_history. DATA CACHED: Raw papers in block instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

PROMPT:
```
Parse this pivot table request.
Available dimensions: cluster, mutation_type, decade, field_of_study, text_tier
Available aggregations: count, sum(citation_count), avg(pagerank), avg(citation_count)

User: {user_message}
Return JSON: {"row_dim": "...", "col_dim": "...", "aggregation": "count"}
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with dimensions and aggregation
FALLBACK: Default to cluster x mutation_type with count
TOKEN BUDGET: ~150 tokens

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element        | Action | Response                                       |
|----------------|--------|------------------------------------------------|
| Cell           | Click  | Expand to show matching papers                 |
| Agg dropdown   | Change | Recalculate all cells with new function         |
| Row header     | Click  | Filter graph to that cluster                   |
| Column header  | Click  | Highlight papers with that mutation type        |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Cell expand 200ms, agg change 100ms recalc.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: role="grid", row/column headers with scope, cell values announced.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Dimension has too many unique values (>20 columns)
   Expected: Top 10 values shown, rest grouped as "Other."
   Implementation: Cap at 10 columns, aggregate remainder.

2. Scenario: Cell with zero papers
   Expected: Display "0" or "--" in empty cells. Not blank.
   Implementation: Default cell value to 0 for count, "--" for avg.

3. Scenario: Papers missing cluster assignment
   Expected: Grouped under "Unassigned" row.
   Implementation: Default null cluster to "Unassigned."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #141 (Table Builder), #144 (Natural Language Queries)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Data exploration, pattern detection across dimensions
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Extends query_executor.py with pivot operation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Pivot table with two dimensions and aggregation
[ ] Aggregation function switchable (count, sum, avg)
[ ] Cell click expands to paper list
[ ] Row and column totals displayed
[ ] Client-side aggregation recalculation

VISUAL: [ ] Grid layout with headers, dark theme, cell expansion
PERFORMANCE: [ ] Pivot of 200 papers across 10x7 grid within 200ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Grid roles, header scopes, cell values announced
EDGE CASES: [ ] >20 columns capped, zero cells, missing dimensions

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-blocks.js (pivot), query_executor.py 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST pivot with various dimensions 4. CHECK aggregation accuracy 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase H. Extends #141 table builder with multi-dimensional grouping.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #147 Time Series in Chat
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #147: TIME SERIES IN CHAT
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Learning & Research
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           F
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #025 (Sentiment Heatmap), #015 (Timeline Narration)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #011 (Artifact Cards),
                 #141 (Table Builder)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Time Series in Chat gives Athena the ability to render inline temporal charts
within the chat panel. When a user asks "Show me citation trends over time" or
"How has this research area grown?" Athena generates a compact SVG line chart
or area chart directly in the chat message, showing how metrics evolve across
publication years.

This feature differs from the Timeline Narration (#015) which tells a story
about the lineage chronology. Time Series in Chat produces a quantitative
visualization: X-axis is time (years), Y-axis is a metric (paper count,
cumulative citations, average PageRank, cluster emergence). The chart is
interactive: hover on data points to see values, click to see papers from
that year.

The backend aggregates graph data by year using the publication dates from the
papers in the lineage. Available metrics for time series:
  - Paper count per year (how many papers published each year)
  - Cumulative paper count (growth curve)
  - Citation count per year (aggregate citations of papers published that year)
  - Average PageRank per year (structural importance over time)
  - Mutation type distribution per year (stacked area chart)
  - Cluster emergence timeline (when each cluster's first paper appeared)

Charts are rendered as SVG elements within an artifact block. The SVG uses
Arivu's color palette: line color --accent-gold, area fill with 0.2 opacity,
grid lines with --text-muted, axis labels with --text-secondary. Data points
are small circles that enlarge on hover with a tooltip showing the exact value.

Multiple series can be overlaid. "Compare citation growth between the
Transformer cluster and the CNN cluster" renders two lines in different colors
(--field-cs for Transformer, --field-physics for CNN) with a legend.

The chart is compact (300px height, full chat-panel width) to fit naturally
in the conversation flow. Users can expand to full size by clicking an expand
icon in the artifact toolbar.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User asks about trends, growth, temporal patterns: "Show me how this field
grew over time" or "Citation trends for the transformer cluster" or types
/timeseries.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User: "Show me how many papers were published each year in this lineage."

2. Athena: "Generating time series..." (1 second).

3. A compact SVG chart appears in the chat:

   ┌─ Papers per Year ──────────────────────────────────┐
   │ 30 |                                     ●         │
   │    |                              ●    /  \        │
   │ 20 |                       ●    /  \ /    \       │
   │    |                     /  \ /                    │
   │ 10 |          ●    ●  /                            │
   │    |   ●    /  \ /                                 │
   │  0 ├──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──                 │
   │    2012 13  14  15  16  17  18  19  20  21         │
   │                                                     │
   │ [Expand] [Export PNG] [Overlay Another Metric]      │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

4. User hovers over the 2017 peak. Tooltip: "2017: 28 papers. Notable:
   Attention Is All You Need, Transformer-XL."

5. User clicks [Overlay Another Metric]. A dropdown: "Citations per year."
   A second line appears in --accent-teal showing citation volume over time.

6. User clicks [Export PNG]. Chart downloaded as a PNG image.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Chart persists in chat. Interactive on revisit. Overlaid metrics remembered.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: TimeSeriesBlock renderer. SVG chart with
  axes, data points, tooltips, overlay support.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Time series intent detection.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Chart styles, tooltip, expand state.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat returns timeseries block with data arrays.
- backend/graph_engine.py: Temporal aggregation methods (papers_per_year,
  citations_per_year, pagerank_per_year, mutations_per_year).

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaTimeSeriesBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.series = data.series; // [{name, color, values: [{x: year, y: value}]}]
      this.xLabel = data.x_label;
      this.yLabel = data.y_label;
      this.width = 560; this.height = 300;
    }
    render() { /* SVG with axes, lines, points, tooltips */ }
    addOverlay(newSeries) { /* append second line with different color */ }
    exportPNG() { /* canvas render from SVG, then PNG download */ }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  def papers_per_year(self):
      counts = {}
      for p in self.papers.values():
          if p.year: counts[p.year] = counts.get(p.year, 0) + 1
      years = sorted(counts.keys())
      return [{'x': y, 'y': counts[y]} for y in years]

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response blocks[]: { "type": "timeseries", "x_label": "Year",
  "y_label": "Paper Count", "series": [{...}] }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Time Series Chart

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │  Here is the publication trend for this lineage:          │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┌─ Papers per Year ────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │  30│              ●                               │     │
  │  │    │         ●  /  \  ●                           │     │
  │  │  20│       /  \/     \/                           │     │
  │  │    │     /                                        │     │
  │  │  10│   ●                                          │     │
  │  │    │ /                                            │     │
  │  │   0├──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──                          │     │
  │  │    12  14  16  18  20  22                          │     │
  │  │ [Expand] [Export PNG] [+Metric]                   │     │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Chart draws in left-to-right (line SVG path animation 600ms).
Hover: data point scales 1.5x (100ms), tooltip fades in. Overlay: new line
draws in same left-to-right animation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User asks about trends
  -> Backend aggregates graph data by year
  -> Returns series data with x/y values
  -> Frontend renders SVG chart
  -> User hovers -> tooltip with year details
  -> User adds overlay -> new series added client-side or via API
  -> User exports -> SVG to canvas to PNG

ERROR PATH: No year data on papers -> "Year data unavailable for this lineage."
DATA STORED: Series data in chat_history blocks.
DATA CACHED: SVG elements in block instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

PROMPT:
```
Determine the time series to generate.
Available metrics: paper_count, cumulative_count, citation_sum, avg_pagerank,
  mutation_distribution, cluster_emergence

User request: {user_message}
Return JSON: {"metric": "paper_count", "title": "Papers per Year"}
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with metric and title
FALLBACK: Default to paper_count
TOKEN BUDGET: ~100 tokens

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element        | Action | Response                                       |
|----------------|--------|------------------------------------------------|
| Data point     | Hover  | Scale 1.5x, tooltip with value + paper names   |
| Data point     | Click  | Show papers from that year in expandable list   |
| Expand         | Click  | Chart grows to full panel width                |
| Export PNG     | Click  | Download chart as PNG                          |
| +Metric        | Click  | Dropdown to overlay additional time series      |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Line draw-in 600ms, hover 100ms, overlay draw-in 600ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Chart has role="img" with aria-label describing the trend.
  Data table alternative: "Also available as table" link renders tabular data.
  Screen reader: "Time series chart showing papers per year from 2012 to 2022.
  Peak: 28 papers in 2017."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: All papers from the same year
   Expected: Single data point shown. Chart still renders but note: "All papers
   are from the same year. Time series is not meaningful."
   Implementation: Check for min 2 distinct years.

2. Scenario: Large year gaps (e.g., 1998 then 2017)
   Expected: X-axis shows all years including gaps. Gap years show 0 values.
   Implementation: Fill missing years with zero values.

3. Scenario: Overlay 5+ metrics (too cluttered)
   Expected: Maximum 3 overlays. Fourth attempt: "Maximum 3 metrics can be
   overlaid. Remove one to add another."
   Implementation: Cap overlays at 3.

4. Scenario: Export while zoomed/expanded
   Expected: PNG captures the expanded view at current dimensions.
   Implementation: Export uses current SVG dimensions.

5. Scenario: Mobile view (narrow chat panel)
   Expected: Chart scales to available width. Data points remain tappable.
   Implementation: Responsive SVG viewBox.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context), #011 (Artifact Cards), #141 (Table Builder)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #015 (Timeline Narration), #025 (Sentiment Heatmap)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: SVG rendering. Canvas API for PNG export.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] SVG time series chart rendered in chat
[ ] Multiple metrics: paper count, citations, PageRank, mutations
[ ] Hover tooltips with year values and notable papers
[ ] Click data points to see papers from that year
[ ] Overlay up to 3 metrics with distinct colors
[ ] Export as PNG
[ ] Expand to full panel width

VISUAL:
[ ] --accent-gold line, --bg-surface background, --text-muted grid
[ ] Data points as circles, enlarge on hover
[ ] Line draw-in animation left to right (600ms)

PERFORMANCE: [ ] Chart renders within 300ms for 50-year span
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] role="img", aria-label, data table alternative
EDGE CASES: [ ] Single year, year gaps, overlay cap, mobile responsive

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-blocks.js (timeseries), graph_engine.py (aggregation), CSS 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST chart render, hover, overlay, export 4. CHECK data accuracy against graph 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase F. Inline SVG charting. Depends on graph temporal data.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #148 Radar Scan
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #148: RADAR SCAN
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Slash Commands
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #005 (Graph-Aware Commands), #032 (Paper Recommendation)
BLOCKS:          NONE
