BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #005 (Graph-Aware Commands),
                 #032 (Paper Recommendation Engine)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Radar Scan is a slash command (/radar) that performs a 360-degree scan of the
current graph state and returns a structured intelligence briefing. It answers
the question "What is the state of this research lineage right now?" by
aggregating multiple analytical signals into a single, digestible report.

When the user types /radar, Athena runs five analytical passes on the current
graph and returns a structured briefing block with five sections:

1. STRUCTURAL SUMMARY: Node count, edge count, depth, density, number of
   clusters. One-line characterization: "A moderately deep lineage with strong
   clustering around transformer architecture."

2. KEY PLAYERS: Top 5 papers by PageRank centrality with their structural
   roles (hub, bridge, leaf, bottleneck). Derived from the graph engine's
   PageRank scores and degree analysis.

3. MUTATION LANDSCAPE: Distribution of mutation types across edges. Rendered
   as a compact horizontal bar chart. "adoption: 34%, generalization: 22%,
   specialization: 18%, hybridization: 12%, contradiction: 8%, other: 6%."

4. VULNERABILITY REPORT: Papers whose removal would cause the highest cascade
   damage (from precomputed_pruning). Top 3 bottleneck papers with their
   impact percentages. "Removing 'Attention Is All You Need' collapses 31%."

5. FRONTIER ASSESSMENT: Leaf nodes (papers with no outgoing citations in the
   graph) analyzed for recency and citation velocity. Papers at the frontier
   that are growing fastest represent active research directions. Papers at
   the frontier that have stalled may represent dead ends.

The briefing uses a dot-matrix style header for each section (● for complete
data, ◐ for partial data, ○ for missing data) so the user can see data
coverage at a glance.

This feature is valuable because researchers often load a graph and do not
know where to start exploring. The radar scan gives them an instant orientation:
here are the important papers, here are the vulnerabilities, here is where the
frontier is. It is the "executive summary" of a lineage.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User types /radar in the chat input and presses Enter. No additional arguments
needed. The scan runs on the currently loaded graph.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types: /radar

2. Athena: "Scanning lineage..." with a pulsing radar icon animation (2-3
   seconds while five analyses run).

3. Athena renders the briefing block:

   ┌─ RADAR SCAN: Attention Mechanism Lineage ──────────────┐
   │                                                          │
   │ ● STRUCTURE                                              │
   │   152 papers, 203 edges, depth 3, 7 clusters            │
   │   Dense mid-layer with sparse frontier.                  │
   │                                                          │
   │ ● KEY PLAYERS                                            │
   │   1. Attention Is All You Need  PR: 0.087  [Hub]        │
   │   2. BERT                       PR: 0.063  [Hub]        │
   │   3. ResNet                     PR: 0.055  [Bridge]     │
   │   4. Word2Vec                   PR: 0.049  [Foundation] │
   │   5. AlexNet                    PR: 0.041  [Foundation] │
   │                                                          │
   │ ● MUTATION LANDSCAPE                                     │
   │   adoption      [━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━    ] 34%             │
   │   generalization[━━━━━━━━━━━          ] 22%             │
   │   specialization[━━━━━━━━━            ] 18%             │
   │   hybridization [━━━━━━               ] 12%             │
   │   contradiction [━━━━                 ]  8%             │
   │   other         [━━━                  ]  6%             │
   │                                                          │
   │ ● VULNERABILITY                                          │
   │   Attention Is All You Need: 31% cascade on removal     │
   │   BERT: 18% cascade on removal                          │
   │   ResNet: 12% cascade on removal                        │
   │                                                          │
   │ ◐ FRONTIER (partial: 3 of 12 leaves analyzed)            │
   │   Active: "Vision Transformer" (2021, growing 45%/yr)   │
   │   Active: "Swin Transformer" (2021, growing 38%/yr)     │
   │   Stalled: "Reformer" (2020, citations plateaued)       │
   │                                                          │
   │ [Deep Dive: Key Players] [Deep Dive: Vulnerabilities]   │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

4. User clicks [Deep Dive: Vulnerabilities]. Athena generates a longer
   analysis of the top 3 bottleneck papers and what would survive if each
   were removed.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Briefing persists in chat. Deep dive buttons trigger follow-up responses
in the conversation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-commands.js: /radar command handler. Triggers backend scan.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: RadarBriefingBlock renderer with five sections.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Radar briefing styles, bar chart, dot-matrix headers.
- app.py: /api/athena/radar endpoint. Runs five analyses on current graph.
- backend/graph_engine.py: Exposes structural_summary(), key_players(),
  mutation_landscape(), vulnerability_report(), frontier_assessment().

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/radar', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_radar():
      graph = load_current_graph(session_id)
      briefing = {
          'structure': graph.structural_summary(),
          'key_players': graph.key_players(top_n=5),
          'mutations': graph.mutation_landscape(),
          'vulnerability': graph.vulnerability_report(top_n=3),
          'frontier': graph.frontier_assessment()
      }
      return jsonify({'briefing': briefing})

  # In graph_engine.py:
  def key_players(self, top_n=5):
      ranked = sorted(self.pagerank_scores.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:top_n]
      return [{
          'paper_id': pid,
          'title': self.papers[pid].title,
          'pagerank': round(score, 4),
          'role': self._classify_role(pid)
      } for pid, score in ranked]

  def _classify_role(self, paper_id):
      in_deg = self.G.in_degree(paper_id)
      out_deg = self.G.out_degree(paper_id)
      if in_deg > 5 and out_deg > 5: return 'Hub'
      if in_deg > 3 and out_deg > 3: return 'Bridge'
      if out_deg == 0: return 'Leaf'
      if in_deg == 0: return 'Foundation'
      return 'Node'

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/radar
Request: { "graph_id": "..." }
Response: { "briefing": { "structure": {...}, "key_players": [...],
  "mutations": {...}, "vulnerability": [...], "frontier": [...] } }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Radar Briefing

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┌─ RADAR SCAN ─────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ ● STRUCTURE                                      │     │
  │  │   152 papers, 203 edges, 7 clusters, depth 3     │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ ● KEY PLAYERS                                     │     │
  │  │   (top 5 by PageRank with role badges)            │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ ● MUTATIONS                                       │     │
  │  │   (horizontal bar chart of mutation distribution)  │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ ● VULNERABILITY                                   │     │
  │  │   (top 3 bottleneck papers with impact %)          │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ ◐ FRONTIER                                        │     │
  │  │   (leaf analysis, active vs stalled)              │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ [Deep Dive: Players] [Deep Dive: Vulnerabilities] │     │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Sections appear sequentially (stagger 100ms per section).
Bar chart bars grow from left (200ms per bar).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User types /radar
  -> Frontend sends POST /api/athena/radar with graph_id
  -> Backend runs 5 analysis passes on the graph
  -> Returns structured briefing JSON
  -> Frontend renders RadarBriefingBlock with 5 sections
  -> Deep dive buttons trigger follow-up /api/athena/chat messages

ERROR PATH: No graph loaded -> "Load a graph first to run a radar scan."
DATA STORED: Briefing JSON in chat_history.
DATA CACHED: Briefing in block instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

STAGE: Post-analysis characterization only. The five analyses are computed
from graph data (no LLM needed for numbers). The LLM generates one-line
characterizations for the structure summary and frontier assessment.

PROMPT:
```
Characterize this research lineage in one sentence.
{node_count} papers, {edge_count} edges, {cluster_count} clusters, depth {depth}.
Top paper: {top_paper_title} (PageRank {top_pr}).
Dominant mutation: {dominant_mutation} ({dominant_pct}%).
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: Single sentence characterization
FALLBACK: Generic "A research lineage with {n} papers across {c} clusters."
TOKEN BUDGET: ~100 tokens per scan

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element             | Action | Response                                    |
|---------------------|--------|---------------------------------------------|
| Deep Dive button    | Click  | Triggers detailed follow-up in conversation |
| Paper name          | Click  | Highlights paper node in graph              |
| Bar chart segment   | Hover  | Tooltip with exact count and percentage     |
| Section header (●)  | Hover  | Tooltip: "Complete data" or "Partial data"  |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Sections stagger in 100ms each. Bars grow 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Briefing uses role="region" with section headings. Bar chart
  values in aria-label. Screen reader: "Radar scan complete. 5 sections."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Very small graph (3 papers)
   Expected: Radar scan still runs but sections are abbreviated. "Key Players"
   shows all 3 papers. "Vulnerability" shows all 3. "Frontier" may be empty.
   Implementation: Adapt top_n to min(top_n, graph_size).

2. Scenario: No precomputed pruning available
   Expected: Vulnerability section shows ◐ (partial) with note: "Pruning data
   not yet computed. Run full build to enable vulnerability analysis."
   Implementation: Check precomputed_pruning existence.

3. Scenario: Graph still building (SSE in progress)
   Expected: "Graph is still building. Run /radar after build completes."
   Implementation: Check build_jobs status before running scan.

4. Scenario: User runs /radar twice in a row
   Expected: Second scan runs fresh (data may have changed if user navigated).
   No caching of radar results between commands.
   Implementation: Always compute fresh.

5. Scenario: Graph has no mutation data (all edges "incidental")
   Expected: Mutation landscape shows 100% incidental with note: "Limited NLP
   analysis. Most edges classified as incidental."
   Implementation: Display actual distribution regardless.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON:
  #001 -- Context-Aware Responses (graph state access)
  #005 -- Graph-Aware Commands (slash command infrastructure)
  #032 -- Paper Recommendation Engine (frontier assessment logic)

ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: New user orientation, quick graph comprehension
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Reuses graph_engine computed data (PageRank, pruning, clusters).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] /radar command triggers 5-section briefing
[ ] Structure summary with counts and one-line characterization
[ ] Key Players: top 5 by PageRank with role classification
[ ] Mutation Landscape: horizontal bar chart with percentages
[ ] Vulnerability: top 3 bottleneck papers with cascade percentages
[ ] Frontier: leaf node analysis (active vs stalled)
[ ] Dot-matrix coverage indicators (● ◐ ○)
[ ] Deep Dive buttons trigger follow-up conversation

VISUAL:
[ ] Dark theme briefing block with section headers
[ ] Bar chart with --accent-gold bars
[ ] Staggered section appearance animation

PERFORMANCE: [ ] Full scan within 3 seconds for 600-node graph
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Section headings, bar values in aria-label, keyboard nav
EDGE CASES: [ ] Small graph, missing pruning data, in-progress graph

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-commands.js, athena-blocks.js (radar), graph_engine.py (analysis methods), app.py endpoint 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST /radar command, verify 5 sections, check data accuracy 4. CHECK dot-matrix indicators match data availability 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase E. Slash command. Aggregates 5 analytical signals into one briefing.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #149 Heatmap Overlay (P3, H -- shorter)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #149: HEATMAP OVERLAY
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 18
OVERLAPS WITH:   #025 (Sentiment Heatmap), #012 (AI Annotations on Graph)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #004 (Discuss Integration), #012 (AI Annotations on Graph),
                 #025 (Sentiment Heatmap)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Heatmap Overlay allows Athena to project computed metrics onto the graph as
color intensity gradients. When a user asks "highlight the most-cited papers"
or "show me where the bottlenecks are," Athena overlays a heatmap on the graph
where node color intensity maps to the requested metric.

Available heatmap metrics: citation count, PageRank centrality, pruning impact
percentage, text tier (full text availability), mutation diversity (number of
distinct mutation types on adjacent edges), and cluster cohesion score. Each
metric maps to a color gradient from cool (--accent-blue at low values) through
warm (--danger at high values).

The overlay is toggled by Athena in response to a chat message. The user can
also type /heatmap [metric] as a slash command. The overlay co-exists with the
base graph colors and can be toggled off by clicking "Clear Overlay" or asking
Athena to remove it.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User asks "show me where the bottlenecks are" or types /heatmap pruning_impact.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User: "Highlight the most structurally important papers."
2. Athena: "Overlaying PageRank heatmap..." (500ms).
3. Graph nodes gain color intensity proportional to their PageRank. High-PR
   nodes glow warm (red/orange). Low-PR nodes stay cool (blue/dim).
4. A legend appears in the graph panel corner: gradient bar with min/max labels.
5. User: "Clear the heatmap." Nodes return to default field-of-study colors.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Heatmap overlay active until cleared. Legend visible while overlay is on.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/graph.js: Heatmap overlay renderer. Node color override based
  on metric values. Legend rendering.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Heatmap intent detection, metric selection.
- static/css/graph.css: Heatmap node styles, legend, gradient transitions.
- app.py: /api/athena/heatmap returns metric values per node.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class HeatmapOverlay {
    apply(graph, metric, values) {
      const min = Math.min(...Object.values(values));
      const max = Math.max(...Object.values(values));
      graph.nodes.forEach(node => {
        const normalized = (values[node.paper_id] - min) / (max - min || 1);
        node.heatmapColor = interpolateColor('#3B82F6', '#EF4444', normalized);
      });
      this.showLegend(metric, min, max);
    }
    clear(graph) { graph.nodes.forEach(n => delete n.heatmapColor); this.hideLegend(); }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/heatmap', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_heatmap():
      metric = request.json['metric']
      graph = load_current_graph(session_id)
      values = graph.compute_metric_map(metric)  # {paper_id: float}
      return jsonify({'metric': metric, 'values': values})

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/heatmap
Request: { "metric": "pagerank" }
Response: { "metric": "pagerank", "values": {"paper_id_1": 0.087, ...} }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Heatmap Active

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  [Graph with node colors overridden by heatmap]      │
  │                                                      │
  │   ○ dim nodes (low metric) ... ● bright nodes (high) │
  │                                                      │
  │  ┌─ PageRank Heatmap ──────┐                         │
  │  │ Low [blue ━━━━━━ red] High                        │
  │  │ 0.001         0.087                               │
  │  └─────────────────────────┘                         │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Node colors crossfade from field colors to heatmap (400ms). Legend fades in (200ms).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User requests heatmap
  -> Backend computes metric values per node
  -> Returns {paper_id: value} map
  -> Frontend overrides node colors with gradient
  -> Legend displays metric name and range
  -> User clears -> colors revert to field-of-study defaults

ERROR PATH: Metric unavailable -> "Metric not computed for this graph."
DATA STORED: None (overlay is ephemeral). DATA CACHED: Metric values in overlay instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (for metric selection from natural language only)
PROMPT: "Map this request to a metric: {user_message}. Options: citation_count, pagerank, pruning_impact, text_tier, mutation_diversity."
OUTPUT FORMAT: Single metric name. FALLBACK: Default to pagerank.
TOKEN BUDGET: ~50 tokens.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element        | Action | Response                                       |
|----------------|--------|------------------------------------------------|
| Heatmap node   | Hover  | Tooltip shows metric name and value            |
| Legend          | Hover  | Legend expands with metric description          |
| Clear overlay  | Click  | Removes heatmap, restores default colors       |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Color crossfade 400ms, legend fade 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Legend has alt text. Heatmap values in node tooltips. Color
  changes do not remove shape-based field indicators.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: All nodes have the same metric value
   Expected: All nodes same color. Legend shows single value.
   Implementation: Normalize handles max==min gracefully.

2. Scenario: Heatmap requested with no graph loaded
   Expected: "Load a graph first to apply a heatmap overlay."

3. Scenario: User applies two heatmaps in sequence
   Expected: Second replaces first. Only one overlay at a time.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #004 (Discuss Integration), #012 (AI Annotations), #025 (Sentiment Heatmap)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Visual graph exploration, metric comprehension
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: D3.js color interpolation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Heatmap overlay applied from chat or /heatmap command
[ ] 6 metrics supported (citations, PageRank, pruning, text_tier, mutation diversity, cohesion)
[ ] Legend with gradient bar and min/max values
[ ] Clear overlay restores default colors

VISUAL: [ ] Smooth color crossfade (400ms), legend corner placement
PERFORMANCE: [ ] Overlay applies within 200ms for 600 nodes
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Legend alt text, metric values in tooltips, shapes preserved
EDGE CASES: [ ] Same-value nodes, no graph, sequential overlays

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ graph.js (overlay), athena-engine.js, CSS 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST each metric, clear, re-apply 4. CHECK color accuracy 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase H. Graph integration. Color overlay on nodes by metric.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #150 Paper Neighborhood Explorer
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #150: PAPER NEIGHBORHOOD EXPLORER
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Graph Integration
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           C
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #004 (Discuss Integration), #008 (Pathfinder Integration)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #004 (Discuss Integration)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Paper Neighborhood Explorer gives Athena deep awareness of the local
neighborhood around any paper in the graph. When a user clicks a paper node
or mentions a paper by name, Athena can analyze not just that paper but its
immediate citation neighborhood: which papers cite it, which it cites, what
mutation types connect it to its neighbors, and what structural role it plays
in the local subgraph.

This feature solves the problem of understanding a paper in context. Looking
at a single node in the graph tells you almost nothing. But looking at its
1-hop neighborhood reveals its entire intellectual relationship structure:
which ideas it inherited (incoming edges), which ideas it spawned (outgoing
edges), whether it is a hub (many connections), a bridge (connecting two
clusters), or an isolated leaf.

When activated, Athena generates a structured neighborhood report:

  PAPER: "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017)

  ANCESTRY (papers it cites in this lineage):
    "Sequence to Sequence" (adoption, HIGH confidence)
    "Neural Machine Translation" (generalization, MEDIUM confidence)
    3 more ancestors...

  DESCENDANTS (papers citing it in this lineage):
    "BERT" (specialization, HIGH confidence)
    "GPT-2" (adoption, HIGH confidence)
    "Vision Transformer" (generalization, MEDIUM confidence)
    12 more descendants...

  STRUCTURAL ROLE:
    Type: Hub (8 incoming, 15 outgoing edges)
    PageRank: 0.087 (rank 1 of 152)
    Cluster: Transformer Architecture
    Pruning Impact: 31% of lineage collapses if removed

  MUTATION PROFILE:
    Outgoing: 40% adoption, 27% generalization, 20% specialization, 13% other
    Incoming: 67% adoption, 33% generalization

The report is interactive: clicking any ancestor or descendant paper name
highlights that paper in the graph and scrolls the graph view to center on it.
The mutation profile is a compact bar chart matching the style from #148 Radar
Scan.

This feature also enables the "neighborhood walk" pattern. The user reads
about a paper's neighborhood, clicks on a descendant, and Athena automatically
generates that paper's neighborhood report. This creates a guided exploration
flow through the graph that is more structured than free-form clicking.

The backend extracts neighborhood data directly from the NetworkX graph using
G.predecessors(node) and G.successors(node), enriching each neighbor with
its edge analysis data (mutation type, confidence, citation intent) from the
edge_analysis entries stored during graph build.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User clicks a paper node in the graph and Athena automatically offers
neighborhood analysis. Or user asks: "Tell me about Attention Is All You Need's
connections" or "What papers cite BERT?" Or types /neighborhood [paper name].

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User clicks the "Attention Is All You Need" node in the graph.

2. Athena detects the click via the Discuss Integration (#004) event bridge.
   It automatically generates a neighborhood report:

   ┌─ Neighborhood: Attention Is All You Need ──────────────┐
   │                                                          │
   │ ANCESTRY (cites in lineage): 5 papers                   │
   │   ● Seq2Seq (Sutskever 2014)                            │
   │     adoption, HIGH confidence                            │
   │   ● Neural MT (Bahdanau 2014)                            │
   │     generalization, MEDIUM confidence                    │
   │   ○ 3 more...  [Expand]                                 │
   │                                                          │
   │ DESCENDANTS (cited by in lineage): 15 papers             │
   │   ● BERT (Devlin 2018)                                   │
   │     specialization, HIGH confidence                      │
   │   ● GPT-2 (Radford 2019)                                │
   │     adoption, HIGH confidence                            │
   │   ● Vision Transformer (Dosovitskiy 2020)                │
   │     generalization, MEDIUM confidence                    │
   │   ○ 12 more...  [Expand]                                │
   │                                                          │
   │ STRUCTURAL ROLE                                          │
   │   Hub (8 in, 15 out) | PR: 0.087 (#1) | Cluster: Transf│
   │   Pruning: 31% cascade                                  │
   │                                                          │
   │ MUTATION PROFILE (outgoing)                              │
   │   adoption      [━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━   ] 40%              │
   │   generalization[━━━━━━━━━━          ] 27%              │
   │   specialization[━━━━━━━━            ] 20%              │
   │   other         [━━━━━               ] 13%              │
   │                                                          │
   │ [Explore Ancestry] [Explore Descendants] [Compare]       │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

3. User clicks "BERT" in the descendants list. The graph pans to center on
   BERT. Athena generates BERT's neighborhood report in the next message.

4. User clicks [Explore Ancestry]. Athena expands the ancestry section to
   show all 5 ancestor papers with full mutation details.

5. User clicks [Compare]. Athena compares the current paper with the most
   similar neighbor (by cosine similarity from edge_analysis).

AFTER COMPLETION:
Neighborhood report persists in chat. Clicking papers in the report triggers
graph navigation and optionally generates new neighborhood reports (the
"neighborhood walk" pattern).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: NeighborhoodBlock renderer with ancestry,
  descendants, structural role, mutation profile sections.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Neighborhood request handler, click-to-explore
  event bridge, neighborhood walk state tracking.
- static/js/graph.js: Pan-to-node method for clicking paper names in report.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Neighborhood block styles, bar chart, expand.
- app.py: /api/athena/neighborhood endpoint returning structured neighborhood.
- backend/graph_engine.py: neighborhood_report(paper_id) method.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  def neighborhood_report(self, paper_id):
      paper = self.papers.get(paper_id)
      if not paper:
          return None

      ancestors = []
      for pred_id in self.G.predecessors(paper_id):
          edge_key = f"{paper_id}:{pred_id}"
          edge = self.edge_analyses.get(edge_key)
          ancestors.append({
              'paper_id': pred_id,
              'title': self.papers[pred_id].title,
              'year': self.papers[pred_id].year,
              'mutation_type': edge.mutation_type if edge else 'unknown',
              'confidence_tier': edge.confidence_tier if edge else 'SPECULATIVE',
          })

      descendants = []
      for succ_id in self.G.successors(paper_id):
          edge_key = f"{succ_id}:{paper_id}"
          edge = self.edge_analyses.get(edge_key)
          descendants.append({
              'paper_id': succ_id,
              'title': self.papers[succ_id].title,
              'year': self.papers[succ_id].year,
              'mutation_type': edge.mutation_type if edge else 'unknown',
              'confidence_tier': edge.confidence_tier if edge else 'SPECULATIVE',
          })

      # Structural role
      in_deg = self.G.in_degree(paper_id)
      out_deg = self.G.out_degree(paper_id)
      role = 'Hub' if in_deg > 5 and out_deg > 5 else 'Bridge' if in_deg > 3 and out_deg > 3 else 'Leaf' if out_deg == 0 else 'Foundation' if in_deg == 0 else 'Node'

      # Mutation profile
      outgoing_mutations = {}
      for succ_id in self.G.successors(paper_id):
          edge_key = f"{succ_id}:{paper_id}"
          edge = self.edge_analyses.get(edge_key)
          if edge:
              mt = edge.mutation_type
              outgoing_mutations[mt] = outgoing_mutations.get(mt, 0) + 1

      total = sum(outgoing_mutations.values()) or 1
      mutation_profile = {k: round(v/total*100) for k, v in outgoing_mutations.items()}

      pruning = self.precomputed_pruning.get(paper_id, {})

      return {
          'paper': {'paper_id': paper_id, 'title': paper.title, 'year': paper.year, 'authors': paper.authors},
          'ancestors': sorted(ancestors, key=lambda a: a['year'] or 0),
          'descendants': sorted(descendants, key=lambda d: d['year'] or 0),
          'role': {'type': role, 'in_degree': in_deg, 'out_degree': out_deg},
          'pagerank': round(self.pagerank_scores.get(paper_id, 0), 4),
          'pagerank_rank': self._pagerank_rank(paper_id),
          'cluster': self.dna_clusters.get(paper_id, 'Unassigned'),
          'pruning_impact': pruning.get('impact_percentage', 0),
          'mutation_profile': mutation_profile,
      }

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/neighborhood
Request: { "paper_id": "204e3073870fae3d05bcbc2f6a8e263d9b72e776" }
Response: { "neighborhood": { "paper": {...}, "ancestors": [...],
  "descendants": [...], "role": {...}, ... } }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Neighborhood Report

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┌─ Neighborhood: Attention Is All You Need ────────┐     │
  │  │ ANCESTRY (5 papers)                               │     │
  │  │   ● Seq2Seq | adoption | HIGH                     │     │
  │  │   ● Neural MT | generalization | MEDIUM            │     │
  │  │   ○ 3 more...  [Expand]                           │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ DESCENDANTS (15 papers)                           │     │
  │  │   ● BERT | specialization | HIGH                  │     │
  │  │   ● GPT-2 | adoption | HIGH                      │     │
  │  │   ○ 12 more...  [Expand]                         │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ ROLE: Hub (8 in, 15 out)  PR: 0.087 (#1)         │     │
  │  │ Cluster: Transformer  Pruning: 31%                │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ MUTATIONS (outgoing)                              │     │
  │  │   adoption      [━━━━━━━━━━━━━   ] 40%           │     │
  │  │   generalization[━━━━━━━━        ] 27%            │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │ [Explore Ancestry] [Explore Desc] [Compare]       │     │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Expanded Ancestry

  │ ANCESTRY (5 papers -- expanded)                          │
  │   ● Seq2Seq (Sutskever 2014) | adoption | HIGH          │
  │   ● Neural MT (Bahdanau 2014) | generalization | MEDIUM  │
  │   ● Embedding Layer (2013) | adoption | HIGH             │
  │   ● Encoder-Decoder (2014) | generalization | LOW        │
  │   ● Positional Encoding (2017) | hybridization | MEDIUM  │

TRANSITIONS: Expand: list items slide in sequentially (50ms stagger per item).
Graph pan: smooth 500ms animation to center on clicked paper.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User clicks node or asks about paper
  -> athena-engine.js detects paper reference
  -> POST /api/athena/neighborhood with paper_id
  -> Backend extracts 1-hop neighborhood from NetworkX graph
  -> Enriches with edge_analysis data, PageRank, pruning
  -> Returns structured neighborhood report
  -> Frontend renders NeighborhoodBlock
  -> User clicks paper name in report
  -> Graph pans to that paper
  -> Optionally: new neighborhood report generated (walk pattern)

ERROR PATH: Paper not in graph -> "Paper not found in this lineage."
DATA STORED: Neighborhood report in chat_history.
DATA CACHED: Report in block instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

NOT APPLICABLE. Neighborhood data is entirely computed from graph structure.
No LLM call needed. All data comes from NetworkX graph and edge_analysis cache.

(The one exception: if the user asks a follow-up question about the
neighborhood in natural language, the regular chat pipeline handles it with
the neighborhood report as context.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element             | Action | Response                                    |
|---------------------|--------|---------------------------------------------|
| Paper name          | Click  | Pan graph to that paper, highlight node     |
| Expand link         | Click  | Show remaining papers in section            |
| Explore Ancestry    | Click  | Full ancestry list with details             |
| Explore Descendants | Click  | Full descendants list with details          |
| Compare button      | Click  | Compare this paper with nearest neighbor    |
| Mutation bar        | Hover  | Tooltip with count and percentage           |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Expand 50ms stagger per item. Graph pan 500ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Lists use role="list" with role="listitem". Mutation bar values
  in aria-label. Paper names are links (role="link"). Screen reader: "Paper
  neighborhood for Attention Is All You Need. 5 ancestors, 15 descendants."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Paper has no ancestors (root/seed paper)
   Expected: "ANCESTRY: None (seed paper). This is the origin of the lineage."
   Implementation: Empty ancestors list handled with explanatory text.

2. Scenario: Paper has no descendants (leaf node)
   Expected: "DESCENDANTS: None (frontier paper). This paper has not yet been
   cited by other papers in this lineage."
   Implementation: Empty descendants list with frontier label.

3. Scenario: Paper has 50+ descendants (very influential paper)
   Expected: Show top 5 by PageRank, remaining collapsed. "45 more... [Expand]"
   Implementation: Default collapsed with top 5 visible.

4. Scenario: Edge analysis missing for some neighbors
   Expected: Show "unknown" for mutation type, "SPECULATIVE" for confidence.
   Implementation: Graceful defaults in neighborhood_report().

5. Scenario: User rapidly clicks 5 different nodes
   Expected: Each generates a neighborhood report. Chat shows all 5 in sequence.
   The most recent report is visible; others are scrollable.
   Implementation: No debounce on neighborhood generation (each is fast).

6. Scenario: Neighborhood walk creates very long chat
   Expected: Each report is a separate message. Scroll maintains position on
   current report. Previous reports are scrollable above.
   Implementation: Standard chat message flow.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON:
  #001 -- Context-Aware Responses (graph state access)
  #004 -- Discuss Integration (click event bridge from graph to chat)

ENABLES: NONE (but heavily used by researchers as primary exploration method)

ENHANCES:
  #008 -- Pathfinder Integration (path through neighborhoods)
  #026 -- Paper Relationship Explainer (neighborhood as context)
  #148 -- Radar Scan (per-paper version of the scan)

CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: NetworkX predecessors/successors API.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Neighborhood report generated from paper click or chat request
[ ] Ancestry section with mutation types and confidence tiers
[ ] Descendants section with mutation types and confidence tiers
[ ] Structural role classification (Hub, Bridge, Leaf, Foundation)
[ ] PageRank rank and pruning impact
[ ] Mutation profile bar chart
[ ] Click paper name to pan graph
[ ] Expand collapsed lists
[ ] Neighborhood walk pattern (click descendant -> new report)

VISUAL:
[ ] Dot-matrix indicators (● visible, ○ collapsed)
[ ] Mutation bar chart with Arivu colors
[ ] Compact report fits in chat panel width

PERFORMANCE:
[ ] Neighborhood extraction within 200ms (graph query only)
[ ] Full report render within 300ms

ACCESSIBILITY:
[ ] Lists with proper roles
[ ] Paper names as links
[ ] Bar values in aria-label
[ ] Screen reader summary of neighborhood

EDGE CASES:
[ ] No ancestors (seed paper)
[ ] No descendants (leaf)
[ ] 50+ descendants collapsed
[ ] Missing edge analysis data
[ ] Rapid node clicking

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-blocks.js (neighborhood), graph_engine.py (neighborhood_report), graph.js (pan), app.py endpoint
2. COMPARE against all Done Criteria
3. TEST: click node, verify ancestry/descendants, click paper name, verify graph pan, test walk pattern
4. CHECK mutation types match edge_analysis data
5. VERIFY no regression in click handling, Discuss Integration
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase C. P1 priority. Core exploration feature. Entirely graph-computed, no LLM.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #151 Screenshot Analysis
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #151: SCREENSHOT ANALYSIS
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Input Methods
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           D
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #152 (Whiteboard Input), #153 (Table Paste)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Screenshot Analysis allows users to paste or drop a screenshot into the Athena
chat input and have Athena analyze the image in the context of the current
research lineage. The user might screenshot a figure from a paper, a diagram
from a slide deck, or a table from a PDF, and Athena extracts meaning from
the visual content and relates it to the graph.

This feature bridges the gap between visual content and Athena's text-based
intelligence. A researcher reading a paper encounters a complex architecture
diagram. Rather than describing it in words, they screenshot it, paste it
into Athena, and ask "How does this architecture relate to the papers in my
lineage?" Athena uses vision-capable LLM analysis (via the Groq multimodal
endpoint or a base64-encoded prompt approach) to describe the image content,
then cross-references with the graph.

The input flow supports three methods: Ctrl+V paste from clipboard, drag-and-drop
from the desktop, and a file picker triggered by an image icon button in the
input toolbar. Accepted formats: PNG, JPEG, WebP. Maximum size: 5MB. Images
are resized client-side to 1024px max dimension before sending to the backend
to control payload size and LLM token cost.

The backend receives the image as base64, sends it to the LLM with a system
prompt that includes the graph summary context, and returns a text analysis.
If multimodal capability is unavailable, the system falls back to OCR-only
mode: extracting text from the image (using PyMuPDF's image-to-text or a
basic OCR library) and analyzing the extracted text.

Each screenshot message appears in the chat as a thumbnail preview (200px max
width) above the user's optional text message. Athena's response references
the image content and connects it to specific papers in the lineage.

The feature is intentionally limited to analysis, not generation. Athena does
not produce images. It reads and interprets user-provided images in research
context.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User pastes an image (Ctrl+V), drags an image file into the chat area, or
clicks the image icon in the input toolbar to select a file.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User copies a figure from a research paper showing a transformer architecture
   diagram. They press Ctrl+V in the Athena chat input.

2. A thumbnail preview appears above the input area: the pasted image at 200px
   width with a small "x" to remove it. The input placeholder changes to:
   "Ask about this image..."

3. User types: "How does this architecture compare to the attention mechanism
   in my lineage?" and presses Enter.

4. The message appears in chat: thumbnail image above, user text below.

5. Athena analyzes the image: "Analyzing image..." (2-3 seconds). The response:
   "This diagram shows a multi-head attention mechanism with 8 heads feeding
   into a concatenation layer. This is the architecture described in 'Attention
   Is All You Need' (Vaswani 2017), which is the seed paper of your lineage
   with PageRank 0.087. The key difference from the original: this diagram
   shows cross-attention between encoder and decoder, which was generalized
   by 'BERT' (2018) into self-attention only."

6. Athena includes action buttons: [Highlight in Graph] to highlight the
   referenced papers.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Image and analysis persist in chat history. Thumbnail remains visible on scroll.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Image paste handler, drag-and-drop handler,
  base64 encoding, client-side resize, preview thumbnail.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Image message renderer (thumbnail + text).
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Image preview styles, drag overlay, thumbnail.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat accepts multipart/form-data with image field.
  Sends base64 image to LLM with graph context.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // Paste handler
  chatInput.addEventListener('paste', async (e) => {
    const items = e.clipboardData.items;
    for (const item of items) {
      if (item.type.startsWith('image/')) {
        const file = item.getAsFile();
        if (file.size > 5 * 1024 * 1024) {
          showToast('Image must be under 5MB'); return;
        }
        const resized = await resizeImage(file, 1024);
        showImagePreview(resized);
        athenaEngine.pendingImage = resized;
      }
    }
  });

  async function resizeImage(file, maxDim) {
    const img = await createImageBitmap(file);
    const scale = Math.min(1, maxDim / Math.max(img.width, img.height));
    const canvas = document.createElement('canvas');
    canvas.width = img.width * scale;
    canvas.height = img.height * scale;
    canvas.getContext('2d').drawImage(img, 0, 0, canvas.width, canvas.height);
    return canvas.toDataURL('image/jpeg', 0.85);
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/chat', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_chat():
      image_b64 = request.json.get('image')
      message = request.json.get('message', '')
      if image_b64:
          # Build multimodal prompt
          graph_summary = get_graph_summary_for_chat(current_graph)
          analysis = llm_client.analyze_image(image_b64, message, graph_summary)
          return jsonify({'response': analysis, 'blocks': []})

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Request: { "message": "How does this compare?", "image": "data:image/jpeg;base64,..." }
Response: { "response": "This diagram shows...", "blocks": [] }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Image Preview (before send)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌──────────────┐                                        │
  │  │  [thumbnail]  │ x                                     │
  │  │  (200px max)  │                                       │
  │  └──────────────┘                                        │
  │  [Ask about this image...                         ] [->] │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Image Message in Chat

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ○ You                                                    │
  │  ┌──────────────┐                                        │
  │  │  [thumbnail]  │                                       │
  │  └──────────────┘                                        │
  │  How does this architecture compare to the attention      │
  │  mechanism in my lineage?                                 │
  │                                                           │
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │  This diagram shows a multi-head attention mechanism...   │
  │  [Highlight in Graph]                                     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Drag Overlay

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
  │  │                                                 │      │
  │  │         Drop image here to analyze              │      │
  │  │                                                 │      │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  Dashed gold border, semi-transparent overlay.

TRANSITIONS: Preview: thumbnail slides up (200ms). Drag overlay: fades in (150ms).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User pastes/drops image
  -> Client-side resize to 1024px max dimension
  -> Preview thumbnail shown above input
  -> User types optional text message and sends
  -> Image as base64 + text sent to /api/athena/chat
  -> Backend sends image + graph summary to multimodal LLM
  -> LLM analyzes image content in research context
  -> Response text returned and rendered

ERROR PATH:
  -> Image too large (>5MB) -> "Image must be under 5MB. Please resize."
  -> Unsupported format -> "Only PNG, JPEG, and WebP images are supported."
  -> LLM cannot process image -> fallback to OCR text extraction
  -> No graph loaded -> analyze image without graph context

DATA STORED: Image base64 (compressed) in chat_history. Analysis text in response.
DATA CACHED: Thumbnail in DOM.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (for vision analysis, needs 70b quality)

PROMPT:
```
You are Athena, research intelligence assistant for Arivu.
The user has shared an image from their research context.
Graph context: {graph_summary}

Analyze this image and explain:
1. What the image shows (diagram, table, figure, etc.)
2. How it relates to papers in the user's current lineage
3. Which specific papers in the lineage are most relevant

User's question: {user_message}
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: Natural language analysis with paper references
FALLBACK: If vision unavailable, attempt OCR extraction and analyze text only.
  If OCR also fails: "I cannot process this image. Try describing it in text."
TOKEN BUDGET: ~500 tokens per image analysis

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element             | Action    | Response                                  |
|---------------------|-----------|-------------------------------------------|
| Chat input area     | Ctrl+V    | Image paste captured, preview shown       |
| Chat input area     | Drag file | Drag overlay appears, drop triggers paste |
| Image icon button   | Click     | File picker opens (PNG, JPEG, WebP)       |
| Preview "x" button  | Click     | Remove pending image                      |
| Thumbnail in chat   | Click     | Expand image to full size in modal        |
| Highlight in Graph  | Click     | Highlight referenced papers in graph      |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Preview slide up 200ms. Drag overlay fade 150ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Image preview has alt text "Pasted image preview." Thumbnail in
  chat has alt="User-provided image." Drag zone has aria-label="Drop image to analyze."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User pastes text, not image
   Expected: Normal text paste behavior. No image handling triggered.
   Implementation: Check clipboardData.items for image/ MIME type.

2. Scenario: User pastes image with no text message
   Expected: Image sent alone. Athena analyzes without additional context.
   Prompt includes: "The user shared an image without additional text."
   Implementation: Allow empty message when image is present.

3. Scenario: Very small image (icon, favicon)
   Expected: "This image is too small to analyze meaningfully. Please share a
   larger screenshot."
   Implementation: Minimum dimension check (100px x 100px).

4. Scenario: Screenshot of Arivu's own interface
   Expected: Athena recognizes graph visualizations and can identify papers
   visible in the screenshot. (Best effort, not guaranteed.)
   Implementation: No special handling; LLM may or may not recognize.

5. Scenario: Non-research image (cat photo, meme)
   Expected: "This does not appear to be a research-related image. I can
   best help with figures, diagrams, tables, and text from research papers."
   Implementation: LLM detects non-research content and declines.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #152 (Whiteboard Input), general research workflow
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Groq multimodal API (or fallback OCR via PyMuPDF).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Ctrl+V paste captures images from clipboard
[ ] Drag-and-drop image files accepted
[ ] File picker via image icon button
[ ] Client-side resize to 1024px max dimension
[ ] Thumbnail preview before send
[ ] Image analysis with graph context
[ ] Paper references in analysis linked to graph nodes
[ ] Fallback to OCR when vision unavailable

VISUAL:
[ ] Thumbnail preview above input (200px max width)
[ ] Drag overlay with dashed gold border
[ ] Image message in chat with thumbnail + text

PERFORMANCE:
[ ] Client-side resize within 200ms
[ ] Image analysis within 5 seconds

ACCESSIBILITY:
[ ] Alt text on previews and thumbnails
[ ] Drag zone labeled
[ ] Analysis text is screen-reader friendly

EDGE CASES:
[ ] Text paste not intercepted
[ ] Image-only send (no text)
[ ] Too-small image rejection
[ ] Non-research image handling
[ ] >5MB rejection

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-engine.js (paste/drop handlers), app.py (image endpoint), CSS 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST paste, drag, file picker, various image types 4. CHECK resize output, base64 size 5. VERIFY no regression in text-only chat 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase D. Input method. Vision LLM or OCR fallback.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #152 Whiteboard Input (P3, D -- shorter)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #152: WHITEBOARD INPUT
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Input Methods
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           D
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #151 (Screenshot Analysis), #142 (Mind Map Generator)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #151 (Screenshot Analysis), #001 (Context-Aware Responses)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Whiteboard Input adds a small canvas drawing area above the chat input where
users can sketch diagrams, draw relationship arrows, or write freehand notes.
The sketch is analyzed by Athena as visual input, similar to Screenshot Analysis
(#151) but with a native drawing surface instead of pasted images.

This targets the common researcher workflow of sketching a mental model on paper
and then wanting to compare it against the actual lineage structure. The user
draws boxes for papers, arrows for relationships, and Athena interprets the
sketch as a proposed research structure, comparing it against the real graph.

The canvas uses a minimal drawing toolkit: pen (freehand), rectangle, arrow,
text, eraser, and color picker (limited to 5 Arivu palette colors). Drawings
are exported as PNG and processed identically to Screenshot Analysis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User clicks the whiteboard icon in the input toolbar. A 300px-tall canvas
appears above the chat input.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User clicks the whiteboard icon. Canvas appears with minimal toolbar.
2. User draws three boxes labeled "ResNet," "VGG," "AlexNet" with arrows.
3. User types: "Is this relationship correct?" and sends.
4. Canvas content captured as PNG, sent to /api/athena/chat with image field.
5. Athena analyzes the sketch and responds: "Your sketch shows ResNet depending
   on both VGG and AlexNet. In the actual lineage, ResNet cites AlexNet
   (adoption, HIGH) but does not directly cite VGG. VGG and ResNet share a
   common ancestor in LeNet."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Sketch thumbnail persists in chat. Canvas closes after send.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-whiteboard.js: Canvas drawing toolkit. Pen, rectangle,
  arrow, text, eraser, color picker. Export to PNG.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Whiteboard toggle, PNG capture, send with image.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Canvas styles, toolbar, toggle animation.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaWhiteboard {
    constructor(container) {
      this.canvas = document.createElement('canvas');
      this.ctx = this.canvas.getContext('2d');
      this.tools = ['pen', 'rect', 'arrow', 'text', 'eraser'];
      this.activeTool = 'pen';
    }
    exportPNG() { return this.canvas.toDataURL('image/png'); }
    clear() { this.ctx.clearRect(0, 0, this.canvas.width, this.canvas.height); }
  }

BACKEND: Same as #151 (Screenshot Analysis). Image sent as base64 to LLM.

API CONTRACTS: Same as #151 with image field in /api/athena/chat.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Whiteboard Open

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  (Chat messages above)                                    │
  │  ┌─ Whiteboard ─────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ [Pen] [Rect] [Arrow] [Text] [Eraser] [Color] [x] │    │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  │  (300px drawing canvas)                           │     │
  │  │                                                   │     │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  │  [Describe your sketch...                         ] [->]  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Canvas slides down (200ms). Tools fade in (100ms stagger).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User draws on canvas -> User sends message -> Canvas exported as PNG
  -> Sent as base64 with text to /api/athena/chat -> LLM analyzes sketch
  -> Response references papers in lineage -> Canvas closes after send

ERROR PATH: Empty canvas sent -> "The whiteboard is empty. Draw something first."
DATA STORED: Sketch PNG in chat_history. DATA CACHED: Canvas state until send.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (vision analysis)
PROMPT: Same as #151 with additional context: "The user drew this sketch on a
  whiteboard to illustrate a proposed research structure."
FALLBACK: "I could not interpret the sketch. Try labeling key elements with text."
TOKEN BUDGET: ~500 tokens per sketch analysis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element      | Action | Response                                       |
|--------------|--------|------------------------------------------------|
| Pen tool     | Select | Freehand drawing mode                          |
| Rect tool    | Select | Click-drag to draw rectangle                   |
| Arrow tool   | Select | Click-drag to draw arrow                       |
| Text tool    | Select | Click to place text cursor                     |
| Eraser       | Select | Click-drag to erase                            |
| Color picker | Click  | 5-color palette dropdown                       |
| Close (x)    | Click  | Close whiteboard, preserve drawing              |
| Send button  | Click  | Capture canvas as PNG, send with message        |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Canvas slide 200ms, tool select highlight 100ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Canvas has role="img" with aria-label="Drawing whiteboard."
  Tools have aria-pressed state. Keyboard shortcuts for tool selection (1-5).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Canvas too small for complex diagram
   Expected: Scroll/pan not supported. Encourage simpler sketches.
   Implementation: Fixed 300px height canvas.

2. Scenario: Touch device drawing
   Expected: Touch events mapped to drawing (finger as pen).
   Implementation: Touch event handlers mirror mouse handlers.

3. Scenario: Accidental close with unsaved drawing
   Expected: Confirm: "Close whiteboard? Your drawing will be preserved."
   Implementation: Close confirmation dialog.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #151 (Screenshot Analysis), #001 (Context-Aware Responses)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Visual input workflow, mental model comparison
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: HTML5 Canvas API.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Canvas with pen, rectangle, arrow, text, eraser tools
[ ] Color picker with 5 Arivu palette colors
[ ] Export canvas as PNG on send
[ ] Analysis via same pipeline as #151
[ ] Canvas closes after send

VISUAL: [ ] Minimal toolbar, dark canvas background, tool highlights
PERFORMANCE: [ ] Drawing at 60fps, export within 100ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Canvas labeled, tools with aria-pressed, keyboard shortcuts
EDGE CASES: [ ] Empty canvas rejection, touch support, close confirmation

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-whiteboard.js, athena-engine.js, CSS 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST draw, export, send, analyze 4. CHECK PNG quality 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase D. Canvas-based drawing input. Reuses #151 analysis pipeline.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #153 Table Paste
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #153: TABLE PASTE
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Input Methods
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           D
ESTIMATED HOURS: 12
OVERLAPS WITH:   #141 (Table Builder), #154 (Citation String Parser)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #141 (Table Builder)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Table Paste enables users to paste tabular data from spreadsheets, PDFs, or
web pages directly into the Athena chat and have it parsed into a structured
table. When a user copies cells from Excel, Google Sheets, or a web page table
and pastes into the chat input, Athena detects the tabular structure (via
tab-separated or comma-separated patterns) and renders the data as an
interactive table block (#141).

This solves the problem of researchers who have data in external tools that
they want to analyze in the context of their research lineage. A user might
have a spreadsheet of paper citations, a table of method comparisons from a
review paper, or experimental results they want Athena to cross-reference
with the lineage graph.

The parsing pipeline: clipboard text is analyzed for structure markers (tabs,
commas, pipes, consistent whitespace). If tabular structure is detected, the
data is parsed into a 2D array, columns are auto-typed (number, text, year),
and the result is rendered as an AthenaTableBlock from #141. If the text
is ambiguous, Athena shows a preview and asks: "This looks like a table with
5 columns and 12 rows. Should I parse it as a table?"

After parsing, the user can ask questions about the pasted data in the context
of the lineage. "Which of these papers are in my current graph?" cross-
references the pasted paper titles against graph nodes using fuzzy string
matching (similar to the deduplicator's matching logic). "Sort this by
citation count and add a PageRank column from my lineage" merges pasted data
with graph-computed metrics.

The feature also detects markdown tables (pipes and dashes) and HTML tables
(pasted from web browsers). Each format is parsed by a specialized detector,
but all converge into the same table block rendering path.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User pastes text containing tabular structure (from Excel, Google Sheets, web
table, or markdown table) into the chat input.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User copies 10 rows from a Google Sheets spreadsheet containing paper titles,
   years, and citation counts. They press Ctrl+V in the chat input.

2. Athena detects tabular structure: "I detected a table with 3 columns and 10
   rows. [Render as Table] [Paste as Text]"

3. User clicks [Render as Table]. Data appears as an interactive table block:

   ┌─ Pasted Data ──────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ Paper Title          │ Year │ Citations             │
   ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────┤
   │ Attention Is All...  │ 2017 │ 98201                 │
   │ BERT                 │ 2018 │ 75443                 │
   │ ResNet               │ 2016 │ 62187                 │
   │ ... (7 more rows)    │      │                       │
   └──────────────────────┴──────┴───────────────────────┘

4. User asks: "Which of these are in my current lineage?"

5. Athena cross-references: "7 of 10 papers match nodes in your lineage. 3
   are not present: [Paper X], [Paper Y], [Paper Z]." Matching rows are
   highlighted with a subtle green left border. Non-matching rows get a
   muted orange left border.

6. User asks: "Add PageRank from my lineage as a column."

7. Athena adds a PageRank column to the table. Papers not in the lineage
   show "--" for PageRank. Table is now interactive with sort on the new column.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Parsed table persists in chat with all interactive features from #141.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Paste handler (intercepts Ctrl+V), tabular
  structure detection, format classification (TSV, CSV, markdown, HTML).
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: Reuses AthenaTableBlock from #141.
- static/js/athena-table-parser.js: NEW FILE. Table parsing logic for TSV,
  CSV, markdown, and HTML table formats. Column type inference.
- app.py: /api/athena/table-match endpoint for cross-referencing pasted
  paper titles against graph nodes.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaTableParser {
    static detect(text) {
      if (text.includes('\t') && text.split('\n').length > 1) return 'tsv';
      if (text.match(/^\|.*\|$/m)) return 'markdown';
      if (text.match(/<table/i)) return 'html';
      if (text.split('\n').every(line => line.split(',').length > 1)) return 'csv';
      return null;
    }

    static parse(text, format) {
      const rows = [];
      if (format === 'tsv') {
        text.split('\n').filter(l => l.trim()).forEach(line => rows.push(line.split('\t')));
      } else if (format === 'csv') {
        text.split('\n').filter(l => l.trim()).forEach(line => rows.push(this.parseCSVLine(line)));
      }
      // ... markdown and HTML parsers
      return { headers: rows[0], data: rows.slice(1), format };
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/table-match', methods=['POST'])
  def table_match():
      titles = request.json['titles']
      graph = load_current_graph(session_id)
      matches = {}
      for title in titles:
          best = None; best_score = 0
          for pid, paper in graph.papers.items():
              score = title_similarity(title, paper.title)
              if score > best_score and score > 0.85:
                  best = pid; best_score = score
          matches[title] = {'paper_id': best, 'score': best_score}
      return jsonify({'matches': matches})

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/table-match
Request: { "titles": ["Attention Is All You Need", "BERT", ...] }
Response: { "matches": { "Attention Is All You Need": {"paper_id": "204e...", "score": 0.98}, ... } }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Table Detection Prompt

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │  I detected a table with 3 columns and 10 rows.          │
  │  [Render as Table] [Paste as Text]                        │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STATE: Cross-Referenced Table

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ▌ Attention Is All...  │ 2017 │ 98201 │ 0.087      │  (green border = in lineage)
  │ ▌ BERT                 │ 2018 │ 75443 │ 0.063      │
  │ ▌ Unknown Paper        │ 2019 │ 1200  │ --         │  (orange border = not in lineage)
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Table renders with rows staggering in (50ms per row).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User pastes tabular text
  -> Client-side format detection (TSV, CSV, markdown, HTML)
  -> If detected: show "[Render as Table] [Paste as Text]"
  -> User clicks Render -> AthenaTableParser parses into 2D array
  -> Table rendered via AthenaTableBlock (#141)
  -> User asks cross-reference question -> POST /api/athena/table-match
  -> Matching papers identified via fuzzy title similarity
  -> Table rows annotated with match status (green/orange border)

ERROR PATH: Ambiguous format -> "I'm not sure if this is a table. Paste as text?"
DATA STORED: Parsed table in chat_history blocks.
DATA CACHED: Parse result in block instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

NOT APPLICABLE for parsing (rule-based detection). LLM used only for
cross-reference analysis questions ("Which are in my lineage?") via standard
chat pipeline with table data as context.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element          | Action | Response                                       |
|------------------|--------|------------------------------------------------|
| Render as Table  | Click  | Parse and display as interactive table          |
| Paste as Text    | Click  | Paste as plain text message                    |
| Match indicator  | Hover  | Tooltip: "Matched to: [paper] (98% confidence)"|
| Add Column       | Click  | Add graph metric column (PageRank, etc.)       |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Rows stagger in 50ms each. Match border fades in 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Table follows #141 accessibility patterns. Detection prompt
  has role="alert."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
