--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Paste contains mixed tabular and text content
   Expected: Detect only the tabular portion. Non-table text treated as context.
   Implementation: Split on double-newline, detect table in largest block.

2. Scenario: CSV with quoted commas ("cell, with comma")
   Expected: Proper CSV parsing respecting quoted fields.
   Implementation: parseCSVLine handles quoted commas.

3. Scenario: Pasted table has inconsistent column count
   Expected: "Table has inconsistent columns (3-5 per row). Cannot parse."
   Implementation: Validate column count consistency.

4. Scenario: No matching papers in lineage
   Expected: "None of these papers match nodes in your current lineage."
   All rows get orange border.
   Implementation: Handle zero matches gracefully.

5. Scenario: Very large paste (100+ rows)
   Expected: Parse all rows but paginate display at 25 rows (#141 pattern).
   Implementation: AthenaTableBlock pagination handles large data.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #141 (Table Builder)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Data import workflow, cross-referencing external data with lineage
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Clipboard API for paste interception. New file: athena-table-parser.js.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] TSV paste detection and parsing
[ ] CSV paste detection and parsing
[ ] Markdown table detection and parsing
[ ] HTML table detection and parsing
[ ] Detection prompt with [Render as Table] / [Paste as Text]
[ ] Cross-reference pasted titles against graph nodes
[ ] Match indicators (green/orange borders)
[ ] Add graph metric columns to pasted table

VISUAL: [ ] Table renders via #141 block, match borders, row stagger animation
PERFORMANCE: [ ] Parse 100 rows within 200ms
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Detection prompt as alert, table follows #141 patterns
EDGE CASES: [ ] Mixed content, quoted CSV, inconsistent columns, zero matches, large paste

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #154 Citation String Parser
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #154: CITATION STRING PARSER
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Input Methods
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           D
ESTIMATED HOURS: 14
OVERLAPS WITH:   #153 (Table Paste), #005 (Graph-Aware Commands)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Citation String Parser allows users to paste raw citation strings (from
BibTeX, APA, MLA, Chicago, or plain reference list formats) into the Athena
chat and have them automatically parsed into structured paper references that
can be searched, matched against the graph, or used as input for new graph
builds.

Researchers constantly encounter citations in various formats: a BibTeX entry
copied from Google Scholar, an APA-formatted reference from a paper's
bibliography, or a plain text reference list from a review article. Currently,
they must manually extract the DOI or title and search for it. Citation String
Parser automates this: paste the citation, Athena extracts title, authors,
year, DOI (if present), and either matches it to a paper in the current
lineage or offers to build a new graph from it.

The parser handles these formats:

1. BibTeX: @article{key, title={...}, author={...}, year={...}, doi={...}}
2. APA: Author, A. B. (Year). Title. Journal, Volume(Issue), Pages. DOI
3. MLA: Author. "Title." Journal Volume.Issue (Year): Pages.
4. Chicago: Author. "Title." Journal Volume, no. Issue (Year): Pages.
5. Plain: Author (Year). Title. [Freeform reference text]
6. DOI-only: 10.xxxx/xxxxx or https://doi.org/10.xxxx/xxxxx
7. arXiv ID: arXiv:XXXX.XXXXX or https://arxiv.org/abs/XXXX.XXXXX

The parsing is primarily regex-based for BibTeX and DOI/arXiv extraction,
with LLM fallback for ambiguous formats (APA, MLA, Chicago, and plain text).
When a DOI is extracted, it is used directly for Semantic Scholar lookup. When
only title and author are extracted, fuzzy search is used.

Multi-citation paste is supported. Users can paste an entire reference section
from a paper (20+ citations) and Athena will parse each one, presenting the
results as a structured list with match status per citation.

Each parsed citation is presented in a card format:

  [1] "Attention Is All You Need"
      Vaswani et al., 2017
      DOI: 10.48550/arXiv.1706.03762
      Status: ● In lineage (PageRank: 0.087)
      [Highlight in Graph]

  [2] "FlashAttention"
      Dao et al., 2022
      DOI: 10.48550/arXiv.2205.14135
      Status: ○ Not in lineage
      [Build Graph] [Search Semantic Scholar]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User pastes a citation string into the chat. Athena auto-detects citation
format. Or user types /cite followed by a citation string.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User copies a BibTeX entry from Google Scholar:
   @article{vaswani2017attention, title={Attention is all you need}, author={Vaswani, Ashish and ...}, year={2017}, doi={10.48550/arXiv.1706.03762}}

2. User pastes into chat. Athena detects BibTeX format: "Parsed BibTeX citation:"

3. Citation card appears:
   ┌─ Citation #1 ──────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ "Attention Is All You Need"                        │
   │ Vaswani et al., 2017                               │
   │ DOI: 10.48550/arXiv.1706.03762                     │
   │ ● In lineage (seed paper, PageRank: 0.087)         │
   │ [Highlight in Graph] [Show Neighborhood]            │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

4. User pastes a full reference list (15 citations in APA format).

5. Athena parses all 15: "Parsed 15 citations. 9 are in your lineage, 6 are
   not." Citations listed with match status.

6. User: "Build graphs for the 6 papers not in my lineage."
   Athena offers to build graphs for each.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Parsed citations persist in chat. Match status reflects current graph state.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-citation-parser.js: NEW FILE. Client-side citation format
  detection and BibTeX/DOI/arXiv extraction. Sends ambiguous formats to backend.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: CitationCard block renderer.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Citation paste detection, parser routing.
- app.py: /api/athena/parse-citations endpoint for LLM-assisted parsing.
  /api/athena/match-citations for graph matching.
- backend/normalizer.py: Reused for title normalization in matching.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaCitationParser {
    static detect(text) {
      if (text.match(/@\w+\{/)) return 'bibtex';
      if (text.match(/^10\.\d{4,}/m) || text.match(/doi\.org/i)) return 'doi';
      if (text.match(/arXiv:\d{4}\.\d{4,5}/i)) return 'arxiv';
      return 'freeform'; // Send to LLM for parsing
    }

    static parseBibTeX(text) {
      const entries = [];
      const regex = /@\w+\{[^}]*,([^@]*)\}/g;
      let match;
      while ((match = regex.exec(text)) !== null) {
        const body = match[1];
        entries.push({
          title: this.extractField(body, 'title'),
          author: this.extractField(body, 'author'),
          year: this.extractField(body, 'year'),
          doi: this.extractField(body, 'doi'),
        });
      }
      return entries;
    }

    static extractField(body, field) {
      const match = body.match(new RegExp(`${field}\\s*=\\s*[{"]([^}"]*)[}"]`));
      return match ? match[1].trim() : null;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/parse-citations', methods=['POST'])
  def parse_citations():
      text = request.json['text']
      format_hint = request.json.get('format', 'freeform')
      # Use LLM for freeform parsing
      prompt = f"Parse these citations into structured data: title, authors, year, doi.\n{text}"
      parsed = llm_client.complete(prompt, model=config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL)
      return jsonify({'citations': parsed})

  @app.route('/api/athena/match-citations', methods=['POST'])
  def match_citations():
      citations = request.json['citations']
      graph = load_current_graph(session_id)
      results = []
      for c in citations:
          match = None
          if c.get('doi'):
              match = find_by_doi(graph, c['doi'])
          if not match and c.get('title'):
              match = fuzzy_find_by_title(graph, c['title'])
          results.append({**c, 'matched_paper_id': match, 'in_lineage': match is not None})
      return jsonify({'results': results})

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/parse-citations
Request: { "text": "@article{...}", "format": "bibtex" }
Response: { "citations": [{"title": "...", "author": "...", "year": "...", "doi": "..."}] }

POST /api/athena/match-citations
Request: { "citations": [...] }
Response: { "results": [...with matched_paper_id and in_lineage boolean] }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Parsed Citations

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │  Parsed 3 citations. 2 in lineage, 1 not.                │
  │                                                           │
  │  ┌─ [1] ────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ "Attention Is All You Need"                      │     │
  │  │ Vaswani et al., 2017                             │     │
  │  │ ● In lineage (PR: 0.087)  [Highlight]            │     │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  │  ┌─ [2] ────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ "BERT"                                           │     │
  │  │ Devlin et al., 2018                              │     │
  │  │ ● In lineage (PR: 0.063)  [Highlight]            │     │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  │  ┌─ [3] ────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ "FlashAttention"                                 │     │
  │  │ Dao et al., 2022                                 │     │
  │  │ ○ Not in lineage  [Build Graph] [Search S2]      │     │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Citation cards stagger in 100ms each. Match status fades in 200ms.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User pastes citation text
  -> Client-side format detection (bibtex, doi, arxiv, freeform)
  -> BibTeX/DOI/arXiv: parsed client-side
  -> Freeform: sent to /api/athena/parse-citations for LLM parsing
  -> Parsed citations sent to /api/athena/match-citations
  -> Graph matching via DOI or fuzzy title
  -> Results rendered as citation cards with match status

ERROR PATH: Unparseable text -> "I could not detect citation format. Try pasting
  a DOI or BibTeX entry." Single citation fails -> mark as "Could not parse."
DATA STORED: Parsed citations in chat_history.
DATA CACHED: Match results in block instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL (for freeform citation parsing)

PROMPT:
```
Parse these citations into structured JSON.
For each citation, extract: title, authors (as array), year (integer), doi (if present).

Citations:
{text}

Return JSON array: [{"title": "...", "authors": ["..."], "year": 2017, "doi": "..."}]
If a field cannot be determined, use null.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON array of citation objects
FALLBACK: Return unparseable citations with raw text preserved
TOKEN BUDGET: ~300 tokens per batch of citations

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element            | Action | Response                                    |
|--------------------|--------|---------------------------------------------|
| Highlight          | Click  | Pan graph to matched paper node             |
| Build Graph        | Click  | Start graph build from this paper's DOI     |
| Search S2          | Click  | Open Semantic Scholar search for this paper |
| Show Neighborhood  | Click  | Trigger #150 neighborhood report            |
| Citation card      | Hover  | Subtle elevation shadow                     |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Cards stagger in 100ms. Match status fade 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Citation cards have role="article". Match status in aria-label.
  "Citation 1: Attention Is All You Need by Vaswani, 2017. In lineage."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: BibTeX entry with missing fields (no DOI, no year)
   Expected: Parse what is available. Missing fields shown as "Unknown."
   Implementation: All fields optional in parse result.

2. Scenario: Paste 50+ citations (entire reference section)
   Expected: Parse all but paginate display at 10 citations. "[Show 40 more]"
   Implementation: Paginate citation cards at 10 per view.

3. Scenario: DOI resolves to a paper different from the title
   Expected: DOI takes priority. Note: "DOI matched to a different title than
   expected. Showing DOI match."
   Implementation: DOI match overrides title match.

4. Scenario: Duplicate citations in paste
   Expected: Deduplicate by DOI or exact title match. Show count: "2 duplicates
   removed."
   Implementation: Dedup before matching.

5. Scenario: Non-English citation
   Expected: Parse structure (BibTeX is format-independent). Title preserved
   as-is. Matching may be lower confidence for non-English titles.
   Implementation: Language-agnostic parsing.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context-Aware Responses)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #153 (Table Paste), paper discovery workflow, graph building
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: New file: athena-citation-parser.js. Reuses backend/normalizer.py.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] BibTeX parsing (client-side)
[ ] DOI extraction and Semantic Scholar lookup
[ ] arXiv ID extraction
[ ] APA/MLA/Chicago parsing (LLM-assisted)
[ ] Multi-citation batch parsing (20+ at once)
[ ] Graph matching via DOI or fuzzy title
[ ] Citation cards with match status (in lineage / not)
[ ] Build Graph and Search S2 actions for unmatched papers
[ ] Highlight in Graph for matched papers

VISUAL:
[ ] Citation cards with ● / ○ match indicators
[ ] Cards stagger in 100ms each
[ ] Match status color (green in-lineage, muted not)

PERFORMANCE:
[ ] BibTeX/DOI parse within 100ms (client-side)
[ ] LLM parse within 3 seconds for 20 citations
[ ] Graph matching within 500ms for 20 citations

ACCESSIBILITY:
[ ] Citation cards as role="article"
[ ] Match status in aria-label
[ ] Action buttons labeled

EDGE CASES:

[ ] 50+ citations paginated
[ ] DOI priority over title
[ ] Duplicate removal
[ ] Non-English citations

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

2. COMPARE against all Done Criteria

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #155 Slideshow Mode (P3, H -- shorter)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #155: SLIDESHOW MODE
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 20
OVERLAPS WITH:   #100 (Presentation Slide Artifact), #028 (Speed Run Mode)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #100 (Presentation Slide Artifact), #028 (Speed Run Mode),
                 #002 (Rich Response Formatting)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Slideshow Mode transforms the Athena chat panel into a full-screen presentation
player. When activated via /slideshow, Athena generates a sequence of slides
that walk through the research lineage as a narrative presentation. Each slide
shows a key insight, a graph state (zoomed to relevant cluster or paper), and
speaker notes.

This targets the use case of researchers preparing talks about a field's
intellectual history. Instead of manually building a presentation, they ask
Athena to generate a slideshow from the lineage data. The result is a 10-15
slide narrative covering: introduction (seed paper), key ancestors, major
breakthroughs, mutation patterns, vulnerabilities, and frontier.

Slides are rendered full-screen with large text, relevant graph snippets, and
minimal visual noise. Navigation: arrow keys, spacebar, or swipe. Each slide
transitions with a subtle fade (300ms).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User types /slideshow or asks "Create a presentation about this lineage."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User: /slideshow
2. Athena: "Generating presentation..." (3-5 seconds).
3. Chat panel transforms into slideshow mode. First slide:
   "The Attention Mechanism Lineage: 152 papers, 7 clusters, 2012-2022"
4. User presses right arrow. Slide 2: "It began with Word2Vec (2013)..."
   Graph zooms to the embedding cluster.
5. User navigates through 12 slides, each highlighting different aspects.
6. Final slide: "Open Questions and Future Directions."
7. User presses Escape to exit slideshow mode and return to normal chat.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Slideshow data persists. User can re-enter with /slideshow without regeneration.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-slideshow.js: NEW FILE. Slideshow renderer, navigation,
  full-screen mode, slide transitions.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: /slideshow command handler, slide generation.
- static/css/athena-slideshow.css: NEW FILE. Full-screen slide styles.
- app.py: /api/athena/slideshow generates slide content from graph.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaSlideshowMode {
    constructor(slides) { this.slides = slides; this.current = 0; }
    enter() { document.body.classList.add('slideshow-mode'); this.render(); }
    exit() { document.body.classList.remove('slideshow-mode'); }
    next() { if (this.current < this.slides.length - 1) { this.current++; this.render(); } }
    prev() { if (this.current > 0) { this.current--; this.render(); } }
    render() { /* full-screen slide with graph state and narrative text */ }
  }

BACKEND: LLM generates slide content from graph summary. Each slide has
title, body text, graph_focus (paper_id or cluster to zoom to), and notes.

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/slideshow
Response: { "slides": [{"title": "...", "body": "...", "graph_focus": "...", "notes": "..."}] }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Slideshow Active

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                           │
  │                                                           │
  │    The Attention Mechanism Lineage                        │
  │    152 papers | 7 clusters | 2012-2022                   │
  │                                                           │
  │    [Graph mini-view focused on seed paper]                │
  │                                                           │
  │                                                           │
  │    Slide 1 of 12                          [<] [>] [x]    │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Slide fade 300ms. Graph zoom synchronized with slide transition.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User types /slideshow -> Backend generates 10-15 slides from graph summary
  -> LLM creates narrative per slide -> Frontend enters slideshow mode
  -> Each slide shows text + graph focus -> Arrow keys navigate
  -> Escape exits to normal chat

ERROR PATH: Graph too small (<5 papers) -> "Need a larger lineage for a slideshow."
DATA STORED: Slide data in session memory. DATA CACHED: Slides in slideshow instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (for narrative quality)
PROMPT: "Generate a 12-slide research presentation from this lineage.
Graph: {graph_summary}. Each slide: title (under 10 words), body (2-3 sentences),
graph_focus (paper_id to zoom to). Cover: intro, ancestors, breakthroughs,
mutations, vulnerability, frontier."
OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON array of slide objects.
FALLBACK: Rule-based slides from graph structure.
TOKEN BUDGET: ~1000 tokens per slideshow.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element     | Action     | Response                                    |
|-------------|------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Right arrow | Keypress   | Next slide                                  |
| Left arrow  | Keypress   | Previous slide                              |
| Spacebar    | Keypress   | Next slide                                  |
| Escape      | Keypress   | Exit slideshow mode                         |
| [x] button  | Click      | Exit slideshow mode                         |
| Swipe right | Touch      | Previous slide (mobile)                     |
| Swipe left  | Touch      | Next slide (mobile)                         |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Fade 300ms between slides. Graph zoom 500ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Slides announced: "Slide 3 of 12: Key Breakthroughs."
  Keyboard navigation documented. Full screen uses role="dialog."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User navigates past last slide
   Expected: Stay on last slide. Tooltip: "End of presentation."
2. Scenario: Browser loses full-screen during slideshow
   Expected: Slideshow continues in normal viewport. [Fullscreen] button available.
3. Scenario: Graph state changes during slideshow (unlikely)
   Expected: Slides show cached graph state from generation time.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #100 (Presentation Slides), #028 (Speed Run Mode), #002 (Rich Formatting)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Research presentation workflow
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: New files: athena-slideshow.js, athena-slideshow.css.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] /slideshow generates 10-15 narrative slides
[ ] Full-screen presentation mode
[ ] Arrow key and spacebar navigation
[ ] Graph focus synchronized per slide
[ ] Escape exits to normal chat

VISUAL: [ ] Clean large-text slides, dark background, graph mini-view
PERFORMANCE: [ ] Slide generation within 5 seconds
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Slides announced, keyboard documented, role="dialog"

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-slideshow.js, CSS, app.py endpoint 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST navigation, graph sync, enter/exit 4. CHECK slide content accuracy 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #156 Poster Generator (P3, H -- shorter)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #156: POSTER GENERATOR
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Artifact Outputs
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 22
OVERLAPS WITH:   #011 (Artifact Cards), #100 (Presentation Slides)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #011 (Artifact Cards), #141 (Table Builder),
                 #147 (Time Series in Chat)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Poster Generator creates a single-page research poster from the current
lineage, exportable as PDF or PNG. The poster follows the standard academic
poster layout: title section, abstract/overview, key findings, graph
visualization, methodology timeline, statistics panel, and references.

Athena generates poster content by combining graph-computed data (cluster
summary, mutation landscape, key papers by PageRank, pruning vulnerabilities)
with LLM-generated narrative text. The user can customize the title, select
which sections to include, and choose between two layout templates (landscape
or portrait).

The poster renders as a high-resolution SVG in the chat panel with a preview
zoom. Export as PDF uses the ExportGenerator infrastructure from Phase 5.
Export as PNG uses SVG-to-canvas rendering at 300 DPI.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User types /poster or asks "Generate a research poster for this lineage."

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User: /poster
2. Athena: "What title should the poster have?" (or auto-generates from seed paper)
3. User: "The Evolution of Attention Mechanisms: A Lineage Analysis"
4. Athena: "Generating poster..." (5-8 seconds).
5. Poster preview renders in chat as a scrollable artifact block.
6. User clicks [Export PDF]. High-resolution PDF downloaded.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Poster preview persists in chat. Export available at any time.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-poster.js: NEW FILE. Poster layout engine, SVG generation,
  section rendering, export to PDF/PNG.
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: PosterPreview block renderer.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Poster preview styles.
- app.py: /api/athena/poster generates poster data from graph.
- backend/export_generator.py: Reused for PDF export.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaPosterGenerator {
    constructor(data) { this.sections = data.sections; this.title = data.title; }
    render() { /* Build SVG poster layout with sections */ }
    exportPDF() { /* Use ExportGenerator infrastructure */ }
    exportPNG() { /* SVG to canvas at 300 DPI */ }
  }

BACKEND: LLM generates section text. Graph provides statistics, cluster data,
  mutation landscape, and key paper rankings.

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/poster
Request: { "title": "...", "sections": ["overview", "findings", "graph", "timeline", "stats"] }
Response: { "poster": { "title": "...", "sections": [...] } }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Poster Preview

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │  ┌─ Poster Preview (scroll to see full) ────────────┐     │
  │  │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │     │
  │  │ │ THE EVOLUTION OF ATTENTION MECHANISMS         │ │     │
  │  │ │ A Lineage Analysis | 152 Papers | 2012-2022  │ │     │
  │  │ ├──────────────────┬───────────────────────────┤ │     │
  │  │ │ Overview         │ Key Findings              │ │     │
  │  │ │ (LLM narrative)  │ (top 5 papers by PR)      │ │     │
  │  │ ├──────────────────┴───────────────────────────┤ │     │
  │  │ │ [Graph visualization thumbnail]              │ │     │
  │  │ ├──────────────────┬───────────────────────────┤ │     │
  │  │ │ Mutation Dist.   │ Timeline                  │ │     │
  │  │ └──────────────────┴───────────────────────────┘ │     │
  │  │ [Export PDF] [Export PNG]                         │     │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TRANSITIONS: Poster builds section by section (200ms stagger per section).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User requests poster -> Backend gathers graph statistics, cluster data, key papers
  -> LLM generates narrative sections -> Poster data returned
  -> Frontend builds SVG poster layout -> Preview rendered in chat
  -> User exports as PDF or PNG

ERROR PATH: Graph too small -> "Need at least 10 papers for a meaningful poster."
DATA STORED: Poster data in session memory. DATA CACHED: SVG in block instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL (for poster narrative quality)
PROMPT: "Write sections for an academic poster about this research lineage.
Graph: {graph_summary}. Write: overview (100 words), key findings (5 bullet
points), methodology note (50 words). Formal academic tone."
OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with section texts.
FALLBACK: Rule-based section text from graph statistics.
TOKEN BUDGET: ~800 tokens per poster.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element        | Action | Response                                       |
|----------------|--------|------------------------------------------------|
| Poster preview | Scroll | Vertical scroll within preview container       |
| Export PDF     | Click  | Download high-resolution PDF                   |
| Export PNG     | Click  | Download 300 DPI PNG                           |
| Section        | Click  | Expand section for editing                     |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Sections stagger build 200ms each.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Poster has role="img" with full alt text summarizing content.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Poster content too long for one page
   Expected: Sections auto-truncate to fit single page. Priority: title,
   graph, findings, overview, timeline (in that order).
2. Scenario: Graph has no cluster data (DNA not computed)
   Expected: Omit cluster section, expand other sections.
3. Scenario: Export takes too long (>10 seconds)
   Expected: Show progress bar: "Generating PDF: 60%..."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #011 (Artifact Cards), #141 (Table Builder), #147 (Time Series)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Research presentation and dissemination workflow
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: New file: athena-poster.js. Reuses ExportGenerator for PDF.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Poster generated from graph data + LLM narrative
[ ] Landscape and portrait layout options
[ ] Export as PDF (high-resolution)
[ ] Export as PNG (300 DPI)
[ ] Section customization (include/exclude)

VISUAL: [ ] Academic poster layout, dark/light theme option
PERFORMANCE: [ ] Generation within 8 seconds, export within 5 seconds
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] Poster has descriptive alt text
EDGE CASES: [ ] Content overflow truncation, missing cluster data, export timeout

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-poster.js, export_generator.py, app.py 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST generate, preview, export PDF/PNG 4. CHECK layout accuracy 5. VERIFY no regression 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase H. Creates athena-poster.js. Academic poster from lineage data.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #157 Double-Tap to Summarize (P2, H)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #157: DOUBLE-TAP TO SUMMARIZE
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Message Actions
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 8
OVERLAPS WITH:   #040 (User Message Editing), #037 (Streaming Text)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #037 (Streaming Text)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Double-Tap to Summarize adds a quick action to long Athena responses: double-
clicking (or double-tapping on mobile) any Athena message generates a concise
3-sentence summary that appears as an inline annotation above the original
message. This addresses the problem of long analytical responses that the user
wants to reference later without re-reading.

When Athena produces a detailed analysis (more than 150 words), the response
becomes eligible for summarization. The user double-clicks the message, a brief
"Summarizing..." indicator appears, and a compact summary card slides in above
the message with a distinct visual treatment: slightly elevated background
(--bg-elevated), smaller text (14px), and a "TL;DR" label.

The summary is generated by passing the original response text to the
GROQ_FAST_MODEL with a strict 3-sentence constraint. The summary focuses on
the key finding, the evidence, and the implication. It references specific
papers or metrics from the original response to maintain precision.

The summary persists in session memory so it does not need regeneration on
subsequent views. Users can dismiss the summary by clicking an "x" button,
and it will not reappear unless they double-click again.

On mobile, a long-press (500ms) + release triggers the summary instead of
double-tap, to avoid conflict with text selection.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User double-clicks (desktop) or long-presses (mobile) any Athena message that
is longer than 150 words.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. Athena has produced a 400-word analysis of the attention mechanism cluster.

2. User double-clicks the message. A brief "Summarizing..." label appears
   at the top of the message (200ms).

3. Summary card slides in above the original message:

   ┌─ TL;DR ────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ The attention mechanism cluster contains 23 papers  │
   │ centered on Vaswani 2017 (PageRank 0.087). 68% of  │
   │ edges are adoption or generalization mutations.     │
   │ Removing the seed paper collapses 31% of the        │
   │ lineage.                                       [x]  │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

   [Original 400-word message below]

4. User clicks [x] to dismiss the summary. It slides out (200ms).

5. User double-clicks again later. Summary reappears from cache (instant).

AFTER COMPLETION:
Summary persists above the message for the session. Dismissible and re-triggerable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Double-click handler on Athena messages,
  summary request, cache check.
- static/js/athena-memory.js: Summary cache per message ID.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: TL;DR card styles, slide animation.
- app.py: /api/athena/summarize endpoint for summary generation.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // Double-click handler on Athena messages
  document.addEventListener('dblclick', (e) => {
    const msg = e.target.closest('.msg.athena');
    if (!msg) return;
    const text = msg.dataset.content;
    if (text.split(/\s+/).length < 150) return; // too short
    const cachedSummary = athenaMemory.getSummary(msg.dataset.id);
    if (cachedSummary) { showSummaryCard(msg, cachedSummary); return; }
    showSummarizing(msg);
    fetch('/api/athena/summarize', {
      method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify({ text, message_id: msg.dataset.id })
    }).then(r => r.json()).then(data => {
      athenaMemory.storeSummary(msg.dataset.id, data.summary);
      showSummaryCard(msg, data.summary);
    });
  });

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  @app.route('/api/athena/summarize', methods=['POST'])
  def athena_summarize():
      text = request.json['text']
      summary = llm_client.complete(
          f"Summarize in exactly 3 sentences. Focus on key finding, evidence, "
          f"and implication. Preserve specific paper names and metrics.\n\n{text}",
          model=config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL
      )
      return jsonify({'summary': summary})

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/summarize
Request: { "text": "...", "message_id": "..." }
Response: { "summary": "The attention mechanism cluster contains..." }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Summary Visible

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ● Athena                                                 │
  │  ┌─ TL;DR ──────────────────────────────────────┐        │
  │  │ Summary text here. Three sentences max.   [x] │        │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
  │  (Original long message below, unchanged)                 │
  │  The attention mechanism cluster contains 23 papers...    │
  │  ...400 words of analysis...                              │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TL;DR card: --bg-elevated background, 14px font, --text-secondary color.
"TL;DR" label in --accent-gold, 12px, uppercase.

TRANSITIONS: Summary card slides down (200ms ease-out). Dismiss slides up (200ms).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User double-clicks long Athena message
  -> Check athena-memory.js for cached summary
  -> If cached: show immediately (no API call)
  -> If not cached: POST /api/athena/summarize
  -> LLM generates 3-sentence summary
  -> Summary cached in memory by message_id
  -> Summary card rendered above original message
  -> Dismiss (x) hides card but preserves cache

ERROR PATH: LLM fails -> "Could not generate summary." No card shown.
DATA STORED: Summary in session memory (athena-memory.js). Not in DB.
DATA CACHED: Per message_id in memory for session duration.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

PROMPT:
```
Summarize this research analysis in exactly 3 sentences.
Sentence 1: The key finding.
Sentence 2: The evidence or data supporting it.
Sentence 3: The implication or next step.
Preserve specific paper names and numerical metrics.

Text:
{original_text}
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: 3-sentence plain text
FALLBACK: Truncate original to first 3 sentences with "..." appended.
TOKEN BUDGET: ~150 tokens per summary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element          | Action       | Response                                 |
|------------------|--------------|------------------------------------------|
| Athena message   | Double-click | Generate/show summary card               |
| Athena message   | Long-press   | Same (mobile: 500ms hold)                |
| Dismiss (x)      | Click        | Hide summary card (cache preserved)      |
| Short message    | Double-click | No action (< 150 words)                  |

ANIMATION DETAILS: Summary slide down 200ms. Dismiss slide up 200ms.
SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.
ACCESSIBILITY: Summary card has role="note" with aria-label="Summary of
  previous response." Dismiss button labeled "Close summary."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Message exactly 150 words (borderline)
   Expected: Summarization triggers. The threshold is inclusive (>= 150).
   Implementation: Word count check uses >= 150.

2. Scenario: Double-click selects text instead of triggering summary
   Expected: Text selection takes priority. Summary triggers only if no text
   is selected after the double-click.
   Implementation: Check window.getSelection().toString() is empty.

3. Scenario: Summary generation fails mid-stream
   Expected: "Summarizing..." label replaced with "Summary unavailable."
   Implementation: Error handler on fetch removes loading state.

4. Scenario: User double-clicks while summary is already visible
   Expected: No action (summary already shown). No redundant API call.
   Implementation: Check if summary card already exists for this message.

5. Scenario: Message contains code blocks or tables
   Expected: Summary focuses on prose content, not code or table data.
   Implementation: Strip code blocks and table markup before sending to LLM.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #037 (Streaming Text)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Long response readability, quick reference
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: None. Uses existing LLM infrastructure.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Double-click triggers summary on messages >= 150 words
[ ] 3-sentence summary generated via LLM
[ ] Summary cached per message (no re-generation)
[ ] Dismiss button hides but preserves cache
[ ] Re-trigger shows cached summary instantly
[ ] Long-press on mobile (500ms)
[ ] Short messages ignored (< 150 words)

VISUAL:
[ ] TL;DR card with --bg-elevated background
[ ] 14px text, --text-secondary color
[ ] "TL;DR" label in --accent-gold
[ ] Slide animation (200ms)

PERFORMANCE: [ ] Summary generation within 2 seconds
ACCESSIBILITY: [ ] role="note", aria-label, dismiss button labeled
EDGE CASES: [ ] Text selection conflict, borderline length, code content

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-engine.js (double-click handler), athena-memory.js (summary cache), CSS 2. COMPARE criteria 3. TEST double-click, dismiss, re-trigger, mobile long-press 4. CHECK summary accuracy vs original content 5. VERIFY no regression in text selection, click handlers 6. BROKEN 7. 1+/round 8. Log below

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT LOG
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Empty -- feature not yet implemented]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Status:           NOT_STARTED
Implementation:   Not started
Files:            Not yet created
Verified:         Not yet verified
Last Updated:     2026-03-24
Notes:            Phase H. Simple but useful. 8 hours estimated.
''')

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# #158 Floating Action Button (P2, H)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
features.append(r
================================================================================
FEATURE #158: FLOATING ACTION BUTTON
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Chat Controls
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           H
ESTIMATED HOURS: 10
OVERLAPS WITH:   #003 (Quick Action Buttons), #007 (Visual Redesign)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #007 (Visual Redesign), #003 (Quick Action Buttons)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Floating Action Button (FAB) is a persistent, circular button fixed to the
bottom-right corner of the chat panel. It provides one-tap access to the most
common Athena actions without needing to type a slash command or navigate menus.

When tapped, the FAB expands into a radial menu of 5 quick actions:
  1. /radar (scan current lineage)
  2. /compare (enter comparison mode)
  3. /checklist (generate reading plan)
  4. /timeseries (show citation trends)
  5. New graph (clear and start fresh)

The FAB is always visible, floating above chat messages. It uses the Arivu
gold accent color (--accent-gold) with a "+" icon that rotates 45 degrees
into an "x" when the menu is open. The radial menu fans out in an arc above
the FAB, each action represented by a small labeled icon button.

The FAB is especially valuable on mobile where typing slash commands is
cumbersome. It provides discoverable access to features that users might not
know exist as slash commands.

When the user is actively typing in the chat input, the FAB shrinks to a
smaller, semi-transparent state to avoid visual competition. It returns to
full size when the input is empty.

The FAB position can be dragged to other corners of the chat panel for user
preference. Position is stored in localStorage and persists across sessions.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User taps/clicks the FAB. The radial menu opens. User selects an action.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. FAB is visible at bottom-right of chat panel: a 48px gold circle with
   a "+" icon.

2. User clicks the FAB. The "+" rotates 45 degrees to become "x." Five
   action buttons fan out in an arc above the FAB, each 36px circles with
   icons and small labels.

3. User clicks the "Radar" action. The menu closes, the FAB animates back
   to "+", and /radar is executed in the chat.

4. If user clicks outside the menu or clicks the "x", the menu closes without
   action.

AFTER COMPLETION:
FAB returns to resting state. Selected action executes as if typed as a
slash command.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-fab.js: NEW FILE. FAB component, radial menu, drag
  positioning, localStorage persistence, input-aware size adjustment.
- static/css/athena-fab.css: NEW FILE. FAB styles, radial menu layout,
  animations, responsive sizing.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: FAB action routing to slash command handlers.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  class AthenaFAB {
    constructor() {
      this.isOpen = false;
      this.actions = [
        { icon: 'radar', label: 'Radar Scan', command: '/radar' },
        { icon: 'compare', label: 'Compare', command: '/compare' },
        { icon: 'checklist', label: 'Checklist', command: '/checklist' },
        { icon: 'chart', label: 'Trends', command: '/timeseries' },
        { icon: 'new', label: 'New Graph', command: '/new' },
      ];
      this.position = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('fab-position')) || { right: 16, bottom: 80 };
    }

    toggle() {
      this.isOpen = !this.isOpen;
      this.el.classList.toggle('open', this.isOpen);
      this.renderMenu();
    }

    executeAction(command) {
      this.toggle(); // close menu
      athenaEngine.executeCommand(command);
    }

    render() {
      this.el = document.createElement('div');
      this.el.className = 'athena-fab';
      this.el.style.right = this.position.right + 'px';
      this.el.style.bottom = this.position.bottom + 'px';
      this.el.innerHTML = '<span class="fab-icon">+</span>';
      this.el.onclick = () => this.toggle();
      this.initDrag();
      document.querySelector('.athena-chat').appendChild(this.el);
    }

    initDrag() {
      let isDragging = false;
      this.el.addEventListener('pointerdown', (e) => {
        const start = { x: e.clientX, y: e.clientY };
        const onMove = (me) => {
          if (Math.abs(me.clientX - start.x) > 5 || Math.abs(me.clientY - start.y) > 5) {
            isDragging = true;
          }
          if (isDragging) {
            // Update position
          }
        };
        const onUp = () => {
          if (isDragging) {
            localStorage.setItem('fab-position', JSON.stringify(this.position));
          }
          isDragging = false;
          document.removeEventListener('pointermove', onMove);
        };
        document.addEventListener('pointermove', onMove);
        document.addEventListener('pointerup', onUp, { once: true });
      });
    }
  }

BACKEND: No backend needed. FAB executes existing slash commands.

API CONTRACTS: None. FAB routes through existing athena-engine command handlers.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: FAB Resting

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  (Chat messages)                                          │
  │                                                           │
  │                                                           │
  │                                                     (●+)  │
  │  [Type a message...                               ] [->] │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  FAB: 48px circle, --accent-gold background, white "+" icon.

STATE: FAB Open (radial menu)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                           │
  │                                          ○ New Graph      │
  │                                     ○ Trends              │
  │                                ○ Checklist                │
  │                           ○ Compare                       │
  │                      ○ Radar                              │
  │                                                     (●x)  │
  │  [Type a message...                               ] [->] │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  Menu items: 36px circles fanning in arc. Labels to the left.
  FAB "+" rotated 45 degrees to "x".

STATE: FAB Minimized (user typing)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                      (.)  │
  │  [What is the pruning impact of...█              ] [->]  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  FAB shrinks to 32px, 50% opacity.

TRANSITIONS:
Open: menu items stagger outward 50ms each, FAB icon rotates 45deg (200ms).
Close: menu items stagger inward 30ms each, icon rotates back (200ms).
Minimize: FAB scales 48->32px, opacity 1->0.5 (200ms).
Restore: reverse of minimize (200ms).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User clicks FAB -> Menu opens (client-side only)
  -> User clicks action -> Menu closes
  -> Command string passed to athenaEngine.executeCommand()
  -> Existing slash command handler processes the command
  -> Result appears in chat as normal

User drags FAB -> New position saved to localStorage
  -> Position restored on page load

User starts typing -> FAB minimizes (input event listener)
  -> Input empties -> FAB restores

ERROR PATH: None (client-side only component).
DATA STORED: FAB position in localStorage.
DATA CACHED: None.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

NOT APPLICABLE. FAB is a pure UI component that routes to existing commands.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element        | Action     | Response                                    |
|----------------|------------|---------------------------------------------|
| FAB (closed)   | Click      | Open radial menu, rotate "+" to "x"         |
| FAB (open)     | Click      | Close radial menu, rotate "x" to "+"        |
| FAB            | Drag       | Reposition to new corner                    |
| Action button  | Click      | Execute command, close menu                 |
| Action button  | Hover      | Scale 1.1x, label becomes bold              |
| Outside menu   | Click      | Close menu without action                   |
| Chat input     | Focus      | FAB minimizes (32px, 50% opacity)           |
| Chat input     | Blur/empty | FAB restores (48px, 100% opacity)           |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Menu open: items stagger outward 50ms each (5 items = 250ms total)
- Menu close: items stagger inward 30ms each (150ms total)
- Icon rotate: 200ms ease-in-out
- Minimize: 200ms scale + opacity transition
- Drag: real-time position update (no animation, follows pointer)

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- FAB has role="button" with aria-label="Quick actions menu"
- Open state: aria-expanded="true"
- Menu items have role="menuitem" with aria-label per action
- Keyboard: Tab to FAB, Enter opens, arrow keys navigate menu, Enter selects
- Screen reader: "Quick actions menu. 5 items: Radar Scan, Compare, Checklist,
  Trends, New Graph."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: FAB dragged off-screen
   Expected: Snap back to nearest valid position (within chat panel bounds).
   Implementation: Clamp position to panel dimensions.

2. Scenario: Chat panel too narrow for radial menu (mobile portrait)
   Expected: Menu changes from radial arc to vertical stack above FAB.
   Implementation: Check available space, switch layout mode.

3. Scenario: User clicks FAB while Athena is streaming
   Expected: Menu still opens. Actions execute normally but may queue behind
   the streaming response.
   Implementation: No blocking on streaming state.

4. Scenario: FAB overlaps with scroll-to-bottom button
   Expected: FAB positioned to avoid overlap. Scroll button takes priority
   for positioning.
   Implementation: Coordinate positions between FAB and scroll button.

5. Scenario: Five different users have different FAB positions (localStorage)
   Expected: Each browser profile has independent FAB position storage.
   Implementation: localStorage is per-origin, per-browser profile.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #007 (Visual Redesign), #003 (Quick Action Buttons)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: Command discoverability, mobile usability
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: New files: athena-fab.js, athena-fab.css. localStorage for position.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] FAB visible at bottom-right of chat panel
[ ] Click opens radial menu with 5 actions
[ ] Click action executes corresponding slash command
[ ] Click outside or FAB closes menu
[ ] FAB draggable to other corners
[ ] Position persisted in localStorage
[ ] FAB minimizes when user is typing
[ ] FAB restores when input is empty

VISUAL:
[ ] 48px gold circle with white "+" icon
[ ] "+" rotates 45 degrees to "x" on open
[ ] 36px action buttons in radial arc
[ ] Menu stagger animation (50ms per item)
[ ] Minimize to 32px / 50% opacity

PERFORMANCE:
[ ] Menu open/close within 250ms
[ ] No jank during drag

ACCESSIBILITY:
[ ] role="button" with aria-expanded
[ ] Menu items as role="menuitem"
[ ] Keyboard: Tab, Enter, Arrow keys
[ ] Screen reader announces menu items

EDGE CASES:
[ ] Off-screen drag snap-back
[ ] Mobile vertical stack layout
[ ] Streaming state does not block
[ ] No overlap with scroll button

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

2. COMPARE against all Done Criteria
3. TEST: open/close, each action, drag reposition, minimize on typing, localStorage persistence
4. CHECK mobile layout (vertical stack vs radial)
5. VERIFY no regression in chat input, click handlers, slash commands
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below
