FEATURE #043: FILE/IMAGE ATTACHMENTS
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Input Methods
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           D
ESTIMATED HOURS: 18
OVERLAPS WITH:   #050 (Rich Input Area)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #050 (Rich Input Area), #044 (Multi-Turn Context)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

File/Image Attachments allows users to attach files and images to their
messages in the Athena chat panel. The primary use case is uploading a
research paper PDF so that Athena can analyze it in the context of the
current citation graph. A researcher analyzing a lineage might have a
paper that is not yet indexed by Semantic Scholar but is critical to
understanding the graph's structure. By attaching the PDF, Athena can
extract text using PyMuPDF (import fitz), run it through the section
parser, and incorporate its content into the conversation context.

Beyond PDFs, the feature supports image attachments (PNG, JPEG, WEBP)
for sharing screenshots of related graphs, figures from papers, or
hand-drawn concept diagrams. Images are displayed inline in the chat
message as thumbnails (max 300px wide) that expand to full size on click.
The system does not perform OCR or image analysis; images serve as visual
reference only, displayed alongside the user's text message.

The attachment workflow uses a paperclip icon button positioned to the
left of the send button in the Rich Input Area (#050). Clicking the
icon opens a native file picker filtered to accepted types (PDF, PNG,
JPEG, WEBP). Alternatively, users can drag and drop files directly onto
the chat input area. A drop zone indicator appears when a file is dragged
over the input: a dashed border in accent-gold with the text "Drop file
here" centered in the zone.

Uploaded files are sent to a dedicated backend endpoint that validates
file type, enforces size limits (MAX_UPLOAD_MB from config, default 10MB),
and stores the file temporarily in Cloudflare R2 under the key pattern
uploads/{session_id}/{timestamp}_{filename}. The R2 URL is returned
to the frontend, which renders the attachment inline in the message.

For PDF attachments, the backend additionally extracts text using the
section_parser module (backend/section_parser.py) and stores the extracted
content in the session context so that subsequent Athena responses can
reference the uploaded paper's content. This extraction happens
asynchronously; while it processes, a small spinner appears on the PDF
attachment thumbnail with the label "Extracting text..."

The attachment metadata (filename, file type, R2 key, extraction status)
is stored in the chat_history row as a JSONB attachments column. Each
message can have at most 3 attachments. The total upload budget per
session is 50MB.

Security considerations: all uploads pass through the SecureFileUploadHandler
(backend/secure_upload.py) which validates MIME types against a whitelist,
checks magic bytes, and rejects executable content. Filenames are sanitized
to remove path traversal characters.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User clicks the paperclip icon in the input area, or drags a file onto
the chat input.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User has a PDF of a recent paper not yet in Semantic Scholar. They click
   the paperclip icon next to the send button.
2. A native file picker opens, filtered to PDF, PNG, JPEG, WEBP. User
   selects "novel_approach_2025.pdf" (2.3MB).
3. The file appears as a compact attachment preview above the input area:
   a small PDF icon, the filename truncated to 30 chars, the file size,
   and an X button to remove it.
4. User types "Analyze this paper in the context of the current graph"
   and clicks Send.
5. The message appears in the chat with the PDF attachment rendered as a
   clickable card (PDF icon + filename + "2.3 MB"). A spinner label
   reads "Extracting text..."
6. After 3-5 seconds, the spinner is replaced by a green checkmark and
   "Text extracted." Athena now has the paper content in its context.
7. Athena responds: "I have extracted the paper content. This paper
   introduces a generalization of the core method from Node X (PageRank
   0.065) and appears to create a new hybridization edge..."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Attachment remains visible in the message. Extracted text is available in
the conversation context for all subsequent messages. R2 storage is cleaned
up after session expiry.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-attachments.js: AttachmentManager class, drag-and-drop,
  file picker, preview rendering, upload progress.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Integrate AttachmentManager with message
  submission; pass attachment metadata with message payload.
- static/js/athena-input.js: Paperclip icon button, drop zone indicator.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Attachment preview, drop zone, inline card,
  spinner, progress bar styles.
- app.py: /api/chat/upload POST endpoint for file upload and extraction.
- backend/secure_upload.py: SecureFileUploadHandler for validation.
- backend/section_parser.py: PDF text extraction (import fitz).
- backend/r2_client.py: R2 upload for attachment storage.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // In athena-attachments.js
  class AttachmentManager {
    constructor(inputArea) {
      this.inputArea = inputArea;
      this.attachments = [];
      this.maxAttachments = 3;
      this.maxSizeMB = 10;
      this.setupDragDrop();
      this.setupPickerButton();
    }

    setupDragDrop() {
      this.inputArea.addEventListener('dragover', (e) => {
        e.preventDefault();
        this.inputArea.classList.add('athena-input--drop-active');
      });
      this.inputArea.addEventListener('dragleave', () => {
        this.inputArea.classList.remove('athena-input--drop-active');
      });
      this.inputArea.addEventListener('drop', (e) => {
        e.preventDefault();
        this.inputArea.classList.remove('athena-input--drop-active');
        const files = Array.from(e.dataTransfer.files);
        files.forEach(f => this.addFile(f));
      });
    }

    async addFile(file) {
      if (this.attachments.length >= this.maxAttachments) {
        showToast('Maximum 3 attachments per message.');
        return;
      }
      if (file.size > this.maxSizeMB * 1024 * 1024) {
        showToast('File exceeds 10MB limit.');
        return;
      }
      const allowed = ['application/pdf', 'image/png', 'image/jpeg', 'image/webp'];
      if (!allowed.includes(file.type)) {
        showToast('Unsupported file type.');
        return;
      }
      this.attachments.push({ file, status: 'pending' });
      this.renderPreview(file);
    }

    async uploadAll(sessionId) {
      const results = [];
      for (const att of this.attachments) {
        const formData = new FormData();
        formData.append('file', att.file);
        formData.append('session_id', sessionId);
        const resp = await fetch('/api/chat/upload', {
          method: 'POST',
          body: formData
        });
        const data = await resp.json();
        results.push(data);
      }
      this.attachments = [];
      return results;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  # In app.py
  @app.route('/api/chat/upload', methods=['POST'])
  def upload_attachment():
      from backend.secure_upload import SecureFileUploadHandler
      from backend.r2_client import R2Client
      handler = SecureFileUploadHandler()
      file = request.files.get('file')
      session_id = request.form.get('session_id')
      if not file or not session_id:
          return jsonify({'error': 'Missing file or session_id'}), 400
      validated = handler.validate(file)
      if not validated['ok']:
          return jsonify({'error': validated['reason']}), 400
      r2 = R2Client()
      import time
      key = f'uploads/{session_id}/{int(time.time())}_{validated["safe_name"]}'
      r2.upload(key, file.read(), content_type=validated['mime_type'])
      result = {'key': key, 'filename': validated['safe_name'],
                'size': validated['size'], 'type': validated['mime_type']}
      if validated['mime_type'] == 'application/pdf':
          from backend.section_parser import extract_sections
          import fitz
          file.seek(0)
          text = extract_sections(file.read())
          result['extracted_text'] = text[:5000]
          result['extraction_status'] = 'complete'
      return jsonify(result)

```css
.athena-input--drop-active {

}
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/chat/upload (multipart/form-data)
  Request: file (binary), session_id (string)
  Response: { key, filename, size, type, extracted_text?, extraction_status? }
  Errors: 400 (invalid file/missing fields), 413 (file too large)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Drop Zone Active (File Dragged Over Input)

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |                                                       |
  |  - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -  |
  |  |                                                 |  |
  |  |              Drop file here                     |  |
  |  |                                                 |  |
  |  - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -  |
  |                                                       |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
    Dashed border: var(--accent-gold)
    Background: rgba(212, 168, 67, 0.05)

STATE: Attachment Preview (Before Send)

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [PDF] novel_approach_2025.pdf  2.3 MB  [X]           |
  |                                                       |
  |  Analyze this paper in the context of...  |  [Send]   |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Attachment In Message (After Send)

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  You:                                                 |
  |  Analyze this paper in the context of the current     |
  |  graph.                                               |
  |                                                       |
  |  +------------------------------------------+        |
  |  | [PDF]  novel_approach_2025.pdf   2.3 MB   |        |
  |  |        ● Extracting text...               |        |
  |  +------------------------------------------+        |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Extraction Complete

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  +------------------------------------------+        |
  |  | [PDF]  novel_approach_2025.pdf   2.3 MB   |        |
  |  |        ● Text extracted                   |        |
  |  +------------------------------------------+        |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
    ● indicator: green (--success) when complete

TRANSITIONS:
Drop zone appears on dragover (immediate), disappears on dragleave (immediate).
Attachment preview fades in (150ms) when file is added.
Extraction spinner rotates continuously; replaced by checkmark (200ms crossfade).
Image thumbnails fade in (200ms) after load.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User selects or drops file
  -> AttachmentManager.addFile(file) validates type and size
  -> Attachment preview rendered above input area
  -> User clicks Send
  -> AttachmentManager.uploadAll(sessionId) uploads each file
  -> POST /api/chat/upload sends file + session_id
  -> SecureFileUploadHandler validates MIME, magic bytes, size
  -> R2Client uploads to uploads/{session_id}/{timestamp}_{filename}
  -> If PDF: section_parser extracts text, stored in response
  -> Frontend renders attachment card in message with extraction status
  -> Extracted text added to conversation context for future LLM calls

ERROR PATH: Upload fails (network or server error)
  -> Toast: "Upload failed. Please try again."
  -> Attachment preview shows red X icon
  -> Message is sent without the failed attachment

ERROR PATH: PDF extraction fails
  -> Attachment card shows "Extraction failed" in warning color
  -> User can still reference the file by name; Athena acknowledges attachment

DATA STORED: Attachment metadata in chat_history.attachments JSONB column.
  File binary stored in Cloudflare R2. Extracted text in session context.
DATA CACHED: Attachment previews cached in AttachmentManager.attachments array.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

NOT APPLICABLE. File upload and extraction do not directly invoke the LLM.
The extracted text from PDFs is added to the conversation context, which is
consumed by subsequent Athena responses (handled by #044 Multi-Turn Context).
The LLM sees the extracted text as part of the context window, not as a
separate tool call.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element                | Action      | Response                                |
|------------------------|-------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Paperclip icon         | Click       | Opens native file picker                |
| Input area             | Drag over   | Drop zone indicator appears             |
| Input area             | Drop file   | File added to attachment preview        |
| Attachment preview X   | Click       | Removes attachment from pending list    |
| Attachment card (PDF)  | Click       | Opens PDF in new tab (R2 URL)           |
| Attachment card (image)| Click       | Opens lightbox with full-size image     |
| Image thumbnail        | Hover       | Subtle scale(1.02) zoom effect          |
| Send button            | Click       | Uploads all pending attachments + sends |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
  Drop zone: immediate border and background change on dragover.
  File preview: 150ms fade-in on add, 150ms fade-out on remove.
  Extraction spinner: continuous rotation, 1s per revolution.
  Extraction complete: 200ms crossfade from spinner to checkmark.
  Image thumbnail: 200ms fade-in after image loads.

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
  Paperclip button has aria-label="Attach file."
  Drop zone has aria-live="polite" and announces "Drop zone active" on dragover.
  Attachment previews are listed with role="list" and role="listitem."
  Remove button has aria-label="Remove attachment [filename]."
  Extraction status announced via aria-live="polite."

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: User attaches a 4th file (exceeds limit of 3)
   Expected: Toast: "Maximum 3 attachments per message." File rejected.
   Implementation: addFile() checks this.attachments.length >= 3.

2. Scenario: File exceeds 10MB
   Expected: Toast: "File exceeds 10MB limit." File rejected.
   Implementation: Client-side check before upload; server-side enforcement.

3. Scenario: User attaches a .exe disguised as .pdf
   Expected: Server rejects after magic byte validation. Toast: "Invalid file."
   Implementation: SecureFileUploadHandler checks magic bytes, not just extension.

4. Scenario: R2 storage is unavailable
   Expected: Upload fails gracefully. Toast: "Upload failed." Message sent without.
   Implementation: R2Client operates in no-op mode; upload returns error.

5. Scenario: PDF is password-protected
   Expected: Extraction fails. Card shows "Extraction failed (encrypted PDF)."
   Implementation: PyMuPDF raises exception on encrypted PDF; caught and reported.

6. Scenario: User drops 5 files at once
   Expected: First 3 accepted, remaining 2 rejected with toast.
   Implementation: Drop handler iterates files, addFile() enforces limit.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #050 (Rich Input Area), #044 (Multi-Turn Context)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #005 (Athena Chat Panel), #034 (Chat-Driven Graph Navigation)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Cloudflare R2 (file storage), PyMuPDF (PDF extraction)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Paperclip icon opens native file picker
[ ] Drag-and-drop onto input area works
[ ] PDF, PNG, JPEG, WEBP accepted; other types rejected
[ ] Max 3 attachments per message, max 10MB per file
[ ] PDF text extraction runs and result available in context
[ ] Attachment cards rendered inline in messages
[ ] Files uploaded to R2 with correct key pattern

VISUAL:
[ ] Drop zone: dashed gold border with "Drop file here" text
[ ] Attachment preview: icon + filename + size + remove button
[ ] Inline card: file icon + filename + size + extraction status
[ ] Image thumbnails: max 300px wide, clickable for full size

PERFORMANCE:
[ ] Upload of 5MB file completes within 5 seconds
[ ] PDF text extraction completes within 10 seconds
[ ] Attachment preview renders within 100ms of file selection

ACCESSIBILITY:
[ ] Paperclip button labeled
[ ] Drop zone announced
[ ] Attachment list uses proper ARIA roles
[ ] Extraction status announced via aria-live

EDGE CASES:
[ ] 4th file rejected with toast
[ ] Oversized file rejected
[ ] Malicious file type rejected by magic byte check

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified in Chrome, Firefox, Safari
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec
[ ] SECURITY: SecureFileUploadHandler tested with malicious inputs
[ ] R2: Upload and retrieval verified in dev environment

PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

2. COMPARE against all Done Criteria line by line
3. TEST file picker, drag-and-drop, upload, and inline rendering
4. TEST PDF extraction: valid PDF, encrypted PDF, corrupted PDF
5. VERIFY R2 upload keys match expected pattern
6. CHECK security: upload .exe with .pdf extension, oversized file, 4th attachment
7. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN until every criterion is verified
8. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round; records ALL findings below

FEATURE #044: MULTI-TURN CONTEXT
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Core Infrastructure
PRIORITY:        P0
PHASE:           A
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #006 (Conversation Memory)
BLOCKS:          #042, #043, #045, #048, #049
BLOCKED BY:      #037 (Streaming Text)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Multi-Turn Context is the foundational system that maintains conversation
coherence across multiple exchanges between the user and Athena. Without
this feature, every message would be treated as an independent query with
no awareness of what was discussed before. With it, Athena can reference
previous findings, build on earlier analysis, and maintain a progressive
understanding of the research lineage being explored.

The context system works by constructing a sliding window of conversation
history that is passed to the GROQ model with every request. The window
includes the last N messages (configurable, default 20) from the
chat_history table, formatted as alternating user/assistant turns. Each
turn is prefixed with its role. The system prompt includes a compact
graph summary generated by get_graph_summary_for_chat() from
backend/utils.py, which provides the top 15 papers by PageRank, the top
5 bottleneck nodes by pruning impact, and the top 20 edges by mutation
confidence.

The context window is not just a naive array of messages. It implements
a priority-based truncation strategy: when the total token count approaches
the model's context limit, older messages are summarized rather than
dropped. A "context summary" is generated from the oldest messages using
the GROQ fast model, condensed into a 200-token paragraph that captures
key findings, paper references, and analytical conclusions. This summary
replaces the oldest N messages, preserving semantic continuity while
freeing token budget.

The conversation history is stored server-side in the chat_history table,
keyed by session_id. The frontend never sends a messages array; it sends
only the current user message. The backend loads history from the database,
constructs the context window, and passes it to the LLM. This is a critical
security design: the client cannot inject fake conversation history.

Each message in the context window carries metadata: the message role
(user or assistant), the content, the timestamp, and optionally a
reference to graph state at that point (which nodes were visible, which
paper was focused). This metadata is not sent to the LLM but is used
by the context manager to make intelligent truncation decisions. Messages
that reference specific papers or contain analytical results are
prioritized over casual messages.

The system also injects "context anchors" at key points in the conversation.
When a user performs a significant action (loads a new graph, triggers a
pruning cascade, switches to a different DNA cluster), the context manager
inserts a synthetic system message summarizing the state change. These
anchors ensure that even after truncation, the LLM knows the current
analytical state.

Token counting uses a simple heuristic: 1 token per 4 characters for
English text. The total context budget is 6000 tokens (leaving room for
the system prompt and response). When the conversation exceeds this,
the oldest messages are summarized.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User sends any message to Athena after the first exchange.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User sends: "What is the most cited paper in this graph?" Athena
   responds with the answer (Message 1-2 in history).
2. User sends: "What mutation types does it have?" The backend loads
   messages 1-2 from chat_history, appends the new user message, and
   constructs the context window. Athena responds referencing "it" as
   the paper from the previous answer (Message 3-4).
3. After 20 exchanges, the context window is nearing its token budget.
   The context manager summarizes messages 1-10 into a 200-token
   paragraph and replaces them with a single context summary message.
4. User asks about something from message 3. The context summary
   preserves the key finding, so Athena can still reference it.
5. User loads a new graph for a different paper. The context manager
   inserts a synthetic anchor: "[Context: User loaded graph for Paper
   Y. Previous analysis was for Paper X.]" This prevents confusion.

AFTER COMPLETION:
The user experiences a seamless conversation where Athena remembers
prior context, references earlier findings by name, and maintains
analytical coherence across dozens of exchanges.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- backend/chat_guide.py: ChatGuide class, context window construction,
  priority-based truncation, context summary generation.
- backend/utils.py: get_graph_summary_for_chat() for compact graph context.
- backend/llm_client.py: ArivuLLMClient passes constructed context to GROQ.
- app.py: /api/chat POST endpoint loads history, calls ChatGuide.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Sends only current message (no history array).

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // In athena-engine.js
  class AthenaEngine {
    async sendMessage(text) {
      const response = await fetch('/api/chat', {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: JSON.stringify({
          session_id: this.sessionId,
          message: text
        })
      });
      // NOTE: No messages array sent. Server loads history from DB.
      return response;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  # In backend/chat_guide.py
  class ChatGuide:
      MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS = 6000
      SUMMARY_TOKEN_BUDGET = 200
      MAX_HISTORY_MESSAGES = 20

      def __init__(self, session_id):
          self.session_id = session_id

      def build_context_window(self, current_message, graph_summary):
          from backend import db
          rows = db.fetchall(
              'SELECT role, content, created_at FROM chat_history '
              'WHERE session_id = %s ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT %s',
              (self.session_id, self.MAX_HISTORY_MESSAGES)
          )
          messages = list(reversed(rows))
          total_tokens = self._estimate_tokens(messages)
          if total_tokens > self.MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS:
              messages = self._truncate_with_summary(messages)
          context = [
              {'role': 'system', 'content': self._build_system_prompt(graph_summary)},
          ]
          for msg in messages:
              context.append({'role': msg['role'], 'content': msg['content']})
          context.append({'role': 'user', 'content': current_message})
          return context

      def _estimate_tokens(self, messages):
          total = sum(len(m['content']) for m in messages)
          return total // 4

      def _truncate_with_summary(self, messages):
          split = len(messages) // 2
          old_messages = messages[:split]
          recent_messages = messages[split:]
          summary = self._summarize_messages(old_messages)
          return [{'role': 'system', 'content': f'Prior context: {summary}'}] + recent_messages

      def _summarize_messages(self, messages):
          from backend.llm_client import ArivuLLMClient
          llm = ArivuLLMClient()
          text = '\n'.join(f"{m['role']}: {m['content']}" for m in messages)
          result = llm.query(
              model='fast',
              system_prompt='Summarize this conversation in under 200 tokens. '
                            'Preserve paper names, key findings, and analytical conclusions.',
              user_prompt=text[:3000],
              max_tokens=self.SUMMARY_TOKEN_BUDGET
          )
          return result

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```python
# Context anchor injection (in ChatGuide)
def inject_context_anchor(self, event_type, details):
    anchor = f'[Context: {event_type}. {details}]'
    from backend import db
    db.execute(
        'INSERT INTO chat_history (session_id, role, content) '
        'VALUES (%s, %s, %s)',
        (self.session_id, 'system', anchor)
    )

# Token estimation heuristic
def _estimate_tokens(self, messages):
    total_chars = sum(len(m['content']) for m in messages)
    return total_chars // 4  # ~4 chars per token for English
```

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/chat
  Request: { session_id, message }
  Response: SSE stream of Athena response tokens
  NOTE: No messages array in request. History loaded server-side.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Normal Conversation (Context Active, Invisible to User)

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  You: What is the most cited paper?                   |
  |                                                       |
  |  Athena: The most cited paper is "Attention Is All    |
  |  You Need" (Vaswani 2017) with PageRank 0.087.       |
  |                                                       |
  |  You: What mutation types does it have?               |
  |                                                       |
  |  Athena: It has 12 adoption edges, 5 generalization   |
  |  edges, and 3 hybridization edges. The dominant...    |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+

  No visible context UI. The system is invisible to the user.
  Context operates entirely in the backend.

STATE: Context Summary Triggered (Debug Only)

  In debug mode (FLASK_DEBUG=true), a small indicator appears
  in the chat header:

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  Athena              [Context: 18/20 msgs | 5200 tkn] |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+

  This indicator is hidden in production.

TRANSITIONS:
No visible transitions. Context management is backend-only.
Debug indicator updates on each message (if debug mode is active).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User sends message to Athena
  -> Frontend POST /api/chat with { session_id, message }
  -> Backend stores user message in chat_history
  -> ChatGuide.build_context_window() loads last 20 messages from DB
  -> Token estimation: if over 6000 tokens, summarize oldest half
  -> Context window constructed: system prompt + history + current message
  -> ArivuLLMClient sends context to GROQ model
  -> GROQ returns response tokens (streamed)
  -> Backend stores assistant response in chat_history
  -> SSE stream delivers response to frontend

ERROR PATH: chat_history query fails
  -> Fallback: send only system prompt + current message (no history)
  -> Log warning: "Context history unavailable, single-turn mode"

ERROR PATH: Context summarization fails (GROQ error)
  -> Fallback: drop oldest messages instead of summarizing
  -> Recent messages preserved; older context lost

DATA STORED: All messages in chat_history (session_id, role, content, created_at).
  Context anchors stored as role='system' messages.
DATA CACHED: Context window cached in ChatGuide instance per request.
  Graph summary cached via get_graph_summary_for_chat() (recomputed on graph change).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MODEL: GROQ fast model (llama-3.1-8b-instant) for context summarization.
  GROQ smart model (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) for main conversation via ChatGuide.
STAGE: Context window construction (before every LLM call)
PROMPT:
  For context summarization:
  System: "Summarize this conversation in under 200 tokens. Preserve paper
  names, PageRank scores, mutation types, key findings, and analytical
  conclusions. Do not add new information."
  User: [Concatenated old messages, truncated to 3000 chars]

  For main conversation:
  System: "You are Athena, the AI research guide for Arivu. You have access
  to a citation ancestry graph. Here is the current graph summary:
  [graph_summary]. Answer the user's question using the graph data and
  conversation history."
  User: [Current user message]

OUTPUT FORMAT: Free-form text (for summarization: paragraph under 200 tokens).
FALLBACK: If summarization fails, drop oldest messages. If main call fails,
  return error message to user.
TOKEN BUDGET: Context summary: 200 output tokens. Main conversation: system
  prompt ~800 tokens, history ~5000 tokens, response ~2000 tokens.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element             | Action       | Response                                  |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Chat input          | Send message | Context window built, LLM called          |
| Graph state change  | Load/prune   | Context anchor injected into chat_history  |
| Debug indicator     | Hover        | Shows token count and message count        |
| Context overflow    | Automatic    | Oldest messages summarized silently        |
| Session switch      | Navigate     | New context window loaded from new session |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
  No user-facing animations. Context management is invisible.
  Debug indicator (if visible): text updates on each message without animation.

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
  No accessibility impact. Feature is entirely backend. The conversation
  UI already handles screen reader support via #037 (Streaming Text).
  Context anchors are not displayed to users and do not affect ARIA.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Conversation exceeds 100 messages
   Expected: Only last 20 loaded. Older messages summarized if needed.
   Implementation: LIMIT 20 in SQL query. Summary covers oldest half.

2. Scenario: User switches to a completely different graph mid-conversation
   Expected: Context anchor injected. LLM is aware of the switch.
   Implementation: inject_context_anchor('graph_change', details) called on load.

3. Scenario: GROQ rate limit hit during context summarization
   Expected: Skip summarization, drop oldest messages as fallback.
   Implementation: try/except around summarize call; fallback to drop.

4. Scenario: Single message is extremely long (over 4000 chars)
   Expected: Message truncated to first 2000 chars in context window.
   Implementation: Content truncated in build_context_window loop.

5. Scenario: Session has no prior messages (first message)
   Expected: Context window contains only system prompt + user message.
   Implementation: Empty history list handled; no summary needed.

6. Scenario: chat_history table is temporarily unavailable
   Expected: Single-turn mode. Athena responds without history context.
   Implementation: DB query wrapped in try/except; fallback to empty history.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON: #037 (Streaming Text)
ENABLES: #042 (Suggested Follow-Ups), #043 (File/Image Attachments),
  #045 (Collapsible Long Responses), #048 (Conversation History Sidebar),
  #049 (Model/Mode Selector)
ENHANCES: #005 (Athena Chat Panel), #006 (Conversation Memory)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: GROQ API (for both summarization and conversation)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

[ ] Backend loads last 20 messages from chat_history per session
[ ] Context window includes system prompt, history, and current message
[ ] No messages array accepted from client (server-side only)
[ ] Context summarization triggers when tokens exceed 6000
[ ] Context anchors injected on graph state changes
[ ] Conversation remains coherent across 20+ exchanges

VISUAL:

[ ] Debug indicator hidden in production
[ ] Chat messages display normally (no change to rendering)

PERFORMANCE:

[ ] Context summarization completes within 3 seconds
[ ] Database query for 20 messages completes within 50ms

ACCESSIBILITY:

[ ] Existing screen reader support unaffected
[ ] Context anchors do not appear in user-visible message list

EDGE CASES:

[ ] 100+ message conversations handled (truncation works)
[ ] GROQ failure during summarization handled (fallback to drop)

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Multi-turn conversation verified coherent
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec
[ ] SECURITY: Verified no client-side history injection possible
[ ] TOKEN COUNT: Verified context stays within budget

2. COMPARE against all Done Criteria line by line

FEATURE #045: COLLAPSIBLE LONG RESPONSES
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 8
OVERLAPS WITH:   #038 (Markdown Rendering)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #037 (Streaming Text), #038 (Markdown Rendering)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Collapsible Long Responses adds an automatic collapse mechanism to Athena
responses that exceed a configurable line threshold. When Athena generates
a detailed analysis of a paper's ancestry graph, the response can easily
reach 40-80 lines covering PageRank rankings, mutation type breakdowns,
DNA cluster compositions, pruning impact calculations, and genealogy
narratives. Displaying the full response inline can overwhelm the chat
scroll and push the input area far below the viewport, breaking the
conversational flow.

This feature detects responses that exceed 15 lines (configurable via a
constant in athena-engine.js) and automatically collapses the content
after the first 8 lines, showing a "Show more" button. The collapsed
state shows a subtle gradient fade at the bottom of the visible content,
transitioning from var(--text-primary) to transparent over the last 2
lines, creating a visual hint that more content exists below.

The "Show more" button is styled in the dot matrix aesthetic: a compact
bar spanning the full width of the message bubble with the text "Show
more (32 more lines)" in var(--text-secondary) and a small downward
chevron icon. Clicking the button expands the response smoothly over
300ms (max-height transition) and replaces the button with "Show less"
plus an upward chevron. The expand/collapse state is tracked per message
in a lightweight Map object (message ID to boolean) on the frontend.

The collapse system operates on the rendered HTML, not the raw markdown.
It counts the number of visible block-level elements (paragraphs, list
items, code blocks, headings) rather than raw newlines. This prevents
a single long paragraph from being collapsed prematurely and ensures
that structured responses with tables, code blocks, and lists are
measured by their visual footprint.

Collapsible responses interact with Markdown Rendering (#038): the full
markdown is always rendered to HTML first, then the collapse wrapper
is applied to the rendered output. This ensures that partial collapse
does not break markdown structure (no orphaned list items or unclosed
code blocks). The collapse boundary is always placed between complete
block-level elements.

During streaming (#037), the collapse is not applied until streaming
completes. While Athena is actively typing, the full response is visible.
Once the stream ends, the collapse check runs and applies the wrapper
if the line count exceeds the threshold. This prevents the jarring
experience of content collapsing while the user is reading it.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
Athena completes a response that exceeds 15 rendered block elements.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "Give me a complete breakdown of all mutation types in this
   graph." Athena streams a 45-line response covering 7 mutation types
   with counts, examples, and percentages for each.
2. As the stream completes, the response is measured: 45 block elements.
   Since 45 > 15, the collapse wrapper is applied after the first 8
   block elements.
3. The user sees the first 8 lines of the response, followed by a
   gradient fade and a "Show more (37 more lines)" button.
4. User clicks "Show more." The response expands smoothly over 300ms.
   The button changes to "Show less" with an upward chevron.
5. User scrolls through the full response, then clicks "Show less."
   The response collapses back to 8 lines over 300ms.
6. The collapse state is remembered for this message. If the user
   scrolls away and returns, the message remains in its last state.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Long responses are manageable. The chat scroll is not overwhelmed.
The user can expand any collapsed response individually. Each message
maintains its own expand/collapse state independently.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-collapse.js: CollapseManager class, block counting,
  collapse wrapper, expand/collapse toggle.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Hook into onStreamComplete to trigger
  collapse check on completed responses.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Gradient fade overlay, "Show more/less"
  button styles, max-height transition.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // In athena-collapse.js
  class CollapseManager {
    constructor() {
      this.COLLAPSE_THRESHOLD = 15;
      this.VISIBLE_LINES = 8;
      this.expandedMessages = new Map();
    }

    checkAndCollapse(messageElement, messageId) {
      const blocks = messageElement.querySelectorAll(
        'p, li, pre, h1, h2, h3, h4, h5, h6, tr, blockquote'
      );
      if (blocks.length <= this.COLLAPSE_THRESHOLD) return;

      const wrapper = document.createElement('div');
      wrapper.className = 'athena-collapsible';
      wrapper.dataset.messageId = messageId;
      wrapper.dataset.totalBlocks = blocks.length;

      const visibleHeight = this._measureHeight(blocks, this.VISIBLE_LINES);
      wrapper.style.maxHeight = visibleHeight + 'px';
      wrapper.classList.add('athena-collapsible--collapsed');

      messageElement.querySelector('.message-content').appendChild(
        this._createToggleButton(messageId, blocks.length - this.VISIBLE_LINES)
      );
    }

    toggle(messageId) {
      const wrapper = document.querySelector(
        `.athena-collapsible[data-message-id="${messageId}"]`
      );
      if (!wrapper) return;
      const isExpanded = this.expandedMessages.get(messageId) || false;
      if (isExpanded) {
        wrapper.classList.add('athena-collapsible--collapsed');
        wrapper.classList.remove('athena-collapsible--expanded');
      } else {
        wrapper.classList.remove('athena-collapsible--collapsed');
        wrapper.classList.add('athena-collapsible--expanded');
      }
      this.expandedMessages.set(messageId, !isExpanded);
      this._updateToggleButton(messageId);
    }

    _createToggleButton(messageId, hiddenCount) {
      const btn = document.createElement('button');
      btn.className = 'athena-collapse-toggle';
      btn.innerHTML = `Show more (${hiddenCount} more lines) <span class="chevron-down"></span>`;
      btn.addEventListener('click', () => this.toggle(messageId));
      return btn;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  No backend changes required. Collapse is a purely frontend feature.
  The backend always returns the full response content.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```css
.athena-collapsible--collapsed {
  max-height: var(--collapse-height);
  overflow: hidden;
  transition: max-height 300ms ease-in-out;
  position: relative;
}
.athena-collapsible--collapsed::after {
  content: '';
  position: absolute;
  bottom: 0;
  left: 0;
  right: 0;
  height: 48px;
  background: linear-gradient(
    to bottom,
    transparent,
    var(--bg-primary)
  );
  pointer-events: none;
}
.athena-collapsible--expanded {
  max-height: none;
  overflow: visible;
  transition: max-height 300ms ease-in-out;
}
.athena-collapse-toggle {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
  width: 100%;
  padding: 8px;
  background: var(--bg-surface);
  border: 1px solid var(--bg-elevated);
  border-radius: 6px;
  color: var(--text-secondary);
  font-size: 13px;
  cursor: pointer;
  margin-top: 8px;
  transition: background 150ms;
}
.athena-collapse-toggle:hover {
  background: var(--bg-elevated);
}
.chevron-down, .chevron-up {
  display: inline-block;
  width: 12px;
  height: 12px;
  margin-left: 6px;
  border-right: 2px solid var(--text-secondary);
  border-bottom: 2px solid var(--text-secondary);
}
.chevron-down { transform: rotate(45deg); }
.chevron-up { transform: rotate(-135deg); }
```

API CONTRACTS:
No API changes. This is a frontend-only feature.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Response Collapsed (Default for Long Responses)

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  Athena:                                              |
  |  The mutation type breakdown for this graph shows:    |
  |                                                       |
  |  - Adoption: 34 edges (42%)                           |
  |  - Generalization: 18 edges (22%)                     |
  |  - Specialization: 12 edges (15%)                     |
  |  - Hybridization: 8 edges (10%)                       |
  |  ~~~ gradient fade ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~    |
  |                                                       |
  |  [ Show more (37 more lines)              v ]         |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Response Expanded

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  Athena:                                              |
  |  The mutation type breakdown for this graph shows:    |
  |  ... (full 45-line response visible) ...              |
  |  The conclusion is that adoption dominates this       |
  |  particular lineage, consistent with the PageRank     |
  |  distribution clustering around the 2017 papers.      |
  |                                                       |
  |  [ Show less                              ^ ]         |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+

TRANSITIONS:
Collapse: max-height transitions from full height to collapsed height (300ms
  ease-in-out). Gradient fade fades in simultaneously.
Expand: max-height transitions from collapsed to full (300ms ease-in-out).
  Gradient fade fades out. Toggle button text changes.
Toggle button hover: background darkens over 150ms.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Athena completes streaming a response
  -> athena-engine.js calls onStreamComplete
  -> CollapseManager.checkAndCollapse(messageElement, messageId)
  -> Count block-level elements in rendered HTML
  -> If count > 15: apply collapse wrapper with max-height
  -> Add gradient fade overlay and "Show more" toggle button
  -> User clicks toggle -> CollapseManager.toggle(messageId)
  -> Expand/collapse state toggled in expandedMessages Map
  -> CSS class toggled for smooth animation

ERROR PATH: Block counting fails (unusual HTML structure)
  -> Fallback: do not collapse. Show full response.
  -> Implementation: try/catch around checkAndCollapse.

DATA STORED: None. Collapse state is frontend-only, per-session.
DATA CACHED: expandedMessages Map (messageId -> boolean) in CollapseManager.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

NOT APPLICABLE. Collapsible responses is a purely frontend rendering
feature. The LLM generates the full response regardless of collapse.
No LLM interaction occurs during expand/collapse operations.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action      | Response                                  |
|----------------------|-------------|-------------------------------------------|
| "Show more" button   | Click       | Expands response, button becomes "less"   |
| "Show less" button   | Click       | Collapses response, button becomes "more" |
| Toggle button        | Hover       | Background darkens (bg-elevated)          |
| Toggle button        | Focus       | Outline ring for keyboard navigation      |
| Toggle button        | Enter key   | Same as click (expand/collapse)           |
| Collapsed content    | Select text | User can still select visible text        |
| Gradient fade        | Visual only | Indicates more content below              |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
  Expand: 300ms ease-in-out max-height transition from collapsed to full.
  Collapse: 300ms ease-in-out max-height transition from full to collapsed.
  Gradient fade: CSS pseudo-element, appears/disappears with collapse state.
  Toggle button hover: 150ms background-color transition.

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
  Toggle button has aria-expanded="true/false" attribute.
  Toggle button has aria-controls pointing to the collapsible content ID.
  Collapsed content has aria-hidden="false" (content is still in DOM).
  Screen reader announces: "Show more, 37 more lines" / "Show less."
  Keyboard: Tab to toggle button, Enter/Space to activate.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Response has exactly 15 block elements (threshold boundary)
   Expected: Not collapsed. Threshold is "greater than 15."
   Implementation: blocks.length <= this.COLLAPSE_THRESHOLD returns early.

2. Scenario: Response is a single very long paragraph (no block breaks)
   Expected: Not collapsed (1 block element, under threshold).
   Implementation: Block counting counts <p> tags; single paragraph = 1.

3. Scenario: Response contains a large code block (30 lines in <pre>)
   Expected: The <pre> counts as 1 block. Not collapsed unless other
   blocks push total above 15.
   Implementation: querySelectorAll counts <pre> as single element.

4. Scenario: User expands a response, scrolls away, scrolls back
   Expected: Response remains expanded (state preserved in Map).
   Implementation: expandedMessages Map persists during session.

5. Scenario: Streaming is interrupted mid-response
   Expected: No collapse applied until stream completes (or fails).
   Implementation: checkAndCollapse only called from onStreamComplete.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #005 (Athena Chat Panel)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: None

[ ] Collapse does not apply during active streaming

FEATURE #046: TYPING INDICATOR
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Core Infrastructure
PRIORITY:        P0
PHASE:           A
ESTIMATED HOURS: 4
OVERLAPS WITH:   #037 (Streaming Text)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      NONE

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Typing Indicator provides visual feedback that Athena is processing the
user's message and preparing a response. Between the moment the user
sends a message and the moment the first token arrives via SSE streaming,
there is a latency gap that can range from 500ms to 5 seconds depending
on GROQ API load, context window construction, and graph summary
generation. Without a typing indicator, the user stares at a static chat
with no confirmation that their message was received or that anything is
happening. This creates uncertainty and breaks the conversational rhythm.

The typing indicator appears as a small animated element in the Athena
response area, positioned where the next message will appear. It consists
of three dots arranged horizontally that pulse in sequence, following the
dot matrix aesthetic of the Arivu interface. The dots use var(--accent-teal)
at their peak opacity and fade to var(--bg-elevated) at their lowest,
creating a smooth wave-like animation that loops continuously.

The indicator appears immediately when the user sends a message (within
one animation frame of the send action). It disappears the moment the
first SSE token is received from the streaming response. This creates
a seamless handoff: dots pulsing, then the first word of Athena's
response replaces them. The transition is a 100ms crossfade where the
dots fade out while the first token fades in.

The typing indicator is accompanied by a status label below the dots
that provides context about what Athena is doing. The label cycles
through contextual messages based on the operation in progress:
"Analyzing graph context..." (during context window construction),
"Consulting Pathfinder..." (during LLM inference), "Preparing
response..." (generic fallback). These labels are driven by SSE events
from the backend that indicate processing stage.

The typing indicator also serves as an implicit timeout detection
mechanism. If the indicator has been visible for more than 30 seconds
without receiving a first token, a warning state activates: the dots
change color to var(--warning) and the label changes to "Taking longer
than expected..." After 60 seconds, the indicator is replaced by an
error message: "Response timed out. Please try again." with a Retry
button.

The indicator is sensitive to connection state. If the SSE connection
drops while the indicator is active, it switches to a reconnection
state with the label "Reconnecting..." and the dots animating in
var(--warning). Once reconnected, it resumes normal state.

Implementation uses pure CSS animations for the dots (no JavaScript
animation loops) to ensure smooth 60fps rendering even under heavy
DOM activity from graph rendering. The dot elements are created once
and shown/hidden via CSS classes.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User sends a message (clicks Send or presses Enter).

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User types "What is the PageRank distribution in this graph?" and
   presses Enter. The message appears in the chat immediately.
2. Within one frame, the typing indicator appears below the user's
   message: three pulsing teal dots with the label "Analyzing graph
   context..."
3. After 800ms, the SSE connection is established. The label changes
   to "Consulting Pathfinder..." as the GROQ model processes.
4. After 2.1 seconds total, the first token arrives. The dots fade out
   (100ms) and the first word of Athena's response fades in (100ms).
5. Streaming continues normally. The typing indicator is no longer
   visible.
6. If instead 30 seconds pass with no token, the dots turn amber
   and the label changes to "Taking longer than expected..."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Typing indicator is hidden. Athena's response streams normally. The
indicator DOM elements remain in the document (hidden) ready for the
next message.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-typing.js: TypingIndicator class, show/hide/timeout
  logic, status label management.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Call TypingIndicator.show() on send,
  TypingIndicator.hide() on first SSE token.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Dot animation keyframes, indicator layout,
  status label styles, warning state colors.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // In athena-typing.js
  class TypingIndicator {
    constructor(container) {
      this.container = container;
      this.element = this._createIndicatorElement();
      this.timeoutId = null;
      this.warningTimeoutId = null;
      this.WARNING_DELAY = 30000;
      this.ERROR_DELAY = 60000;
      container.appendChild(this.element);
    }

    _createIndicatorElement() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-typing-indicator';
      el.setAttribute('aria-live', 'polite');
      el.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Athena is typing');
      el.innerHTML = `
        <div class="typing-dots">
          <span class="typing-dot"></span>
          <span class="typing-dot"></span>
          <span class="typing-dot"></span>
        </div>
        <span class="typing-label">Preparing response...</span>
      `;
      el.style.display = 'none';
      return el;
    }

    show(stage) {
      this.element.style.display = 'flex';
      this.setStage(stage || 'Preparing response...');
      this.element.classList.remove('typing--warning', 'typing--error');
      this.warningTimeoutId = setTimeout(() => this.showWarning(), this.WARNING_DELAY);
      this.timeoutId = setTimeout(() => this.showError(), this.ERROR_DELAY);
      this.scrollIntoView();
    }

    hide() {
      this.element.style.display = 'none';
      clearTimeout(this.warningTimeoutId);
      clearTimeout(this.timeoutId);
    }

    setStage(label) {
      this.element.querySelector('.typing-label').textContent = label;
    }

    showWarning() {
      this.element.classList.add('typing--warning');
      this.setStage('Taking longer than expected...');
    }

    showError() {
      this.hide();
      this.container.dispatchEvent(
        new CustomEvent('athena-timeout', { detail: { message: 'Response timed out.' } })
      );
    }

    scrollIntoView() {
      this.element.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'end' });
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  No backend changes required for the typing indicator itself. The SSE
  stream already sends events; the first token event triggers the hide.
  Stage labels are driven by existing SSE event types:
  - "building_context" -> "Analyzing graph context..."
  - "querying_llm" -> "Consulting Pathfinder..."
  - "streaming" -> indicator hides (first token)

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```css
.athena-typing-indicator {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  gap: 10px;
  padding: 12px 16px;
  opacity: 1;
  transition: opacity 100ms ease-out;
}
.typing-dots {
  display: flex;
  gap: 4px;
}
.typing-dot {
  width: 8px;
  height: 8px;
  border-radius: 50%;
  background: var(--accent-teal);
  animation: typingPulse 1.4s infinite ease-in-out;
}
.typing-dot:nth-child(2) { animation-delay: 0.2s; }
.typing-dot:nth-child(3) { animation-delay: 0.4s; }
@keyframes typingPulse {
  0%, 80%, 100% { opacity: 0.3; transform: scale(0.8); }
  40% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); }
}
.typing-label {
  font-size: 12px;
  color: var(--text-muted);
  font-style: italic;
}
.typing--warning .typing-dot {
  background: var(--warning);
}
.typing--warning .typing-label {
  color: var(--warning);
}
```

API CONTRACTS:
No new API endpoints. Uses existing SSE stream from /api/graph/stream
and /api/chat. Stage labels derived from SSE event types.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Typing Active (Normal)

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  You: What is the PageRank distribution?              |
  |                                                       |
  |  ● ● ●   Analyzing graph context...                  |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
    Dots: var(--accent-teal), 8px, pulsing wave
    Label: var(--text-muted), 12px, italic

STATE: Typing Warning (30s+ without response)

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  You: What is the PageRank distribution?              |
  |                                                       |
  |  ● ● ●   Taking longer than expected...              |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
    Dots: var(--warning), same animation
    Label: var(--warning), 12px, italic

STATE: Typing to Response Transition

  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  You: What is the PageRank distribution?              |
  |                                                       |
  |  Athena: The PageRank distribution shows...           |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
    Dots fade out (100ms), first token fades in (100ms)

TRANSITIONS:
Show: display changes from none to flex (immediate).
Hide: 100ms opacity fade-out, then display: none.
Warning: dots change color via CSS class toggle (150ms transition).
Stage label: text changes immediately (no animation).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User clicks Send
  -> athena-engine.js calls TypingIndicator.show('Preparing response...')
  -> Indicator visible with pulsing dots and label
  -> SSE connection opened to /api/chat
  -> Backend sends stage events (building_context, querying_llm)
  -> Frontend updates label via TypingIndicator.setStage()
  -> First response token arrives via SSE
  -> athena-engine.js calls TypingIndicator.hide()
  -> Response text begins rendering in place of indicator

ERROR PATH: No token received within 60 seconds
  -> At 30s: typing--warning class applied, label changes
  -> At 60s: TypingIndicator.showError() fires custom event
  -> UI shows "Response timed out. Please try again." with Retry button

ERROR PATH: SSE connection drops during typing
  -> Reconnection logic (from #037) activates
  -> Indicator label changes to "Reconnecting..."
  -> Dots animate in warning color
  -> On reconnection: label reverts to previous stage

DATA STORED: None. Indicator state is ephemeral.
DATA CACHED: None.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

NOT APPLICABLE. The typing indicator is a frontend UI element that does
not interact with the LLM. It reacts to SSE events generated by the
backend during LLM processing but does not invoke or configure the LLM.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element              | Action        | Response                                |
|----------------------|---------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Send button          | Click         | Typing indicator appears immediately    |
| Enter key            | Press in input| Typing indicator appears immediately    |
| First SSE token      | Received      | Typing indicator hides, response starts |
| 30s timeout          | Automatic     | Warning state activates (amber dots)    |
| 60s timeout          | Automatic     | Error message replaces indicator        |
| Retry button         | Click         | Re-sends last message, indicator shows  |
| SSE disconnect       | Automatic     | "Reconnecting..." label shown           |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
  Dot pulse: 1.4s infinite ease-in-out. Each dot offset by 0.2s.
  Peak: opacity 1, scale(1). Valley: opacity 0.3, scale(0.8).
  Warning transition: 150ms color change on dots and label.
  Hide: 100ms opacity fade-out.

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
  Indicator container has aria-live="polite" for screen reader updates.
  aria-label="Athena is typing" on the indicator element.
  Label text is read by screen readers as it changes.
  Warning state announced via aria-live update.
  Error state: Retry button is focusable and labeled.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Scenario: Response arrives within 50ms (very fast)
   Expected: Indicator briefly flashes, then hides. Acceptable.
   Implementation: No minimum display time; immediate hide on first token.

2. Scenario: User sends another message while indicator is active
   Expected: Previous request cancelled. Indicator resets for new request.
   Implementation: hide() + show() called in sequence on new send.

3. Scenario: SSE connection never established (blocked network)
   Expected: 60-second timeout triggers error message.
   Implementation: Same timeout logic applies regardless of connection state.

4. Scenario: Multiple rapid sends (spam clicking)
   Expected: Only the latest message's indicator is active. Previous cancelled.
   Implementation: Each send resets the indicator and timeout timers.

5. Scenario: Page is in background tab (browser throttles animation)
   Expected: Animation may stutter but indicator logic still works.
   Implementation: CSS animation; browser may throttle but resumes on focus.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #037 (Streaming Text), #005 (Athena Chat Panel)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: None

FEATURE #047: NEW CHAT / CLEAR CONVERSATION
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Chat Controls
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           A
ESTIMATED HOURS: 6
OVERLAPS WITH:   #048 (Conversation History Sidebar)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #044 (Multi-Turn Context)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

New Chat / Clear Conversation provides a mechanism for the user to start
a fresh conversation with Athena while optionally preserving the current
conversation in history. When a researcher finishes analyzing one paper's
citation graph and wants to explore a different paper, they need to reset
the conversation context. Continuing in the same thread would confuse
Athena's multi-turn context (#044) by mixing references to two different
graphs and their respective PageRank scores, mutation types, and pruning
impacts.

The feature adds a "New Chat" button to the Athena chat header. The button
is styled as a compact icon (a plus sign inside a speech bubble) positioned
at the right edge of the header bar. Clicking it opens a confirmation
dialog styled in the dot matrix aesthetic: a small modal with the message
"Start a new conversation? Current chat will be saved to history." and
two buttons: "New Chat" (accent-gold) and "Cancel" (text-secondary).

When confirmed, the system performs three actions in sequence:
1. Archives the current conversation by storing its session_id and a
   summary (first user message + message count) in the conversation
   history index (used by #048 Conversation History Sidebar).
2. Generates a new session_id via the SessionManager (backend/
   session_manager.py) and updates the frontend reference.
3. Clears the chat messages container, resets the multi-turn context
   window, and resets the typing indicator state.

The new conversation starts with a clean slate: no chat history in the
database for the new session_id, no context window, and a fresh Athena
greeting message that reflects the current graph state. If a graph is
loaded, the greeting is: "New conversation started. I can see the ancestry
graph for [Paper Title]. What would you like to explore?" If no graph
is loaded: "New conversation started. Load a paper to begin exploring."

There is also a "Clear" variant accessible via the slash command /clear.
Unlike "New Chat," the /clear command does not archive the conversation.
It permanently removes all messages from the current session and resets
context. A confirmation dialog appears: "Clear all messages? This cannot
be undone." This is a destructive action intended for conversations the
user does not want to keep.

The keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Shift+N (Cmd+Shift+N on macOS) triggers the
"New Chat" action directly, bypassing the button click.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User clicks the "New Chat" button in the chat header, or types /clear,
or presses Ctrl+Shift+N.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User has been analyzing the "Attention Is All You Need" graph for 20
   messages. They now want to explore a different paper.
2. User clicks the "New Chat" button (plus-in-speech-bubble icon) in
   the chat header.
3. A confirmation dialog appears: "Start a new conversation? Current
   chat will be saved to history."
4. User clicks "New Chat." The current conversation is archived.
5. The chat container clears with a 200ms fade-out animation. All
   messages disappear.
6. A new session_id is generated. The context window is reset.
7. Athena's greeting message fades in (200ms): "New conversation started.
   I can see the ancestry graph for Attention Is All You Need. What
   would you like to explore?"
8. The user types their first question in the new conversation.

AFTER COMPLETION:
New conversation is active with a clean context. Previous conversation
is archived and accessible via the Conversation History Sidebar (#048)
if implemented. The graph remains loaded (conversation reset does not
affect graph state).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena-engine.js: newChat() and clearChat() methods, keyboard
  shortcut handler, confirmation dialog rendering.
- static/js/athena-chat.js: Chat container clear animation, greeting
  message rendering.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Confirmation dialog styles, clear animation.
- app.py: /api/chat/new POST endpoint for archiving and session creation.
- backend/session_manager.py: SessionManager.create_session() for new ID.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  // In athena-engine.js
  class AthenaEngine {
    newChat() {
      this.showConfirmDialog(
        'Start a new conversation? Current chat will be saved to history.',
        'New Chat',
        async () => {
          await this.archiveCurrentSession();
          await this.resetSession();
        }
      );
    }

    clearChat() {
      this.showConfirmDialog(
        'Clear all messages? This cannot be undone.',
        'Clear',
        async () => {
          await fetch('/api/chat/clear', {
            method: 'POST',
            headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
            body: JSON.stringify({ session_id: this.sessionId })
          });
          this.resetUI();
        }
      );
    }

    async archiveCurrentSession() {
      await fetch('/api/chat/archive', {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: JSON.stringify({ session_id: this.sessionId })
      });
    }

    async resetSession() {
      const resp = await fetch('/api/chat/new', {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: JSON.stringify({ previous_session_id: this.sessionId })
      });
      const data = await resp.json();
      this.sessionId = data.session_id;
      this.resetUI();
      this.showGreeting(data.greeting);
    }

    resetUI() {
      const container = this.messagesContainer;
      container.classList.add('chat-fade-out');
      setTimeout(() => {
        container.innerHTML = '';
        container.classList.remove('chat-fade-out');
      }, 200);
    }
  }

  // Keyboard shortcut
  document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {
    if ((e.ctrlKey || e.metaKey) && e.shiftKey && e.key === 'N') {
      e.preventDefault();
      athenaEngine.newChat();
    }
  });

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
  # In app.py
  @app.route('/api/chat/new', methods=['POST'])
  def new_chat():
      data = request.json
      previous_session_id = data.get('previous_session_id')
      from backend.session_manager import SessionManager
      sm = SessionManager()
      new_session = sm.create_session()
      graph_title = None
      if previous_session_id:
          row = db.fetchone(
              'SELECT seed_paper_title FROM graphs g '
              'JOIN session_graphs sg ON g.graph_id = sg.graph_id '
              'WHERE sg.session_id = %s ORDER BY g.last_accessed DESC LIMIT 1',
              (previous_session_id,)
          )
          if row:
              graph_title = row['seed_paper_title']
      greeting = 'New conversation started. '
      if graph_title:
          greeting += f'I can see the ancestry graph for {graph_title}. What would you like to explore?'
      else:
          greeting += 'Load a paper to begin exploring.'
      db.execute(
          'INSERT INTO chat_history (session_id, role, content) VALUES (%s, %s, %s)',
          (new_session['session_id'], 'assistant', greeting)
      )
      return jsonify({'session_id': new_session['session_id'], 'greeting': greeting})

  @app.route('/api/chat/clear', methods=['POST'])
  def clear_chat():
      data = request.json
      session_id = data['session_id']
      db.execute('DELETE FROM chat_history WHERE session_id = %s', (session_id,))
      return jsonify({'status': 'cleared'})

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```css
.chat-fade-out {
```
