════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #112: EXTENDED DEBATE MODE
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Special Modes
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           E
ESTIMATED HOURS: 18
OVERLAPS WITH:   #035 (Peer Discussion Simulator), #049 (Mode Selector),
                 #113 (Devil's Advocate Mode)
BLOCKS:          None
BLOCKED BY:      #049 (Mode Selector), #001 (Context-Aware Responses),
                 #037 (Streaming Text), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
                 #051 (Inline Citations/Sources)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Extended Debate Mode transforms Athena from a single-voice research assistant
into a multi-perspective arena where AI personas argue different positions
about a research topic, each grounded in the actual citation graph data. When
activated, the user poses a research question -- "Did deep learning kill
traditional computer vision?" or "Is the transformer architecture overfitted
to language tasks?" -- and three to five AI personas take positions, present
evidence from the graph, challenge each other's claims, and progressively
refine their arguments through structured rounds of debate. The user observes,
moderates, and can intervene at any point.

This feature exists because research understanding requires confrontation of
ideas, not just summarization. A single-voice assistant presents the consensus
view. Extended Debate Mode reveals the TENSIONS in the citation graph: which
papers contradict each other, which adoption chains might be methodologically
fragile, which clusters represent genuinely competing paradigms versus
incremental variations. The debate format forces the LLM to steel-man multiple
positions, each backed by specific papers, PageRank scores, mutation types,
and pruning impact data from the graph.

Each persona is constructed from the citation graph itself. The "Traditionalist"
persona draws from older, high-PageRank papers with many adoption chains. The
"Innovator" persona champions recent papers with generalization or hybridization
mutations. The "Skeptic" challenges all claims using contradiction edges and
low-confidence analyses. The "Synthesizer" seeks common ground by identifying
cross-cluster connections. An optional "Methodologist" focuses exclusively on
the methodological adoption chains and their validity. Personas are NOT generic
archetypes -- they are data-driven viewpoints anchored to specific regions of
the citation graph.

The debate proceeds in rounds. Round 1: Opening Statements (each persona
presents their position with 2-3 cited claims). Round 2: Challenges (each
persona responds to the strongest opposing claim). Round 3: Rebuttals (direct
responses to challenges, introducing new evidence). Round 4: Closing (each
persona summarizes their final position, noting where they concede). The user
can extend the debate with additional rounds, redirect the topic, or ask a
specific persona to elaborate. The entire debate is rendered in a distinctive
panel layout with persona avatars (dot-matrix styled geometric shapes, not
photographs), color-coded argument threads, and inline citations linking to
graph nodes.

This feature leverages the mutation type taxonomy deeply. When the
Traditionalist says "The original convolution approach has 34 direct adoption
descendants," that 34 comes from counting adoption-type edges in the graph.
When the Innovator responds "But 8 of those are generalization mutations that
already moved beyond the original," the 8 is a real count of generalization
edges. The debate is not LLM roleplay -- it is structured argumentation
grounded in computed graph metrics.

The dot matrix aesthetic shapes the debate presentation: persona identifiers
use geometric dot-matrix icons (triangle for Traditionalist, star for Innovator,
circle for Skeptic, diamond for Synthesizer), argument cards use the elevated
surface background, citation numbers glow gold on hover, and round transitions
use a subtle scan-line animation. The overall feeling is a research seminar
rendered in Arivu's visual language.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
User activates Debate Mode from the Mode Selector dropdown (Feature #049) or
types "/debate" in the Athena input. A topic prompt appears: "What research
question should the panel debate?" The mode remains active until the user
switches modes or closes the debate.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User selects "Debate Mode" from the mode selector. The Athena panel
   transitions: the normal chat input is replaced by a wider topic input
   with a placeholder "Enter a research question for the panel to debate..."
   and a "Begin Debate" button. Below the input, a persona selection panel
   shows 5 personas with checkboxes (minimum 3 required). Each persona has
   a dot-matrix geometric icon and a one-line description derived from the
   current graph.

2. User types: "Is attention more fundamental than convolution in visual
   recognition?" and selects Traditionalist, Innovator, Skeptic, and
   Synthesizer (4 personas). Clicks "Begin Debate." The panel transitions
   to debate layout: a vertical timeline on the left showing round progress,
   and argument cards on the right.

3. Round 1 -- Opening Statements. Each persona generates a 100-150 word
   opening position. The Traditionalist card appears first: "Convolution
   has 89 direct descendants[1] with PageRank dominance of 0.34[2] across
   the detection cluster. Visual recognition was BUILT on convolution."
   Cards appear with a 2-second stagger between personas. Each card has
   the persona's geometric icon, name, and position color.

4. Round 2 -- Challenges. The system identifies the strongest opposing
   claims (by citation count and metric magnitude). The Innovator challenges
   the Traditionalist: "Those 89 descendants include 23 generalization
   mutations[3] that already transcend pure convolution. The trend shows
   attention mechanisms absorbing convolutional functions[4]." Challenge
   cards connect to the original claim with a thin colored line.

5. Round 3 -- Rebuttals. Each persona responds to their challenger. The
   Skeptic interjects against both: "Neither position accounts for the 7
   contradiction edges[5] between hybrid attention-convolution papers. The
   evidence for a clean transition is weaker than either side claims." The
   Synthesizer draws connections: "Both convolution and attention contribute
   to a hybrid paradigm visible in the cross-cluster bridge papers[6]."

6. Round 4 -- Closing. Each persona issues a closing statement acknowledging
   concessions. The Traditionalist: "I concede that generalization mutations
   erode pure convolution's dominance, but the foundational adoption chains
   remain intact." A "Debate Summary" card appears at the bottom synthesizing
   the key points of agreement and disagreement with full citation counts.

7. User can click "Extend Debate" to add a Round 5 with a new sub-question,
   or click any persona to ask a direct question. The debate history is
   scrollable and all citations remain interactive.

AFTER COMPLETION:
The debate transcript is saved to conversation history with full citation
metadata. It can be exported as Markdown or PDF. The debate summary is
available as a session artifact. Switching away from Debate Mode preserves
the transcript for later review.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena/athena-debate.js: DebateModeController class. Manages
  persona selection, round progression, argument card rendering, challenge
  identification, and user intervention handling. ~600 lines.
- static/js/athena/athena-debate-personas.js: PersonaFactory class. Constructs
  personas from graph data (paper clusters, mutation distributions, PageRank
  scores). ~250 lines.
- static/css/athena-debate.css: Debate panel layout, persona cards, round
  timeline, argument cards, challenge connection lines. ~200 lines.
- app.py (POST /api/athena/debate/round): Receives round state, persona
  definitions, and topic. Returns generated arguments for all active personas.
- backend/athena_debate_engine.py: DebateEngine class. Builds per-persona
  prompts with graph evidence, manages round state, identifies strongest
  claims for challenge pairing. ~400 lines.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
DebateModeController initializes when mode selector dispatches
athena:mode-change with mode="debate". It renders the topic input UI and
persona selection panel. PersonaFactory.buildFromGraph(graphData) creates
persona definitions based on actual graph structure: oldest high-impact cluster
becomes Traditionalist territory, newest cluster becomes Innovator territory,
contradiction edges define Skeptic territory, cross-cluster bridges define
Synthesizer territory.

On "Begin Debate," the controller sends the topic and persona definitions to
the backend. Each round is a separate API call. Arguments are rendered as
cards with staggered animation. Challenge connections use SVG lines drawn
between related argument cards. The round timeline on the left updates with
each completed round.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
DebateEngine receives the topic, active personas, round number, and previous
round arguments. For each persona, it builds a custom system prompt:
"You are the {persona_name} in a research debate. Your territory is papers
from the {cluster_name} cluster. Argue from the perspective of: {persona_desc}.
Use ONLY these papers as evidence: {paper_list_with_metrics}."

Each persona prompt includes a RESTRICTED paper list from their assigned
cluster, forcing genuine multi-perspective analysis rather than all personas
citing the same papers. The engine uses Groq smart model for argument
generation (narrative quality) and fast model for challenge identification
(quick classification of claim strength).

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class DebateModeController {
    constructor(engine, graphData) {
        this.engine = engine;
        this.graphData = graphData;
        this.personas = [];
        this.rounds = [];
        this.currentRound = 0;
        this.maxRounds = 4;
    }

    async beginDebate(topic, selectedPersonas) {
        this.topic = topic;
        this.personas = selectedPersonas;
        this.renderDebateLayout();
        await this.executeRound('opening');
    }

    async executeRound(roundType) {
        this.currentRound++;
        this.updateTimeline(this.currentRound, 'active');
        const prevArgs = this.rounds[this.rounds.length - 1] || [];
        const response = await fetch('/api/athena/debate/round', {
            method: 'POST',
            headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
            body: JSON.stringify({
                topic: this.topic,
                round_number: this.currentRound,
                round_type: roundType,
                personas: this.personas.map(p => p.toJSON()),
                previous_arguments: prevArgs,
                graph_context: this.engine.context.getStaticContext()
            })
        });
        const data = await response.json();
        this.rounds.push(data.arguments);
        this.renderRoundArguments(data.arguments, roundType);
        this.updateTimeline(this.currentRound, 'complete');
    }
}
```

```python
class DebateEngine:
    ROUND_TYPES = ['opening', 'challenge', 'rebuttal', 'closing']

    def generate_round(self, topic, round_number, round_type,
                       personas, previous_arguments, graph_context):
        arguments = []
        for persona in personas:
            prompt = self._build_persona_prompt(
                persona, topic, round_type,
                previous_arguments, graph_context
            )
            response = self.llm_client.generate(
                model="groq_smart",
                system=prompt['system'],
                user=prompt['user'],
                max_tokens=500
            )
            arguments.append({
                'persona_id': persona['id'],
                'persona_name': persona['name'],
                'content': response,
                'citations': self._extract_citations(response),
                'claim_strength': self._score_claims(response, graph_context)
            })
        if round_type == 'challenge':
            arguments = self._pair_challenges(arguments, previous_arguments)
        return {'round': round_number, 'type': round_type,
                'arguments': arguments}

    def _build_persona_prompt(self, persona, topic, round_type,
                              prev_args, graph_ctx):
        territory = self._get_persona_territory(persona, graph_ctx)
        system = (
            f"You are {persona['name']}, a {persona['archetype']} "
            f"in an academic debate. Your evidence comes ONLY from "
            f"these papers:\n{territory}\n\n"
            f"Round type: {round_type}. "
            f"Argue with citations [cite:paper_id:metric]."
        )
        user = f"TOPIC: {topic}"
        if prev_args:
            user += f"\n\nPREVIOUS ARGUMENTS:\n{self._format_prev(prev_args)}"
        return {'system': system, 'user': user}
```

API CONTRACTS:
```
POST /api/athena/debate/round
Request: {
    "topic": "Is attention more fundamental than convolution?",
    "round_number": 1,
    "round_type": "opening",
    "personas": [
        {"id": "traditionalist", "name": "Traditionalist",
         "archetype": "defender of established methods",
         "cluster_territory": "early_convolution"},
        {"id": "innovator", "name": "Innovator",
         "archetype": "champion of emerging methods",
         "cluster_territory": "attention_mechanisms"}
    ],
    "previous_arguments": [],
    "graph_context": {
        "totalPapers": 601, "totalEdges": 968,
        "clusters": [...], "bottlenecks": [...]
    }
}
Response: {
    "round": 1,
    "type": "opening",
    "arguments": [
        {
            "persona_id": "traditionalist",
            "persona_name": "Traditionalist",
            "content": "Convolution has 89 direct descendants[cite:abc:adoption_count]...",
            "citations": [{"paper_id": "abc...", "metric": "adoption_count"}],
            "claim_strength": 0.82
        },
        {
            "persona_id": "innovator",
            "persona_name": "Innovator",
            "content": "Attention mechanisms show rapid generalization[cite:def:mutation]...",
            "citations": [{"paper_id": "def...", "metric": "mutation"}],
            "claim_strength": 0.78
        }
    ],
    "challenge_pairs": null
}
```

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Topic Input (debate not yet started)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  DEBATE MODE                                  [Exit Debate]   |
|                                                               |
|  ┌─ RESEARCH QUESTION ──────────────────────────────────────────┐  |
|  │  Enter a research question for the panel to debate:     │  |
|  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │  |
|  │  │ Is attention more fundamental than convolution?  │   │  |
|  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │  |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  |
|                                                               |
|  ┌─ SELECT PERSONAS (min 3) ──────────────────────────────────────┐  |
|  │  [x] /\ Traditionalist -- early convolution cluster     │  |
|  │  [x] *  Innovator -- attention mechanisms cluster       │  |
|  │  [x] O  Skeptic -- contradiction edges (7 found)        │  |
|  │  [x] <> Synthesizer -- cross-cluster bridges            │  |
|  │  [ ] || Methodologist -- methodological adoption chains │  |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  |
|                                                               |
|  [BEGIN DEBATE]                                               |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
```
Persona icons: /\ = triangle, * = star, O = circle, <> = diamond,
|| = parallel lines. All rendered as dot-matrix SVG, not emoji.

STATE: Debate in Progress (Round 2 -- Challenges)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  DEBATE: Is attention more fundamental than convolution?      |
|                                                    [Extend]   |
|  ROUND     ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  |
|  ● 1 Open  │ /\ TRADITIONALIST                            │  |
|  ◐ 2 Chal  │ Convolution has 89 direct descendants 1 with │  |
|  ○ 3 Rebut │ PageRank dominance of 0.34 2 across the      │  |
|  ○ 4 Close │ detection cluster. Visual recognition was     │  |
|             │ BUILT on convolution.                         │  |
|             └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  |
|                        │                                      |
|                  challenge line                                |
|                        │                                      |
|             ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  |
|             │ *  INNOVATOR [challenges Traditionalist]      │  |
|             │ Those 89 descendants include 23 generalizat- │  |
|             │ ion mutations 3 that transcend pure convol-   │  |
|             │ ution. Attention absorbs convolutional func-  │  |
|             │ tions 4.                                      │  |
|             └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
```
● = completed round, ◐ = current round, ○ = pending round.
Challenge lines: 1px dashed, color matches challenger persona.

STATE: Debate Summary (after final round)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  DEBATE SUMMARY                         [Export] [New Topic]  |
|                                                               |
|  ┌─ CONSENSUS ─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ |
|  │  Both sides acknowledge the emergence of hybrid          │ |
|  │  attention-convolution architectures. 5 bridge papers    │ |
|  │  identified across clusters.                              │ |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ |
|  ┌─ UNRESOLVED ────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ |
|  │  ● Methodological validity of attention-only models      │ |
|  │  ● 7 contradiction edges remain unexplained              │ |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ |
|  ┌─ CITED PAPERS (12 unique) ────────────────────────────────────┐ |
|  │  [VGGNet 2014] [AlexNet 2012] [ViT 2021] [ResNet 2016] │ |
|  │  [DETR 2020] [ConvNeXt 2022] ...                        │ |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
```

TRANSITIONS:
Normal -> Topic Input: mode selector triggers panel morph, 400ms ease-out.
Topic Input -> Round 1: topic input fades out, debate layout fades in, 350ms.
Between rounds: timeline dot fills with scan-line animation, 200ms per dot.
Argument cards: stagger in with 2000ms delay between personas, each card
slides right and fades in over 300ms.
Challenge lines: draw from top card to bottom card, 500ms, SVG stroke-dasharray
animation.
Debate -> Summary: all argument cards slide up, summary card fades in, 400ms.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User selects Debate Mode and enters topic
    |
PersonaFactory.buildFromGraph(graphData) [athena-debate-personas.js]
    |--- Analyzes clusters: oldest high-PageRank = Traditionalist territory
    |--- Newest cluster = Innovator territory
    |--- Contradiction edges = Skeptic territory
    |--- Cross-cluster bridges = Synthesizer territory
    |--- Methodological adoptions = Methodologist territory
    |
User selects personas and clicks "Begin Debate"
    |
DebateModeController.beginDebate(topic, personas) [athena-debate.js]
    |--- Renders debate layout (timeline + argument area)
    |--- Calls executeRound('opening')
    |
POST /api/athena/debate/round [app.py]
    |--- DebateEngine.generate_round() [backend/athena_debate_engine.py]
    |--- For each persona: builds restricted-territory prompt
    |--- LLM call: Groq smart model per persona (sequential, not parallel)
    |--- Extracts citations, scores claim strength
    |--- Returns all arguments for the round
    |
Frontend renders argument cards with stagger animation
    |
Repeat for rounds 2-4 (challenge, rebuttal, closing)
    |--- Challenge round: _pair_challenges() matches strongest opposing claims
    |--- Rebuttal round: each persona responds to their specific challenge
    |--- Closing round: personas summarize, acknowledge concessions
    |
After Round 4: DebateEngine generates summary
    |--- Consensus points, unresolved tensions, cited papers list
    |
All arguments stored in chat_history with debate metadata

ERROR PATH:
LLM fails for one persona in a round
    |--- Other personas' arguments still rendered
    |--- Failed persona shows: "{Name} could not formulate a response."
    |--- Next round proceeds without the failed persona's argument

Topic too vague for structured debate
    |--- DebateEngine returns a suggestion: "Try narrowing the topic to..."
    |--- Frontend shows suggestion card above topic input

Graph has fewer than 3 clusters (cannot differentiate personas)
    |--- PersonaFactory falls back to archetype-only personas (no territory)
    |--- Debate proceeds but arguments are less data-specific
    |--- Warning: "Limited graph structure. Debate may be less specific."

DATA STORED:
Full debate transcript stored in chat_history table as a special message type
"debate_transcript" with JSONB containing all rounds, persona definitions,
topic, citations, and generated summary.

DATA CACHED:
Persona definitions cached per graph load (PersonaFactory output). Debate
rounds are not cached individually. Summary cached with transcript.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: Yes (multiple LLM calls per round, one per persona)

MODEL: Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile (smart model) for argument generation,
       Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant (fast model) for challenge pairing
STAGE: Multi-call generation (one per persona per round)

PROMPT TEMPLATE (per persona per round):
```
You are {persona_name}, a {persona_archetype} participating in an academic
debate about research lineages.

YOUR TERRITORY (papers you can cite as primary evidence):
{restricted_paper_list with titles, years, PageRank, pruning_impact,
 mutation_types for each paper}

DEBATE RULES:
1. Argue ONLY from your territory papers using [cite:paper_id:metric]
2. You may reference opponents' cited papers to challenge their claims
3. Be specific: use exact numbers from metrics, not vague language
4. Acknowledge strong opposing arguments -- do not strawman
5. Maximum 150 words per round

ROUND TYPE: {round_type}
{if challenge: "Challenge the strongest claim from: {opponent_argument}"}
{if rebuttal: "Respond to this challenge: {challenge_text}"}
{if closing: "Summarize your final position. Acknowledge concessions."}

TOPIC: {topic}

{if previous_arguments:}
PREVIOUS ROUND ARGUMENTS:
{formatted previous arguments with persona labels}
```

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Plain text with embedded [cite:paper_id:metric] markers. Maximum 150 words.

FALLBACK IF LLM FAILS:
Per-persona fallback: "This participant could not formulate a response for
this round." Debate continues with remaining personas.
Total LLM failure: "Debate could not proceed. Try again or switch to a
different mode." Preserves any completed rounds.

TOKEN BUDGET:
Per persona per round: ~400 input tokens (prompt + territory), ~200 output
Total per round (4 personas): ~2400 tokens input, ~800 output
Full 4-round debate: ~12800 tokens input, ~3200 output
Challenge pairing (fast model): ~300 tokens input, ~50 output
Well within Groq 128K context limits per call.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element               | Action       | Response                                          |
|-----------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Begin Debate button   | Click        | Topic validated, debate layout rendered, Round 1   |
| Persona checkbox      | Click        | Toggles persona selection (min 3 enforced)         |
| Argument card         | Click        | Expands card to show full text with citations      |
| Citation in argument  | Hover/Click  | Same as Feature #051 (tooltip, graph navigation)   |
| Timeline round dot    | Click        | Scrolls to that round's arguments                  |
| Extend Debate button  | Click        | Prompts for sub-question, adds Round 5             |
| Persona icon          | Click        | Opens direct question input for that persona       |
| Exit Debate button    | Click        | Returns to normal Athena chat, preserves transcript|
| Export button          | Click        | Exports full transcript as Markdown or PDF         |
| New Topic button      | Click        | Clears debate, returns to topic input              |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Topic input to debate layout: cross-fade, 350ms ease-out
- Argument card stagger: 2000ms between personas, each card 300ms slide-right
- Challenge line draw: SVG stroke-dasharray, 500ms linear
- Timeline dot fill: radial fill animation (scan-line), 200ms per dot
- Round transition: 500ms pause, then next round cards begin staggering
- Summary card: fade-in from opacity 0, 400ms ease-out

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Debate panel: role="region", aria-label="Research debate"
- Argument cards: role="article", aria-label="{Persona}: {round_type}"
- Timeline: role="navigation", aria-label="Debate rounds"
- Persona checkboxes: standard checkbox accessibility, label linked
- All citations: inherit Feature #051 accessibility patterns
- Screen reader: announces new round start, each persona argument
- Keyboard: Tab between cards, Enter to expand, Escape to exit debate

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

EDGE CASE 1: User starts debate on graph with 2 clusters only
Scenario: Graph has only 2 identifiable clusters, but user selects 4 personas.
Expected behavior: Traditionalist and Innovator get distinct clusters. Skeptic
and Synthesizer operate on cross-cutting data (contradiction edges, bridge
papers) rather than exclusive clusters.
Implementation: PersonaFactory handles < 5 clusters by sharing territory
intelligently. Skeptic always uses contradiction edges regardless of cluster
count. Synthesizer uses all cross-cluster edges.

EDGE CASE 2: LLM generates argument without any citations
Scenario: Persona argues but includes no [cite:...] markers.
Expected behavior: Argument renders with a "No citations provided" warning
badge. Claim strength scored as 0.0. Other personas still cite normally.
Implementation: _extract_citations() returns empty array. UI renders warning
icon (dot-matrix exclamation, not emoji) next to persona name.

EDGE CASE 3: User extends debate beyond 4 rounds repeatedly
Scenario: User clicks "Extend Debate" 5 times, reaching Round 9.
Expected behavior: Maximum 8 rounds enforced. After Round 8, "Extend Debate"
button disabled with tooltip "Maximum rounds reached."
Implementation: DebateModeController.maxRounds = 8 (configurable). Button
disabled state added when currentRound >= maxRounds.

EDGE CASE 4: User asks a direct question to a persona mid-debate
Scenario: Between Round 2 and Round 3, user clicks the Skeptic icon and types
"What about the reproducibility crisis?"
Expected behavior: Skeptic generates a targeted response using their territory
papers. This is inserted as a "sidebar" between rounds, not replacing Round 3.
Implementation: Direct questions rendered as indented sidebar cards. Round
progression continues independently. Sidebar arguments not included in
challenge pairing for subsequent rounds.

EDGE CASE 5: All personas reach the same conclusion
Scenario: Debate topic has clear consensus -- all personas agree.
Expected behavior: Summary acknowledges consensus. "All participants agree
that {topic resolution}. No significant disagreements remain." Debate summary
flags this as a strong consensus.
Implementation: DebateEngine checks if closing arguments have < 2 distinct
positions. If so, summary template changes to consensus mode.

EDGE CASE 6: Very small graph (< 20 papers, < 3 clusters)
Scenario: Graph has 15 papers and no distinct clusters.
Expected behavior: PersonaFactory warns "Limited graph structure" and creates
generalized personas without territory restrictions. Debate proceeds but
arguments may overlap in paper references.
Implementation: If clusters.length < 2, all personas share the full paper
list. Debate still functions but quality disclaimer shown.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON (must be built first):
- Feature #049 (Mode Selector): Debate Mode activated via mode selector
  infrastructure. Mode selector manages panel transitions.
- Feature #001 (Context-Aware Responses): Persona territory assignment uses
  static context (clusters, bottlenecks, mutation distributions).
- Feature #037 (Streaming Text): Argument cards use streaming for real-time
  rendering of persona responses.
- Feature #002 (Rich Response Formatting): Arguments use markdown formatting,
  bold claims, and inline citations.
- Feature #051 (Inline Citations): Citation markers in arguments link to
  graph nodes using the citation renderer infrastructure.

ENABLES (can be built after this):
- Feature #113 (Devil's Advocate Mode): A simplified single-persona version
  of the debate framework. Can reuse DebateEngine with 2 personas.

ENHANCES (improves but does not require):
- Feature #035 (Peer Discussion Simulator): Debate Mode provides structured
  multi-round argumentation that Peer Discussion can reference.
- Feature #014 (Comparison Mode): Debate can be triggered from comparison
  results to argue about relative importance.

CONFLICTS WITH:
- Feature #049 (Mode Selector): Debate Mode must register as a mode. Mode
  selector must support the unique debate panel layout (not standard chat).
  Resolution: Debate Mode provides its own panel content, mode selector
  provides the activation trigger and mode switching infrastructure.

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES:
- Groq API (groq==0.8.0): Smart model for argument generation, fast model
  for challenge pairing. Falls back to single-model if fast unavailable.
- Graph clustering data: Requires DNA profiling clusters from graph engine.
  If clusters unavailable, personas use generalized archetypes.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
[] PersonaFactory builds personas from graph clusters correctly
[] Minimum 3 personas enforced in selection UI
[] All 4 standard rounds execute with per-persona arguments
[] Challenge pairing connects strongest opposing claims
[] Inline citations in arguments link to graph nodes
[] Debate summary generated after final round with consensus/unresolved
[] Direct persona questions work as sidebar between rounds
[] Extend Debate adds rounds up to maximum of 8
[] Full transcript exportable as Markdown and PDF

VISUAL:
[] Persona icons are dot-matrix geometric shapes (not emoji, not photos)
[] Argument cards use elevated surface background with persona color coding
[] Challenge lines drawn as SVG between related argument cards
[] Timeline dots use filled/half-filled/empty states correctly

PERFORMANCE:
[] Single round generates in < 8 seconds (4 personas sequential)
[] Argument card stagger animation runs at 60fps
[] Full 4-round debate completes in < 35 seconds total

ACCESSIBILITY:
[] Argument cards have role="article" with persona labels
[] Timeline navigable via keyboard (Tab + Enter)
[] Screen reader announces new rounds and persona arguments
[] All citations inherit Feature #051 accessibility

EDGE CASES:
[] Handles graph with < 3 clusters (generalized personas)
[] Handles LLM failure for one persona (others continue)
[] Maximum 8 rounds enforced with disabled extend button

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed -- all findings addressed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed -- all findings addressed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round -- agent confirms ZERO remaining issues
[] BROWSER TEST: Feature verified in Chrome via browsermcp
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Implementation matches this spec document exactly

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-debate.js (DebateModeController), athena-debate-personas.js
   (PersonaFactory), athena-debate.css, backend/athena_debate_engine.py
   (DebateEngine), app.py (POST /api/athena/debate/round)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes above
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Start debate with 4 personas on a 601-paper graph, verify all 4 rounds
   b. Verify persona territories match actual graph clusters
   c. Click citation in argument card, verify graph navigation
   d. Click persona icon mid-debate, verify sidebar question works
   e. Extend debate to Round 5, verify new arguments generated
   f. Attempt to extend past Round 8, verify button disabled
   g. Start debate on small graph (< 20 papers), verify warning shown
   h. Export debate transcript as Markdown, verify all rounds included
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. PersonaFactory fails on graph with 0 clusters (division by zero)
   b. Challenge pairing crashes when all claims have equal strength
   c. Persona prompt exceeds token limit with large territory paper list
   d. Concurrent debate rounds if user clicks too fast
5. VERIFY no regression in: #049 (Mode Selector), #051 (Citations), #037
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FEATURE #121: AUTO-COMPLETE SUGGESTIONS
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CATEGORY:        Proactive AI
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           F
ESTIMATED HOURS: 12
OVERLAPS WITH:   #009 (Proactive Suggestions), #042 (Suggested Follow-Ups),
                 #003 (Quick Action Buttons)
BLOCKS:          None
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #037 (Streaming Text),
                 #006 (Conversation Memory)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DESCRIPTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Auto-Complete Suggestions is the real-time intelligence layer that predicts
what the researcher wants to ask BEFORE they finish typing. As the user types
in the Athena input area, the system generates context-aware completion
suggestions that appear in a dropdown below the input. These are not generic
text completions like a search engine -- they are research-specific suggestions
informed by the current citation graph, conversation history, recent graph
interactions, and the semantic content of the partially typed query.

When a user types "What is the impact of" in the ResNet lineage, the
auto-complete dropdown shows: "What is the impact of removing VGGNet?" (based
on highest-impact bottleneck), "What is the impact of the BatchNorm
contradiction?" (based on recent contradiction filter), "What is the impact
of adoption chains in the detection cluster?" (based on dominant mutation
type in the largest cluster). Each suggestion is a complete, meaningful
research question, not a word-level completion.

This feature exists because research exploration is cognitively demanding.
Researchers often know the DIRECTION they want to explore but struggle to
formulate the exact question. Auto-Complete bridges this gap by offering
concrete questions that reference specific papers, clusters, mutation types,
and computed metrics from the graph. It transforms "I want to know about..."
into "What is the pruning impact of the 1996 foundational paper on the
detection methods cluster?" -- a question the user might not have known to
ask but immediately recognizes as valuable.

The system operates on three levels of suggestion source: (1) Template-based
completions derived from graph structure (bottlenecks, clusters, contradictions,
orphans) that require no LLM call. (2) History-based completions derived from
conversation patterns (if the user asked about VGGNet, suggest follow-ups
about VGGNet's descendants). (3) Semantic completions from a lightweight LLM
call using the fast model when the partially typed query is sufficiently
specific (> 15 characters) and template matches are insufficient.

The visual presentation follows the dot matrix aesthetic: the dropdown uses
the elevated surface background, suggestions are rendered in Inter with paper
names in JetBrains Mono gold, the currently selected suggestion is highlighted
with a subtle left border accent. The dropdown appears after a debounce delay
of 300ms, disappears when the input is empty or the user sends the message,
and navigates with arrow keys and Tab for selection.

Performance is critical: auto-complete must feel instantaneous. Template-based
suggestions are precomputed on graph load and filtered client-side (< 5ms).
History-based suggestions are computed from in-memory conversation state
(< 10ms). LLM-based suggestions use the fast model with a 2-second timeout
and cancel-on-keystroke to prevent stale suggestions from appearing. If the
LLM is slow, template and history suggestions appear immediately while the
LLM suggestion loads in the background.

The suggestion pipeline respects the input context: typing a "/" triggers
command completions (Feature #005 graph-aware commands), typing "@" triggers
paper name completions, and natural language triggers question completions.
These prefix triggers are hardcoded and take priority over the general
suggestion engine.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRIGGER:
Automatic. Suggestions appear as the user types in the Athena input area after
300ms of inactivity (debounce). No explicit activation needed. Suggestions
disappear when input is cleared or message is sent.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User starts typing in the Athena input: "What is the" -- after 300ms of
   no further typing, a dropdown appears below the input with 3-5 suggestions.
   Template-based suggestions load first (< 5ms): "What is the most important
   paper?", "What is the impact of removing [VGGNet]?", "What is the oldest
   paper in this lineage?" Paper names appear in gold JetBrains Mono.

2. User continues typing: "What is the relationship between" -- the dropdown
   updates. New suggestions appear: "What is the relationship between VGGNet
   and AlexNet?", "What is the relationship between the detection and
   recognition clusters?", "What is the relationship between adoption and
   generalization mutations?" Each suggestion reflects actual graph structure.

3. User presses the down arrow key to highlight the second suggestion. The
   highlighted suggestion shows a subtle gold left border. Pressing Enter
   or Tab fills the input with the selected suggestion text. The dropdown
   closes and the user can press Enter again to send.

4. User instead keeps typing: "What is the relationship between conv and"
   -- the system detects a potential paper reference ("conv") and shows
   paper-name completions: "What is the relationship between [ConvNeXt (2022)]
   and [VGGNet (2014)]?", "What is the relationship between convolution and
   attention mechanisms?" The "@" prefix is not needed for inline paper
   detection.

5. User selects the ConvNeXt suggestion by clicking it. The full text fills
   the input. User presses Enter to send. The suggestion click is logged in
   the action log for session summary tracking.

6. In the next interaction, user starts typing "Tell me" -- history-based
   suggestions appear: "Tell me more about ConvNeXt" (references the paper
   just discussed), "Tell me about contradictions in this lineage" (common
   follow-up pattern). These are ranked by relevance to recent conversation.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Auto-complete is persistent throughout the session. Suggestion quality improves
as more conversation context accumulates. Suggestion selections are tracked in
the action log for session summary and research pattern analysis.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FILES:
- static/js/athena/athena-autocomplete.js: AutoCompleteEngine class. Manages
  debounce timing, suggestion pipeline (template -> history -> LLM), dropdown
  rendering, keyboard navigation, and suggestion selection. ~450 lines.
- static/js/athena/athena-autocomplete-templates.js: TemplateSuggestionBuilder
  class. Precomputes suggestion templates from graph data on load. ~200 lines.
- static/css/athena-autocomplete.css: Dropdown panel, suggestion items,
  highlight states, paper name styling. ~100 lines.
- app.py (POST /api/athena/autocomplete): Receives partial query and context,
  returns LLM-generated suggestions. Only called when template and history
  suggestions are insufficient.
- backend/athena_autocomplete.py: AutoCompleteBackend class. Generates
  semantic suggestions using Groq fast model. ~150 lines.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:
AutoCompleteEngine attaches to the Athena input textarea. On 'input' event,
it starts a 300ms debounce timer. When the timer fires, it runs the suggestion
pipeline:

Phase 1 (immediate, < 5ms): TemplateSuggestionBuilder.match(partialQuery)
filters precomputed templates against the typed text using prefix matching
and keyword intersection. Returns 0-5 template suggestions.

Phase 2 (immediate, < 10ms): HistorySuggestionBuilder.match(partialQuery,
conversationHistory) finds relevant follow-ups based on recent topics and
paper references. Returns 0-3 history suggestions.

Phase 3 (async, 2s timeout): If Phase 1+2 produce < 3 suggestions AND
partialQuery.length > 15, calls POST /api/athena/autocomplete for LLM-
generated suggestions. Request is cancelled on next keystroke via
AbortController. Returns 0-3 semantic suggestions.

Results are merged, deduplicated, and ranked: template suggestions first
(highest confidence), history suggestions second, LLM suggestions third.
Maximum 5 suggestions displayed.

The dropdown is a positioned div below the input. Keyboard navigation: ArrowUp/
ArrowDown to move highlight, Tab/Enter to accept, Escape to dismiss. Mouse
click on any suggestion selects it.

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
AutoCompleteBackend receives the partial query, static context summary, and
recent conversation topics. It builds a compact prompt for Groq fast model:
"Given this partial query and research context, suggest 3 complete questions
the researcher might be asking." Response parsed as a JSON array of strings.
Hard timeout of 2 seconds; if exceeded, returns empty array.

KEY CODE PATTERNS:
```javascript
class AutoCompleteEngine {
    constructor(inputEl, engine) {
        this.input = inputEl;
        this.engine = engine;
        this.dropdown = null;
        this.debounceTimer = null;
        this.debounceMs = 300;
        this.abortController = null;
        this.selectedIndex = -1;
        this.suggestions = [];
        this.templateBuilder = new TemplateSuggestionBuilder();
        this.historyBuilder = new HistorySuggestionBuilder();
        this.input.addEventListener('input', () => this._onInput());
        this.input.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => this._onKeydown(e));
        document.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
            if (!this.dropdown?.contains(e.target)
                && e.target !== this.input) {
                this.hide();
            }
        });
    }

    _onInput() {
        clearTimeout(this.debounceTimer);
        if (this.abortController) this.abortController.abort();
        const query = this.input.value.trim();
        if (query.length < 3) { this.hide(); return; }
        this.debounceTimer = setTimeout(
            () => this._generateSuggestions(query), this.debounceMs
        );
    }

    async _generateSuggestions(query) {
        const templates = this.templateBuilder.match(query);
        const history = this.historyBuilder.match(
            query, this.engine.getConversationHistory()
        );
        let suggestions = [...templates, ...history];
        if (suggestions.length < 3 && query.length > 15) {
            this.abortController = new AbortController();
            try {
                const llm = await this._fetchLLMSuggestions(
                    query, this.abortController.signal
                );
                suggestions = [...suggestions, ...llm];
            } catch (e) {
                if (e.name !== 'AbortError') console.warn(e);
            }
        }
        this.suggestions = this._dedup(suggestions).slice(0, 5);
        this.selectedIndex = -1;
        this.render();
    }

    _onKeydown(e) {
        if (!this.dropdown || this.suggestions.length === 0) return;
        if (e.key === 'ArrowDown') {
            e.preventDefault();
            this.selectedIndex = Math.min(
                this.selectedIndex + 1, this.suggestions.length - 1
            );
            this._updateHighlight();
        } else if (e.key === 'ArrowUp') {
            e.preventDefault();
            this.selectedIndex = Math.max(this.selectedIndex - 1, -1);
            this._updateHighlight();
        } else if ((e.key === 'Tab' || e.key === 'Enter')
                   && this.selectedIndex >= 0) {
            e.preventDefault();
            this._selectSuggestion(this.selectedIndex);
        } else if (e.key === 'Escape') {
            this.hide();
        }
    }
}
```

```python
class AutoCompleteBackend:
    TIMEOUT_S = 2

    def suggest(self, partial_query, static_context, recent_topics):
        prompt = self._build_prompt(
            partial_query, static_context, recent_topics
        )
        try:
            response = self.llm_client.generate(
                model="groq_fast",
                system="You suggest research questions for a citation graph tool.",
                user=prompt,
                max_tokens=200,
                timeout=self.TIMEOUT_S
            )
            return self._parse_suggestions(response)
        except Exception:
            return []

    def _build_prompt(self, partial_query, context, topics):
        return (
            f"The user is typing: \"{partial_query}\"\n"
            f"Graph: {context.get('seedPaper',{}).get('title','?')} "
            f"({context.get('totalPapers',0)} papers)\n"
            f"Recent topics: {', '.join(topics[:5])}\n"
            f"Bottlenecks: {', '.join(b['title'] for b in context.get('bottlenecks',[])[:3])}\n\n"
            f"Suggest exactly 3 complete research questions the user "
            f"might be typing. Return as JSON array of strings."
        )
```

API CONTRACTS:
```
POST /api/athena/autocomplete
Request: {
    "partial_query": "What is the relationship between conv",
    "static_context": {
        "totalPapers": 601,
        "seedPaper": {"title": "Deep Residual Learning...", "year": 2016},
        "bottlenecks": [{"title": "VGGNet", "paper_id": "abd1c..."}],
        "clusters": [{"name": "detection_methods", "count": 42}]
    },
    "recent_topics": ["VGGNet", "pruning impact", "adoption chains"]
}
Response: {
    "suggestions": [
        "What is the relationship between ConvNeXt and attention mechanisms?",
        "What is the relationship between convolution and the detection cluster?",
        "What is the relationship between convolutional adoption chains?"
    ]
}
```

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE: Dropdown Visible (suggestions showing)
```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  ATHENA                                                       |
|                                                               |
|  (conversation messages above...)                             |
|                                                               |
|  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    |
|  │ What is the impact of                            [->│    |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    |
|  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    |
|  │  ○ What is the impact of removing [VGGNet]?          │    |
|  │  ● What is the impact of the BatchNorm contradiction?│    |
|  │  ○ What is the impact of adoption chains in the      │    |
|  │    detection cluster?                                │    |
|  │  ○ What is the impact of the 1996 foundational paper?│    |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```
● = currently highlighted suggestion (arrow key selected), gold left border.
○ = unselected suggestion. Paper names in [brackets] rendered in gold
JetBrains Mono. Suggestion text in Inter 14px.

STATE: LLM Suggestions Loading (templates shown, LLM pending)
```
|  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    |
|  │  ○ What is the impact of removing [VGGNet]?          │    |
|  │  ○ What is the impact of the BatchNorm contradiction?│    |
|  │  ◐ Loading more suggestions...                       │    |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    |
```
◐ = half-filled dot matrix circle indicating async loading. Disappears
when LLM suggestions arrive or timeout occurs.

STATE: No Suggestions Available
```
|  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    |
|  │ xyzzy foobar blah                                [->│    |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    |
|  (no dropdown shown -- input does not match any templates)   |
```
If zero suggestions match, dropdown is not rendered at all.

STATE: Command Prefix Detected
```
|  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    |
|  │ /com                                             [->│    |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    |
|  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    |
|  │  /compare [paper1] [paper2]                          │    |
|  │  /commands -- list all available commands             │    |
|  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    |
```
"/" prefix triggers command completions from Feature #005 command registry.

TRANSITIONS:
Hidden -> Visible: opacity 0 to 1, translateY(-4px) to translateY(0), 150ms.
Visible -> Hidden: opacity 1 to 0, 100ms.
Highlight move: background-color transition, 100ms.
Suggestion selected: dropdown hides immediately, input populated.
LLM loading indicator: pulse animation on ◐ dot, 800ms infinite.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA FLOW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

User types in Athena input
    |
AutoCompleteEngine._onInput() [athena-autocomplete.js]
    |--- Clears previous debounce timer
    |--- Aborts any in-flight LLM request
    |--- Starts 300ms debounce timer
    |
After 300ms of no typing:
    |
Phase 1: TemplateSuggestionBuilder.match(query)
    |--- Filters precomputed templates by prefix + keyword match
    |--- Templates built from: bottleneck names, cluster names,
    |    mutation types, contradiction papers, orphan ideas
    |--- Returns 0-5 matches in < 5ms
    |
Phase 2: HistorySuggestionBuilder.match(query, history)
    |--- Scans conversation history for referenced papers and topics
    |--- Generates follow-up patterns: "Tell me more about {paper}",
    |    "Compare {paper1} and {paper2}", "What about {cluster}?"
    |--- Returns 0-3 matches in < 10ms
    |
Phase 3 (conditional): POST /api/athena/autocomplete
    |--- Only if Phase 1+2 < 3 suggestions AND query > 15 chars
    |--- AbortController attached for cancel-on-keystroke
    |--- AutoCompleteBackend.suggest() [backend/athena_autocomplete.py]
    |--- Groq fast model, 2-second timeout
    |--- Returns 0-3 semantic suggestions
    |
Merge, deduplicate, rank, display (max 5 suggestions)
    |
User selects suggestion (keyboard or click)
    |--- Input populated with full suggestion text
    |--- Dropdown hidden
    |--- Selection logged in action_log

ERROR PATH:
LLM request times out (> 2 seconds)
    |--- AbortController.abort() fires
    |--- Template + history suggestions remain visible
    |--- No error shown to user (graceful degradation)

LLM returns invalid JSON
    |--- _parse_suggestions() catches, returns empty array
    |--- Template + history suggestions remain visible

Graph not loaded (no static context)
    |--- TemplateSuggestionBuilder has empty templates
    |--- Only history-based and generic suggestions available
    |--- Dropdown still functional but less specific

DATA STORED:
Suggestion selections logged in action_log table: action_type="autocomplete",
action_data includes selected suggestion text and source (template/history/llm).
Templates are not persisted -- rebuilt on every graph load.

DATA CACHED:
Template suggestions precomputed on graph load, stored in memory. Invalidated
by arivu:graph-loaded event. LLM suggestions not cached (queries are unique).
History suggestions recomputed on every debounce trigger from in-memory state.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LLM INTEGRATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

REQUIRES LLM: Partially (LLM is Phase 3 only, template/history work without)

MODEL: Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant (fast model for low latency)
STAGE: Single-call generation with JSON output parsing

PROMPT TEMPLATE:
```
You suggest research questions for Arivu, a citation graph analysis tool.

The user is typing: "{partial_query}"
Graph: {seed_paper_title} ({total_papers} papers, {total_edges} edges)
Recent topics discussed: {recent_topics_list}
Key bottleneck papers: {bottleneck_names}
Identified clusters: {cluster_names}

Suggest exactly 3 complete research questions the user might be asking.
Questions must reference specific papers, clusters, or metrics from the graph.
Do not suggest generic questions unrelated to the graph data.
Return as a JSON array of 3 strings. Nothing else.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT:
JSON array of 3 strings: ["question 1", "question 2", "question 3"]
Parsed by _parse_suggestions() with JSON.parse or regex fallback.

FALLBACK IF LLM FAILS:
Template and history suggestions displayed without LLM augmentation. If both
also return 0 results, no dropdown shown. Never show an error for autocomplete
failure -- it is entirely invisible to the user.

TOKEN BUDGET:
Input: ~150-300 tokens (compact prompt with minimal context)
Output: ~50-100 tokens (3 short question strings)
Total: well within fast model limits
Timeout: 2 seconds hard cap via AbortController + server-side timeout

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

| Element               | Action       | Response                                          |
|-----------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Input textarea        | Type         | 300ms debounce, then suggestions appear            |
| Input textarea        | Clear        | Dropdown hidden immediately                        |
| Suggestion item       | Hover        | Subtle highlight (background lighten)              |
| Suggestion item       | Click        | Input populated, dropdown hidden                   |
| Arrow Down key        | Press        | Next suggestion highlighted                        |
| Arrow Up key          | Press        | Previous suggestion highlighted                    |
| Tab key               | Press        | Selected suggestion accepted (if highlighted)      |
| Enter key             | Press        | Selected suggestion accepted (if highlighted),     |
|                       |              | or message sent (if no highlight)                  |
| Escape key            | Press        | Dropdown hidden, focus stays in input              |
| "/" prefix            | Type         | Command completions shown instead of questions     |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Dropdown appear: translateY(-4px) to translateY(0), opacity fade-in, 150ms
- Dropdown disappear: opacity fade-out, 100ms
- Highlight move: background-color transition, 100ms ease
- Selection accept: dropdown hides immediately, no animation
- LLM loading dot: pulse animation (opacity 0.3 to 1), 800ms infinite
- Paper name gold color: applied via CSS class, no animation

SOUND EFFECTS:
NOT APPLICABLE

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Dropdown: role="listbox", aria-label="Autocomplete suggestions"
- Suggestion items: role="option", aria-selected for highlighted item
- Input: aria-expanded="true" when dropdown visible, aria-activedescendant
  points to highlighted suggestion ID
- Screen reader: announces suggestion count when dropdown appears
- Screen reader: announces highlighted suggestion text on arrow key
- Keyboard fully functional: Arrow keys, Tab, Enter, Escape all mapped
- No mouse required for full interaction

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EDGE CASES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

EDGE CASE 1: User types very fast (multiple keystrokes within 300ms)
Scenario: User types "What is the impact of VGGNet" in one burst.
Expected behavior: Only one suggestion generation fires (after final 300ms
debounce). Previous in-flight LLM requests cancelled by AbortController.
Implementation: Each keystroke clears the debounce timer and aborts any
pending request. Only the final stable state triggers suggestion generation.

EDGE CASE 2: User selects suggestion then modifies it
Scenario: User accepts "What is the impact of removing VGGNet?" then
backspaces to change "VGGNet" to "AlexNet".
Expected behavior: After 300ms of the edit, new suggestions appear based on
the modified text "What is the impact of removing AlexNet?"
Implementation: _onInput() fires on every change, including edits to accepted
suggestions. The pipeline runs again with the updated text.

EDGE CASE 3: Dropdown conflicts with mobile keyboard
Scenario: On mobile, the virtual keyboard covers the suggestion dropdown.
Expected behavior: Dropdown appears ABOVE the input instead of below when
viewport height is constrained (< 500px available below input).
Implementation: AutoCompleteEngine checks available space below input. If
insufficient, adds CSS class for above-input positioning.

EDGE CASE 4: LLM returns suggestions with papers not in graph
Scenario: Fast model hallucinates a paper name not in the current graph.
Expected behavior: Suggestion still shown (it is a question, not a verified
claim). If user sends it, Athena responds with context-aware answer.
Implementation: Suggestions are not validated against graph data. They are
question text, not assertions. Validation happens at response time.

EDGE CASE 5: "/" command prefix mixed with natural language
Scenario: User types "/compare VGGNet and" -- should trigger command
completion, not question completion.
Expected behavior: Command completions shown: "/compare [VGGNet] [AlexNet]",
"/compare [VGGNet] [ResNet]". Command prefix takes priority.
Implementation: _onInput() checks first character. If "/", delegates to
CommandCompletionEngine (Feature #005). If "@", delegates to paper name
completion. Otherwise, uses question suggestion pipeline.

EDGE CASE 6: Empty graph (no papers loaded yet)
Scenario: User opens Athena before a graph is loaded and starts typing.
Expected behavior: Generic suggestions only: "Load a paper to explore",
"Search for a paper by title or DOI". No graph-specific suggestions.
Implementation: TemplateSuggestionBuilder.match() returns generic prompts
when this.templates is empty. History suggestions may still have content
from previous session.

EDGE CASE 7: User pastes a long query (500+ characters)
Scenario: User pastes a multi-sentence research question from clipboard.
Expected behavior: No autocomplete dropdown shown (query is already complete).
Input validation in Athena handles length limits separately.
Implementation: _onInput() checks query.length. If > 300 characters, skips
suggestion generation entirely (assume complete query).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPENDENCIES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEPENDS ON (must be built first):
- Feature #001 (Context-Aware Responses): Template suggestions built from
  static context data (bottlenecks, clusters, mutation types).
- Feature #037 (Streaming Text): Input area must exist and be functional.
  Autocomplete attaches to the same input element.
- Feature #006 (Conversation Memory): History-based suggestions use
  conversation memory for topic and paper reference extraction.

ENABLES (can be built after this):
- Feature #009 (Proactive Suggestions): Autocomplete data (which suggestions
  users select) informs proactive suggestion quality.

ENHANCES (improves but does not require):
- Feature #005 (Graph-Aware Commands): "/" prefix completions integrated
  into the same dropdown infrastructure.
- Feature #042 (Suggested Follow-Ups): Follow-up suggestions can reuse
  the HistorySuggestionBuilder for consistency.
- Feature #003 (Quick Action Buttons): Button suggestions and autocomplete
  suggestions draw from the same template pool.

CONFLICTS WITH:
- Feature #005 (Graph-Aware Commands): Both use the input textarea for
  prefix detection. Resolution: "/" detected first, delegates to command
  system. Natural language goes to autocomplete. Mutually exclusive paths.

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES:
- Groq API (groq==0.8.0): Fast model for semantic suggestions. Optional --
  template and history suggestions work without Groq.
- Graph data: Required for template suggestions. If graph not loaded,
  only generic and history-based suggestions available.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DONE CRITERIA
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTIONAL:
[] Suggestions appear after 300ms debounce when user types 3+ characters
[] Template suggestions computed from graph data (bottlenecks, clusters)
[] History suggestions reference recent conversation topics and papers
[] LLM suggestions generated when template+history < 3 and query > 15 chars
[] Arrow keys navigate suggestions, Tab/Enter selects
[] Escape dismisses dropdown
[] "/" prefix triggers command completions instead of question suggestions
[] Selected suggestion populates input with full text
[] Cancel-on-keystroke aborts in-flight LLM requests

VISUAL:
[] Dropdown uses elevated surface background, consistent with Athena design
[] Paper names in suggestions use gold JetBrains Mono
[] Highlighted suggestion shows gold left border accent
[] LLM loading state shows half-filled dot indicator

PERFORMANCE:
[] Template suggestions appear in < 5ms after debounce
[] History suggestions appear in < 10ms after debounce
[] LLM suggestions timeout at 2 seconds (never block UI)

ACCESSIBILITY:
[] Dropdown has role="listbox", items have role="option"
[] Input has aria-expanded and aria-activedescendant attributes
[] Screen reader announces suggestion count and highlighted text
[] Full keyboard navigation without mouse

EDGE CASES:
[] Fast typing only triggers one suggestion generation (debounce works)
[] Empty graph shows generic suggestions (no crash)
[] 500+ character paste does not trigger autocomplete

VERIFICATION:
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 1 completed -- all findings addressed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Round 2 completed -- all findings addressed
[] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: Final round -- agent confirms ZERO remaining issues
[] BROWSER TEST: Feature verified in Chrome via browsermcp
[] CROSS-REFERENCE: Implementation matches this spec document exactly

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The pessimistic agent MUST verify this feature by:

1. READ: athena-autocomplete.js (AutoCompleteEngine), athena-autocomplete-
   templates.js (TemplateSuggestionBuilder), athena-autocomplete.css,
   backend/athena_autocomplete.py (AutoCompleteBackend),
   app.py (POST /api/athena/autocomplete)
2. COMPARE: Implementation against all Done Criteria checkboxes above
3. TEST these specific scenarios:
   a. Type "What is the" -- verify template suggestions with paper names
   b. Type "Tell me more" after discussing VGGNet -- verify history suggestion
   c. Type 20+ char query with no template match -- verify LLM call fires
   d. Type rapidly (10 chars in 200ms) -- verify only one suggestion generation
   e. Navigate suggestions with Arrow keys, select with Tab -- verify input
   f. Type "/" -- verify command completions appear instead of questions
   g. Press Escape -- verify dropdown hides, input keeps focus
   h. Test with no graph loaded -- verify generic suggestions, no crash
4. CHECK for these failure modes:
   a. Debounce timer accumulates if clearTimeout fails (memory leak)
   b. AbortController not properly cleaned up (stale requests)
   c. Dropdown positioned off-screen for inputs near viewport bottom
   d. Enter key both selects suggestion AND sends message simultaneously
   e. Template matching performance degrades with 600+ node graph
5. VERIFY no regression in: #001 (Context-Aware), #037 (Streaming), #005
6. The agent assumes the implementation is BROKEN until proven otherwise
7. The agent must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round or state with evidence why
8. The agent records ALL findings in the Pessimistic Agent Log section below
