================================================================================
FEATURE #095: INLINE POLL
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Input Methods
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           D
ESTIMATED HOURS: 10
OVERLAPS WITH:   #093 (Rating Input), #003 (Quick Action Buttons)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #007 (Visual Redesign), #050 (Rich Input Area)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Inline Poll widget allows Athena to present multiple-choice questions
directly in the chat conversation, collecting structured preferences from
the user. Instead of asking open-ended questions ("Which analysis would you
like next?") and parsing free text, Athena renders a poll with clickable
option cards that the user can select.

This matters for Arivu because the research analysis pipeline offers many
branching paths. After building a graph, Athena can offer: DNA clustering,
pruning impact analysis, gap finding, orphan detection, diversity scoring,
or genealogy storytelling. Presenting these as a poll with descriptions
gives the user a clear decision point without requiring them to know all
available analyses by name.

Polls support three modes: single-select (radio), multi-select (checkbox),
and ranked (drag to reorder). Single-select is the default for "choose one
analysis" scenarios. Multi-select is for "which filters to apply" scenarios.
Ranked is for "prioritize these analyses" scenarios where order matters.

Each poll option is a card with: a short title (20 chars max), a description
(60 chars max), and an optional icon or dot-matrix indicator. In single-
select mode, clicking an option highlights it with a --accent-gold border
and deselects others. In multi-select mode, clicking toggles selection.
In ranked mode, options can be dragged to reorder.

The poll result is a structured event sent as a message to the Athena
backend:
  { type: "poll_response", mode: "single", selected: ["pruning_analysis"] }
  { type: "poll_response", mode: "multi", selected: ["dna", "orphans"] }
  { type: "poll_response", mode: "ranked", order: ["pruning", "dna", "gaps"] }

Polls can also be used for research methodology preferences ("Which
clustering method do you prefer?"), confidence threshold selection ("How
much speculation should I include?"), and output format preferences
("How would you like results presented?").

After the user submits their poll response, the poll becomes read-only with
the selected option(s) highlighted. This preserves context in the
conversation history.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
Athena needs the user to choose between discrete options. Triggered when:
(a) user asks "what can you analyze?" and Athena presents available analyses,
(b) Athena proactively offers next-step options, (c) /analyze command
without parameters, (d) branching point in a multi-step analysis flow.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "What analyses are available for this graph?"

2. Athena responds with a poll:
   "This lineage has 152 papers and 203 edges. Here are the analyses
   I can run:"
   [Poll: Choose an analysis]
     [ ] DNA Clustering -- Identify intellectual DNA strands
     [ ] Pruning Impact -- Simulate removing foundational papers
     [ ] Gap Finding    -- Discover under-explored research spaces
     [ ] Orphan Detection -- Find abandoned research threads
     [ ] Diversity Score  -- Measure intellectual diversity

3. User clicks "Pruning Impact." The card highlights with gold border.
   Other cards dim slightly. A "Run Analysis" button becomes active.

4. User clicks "Run Analysis." The poll locks. Athena begins the pruning
   impact computation with a progress indicator.

5. Athena presents the pruning analysis results below the poll.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Poll shows "Pruning Impact" as selected (highlighted), others dimmed.
Read-only state. The user can reference the poll to see what they chose.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AthenaPollBlock class. Renders option cards,
  handles selection, drag reorder for ranked mode, submission.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Recognizes poll blocks in response JSON.
  Routes poll responses to /api/athena/chat.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Poll card styles, selected state, ranked mode
  drag handle, multi-select checkbox.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat includes poll blocks when options needed.
- backend/chat_guide.py: Generates poll configuration from available analyses.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  class AthenaPollBlock {
    constructor(config) {
      this.question = config.question;
      this.mode = config.mode; // 'single', 'multi', 'ranked'
      this.options = config.options.map(o => ({
        key: o.key, title: o.title, description: o.description,
        selected: false
      }));
      this.submitLabel = config.submit_label || 'Submit';
      this.locked = false;
    }

    render() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-poll-block';
      el.innerHTML = `<div class="poll-question">${this.question}</div>`;
      const list = document.createElement('div');
      list.className = `poll-options poll-${this.mode}`;
      this.options.forEach(opt => {
        const card = this._renderOption(opt);
        list.appendChild(card);
      });
      el.appendChild(list);
      const btn = document.createElement('button');
      btn.className = 'athena-btn-gold poll-submit';
      btn.textContent = this.submitLabel;
      btn.disabled = true;
      btn.addEventListener('click', () => this.submit());
      el.appendChild(btn);
      return el;
    }

    _renderOption(opt) {
      const card = document.createElement('div');
      card.className = 'poll-option';
      card.dataset.key = opt.key;
      card.innerHTML = `
        <span class="poll-indicator">${this.mode === 'multi' ? '[ ]' : '( )'}</span>
        <div class="poll-option-text">
          <strong>${opt.title}</strong>
          <span class="poll-desc">${opt.description}</span>
        </div>`;
      card.addEventListener('click', () => this._select(opt, card));
      return card;
    }

    _select(opt, card) {
      if (this.locked) return;
      if (this.mode === 'single') {
        this.options.forEach(o => o.selected = false);
        opt.selected = true;
      } else {
        opt.selected = !opt.selected;
      }
      this._updateVisuals();
    }

    submit() {
      this.locked = true;
      const selected = this.options.filter(o => o.selected).map(o => o.key);
      window.athenaEngine.sendStructured({
        type: 'poll_response', mode: this.mode,
        selected: this.mode === 'ranked' ? this._getRankedOrder() : selected
      });
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  # In chat_guide.py
  def build_analysis_poll(self, graph_stats):
      return {
          'type': 'poll',
          'question': 'Choose an analysis to run:',
          'mode': 'single',
          'options': [
              {'key': 'dna', 'title': 'DNA Clustering',
               'description': 'Identify intellectual DNA strands in the lineage'},
              {'key': 'pruning', 'title': 'Pruning Impact',
               'description': 'Simulate removing foundational papers'},
              {'key': 'gaps', 'title': 'Gap Finding',
               'description': 'Discover under-explored research spaces'},
              {'key': 'orphans', 'title': 'Orphan Detection',
               'description': 'Find abandoned research threads'},
              {'key': 'diversity', 'title': 'Diversity Score',
               'description': 'Measure intellectual diversity of the field'},
          ],
          'submit_label': 'Run Analysis'
      }

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response (when poll needed):
{
  "response": "Here are the available analyses:",
  "blocks": [{
    "type": "poll",
    "question": "Choose an analysis to run:",
    "mode": "single",
    "options": [
      {"key": "dna", "title": "DNA Clustering", "description": "..."}
    ],
    "submit_label": "Run Analysis"
  }]
}

Poll submission:
POST /api/athena/chat
{
  "session_id": "...",
  "structured_input": {
    "type": "poll_response",
    "mode": "single",
    "selected": ["pruning"]
  }
}

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Single-Select Poll (none selected)

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [Athena]                                                     |
  |  Choose an analysis to run:                                   |
  |                                                               |
  |  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  | ( ) DNA Clustering                                      |  |
  |  |     Identify intellectual DNA strands in the lineage    |  |
  |  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  | ( ) Pruning Impact                                      |  |
  |  |     Simulate removing foundational papers               |  |
  |  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  | ( ) Gap Finding                                         |  |
  |  |     Discover under-explored research spaces             |  |
  |  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |                                                               |
  |  [ Run Analysis ]  (disabled, gray)                           |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Single-Select with Pruning Impact Selected

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  | ( ) DNA Clustering                        (dimmed)      |  |
  |  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  +========================================================+  |
  |  | (*) Pruning Impact                   [GOLD BORDER]      |  |
  |  |     Simulate removing foundational papers               |  |
  |  +========================================================+  |
  |  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  | ( ) Gap Finding                           (dimmed)      |  |
  |  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |                                                               |
  |  [ Run Analysis ]  (active, gold)                             |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Locked After Submission

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  +========================================================+  |
  |  | (*) Pruning Impact  [checkmark]     (selected, locked)  |  |
  |  +========================================================+  |
  |  Other options hidden or fully dimmed.                        |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

TRANSITIONS:
Select: card border transitions to gold (150ms), other cards dim (150ms).
Submit: selected card stays, others collapse (200ms height to 0).
Locked: selected card gets checkmark icon, dimmed to 70%.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Athena determines branching options exist
  -> chat_guide.py builds poll block JSON
  -> /api/athena/chat response includes blocks[{type:"poll",...}]
  -> athena-blocks.js renders AthenaPollBlock
  -> User clicks option card (single: exclusive; multi: toggle)
  -> Submit button activates when at least 1 option selected
  -> User clicks Submit
  -> athena-engine.js sends structured_input with selected keys
  -> Backend routes to appropriate analysis module
  -> Athena responds with analysis results

ERROR PATH:
  -> No options selected on submit: button remains disabled.
  -> Backend cannot run selected analysis (missing data): "Unable to run
     Pruning Impact -- graph has fewer than 5 papers. Try DNA Clustering."
  -> Ranked mode with only 1 option: auto-submit without ranking.

DATA STORED: Poll selection in action_log (action_type: "poll_response").
DATA CACHED: Poll state in AthenaPollBlock instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

STAGE: Option generation. Athena uses the LLM to determine which analyses
are relevant for the current graph state and generate appropriate descriptions.

PROMPT:
```
You are Athena, the research intelligence assistant for Arivu.
Graph stats: {node_count} papers, {edge_count} edges, {cluster_count} clusters.
Available analyses: dna, pruning, gaps, orphans, diversity.

The user asked: {user_message}

Return JSON listing which analyses are relevant and brief descriptions:
{
  "options": [
    {"key": "analysis_key", "title": "Short Name", "description": "One line (max 60 chars)"}
  ],
  "recommended": "key of most relevant analysis"
}
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with options and recommendation
FALLBACK: Present all five analyses with static descriptions
TOKEN BUDGET: ~200 tokens per poll generation

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element           | Action     | Response                                   |
|-------------------|------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Option card       | Click      | Select (single: exclusive; multi: toggle)  |
| Option card       | Hover      | Subtle border highlight (--bg-elevated)    |
| Submit button     | Click      | Lock poll, send selection, show loading    |
| Ranked option     | Drag       | Reorder in list (drag handle on left)      |
| Locked poll       | Click      | No interaction (read-only)                 |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Select: border-color to --accent-gold, 150ms ease
- Deselect: border-color to transparent, 150ms ease
- Dim: opacity 0.5, 150ms ease
- Collapse (after submit): height to 0, opacity to 0, 200ms ease
- Ranked drag: translateY follows pointer, 60fps

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Single-select: role="radiogroup" with role="radio" per option
- Multi-select: role="group" with role="checkbox" per option
- Ranked: role="listbox" with role="option" and aria-grabbed
- Keyboard: arrow keys to navigate, Space/Enter to select
- Screen reader: "DNA Clustering, radio button, not selected, 1 of 5"
- Submit button: aria-label "Submit selected analysis"

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Only one option available
   Expected: Auto-select it. Show as informational: "Running DNA Clustering."
   Implementation: If options.length === 1, auto-submit.

2. Scenario: User tries to submit with nothing selected
   Expected: Submit button stays disabled. No error message needed.
   Implementation: Button disabled until selection count >= 1.

3. Scenario: Ranked mode with 10 options (long list)
   Expected: Scrollable list with max-height. Drag handles visible.
   Implementation: overflow-y: auto, max-height: 400px.

4. Scenario: Poll rendered but user types a response instead of clicking
   Expected: Athena parses text ("run pruning") and matches to poll option.
   Implementation: Fallback text matching in athena-engine.js.

5. Scenario: Multi-select with conflicting options
   Expected: Athena warns when incompatible options selected together.
   Implementation: Backend validates option combinations.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

================================================================================
FEATURE #096: RESEARCH NOTE ARTIFACT
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Artifact Outputs
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           G
ESTIMATED HOURS: 12
OVERLAPS WITH:   #011 (Artifact Cards), #098 (Reading List Artifact)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
                 #011 (Artifact Cards)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Research Note Artifact allows users to save insights from an Athena
conversation as structured, persistent notes. When a user discovers a
valuable finding during their exploration -- a key mutation chain, a cluster
relationship, a pruning insight, a gap in the literature -- they can pin it
as a Research Note. The note captures the insight text, relevant paper
references, graph context, and a user-editable title and tags.

This feature exists because Arivu generates dense, high-value analysis that
is easily lost when the conversation scrolls. A user may spend 20 minutes
exploring a lineage, uncovering that the transformer architecture's key
innovation was a generalization of attention mechanisms from Bahdanau (2014)
through Luong (2015), creating a dependency chain that 47% of subsequent
work relies on. That insight deserves to survive beyond the chat session.

Research Notes are stored as artifacts in the session and can be exported
individually or as a collection. Each note has a canonical structure:

  Title: [user-editable, auto-suggested by Athena]
  Tags: [user-editable, auto-suggested from graph metadata]
  Insight: [the captured text from the conversation]
  Papers Referenced: [list of paper IDs mentioned in the insight]
  Graph Context: [snapshot of relevant graph state -- seed paper, cluster,
    edges referenced]
  Created: [timestamp]
  Source Message: [link/reference to the originating chat message]

The backend stores notes in the session memory (graph_memory JSONB) and
optionally in a dedicated artifact storage if the user has an account. Notes
are accessible from a "Notes" tab in the right panel and can be browsed,
searched, edited, and exported.

Athena auto-suggests notes when it detects particularly significant findings.
A small "Save as Note" button appears below high-value messages (those
containing pruning impact percentages, mutation chain descriptions, cluster
analyses, or gap identifications). The user can also manually pin any
message as a note via long-press or a "Pin" button.

The note editor follows the Arivu dark theme with a compact card layout.
Title in Inter 600 weight, tags as small gold pills, insight text in Inter
400, paper references as clickable mini-cards that highlight the referenced
node in the graph.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User wants to save an insight. Triggered by: (a) clicking "Save as Note"
on a high-value Athena message, (b) selecting text and choosing "Create
Note," (c) typing /note to save the last message, (d) Athena proactively
suggesting "This finding seems important. Save as note?"

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. Athena presents a pruning analysis: "Removing Vaswani (2017) would
   collapse 47 papers (31% of the lineage). The surviving papers all
   trace through Bahdanau (2014) via an independent attention path."

2. A subtle "Save as Note" button appears below the message (fade in
   after 1 second). User clicks it.

3. A note editor card expands below the message:
   Title: [Vaswani Pruning Impact -- 31% Collapse] (auto-suggested)
   Tags: [pruning] [attention] [vaswani-2017] (auto-suggested)
   Insight: (pre-filled with the message text)
   Papers: Vaswani (2017), Bahdanau (2014) (auto-detected)
   [Save Note] [Cancel]

4. User edits the title to "Critical Dependency: Vaswani -> Bahdanau"
   and adds a tag "thesis-chapter-3". Clicks Save.

5. Note saved. A compact card appears in the chat: "Note saved:
   Critical Dependency: Vaswani -> Bahdanau" with a gold bookmark icon.
   The Notes tab in the right panel shows the new note.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Note persists in session. Visible in Notes tab. Can be exported as
markdown, included in literature review, or referenced in future messages.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AthenaResearchNoteBlock class. Renders note
  editor, save button, and inline note cards.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Auto-detect high-value messages for note
  suggestion. Handles /note command.
- static/js/panels.js: Notes tab in right panel (list, search, export).
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Note editor card, tag pills, paper refs.
- app.py: /api/athena/notes endpoint for CRUD operations.
- backend/chat_guide.py: Auto-suggest titles and tags from message content.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  class AthenaResearchNoteBlock {
    constructor(messageData) {
      this.title = messageData.suggested_title || '';
      this.tags = messageData.suggested_tags || [];
      this.insight = messageData.text;
      this.papers = messageData.referenced_papers || [];
      this.sourceMessageId = messageData.message_id;
      this.saved = false;
    }

    renderEditor() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-note-editor';
      el.innerHTML = `
        <input class="note-title" value="${this.title}" placeholder="Note title..."/>
        <div class="note-tags">${this.tags.map(t =>
          `<span class="tag-pill">${t} <button class="tag-remove">x</button></span>`
        ).join('')}<input class="tag-input" placeholder="Add tag..."/></div>
        <div class="note-insight">${this.insight}</div>
        <div class="note-papers">${this.papers.map(p =>
          `<span class="paper-ref" data-id="${p.paper_id}">${p.title} (${p.year})</span>`
        ).join('')}</div>
        <div class="note-actions">
          <button class="athena-btn-gold note-save">Save Note</button>
          <button class="athena-btn-muted note-cancel">Cancel</button>
        </div>`;
      return el;
    }

    save() {
      this.saved = true;
      const note = {
        title: this.title,
        tags: this.tags,
        insight: this.insight,
        papers: this.papers.map(p => p.paper_id),
        source_message_id: this.sourceMessageId,
        created_at: new Date().toISOString()
      };
      fetch('/api/athena/notes', {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: {'Content-Type': 'application/json'},
        body: JSON.stringify(note)
      });
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  # In app.py -- notes endpoint
  @app.route('/api/athena/notes', methods=['POST'])
  def save_research_note():
      data = request.json
      session_id = data.get('session_id') or request.cookies.get('session_id')
      note = {
          'title': data['title'],
          'tags': data.get('tags', []),
          'insight': data['insight'],
          'papers': data.get('papers', []),
          'source_message_id': data.get('source_message_id'),
          'created_at': data.get('created_at')
      }
      update_graph_memory(session_id, {'notes': {note['created_at']: note}})
      return jsonify({'status': 'saved', 'note_id': note['created_at']})

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/notes
Request: { session_id, title, tags, insight, papers, source_message_id }
Response: { status: "saved", note_id: "..." }

GET /api/athena/notes?session_id=...
Response: { notes: [...] }

DELETE /api/athena/notes/{note_id}
Response: { status: "deleted" }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  |  the lineage). The surviving papers all trace through         |

STATE: Note Editor Open

  |  |                                                     |      |
  |  | Removing Vaswani (2017) would collapse 47 papers    |      |
  |  | (31% of the lineage)...                             |      |
  |  |                                                     |      |
  |  | Papers: Vaswani (2017)  Bahdanau (2014)             |      |
  |  |                                                     |      |
  |  | [ Save Note ]  [ Cancel ]                           |      |
  |  +-----------------------------------------------------+      |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Note Saved (compact card in chat)

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [bookmark] Note saved: Critical Dependency: Vaswani -> B... |
  |  [pruning] [attention]  --  2 papers referenced               |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

TRANSITIONS:
Save button: fade in (opacity 0 to 1, 300ms, after 1s delay).
Editor open: height 0 to auto, 250ms ease.
Save: editor collapses to compact card (200ms).
Cancel: editor collapses to nothing (200ms).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Athena sends high-value message with referenced papers
  -> Frontend detects high-value signals (pruning %, mutation chain, gap)
  -> "Save as Note" button fades in below message
  -> User clicks -> note editor expands with auto-populated fields
  -> User edits title, tags, optionally edits insight text
  -> User clicks Save
  -> POST /api/athena/notes with note data
  -> Backend stores in session graph_memory JSONB
  -> Compact confirmation card appears in chat
  -> Notes tab in right panel updates

Auto-suggestion flow:
  -> chat_guide.py analyzes message for key metrics and paper references
  -> Generates suggested_title from first significant finding
  -> Generates suggested_tags from: mutation types, paper names, cluster IDs
  -> Tags and title sent as metadata alongside the chat response

ERROR PATH:
  -> Save fails (DB error): "Could not save note. Please try again."
  -> Title empty: "Please add a title to your note."
  -> Session expired: save to localStorage as fallback.

DATA STORED: Note object in sessions.graph_memory (JSONB).
DATA CACHED: Note list in frontend memory for Notes tab.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

STAGE: Title and tag auto-suggestion when "Save as Note" is triggered.

PROMPT:
```
You are Athena, the research intelligence assistant for Arivu.
The user wants to save this insight as a research note:
"{insight_text}"

Papers mentioned: {paper_list}

Generate:
{
  "title": "concise title for the note (under 60 chars)",
  "tags": ["tag1", "tag2", "tag3"] (3-5 tags, lowercase, hyphenated)
}

Tags should capture: topic, analysis type, key papers, finding type.
Return ONLY valid JSON.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with title and tags
FALLBACK: Title = first 60 chars of insight. Tags = extracted paper names.
TOKEN BUDGET: ~100 tokens per suggestion

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action     | Response                                |
|----------------------|------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Save as Note button  | Click      | Open note editor below message          |
| Title input          | Type       | Live edit, auto-save on blur            |
| Tag pill             | Click x    | Remove tag                              |
| Tag input            | Type+Enter | Add new tag                             |
| Paper reference      | Click      | Highlight node in graph                 |
| Save Note button     | Click      | Save note, collapse to compact card     |
| Cancel button        | Click      | Close editor, no save                   |
| Compact note card    | Click      | Scroll to source message                |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Save button fade: opacity 0 to 0.8, 300ms ease, 1s delay
- Editor expand: max-height 0 to 400px, 250ms ease
- Tag add: pill slides in from right (100ms)
- Tag remove: pill shrinks and fades (100ms)
- Compact card: slide-in from top (150ms)

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Note editor has role="dialog" with aria-label "Research note editor"
- Title input: aria-label "Note title"
- Tags: role="list" with role="listitem" per tag
- Save/Cancel: standard button roles
- Paper refs: role="link" with aria-label "View [paper] in graph"
- Keyboard: Tab between fields, Enter to save, Escape to cancel

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Message has no referenced papers (general advice)
   Expected: Papers section is empty. Note still saveable.
   Implementation: Papers field is optional; hide section if empty.

2. Scenario: User saves 50+ notes in one session
   Expected: Notes tab in right panel becomes scrollable with search.
   Implementation: Virtual scrolling for large note lists.

3. Scenario: Note insight text is very long (2000+ chars from complex analysis)
   Expected: Truncate in editor to 1500 chars with "Show full" toggle.
   Implementation: Clamp with expandable overflow.

4. Scenario: Session expires before note is exported
   Expected: Notes still accessible until browser tab is closed (localStorage).
   Implementation: Mirror notes to localStorage on each save.

5. Scenario: User edits a saved note
   Expected: Notes tab allows title and tag editing. Insight is read-only.
   Implementation: PUT /api/athena/notes/{note_id} for metadata updates.

================================================================================
FEATURE #097: BIBTEX OUTPUT BLOCK
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Artifact Outputs
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           G
ESTIMATED HOURS: 10
OVERLAPS WITH:   #011 (Artifact Cards), #098 (Reading List Artifact)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
                 #011 (Artifact Cards)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The BibTeX Output Block generates properly formatted BibTeX citation entries
for papers discussed in an Athena conversation. When a user asks "Give me the
BibTeX for the papers we discussed" or "Cite these papers," Athena renders a
code block containing valid BibTeX entries that can be copied to clipboard or
downloaded as a .bib file.

This is essential for Arivu's target audience -- academic researchers. Every
paper exploration session eventually leads to writing. When a researcher has
spent time understanding a lineage through Athena, the natural next step is
to cite the relevant papers in their own work. Generating BibTeX directly
from the conversation eliminates the tedious step of looking up each paper
on Google Scholar or DBLP.

The BibTeX block uses the paper metadata already available in the graph:
title, authors, year, venue (journal/conference), DOI, and URL. The citation
key is generated from first author surname + year (e.g., vaswani2017). If
the venue is unknown, it falls back to an @misc entry type. All entries are
validated for BibTeX syntax correctness before rendering.

The block is rendered as a code block with BibTeX syntax highlighting:
entry types (@article, @inproceedings, @misc) in --accent-blue, keys in
--accent-gold, field names in --text-secondary, values in --text-primary.
A "Copy All" button copies all entries to clipboard. A "Download .bib" button
downloads the entries as a file. Individual entries can be copied via a
per-entry copy button.

Athena intelligently selects which papers to include. If the user asks "cite
the papers we discussed," it includes all papers explicitly mentioned in the
conversation (tracked in chat_history). If the user asks "cite the top 10
by PageRank," it extracts the relevant subset. If the user names specific
papers, only those are included.

The block also supports BibTeX format variants: standard, ACM, IEEE, APA
style keys. The user can switch between formats via a dropdown in the block
header.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User requests citations. Triggered by: (a) "Give me the BibTeX for these
papers," (b) /cite command, (c) Athena suggests citations after a detailed
analysis, (d) "export references" in conversation.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "Give me BibTeX citations for Vaswani (2017) and Bahdanau (2014)."

2. Athena responds with a BibTeX block:
   "Here are the BibTeX entries for the two papers:"
   [BibTeX Block: 2 entries]
   @inproceedings{vaswani2017,
     title = {Attention Is All You Need},
     author = {Vaswani, Ashish and ...},
     year = {2017},
     ...
   }
   @article{bahdanau2014,
     ...
   }
   [Copy All] [Download .bib] [Format: Standard v]

3. User clicks "Copy All." Clipboard contains all BibTeX text. A toast
   confirms: "2 BibTeX entries copied to clipboard."

4. User clicks "Download .bib." Browser downloads "arivu-references.bib".

5. The block remains in chat for future reference.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Block persists in chat history as a rendered code block. User can re-copy
or re-download at any time.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AthenaBibTeXBlock class. Renders syntax-
  highlighted BibTeX, copy/download handlers.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes BibTeX requests to backend.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: BibTeX syntax highlighting colors.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat generates BibTeX blocks.
- backend/chat_guide.py: Generates BibTeX from paper metadata.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  class AthenaBibTeXBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.entries = data.entries;  // array of {key, type, fields, raw}
      this.format = data.format || 'standard';
    }

    render() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-bibtex-block';
      el.innerHTML = `
        <div class="bibtex-header">
          <span class="bibtex-count">${this.entries.length} entries</span>
          <select class="bibtex-format">
            <option value="standard">Standard</option>
            <option value="acm">ACM</option>
            <option value="ieee">IEEE</option>
          </select>
          <button class="bibtex-copy-all">Copy All</button>
          <button class="bibtex-download">Download .bib</button>
        </div>
        <pre class="bibtex-code"><code>${this._highlight()}</code></pre>`;
      this._attachHandlers(el);
      return el;
    }

    _highlight() {
      return this.entries.map(e => {
        let h = e.raw;
        h = h.replace(/(@\w+)/g, '<span class="bib-type">$1</span>');
        h = h.replace(/(\w+)\s*=/g, '<span class="bib-field">$1</span> =');
        return `<div class="bib-entry">${h}<button class="bib-copy-one" data-key="${e.key}">Copy</button></div>`;
      }).join('\n\n');
    }

    copyAll() {
      const text = this.entries.map(e => e.raw).join('\n\n');
      navigator.clipboard.writeText(text);
    }

    download() {
      const text = this.entries.map(e => e.raw).join('\n\n');
      const blob = new Blob([text], {type: 'text/plain'});
      const a = document.createElement('a');
      a.href = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
      a.download = 'arivu-references.bib';
      a.click();
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  # In chat_guide.py
  def generate_bibtex(self, paper_ids):
      entries = []
      for pid in paper_ids:
          paper = self.graph.papers.get(pid)
          if not paper: continue
          key = self._bibtex_key(paper)
          entry_type = self._determine_entry_type(paper)
          fields = {
              'title': paper.title,
              'author': ' and '.join(paper.authors[:5]),
              'year': str(paper.year) if paper.year else '',
              'doi': paper.doi or '',
              'url': paper.url or '',
          }
          if paper.venue:
              if entry_type == '@article':
                  fields['journal'] = paper.venue
              else:
                  fields['booktitle'] = paper.venue
          raw = self._format_bibtex_entry(entry_type, key, fields)
          entries.append({'key': key, 'type': entry_type, 'fields': fields, 'raw': raw})
      return entries

  def _bibtex_key(self, paper):
      surname = paper.authors[0].split()[-1].lower() if paper.authors else 'unknown'
      year = paper.year or 'nd'
      return f"{surname}{year}"

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response (when BibTeX requested):
{
  "response": "Here are the BibTeX entries:",
  "blocks": [{
    "type": "bibtex",
    "entries": [
      {"key": "vaswani2017", "type": "@inproceedings", "fields": {...}, "raw": "@inproceedings{vaswani2017,...}"}
    ],
    "format": "standard"
  }]
}

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: BibTeX Block Rendered

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [Athena]                                                     |
  |  Here are the BibTeX entries:                                 |
  |                                                               |
  |  +-- BibTeX (2 entries) ---- [Standard v] [Copy] [DL] ---+  |
  |  |                                                         |  |
  |  |  @inproceedings{vaswani2017,              [Copy]        |  |
  |  |    title = {Attention Is All You Need},                 |  |
  |  |    author = {Vaswani, Ashish and Shazeer, ...},         |  |
  |  |    year = {2017},                                       |  |
  |  |    booktitle = {NeurIPS},                               |  |
  |  |    doi = {10.48550/arXiv.1706.03762}                    |  |
  |  |  }                                                      |  |
  |  |                                                         |  |
  |  |  @article{bahdanau2014,                   [Copy]        |  |
  |  |    title = {Neural Machine Translation...},             |  |
  |  |    author = {Bahdanau, Dzmitry and ...},                |  |
  |  |    year = {2014},                                       |  |
  |  |    journal = {arXiv preprint}                           |  |
  |  |  }                                                      |  |
  |  +---------------------------------------------------------+  |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Copy Confirmation

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [Copy All] -> [Copied!] (text changes for 2s, then reverts) |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

TRANSITIONS:
Block render: code block fades in (200ms).
Copy: button text changes "Copy" -> "Copied!" (150ms), reverts after 2s.
Format change: block content fades out/in (200ms) as entries regenerate.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User requests BibTeX
  -> athena-engine.js sends query to /api/athena/chat
  -> chat_guide.py identifies requested papers from conversation context
  -> generate_bibtex() builds entries from paper metadata
  -> Response includes blocks[{type:"bibtex", entries:[...]}]
  -> athena-blocks.js renders AthenaBibTeXBlock with syntax highlighting
  -> User clicks Copy All -> clipboard write
  -> User clicks Download -> .bib file download

ERROR PATH:
  -> Paper has no authors: use "Unknown Author" in citation key.
  -> Paper has no venue: fall back to @misc entry type.
  -> Paper has no year: use "n.d." (no date) in key and entry.
  -> Clipboard API blocked: show "Copy failed" toast, fall back to select-all.

DATA STORED: Generated BibTeX in chat_history blocks.
DATA CACHED: Rendered block in DOM.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

STAGE: Paper identification. When the user asks for "BibTeX for papers we
discussed," the LLM identifies which papers from the conversation to include.

PROMPT:
```
You are Athena, the research intelligence assistant for Arivu.
The user wants BibTeX citations.
Conversation papers: {list of paper titles and IDs mentioned}
User query: {user_message}

Return JSON listing which papers to include:
{
  "paper_ids": ["id1", "id2", ...],
  "note": "optional note about the selection"
}

If the user specified papers by name, match them. If they said "all discussed
papers," include all from the conversation. If they said "top N by X,"
select accordingly.

Return ONLY valid JSON.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON with paper_ids array
FALLBACK: Include all papers mentioned in conversation
TOKEN BUDGET: ~150 tokens per BibTeX request

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action | Response                                   |
|----------------------|--------|--------------------------------------------|
| Copy All button      | Click  | Copy all entries to clipboard              |
| Download .bib button | Click  | Download as arivu-references.bib           |
| Per-entry Copy       | Click  | Copy single entry to clipboard             |
| Format dropdown      | Change | Regenerate entries in selected format       |
| Code block           | Select | Standard text selection for manual copy     |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Code block: fade in, 200ms ease
- Copy confirmation: text swap "Copy" <-> "Copied!", 150ms, revert 2s
- Format switch: content cross-fade, 200ms

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Code block has role="region" with aria-label "BibTeX citations"
- Copy buttons have aria-label "Copy BibTeX entry for [paper]"
- Format dropdown: standard select with aria-label "BibTeX format"
- Screen reader: "BibTeX citations region. 2 entries. Copy All button."
- Keyboard: Tab to buttons, Enter to activate

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Paper has duplicate citation key (two "smith2019" papers)
   Expected: Append letter suffix: smith2019a, smith2019b.
   Implementation: Track used keys, append suffix on collision.

2. Scenario: 50+ papers requested (large lineage export)
   Expected: Block scrollable with max-height 400px. Download button
   prominent since copy-paste is impractical.
   Implementation: overflow-y: auto on code block.

3. Scenario: Special characters in title (LaTeX-incompatible)
   Expected: Escape special characters: & -> \&, % -> \%, etc.
   Implementation: BibTeX sanitization pass on all field values.

4. Scenario: Paper metadata incomplete (missing DOI, venue, authors)
   Expected: Omit empty fields. Use @misc type. Note in entry comment.
   Implementation: Only include fields with non-empty values.

5. Scenario: Clipboard API not available (older browser, iframe)
   Expected: Select all text in code block, prompt manual Ctrl+C.
   Implementation: Fallback selection via window.getSelection().

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
            #011 (Artifact Cards)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #098 (Reading List Artifact), #099 (Literature Review Draft)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: Paper metadata from graph engine. Clipboard API for copy.

[ ] Dark background (--bg-elevated)
[ ] Copy confirmation text swap

PERFORMANCE:

[ ] Clipboard write within 50ms
[ ] Download within 100ms

ACCESSIBILITY:

[ ] Buttons with descriptive aria-labels
[ ] Keyboard navigable
[ ] Screen reader announces entry count

EDGE CASES:

[ ] Large entry count (scrollable)
[ ] LaTeX special character escaping
[ ] Missing metadata graceful degradation

VERIFICATION:

[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec
[ ] BIBTEX VALIDITY: Entries parse in LaTeX
[ ] CLIPBOARD: Copy verified in 3 browsers

================================================================================
FEATURE #098: READING LIST ARTIFACT
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Artifact Outputs
PRIORITY:        P1
PHASE:           G
ESTIMATED HOURS: 12
OVERLAPS WITH:   #096 (Research Note), #097 (BibTeX Output)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
                 #011 (Artifact Cards)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Reading List Artifact generates a curated, prioritized reading list from
the papers discussed in an Athena conversation. Unlike the BibTeX block which
exports raw citations, the Reading List is an annotated guide that tells the
user which papers to read first, why each paper matters, and how they connect
to each other within the lineage.

This is one of Arivu's highest-value practical outputs. A researcher new to
a field can spend an hour with Athena exploring a lineage graph and walk away
with a structured reading plan that would otherwise take days of manual
literature searching to assemble. The reading list leverages every metric
Arivu computes: PageRank centrality (structural importance), pruning impact
(foundational dependency), DNA cluster membership (topical grouping), and
mutation types (intellectual contribution type).

Each reading list entry contains:
  - Paper title, authors (first 3), year
  - Priority level: "Essential" / "Important" / "Recommended" / "Optional"
  - Reading order number (1, 2, 3, ...)
  - Brief annotation: why this paper matters (1-2 sentences)
  - Lineage context: where it sits in the graph structure
  - Estimated reading time (based on text_tier: full-text papers estimated
    at 30-60 min, abstract-only at 5-10 min)

The reading list is organized into sections by DNA cluster. Within each
section, papers are ordered by priority. The overall structure follows the
intellectual narrative of the field: foundational papers first, then key
innovations, then recent extensions.

The artifact renders as a scrollable card in the chat with: a title bar,
section dividers per cluster, paper entries with priority badges, and action
buttons: "Copy as Markdown," "Download as PDF," "Export to BibTeX," and
"Send to Email" (Phase 6+ only).

The reading list can be regenerated with different parameters: "Focus on
methodology papers," "Only essential papers," "Include all 152 papers,"
"Papers from the last 5 years only."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User requests a reading list. Triggered by: (a) "Create a reading list,"
(b) /readinglist command, (c) Athena suggests after in-depth exploration,
(d) "What should I read first?"

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "Create a reading list for this lineage. I am new to the
   attention mechanism field."

2. Athena generates the list with a progress indicator: "Analyzing 152
   papers across 4 clusters to build your reading plan..."

3. Reading list appears as a structured card:
   [Reading List: Attention Mechanism Lineage -- 23 papers]
   Section 1: Foundational Sequence Models (5 papers)
     1. [Essential] Bahdanau (2014) -- "Introduced attention for NMT..."
     2. [Essential] Luong (2015) -- "Simplified attention variants..."
   Section 2: The Transformer Revolution (6 papers)
     3. [Essential] Vaswani (2017) -- "Generalized attention to..."
     ...
   [Copy Markdown] [Download PDF] [Export BibTeX]

4. User clicks "Copy Markdown." Formatted markdown copied to clipboard.

5. User asks: "Remove the optional papers and focus on methodology."
   Athena regenerates with updated filters.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Reading list persists as an artifact card. Can be re-downloaded, modified,
or referenced later in the conversation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AthenaReadingListBlock class. Renders sectioned
  list, priority badges, action buttons.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes reading list requests.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Reading list card, section headers, priority
  badges, paper entries.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat generates reading list artifact.
- backend/chat_guide.py: Reading list generation logic using graph metrics.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  class AthenaReadingListBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.title = data.title;
      this.sections = data.sections;
      this.totalPapers = data.total_papers;
    }

    render() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-reading-list';
      el.innerHTML = `
        <div class="rl-header">
          <h3>${this.title}</h3>
          <span class="rl-count">${this.totalPapers} papers</span>
        </div>`;
      this.sections.forEach(sec => el.appendChild(this._renderSection(sec)));
      el.appendChild(this._renderActions());
      return el;
    }

    _renderSection(section) {
      const div = document.createElement('div');
      div.className = 'rl-section';
      div.innerHTML = `<h4 class="rl-section-title">${section.name}</h4>`;
      section.papers.forEach(p => {
        div.innerHTML += `
          <div class="rl-entry" data-id="${p.paper_id}">
            <span class="rl-order">${p.order}.</span>
            <span class="rl-priority rl-${p.priority.toLowerCase()}">${p.priority}</span>
            <div class="rl-paper">
              <strong>${p.title}</strong> (${p.year})
              <div class="rl-annotation">${p.annotation}</div>
              <div class="rl-meta">${p.reading_time}</div>
            </div>
          </div>`;
      });
      return div;
    }

    copyMarkdown() {
      const md = this._toMarkdown();
      navigator.clipboard.writeText(md);
    }

    downloadPDF() {
      window.open(`/api/athena/reading-list/pdf?session_id=${this.sessionId}`);
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  # In chat_guide.py
  def generate_reading_list(self, graph, user_context=''):
      papers = list(graph.papers.values())
      pagerank = nx.pagerank(graph.nx_graph, alpha=0.85)
      clusters = graph.dna_clusters or {}
      sections = {}
      for p in papers:
          cluster = clusters.get(p.paper_id, 'Uncategorized')
          if cluster not in sections:
              sections[cluster] = []
          priority = self._compute_priority(p, pagerank, graph)
          annotation = self._generate_annotation(p, graph)
          sections[cluster].append({
              'paper_id': p.paper_id, 'title': p.title,
              'year': p.year, 'authors': p.authors[:3],
              'priority': priority, 'annotation': annotation,
              'reading_time': self._estimate_reading_time(p)
          })
      # Sort sections by average PageRank, papers within by priority
      ordered = self._order_sections(sections, pagerank)
      return self._assign_reading_order(ordered)

  def _compute_priority(self, paper, pagerank, graph):
      pr = pagerank.get(paper.paper_id, 0)
      pruning = graph.precomputed_pruning.get(paper.paper_id, {})
      impact = pruning.get('impact_percentage', 0)
      if pr > 0.05 or impact > 20: return 'Essential'
      elif pr > 0.02 or impact > 10: return 'Important'
      elif pr > 0.01: return 'Recommended'
      else: return 'Optional'

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response (reading list):
{
  "response": "Here is your reading list:",
  "blocks": [{
    "type": "reading_list",
    "title": "Attention Mechanism Lineage",
    "total_papers": 23,
    "sections": [{
      "name": "Foundational Sequence Models",
      "papers": [{
        "paper_id": "...", "title": "...", "year": 2014,
        "priority": "Essential", "order": 1,
        "annotation": "Introduced attention for NMT...",
        "reading_time": "45 min"
      }]
    }]
  }]
}

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Reading List Rendered

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [Athena]                                                     |
  |  Here is your reading list:                                   |

  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  |  -- Foundational Sequence Models --                      |  |
  |  |  1. [ESSENTIAL] Bahdanau (2014)                          |  |
  |  |     Introduced attention mechanism for neural machine    |  |
  |  |     translation. The seed of all subsequent work.        |  |
  |  |     ~45 min read                                         |  |
  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  |  2. [ESSENTIAL] Luong (2015)                             |  |
  |  |     Simplified and extended attention variants...         |  |
  |  |     ~30 min read                                         |  |
  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  |  -- The Transformer Revolution --                        |  |
  |  |  3. [ESSENTIAL] Vaswani (2017)                           |  |
  |  |     Generalized attention, removing recurrence...         |  |
  |  |     ~60 min read                                         |  |
  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  |  [Copy Markdown] [Download PDF] [Export BibTeX]          |  |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------------+  |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Priority Badges

  [ESSENTIAL]  -- gold background (--accent-gold), dark text
  [IMPORTANT]  -- blue background (--accent-blue), white text
  [RECOMMENDED] -- teal background (--accent-teal), white text
  [OPTIONAL]   -- muted background (--bg-elevated), muted text

TRANSITIONS:
List render: sections stagger in (100ms per section, slide from left).
Badge: no animation (static).
Paper entry hover: background lightens (--bg-elevated), 100ms.
Action click: button text swaps "Copy" -> "Copied!" 2s.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User requests reading list
  -> athena-engine.js sends to /api/athena/chat
  -> chat_guide.py calls generate_reading_list(graph, user_context)
  -> PageRank, pruning impacts, DNA clusters queried from graph state
  -> LLM generates per-paper annotations
  -> Sections organized by cluster, papers ordered by priority
  -> Reading order assigned sequentially
  -> Response includes blocks[{type:"reading_list",...}]
  -> athena-blocks.js renders AthenaReadingListBlock

Export flows:
  -> Copy Markdown: client-side generation and clipboard write
  -> Download PDF: GET /api/athena/reading-list/pdf (server-side PDF gen)
  -> Export BibTeX: client-side BibTeX generation from paper metadata

ERROR PATH:
  -> Graph has no clusters: use single "All Papers" section.
  -> Graph has 1 paper: "Reading list requires at least 3 papers."
  -> PDF generation fails: "PDF unavailable. Use Copy Markdown instead."

DATA STORED: Reading list JSON in chat_history blocks.
DATA CACHED: Rendered block in DOM; PDF cached on server for 1 hour.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL

STAGE: Annotation generation. For each paper in the reading list, the LLM
generates a 1-2 sentence annotation explaining why it matters.

PROMPT:
```
You are Athena, generating a research reading list for Arivu.
Paper: {title} ({year}) by {authors}
Role in lineage: PageRank={pagerank}, pruning_impact={impact}%,
  cluster={cluster}, mutation_types={mutations}
User context: {user_context}

Write a 1-2 sentence annotation explaining:
- What this paper contributes to the field
- Why the reader should read it at this point in the sequence

Be specific. Reference concrete contributions, not generic praise.
Max 100 words.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: Plain text annotation
FALLBACK: "Key paper in the {cluster} cluster. Cited {n} times."
TOKEN BUDGET: ~150 tokens per paper, batched for efficiency

|----------------------|--------|--------------------------------------------|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Graph with 200+ papers (reading list too long)
   Expected: Show top 30 by priority. "Showing 30 of 200 papers.
   [Show all] to see the full list."
   Implementation: Default cap at 30, expandable.

2. Scenario: User asks to filter by year ("only papers after 2018")
   Expected: Reading list regenerated with year filter. Priority
   recomputed within filtered set.
   Implementation: Accept filter parameters, rerun generation.

3. Scenario: No clusters computed yet (DNA analysis not run)
   Expected: Single section "All Papers" with no cluster grouping.
   Implementation: Check for dna_json; fallback to flat list.

4. Scenario: PDF export for 100+ papers
   Expected: Multi-page PDF with table of contents. Sections as chapters.
   Implementation: Server-side PDF generation with reportlab.

5. Scenario: Two papers with identical titles from different years
   Expected: Disambiguation by year and first author in list.
   Implementation: Always show year and first author alongside title.

================================================================================
FEATURE #099: LITERATURE REVIEW DRAFT ARTIFACT
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Artifact Outputs
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           G
ESTIMATED HOURS: 18
OVERLAPS WITH:   #098 (Reading List), #097 (BibTeX Output)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
                 #011 (Artifact Cards), #096 (Research Note Artifact)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Literature Review Draft Artifact generates a structured, multi-section
literature review document from the papers and insights in the current Arivu
graph. This is the most sophisticated artifact Athena produces: a coherent
narrative that traces the intellectual evolution of a research field, organized
by thematic clusters, with proper citation flow and analytical commentary.

This feature leverages Arivu's unique advantage: it does not just know that
Paper A cites Paper B -- it knows the mutation type (how the idea changed),
the confidence level, the DNA cluster membership, and the structural importance
(PageRank). A human writing a literature review must manually reconstruct
these relationships. Athena can generate a first draft that captures the
intellectual narrative automatically.

The literature review uses GROQ_SMART_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) for
generation because it requires multi-paper reasoning, thematic synthesis,
and coherent long-form writing. The review is generated section by section,
with each section corresponding to a DNA cluster. Within each section, the
narrative follows the chronological and intellectual flow of ideas.

Structure of the generated review:
  1. Introduction: Overview of the field and the seed paper's significance
  2. Per-cluster sections: One section per DNA cluster, narrating the
     intellectual thread (how ideas mutated from adoption to generalization
     to specialization)
  3. Cross-cluster connections: How different research threads interact
  4. Gaps and future directions: Derived from gap_finder and orphan_detector
  5. References: Auto-generated BibTeX

Each section includes inline citations in the format (Author, Year) that
correspond to the BibTeX entries. The user can switch between academic
citation styles: (Author, Year), [Number], or footnote.

The draft is editable within the artifact card. The user can highlight text
and ask Athena to "expand this section," "add more detail about transformers,"
or "rewrite this paragraph for a CS audience." The artifact supports iterative
refinement through conversation.

The review is exportable as Markdown, PDF, or DOCX.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User requests a literature review. Triggered by: (a) "Write a literature
review for this lineage," (b) /litreview command, (c) Athena suggests after
comprehensive exploration.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "Draft a literature review of the attention mechanism lineage."

2. Athena shows progress: "Generating literature review...
   [====------] Analyzing 4 clusters, 152 papers, 203 edges..."
   Sections generate one at a time with streaming display.

3. The review appears as a scrollable document artifact:
   [Literature Review: The Evolution of Attention Mechanisms]
   [Style: (Author, Year) v] [Copy] [Download PDF] [Download DOCX]

   1. Introduction
   The attention mechanism, first proposed by Bahdanau et al. (2014)...

   2. Foundational Sequence-to-Sequence Models
   ...

4. User highlights a paragraph and asks: "Expand the part about
   multi-head attention."

5. Athena regenerates that section with more detail, streaming the
   update into the document.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Document persists as artifact. Fully editable and exportable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AthenaLitReviewBlock class. Scrollable document
  with editable sections, style switcher, export buttons.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes lit review requests and refinements.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Document styles, section headers, citations.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat generates review; /api/athena/lit-review/export
  handles PDF/DOCX export.
- backend/chat_guide.py: Orchestrates section-by-section LLM generation.
- backend/llm_client.py: Handles GROQ_SMART_MODEL calls for review text.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  class AthenaLitReviewBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.title = data.title;
      this.sections = data.sections;
      this.citationStyle = data.citation_style || 'author-year';
      this.editable = true;
    }

    render() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-lit-review';
      el.innerHTML = this._buildHeader() + this._buildDocument() + this._buildActions();
      return el;
    }

    _buildDocument() {
      return this.sections.map((sec, i) => `
        <div class="lr-section" data-index="${i}">
          <h3>${i + 1}. ${sec.title}</h3>
          <div class="lr-content" contenteditable="true">${sec.content}</div>
        </div>
      `).join('');
    }

    exportMarkdown() {
      let md = `# ${this.title}\n\n`;
      this.sections.forEach((sec, i) => {
        md += `## ${i + 1}. ${sec.title}\n\n${sec.content}\n\n`;
      });
      navigator.clipboard.writeText(md);
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  # In chat_guide.py
  def generate_literature_review(self, graph, seed_paper):
      clusters = graph.dna_clusters or {}
      sections = []
      # Introduction
      intro = self._generate_section('introduction', seed_paper, graph)
      sections.append({'title': 'Introduction', 'content': intro})
      # Per-cluster sections
      for cluster_name, paper_ids in clusters.items():
          papers = [graph.papers[pid] for pid in paper_ids if pid in graph.papers]
          content = self._generate_cluster_section(cluster_name, papers, graph)
          sections.append({'title': cluster_name, 'content': content})
      # Cross-cluster connections
      cross = self._generate_cross_cluster(clusters, graph)
      sections.append({'title': 'Cross-Cluster Connections', 'content': cross})
      # Gaps and future directions
      gaps = self._generate_gaps_section(graph)
      sections.append({'title': 'Gaps and Future Directions', 'content': gaps})
      return {'title': f'Literature Review: {seed_paper.title}', 'sections': sections}

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response (literature review):
{
  "response": "Here is the literature review draft:",
  "blocks": [{
    "type": "lit_review",
    "title": "Literature Review: ...",
    "sections": [
      {"title": "Introduction", "content": "The attention mechanism..."},
      {"title": "Foundational Models", "content": "..."}
    ],
    "citation_style": "author-year"
  }]
}

GET /api/athena/lit-review/export?format=pdf&session_id=...
Response: PDF file download

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Literature Review Document

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [Athena]                                                     |
  |  Here is your literature review draft:                        |
  |                                                               |
  |  +-- Literature Review: Attention Mechanisms ---------------+ |
  |  | [Style: (Author,Year) v] [Copy MD] [PDF] [DOCX]         | |
  |  |                                                           | |
  |  | 1. Introduction                                           | |
  |  | -------------------------------------------------------  | |
  |  | The attention mechanism, first proposed by Bahdanau et    | |
  |  | al. (2014) for neural machine translation, represents     | |
  |  | one of the most transformative ideas in modern deep       | |
  |  | learning. This review traces the intellectual lineage...  | |
  |  |                                                           | |
  |  | 2. Foundational Sequence Models                           | |
  |  | -------------------------------------------------------  | |
  |  | The sequence-to-sequence paradigm established by          | |
  |  | Sutskever et al. (2014) provided the initial framework... | |
  |  |                                                           | |
  |  | (scrollable, max-height: 500px)                           | |
  |  +-----------------------------------------------------------+ |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Section Being Refined

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  | 2. Foundational Sequence Models        [Refining...]      | |
  |  | -------------------------------------------------------  | |
  |  | [streaming text replacing previous content]               | |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

TRANSITIONS:
Initial render: sections stagger in (200ms each, fade + slide from left).
Streaming: text appears character-by-character (typewriter effect).
Section refine: old text fades out (200ms), new text streams in.
Style switch: citation markers swap with cross-fade (200ms).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User requests literature review
  -> athena-engine.js sends to /api/athena/chat
  -> chat_guide.py calls generate_literature_review()
  -> For each section: LLM call with cluster context, paper data, mutation info
  -> Sections streamed to frontend as they complete
  -> Response includes blocks[{type:"lit_review",...}]
  -> athena-blocks.js renders AthenaLitReviewBlock

Refinement flow:
  -> User highlights text, asks "expand this"
  -> athena-engine.js sends refinement request with section index
  -> Backend regenerates that section only
  -> Frontend replaces section content with streaming update

Export flow:
  -> Copy MD: client-side markdown generation
  -> PDF: server-side generation via reportlab
  -> DOCX: server-side generation via python-docx

ERROR PATH:
  -> LLM timeout on large review: generate available sections, note incomplete.
  -> GROQ_SMART_MODEL unavailable: fall back to GROQ_FAST_MODEL with simpler prompts.
  -> PDF export fails: offer Markdown copy as alternative.

DATA STORED: Review JSON in chat_history blocks. Full text in session memory.
DATA CACHED: Section content cached per session for fast re-export.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL

STAGE: Section-by-section generation. Each section requires multi-paper
reasoning and coherent academic writing.

PROMPT (per cluster section):
```
You are Athena, generating a literature review section for Arivu.
Cluster: {cluster_name}
Papers in this cluster (ordered chronologically):
{paper_list_with_metadata}

Mutation types in this cluster:
{mutation_summary}

Key edges (intellectual inheritance paths):
{edge_summary}

Write a literature review section (300-500 words) that:
1. Traces the chronological development of ideas
2. Uses mutation types to explain how ideas evolved (adoption, generalization, etc.)
3. Includes inline citations as (Author, Year)
4. Highlights the most structurally important papers (highest PageRank)
5. Notes any contradictions or abandonments

Write in an academic but accessible tone. No bullet points. Flowing paragraphs.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: Plain text (academic prose)
FALLBACK: Per-paper summaries concatenated with transition sentences
TOKEN BUDGET: ~800 tokens per section, ~4000 total for 5-section review

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action     | Response                                |
|----------------------|------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Section content      | Click      | Place cursor for editing                |
| Section content      | Select+ask | Refine selected text via Athena         |
| Style dropdown       | Change     | Switch citation format in all sections  |
| Copy MD button       | Click      | Copy full review as markdown            |
| PDF button           | Click      | Download as PDF                         |
| DOCX button          | Click      | Download as DOCX                        |
| Section header       | Click      | Collapse/expand section                 |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Section stagger: 200ms gap per section, slide from left
- Streaming text: characters appear at 30 chars/sec
- Style switch: citation cross-fade 200ms
- Section collapse: height transition 250ms ease

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Document has role="article" with aria-label "Literature review"
- Sections: role="region" with aria-label per section title
- Editable content: aria-label "Editable section"
- Action buttons: aria-labels "Copy as Markdown", "Download PDF"
- Screen reader: "Literature review article. 5 sections."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Graph with only 3 papers (too few for meaningful review)
   Expected: "A full literature review requires at least 10 papers.
   Try a reading list instead." Offer reading list artifact.
   Implementation: Minimum paper threshold check before generation.

2. Scenario: Review generation exceeds LLM token limit
   Expected: Generate shorter sections. Note truncation in introduction.
   Implementation: Reduce per-section word count dynamically.

3. Scenario: User edits review text, then asks for refinement
   Expected: Refinement preserves user edits in other sections.
   Implementation: Only regenerate the targeted section.

4. Scenario: DOCX export with special characters and citations
   Expected: Clean DOCX with proper formatting, no garbled characters.
   Implementation: python-docx with UTF-8 encoding, paragraph styles.

5. Scenario: Two DNA clusters with overlapping papers
   Expected: Paper appears in primary cluster only. Cross-reference noted.
   Implementation: Assign each paper to highest-affinity cluster.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #001, #002, #011, #096 (Research Note)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #097 (BibTeX), #098 (Reading List)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: GROQ_SMART_MODEL for generation. reportlab for PDF. python-docx for DOCX.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DONE CRITERIA
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTIONAL:
[ ] Literature review generates with introduction + cluster sections + gaps
[ ] Per-section streaming display during generation
[ ] Inline citations in (Author, Year) format
[ ] Citation style switchable (author-year, numbered, footnote)
[ ] Sections editable (contenteditable)
[ ] Section refinement via Athena conversation
[ ] Copy Markdown, Download PDF, Download DOCX

VISUAL:
[ ] Document card with academic formatting
[ ] Section headers with horizontal rules
[ ] Scrollable with max-height 500px
[ ] Inter font, proper paragraph spacing

PERFORMANCE:
[ ] Full review generation within 15 seconds for 50 papers
[ ] Section streaming begins within 2 seconds
[ ] PDF/DOCX export within 5 seconds

ACCESSIBILITY:
[ ] role="article" with section regions
[ ] Editable content labeled
[ ] Export buttons with aria-labels

EDGE CASES:
[ ] Minimum paper threshold (10)
[ ] Token limit handling
[ ] User edit preservation during refinement
[ ] Special character handling in exports

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec
[ ] EXPORT: PDF and DOCX open correctly
[ ] ACADEMIC: Citations formatted properly

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PESSIMISTIC AGENT PROTOCOL
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. READ athena-blocks.js (AthenaLitReviewBlock), chat_guide.py, llm_client.py
2. COMPARE against all Done Criteria
3. TEST: generate review, edit section, refine via chat, export all formats
4. CHECK: citation style switching works across all sections
5. VERIFY: PDF and DOCX exports contain all sections with proper formatting
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below
