================================================================================
FEATURE #100: PRESENTATION SLIDE ARTIFACT
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Artifact Outputs
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           G
ESTIMATED HOURS: 16
OVERLAPS WITH:   #099 (Lit Review), #011 (Artifact Cards)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #001 (Context-Aware Responses), #002 (Rich Response Formatting),
                 #011 (Artifact Cards)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Presentation Slide Artifact generates structured slide content from graph
data and Athena conversation insights. When a researcher needs to present
their findings -- in a lab meeting, conference talk, or thesis defense -- they
can ask Athena to create a slide deck outline with key talking points, data
visualizations descriptions, and narrative flow derived from the lineage graph.

This feature does not generate actual PowerPoint or Google Slides files (that
would require heavyweight dependencies). Instead, it produces a structured
slide-by-slide outline with: slide title, bullet points, speaker notes, and
suggested visual (described textually: "graph showing pruning impact of
Vaswani removal," "DNA cluster donut chart," "timeline of key papers").

Each slide is rendered as a card in the Athena chat with a title bar, content
area, and speaker notes collapsible section. The user can reorder slides via
drag-and-drop, edit content inline, add or remove slides, and export the deck
as Markdown (for import into Marp, Slidev, or reveal.js) or as a structured
JSON for programmatic use.

Athena generates slides intelligently based on the conversation context. A
presentation about "the evolution of attention mechanisms" would produce:
  Slide 1: Title slide with lineage overview
  Slide 2: Historical context (pre-attention era)
  Slides 3-6: Key innovations per DNA cluster
  Slide 7: Structural analysis (PageRank, pruning impacts)
  Slide 8: Current state and gaps
  Slide 9: Future directions
  Slide 10: References

The slide content uses data from the graph engine: citation counts, mutation
chains, cluster memberships, pruning impacts. Speaker notes include detailed
talking points that the presenter can reference but that would not appear on
the slide itself.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User requests presentation slides. Triggered by: (a) "Create slides for
a presentation on this lineage," (b) /slides command, (c) Athena suggests
after extensive exploration.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "I need to present this attention mechanism research in a
   15-minute lab meeting. Create slides."

2. Athena generates with progress: "Creating presentation... Analyzing
   graph structure for key themes..."

3. Slides appear as a sequence of cards:
   [Slide 1/10: The Attention Revolution]
     - From sequence-to-sequence to transformers
     - 152 papers, 7 years, 4 research clusters
     Speaker notes: "Open by asking the audience..."

   [Slide 2/10: Origins (2014-2015)]
     - Bahdanau (2014): attention for NMT
     - Luong (2015): attention variants
     Suggested visual: timeline with 2 nodes highlighted
     Speaker notes: "These two papers are essential..."

4. User drags Slide 3 before Slide 2 to reorder. Slides renumber.

5. User edits Slide 1 title to "Attention: From NMT to Everything."

6. User clicks "Export Markdown." Downloads presentation.md compatible
   with Marp slide framework.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Slides persist as artifact cards. Editable and exportable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AthenaSlideBlock class. Renders slide cards,
  drag reorder, inline editing, export.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes slide generation requests.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Slide card styles, speaker notes toggle.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat generates slide content.
- backend/chat_guide.py: Slide generation using graph data and LLM.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  class AthenaSlideBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.slides = data.slides;
      this.title = data.title;
      this.totalSlides = data.slides.length;
    }

    render() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-slides-artifact';
      el.innerHTML = `<div class="slides-header">
        <h3>${this.title}</h3>
        <span>${this.totalSlides} slides</span>
        <button class="athena-btn-gold slides-export">Export Markdown</button>
      </div>`;
      const list = document.createElement('div');
      list.className = 'slides-list';
      this.slides.forEach((slide, i) => {
        list.appendChild(this._renderSlide(slide, i));
      });
      el.appendChild(list);
      this._attachDragReorder(list);
      return el;
    }

    _renderSlide(slide, index) {
      const card = document.createElement('div');
      card.className = 'slide-card';
      card.draggable = true;
      card.innerHTML = `
        <div class="slide-number">Slide ${index + 1}/${this.totalSlides}</div>
        <h4 class="slide-title" contenteditable="true">${slide.title}</h4>
        <ul class="slide-bullets">${slide.bullets.map(b =>
          `<li contenteditable="true">${b}</li>`).join('')}</ul>
        ${slide.visual ? `<div class="slide-visual">[Visual: ${slide.visual}]</div>` : ''}
        <details class="slide-notes">
          <summary>Speaker notes</summary>
          <p contenteditable="true">${slide.speaker_notes}</p>
        </details>`;
      return card;
    }

    exportMarkdown() {
      let md = `---\nmarp: true\ntheme: default\n---\n\n`;
      this.slides.forEach(s => {
        md += `# ${s.title}\n\n`;
        s.bullets.forEach(b => { md += `- ${b}\n`; });
        md += `\n<!--\n${s.speaker_notes}\n-->\n\n---\n\n`;
      });
      const blob = new Blob([md], {type: 'text/markdown'});
      const a = document.createElement('a');
      a.href = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
      a.download = 'arivu-presentation.md';
      a.click();
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  # In chat_guide.py
  def generate_slides(self, graph, seed_paper, duration_minutes=15):
      slide_count = max(5, min(duration_minutes, 20))
      clusters = graph.dna_clusters or {}
      pagerank = nx.pagerank(graph.nx_graph, alpha=0.85)
      slides = []
      # Title slide
      slides.append(self._title_slide(seed_paper, graph))
      # Per-cluster slides
      for cluster_name, paper_ids in clusters.items():
          slides.append(self._cluster_slide(cluster_name, paper_ids, graph, pagerank))
      # Analysis slides
      slides.append(self._structural_slide(graph, pagerank))
      slides.append(self._gaps_slide(graph))
      slides.append(self._references_slide(graph))
      return {'title': f'Presentation: {seed_paper.title}', 'slides': slides[:slide_count]}

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response (slides):
{
  "response": "Here are your presentation slides:",
  "blocks": [{
    "type": "slides",
    "title": "Presentation: Attention Mechanisms",
    "slides": [{
      "title": "The Attention Revolution",
      "bullets": ["From seq2seq to transformers", "152 papers, 4 clusters"],
      "visual": "Lineage graph with clusters highlighted",
      "speaker_notes": "Open by asking the audience..."
    }]
  }]
}

VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Slide Deck Rendered

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [Athena]                                                     |
  |  Here are your presentation slides:                           |

  |  | | - From sequence-to-sequence to transformers       |    |  |
  |  | | - 152 papers, 7 years, 4 research clusters        |    |  |
  |  | | [Visual: Lineage overview graph]                   |    |  |
  |  | | > Speaker notes (click to expand)                  |    |  |
  |  | +---------------------------------------------------+    |  |
  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  | +-- Slide 2/10 ------------------------------------+    |  |
  |  | | Origins (2014-2015)                               |    |  |
  |  | | - Bahdanau: attention for NMT                     |    |  |
  |  | | - Luong: simplified attention variants             |    |  |
  |  | | [Visual: Timeline with 2 highlighted nodes]        |    |  |
  |  | | > Speaker notes (click to expand)                  |    |  |
  |  | +---------------------------------------------------+    |  |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------------+  |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Slide Being Dragged for Reorder

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

  |  | | (semi-transparent, elevated)    |                        |  |
  |  | +---------------------------------+                        |  |
  |  | [drop indicator line between slides]                       |  |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

TRANSITIONS:
Slide render: cards stagger in (100ms per slide, fade + slide up).
Drag: card becomes semi-transparent (opacity 0.6), elevated (box-shadow).
Drop: cards rearrange with translateY animation (200ms).
Notes toggle: height transition 200ms ease.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User requests slides
  -> athena-engine.js sends to /api/athena/chat
  -> chat_guide.py calls generate_slides()
  -> LLM generates slide content per section using graph data
  -> Response includes blocks[{type:"slides",...}]
  -> athena-blocks.js renders AthenaSlideBlock

Reorder:
  -> User drags slide card -> client-side array reorder -> renumber

Edit:
  -> User edits contenteditable fields -> local state update

Export:
  -> Export Markdown: client-side Marp-compatible markdown generation
  -> Download as .md file

ERROR PATH:
  -> Graph too small (<5 papers): "Need at least 5 papers for slides."
  -> LLM timeout: generate available slides, mark remaining as "TODO."
  -> Export fails: copy to clipboard as fallback.

DATA STORED: Slide JSON in chat_history blocks.
DATA CACHED: Slide state in AthenaSlideBlock instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_SMART_MODEL

STAGE: Slide content generation. Each slide requires concise, presentation-
ready language with clear talking points.

PROMPT (per slide):
```
You are Athena, creating presentation slides for a research talk.
Slide topic: {topic}
Papers: {paper_list}
Duration target: {minutes} minutes total, this is slide {n}/{total}

Generate:
{
  "title": "slide title (max 8 words)",
  "bullets": ["point 1", "point 2", "point 3"] (3-5 bullets, max 15 words each),
  "visual": "description of suggested visual",
  "speaker_notes": "2-3 sentences of talking points for the presenter"
}

Keep bullets concise. No full sentences in bullets. Save detail for speaker notes.
Return ONLY valid JSON.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON per slide
FALLBACK: Title + "Key points" bullets from paper titles
TOKEN BUDGET: ~200 tokens per slide

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ANIMATION DETAILS:

- Drag: opacity 0.6, box-shadow elevation

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.

ACCESSIBILITY:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User requests 30+ slides for a 1-hour talk
   Expected: Generate up to 20 slides max. Suggest splitting into sections.
   Implementation: Cap at 20 slides, note suggestion in response.

2. Scenario: Graph has 1 cluster (homogeneous field)
   Expected: Split by time period instead of cluster. 2014-2016, 2016-2018, etc.
   Implementation: Fallback to chronological sectioning.

3. Scenario: User reorders all slides then exports
   Expected: Export reflects current order, not original.
   Implementation: Export reads from current DOM order.

4. Scenario: Speaker notes contain special Marp syntax characters
   Expected: Escaped in Markdown export to prevent rendering issues.
   Implementation: Sanitize speaker notes in HTML comment block.

5. Scenario: Mobile view (narrow chat panel)
   Expected: Slide cards stack vertically, drag still works.
   Implementation: Touch-friendly drag with larger handles.

================================================================================
FEATURE #101: PYTHON CODE BLOCK
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 8
OVERLAPS WITH:   #102 (SQL Query Block), #103 (JSON Data Block)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Python Code Block is a specialized response block that Athena uses to
display syntax-highlighted Python code within the chat panel. When Athena
generates code snippets -- for data analysis scripts, graph querying examples,
API usage demonstrations, or NLP pipeline explanations -- the code is rendered
in a dedicated block with proper Python syntax highlighting, line numbers,
and a one-click copy button.

This is needed because Arivu's target audience includes computational
researchers who frequently write Python code for data analysis. When a user
asks "How would I query this graph programmatically?" or "Show me a Python
script to analyze the mutation types," Athena should return properly formatted
code, not inline monospace text in a paragraph.

The code block uses JetBrains Mono font (already in the Arivu design system)
with a syntax highlighting scheme that matches the Arivu dark theme:
  - Keywords (def, class, import, for, if): --accent-blue (#3B82F6)
  - Strings: --accent-teal (#06B6D4)
  - Numbers: --accent-gold (#D4A843)
  - Comments: --text-muted (#64748B)
  - Functions/methods: --success (#22C55E)
  - Variables: --text-primary (#E2E8F0)
  - Operators: --text-secondary (#94A3B8)

The block has a header bar showing the language name ("Python"), an optional
filename label, and a "Copy" button. Line numbers appear in a left gutter in
--text-muted. The background is --bg-elevated to distinguish it from the
surrounding message.

Syntax highlighting is performed client-side using a lightweight tokenizer
(not a full parser). The tokenizer recognizes Python keywords, string literals,
numeric literals, comments, decorators, f-strings, and common built-in
function names. It does NOT execute the code or validate its correctness.

The code block is scrollable horizontally for long lines. A maximum height
of 400px is enforced with a "Show all" toggle for longer snippets. The
block supports text selection for partial copying.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
Athena generates Python code in its response. Detected by: (a) LLM returns
a response containing a code fence with python tag, (b) user asks for code
examples, (c) /code command, (d) Athena explains a pipeline step with code.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "Show me how to query the graph for all generalization edges."

2. Athena responds with explanatory text and a Python code block:
   "Here is a Python snippet that filters the graph for generalization
   mutation edges:"

   [Python]                                          [Copy]
    1 | import networkx as nx
    2 |
    3 | def find_generalizations(graph_json):
    4 |     """Find all edges with mutation_type == 'generalization'."""
    5 |     edges = graph_json['edges']
    6 |     return [
    7 |         e for e in edges
    8 |         if e.get('mutation_type') == 'generalization'
    9 |     ]

3. User clicks "Copy." Button text changes to "Copied!" (2 seconds).
   Code text copied to clipboard without line numbers.

4. Code block remains in chat for reference.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Block persists in conversation. Fully selectable text. Copy button always
available.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AthenaPythonBlock class. Renders syntax-
  highlighted code with line numbers and copy button.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Detects code fences in LLM responses.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Code block styles, syntax colors, line numbers.
- No backend changes required (code is text in response).

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  class AthenaPythonBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.code = data.code;
      this.filename = data.filename || '';
      this.language = data.language || 'python';
    }

    render() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-code-block';
      el.innerHTML = `
        <div class="code-header">
          <span class="code-lang">${this.language}</span>
          ${this.filename ? `<span class="code-file">${this.filename}</span>` : ''}
          <button class="code-copy">Copy</button>
        </div>
        <pre class="code-body"><code>${this._highlight()}</code></pre>`;
      el.querySelector('.code-copy').addEventListener('click', () => this.copy(el));
      return el;
    }

    _highlight() {
      const lines = this.code.split('\n');
      return lines.map((line, i) => {
        const num = `<span class="line-num">${i + 1}</span>`;
        const highlighted = this._highlightLine(line);
        return `<span class="code-line">${num}${highlighted}</span>`;
      }).join('\n');
    }

    _highlightLine(line) {
      let h = line;
      // Comments
      h = h.replace(/(#.*)$/, '<span class="syn-comment">$1</span>');
      // Strings (single and double, including f-strings)
      h = h.replace(/(f?["]{3}[\s\S]*?["]{3}|f?[']{3}[\s\S]*?[']{3}|f?"[^"]*"|f?'[^']*')/g,
        '<span class="syn-string">$1</span>');
      // Keywords
      const kw = /\b(def|class|import|from|return|if|elif|else|for|while|with|as|try|except|finally|raise|yield|lambda|and|or|not|in|is|None|True|False|self)\b/g;
      h = h.replace(kw, '<span class="syn-keyword">$1</span>');
      // Numbers
      h = h.replace(/\b(\d+\.?\d*)\b/g, '<span class="syn-number">$1</span>');
      // Decorators
      h = h.replace(/(@\w+)/g, '<span class="syn-decorator">$1</span>');
      return h;
    }

    copy(el) {
      navigator.clipboard.writeText(this.code);
      const btn = el.querySelector('.code-copy');
      btn.textContent = 'Copied!';
      setTimeout(() => { btn.textContent = 'Copy'; }, 2000);
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No backend changes. Code blocks are detected from LLM response format:
```python
code here
```

The athena-engine.js parser extracts code fences and routes them to
AthenaPythonBlock for rendering.

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response includes code in blocks array:
{
  "response": "Here is the Python code:",
  "blocks": [{
    "type": "code",
    "language": "python",
    "code": "import networkx as nx\n...",
    "filename": "query_graph.py"
  }]
}

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Python Code Block Rendered

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [Athena]                                                     |
  |  Here is a Python snippet:                                    |
  |                                                               |
  |  +-- Python -- query_graph.py ----------------- [Copy] ---+  |
  |  |  1 | import networkx as nx                              |  |
  |  |  2 |                                                    |  |
  |  |  3 | def find_generalizations(graph_json):              |  |
  |  |  4 |     """Find generalization edges."""               |  |
  |  |  5 |     edges = graph_json['edges']                    |  |
  |  |  6 |     return [                                       |  |
  |  |  7 |         e for e in edges                           |  |
  |  |  8 |         if e.get('mutation_type') == 'general...'  |  |
  |  |  9 |     ]                                              |  |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |                                                               |
  |  Color key:                                                   |
  |  import, def, return = blue (--accent-blue)                   |
  |  'edges', '...' = teal (--accent-teal)                        |
  |  line numbers = muted (--text-muted)                           |
  |  comments = muted (--text-muted)                               |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Copy Confirmed

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  +-- Python -------------------------------- [Copied!] ---+  |
  |  Button text swaps for 2 seconds, then reverts to "Copy"     |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

TRANSITIONS:
Block render: fade in (200ms ease).
Copy: button text swap (150ms), revert after 2s.
Horizontal scroll: smooth scroll for long lines.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Athena generates code in LLM response
  -> LLM returns response with code fences
  -> athena-engine.js detects ```python fences
  -> Extracts code content and creates code block data
  -> athena-blocks.js renders AthenaPythonBlock
  -> Client-side syntax highlighting via regex tokenizer
  -> User clicks Copy -> clipboard write (code only, no line numbers)

ERROR PATH:
  -> Code contains HTML special chars: escape before highlighting.
  -> Very long code (500+ lines): truncate at 50 lines, "Show all" button.
  -> Clipboard API blocked: fallback to text selection.

DATA STORED: Code block in chat_history blocks.
DATA CACHED: Rendered DOM element.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

STAGE: Code generation. When the user asks for code, Athena generates Python
snippets using the Arivu graph data context.

PROMPT:
```
You are Athena, the research intelligence assistant for Arivu.
The user wants Python code for: {user_request}
Graph context: {graph_summary}

Available data structures:
- graph_json: {"nodes": [...], "edges": [...], "metadata": {...}}
- Each node: {"paper_id", "title", "year", "citation_count", ...}
- Each edge: {"source", "target", "mutation_type", "similarity_score", ...}

Write clean, well-commented Python code. Use standard libraries (networkx,
json, collections). Include docstrings. Return code inside ```python fences.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown with ```python code fence
FALLBACK: No code block; explain in prose
TOKEN BUDGET: ~400 tokens per code generation

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element           | Action     | Response                                   |
|-------------------|------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Copy button       | Click      | Copy code to clipboard (no line numbers)   |
| Code text         | Select     | Standard text selection                    |
| Code block        | Scroll-h   | Horizontal scroll for long lines           |
| Show all toggle   | Click      | Expand to show full code (remove max-height)|
| Line number       | --         | Display only (not selectable in copy)      |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Block fade: opacity 0 to 1, 200ms ease
- Copy text swap: 150ms, revert after 2s
- Show all: max-height transition from 400px to none, 300ms

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Code block has role="region" with aria-label "Python code"
- Copy button: aria-label "Copy code to clipboard"
- Code content: tabindex="0" for keyboard focus
- Screen reader: "Python code block. 9 lines. Copy button."
- Keyboard: Tab to copy button, Enter to activate

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Code contains HTML characters (<, >, &)
   Expected: Characters escaped to prevent XSS. &lt; &gt; &amp;
   Implementation: HTML-escape code before syntax highlighting.

2. Scenario: Code block with 500 lines
   Expected: Truncated to 50 lines with "Show all (500 lines)" button.
   Implementation: max-height with overflow hidden, toggle button.

3. Scenario: Code in language other than Python (user asks for R or Julia)
   Expected: Generic code block with language label, no Python highlighting.
   Implementation: Language-specific highlighting only for Python; others
   get monospace without syntax colors.

4. Scenario: Code block in mobile viewport
   Expected: Full-width, horizontal scroll for long lines.
   Implementation: overflow-x: auto, -webkit-overflow-scrolling: touch.

5. Scenario: Multiple code blocks in one message
   Expected: Each rendered independently with own copy button.
   Implementation: Each code fence creates separate AthenaPythonBlock.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DEPENDS ON: #002 (Rich Response Formatting)
ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #005 (Graph-Aware Commands), #103 (JSON Data Block)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: No external syntax highlighting library. Client-side regex tokenizer.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[ ] Line numbers displayed in left gutter
[ ] Copy button copies code without line numbers
[ ] Long code truncated with "Show all" toggle
[ ] Multiple code blocks per message supported
[ ] Language label in header bar

VISUAL:

[ ] --bg-elevated background
[ ] Syntax colors: blue keywords, teal strings, gold numbers, muted comments
[ ] Line numbers in --text-muted

PERFORMANCE:
[ ] Syntax highlighting within 50ms for 100 lines
[ ] Clipboard write within 50ms
[ ] No layout shift on render

ACCESSIBILITY:
[ ] role="region" with aria-label
[ ] Copy button accessible via keyboard
[ ] Screen reader announces line count

EDGE CASES:
[ ] HTML character escaping (XSS prevention)
[ ] 500-line truncation with expand
[ ] Mobile horizontal scroll
[ ] Non-Python language fallback

VERIFICATION:
[ ] PESSIMISTIC AGENT: All rounds completed
[ ] BROWSER TEST: Verified
[ ] CROSS-REFERENCE: Matches spec
[ ] COPY: Pasted code compiles (no line numbers or HTML)
[ ] XSS: HTML entities properly escaped

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

2. COMPARE against all Done Criteria
3. TEST: render code, copy, paste into editor, verify no line numbers in paste
4. CHECK: HTML characters properly escaped (XSS prevention)
5. VERIFY: long code truncation, mobile horizontal scroll
6. Agent assumes implementation is BROKEN
7. Must find AT LEAST 1 issue per round
8. Records ALL findings below

================================================================================
FEATURE #102: SQL QUERY BLOCK
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 8
OVERLAPS WITH:   #101 (Python Code Block), #103 (JSON Data Block)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #101 (Python Code Block)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The SQL Query Block renders formatted PostgreSQL queries within Athena's
chat responses. When a user asks about the database schema, requests
debugging help for their own queries against the Arivu API, or when Athena
explains internal data operations, SQL is displayed in a dedicated block
with PostgreSQL-specific syntax highlighting and an "Explain" button that
breaks down what the query does in plain language.

This matters because Arivu is built on PostgreSQL with complex schema
(17+ tables, pgvector extensions, JSONB columns). Power users and developers
using the public API (Phase 7) frequently need to understand the data model.
When a user asks "How does the edge analysis table work?" Athena can show
the relevant SQL alongside its explanation.

The SQL block uses the same code block infrastructure as the Python block
(Feature #101) with SQL-specific syntax highlighting:
  - Keywords (SELECT, FROM, WHERE, JOIN, INSERT): --accent-blue
  - Table/column names: --text-primary
  - Strings: --accent-teal
  - Numbers: --accent-gold
  - Functions (COUNT, AVG, COALESCE): --success
  - Comments (-- and /* */): --text-muted
  - Operators (=, !=, >, <, AND, OR): --text-secondary

The unique feature of the SQL block is the "Explain" button. Clicking it
triggers an LLM call that generates a plain-language breakdown of the query,
explaining each clause, its purpose, and what data it operates on. This is
rendered as an expandable section below the query.

The SQL block also has a "Copy" button that copies the raw SQL without line
numbers or highlighting markup. A "Run" button is NOT included -- Athena
never executes arbitrary SQL against the production database. The block is
display-only.

The block supports multi-statement SQL (separated by semicolons) and
highlights PostgreSQL-specific syntax (pgvector operators like <->, JSONB
operators like ->, ->>, @>, and array syntax).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
Athena generates SQL in response. Triggered by: (a) user asks about database
schema, (b) user asks "show me the query for edge analysis," (c) Athena
explains an internal operation, (d) /sql command for schema exploration.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "How does Arivu query papers by embedding similarity?"

2. Athena responds with explanation and SQL:
   "Arivu uses pgvector's cosine distance operator to find similar papers.
   Here is the query pattern:"

   [SQL]                                           [Copy] [Explain]
    1 | SELECT p.title, p.year,
    2 |        1 - (pe.embedding <=> target_emb) AS similarity
    3 | FROM papers p
    4 | JOIN paper_embeddings pe ON p.paper_id = pe.paper_id
    5 | WHERE 1 - (pe.embedding <=> target_emb) > 0.25
    6 | ORDER BY similarity DESC
    7 | LIMIT 50;

3. User clicks "Explain." An explanation section expands:
   "This query: (1) Joins the papers table with embeddings, (2) computes
   cosine similarity using pgvector's <=> operator, (3) filters papers
   above the NLP_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD (0.25), and (4) returns the top 50
   most similar papers."

4. User clicks "Copy." SQL copied to clipboard.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Block persists in chat. Explain section stays expanded if toggled.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AthenaSQLBlock class (extends code block pattern).
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Detects SQL fences in LLM responses.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: SQL-specific syntax highlighting colors.
- app.py: /api/athena/explain-sql endpoint for query explanation.
- backend/chat_guide.py: SQL explanation generation.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  class AthenaSQLBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.code = data.code;
      this.filename = data.filename || '';
      this.explanation = null;
    }

    render() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-code-block athena-sql-block';
      el.innerHTML = `
        <div class="code-header">
          <span class="code-lang">SQL</span>
          <button class="code-copy">Copy</button>
          <button class="code-explain">Explain</button>
        </div>
        <pre class="code-body"><code>${this._highlight()}</code></pre>
        <div class="sql-explanation" hidden></div>`;
      el.querySelector('.code-copy').addEventListener('click', () => this.copy(el));
      el.querySelector('.code-explain').addEventListener('click', () => this.explain(el));
      return el;
    }

    _highlightLine(line) {
      let h = this._escapeHtml(line);
      h = h.replace(/(--.*$)/gm, '<span class="syn-comment">$1</span>');
      const kw = /\b(SELECT|FROM|WHERE|JOIN|LEFT|RIGHT|INNER|OUTER|ON|AND|OR|NOT|INSERT|INTO|UPDATE|DELETE|SET|VALUES|CREATE|TABLE|ALTER|ADD|DROP|INDEX|ORDER|BY|GROUP|HAVING|LIMIT|OFFSET|AS|IN|EXISTS|BETWEEN|LIKE|ILIKE|IS|NULL|DISTINCT|UNION|CASE|WHEN|THEN|ELSE|END|WITH|RETURNING)\b/gi;
      h = h.replace(kw, '<span class="syn-keyword">$1</span>');
      const funcs = /\b(COUNT|SUM|AVG|MAX|MIN|COALESCE|ARRAY_AGG|ROW_NUMBER|RANK|NOW|EXTRACT|CAST)\b/gi;
      h = h.replace(funcs, '<span class="syn-function">$1</span>');
      h = h.replace(/'([^']*)'/g, "'<span class=\"syn-string\">$1</span>'");
      h = h.replace(/\b(\d+\.?\d*)\b/g, '<span class="syn-number">$1</span>');
      return h;
    }

    async explain(el) {
      const resp = await fetch('/api/athena/explain-sql', {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: {'Content-Type': 'application/json'},
        body: JSON.stringify({sql: this.code})
      });
      const data = await resp.json();
      const div = el.querySelector('.sql-explanation');
      div.textContent = data.explanation;
      div.hidden = false;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  @app.route('/api/athena/explain-sql', methods=['POST'])
  def explain_sql():
      sql = request.json.get('sql', '')
      explanation = chat_guide.explain_query(sql)
      return jsonify({'explanation': explanation})

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/explain-sql
Request: { "sql": "SELECT ... FROM ..." }
Response: { "explanation": "This query does..." }

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: SQL Block Rendered

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [Athena]                                                     |
  |  Here is the similarity query:                                |
  |                                                               |
  |  +-- SQL -------------------------------- [Copy] [Explain]-+  |
  |  |  1 | SELECT p.title, p.year,                            |  |
  |  |  2 |        1 - (pe.embedding <=> target) AS sim        |  |
  |  |  3 | FROM papers p                                      |  |
  |  |  4 | JOIN paper_embeddings pe                            |  |
  |  |  5 |   ON p.paper_id = pe.paper_id                      |  |
  |  |  6 | WHERE 1 - (pe.embedding <=> target) > 0.25         |  |
  |  |  7 | ORDER BY sim DESC                                  |  |
  |  |  8 | LIMIT 50;                                          |  |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------------+  |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Explanation Expanded

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  +-- SQL -------------------------------- [Copy] [Explain]-+  |
  |  | (code as above)                                          |  |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  | Explanation:                                              |  |
  |  | This query joins papers with their vector embeddings,     |  |
  |  | computes cosine similarity using pgvector's <=> operator, |  |
  |  | filters for similarity above 0.25 (the configured         |  |
  |  | threshold), and returns the top 50 matches sorted by      |  |
  |  | relevance.                                                |  |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------------+  |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

TRANSITIONS:
Explain expand: height 0 to auto, 250ms ease, text fades in 200ms.
Copy: button text swap "Copy" -> "Copied!" 2s.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Athena generates SQL in response
  -> athena-engine.js detects ```sql code fence
  -> athena-blocks.js renders AthenaSQLBlock
  -> User clicks Copy -> clipboard write (raw SQL)
  -> User clicks Explain -> POST /api/athena/explain-sql
  -> Backend sends SQL to LLM for plain-language explanation
  -> Explanation rendered in expandable section

ERROR PATH:
  -> Explain API fails: "Could not generate explanation. Try again."
  -> SQL contains XSS: HTML-escaped before rendering.
  -> Very long query (100+ lines): truncate with "Show all."

DATA STORED: SQL block in chat_history blocks.
DATA CACHED: Explanation cached in DOM after first fetch.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

STAGE: Query explanation when user clicks "Explain" button.

PROMPT:
```
You are Athena, explaining a PostgreSQL query to a researcher.
The query operates on the Arivu database which has tables:
papers, paper_embeddings, edge_analysis, graphs, sessions, etc.

Query:
{sql}

Explain what this query does in 2-4 sentences. Be specific about:
1. Which tables and columns are involved
2. What the JOIN conditions mean
3. What the WHERE clause filters for
4. What the result set contains

Use plain language. Assume the reader knows basic SQL but not
the Arivu schema.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: Plain text explanation
FALLBACK: "This query accesses the Arivu database. Click Copy to use it."
TOKEN BUDGET: ~200 tokens per explanation

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element           | Action     | Response                                   |
|-------------------|------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Copy button       | Click      | Copy raw SQL to clipboard                  |
| Explain button    | Click      | Fetch and display explanation               |
| Code text         | Select     | Standard text selection                    |
| Explanation       | --         | Read-only text display                     |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Block fade: 200ms ease on render
- Explain expand: max-height 0 to auto, 250ms ease
- Copy text swap: 150ms, revert 2s

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Code block: role="region" aria-label "SQL query"
- Explain button: aria-label "Explain this SQL query"
- Explanation: role="note" aria-live="polite"
- Keyboard: Tab to buttons, Enter to activate
- Screen reader: "SQL query block. 8 lines. Copy button. Explain button."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: SQL with pgvector operators (<=>)
   Expected: Operators displayed correctly (not confused with HTML tags).
   Implementation: HTML-escape before highlighting, special handling for <=>.

2. Scenario: Multi-statement SQL separated by semicolons
   Expected: Each statement highlighted independently. Copy includes all.
   Implementation: Highlight entire block; semicolons are just syntax.

3. Scenario: User clicks Explain twice
   Expected: Second click does not re-fetch (cached explanation).
   Implementation: Cache explanation after first fetch.

4. Scenario: SQL contains CREATE TABLE with extensive column definitions
   Expected: Long block truncated at 30 lines, "Show all" toggle.
   Implementation: Same max-height mechanism as Python block.

5. Scenario: JSONB operators (->>, @>) in SQL
   Expected: Operators render correctly, not parsed as HTML.
   Implementation: HTML-escape first, then apply syntax highlighting.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #005 (Graph-Aware Commands), #104 (API Endpoint Block)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: /api/athena/explain-sql endpoint for LLM explanation.

================================================================================
FEATURE #103: JSON DATA BLOCK
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P2
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 8
OVERLAPS WITH:   #101 (Python Code Block), #104 (API Endpoint Block)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #101 (Python Code Block)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The JSON Data Block renders formatted, collapsible JSON structures within
Athena's chat responses. When Athena returns graph data, API response
examples, configuration details, or structured analysis results, the JSON
is displayed in a dedicated block with syntax highlighting, tree-view
collapse/expand, and a one-click copy button.

This feature is critical because Arivu's entire data pipeline speaks JSON.
The graph engine exports JSON (nodes, edges, metadata). The API returns JSON.
The NLP worker communicates in JSON. When a user asks "Show me the graph
metadata" or "What does the edge analysis look like for Vaswani -> BERT?"
Athena should return the actual JSON data structure, not a prose description.

The JSON block differs from the Python code block in two important ways:
First, it supports interactive tree-view navigation. Objects and arrays can
be collapsed/expanded by clicking on their opening brace/bracket. This is
essential for large JSON structures (a graph export can be 50KB+) where
the user only wants to inspect specific parts.

Second, it provides value-type highlighting rather than language-keyword
highlighting:
  - Keys: --accent-gold (#D4A843) in JetBrains Mono
  - Strings: --accent-teal (#06B6D4)
  - Numbers: --success (#22C55E)
  - Booleans: --accent-blue (#3B82F6)
  - Null: --text-muted (#64748B)
  - Braces/brackets: --text-secondary (#94A3B8)

The block header shows "JSON" label, the top-level data type (object/array),
and approximate size. Action buttons: "Copy," "Copy Path" (copies the
JSONPath of the currently focused key), and "Collapse All" / "Expand All."

Each key-value pair is a clickable row. Clicking a key copies its JSONPath.
Hovering shows a tooltip with the value type and length (for strings/arrays).
Nested objects show an expand/collapse triangle indicator.

For very large JSON (>100 keys at any level), the block paginates: "Showing
20 of 152 keys. [Show more]." This prevents the chat from being overwhelmed
by a full graph export.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
Athena returns JSON data. Triggered by: (a) user asks to see graph data,
(b) user asks about API response format, (c) Athena shows analysis results
in structured format, (d) /json command for raw data inspection.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "Show me the metadata for this graph."

2. Athena responds with a JSON block:
   "Here is the graph metadata:"
   [JSON] Object (6 keys)                   [Copy] [Collapse All]
   {
     "graph_id": "a1b2c3d4e5f6...",
     "seed_paper_id": "204e307387...",
     "seed_paper_title": "Attention Is All You Need",
     "total_nodes": 152,
     "total_edges": 203,
     "model_version": "1.0.0"
   }

3. User sees gold keys, teal strings, green numbers. Clicks "total_nodes"
   key -- JSONPath "$.total_nodes" copied to clipboard.

4. User asks to see the full graph. Athena shows a large JSON with
   collapsible nodes and edges arrays.

5. User clicks the triangle next to "nodes" to expand. First 20 nodes
   shown. "Show more (132 remaining)."

AFTER COMPLETION:
Block persists in chat. Collapsed state maintained when scrolling.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AthenaJSONBlock class. Renders tree view,
  collapse/expand, pagination, copy.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Detects JSON fences or structured JSON data.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: JSON tree styles, type colors, expand arrows.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  class AthenaJSONBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.json = typeof data.data === 'string' ? JSON.parse(data.data) : data.data;
      this.collapsed = new Set();
      this.pageSize = 20;
    }

    render() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-json-block';
      const type = Array.isArray(this.json) ? 'Array' : 'Object';
      const size = Array.isArray(this.json) ? this.json.length : Object.keys(this.json).length;
      el.innerHTML = `
        <div class="code-header">
          <span class="code-lang">JSON</span>
          <span class="json-type">${type} (${size} ${size === 1 ? 'item' : 'items'})</span>
          <button class="code-copy">Copy</button>
          <button class="json-collapse-all">Collapse All</button>
        </div>
        <div class="json-tree">${this._renderValue(this.json, '$', 0)}</div>`;
      this._attachHandlers(el);
      return el;
    }

    _renderValue(value, path, depth) {
      if (value === null) return '<span class="json-null">null</span>';
      if (typeof value === 'boolean') return `<span class="json-bool">${value}</span>`;
      if (typeof value === 'number') return `<span class="json-num">${value}</span>`;
      if (typeof value === 'string') return `<span class="json-str">"${this._escapeHtml(value)}"</span>`;
      if (Array.isArray(value)) return this._renderArray(value, path, depth);
      if (typeof value === 'object') return this._renderObject(value, path, depth);
      return String(value);
    }

    _renderObject(obj, path, depth) {
      const keys = Object.keys(obj);
      const collapsed = this.collapsed.has(path);
      let html = `<span class="json-toggle" data-path="${path}">${collapsed ? '+' : '-'}</span>{`;
      if (collapsed) {
        html += `<span class="json-preview">...${keys.length} keys</span>}`;
      } else {
        const show = keys.slice(0, this.pageSize);
        show.forEach(k => {
          const p = `${path}.${k}`;
          html += `<div class="json-row" style="padding-left:${(depth+1)*16}px">
            <span class="json-key" data-path="${p}">"${k}"</span>: ${this._renderValue(obj[k], p, depth+1)}</div>`;
        });
        if (keys.length > this.pageSize) {
          html += `<div class="json-more">Show more (${keys.length - this.pageSize} remaining)</div>`;
        }
        html += '}';
      }
      return html;
    }

    copy(el) {
      navigator.clipboard.writeText(JSON.stringify(this.json, null, 2));
      const btn = el.querySelector('.code-copy');
      btn.textContent = 'Copied!';
      setTimeout(() => { btn.textContent = 'Copy'; }, 2000);
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:
No dedicated backend endpoint. JSON blocks are created from structured
data already in the response.

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response includes JSON block:
{
  "response": "Here is the graph metadata:",
  "blocks": [{
    "type": "json",
    "data": { "graph_id": "...", "total_nodes": 152, ... }
  }]
}

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: JSON Block Rendered (all expanded)

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [Athena]                                                     |
  |  Here is the graph metadata:                                  |
  |                                                               |
  |  +-- JSON -- Object (6 items) ---------- [Copy] [Collapse]-+  |
  |  | - {                                                      |  |
  |  |     "graph_id": "a1b2c3d4...",                           |  |
  |  |     "seed_paper_id": "204e3073...",                      |  |
  |  |     "seed_paper_title": "Attention Is All...",           |  |
  |  |     "total_nodes": 152,                                  |  |
  |  |     "total_edges": 203,                                  |  |
  |  |     "model_version": "1.0.0"                             |  |
  |  |   }                                                      |  |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  Keys = gold, strings = teal, numbers = green                 |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Nested Object with Collapsed Array

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  | - {                                                      |  |
  |  |     "metadata": { ... 6 keys },                          |  |
  |  |   + "nodes": [ ... 152 items ],                          |  |
  |  |   + "edges": [ ... 203 items ],                          |  |
  |  |   }                                                      |  |
  |  + = collapsed (click to expand), - = expanded               |  |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Array Expanded with Pagination

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  |   - "nodes": [                                           |  |
  |  |       { "paper_id": "204e...", "title": "Att..." },      |  |
  |  |       { "paper_id": "df2b...", "title": "BER..." },      |  |
  |  |       ... (20 shown)                                     |  |
  |  |       [Show more (132 remaining)]                         |  |
  |  |     ]                                                    |  |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

TRANSITIONS:
Expand/collapse: height transition 200ms ease.
Pagination "Show more": new items fade in (150ms).
Key click (copy path): brief gold flash on key (200ms).
Copy button: text swap 150ms, revert 2s.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Athena includes structured JSON in response
  -> athena-engine.js detects JSON block or ```json fence
  -> athena-blocks.js renders AthenaJSONBlock with tree view
  -> User clicks expand/collapse arrows -> client-side toggle
  -> User clicks key -> JSONPath copied to clipboard
  -> User clicks Copy -> full JSON (pretty-printed) to clipboard
  -> User clicks Collapse All -> all nested objects/arrays collapsed

ERROR PATH:
  -> Invalid JSON string: show raw text as code block instead.
  -> JSON too deep (>10 levels): auto-collapse beyond depth 5.
  -> Circular reference: not possible in valid JSON, but guard against.

DATA STORED: JSON data in chat_history blocks.
DATA CACHED: Parsed JSON and collapse state in AthenaJSONBlock instance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: NOT DIRECTLY USED

The JSON block does not require LLM calls. JSON data comes from:
- Graph engine exports (graph_json)
- API response examples (static documentation)
- Structured analysis results (from other Athena features)

The LLM may generate the surrounding explanatory text, but the JSON
itself is always real data from the system, never LLM-generated JSON.

FALLBACK: N/A
TOKEN BUDGET: 0 (no LLM cost for JSON rendering)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action | Response                                   |
|----------------------|--------|--------------------------------------------|
| Expand arrow (+/-)   | Click  | Toggle object/array expand/collapse        |
| JSON key             | Click  | Copy JSONPath to clipboard                 |
| JSON key             | Hover  | Tooltip showing value type and length      |
| Copy button          | Click  | Copy pretty-printed JSON to clipboard      |
| Collapse All         | Click  | Collapse all nested objects/arrays          |
| Show more            | Click  | Load next page of items (20 more)          |
| Code block           | Select | Standard text selection                    |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Expand/collapse: height transition 200ms ease
- Key click: background flash gold (200ms)
- Pagination: items fade in 150ms
- Copy: text swap 150ms, revert 2s
- Collapse All: batch collapse (no animation, instant)

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Tree view: role="tree" with role="treeitem" per key
- Expand/collapse: aria-expanded="true"/"false"
- Key: aria-label "Key: graph_id, string value"
- Copy: aria-label "Copy JSON to clipboard"
- Keyboard: Enter to expand/collapse, Tab between items
- Screen reader: "JSON tree. Object with 6 items. graph_id: string."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: Very large JSON (graph export with 500 nodes)
   Expected: Top-level arrays paginated at 20 items. Deep nesting
   auto-collapsed beyond depth 3.
   Implementation: Default collapse depth + pagination.

2. Scenario: JSON with very long string values (2000+ char abstracts)
   Expected: String truncated to 100 chars with "..." and "Show full."
   Implementation: Truncate in render, expand on click.

3. Scenario: JSON with nested arrays of arrays (unusual structure)
   Expected: Each level collapsible independently.
   Implementation: Recursive rendering handles any depth.

4. Scenario: Empty JSON object or array
   Expected: Show "{}" or "[]" with label "Object (0 items)."
   Implementation: Handle empty case in render.

5. Scenario: JSON contains special characters in string values
   Expected: Characters HTML-escaped in display but preserved in copy.
   Implementation: Escape for display, use original JSON for copy.

DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ENABLES: NONE
ENHANCES: #104 (API Endpoint Block), #005 (Graph-Aware Commands)
CONFLICTS: NONE
EXTERNAL: No external libraries. Pure JS tree renderer.

================================================================================
FEATURE #104: API ENDPOINT BLOCK
================================================================================

CATEGORY:        Response Blocks
PRIORITY:        P3
PHASE:           B
ESTIMATED HOURS: 10
OVERLAPS WITH:   #102 (SQL Query Block), #103 (JSON Data Block)
BLOCKS:          NONE
BLOCKED BY:      #002 (Rich Response Formatting), #101 (Python Code Block)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The API Endpoint Block renders HTTP endpoint documentation inline within
Athena's chat responses. When a user asks about Arivu's API, requests
documentation for a specific endpoint, or when Athena explains how to
interact with the platform programmatically, the endpoint is displayed in
a structured documentation card rather than plain text.

This feature becomes critical in Phase 7 when the public REST API launches.
Developers building integrations need clear, structured endpoint documentation.
Rather than directing them to a separate docs page, Athena can serve endpoint
documentation directly in the conversation, contextualized to the developer's
specific question.

Each endpoint block contains:
  - HTTP method badge (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) with distinct colors
  - Endpoint URL path with parameter highlighting
  - Brief description
  - Request parameters (query params for GET, body for POST)
  - Response format (JSON schema summary)
  - Example request (curl or fetch)
  - Example response (collapsible JSON block)
  - Authentication requirements
  - Rate limits

The method badge follows a standard color scheme:
  - GET: --accent-teal (read operation)
  - POST: --accent-gold (create operation)
  - PUT: --accent-blue (update operation)
  - DELETE: --danger (delete operation)

URL parameters are highlighted inline: /api/v1/graphs/{graph_id} where
{graph_id} is shown in --accent-gold with a tooltip explaining the parameter.

The block includes a "Try it" section where the user can see a pre-filled
curl command and copy it. This is NOT a live execution environment -- Athena
never executes API calls on behalf of the user. It is a documentation
reference with ready-to-use examples.

The example request and response sections use the Python and JSON code blocks
(Features #101 and #103) internally, providing syntax highlighting and copy
functionality for the code snippets within the endpoint documentation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
USER EXPERIENCE NARRATIVE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TRIGGER:
User asks about API endpoints. Triggered by: (a) "How do I fetch a graph
via API?" (b) /api command, (c) Athena explains integration options,
(d) developer asks about authentication or rate limits.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. User asks: "How do I fetch a graph using the API?"

2. Athena responds with an endpoint documentation block:
   "Here is the endpoint to retrieve a graph:"

   [GET] /api/v1/graphs/{graph_id}
   Retrieve the full graph JSON for a previously built graph.

   Parameters:
     graph_id (path, required) -- 32-char hex graph identifier

   Headers:
     Authorization: Bearer {api_key}  (required)

   Response (200):
     { nodes: [...], edges: [...], metadata: {...} }

   Example:
     curl -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_..." \
       https://arivu.app/api/v1/graphs/a1b2c3d4...

   Rate Limit: 100 requests/hour (researcher tier)
   [Copy curl] [View full response schema]

3. User clicks "Copy curl." The curl command is copied to clipboard.

4. User clicks "View full response schema." An expandable JSON block
   shows the complete response structure.

AFTER COMPLETION:
Endpoint documentation persists in chat. Copyable examples always available.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FILES:
- static/js/athena-blocks.js: AthenaEndpointBlock class. Renders endpoint
  card with method badge, parameters, examples, response schema.
- static/js/athena-engine.js: Routes API documentation requests.
- static/css/athena-chat.css: Endpoint card styles, method badges, param table.
- app.py: /api/athena/chat generates endpoint blocks.
- backend/chat_guide.py: Endpoint documentation data for Arivu API.

FRONTEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  class AthenaEndpointBlock {
    constructor(data) {
      this.method = data.method;       // GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
      this.path = data.path;           // /api/v1/graphs/{graph_id}
      this.description = data.description;
      this.params = data.params || [];
      this.headers = data.headers || [];
      this.responseSchema = data.response_schema;
      this.example = data.example;
      this.rateLimit = data.rate_limit;
      this.auth = data.auth;
    }

    render() {
      const el = document.createElement('div');
      el.className = 'athena-endpoint-block';
      el.innerHTML = `
        <div class="endpoint-header">
          <span class="method-badge method-${this.method.toLowerCase()}">${this.method}</span>
          <code class="endpoint-path">${this._highlightPath()}</code>
        </div>
        <p class="endpoint-desc">${this.description}</p>
        ${this._renderParams()}
        ${this._renderHeaders()}
        ${this._renderAuth()}
        ${this._renderExample()}
        ${this._renderResponse()}
        ${this._renderRateLimit()}`;
      return el;
    }

    _highlightPath() {
      return this.path.replace(/\{(\w+)\}/g,
        '<span class="path-param">{$1}</span>');
    }

    _renderParams() {
      if (!this.params.length) return '';
      let html = '<div class="endpoint-section"><h5>Parameters</h5><table class="param-table">';
      this.params.forEach(p => {
        html += `<tr>
          <td><code>${p.name}</code></td>
          <td><span class="param-in">${p.in}</span></td>
          <td>${p.required ? '<span class="param-req">required</span>' : 'optional'}</td>
          <td>${p.description}</td>
        </tr>`;
      });
      return html + '</table></div>';
    }

    _renderExample() {
      if (!this.example) return '';
      return `<div class="endpoint-section">
        <h5>Example</h5>
        <pre class="endpoint-curl"><code>${this._escapeHtml(this.example.curl)}</code></pre>
        <button class="code-copy endpoint-copy-curl">Copy</button>
      </div>`;
    }
  }

BACKEND IMPLEMENTATION:

  # In chat_guide.py -- endpoint documentation registry
  ENDPOINT_DOCS = {
      'get_graph': {
          'method': 'GET',
          'path': '/api/v1/graphs/{graph_id}',
          'description': 'Retrieve the full graph JSON for a built graph.',
          'params': [
              {'name': 'graph_id', 'in': 'path', 'required': True,
               'description': '32-char hex graph identifier'}
          ],
          'headers': [
              {'name': 'Authorization', 'value': 'Bearer {api_key}', 'required': True}
          ],
          'auth': 'Required. Any tier.',
          'rate_limit': '100 req/hour (researcher), 1000 req/hour (developer)',
          'response_schema': {'nodes': '[...]', 'edges': '[...]', 'metadata': '{...}'},
          'example': {
              'curl': 'curl -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_..." https://arivu.app/api/v1/graphs/a1b2c3d4'
          }
      }
  }

API CONTRACTS:
POST /api/athena/chat
Response (API documentation):
{
  "response": "Here is the endpoint:",
  "blocks": [{
    "type": "endpoint",
    "method": "GET",
    "path": "/api/v1/graphs/{graph_id}",
    "description": "Retrieve graph JSON",
    "params": [...],
    "headers": [...],
    "auth": "Required",
    "rate_limit": "100 req/hour",
    "response_schema": {...},
    "example": {"curl": "curl ..."}
  }]
}

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
VISUAL SPECIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

STATE: Endpoint Block Rendered

  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
  |  [Athena]                                                     |
  |  Here is the endpoint to retrieve a graph:                    |
  |                                                               |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------------+  |
  |  | [GET] /api/v1/graphs/{graph_id}                          |  |
  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  | Retrieve the full graph JSON for a previously built      |  |
  |  | graph.                                                   |  |
  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  | Parameters                                               |  |
  |  | +---------+------+----------+------------------------+   |  |
  |  | | graph_id | path | required | 32-char hex identifier |   |  |
  |  | +---------+------+----------+------------------------+   |  |
  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  | Headers                                                  |  |
  |  |   Authorization: Bearer {api_key}  (required)            |  |
  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  | Example                                    [Copy curl]   |  |
  |  | curl -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_..." \                 |  |
  |  |   https://arivu.app/api/v1/graphs/a1b2...               |  |
  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  | Response (200)              [View full schema]            |  |
  |  |   { nodes: [...], edges: [...], metadata: {...} }        |  |
  |  |                                                          |  |
  |  | Rate Limit: 100 req/hour (researcher)                    |  |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------------+  |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+

STATE: Method Badges

  [GET]    -- teal background (--accent-teal), white text
  [POST]   -- gold background (--accent-gold), dark text
  [PUT]    -- blue background (--accent-blue), white text
  [DELETE] -- red background (--danger), white text

STATE: Path Parameters Highlighted

  /api/v1/graphs/{graph_id}
                  ^^^^^^^^^^^ gold text (--accent-gold)

TRANSITIONS:
Block render: fade in 200ms.
Schema expand: height 0 to auto, 250ms ease.
Copy curl: button text swap "Copy" -> "Copied!" 2s.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DATA FLOW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User asks about API endpoint
  -> athena-engine.js sends to /api/athena/chat
  -> chat_guide.py matches user query to ENDPOINT_DOCS registry
  -> Returns endpoint block with full documentation
  -> athena-blocks.js renders AthenaEndpointBlock
  -> User clicks Copy curl -> clipboard write
  -> User clicks View schema -> JSON block expands

ERROR PATH:
  -> Unknown endpoint: "I do not have documentation for that endpoint.
     Available endpoints: [list]."
  -> Endpoint documentation outdated: note API version in block.
  -> Copy fails: fallback to text selection.

DATA STORED: Endpoint block in chat_history.
DATA CACHED: Rendered DOM.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LLM INTEGRATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MODEL: config.GROQ_FAST_MODEL

STAGE: Endpoint identification. When the user asks about API functionality,
the LLM identifies which endpoint to document.

PROMPT:
```
You are Athena, the research intelligence assistant for Arivu.
The user wants API documentation.
User query: {user_message}

Available endpoints:
- get_graph: GET /api/v1/graphs/{graph_id}
- build_graph: POST /api/v1/graphs/build
- search_papers: GET /api/v1/papers/search
- get_edge: GET /api/v1/edges/{edge_id}
- user_profile: GET /api/v1/user

Return JSON: {"endpoint_key": "get_graph"}
If the user is asking a general API question, return: {"endpoint_key": null, "general": true}
Return ONLY valid JSON.
```

OUTPUT FORMAT: JSON identifying endpoint
FALLBACK: List all available endpoints
TOKEN BUDGET: ~100 tokens per endpoint identification

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERACTIVE BEHAVIORS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Element              | Action | Response                                   |
|----------------------|--------|--------------------------------------------|
| Method badge         | --     | Static display                             |
| Path parameter       | Hover  | Tooltip: "graph_id: 32-char hex string"    |
| Copy curl button     | Click  | Copy curl command to clipboard             |
| View schema button   | Click  | Expand JSON response schema                |
| Param table          | --     | Static display                             |
| Rate limit           | --     | Static display                             |

ANIMATION DETAILS:
- Block fade: 200ms ease
- Schema expand: height transition 250ms
- Copy: text swap 150ms, revert 2s
- Path param hover: tooltip fade in 100ms

SOUND EFFECTS: NOT APPLICABLE.

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Endpoint block: role="region" aria-label "API endpoint documentation"
- Method badge: aria-label "HTTP method: GET"
- Path param: aria-describedby for tooltip
- Param table: standard table role with headers
- Keyboard: Tab between interactive elements
- Screen reader: "API endpoint. GET. /api/v1/graphs/{graph_id}."

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EDGE CASES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Scenario: User asks about multiple endpoints
   Expected: Multiple endpoint blocks rendered sequentially.
   Implementation: Each endpoint is a separate block.

2. Scenario: Endpoint with 10+ parameters (complex POST body)
   Expected: Parameters table scrollable or collapsible by category.
   Implementation: Collapse optional parameters by default.

3. Scenario: Rate limit varies by tier
   Expected: Show all tiers: "100/hr (researcher), 1000/hr (developer)."
   Implementation: rate_limit field supports multi-tier display.

4. Scenario: User has no API key (free tier, no API access)
   Expected: Block shows endpoint but notes: "API access requires
   researcher tier or above."
   Implementation: auth field indicates tier requirements.

5. Scenario: Endpoint URL very long (deeply nested resource)
   Expected: URL wraps cleanly. Parameters remain highlighted.
   Implementation: word-break: break-all on endpoint-path.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DEPENDENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
